topics.title: SRS for College Library Management Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SRS for College Library Management Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Software Requirements Specification (SRS)?

A **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal, structured, and comprehensive document that completely describes the intended behaviour, functionality, constraints, and quality attributes of a software system. It serves as the single source of truth that bridges the communication gap between the **stakeholders** (users, customers, management) and the **development team** (designers, programmers, testers).

According to the **IEEE 830-1998 Standard**, an SRS is defined as:

> *"A software requirements specification is a document that clearly and precisely describes each of the essential requirements (functions, performance, design constraints, and external interfaces) of the software and the external interfaces that the software must satisfy."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the KTU Software Engineering (PECST411) syllabus, Module 1 explicitly trains students to **identify requirements, write functional & non-functional requirements, and prepare a structured SRS document** as a foundational engineering artefact. The "College Library Management Software" is the prescribed illustrative case study.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — The Building Blueprint

Think of the SRS exactly like the **architectural blueprint of a building**:

| Real World | Software Engineering World |
|---|---|
| Architectural Blueprint | Software Requirements Specification (SRS) |
| Client (Homeowner) | Stakeholder / End User |
| Architect | System Analyst / Business Analyst |
| Construction Workers | Software Developers |
| Building Inspector | Quality Assurance / Testing Team |

Just as a homeowner cannot tell a mason *"build me a nice house"* without a blueprint (and the mason cannot guess the number of rooms, plumbing layout, or load-bearing walls), the software development team **cannot write code** based on vague statements like *"make a good library system"*. The SRS is the **blueprint of the software**.

## 1.3 Why is SRS Important for College Library Management Software?

A College Library handles **hundreds of students, thousands of books, multiple categories, late returns, fines, reservations, e-resources, and inter-library loans** every day. Without a formal SRS:

- The **Librarian** might expect barcode-based issuing, but the developer builds a manual ledger system.
- The **Student** might want a mobile-friendly search, but the team only builds a desktop GUI.
- The **Accounts Department** might want fine calculation reports, but the team forgets to include that module.

The SRS eliminates these ambiguities *before* a single line of code is written.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Benefits of a Good SRS (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. Provides a **basis for software design** and **coding**.
> 2. Acts as a **validation and verification** baseline for V&V testing.
> 3. Serves as a **legal contract** between client and developer.
> 4. Enables **cost estimation, scheduling, and feasibility study**.
> 5. Forms the basis for **software maintenance** and future enhancements.

## 1.4 Position of SRS in the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)

The SRS is produced at the **end of the Requirements Engineering phase**, after feasibility analysis and requirement elicitation, and **before** the system design phase.

$$\text{Problem Statement} \rightarrow \text{Feasibility Study} \rightarrow \text{Requirement Elicitation} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{SRS Document}} \rightarrow \text{System Design} \rightarrow \text{Coding} \rightarrow \text{Testing} \rightarrow \text{Deployment}$$

The SRS document is the **transition artefact** from the *problem domain* (what the user wants) to the *solution domain* (what the developer builds).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics of a Good SRS Document (IEEE 830 Properties)

A high-quality SRS must be:

- **Correct** – Every requirement stated is one that the software must meet.
- **Unambiguous** – Each requirement has only one possible interpretation.
- **Complete** – All significant requirements (functional, performance, constraints, design) are included.
- **Consistent** – No conflicts between individual requirements.
- **Ranked for Importance/Stability** – Each requirement is tagged with priority (e.g., High, Medium, Low) and stability.
- **Verifiable** – Every requirement can be tested and validated objectively.
- **Modifiable** – Changes can be made easily while maintaining consistency.
- **Traceable** – Each requirement has a unique ID so it can be tracked forward (to design/code) and backward (to stakeholder need).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (KTU Exam Tip): "C U C R R V M T"** — Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable.

## 2.2 The Structure of an SRS Document (IEEE 830 Standard Template)

A standard SRS document follows this hierarchical structure. We will use this exact structure for the College Library Management Software.

| Section | Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Introduction** | Purpose, Scope, Definitions, References, Overview |
| 1.1 | Purpose | Why this document exists |
| 1.2 | Scope | What the software does and does not do |
| 1.3 | Definitions & Abbreviations | Glossary of terms |
| 1.4 | References | Other documents cited |
| 1.5 | Overview | Summary of remaining sections |
| 2 | **Overall Description** | Product perspective, functions, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions |
| 3 | **Specific Requirements** | Detailed functional + non-functional + external interface requirements |
| 4 | **Appendices** | Supporting data, mockups, sample forms |

## 2.3 Types of Requirements

### 2.3.1 Functional Requirements (FR)
These define **what the system must do** — the specific behaviours, functions, and services of the system.

*Examples for Library System:* Search for a book, Issue a book, Return a book, Calculate fine, Generate member ID card.

### 2.3.2 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)
These define **how the system must perform** — the quality attributes, constraints, and standards.

*Examples for Library System:* The search result must appear within **2 seconds**, the system must support **500 concurrent users**, available **99.9% of the time** (uptime).

### 2.3.3 Domain Requirements
These come from the application domain and reflect characteristics of the problem world.

*Example:* The library follows the **Dewey Decimal Classification (DDC) system** for organising books.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Example (Library System) |
|---|---|---|
| **SRS** | Software Requirements Specification | Document describing Library System |
| **IEEE 830** | IEEE Standard for SRS structure | Template used to write SRS |
| **FR** | Functional Requirement | "System shall allow book issue" |
| **NFR** | Non-Functional Requirement | "Response time less than 2 seconds" |
| **DFD** | Data Flow Diagram | Level 0/1/2 DFD of Library System |
| **ERD** | Entity Relationship Diagram | Shows BOOK, MEMBER, ISSUED entities |
| **UC** | Use Case | UC01: Issue Book, UC02: Return Book |
| **UML** | Unified Modelling Language | Language for software modelling |
| **PI** | Performance Index (Quality Metric) | Books issued per hour $\geq 30$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Definition Box (Board Favourites)

> [!NOTE]
> **Requirements Engineering (RE):** The systematic process of developing requirements through activities such as **elicitation, analysis, specification, validation, and management** of stakeholder needs.
>
> **Use Case:** A description of a sequence of actions performed by a system to yield an **observable result of value** to a particular actor.
>
> **Feasibility Study:** An investigation carried out to determine whether a proposed software system is **technically, economically, operationally, legally, and schedule-wise** viable.

## 2.6 Why is this Topic Engineering-Critical?

In the professional world, **nearly 60-70% of software project failures** are attributed to poor, incomplete, or ambiguous requirements. The SRS is therefore not just a documentation formality — it is a **risk-mitigation artefact** and a **legal contract** that directly impacts:
- Project cost overruns
- Schedule slippage
- User satisfaction
- Maintainability of the final product
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step SRS Construction — College Library Management Software (CLMS)

Below is the **complete, exhaustive, KTU-examination-ready SRS** for the College Library Management Software. Every sub-section follows the IEEE 830 template.

---

## 3.1 SECTION 1: INTRODUCTION

### 3.1.1 Purpose of the Document
The purpose of this SRS is to provide a detailed and complete description of the **College Library Management Software (CLMS)**. It specifies the functional and non-functional requirements, external interfaces, design constraints, and quality attributes. The intended audience includes:

- The College Library Committee (client)
- The Project Manager and Development Team
- The Quality Assurance and Testing Team
- Future maintenance engineers

### 3.1.2 Scope of the Software
The **College Library Management Software (CLMS)** is a web-based, multi-user application intended to automate and streamline the day-to-day operations of a college library. The system will support:

**In-Scope Features:**
- Book acquisition, cataloguing, and inventory
- Member registration (students, faculty, staff)
- Book issue, return, and renewal
- Fine calculation for overdue books
- Online search and reservation
- Report generation (issue, return, inventory, fine)
- User authentication and role-based access

**Out-of-Scope Features:**
- Inter-library loan across multiple colleges
- E-book purchase/licensing
- Integration with external publishers

### 3.1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| CLMS | College Library Management Software |
| ISBN | International Standard Book Number |
| DDC | Dewey Decimal Classification |
| FR | Functional Requirement |
| NFR | Non-Functional Requirement |
| UI | User Interface |
| DBMS | Database Management System |
| Admin | System Administrator |
| Librarian | Library Staff Member |
| Member | Registered library user (student/faculty) |
| Borrower | A member who has currently issued a book |

### 3.1.4 References
- IEEE Std 830-1998 — Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
- IEEE Std 1233 — Guide for Developing System Requirements Specifications.
- College Library Operational Manual, Internal College Document.

### 3.1.5 Document Overview
Section 2 describes the overall product, its users, constraints, and assumptions. Section 3 contains the specific functional, non-functional, and interface requirements. Appendices contain data dictionaries and sample screen mock-ups.

---

## 3.2 SECTION 2: OVERALL DESCRIPTION

### 3.2.1 Product Perspective
The CLMS is a **self-contained, new software product** intended to replace the existing manual register-based library system. It will operate as a **client-server web application** with a central relational database. The system is expected to interact with:

- The College ERP for student data synchronisation.
- The biometric attendance system for ID verification.
- The barcode scanner hardware for book identification.

### 3.2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
The major functions of the CLMS are:
1. Manage Books (Add, Update, Remove, Search)
2. Manage Members (Register, Update, Suspend)
3. Issue / Return / Renew Books
4. Calculate and Track Fines
5. Generate Reports
6. User Authentication and Authorisation

### 3.2.3 User Characteristics

| User Class | Technical Skill | Frequency of Use | Privilege |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Student Member** | Basic computer literacy | Daily (high) | Search, Reserve, View own history |
| **Faculty Member** | Basic-Moderate | Weekly (medium) | All of student + Request bulk loan |
| **Librarian** | High | Daily (very high) | Issue, Return, Fine, Add Books |
| **System Admin** | Expert | As needed | All privileges + DB management |

### 3.2.4 Constraints
- The software must be developed using **Java (Spring Boot) or Python (Django)** with MySQL/PostgreSQL DBMS.
- It must run on any standard web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge).
- It must comply with the **college's data privacy policy**.
- Internet connectivity is assumed for the web portal; offline mode only for circulation desk operations.

### 3.2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies
- A stable power supply and LAN are available in the library.
- Every book has a unique **barcode / RFID tag**.
- Every member has a unique **college-issued ID number**.
- The college has at most **5,000 active members** and **50,000 book records**.

---

## 3.3 SECTION 3: SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS

### 3.3.1 External Interface Requirements

**3.3.1.1 User Interfaces**
- The UI must follow the college's existing branding (logo, colour scheme).
- The system shall provide separate dashboards for Librarian, Admin, Faculty, and Student.
- Input forms must include on-screen validation and helpful error messages.

**3.3.1.2 Hardware Interfaces**
- Barcode scanner (USB) for book identification.
- Printer (for receipts and reports).
- Barcode printer (for new book tagging).

**3.3.1.3 Software Interfaces**
- DBMS: PostgreSQL 14 or higher.
- Web Server: Apache Tomcat 10.
- Operating System: Linux (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS) / Windows Server 2019.

**3.3.1.4 Communication Interfaces**
- HTTPS protocol (port 443) for secure communication.
- RESTful API endpoints for ERP integration.

---

### 3.3.2 Functional Requirements (FR)

The following functional requirements are written in the form: *"The system shall…"*

| FR-ID | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|
| FR-01 | High | The system shall allow the **Admin to add, update, and delete book records** including Title, Author, ISBN, Publisher, DDC code, Edition, and Number of Copies. |
| FR-02 | High | The system shall allow the **Librarian to register a new member** by capturing Name, ID, Role (Student/Faculty), Department, Contact, and Photograph. |
| FR-03 | High | The system shall allow the **Librarian to issue a book** to a registered member by scanning the book barcode and entering the member ID. |
| FR-04 | High | The system shall **validate the issue request** by checking (a) member's active status, (b) outstanding fines, (c) maximum issue limit (3 for students, 5 for faculty), and (d) book availability. |
| FR-05 | High | The system shall generate a **unique transaction ID** for every issue and print a receipt. |
| FR-06 | High | The system shall allow the **Librarian to accept a returned book**, mark it as available, and compute the fine if overdue. |
| FR-07 | Medium | The system shall allow the **member to renew a book** online for a maximum of two times, provided no other member has reserved it. |
| FR-08 | High | The system shall **calculate the fine** at the rate of ₹2 per day for students and ₹5 per day for faculty for overdue books. |
| FR-09 | High | The system shall provide a **search facility** (by Title, Author, ISBN, DDC, or Keyword) with results paginated 20 per page. |
| FR-10 | Medium | The system shall allow a member to **reserve a book** that is currently issued, and notify them via email when the book becomes available. |
| FR-11 | Medium | The system shall send **automatic email/SMS reminders** to members 3 days before the due date. |
| FR-12 | High | The system shall generate **daily, weekly, and monthly reports** for: books issued, books returned, overdue list, fine collected, and most-borrowed books. |
| FR-13 | High | The system shall enforce **role-based access control (RBAC)** — only Admins can add/delete books, only Librarians can issue/return, and Members can only search/reserve. |
| FR-14 | Medium | The system shall maintain an **audit log** of all critical operations (issue, return, fine waiver, member deletion). |
| FR-15 | High | The system shall support **secure login** using username and password, with password hashing (BCrypt) and lockout after 5 failed attempts. |

---

### 3.3.3 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)

| NFR-ID | Category | Description |
|---|---|---|
| NFR-01 | **Performance** | The system shall return search results within **2 seconds** for 95% of queries. |
| NFR-02 | **Performance** | The system shall support at least **500 concurrent users** without degradation. |
| NFR-03 | **Availability** | The system shall have an availability of **99.5%** during college working hours (8 AM – 8 PM). |
| NFR-04 | **Security** | All data transmission shall be over **HTTPS (TLS 1.3)**. Passwords shall be stored using **BCrypt hashing**. |
| NFR-05 | **Reliability** | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) shall be at least **720 hours**. |
| NFR-06 | **Usability** | A new librarian shall be able to perform all core operations (issue, return, search) within **1 hour of training**. |
| NFR-07 | **Maintainability** | Code shall follow standard coding conventions; new modules shall be addable without modifying existing code (Open/Closed Principle). |
| NFR-08 | **Portability** | The web application shall run on Chrome 100+, Firefox 100+, and Edge 100+ browsers. |
| NFR-09 | **Scalability** | The system architecture shall allow horizontal scaling of the application server. |
| NFR-10 | **Backup** | Automated database backup shall occur every **24 hours**, retained for **30 days**. |
| NFR-11 | **Compliance** | The system shall comply with the **IT Act 2000 (India)** and the college's data retention policy. |
| NFR-12 | **Localization** | The user interface shall support **English and Hindi** languages. |

---

### 3.3.4 Logical Database Requirements (Conceptual Data Model)

The following core entities and relationships are required:

- **BOOK** (book_id, title, author, isbn, ddc_code, publisher, year, edition, total_copies, available_copies)
- **MEMBER** (member_id, name, role, department, contact, email, status, max_books)
- **TRANSACTION** (trans_id, book_id, member_id, issue_date, due_date, return_date, fine_amount)
- **RESERVATION** (res_id, book_id, member_id, reservation_date, status)
- **CATEGORY** (category_id, category_name, ddc_range)
- **FINE** (fine_id, trans_id, amount, paid_status, payment_date)
- **USER** (user_id, username, password_hash, role, last_login)

### 3.3.5 Design Constraints
- The frontend shall be developed using **React.js** or **Angular**.
- The backend shall expose a **REST API**.
- The application must be deployable on a **Docker container**.

### 3.3.6 Software System Attributes
- **Reliability:** Recovery time after failure $\leq 15$ minutes.
- **Maintainability:** Modular code, with comment ratio $\geq 15\%$.
- **Reusability:** Core utilities (fine calculator, barcode validator) shall be reusable across modules.

---

## 3.4 Appendices

**Appendix A: Use Case — Issue a Book**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Use Case ID** | UC-01 |
| **Name** | Issue a Book |
| **Actor** | Librarian |
| **Pre-condition** | Librarian is logged in; book is available; member is registered and active. |
| **Main Flow** | 1. Librarian scans book barcode. 2. System displays book details. 3. Librarian enters Member ID. 4. System validates eligibility. 5. System sets due date (14 days). 6. System records transaction and updates book status. 7. System prints receipt. |
| **Post-condition** | Book is marked as issued; transaction is stored. |
| **Alternate Flow** | If member has overdue books, system displays message and blocks issue. |
| **Exception Flow** | If book barcode is invalid, system shows error "Invalid Book". |

**Appendix B: Data Dictionary (Sample)**

| Field Name | Data Type | Size | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| book_id | INT | 10 | Primary Key, Auto Increment |
| isbn | VARCHAR | 13 | Unique, Not Null |
| title | VARCHAR | 200 | Not Null |
| available_copies | INT | 4 | $\geq 0$ |
| fine_amount | DECIMAL | $(10,2)$ | $\geq 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use Case Diagram — College Library Management Software

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    actor1([Student Member])
    actor2([Faculty Member])
    actor3([Librarian])
    actor4([System Admin])

    uc1((UC01\nRegister Member))
    uc2((UC02\nLogin to System))
    uc3((UC03\nSearch Book))
    uc4((UC04\nReserve Book))
    uc5((UC05\nRenew Book))
    uc6((UC06\nView Own History))
    uc7((UC07\nRequest Bulk Loan))
    uc8((UC08\nIssue Book))
    uc9((UC09\nReturn Book))
    uc10((UC10\nCalculate Fine))
    uc11((UC11\nGenerate Report))
    uc12((UC12\nAdd or Update Book))
    uc13((UC13\nManage Users))
    uc14((UC14\nView Audit Log))

    actor1 --> uc2
    actor1 --> uc3
    actor1 --> uc4
    actor1 --> uc5
    actor1 --> uc6

    actor2 --> uc2
    actor2 --> uc3
    actor2 --> uc4
    actor2 --> uc6
    actor2 --> uc7

    actor3 --> uc2
    actor3 --> uc8
    actor3 --> uc9
    actor3 --> uc10
    actor3 --> uc11
    actor3 --> uc15

    actor4 --> uc1
    actor4 --> uc12
    actor4 --> uc13
    actor4 --> uc14
    actor4 --> uc11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Student Member** can: Login, Search, Reserve, Renew, View History.
> - **Librarian** can: Login, Issue, Return, Calculate Fine, Generate Report, Send Reminder.
> - **System Admin** can: Register Members, Add/Update Books, Manage Users, View Audit Log, Generate Reports.

---

## 4.2 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) — Level 0 (Context Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1([Student]) -- book issue request, search query --> sysC[CLMS System]
    s2([Faculty]) -- bulk loan request, search query --> sysC
    s3([Librarian]) -- issue, return, fine data --> sysC
    s4([Admin]) -- book and member data --> sysC
    sysC -- issue receipt, return confirmation, fine receipt --> s1
    sysC -- reports, renewal approval --> s2
    sysC -- daily report, fine report --> s3
    sysC -- system logs, audit report --> s4
    sysC -- overdue notice, due reminder --> ext1[(Email or SMS Server)]
    sysC -- member data sync --> ext2[(College ERP)]
```

---

## 4.3 DFD Level 1 — Major Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    memberIn([Member Data]) --> p1[Process 1\nMember Management]
    bookIn([Book Data]) --> p2[Process 2\nBook Management]
    issueIn([Issue Request]) --> p3[Process 3\nCirculation]
    returnIn([Return Request]) --> p3
    p1 --> db1[(Member Database)]
    p2 --> db2[(Book Database)]
    p3 --> db3[(Transaction Database)]
    p3 --> db2
    p3 --> db1
    p3 --> p4[Process 4\nFine Calculator]
    p4 --> db3
    db1 --> p5[Process 5\nReport Generator]
    db2 --> p5
    db3 --> p5
    p5 --> outR([Reports and Receipts])
```

---

## 4.4 Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[BOOK]
    M[MEMBER]
    T[TRANSACTION]
    R[RESERVATION]
    F[FINE]
    C[CATEGORY]
    U[USER]

    C -- categorizes --> B
    B -- is issued in --> T
    M -- initiates --> T
    T -- generates --> F
    M -- places --> R
    B -- is reserved by --> R
    M -- has login --> U
```

**Cardinality Summary:**

| Relationship | Cardinality | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| MEMBER : TRANSACTION | 1 : N | One member can have many transactions |
| BOOK : TRANSACTION | 1 : N | One book can appear in many transactions |
| TRANSACTION : FINE | 1 : 0..1 | A transaction may have zero or one fine |
| MEMBER : RESERVATION | 1 : N | A member can place many reservations |
| CATEGORY : BOOK | 1 : N | One category contains many books |
| MEMBER : USER | 1 : 1 | Each member has exactly one login |

---

```mermaid
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1

    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B4 --> D1
    D2 --> B4
    B3 --> D3
```
