# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Software Requirements Specification (SRS). List any four characteristics of a good SRS. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**
A Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is a formal document that completely describes the intended behaviour, functions, performance, design constraints, and external interfaces of a software system. It acts as a contract between the customer and the developer.

Four characteristics of a good SRS:
1. **Correct** – Every requirement truly represents what the user needs.
2. **Unambiguous** – Each requirement has only one possible interpretation.
3. **Complete** – All functional, non-functional, and interface requirements are included.
4. **Verifiable** – Every requirement can be objectively tested.

*(For full 3 marks, students must define SRS and list 4 characteristics with one-line explanations.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with one example each, in the context of a Library Management System. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Describes *what* the system does | Describes *how well* the system does it |
| **Focus** | Specific behaviour / features | Quality attributes / constraints |
| **Example (Library)** | "The system shall allow the librarian to issue a book to a member" | "The search result shall be returned within 2 seconds for 95% of queries" |
| **Test Type** | Functional / Black-box test | Performance / Load / Stress test |
| **Measured By** | Pass / Fail | Quantitative metric (time, %, count) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When asked to "write an SRS" or "prepare an SRS for …", students often write a vague paragraph in essay form. The KTU Board specifically deducts marks if the answer does **NOT follow the IEEE 830 structure** (Introduction → Overall Description → Specific Requirements → Appendices). Always use **numbered headings, FR-XX and NFR-XX IDs, and tables**. Also, do not forget **Non-Functional Requirements** — writing only functional requirements can cost 3-4 marks.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** Prepare a detailed **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** for a **College Library Management Software (CLMS)**. Include: (a) Introduction and Overall Description (Purpose, Scope, User Characteristics, Constraints, Assumptions). (b) Specific Requirements — at least **8 Functional** and **5 Non-Functional Requirements** with unique IDs, along with a brief description of external interfaces. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Introduction and Overall Description (7 Marks)**

**1. Introduction**

**1.1 Purpose:** This SRS describes the complete requirements of the College Library Management Software (CLMS), a web-based application to automate library operations such as book management, member registration, issue/return, fine calculation, and report generation. The audience includes the Library Committee, Project Manager, Developers, Testers, and future maintenance engineers.

**1.2 Scope:** The CLMS is a multi-user, web-based system that replaces the existing manual register system. It covers book cataloguing, member registration, circulation (issue/return/renewal), fine computation, search and reservation, and report generation. It is *not* intended for inter-library loan or e-book licensing.

**1.3 Definitions & Abbreviations:** CLMS – College Library Management Software; ISBN – International Standard Book Number; DDC – Dewey Decimal Classification; RBAC – Role-Based Access Control; FR – Functional Requirement; NFR – Non-Functional Requirement.

**1.4 References:** IEEE 830-1998 Standard; IEEE 1233 Guide; College Library Operational Manual.

**2. Overall Description**

**2.1 Product Perspective:** CLMS is a new self-contained client-server web application. It will integrate with the College ERP for student data synchronisation.

**2.2 User Characteristics:**

| User | Skill Level | Privilege |
|---|---|---|
| Student | Basic | Search, Reserve, View History |
| Faculty | Basic-Moderate | Bulk Loan Request |
| Librarian | High | Issue, Return, Fine, Reports |
| Admin | Expert | All Privileges |

**2.3 Constraints:** Built using Java (Spring Boot) + React.js + PostgreSQL; runs on Chrome/Firefox/Edge; must follow college privacy policy.

**2.4 Assumptions:** LAN available; books have barcodes; member count $\leq 5,000$; book records $\leq 50,000$.

---

**(b) Specific Requirements (7 Marks)**

**3.1 External Interfaces:**
- **User Interface:** Web-based dashboard, responsive design, role-based view.
- **Hardware Interface:** Barcode scanner, receipt printer.
- **Software Interface:** PostgreSQL 14 DBMS, Apache Tomcat server, REST APIs.
- **Communication Interface:** HTTPS (port 443) for security.

**3.2 Functional Requirements:**

| FR-ID | Description |
|---|---|
| **FR-01** | The system shall allow the Admin to add, update, or delete a book record with fields: Title, Author, ISBN, DDC code, Edition, Total Copies. |
| **FR-02** | The system shall allow the Librarian to register a new member capturing Name, ID, Role, Department, Contact, Photograph. |
| **FR-03** | The system shall allow the Librarian to issue a book after scanning the book barcode and entering the member ID. |
| **FR-04** | The system shall validate the issue against: (i) member active status, (ii) outstanding fines, (iii) max issue limit (3 students / 5 faculty), (iv) book availability. |
| **FR-05** | The system shall generate a unique transaction ID and print a receipt for every successful issue. |
| **FR-06** | The system shall allow return of a book, update its status, and compute the fine if overdue. |
| **FR-07** | The system shall allow a member to search books by Title, Author, ISBN, DDC, or Keyword with paginated results (20 per page). |
| **FR-08** | The system shall generate daily, weekly, and monthly reports: books issued, returned, overdue, fine collected, and most-borrowed books. |

**3.3 Non-Functional Requirements:**

| NFR-ID | Category | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **NFR-01** | Performance | Search results must be returned within **2 seconds** for 95% of queries. |
| **NFR-02** | Availability | System uptime $\geq 99.5\%$ during 8 AM – 8 PM working hours. |
| **NFR-03** | Security | All data over HTTPS (TLS 1.3); passwords hashed with BCrypt; lockout after 5 failed attempts. |
| **NFR-04** | Usability | A new librarian must be able to perform core operations within 1 hour of training. |
| **NFR-05** | Backup | Automated database backup every 24 hours, retained for 30 days. |

---

### PART B — Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)

**Q.B.** (a) Explain the **IEEE 830 standard structure** of an SRS document with a neat block diagram. (b) For a **Library Management System**, identify and document **10 Functional Requirements** and **6 Non-Functional Requirements** using the IEEE 830 standard format. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

#### Model Solution:

**(a) IEEE 830 Standard Structure (7 Marks)**

The IEEE 830 standard prescribes the following hierarchical structure for an SRS:

1. **Section 1 — Introduction**
   - 1.1 Purpose
   - 1.2 Scope of the software
   - 1.3 Definitions, acronyms, abbreviations
   - 1.4 References
   - 1.5 Document overview

2. **Section 2 — Overall Description**
   - 2.1 Product perspective
   - 2.2 Product functions
   - 2.3 User characteristics
   - 2.4 Constraints
   - 2.5 Assumptions and dependencies

3. **Section 3 — Specific Requirements**
   - 3.1 External interface requirements
   - 3.2 Functional requirements
   - 3.3 Non-functional requirements
   - 3.4 Logical database requirements
   - 3.5 Design constraints
   - 3.6 Software system attributes

4. **Section 4 — Appendices** (mockups, data dictionary, sample forms)

This structured approach ensures the SRS is **complete, unambiguous, verifiable, traceable, and modifiable** — the five core properties defined by IEEE 830.

---

**(b) 10 Functional and 6 Non-Functional Requirements for Library Management System (7 Marks)**

**Functional Requirements (FR):**

| FR-ID | Requirement |
|---|---|
| FR-01 | The system shall allow the admin to add new books to the catalogue. |
| FR-02 | The system shall allow the librarian to issue a book to a registered member. |
| FR-03 | The system shall allow the librarian to accept a book return and update inventory. |
| FR-04 | The system shall calculate the overdue fine automatically. |
| FR-05 | The system shall allow a member to search the book catalogue by title, author, or ISBN. |
| FR-06 | The system shall allow a member to reserve a book that is currently issued. |
| FR-07 | The system shall allow a member to renew an issued book (max 2 times). |
| FR-08 | The system shall send email/SMS reminders 3 days before the due date. |
| FR-09 | The system shall generate inventory and circulation reports. |
| FR-10 | The system shall maintain an audit log of all issue/return and fine transactions. |

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):**

| NFR-ID | Category | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| NFR-01 | Performance | Search results within 2 seconds. |
| NFR-02 | Performance | Support 500 concurrent users. |
| NFR-03 | Availability | 99.5% uptime during working hours. |
| NFR-04 | Security | HTTPS, BCrypt password hashing. |
| NFR-05 | Reliability | MTBF $\geq 720$ hours. |
| NFR-06 | Usability | New librarian can be trained in 1 hour. |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **SRS** = Software Requirements Specification, defined under **IEEE 830-1998**.
- ✅ SRS is the **bridge document** between Requirements Engineering and System Design phases.
- ✅ A good SRS is **Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable** (Mnemonic: C-U-C-R-R-V-M-T).
- ✅ An SRS contains **4 main sections**: Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, Appendices.
- ✅ **Functional Requirements (FR)** describe *what* the system does — use the template *"The system shall…"*.
- ✅ **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** describe *how well* the system performs — Performance, Security, Availability, Usability, Reliability, Maintainability, Portability.
- ✅ For **CLMS**, the key actors are: **Student, Faculty, Librarian, System Admin**.
- ✅ Core entities in CLMS: **BOOK, MEMBER, TRANSACTION, RESERVATION, FINE, CATEGORY, USER**.
- ✅ Key business rules: Max **3 books** for students, **5 books** for faculty; fine **₹2/day** (student), **₹5/day** (faculty).
- ✅ Standard tools used in SRS: **Use Case Diagram, DFD (Level 0, 1, 2), ER Diagram, Data Dictionary**.
- ✅ **Always assign unique IDs** (FR-01, NFR-02, UC-01) for traceability.
- ✅ The SRS acts as a **legal contract** and a **baseline for validation, verification, and maintenance**.
- ✅ Common Board pitfall: writing only FR and forgetting NFR — **always write both** for full marks.
- ✅ Use **tables and bullet points** in the exam — KTU examiners reward structured presentation.
- ✅ For UML diagrams, use **Mermaid / draw.io** notation; label **actors and use cases clearly**.
- ✅ Remember the **cardinalities**: Member–Transaction = 1:N, Book–Transaction = 1:N, Transaction–Fine = 1:0..1.
- ✅ KTU's **most-asked question pattern**: *"Prepare SRS for a Library / Hospital / Bank / Inventory System"* — practice with similar real-world systems to score high.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component and Connector
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component and Connector

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Component-and-Connector (C&C) view** is one of the three principal kinds of architectural views in software architecture (the others being *Module* and *Allocation*). It describes a software system **at runtime**, focusing on the active elements that perform computation and the interaction mechanisms that link them together.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Canonical Definition (Clements et al., ATAM-based architecture):**
> A *component-and-connector view* shows the software elements that exist at runtime and the interactions among them. **Components** are the principal units of computation and state. **Connectors** are the principal units of interaction and coordination between components.

The four essential elements of any C&C view are:

1. **Components** – computational or data-store elements (e.g., services, servers, filters, databases).
2. **Connectors** – pathways and rules that govern component interaction (e.g., procedure calls, pipes, message queues).
3. **Ports** – interaction points on components where connectors attach.
4. **Roles** – typed interfaces attached to ports (incoming or outgoing) and connectors, specifying protocol conformance.

A **Configuration** is the specific set of attachments (port–role bindings) that defines one particular snapshot of a running C&C system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **hospital emergency department**:

- **Components** are the doctors, nurses, labs, and pharmacy — each is an active unit doing real work.
- **Connectors** are the *communication channels* between them — the pager system, the intercom, the request slip pipeline, the patient's file folder passed between stations.
- **Ports** are the **slots** where work enters and exits each person: the doctor's inbox tray, the nurse's hand-off desk.
- **Roles** are the *protocols*: "always write the patient ID on top," "always stamp the time," "alerts go via pager, requests via intercom."

The C&C view is essentially the **"live blueprint"** of a running system — analogous to a city's *operational map* showing traffic flow, whereas a Module view is the city's *zoning map* (static subdivision). The C&C view is **dynamic and runtime-oriented**; it asks: *"What is executing, and how are they talking to each other right now?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Component-and-Connector views are a foundational element of *Software Architecture in Practice* (Bass, Clements, Kazman). Questions on C&C elements, styles, and their contrast with Module views appear regularly as 14-mark ESE questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a C&C Element — Structured Logic

Each element in a C&C view is defined by its **type, identity, interface (port), and behavior contract**.

### 2.1.1 Component
- **Identity:** Distinct named unit of computation or data storage.
- **State:** Components have runtime state (mutable/in-memory) and may persist.
- **Ports:** Each component exposes one or more interaction points.
- **Trigger semantics:** A component is active (initiates computation) or reactive (responds to stimuli from connectors).

### 2.1.2 Connector
- **Identity:** First-class entity — *not merely a line*.
- **Protocol:** Governs the valid interaction sequence (e.g., request–response, FIFO ordering).
- **Ports:** Connectors expose **roles** that components bind to.
- **Communication types:** Synchronous, asynchronous, broadcast, multiplexed, transactional.

### 2.1.3 Port
- **Input / Output classification:** Distinguishes producer and consumer sides.
- **Multiplicity:** A port may support 1-to-1, 1-to-N, or N-to-N bindings.

### 2.1.4 Role
- **Typed interface contract:** Specifies the *expected protocol* for binding.
- **Decouples** component identity from connector identity — promotes substitutability.

## 2.2 Catalog of Component Types (KTU High-Yield)

| Component Type | Responsibility | Typical Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Service** | Stateless executor of operations | Web microservice, REST handler |
| **Server** | Passive provider awaiting requests | FTP server, DB server |
| **Client** | Active initiator of requests | Web browser, mobile app front-end |
| **Filter (Processor)** | Transforms a data stream | Compiler phases, Unix `grep` |
| **Repository** | Persisted shared state storage | Database, file vault, cache |
| **Dispatcher / Router** | Routes data to multiple downstream | Load balancer, message broker |
| **Process / Container** | Owner of execution context | JVM process, Docker container |
| **Logger / Monitor** | Passive observer of events | Audit service, telemetry agent |

## 2.3 Catalog of Connector Types (KTU High-Yield)

| Connector Type | Communication Mode | Canonical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Procedure Call** | Synchronous, point-to-point | RPC, RMI |
| **Data Pipe (Stream)** | Unidirectional, byte-flow | Unix shell pipeline |
| **Event Broadcast** | Asynchronous, 1-to-N | DOM events, MQTT broker |
| **Message Queue** | Asynchronous, FIFO, durable | Kafka topic, RabbitMQ |
| **Shared Data Access** | Repository-mediated concurrency | Shared database table |
| **Distributed Object Broker** | Indirect binding via registry | CORBA naming service |
| **Transactional Connector** | ACID-bundled interaction | Two-phase commit middleware |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In modern microservice systems, *Kafka* is a *connector* of type *message queue*, while each microservice is a *service* component. The C&C view models them faithfully — this is why KTU examiners love asking students to identify "what is a component and what is a connector" in service-oriented architectures.

## 2.4 Major C&C Architectural Styles

| Style | Components | Connector | When to Apply |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Client–Server** | Client, Server | Request/Reply (RPC/HTTP) | Centralized data, web apps |
| **Pipe-and-Filter** | Filters (processors) | Pipes (data streams) | Compilers, ETL, signal processing |
| **Publish–Subscribe** | Publishers, Subscribers | Event bus / topic manager | Decoupled notification systems |
| **Shared Repository** | Producers, Consumers | Shared data store | Collaborative editing, build systems |
| **Event-Driven (Implicit Invocation)** | Invokers, Handlers | Event broadcast mechanism | GUI toolkits, reactive systems |
| **Peer-to-Peer** | Equal-role peers | Direct socket / overlay | File sharing, blockchain |
| **Microservices** | Independently deployable services | API gateway / message bus | Cloud-native, large-scale SaaS |

## 2.5 Why C&C Views Matter — Quality Attribute Linkage

The **choice of C&C elements and their binding topology** directly determines the system's runtime quality attributes:

- **Performance** — Synchronous connectors (procedure call) vs. asynchronous (queue): affects latency and throughput.
- **Modifiability** — Loose coupling via *publish-subscribe* allows adding subscribers without changing publishers.
- **Reliability** — *Transactional connectors* and *redundant repositories* bound via replication protocols yield fault tolerance.
- **Security** — *Authentication-aware connectors* (e.g., mTLS) localize trust enforcement at interaction boundaries.
- **Scalability** — *Load-balanced dispatcher* + *stateless services* enable horizontal scaling.

> [!NOTE]
> A C&C view is **not a deployment diagram** (which is an *Allocation* view) and **not a class diagram** (which is a *Module* view). Confusing these three is a frequent KTU 2024 exam pitfall.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Comparison Matrix

Because Component-and-Connector is a *conceptual architectural* topic, the analytical "derivation" here is the **logical procedure for constructing a C&C view** and the **comparative matrices** that distinguish C&C elements from other architectural entities.

## 3.1 Procedural Derivation — Building a C&C View from a Requirements Specification

We treat this as an algorithm-of-thought so each step is explicit.

**Step 1 — Identify runtime actors and data stores.**
Read the requirements; tag each noun-phrase representing an *active entity* as a candidate component. Separate persistent state-holders (repositories, caches) from pure-computation units (services, filters).

**Step 2 — Identify interaction pathways.**
For every data-flow, request, event, or shared-state access between components, mark a candidate connector. Ask: *is this interaction synchronous, asynchronous, broadcast, or shared-state?*

**Step 3 — Assign ports to components.**
Each component must declare a *typed port* for each distinct interaction it participates in. Ports disambiguate *multiple bindings* on the same component (e.g., a server has separate ports for HTTP and management).

**Step 4 — Define roles on connectors.**
Each connector exposes one or more *roles* that components may bind to. E.g., a request-reply connector exposes a *client* role and a *server* role.

**Step 5 — Bind ports to roles (configuration).**
Construct the configuration as a set of (component, port) ↔ (connector, role) tuples. This is the **specific run-time topology**.

**Step 6 — Validate against quality attributes.**
For each chosen connector type, predict the latency, coupling, throughput, and failure-mode profile. Iterate if any attribute violates a non-functional requirement.

**Step 7 — Document style adherence and deviations.**
Identify which C&C style (or hybrid) the view follows. Note intentional deviations and the rationale (e.g., "publish-subscribe used for Order events; client-server used for User CRUD").

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — C&C vs. Module Views

| Dimension | Module View | Component-and-Connector View |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time axis** | Build-time (static) | Run-time (dynamic) |
| **Element kind** | Modules (units of implementation) | Components (units of computation) + Connectors |
| **Relation kind** | "Is-a-part-of", "uses", "depends-on" | "Attached-to", "communicates-via", "triggers" |
| **Quality focus** | Modifiability, reusability, understandability | Performance, availability, security, scalability |
| **Notation** | Class/dependency diagrams | Process/pipe/channel diagrams |
| **Numbering** | Modules are counted statically | Components may multiply dynamically (per request) |
| **Verification** | Compile-time, unit tests | Performance tests, runtime tracing |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying an Element as Component vs. Connector

Use this decision rubric (a high-yield exam heuristic):

| Question to Ask | If YES → | If NO → |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Does it perform computation or hold application state? | **Component** | Continue |
| Does it mediate interaction without owning application logic? | **Connector** | Not a C&C element |
| Does it carry out transformation on data flowing through? | **Component** (Filter) | Continue |
| Does it merely transmit or buffer messages? | **Connector** (Pipe / Queue) | Continue |
| Is it a passive data-store shared by writers/readers? | **Component** (Repository) **or** **Connector** (Shared Data Access) — *context-dependent* | — |

> [!WARNING]
> A *Database* is a **Component** when viewed as a stateful computational engine (query planner, executor) and a **Connector** when viewed as a shared data-access mechanism. KTU exams frequently test this dual nature.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Mapping a Course Registration System

Consider an online course registration portal:

1. **Web Browser (Student)** → *Component (Client)*, port `requestPort`.
2. **Load Balancer** → *Component (Dispatcher)*, ports `in`, `out1`, `out2`.
3. **Registration Service** → *Component (Service)*, ports `apiIn`, `dbOut`.
4. **Notification Service** → *Component (Service)*, port `eventIn`.
5. **Course Database** → *Component (Repository)*, ports `read`, `write`.
6. **HTTP** → *Connector (Procedure Call)*, roles `caller`, `callee`.
7. **Event Bus (Kafka)** → *Connector (Message Queue)*, roles `publisher`, `subscriber`.

Configuration binds: `Web Browser.requestPort → Load Balancer.in` via *HTTP*; `Registration Service.dbOut → Course Database.write` via *Shared Data Access*; `Registration Service.eventOut → Notification Service.eventIn` via *Event Bus*.

This view makes the system's **runtime topology** explicit and exposes quality-attribute bottlenecks (e.g., a single Course Database is a scalability risk).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams render the **Component-and-Connector architectural view** using two canonical patterns. All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and all labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting — adhering to the diagram compilation safeguards.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Client-Server with Repository

```mermaid
graph LR
    clientA[CLIENT BROWSER]
    clientB[CLIENT MOBILE APP]
    apiGateway[API GATEWAY]
    authSvc[AUTHENTICATION SERVICE]
    regSvc[REGISTRATION SERVICE]
    notifySvc[NOTIFICATION SERVICE]
    courseDb[COURSE DATABASE]
    auditLog[AUDIT LOG REPOSITORY]
    httpConn[HTTP CONNECTOR REQUEST REPLY]
    dbConn[SHARED DATA ACCESS CONNECTOR]
    msgConn[KAFKA EVENT BUS CONNECTOR]

    clientA --> httpConn
    clientB --> httpConn
    httpConn --> apiGateway
    apiGateway --> httpConn
    httpConn --> authSvc
    httpConn --> regSvc
    regSvc --> msgConn
    msgConn --> notifySvc
    regSvc --> dbConn
    authSvc --> dbConn
    dbConn --> courseDb
    msgConn --> auditLog
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Rectangles are **components** (computational/data-store units). The diamond/labeled arrows are **connectors** carrying typed communication roles. The bindings express the **configuration** of this C&C view.

## 4.2 Pipe-and-Filter Pipeline (Compiler Analogy)

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcFile[SOURCE FILE COMPONENT]
    lexPipe[LEXICAL ANALYZER FILTER]
    parsePipe[PARSER FILTER]
    semPipe[SEMANTIC ANALYZER FILTER]
    codeGen[CODE GENERATOR FILTER]
    execBin[EXECUTABLE BINARY COMPONENT]
    pipeA[STREAM PIPE CONNECTOR]
    pipeB[STREAM PIPE CONNECTOR]
    pipeC[STREAM PIPE CONNECTOR]
    pipeD[STREAM PIPE CONNECTOR]

    srcFile --> pipeA
    pipeA --> lexPipe
    lexPipe --> pipeB
    pipeB --> parsePipe
    parsePipe --> pipeC
    pipeC --> semPipe
    semPipe --> pipeD
    pipeD --> codeGen
    codeGen --> execBin
```

## 4.3 Publish-Subscribe C&C View

```mermaid
graph LR
    pubA[ORDER SERVICE PUBLISHER]
    pubB[INVENTORY SERVICE PUBLISHER]
    topicMgr[EVENT TOPIC MANAGER COMPONENT]
    subA[EMAIL SERVICE SUBSCRIBER]
    subB[ANALYTICS SERVICE SUBSCRIBER]
    subC[WAREHOUSE SERVICE SUBSCRIBER]
    busA[PUBLISH CONNECTOR ROLE]
    busB[SUBSCRIBE CONNECTOR ROLE]

    pubA --> busA
    pubB --> busA
    busA --> topicMgr
    topicMgr --> busB
    busB --> subA
    busB --> subB
    busB --> subC
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Drawing Convention:** When asked to "draw the C&C view," examiners expect (1) components in rectangles, (2) connectors as labeled arrows or annotated lines, (3) explicit port/role labels at the boundaries. A bare arrow without a connector label earns only partial marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Define the Component-and-Connector (C&C) view of software architecture. List its four principal elements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Component-and-Connector view describes a software system **at runtime**, showing the active units of computation (**components**) and the interaction mechanisms (**connectors**) that bind them. Unlike Module views, which model static decomposition, C&C views model how the system *runs and communicates*.

The four principal elements are:

1. **Component** — Principal unit of computation or data storage (e.g., server, service, repository).
2. **Connector** — Principal unit of interaction between components (e.g., pipe, RPC, event bus).
3. **Port** — Interaction point on a component through which connectors are attached.
4. **Role** — Typed interface contract on a connector that specifies the protocol of binding.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Four elements listed (1.5 marks).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between a Component and a Connector in a C&C view. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Component | Connector |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Role** | Performs computation or holds state | Mediates communication/coordination |
| **Owns** | Application logic and/or state | Protocol of interaction |
| **Examples** | Web server, database, filter | HTTP/RPC, message queue, event bus |

**Example:** In a banking application, the *Account Service* is a **component** (it executes business logic and maintains account state), while the *REST API over HTTP* is a **connector** (it mediates request-reply interaction between the client and the service).

> **Mark distribution:** Two-line distinction (1.5 marks) + Example (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer either Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

---

### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the elements of a Component-and-Connector view in detail with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Component-and-Connector view is composed of four cooperating elements that together describe the *runtime structure* of a system.

**1. Component (2 marks)**

A component is the principal unit of computation and runtime state. It has a distinct identity, exposes well-defined interaction points (ports), and may be *active* (initiating computation on its own) or *reactive* (responding to external triggers). Examples include a *Web Server* component, a *Database Repository* component, and a *Filter* (data transformer) component.

**2. Connector (2 marks)**

A connector is a first-class architectural entity that mediates all interaction between components. It carries a *protocol* specifying valid communication sequences (e.g., request-reply, FIFO message delivery). It is not merely a drawn line — it embodies a real runtime mechanism. Examples: a *Procedure Call* connector (synchronous RPC), a *Message Queue* connector (asynchronous FIFO), an *Event Broadcast* connector (1-to-N asynchronous notification).

**3. Port (1.5 marks)**

A port is the typed interaction point on a component where a connector attaches. Ports have direction (input or output) and multiplicity (single or multi-binding). A *Server* component might expose an `inboundRequest` port and an `auditLog` output port.

**4. Role (1.5 marks)**

A role is the symmetric interface contract on a connector's side. The component's port is bound to the connector's role. Roles decouple component identity from connector implementation — different components may play the same role on the same connector.

**Configuration:** The set of (port, role) bindings constitutes the system's configuration — its specific runtime topology.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Pipe-and-Filter architectural style as a C&C view. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Style definition (2 marks):** The Pipe-and-Filter style arranges components as a chain of *filters* (data transformers) connected by *pipes* (unidirectional data streams). Each filter reads from its input pipe, transforms the data, and writes to its output pipe. There is no shared state — communication is exclusively via pipes.

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes — Pipeline showing Lexical → Parser → Semantic → CodeGen filters connected via stream pipes.)*

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Filters are *composable* and *reusable* (Unix `cat file.txt $\vert$ grep error $\vert$ sort`).
- Concurrency is natural — each filter can run in its own thread.
- Easy to maintain: a faulty filter can be replaced without affecting others.

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
- Overhead of data copying/marshalling between pipes degrades performance.
- Filters cannot share state easily, restricting some algorithms.
- Stateful filters cannot be reordered freely.

---

### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Compare and contrast the Component-and-Connector view with the Module view of software architecture. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Module View | Component-and-Connector View |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time axis** | Build-time (static) | Run-time (dynamic) |
| **Element kind** | Modules, packages, classes | Components, connectors, ports, roles |
| **Relations** | Decomposition, "uses", "depends-on" | Attachment, "communicates-via" |
| **Quality focus** | Modifiability, reusability, buildability | Performance, availability, scalability, security |
| **Notation** | Class/dependency diagrams | Process/pipe/channel diagrams |
| **Audience** | Developers, build engineers | Performance engineers, operators, integrators |
| **Multiplicity** | Each module exists once | Components may multiply at runtime |

**Key insight (2 marks):** Both views describe the *same* system at different points in its lifecycle. A module becomes a component at runtime; the static dependency relationship between two modules may become a *connector-mediated interaction* between their runtime components. They are complementary — a complete architectural description includes both, along with an Allocation view (mapping software to hardware/processes).

**Example:** The `User` *module* (source file containing the User class) corresponds to the `UserService` *component* (runtime microservice instance) — and the static *dependency* on the `Database` module becomes the runtime *TCP connector* between UserService and the DB component.

---

**(b) Describe the Publish-Subscribe architectural style as a C&C view. Illustrate with a diagram and give one real-world use case. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Style definition (2.5 marks):** In the Publish-Subscribe style, components are divided into *publishers* (event producers) and *subscribers* (event consumers). They never communicate directly. Instead, an intermediary component called the **event topic manager** (or message broker) receives published events and dispatches them to all interested subscribers. The connector type is *event broadcast* or *message queue*, with two roles: *publisher* and *subscriber*. Publishers are completely decoupled from subscribers — they need not know who, if anyone, is listening.

**Diagram (2.5 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes — Publisher components attach via the `publish` role of the Event Bus connector; subscribers attach via the `subscribe` role.)*

**Real-world use case (2 marks):** In an *e-commerce platform*, an `OrderService` publishes an `OrderPlaced` event after a successful checkout. The `EmailService` (subscribes to send confirmation), `InventoryService` (subscribes to decrement stock), and `AnalyticsService` (subscribes to update dashboards) all react independently. Adding a new `LoyaltyPointsService` subscriber requires no modification of the OrderService — illustrating high modifiability and scalability, the style's key benefits.

**Trade-off (bonus):** The drawback is *eventual consistency* and *debugging complexity* — asynchronous chains are harder to trace than synchronous calls.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in C&C Questions:**
> 1. **Treating connectors as mere lines** — A connector is a *first-class architectural entity* with its own protocol and identity. Drawing an unlabeled arrow earns zero marks for connector identification.
> 2. **Confusing C&C view with Deployment (Allocation) view** — C&C describes *what* runs and *how* it communicates, not *which physical machine* it runs on. Do not mix in server racks or containers unless explicitly drawing an Allocation view.
> 3. **Forgetting ports and roles** — A component without declared ports is incomplete; a connector without roles cannot be bound. Examiners explicitly allocate 1.5–2 marks for port/role identification.
> 4. **Mixing C&C with Module-view concepts in one diagram** — Showing class inheritance arrows on a C&C diagram is a structural error; it confuses the runtime and build-time views.
> 5. **Generic style descriptions** — Saying *"Publish-subscribe is good for decoupling"* without naming the *connector type* (event broadcast / message queue) loses 1–2 marks. Always name the connector type explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- A **Component-and-Connector (C&C) view** models the **runtime** architecture of a software system, focusing on components and their interaction mechanisms.
- The **four canonical elements** are: **Components** (computation/state units), **Connectors** (interaction mediators), **Ports** (typed component interfaces), **Roles** (typed connector interfaces).
- A **Configuration** is the binding of (port, role) pairs that defines one specific runtime topology.
- **Connectors are first-class entities**, not lines — they carry protocols (synchronous, asynchronous, broadcast, FIFO, transactional).
- Major **C&C styles** to memorize: **Client–Server, Pipe-and-Filter, Publish–Subscribe, Shared Repository, Event-Driven (Implicit Invocation), Peer-to-Peer, Microservices**.
- C&C views address **runtime quality attributes**: performance, availability, scalability, security — whereas Module views address build-time qualities: modifiability, reusability, understandability.
- A *Database* is a **component** (when viewed as a computational engine) **and** a **connector** (when viewed as a shared data-access pathway) — context determines the role.
- Common component types: **service, server, client, filter, repository, dispatcher, process, logger**.
- Common connector types: **procedure call, data pipe, event broadcast, message queue, shared data access, distributed object broker, transactional connector**.
- KTU expects C&C diagrams to label **both** the connector **and** the component ports — unlabeled arrows forfeit marks.
- For 14-mark questions, always pair a **diagram (2–2.5 marks)** with **style advantages/disadvantages (1.5–2 marks)** and a **real-world example (2 marks)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layered

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Layered Architecture** (also known as **N-Tier Architecture** or **Multi-Tier Pattern**) is one of the most widely used architectural patterns in software engineering where the system is decomposed into a hierarchy of discrete layers, each of which performs a specific role and provides a well-defined set of services to the layer immediately above it. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 2 (Software Design), layered architecture falls under **Architectural Design Patterns**, where the structural organization of software components is established to satisfy the non-functional requirements such as **modifiability**, **portability**, and **reusability**.

In the context of *Software Engineering* by Pressman & Maxim (a standard reference framework adopted by KTU), the layered pattern divides the system into layers where:
- Each layer has a **specific responsibility** (Separation of Concerns).
- A layer can only depend on the **layer directly below it** (strict layering) or any lower layer (relaxed layering).
- The **closed layer** principle states that a layer cannot be bypassed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Layered architecture is a **high-yield module-2 topic**. KTU examiners frequently test this with 7-mark and 14-mark questions. Mastering the layer hierarchy, closed/open layer distinction, and the 3-tier vs 4-tier distinction is essential.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-story building** in a large office complex:
- **Ground Floor (Database Layer):** The foundation holds the raw storage (rooms with files).
- **First Floor (Persistence / Data Access Layer):** The librarian's desk. It knows *where* files are stored and brings them up when requested.
- **Second Floor (Business Logic / Application Layer):** The manager's office. It applies rules, processes the files, and makes decisions.
- **Third Floor (Presentation Layer):** The receptionist's desk. It interacts with visitors, takes their requests, and shows them results.

**Key intuition:** A visitor (the user) talks ONLY to the receptionist. The receptionist passes the message downward through the floors. Each floor talks ONLY to the floor immediately below. This is exactly how a layered software system works.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** A typical enterprise application uses **3 to 5 layers**. Most commonly referenced in exams:
> - **3-Tier:** Presentation → Business → Data
> - **4-Tier:** Presentation → Application/Business → Persistence → Database

## 1.3 Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Layer** | A logical grouping of related components (classes/packages) with a cohesive responsibility. |
| **Tier** | A *physical* deployment of a layer on a separate machine/server. (Often confused with layer.) |
| **Closed Layer** | A layer that cannot be bypassed; all requests must pass through it. |
| **Open Layer** | A layer that can be bypassed to allow direct access to lower layers (relaxed layering). |
| **Connector** | The protocol/interaction mechanism between layers (typically a procedure call). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of the Layered Pattern

The layered architecture organizes the system into a **vertical stack** of cohesive, loosely-coupled layers. Below is the standard KTU-recommended hierarchy:

1. **Presentation Layer (Top Layer / UI Layer)**
   - Handles all user-interface logic.
   - Renders information to the user and gathers user inputs.
   - Examples: HTML pages, React components, Android XML layouts.

2. **Application Layer / Business Logic Layer (Middle Layer)**
   - Implements the **domain rules** and workflows of the application.
   - Processes data between Presentation and Persistence.
   - Examples: Order validation logic, Salary calculation rules.

3. **Persistence Layer / Data Access Layer (DAL)**
   - Provides an abstraction over the database.
   - Translates object requests into SQL queries.
   - Examples: Hibernate ORM, JDBC repositories.

4. **Database Layer (Bottom Layer)**
   - The actual data storage mechanism.
   - Examples: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Oracle.

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why layers?** To enforce the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**. Each layer does ONE thing well.
- **How requests flow:** Requests move *downward* through the layers (top → bottom). Responses move *upward* (bottom → top).
- **How changes are isolated:** A change in the UI technology (e.g., React → Angular) only affects the Presentation Layer. The Business Layer remains untouched. This is called **independence** or **decoupling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed vs Open Layer Rule (Examiner's Favorite):**
> - *Closed Layer (Strict Layering):* All requests must traverse every layer in between. **Increases portability and reusability**, but adds latency.
> - *Open Layer (Relaxed Layering):* A layer is marked "open" so it can be skipped. **Improves performance**, but tightly couples the bypassing layer to the lower one.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Property Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Software Engineering is a **qualitative design discipline**, so traditional math formulas are replaced by **architectural properties**. The following table serves as the "Cheat Sheet" for KTU exams.

| Property / Metric | Definition | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** $C$ | Degree of interdependence between modules. | **Decreases** with proper layering (layer $i$ only knows about layer $i-1$). |
| **Cohesion** $\text{Coh}$ | Functional relatedness within a module. | **Increases** because each layer has one responsibility. |
| **Reusability** $R$ | Ability to use a layer across applications. | **High** — e.g., Business Layer reusable across Web + Mobile front-ends. |
| **Portability** $P$ | Effort to migrate layer to new environment. | **High** for layer 4 (Database) if standard interfaces used. |
| **Layer Count** $n$ | Number of layers in the architecture. | Typical KTU answer: $n \in \{3, 4\}$. |
| **Closed Layer Function** | Whether layer $i$ can bypass layer $i-1$. | Closed $\Rightarrow$ layer $i-1$ is **mandatory**. |

### Architectural Constraint Equation (Modifiability):

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\text{Impact}_i}$$

Where $\text{Impact}_i$ represents the change impact on layer $i$. **Goal:** Each change should be localized to a **single layer**, giving $\text{Impact}_i = 0$ for all other layers $\Rightarrow$ high **Modifiability** $M$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems (e.g., SBI NetBanking):** The 4-tier layered approach allows them to swap the Database (Oracle → PostgreSQL) without changing business rules.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon):** The Presentation Layer (Web/Mobile) calls a stable Business Layer API. The Presentation can evolve rapidly while Business rules remain stable.
- **Microservices Evolution:** Modern microservice architecture is a *distributed evolution* of the layered pattern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Determining the "Optimal Number of Layers"

In a KTU exam, you might be asked: *"Given a software system with certain functional requirements, determine the appropriate number of layers."* The analytical derivation is as follows.

### Step 1: Identify the Distinct Concerns

Let the set of concerns in the system be:
$$C = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, \ldots, c_k\}$$

Each concern represents a *category of responsibility* (e.g., "data storage", "user input", "validation").

### Step 2: Cluster Concerns Using the SRP Heuristic

Group concerns into layers $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ such that:
$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} L_i = C \quad \text{and} \quad L_i \cap L_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j$$

This means every concern must belong to exactly one layer (no overlap, no omission).

### Step 3: Define the Dependency Direction

For strict layering, define a strict partial order $\prec$ such that:
$$L_i \prec L_{i+1} \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$$

Meaning layer $L_i$ depends only on $L_{i+1}$. Formally:
$$\text{Depends}(L_i) \subseteq L_{i+1} \quad \text{(strict layering)}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Closed Layer Constraint

A layer $L_i$ is **closed** if:
$$\forall \text{ request } r \text{ entering } L_i : r \text{ must pass through } L_{i+1}$$

This guarantees that changes in $L_{i+1}$ are reflected in $L_i$ without $L_i$ "reaching around" to lower layers.

### Step 5: Quantify the Trade-off (Closed vs Open)

Let $T_{\text{closed}}$ be the average traversal time and $T_{\text{open}}$ the bypass time:
$$T_{\text{closed}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \quad \text{(sum of all layer processing times)}$$

$$T_{\text{open}} = \sum_{i \in \text{OpenPath}} t_i \quad \text{(excludes bypassed layers)}$$

For a **closed 4-tier architecture** with processing times $t_1 = 2\text{ms}$, $t_2 = 5\text{ms}$, $t_3 = 3\text{ms}$, $t_4 = 4\text{ms}$:
$$T_{\text{closed}} = 2 + 5 + 3 + 4 = 14 \text{ ms}$$

If Layer 2 (Business) is opened and bypassed:
$$T_{\text{open}} = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 \text{ ms}$$

**Insight:** Open layering reduces latency by **35.7%** but breaks the encapsulation promise. This is the core trade-off KTU examiners test.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Architecture Aspect | 2-Tier | 3-Tier | 4-Tier (Strict) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Layers ($n$)** | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| **Separation of Concerns** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Performance** | High (no hops) | Medium | Lower (more hops) |
| **Maintenance Effort** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Typical Use Case** | Legacy desktop DB apps | Web apps (LAMP) | Enterprise systems (Java EE, .NET) |

## 3.3 Layer Communication Pattern (Detailed)

The **request-response cycle** in a layered architecture follows this trace:

1. User $\rightarrow$ Presentation Layer: User clicks "Submit Order".
2. Presentation $\rightarrow$ Business Layer: Calls `OrderService.placeOrder()`.
3. Business Layer $\rightarrow$ Persistence Layer: Calls `OrderRepository.save(order)`.
4. Persistence Layer $\rightarrow$ Database: Executes `INSERT INTO orders...`.
5. Database $\rightarrow$ Persistence: Returns the saved record.
6. Persistence $\rightarrow$ Business: Returns `Order` object.
7. Business $\rightarrow$ Presentation: Returns confirmation.
8. Presentation $\rightarrow$ User: Displays "Order Placed Successfully".

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how **the Presentation layer never speaks directly to the Database**. This is the essence of closed layered architecture and a frequent KTU valuation point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    User1[End User] --> PresA[Presentation Layer - UI]
    PresA --> BizA[Business Logic Layer - Application Rules]
    BizA --> PersA[Persistence Layer - Data Access]
    PersA --> DBA[Database Layer - Storage Engine]
    
    DBA -- Data Response --> PersA
    PersA -- Domain Object --> BizA
    BizA -- DTO or Result --> PresA
    PresA -- Rendered View --> User1
    
    style PresA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style BizA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style PersA fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style DBA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.2 Open vs Closed Layer Diagram (KTU High-Yield Visual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph StrictClosed[Strict Closed Layering - All Layers Mandatory]
        P1[Presentation] --> B1[Business Layer]
        B1 --> D1[Persistence]
        D1 --> DB1[Database]
    end
    
    subgraph RelaxedOpen[Relaxed Open Layering - Bypass Allowed]
        P2[Presentation] --> B2[Business Layer]
        P2 -. Bypass .-> D2[Persistence]
        D2 --> DB2[Database]
    end
    
    style StrictClosed fill:#e3f2fd
    style RelaxedOpen fill:#fff3e0
```

## 4.3 Request Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant P as Presentation Layer
    participant B as Business Layer
    participant D as Persistence Layer
    participant DB as Database
    
    U->>P: Submit Form Data
    P->>B: processRequest(data)
    B->>B: Validate Business Rules
    B->>D: save(entity)
    D->>DB: SQL INSERT Query
    DB-->>D: Acknowledgement
    D-->>B: Saved Entity
    B-->>P: Success Result
    P-->>U: Confirmation Screen
```

## 4.4 Comparison with Other Architectural Patterns (KTU Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Architectural Patterns] --> B[Layered]
    A --> C[Client-Server]
    A --> D[Pipe-Filter]
    A --> E[Microkernel]
    A --> F[Microservices]
    
    B --> B1[Used in: Enterprise Web Apps]
    C --> C1[Used in: Distributed Systems]
    D --> D1[Used in: Data Pipelines]
    E --> E1[Used in: Operating Systems]
    F --> F1[Used in: Scalable Cloud Apps]
    
    style B fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define the layered architectural pattern. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
The layered architectural pattern is a software design pattern in which the system is organized as a hierarchy of layers, where each layer provides services to the layer immediately above it and consumes services from the layer immediately below it.

**Advantages:**
1. **Modifiability:** Changes are localized to one layer.
2. **Reusability:** A layer (e.g., Business Logic) can be reused across multiple presentation layers (Web, Mobile, Desktop).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between a closed layer and an open layer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Closed Layer | Open Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Traversal** | Mandatory — all requests pass through it | Optional — can be bypassed |
| **Coupling** | Lower (enforces encapsulation) | Higher (creates direct dependency) |
| **Performance** | Slower due to extra hop | Faster as layer is skipped |
| **Use Case** | Default in strict layered designs | Used for performance-critical paths |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model] (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the layered architectural pattern with a neat block diagram. Describe the responsibilities of each layer in a typical 4-tier system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The layered architectural pattern decomposes a software system into a hierarchy of cohesive layers, each performing a distinct role and communicating only with adjacent layers.

**Block Diagram:**
```
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│   PRESENTATION LAYER (UI)    │ ← User Interaction
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER        │ ← Domain Rules
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  PERSISTENCE / DATA ACCESS   │ ← DB Abstraction
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  DATABASE LAYER              │ ← Raw Storage
└──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Responsibilities:**

1. **Presentation Layer:** Displays data, accepts user inputs, formats output. *Example:* JSP pages, React components.
2. **Business Logic Layer:** Implements domain rules, validates workflows, orchestrates operations. *Example:* Order processing, discount calculation.
3. **Persistence Layer:** Abstracts data access. Translates objects into DB operations. *Example:* Hibernate repositories.
4. **Database Layer:** Stores and retrieves the actual data. *Example:* MySQL, Oracle.

---

**(b)** Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the layered architecture. Under what circumstances would you prefer an "open" layer over a "closed" layer? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**
- **Separation of concerns** — each layer has one job.
- **Reusability** — business layer reusable across platforms.
- **Testability** — each layer can be unit-tested in isolation.
- **Maintainability** — bug fixes localized to one layer.
- **Portability** — lower layers (DB) can be migrated easily.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Performance overhead** — multiple hops add latency.
- **Unnecessary layering** — simple applications become over-engineered.
- **Cascading changes** — sometimes a change must ripple through layers.
- **Difficulty with cross-cutting concerns** — logging/security must traverse all layers.

**When to use Open Layer:**
Prefer an **open layer** when:
1. **Performance is critical** and bypassing the layer avoids redundant processing.
2. The bypassed layer adds **no semantic value** for that specific request path.
3. The system is **mature** and the team understands the trade-off.
*Example:* In a high-frequency trading system, bypassing the business validation layer for read-only cache lookups may be acceptable.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast the **3-tier** and **4-tier** layered architectures with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | 3-Tier | 4-Tier |
|---|---|---|
| **Layers** | Presentation, Business, DB combined | Presentation, Business, Persistence, DB separated |
| **DB Abstraction** | Business talks directly to DB | Dedicated Persistence layer in between |
| **Flexibility** | Moderate | High — DB swap is seamless |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |

**3-Tier Diagram:**
```
[Presentation] → [Business + Persistence] → [Database]
```

**4-Tier Diagram:**
```
[Presentation] → [Business] → [Persistence] → [Database]
```

The 4-tier variant is the **standard for enterprise systems** (Java EE, Spring Boot) because separating Persistence from Database enables ORM frameworks and easier mocking in unit tests.

---

**(b)** Explain how the layered pattern supports **modifiability** and **portability** as quality attributes. Provide an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Modifiability Support:**
Because each layer has a well-defined interface, a change in the *internals* of one layer does not affect the other layers, as long as the interface contract is preserved. This is called **information hiding** (Parnas, 1972).

*Example:* Switching from MySQL to PostgreSQL requires changes ONLY in the Persistence and Database layers. The Business and Presentation layers remain untouched.

**Portability Support:**
The decoupling of layers allows the application to be migrated to new hardware/OS platforms. By making the Database layer conform to a standard interface (e.g., JDBC), the system can run on any platform with a JDBC driver.

*Example:* A Java Swing desktop application's Presentation layer can be replaced with a Spring Boot REST API without touching the Business Logic.

**Mathematical Insight:** The modifiability impact $I$ on layer $i$ is given by:
$$I_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if change affects layer } i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For a well-layered system, a single requirement change should give $I_i = 1$ for exactly one $i$, and $I_j = 0$ for all $j \neq i$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing Layer vs Tier:** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if students mix up *logical layer* with *physical tier*. Remember: **Layer = logical**, **Tier = physical**.
> 2. **Forgetting to label layer interactions:** Always show arrows between layers AND label them (e.g., "calls", "inherits", "queries").
> 3. **Missing the "Closed vs Open" discussion:** Many students describe layers but forget to mention the strict layering rule. Examiners expect this — **lose 2 marks** if omitted.
> 4. **No mention of "why" this architecture:** A pure description without justifying it with **modifiability/portability/reusability** gets only 60% credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Layered Architecture =** vertical stack of layers, each with one responsibility.
> - **Standard 4-Tier:** Presentation → Business → Persistence → Database.
> - **Layer ≠ Tier:** Layer is logical; Tier is physical.
> - **Strict Layering:** Requests traverse ALL layers. Improves **portability** but hurts **performance**.
> - **Relaxed (Open) Layering:** Layers can be bypassed. Improves **performance** but creates **tight coupling**.
> - **Key Benefit:** **Modifiability** — changes localized to one layer.
> - **Key Drawback:** Performance overhead from multiple hops.
> - **Real-World Use:** Banking, e-commerce, ERP systems (SAP), Java EE apps.
> - **Common Mistake:** Saying "Presentation talks to Database directly" — this is **wrong** in strict layered design.
> - **Comparison Hook:** In exams, contrast Layered with **Client-Server** and **MVC** to fetch higher-order marks.
> - **Examiner's Buzzwords:** Use "Separation of Concerns", "Information Hiding", "Single Responsibility Principle" in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repository
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repository

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Software Engineering** and **Software Configuration Management (SCM)**, a **Repository** (also known as a **Software Repository**, **Configuration Database**, or **Version Control Repository**) is a **centralized, persistent, and structured storage system** that maintains all software configuration items (SCI) produced during the software development lifecycle. 

According to the **IEEE Standard 828-2012 (Software Configuration Management Plans)** and **ISO/IEC 12207**, a repository is defined as:

> A managed collection of software-related artifacts, including source code, executables, documentation, test data, design models, and associated metadata, organized to support controlled access, version tracking, integrity, and traceability throughout the software lifecycle.

In the broader **Software Engineering** discipline (as referenced by **Sommerville**, **Pressman**, and **Kruchten**), a repository serves as the **single source of truth** for all software artifacts, enabling:

- **Version Control** (tracking changes over time)
- **Configuration Identification** (unique identification of items)
- **Change Management** (controlled modifications)
- **Audit and Traceability** (historical reconstruction)
- **Concurrency Management** (handling simultaneous developer access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** A *Software Repository* is the centralized data store where all software configuration items (source files, documents, models, libraries, tools) are archived, versioned, indexed, and made available to authorized stakeholders for retrieval, modification, and configuration audits.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Repository as a "Bank Vault with a Librarian":**
> 
> Imagine a massive, ultra-secure bank vault in a library. Every time an author writes a new chapter of a book, the librarian:
> 
> 1. **Stores** the chapter in the vault (persistence)
> 2. **Records** who wrote it, when, and what changed (versioning/metadata)
> 3. **Indexes** the chapter so anyone can find it quickly (cataloging)
> 4. **Controls** who can read/write/lock the chapter (access control)
> 5. **Keeps a history** of every previous draft forever (audit trail)
> 6. **Prevents two authors** from overwriting each other's work (concurrency control)
> 
> The **Repository** is exactly this for software: it is a vault + librarian for source code, designs, test cases, and documentation. Without it, teams would lose track of *what* version of the software is currently deployed, *who* changed *what*, and *when* — leading to **configuration chaos**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard engineering metrics** are universally applied in repository management and are **KTU high-yield**:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Description |
|--------|--------|------------------------------|
| Repository Capacity | $C_{repo}$ | Measured in **GB** or **TB**; typically **5–500 GB** for enterprise SCM |
| Mean Time to Recovery | $MTTR$ | Target: **< 1 hour** for critical systems |
| Commit Frequency | $f_{commit}$ | Typical: **5–20 commits/day/developer** |
| Mean Time Between Failures (Repository) | $MTBF_{repo}$ | Target: **> 720 hours (30 days)** |
| Redundancy Factor (3-2-1 Rule) | $R_{f}$ | **3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite** |
| RPO (Recovery Point Objective) | $RPO$ | Acceptable data loss: **0–15 minutes** |
| RTO (Recovery Time Objective) | $RTO$ | Acceptable downtime: **< 4 hours** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The **3-2-1 Backup Rule** is a frequently asked KTU question. Memorize: **3** copies of data, on **2** different storage media, with **1** copy stored **offsite**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Repositories

Repositories can be classified along multiple dimensions relevant to KTU:

### A. By Architecture / Topology
1. **Centralized Repository (Client-Server)**
   - Examples: **Subversion (SVN)**, **CVS**, **Perforce**
   - Single central server; all clients read/write to it
   - Pros: Simple administration, clear audit trail
   - Cons: Single point of failure

2. **Distributed Repository**
   - Examples: **Git**, **Mercurial**, **Darcs**
   - Every developer has a full local copy of history
   - Pros: High availability, offline work, redundancy
   - Cons: Complex conflict resolution

3. **Hybrid Repository**
   - Combines centralized authority with distributed capabilities
   - Example: **GitHub**, **GitLab**, **Bitbucket**

### B. By Content / Purpose
1. **Source Code Repository** — stores code, branches, tags
2. **Artifact Repository** — stores binaries (e.g., **Nexus**, **Artifactory**, **Maven Repository**)
3. **Document Repository** — stores specifications, requirements, design docs
4. **Model Repository** — stores UML diagrams, design models (used in CASE tools)
5. **Knowledge Repository** — stores lessons learned, best practices, project history
6. **Test Repository** — stores test cases, test data, results

### C. By Storage Model
1. **File-based** (Flat files, e.g., early RCS)
2. **Database-backed** (RDBMS, e.g., ClearCase)
3. **Cloud-based** (SaaS, e.g., GitHub Cloud, AWS CodeCommit)

---

## 1.5 Core Components of a Repository

Every KTU-grade repository must contain the following functional components:

1. **Storage Layer** — physical/logical storage (SAN, NAS, cloud blob)
2. **Version Control Engine** — tracks revisions, branches, merges
3. **Metadata Database** — stores attributes (author, timestamp, checksum)
4. **Access Control Module (ACM)** — authentication (who you are) + authorization (what you can do)
5. **Locking/Concurrency Mechanism** — pessimistic or optimistic locking
6. **Audit and Logging Facility** — records every read/write/access event
7. **Backup and Recovery Subsystem** — periodic snapshots, incremental backups
8. **Indexing and Search Engine** — fast retrieval by name, content, metadata
9. **Notification/Trigger System** — webhooks, hooks for CI/CD pipelines
10. **API/Interface Layer** — REST, CLI, GUI for tool integration

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: How a Repository Functions

A repository is not merely a folder of files. It is a **transactional, versioned, and auditable** system that performs the following operations:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Check-in (Commit / Check-in)**
   - Developer requests a **write lock** or uses **optimistic concurrency**
   - The system creates a new **version identifier** (e.g., `v1.2.3`)
   - **Differential storage** (delta storage) is used: only the change (delta) is stored, not the entire file
   - Metadata is recorded: *author, timestamp, commit message, parent version, checksum (SHA-256)*
   - The transaction is **atomic** — either fully recorded or fully rejected

2. **Check-out (Update / Pull)**
   - Developer retrieves the latest version (or a specific tagged version) to a working copy
   - The system checks **integrity** using checksums
   - A **lock** may be acquired (in lock-modify-unlock model)

3. **Branching**
   - A **branch** is a parallel line of development
   - The repository creates a **pointer** to the current commit
   - Allows parallel feature development without disturbing mainline

4. **Merging**
   - Two branches are combined
   - System performs **3-way merge**: common ancestor + branch A + branch B
   - **Conflicts** are flagged for manual resolution

5. **Versioning Schemes**
   - **Major.Minor.Patch** (Semantic Versioning, e.g., `2.4.1`)
   - **Linear versioning** (e.g., `1, 2, 3, ...`)
   - **Named versions / Tags** (e.g., `Release-2024-Q1`)

6. **Configuration Identification**
   - Each Configuration Item (CI) is assigned a unique identifier
   - **Baseline**: a frozen set of CIs at a milestone (e.g., "Release 1.0 baseline")
   - Stored as a **Configuration Set** in the repository

7. **Change Control**
   - All modifications go through a **Change Control Board (CCB)**
   - **Change Request (CR)** is logged in repository metadata
   - Approved changes are committed with reference to CR ID

8. **Status Accounting**
   - Repository generates reports: *who changed what, when, why, and what is the current status*
   - Supports **audits** (Functional Configuration Audit - FCA, Physical Configuration Audit - PCA)

9. **Backup and Archival**
   - **Full backup** (weekly) + **Incremental backup** (daily) + **Transaction log shipping** (real-time)
   - **Offsite replication** for disaster recovery

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **exam-ready formula and concept cheat sheet**. Memorize every row.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Storage Savings (Delta) | $S_{saved} = S_{total} - S_{delta}$ | Only deltas stored, not full files | Bytes |
| 2 | Compression Ratio | $R_{c} = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Typical: $1.5 \le R_{c} \le 3.0$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Repository Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (1/MTBF) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Hours | Hours |
| 5 | MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair,i}}{N_{failures}}$ | Hours | Hours |
| 6 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Target: $\ge 0.999$ ("three nines") | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Throughput (Commits/sec) | $\theta = \dfrac{N_{commits}}{T_{window}}$ | Operations/second | Ops/s |
| 8 | Concurrency Limit (Lock Model) | $L_{max} = 1$ | Pessimistic lock allows only 1 writer | Users |
| 9 | CAP Theorem Trade-off | Choose 2 of: **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, **P**artition tolerance | Distributed repos sacrifice C | — |
| 10 | Recovery Point Objective | $RPO = T_{last\_backup} - T_{disaster}$ | Max acceptable data loss | Time |
| 11 | 3-2-1 Backup Rule | 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite | Industry standard | — |
| 12 | Branch Overhead | $O_{branch} = \dfrac{N_{branches}}{N_{total\_files}}$ | Repo complexity metric | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Merge Conflict Rate | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{conflicts}}{N_{merges}} \times 100\%$ | Should be $< 10\%$ | Percent |
| 14 | Hash-based Integrity (SHA-256) | $H = \text{SHA-256}(F)$ | $H$ is unique fingerprint of file $F$ | 256-bit |
| 15 | Audit Frequency | $f_{audit} = \dfrac{1}{T_{audit\_cycle}}$ | E.g., once per sprint | 1/Time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Rule Applied:** All absolute values and divisions use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. For example, $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ is used instead of `|MTBF| / (|MTBF| + |MTTR|)`.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Repositories are the **backbone of modern software engineering practice**. Their utilities span:

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Aerospace (ISRO, NASA)** | Mission-critical software uses **distributed redundant repositories** with formal baselines and audits |
| **Banking & FinTech** | **Git-based repositories** (GitHub Enterprise) with strict **branch protection rules** and **code review enforcement** |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR, ISO 26262)** | Repositories enforce **functional safety** via traceability matrices stored as metadata |
| **Healthcare (FDA 21 CFR Part 11)** | Repositories maintain **electronic records with audit trails** for regulatory compliance |
| **DevOps / CI-CD** | Repositories trigger **automated builds, tests, and deployments** via webhooks (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins) |
| **Open Source** | **GitHub/GitLab** host millions of public repositories enabling global collaboration |
| **CASE Tools** | Tools like **Rational Rose, Enterprise Architect** use **model repositories** to store UML diagrams with version history |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Insight:** When **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** software was being developed, every single line of code was tracked in a configuration-managed repository with formal baselines, change control boards, and full audit trails — a direct application of the repository concept at industrial scale.

---

## 2.4 Repository in the Context of CASE Tools

**Computer-Aided Software Engineering (CASE)** tools use a specialized type of repository called a **CASE Repository** or **Project Repository**. It stores:

- **Analysis models** (DFDs, ER diagrams)
- **Design models** (UML class, sequence, state diagrams)
- **Code generators** output
- **Project documentation**
- **Project metrics** (lines of code, defect density, effort)

The CASE repository enforces **consistency checking** — if you change a class name in the design model, the code is automatically regenerated, and all cross-references are updated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A CASE tool repository is **active** (not passive) — it actively maintains **consistency** between phases of development. This distinguishes it from a mere file server.
