| **Microservices Architecture** | Component, Deployment, Sequence | Service contracts and interactions |
| **Healthcare / Medical Software** | State Machine (vital signs), Use-Case (workflows) | Safety-critical state transitions |
| **Gaming** | State Machine, Activity | Complex object lifecycles |

---

## 2.6 Mapping Static ⇄ Dynamic Views (The KTU Favourite Synthesis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **bridge concept** that KTU loves to test in 14-mark questions. The static and dynamic models are *complementary projections* of the same underlying system.

| Static View Entity | Dynamic View Counterpart |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Class | Object lifecycle (State Machine) |
| Attribute | State variable that changes over time |
| Operation (Method) | Message in sequence diagram |
| Association between classes | Message passing between objects in sequence |
| Generalization hierarchy | Polymorphic dispatch at runtime |
| Interface (in class diagram) | Realized method calls in sequence diagram |

> **Example Synthesis:** A static class diagram may show `Account` with a method `withdraw(amount)`. The corresponding dynamic sequence diagram will show *in time order*: `Customer` → `ATM` → `BankServer` → `Account.withdraw()` → `BankServer.response()`.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a Class Diagram for a Library Management System

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most likely 14-mark KTU question** in Module 2. We will derive the static and dynamic views step-by-step.

### Step 1: Identify the Classes (from the problem statement)

A Library Management System has:
- Books
- Members
- Library Staff
- Loans (transactions)
- Catalog

**Extracted Classes:** `Book`, `Member`, `Staff`, `Loan`, `Catalog`, `Library`

### Step 2: Identify Attributes for each class

| Class | Attributes |
|-------|-----------|
| `Book` | `bookID : int`, `title : String`, `author : String`, `ISBN : String`, `isAvailable : boolean` |
| `Member` | `memberID : int`, `name : String`, `address : String`, `phone : String` |
| `Staff` | `staffID : int`, `name : String`, `position : String`, `salary : double` |
| `Loan` | `loanID : int`, `loanDate : Date`, `dueDate : Date`, `returnDate : Date` |
| `Catalog` | `catalogID : int`, `category : String` |
| `Library` | `libraryName : String`, `address : String` |

### Step 3: Identify Operations (Methods)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book :} \quad & + \text{checkOut()} : \text{boolean} \\
                     & + \text{returnBook()} : \text{void} \\
                     & + \text{getDetails()} : \text{String} \\ \\
\text{Member :} \quad & + \text{register()} : \text{void} \\
                      & + \text{borrowBook(b : Book)} : \text{Loan} \\
                      & + \text{viewHistory()} : \text{List<Loan>} \\ \\
\text{Staff :} \quad  & + \text{addBook(b : Book)} : \text{void} \\
                      & + \text{removeBook(b : Book)} : \text{void} \\
                      & + \text{issueLoan(m : Member, b : Book)} : \text{Loan} \\ \\
\text{Loan :} \quad   & + \text{calculateFine()} : \text{double} \\
                      & + \text{extendDueDate(days : int)} : \text{void}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Identify Relationships (Apply the 6-relationship analysis)

**Analytical Matrix for Relationship Identification:**

| Candidate Pair | Semantic Test | Relationship | Multiplicity | Justification |
|----------------|---------------|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| Library — Book | "Library HAS Books" but books have ISBN and may transfer | **Aggregation** | `1` — `0..*` | Books exist independent of any one library branch |
| Library — Member | "Library HAS Members" | **Aggregation** | `1` — `0..*` | Members are listed at one library |
| Member — Loan | "Member HAS-A Loan history" | **Aggregation** | `1` — `0..*` | Past loans persist |
| Loan — Book | "Loan consists-of a Book" | **Composition** | `1` — `1` | If the loan is deleted, the link dissolves; but the book itself survives |
| Staff — Library | "Staff WORKS-AT Library" | **Association** | `*` — `1` | Staff employed by exactly one library |
| Book — Catalog | "Book BELONGS-TO Catalog section" | **Association** | `*` — `1` | Each book in one section |
| Staff ▷ Member | Staff IS-NOT-A Member (generalization) | **No relationship** | N/A | Wrong semantics |
| Loan — depends-on Date | Loan uses Date objects | **Dependency** | N/A | Parameter only |

### Step 5: Final Class Diagram (Rendered as a logical table since markdown can't draw UML rectangles)

```
                    ┌────────────────────────────┐
                    │          Library           │
                    ├────────────────────────────┤
                    │ - libraryName : String     │
                    │ - address : String         │
                    ├────────────────────────────┤
                    │ + registerMember()         │
                    │ + getCatalog()             │
                    └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                                  ◇ (aggregation)
                                  │
        ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
        │                                                   │
        ▼                                                   ▼
┌────────────────┐                              ┌────────────────────┐
│     Book       │                              │      Member        │
├────────────────┤                              ├────────────────────┤
│ - bookID : int │                              │ - memberID : int   │
│ - title : str  │                              │ - name : String    │
│ - isAvailable  │                              │ - phone : String   │
├────────────────┤                              ├────────────────────┤
│ + checkOut()   │                              │ + borrowBook()     │
│ + returnBook() │                              │ + viewHistory()    │
└───────┬────────┘                              └──────┬─────────────┘
        │ ◇ (catalog contains books)                  │ ◇ (members own loans)
        │                                             │
        ▼                                             ▼
┌────────────────┐    ◆ (composition: each   ┌────────────────────┐
│    Catalog     │       loan owns a book)    │       Loan         │
├────────────────┤ ◀─────────────────────────▶├────────────────────┤
│ - catalogID    │                            │ - loanID : int     │
│ - category     │                            │ - loanDate : Date  │
├────────────────┤                            │ - dueDate : Date   │
│ + addBook()    │                            ├────────────────────┤
│ + search()     │                            │ + calculateFine()  │
└────────────────┘                            │ + extendDueDate()  │
                                              └────────────────────┘
                                                      ⇢ (dependency on Date)
```

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving a Sequence Diagram from a Use-Case

**Use-Case:** *"Member Borrows a Book"*

### Step-by-step Derivation of the Sequence

**Step 1:** Identify objects involved → `member1 : Member`, `librarySys : LibrarySystem`, `loan1 : Loan`, `book1 : Book`

**Step 2:** Lay them out horizontally (left to right) on the lifeline axis.

**Step 3:** Determine the chronological message order.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } t_0 &: \text{member1 created} \\
\text{Time } t_1 &: \text{member1.borrowBook(book1)} \rightarrow \text{librarySys} \\
\text{Time } t_2 &: \text{librarySys.checkAvailability(book1)} \rightarrow \text{book1} \\
\text{Time } t_3 &: \text{book1.isAvailable} \rightarrow \text{librarySys} \quad (\text{return value: true}) \\
\text{Time } t_4 &: \text{librarySys.createLoan(member1, book1)} \rightarrow \text{loan1} \\
\text{Time } t_5 &: \text{loan1.calculateDueDate()} \rightarrow \text{loan1} \\
\text{Time } t_6 &: \text{book1.markAsBorrowed()} \rightarrow \text{book1} \\
\text{Time } t_7 &: \text{librarySys.confirmLoan(loan1)} \rightarrow \text{member1} \quad (\text{return})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Sequence Diagram (Logical Description)

```
member1         librarySys         loan1           book1
   │                │                │                │
   │  1: borrowBook(book1)           │                │
   │ ───────────────▶                │                │
   │                │ 2: checkAvailability(book1)      │
   │                │ ──────────────────────────────▶  │
   │                │ ◀────────────── 3: isAvailable = true
   │                │  4: createLoan()  │                │
   │                │ ───────────────▶ │                │
   │                │  5: setDueDate()  │                │
   │                │ ───────────────▶ │                │
   │                │  6: markBorrowed()│                │
   │                │ ──────────────────────────────▶  │
   │ ◀─────────── 7: confirmLoan(loan1)                 │
   │                │                │                │
```

> [!TIP]
> **Numbering Convention:** The first message is `1`, and the return is the *same number with a return arrow*. If `2` spawns sub-messages, they become `2.1`, `2.2`, etc. KTU expects this dot-numbered hierarchy.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — State Machine Diagram for an "ATM Transaction" Object

### Step 1: Identify States

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &: \text{Idle (waiting for card)} \\
S_1 &: \text{Card Inserted} \\
S_2 &: \text{PIN Entered (validating)} \\
S_3 &: \text{Authenticated} \\
S_4 &: \text{Selecting Transaction} \\
S_5 &: \text{Processing Withdrawal} \\
S_6 &: \text{Processing Balance Inquiry} \\
S_7 &: \text{Dispensing Cash} \\
S_8 &: \text{Printing Receipt} \\
S_9 &: \text{Transaction Complete} \\
S_{10} &: \text{Card Ejected} \\
S_{11} &: \text{Error: Invalid PIN} \\
S_{12} &: \text{Locked (after 3 failed attempts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Events & Transitions

| Source State | Event | [Guard] | / Action | Target State |
|--------------|-------|---------|----------|--------------|
| Idle | insertCard() | — | readCard() | Card Inserted |
| Card Inserted | enterPIN(p) | — | validate(p) | PIN Entered |
| PIN Entered | valid | [pin == stored] | — | Authenticated |
| PIN Entered | invalid | [pin ≠ stored] | incrementFailCount() | Error: Invalid PIN |
| Error: Invalid PIN | retry | [failCount < 3] | — | Card Inserted |
| Error: Invalid PIN | lockout | [failCount == 3] | — | Locked |
| Authenticated | selectTransaction(t) | — | — | Selecting Transaction |
| Selecting Transaction | chooseWithdraw | — | checkBalance() | Processing Withdrawal |
| Processing Withdrawal | insufficientFunds | [balance < amount] | — | Transaction Complete |
| Processing Withdrawal | sufficient | [balance ≥ amount] | dispense() | Dispensing Cash |
| Dispensing Cash | cashTaken | — | printReceipt() | Printing Receipt |
| Printing Receipt | ejectCard | — | — | Card Ejected |
| Card Ejected | cardRemoved | — | reset() | Idle |
| Locked | operatorReset | — | — | Idle |

### Step 3: Transition Format (apply the rule: `sourceState — event[guard]/action → targetState`)

$$
\text{Idle} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{insertCard()}\;}\; \text{Card Inserted} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{enterPIN(p)}\;}\; \text{PIN Entered}
$$

$$
\text{PIN Entered} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{valid} \;[\,pin == storedPin\,]\;}\; \text{Authenticated}
$$

---

## 3.4 Multiplicity Derivation (A Common 3-Mark Question)

> **Problem:** *"In a school system, derive the multiplicities of the relationship between Teacher and Student, given:*
> - *Each teacher teaches between 0 and 30 students.*
> - *Each student is taught by exactly one class teacher and 0 or more subject teachers."*

### Derivation

Let $T$ = set of teachers, $S$ = set of students.

The relationship is "teaches", but we have **two semantic sub-relationships**:
1. **Class Teacher ↔ Student** — each student has exactly 1 class teacher.
2. **Subject Teacher ↔ Student** — each student may be taught by 0 or more subject teachers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ClassTeacher} &\;\; \overset{1}{\underset{0..*}{\rightleftarrows}} \;\; \text{Student} \\
\text{SubjectTeacher} &\;\; \overset{0..30}{\underset{0..*}{\rightleftarrows}} \;\; \text{Student}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cardinality table:**

| End | Multiplicity | Reasoning |
|-----|--------------|-----------|
| Student (per class teacher) | `0..*` | A class teacher can have an empty class or many students |
| Class Teacher (per student) | `1` | Each student must have exactly one class teacher |
| Student (per subject teacher) | `0..*` | A subject teacher may have no takers or many |
| Subject Teacher (per student) | `0..*` | A student may learn 0 or more subjects |

> [!TIP]
> **Read the multiplicity on the END OPPOSITE to the class.** The number near `Teacher` is the count of teachers per student, and vice versa. This is a classic KTU trap.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Static vs Dynamic Diagram Mapping

| System Aspect | Static Diagram (Class) | Dynamic Diagram (Sequence) | Dynamic Diagram (State) |
|---------------|------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| `Account` | `balance : double`, `withdraw()` | `Customer → ATM → BankServer → Account.withdraw()` | `Active → Overdrawn → Suspended` |
| `Order` | `orderID`, `total`, `cancel()` | `Customer → Order → Payment → Inventory` | `New → Paid → Shipped → Delivered` |
| `Sensor` | `reading : float`, `calibrate()` | `Timer → Sensor → Processor → Actuator` | `Idle → Sampling → Processing → Reporting` |
| `Student` | `name`, `GPA`, `register()` | `Student → RegistrationSystem → Database` | `Admitted → Enrolled → Graduated` |

> **KTU Synthesis Rule:** For any class, you can derive:
> 1. A **State Machine** from its lifecycle,
> 2. A **Sequence** from the most important use case involving it,
> 3. **Methods** from the operations that change its state.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
*"Differentiate between the static and dynamic models in UML with two examples each."*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Static Model** captures the *structure* of a system that remains *unchanged over time* — it depicts the system's classes, attributes, operations, and the relationships between them.

**Dynamic Model** captures the *behavior* of a system *over time* — it depicts how objects interact, how states change, and the flow of control.

| Aspect | Static Model | Dynamic Model |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Time dependency | Independent of time | Time-ordered |
| Captures | Structure | Behavior |
| Examples | Class Diagram, Component Diagram, Deployment Diagram | Sequence Diagram, State Machine Diagram, Activity Diagram |

**Two examples of each:**
- **Static:** (i) Class Diagram (e.g., `Student` and `Course` classes with attributes); (ii) Deployment Diagram (e.g., Web Server node hosting Tomcat).
- **Dynamic:** (i) Sequence Diagram (e.g., login flow between User, UI, Authenticator, Database); (ii) State Machine Diagram (e.g., Order moving through Placed → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Loss of 1 Mark:** Many students confuse "Use-Case Diagram" as a static one. *Use-Case is a DYNAMIC diagram* (it captures user-system interaction goals). Do not misclassify.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]
*"Explain aggregation and composition with a suitable example."*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Both **aggregation** and **composition** are specialized forms of association that represent *whole–part* relationships.

**Aggregation (◇ hollow diamond):**
- A weak whole–part relationship where the **part can exist independently** of the whole.
- Represented by a **hollow diamond** on the whole side.
- **Example:** A `Department` ◇—— `Professor`. If the department is dissolved, the professor still exists and may join another department.

**Composition (◆ filled diamond):**
- A strong whole–part relationship where the **part's lifetime is bound to the whole**.
- Represented by a **filled diamond** on the whole side.
- **Example:** A `House` ◆—— `Room`. If the house is demolished, the rooms cease to exist physically.

```
Aggregation (Hollow ◇):        Composition (Filled ◆):
   Department ◇──── Professor      House ◆──── Room
   (professor survives)            (room dies with house)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Loss of 1 Mark:** Drawing the diamond on the *wrong side*. The diamond (hollow or filled) is always placed on the **WHOLE (container)** class, not the part. If you place it on `Professor` or `Room`, you will lose 1 mark.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified from Past Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain the six types of relationships in UML with their notation and a real-world example for each. *(7 Marks)*

**Q: (b)** Design a **class diagram** for an "Online Course Enrollment System" with at least **five classes**, complete attributes, methods, and proper multiplicities. Briefly justify each relationship. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Six UML Relationships (7 Marks)

The six (technically seven with realization) fundamental relationships in UML are:

**(1) Association** — Solid line `————`
- A general structural link between two classes indicating that objects communicate.
- **Example:** `Teacher` — `Student` (a teacher teaches students). Multiplicity: `1` to `0..*`.

**(2) Directed Association** — Solid line with arrow `——▶`
- A one-way association, indicating one class knows about another.
- **Example:** `Customer` ▶ `Order` (a customer places orders, but the order doesn't need to know the customer in the same way).

**(3) Aggregation** — Hollow diamond `◇————`
- Whole–part relationship where parts can exist independently.
- **Example:** `Library` ◇—— `Book`. Books survive even if the library closes.

**(4) Composition** — Filled diamond `◆————`
- Strong whole–part relationship; part's lifecycle is tied to the whole.
- **Example:** `Folder` ◆—— `File`. Deleting the folder deletes the files.

**(5) Generalization (Inheritance)** — Hollow triangle `▷`
- IS-A relationship; subclass inherits superclass's structure/behavior.
- **Example:** `SavingsAccount` ▷ `BankAccount`.

**(6) Dependency** — Dashed arrow `- - ▷`
- A weak, often temporary, using relationship.
- **Example:** A `Printer` depends on a `Document` passed as a method parameter.

**(7) Realization** — Dashed line with hollow triangle `- - - ▷`
- A class implements an interface.
- **Example:** `ArrayList` realizes the `List` interface.

**Synthesis Table:**

| Relationship | Notation | Strength | Lifetime |
|--------------|----------|----------|----------|
| Association | Solid line | Weak | Object-level |
| Aggregation | Hollow ◇ | Medium | Independent parts |
| Composition | Filled ◆ | Strong | Dependent parts |
| Generalization | Hollow ▷ | Strong (class-level) | N/A |
| Dependency | Dashed arrow | Weakest | Class-level (temporary) |
| Realization | Dashed + ▷ | Strong (class-level) | N/A |

---

#### Part (b) — Class Diagram for Online Course Enrollment System (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Classes:** `Student`, `Course`, `Instructor`, `Enrollment`, `Department`, `Certificate`

**Step 2 — Attributes & Methods:**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| Student | `studentID`, `name`, `email`, `enrolledDate` | `enroll(course)`, `drop(course)`, `viewGrades()` |
| Course | `courseID`, `courseName`, `credits`, `capacity` | `addStudent()`, `removeStudent()`, `getEnrollments()` |
| Instructor | `instructorID`, `name`, `specialization` | `assignCourse()`, `uploadGrades()` |
| Enrollment | `enrollmentID`, `enrollmentDate`, `grade` | `assignGrade()`, `calculateStatus()` |
| Department | `deptID`, `deptName` | `addCourse()`, `getFaculty()` |
| Certificate | `certID`, `issueDate`, `grade` | `generate()` |

**Step 3 — Relationships & Justification:**

| Relationship | Notation | Multiplicity | Justification |
|--------------|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Student ◇—— Enrollment | Aggregation | `1` to `0..*` | A student can have many enrollments; if student is deleted, historical enrollments remain |
| Course ◆—— Enrollment | Composition | `1` to `0..*` | Enrollments belong to a course; deleting a course removes its enrollments |
| Course ◇—— Department | Aggregation | `*` to `1` | A course belongs to one department; department may be reorganized independently |
| Instructor — Course | Association | `1..*` to `0..*` | An instructor may teach multiple courses; a course needs at least one instructor |
| Student ▷ Person | Generalization | N/A | Student IS-A Person (inheritance of common attributes) |
| Enrollment ⇢ Grade | Dependency | N/A | Enrollment class uses Grade temporarily |

**Final Class Diagram (textual representation):**

```
              ┌──────────────┐                  ┌────────────────┐
              │  Department  │                  │    Student     │
              ├──────────────┤                  ├────────────────┤
              │ - deptID     │                  │ - studentID    │
              │ - deptName   │                  │ - name         │
              ├──────────────┤                  │ - email        │
              │ + addCourse()│                  ├────────────────┤
              └──────┬───────┘                  │ + enroll(c)    │
                     │ ◇ (aggregation)          │ + viewGrades() │
                     │                          └────────┬───────┘
                     ▼                                   │ ◇ (aggregation)
              ┌──────────────┐                           │
              │    Course    │                           ▼
              ├──────────────┤                  ┌────────────────┐
              │ - courseID   │                  │  Enrollment    │
              │ - courseName │                  ├────────────────┤
              │ - credits    │                  │ - enrollID     │
              │ - capacity   │                  │ - enrollDate   │
              ├──────────────┤                  │ - grade        │
              │ + addStudent()│                 ├────────────────┤
              │ + getEnrolls()│                 │ + assignGrade()│
              └──────┬───────┘                  └────────┬───────┘
                     │ ◆ (composition)                   │
                     └──────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            │ ⇢ (depends on Certificate generation)
                            ▼
                     ┌────────────────┐
                     │  Certificate   │
                     ├────────────────┤
                     │ - certID       │
                     │ - issueDate    │
                     ├────────────────┤
                     │ + generate()   │
                     └────────────────┘
```

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between **Sequence Diagram** and **Communication Diagram** with diagrams. Explain when each is preferred. *(7 Marks)*

**Q: (b)** Draw a **State Machine Diagram** for an "Online Order" object showing all possible states, events, guards, and actions. Explain the meaning of each transition. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Sequence vs Communication Diagram (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Sequence Diagram | Communication Diagram |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Focus** | Time-order of messages | Object links and message numbering |
| **Axes** | Vertical = time, Horizontal = objects | Free-form spatial layout |
| **Time** | Explicit (top-to-bottom flow) | Implicit (numbers indicate order) |
| **Message numbering** | Sequential (1, 2, 3, ...) | Hierarchical (1, 1.1, 1.2, 2, ...) |
| **Lifelines** | Explicit vertical lines | Optional / implicit |
| **Standardization** | UML 2 treats both as "Interaction Diagrams" with same semantic content | Same semantic content, different notation |
| **Best for** | Real-time and procedural flow | Complex web of object collaborations |

**Example — Login Scenario:**

**Sequence Diagram (time-explicit):**

```
User       LoginUI      AuthService    Database
 │            │              │             │
 │─1: enter()─▶              │             │
 │            │─2: validate()▶             │
 │            │              │─3: query()──▶│
 │            │              │◀─4: result──│
 │            │◀─5: token────│             │
 │◀─6: success│              │             │
```

**Communication Diagram (link-centered):**

```
        [User] ─1: enter()─▶ [LoginUI]
                              │
                              │ 2: validate()
                              ▼
                        [AuthService]
                              │
                              │ 3: query()
                              ▼
                         [Database]
        [User] ◀─6: success── [LoginUI]
```

**When to use which:**
- **Use Sequence Diagram** when the *temporal flow* is critical (real-time systems, protocol interactions, single linear flows).
- **Use Communication Diagram** when *object topology and link reuse* is more important than time (collaborative web apps, distributed systems).

---

#### Part (b) — State Machine Diagram for "Online Order" (7 Marks)

**States Identified:**

| State | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `New` | Order created, not yet paid |
| `PaymentPending` | Awaiting payment gateway response |
| `Paid` | Payment successful |
| `PaymentFailed` | Payment rejected; customer may retry |
| `InventoryReserved` | Stock held for the order |
| `OutOfStock` | Not enough inventory |
| `Shipped` | Order dispatched |
| `Delivered` | Order received by customer |
| `Cancelled` | Order cancelled (refund issued) |
| `Returned` | Customer returned the order |
| `Refunded` | Money returned to customer |

**Transitions with Guards:**

| Source State | Event | [Guard] | / Action | Target State |
|--------------|-------|---------|----------|--------------|
| New | makePayment() | — | callPaymentGateway() | PaymentPending |
| PaymentPending | paymentOK | [response == success] | reserveInventory() | InventoryReserved |
| PaymentPending | paymentFailed | [response == failure] | notifyUser() | PaymentFailed |
| PaymentFailed | retry | [attempts < 3] | callPaymentGateway() | PaymentPending |
| PaymentFailed | abandon | [attempts ≥ 3] | — | Cancelled |
| InventoryReserved | dispatch | — | shipOrder() | Shipped |
| InventoryReserved | outOfStock | [stock == 0] | refund() | OutOfStock |
| Shipped | deliver | — | notifyCustomer() | Delivered |
| Delivered | returnRequest | [within 30 days] | — | Returned |
| Delivered | closeOrder | [after 30 days] | — | [Final State] |
| Returned | inspect | — | — | Refunded |
| OutOfStock | refund | — | — | Refunded |

**Schematic Representation:**

```
                ┌──────────┐
                │ [Start]  │
                └────┬─────┘
                     │ placeOrder()
                     ▼
                ┌──────────┐  paymentFailed [attempts≥3]   ┌─────────────┐
                │   New    │ ──────────────────────────────▶│  Cancelled  │
                └────┬─────┘                                └─────────────┘
                     │ makePayment()                              │
                     ▼                                            │ refund
                ┌──────────────┐  paymentOK                       │
                │PaymentPending│ ──────────▶ InventoryReserved ───┤
                └────┬─────────┘              │                   │
                     │ paymentFailed          │ outOfStock [stock==0]
                     │ [response==failure]    │                   
                     ▼                       ▼                   │
                ┌──────────────┐        ┌──────────┐             │
                │PaymentFailed │        │OutOfStock│             │
                └────┬─────────┘        └────┬─────┘             │
                     │ retry [attempts<3]     │ refund           │
                     │                       │                   │
                     ▼                       ▼                   ▼
                PaymentPending            ┌──────────┐        ┌──────────┐
                                           │ Refunded │ ◀──────┘         │
                                           └──────────┘                  │
                                                                      [Final]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Loss of 2 Marks:** Students often forget **guards** and **actions** in transitions. The KTU board examiner allocates at least **2 marks** for correctly writing transition labels in the form `event [guard] / action`. A bare arrow with no label will lose you those marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- ✅ **OOSD** binds data + behavior into **objects**; the design output is captured in **UML** diagrams.
- ✅ UML has **14 diagrams** split into **6 Static** + **8 Dynamic** (with some overlap with Physical).
- ✅ **Static models** = structure, time-independent. **Dynamic models** = behavior, time-ordered.
- ✅ **The 6 UML relationships** (Master list): Association, Directed Association, Aggregation, Composition, Generalization, Dependency, Realization.

### High-Yield Rules for Exam Day

- ✅ **Diamond (◇ or ◆) is ALWAYS on the WHOLE side** in aggregation/composition.
- ✅ **Hollow = weak (parts survive whole)**; **Filled = strong (parts die with whole)**.
- ✅ **Generalization = IS-A** (Hollow triangle ▷).
- ✅ **Realization = implements interface** (Dashed + Hollow triangle).
- ✅ **Sequence Diagram numbering:** `1, 2, 3` (top-level) and `1.1, 1.2` (nested).
- ✅ **State transition syntax:** `sourceState — event[guard]/action → targetState`.
- ✅ **Multiplicity trick:** Read the number on the **OPPOSITE end** of the class you're counting for.
- ✅ **Class Diagram = three compartments:** Class Name (bold) | Attributes (with visibility) | Operations (with signatures).

### Diagram-Specific Memorization

- ✅ **Class Diagram** ⭐ — Most important static diagram. Use 3-compartment boxes.
- ✅ **Use-Case Diagram** ⭐ — Actors, use cases, system boundary. `<<include>>` = mandatory, `<<extend>>` = optional.
- ✅ **Sequence Diagram** ⭐ — Time flows top to bottom; lifelines are dashed vertical lines.
- ✅ **State Machine Diagram** ⭐ — Start (●), End (⊕), transitions with `event[guard]/action`.

### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid

- ❌ Confusing **Use-Case Diagram** (dynamic) with Class Diagram (static).
- ❌ Placing the aggregation diamond on the **part** instead of the **whole**.
- ❌ Forgetting to include **multiplicity** on both ends of an association.
- ❌ Writing bare transitions in a state machine **without guards and actions**.
- ❌ Mixing up **dependency (dashed arrow)** with **association (solid line)**.
- ❌ Treating **deployment and component** as "dynamic" — they are structural/physical.

### Final Synthesis — The Big Picture

> *"A Class Diagram tells you **WHAT** the system is. A Sequence Diagram tells you **WHEN** things happen. A State Machine Diagram tells you **WHY** a particular object is in its current condition. Together, they constitute the full **UML Object-Oriented Design Model** — the contract between the analyst's mind and the programmer's code."*

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — OBJECT-ORIENTED SOFTWARE DESIGN, UML DIAGRAMS & RELATIONSHIPS**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | B.Tech CSE/IT*
