# 📘 Module 2 — Class Diagram (UML)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (UML 2.5 / 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Class Diagram** is a **static structural UML (Unified Modeling Language) diagram** that visualizes a system's **classes**, their **internal structure** (attributes and operations), and the **static relationships** that exist between them. It is one of the most critical artifacts produced during the **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** phase of the Unified Process.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blueprint" of a Software City

Imagine you are an architect planning a **city**. Before any building is constructed, you need a **master blueprint** that shows:

- What **buildings** (classes) exist? (e.g., House, School, Hospital)
- What **rooms and furniture** are inside each building? (attributes & operations)
- How are these buildings **connected**? (roads, pipelines, ownership)

A **Class Diagram** is exactly this blueprint — but for **software**. It is the most heavily tested UML diagram in the KTU board exam because it captures the **essence of object-oriented thinking** in a single visual.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Static"?**
> Because it depicts the **structure** of the system at a point in time. It does **not** show message flow, sequence of calls, or state changes — those belong to **behavioral diagrams** (Sequence, Activity, State Machine).

### 🎯 Purpose of a Class Diagram (Syllabus Highlights)

| Purpose | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|
| Identify **classes** (Nouns in problem statement) | Core OOA step |
| Define **attributes** (state) and **operations** (behavior) | Encapsulation design |
| Establish **relationships** between classes | Coupling & cohesion analysis |
| Forward & Reverse Engineering from code | Model-Driven Architecture (MDA) |
| Documentation for maintenance teams | ISO 12207 software lifecycle |

---

## 2. Components of a Class — The 3-Tier Rectangle

Every class in UML is represented as a **rectangle divided into three compartments**:

```
+-----------------------+
|       ClassName        |   ← 1st Compartment: Class Name
+-----------------------+
| - attribute1 : Type    |   ← 2nd Compartment: Attributes
| + attribute2 : Type    |
+-----------------------+
| + method1() : Return   |   ← 3rd Compartment: Operations
| - method2() : Return   |
+-----------------------+
```

### Visibility Modifiers (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Symbol | Visibility | Meaning | Java/C++ Equivalent |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| `+` | **Public** | Accessible from any class | `public` |
| `-` | **Private** | Accessible only within the class | `private` |
| `#` | **Protected** | Accessible in subclass & package | `protected` |
| `~` | **Package** | Accessible within same package | default (no modifier) |
| _italics_ | **Abstract** | Class/method is abstract | `abstract` |

> [!TIP]
> **Static members** are indicated by **underlining** the member name (e.g., `+ count : int` with count underlined = `static int count`).

---

## 3. Types of Relationships — The Heart of the Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the different relationships in a Class Diagram with suitable examples."* This is a **guaranteed 7-14 mark question**.

### A. Association (`————>`)

A general **"uses-a"** semantic relationship where one class references another. It is a **structural relationship** between peer classes.

- **Example:** A `Teacher` *teaches* a `Student`.
- **Drawn as:** A solid line, optionally with a label and arrow.
- **Multiplicity** is written at each end (e.g., `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`).

**Multiplicity Notations (KTU High-Yield):**

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `0..1` | Optional (zero or one) |
| `n..m` | Range between n and m |

### B. Aggregation (Empty Diamond `◇` — "Whole–Part, Weak Ownership")

A **"has-a"** relationship representing a **weak** whole–part association. The part **can exist independently** of the whole.

- **Drawn as:** Solid line with an **empty (hollow) diamond** at the whole side.
- **Example:** A `Department` ◇— `Professor`. A Professor can exist without a Department.
- **Mnemonic:** "**A**ggregation = **A**wkward (no ownership)"

### C. Composition (Filled Diamond `◆` — "Strong Ownership")

A **"contains-a"** relationship representing **strong ownership**. The part's **lifecycle is tied** to the whole — if the whole is destroyed, the parts are destroyed.

- **Drawn as:** Solid line with a **filled (solid) diamond** at the whole side.
- **Example:** A `House` ◆— `Room`. If the House is demolished, Rooms cease to exist.
- **Mnemonic:** "**C**omposition = **C**annot survive without the whole"

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mistake in KTU Exams:**
> Students confuse Aggregation and Composition. The **deciding question is**: *"If the whole (container) is destroyed, does the part also get destroyed?"*
> - **Yes** → **Composition** (filled diamond ◆)
> - **No** → **Aggregation** (empty diamond ◇)

### D. Generalization / Inheritance (`————▷`)

An **"is-a"** relationship where a subclass inherits from a superclass. Represents **taxonomic hierarchy**.

- **Drawn as:** Solid line with a **hollow triangle arrow** pointing to the parent.
- **Example:** `Car` is a `Vehicle`; `SavingsAccount` is a `BankAccount`.

### E. Dependency (`- - - ▷`)

A **"uses-temporarily"** relationship. The dependent class **does not hold a permanent reference**; it merely uses the other class as a parameter, local variable, or in a method call.

- **Drawn as:** **Dashed line** with an open arrow.
- **Example:** A `ShoppingCart` depends on `CreditCard` (passed as a parameter to `checkout()`).

### F. Realization / Implementation (`- - - ▷`)

The relationship between a **class** and the **interface it implements**, or between a **use case** and the **collaboration that realizes it**.

- **Drawn as:** **Dashed line** with a **hollow triangle** arrow pointing to the interface.
- **Example:** `ArrayList` realizes the `List` interface.

### 📊 Master Summary Table of Relationships

| Relationship | UML Symbol | Keyword | Strength | Lifetime Dependency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Association | `——>` | *uses-a* | Peer | Independent |
| Aggregation | `◇——` | *has-a* | Weak whole–part | Independent |
| Composition | `◆——` | *owns-a* | Strong whole–part | Dependent |
| Generalization | `——▷` | *is-a* | Inheritance | N/A |
| Dependency | `- - ▷` | *uses-temporarily* | Weakest | Temporary |
| Realization | `- - ▷` | *implements* | Contract-based | Independent |

---

## 4. Advanced Class Diagram Concepts

### Abstract Classes
- Written in **italics** (e.g., *Shape*).
- **Cannot be instantiated**; meant only for inheritance.
- May have both abstract (italic) and concrete members.

### Interfaces
- Represented as a **circle (lollipop)** or a **rectangle with `<<interface>>` stereotype**.
- Only contains **abstract operations** (no implementation).
- Example: `<<interface>> Drawable` with method `+ draw() : void`.

### Packages
- A **folder-shaped rectangle** used to group related classes.
- Labeled `<<package>>` or with package name in the tab.
- Helps in **modular design** and namespace management.

### Notes and Constraints
- A **dog-eared rectangle** (like a folded paper corner) used to add **free-form explanatory text** to a diagram.
- Constraints can be written in **curly braces** `{ordered, XOR}`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 1. The Class Identification Process (CRC Method)

The **Class-Responsibility-Collaboration (CRC) cards** technique is the standard KTU-recommended approach to derive classes from a problem statement.

### Step-by-Step Class Identification Algorithm

1. **Read the problem statement** thoroughly.
2. **Identify the nouns** — these are **candidate classes**.
3. **Filter the candidates** using the **"Common Classes" checklist**:
   - Tangible things (Car, Book)
   - Roles (Customer, Manager)
   - Events (Sale, Transaction)
   - Interactions (Purchase, Marriage)
4. **Identify the verbs** — these are **candidate operations**.
5. **Identify adjectives / state descriptors** — these are **attributes**.
6. **Eliminate irrelevant classes** (redundant, vague, implementation-specific like `ArrayList`).
7. **Group classes** with high cohesion into **packages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Kent Beck):** A class should have **one clear responsibility** (Single Responsibility Principle). If a class has more than 3–5 responsibilities, consider splitting it.

---

## 2. Multiplicity Rules — The Mathematical Heart

While class diagrams are qualitative, **multiplicity is a precise quantitative specification** following the **Object Constraint Language (OCL)** grammar.

### Formal Multiplicity Grammar (OCL Subset)

$$ multiplicity \;::=\; integer \;|\; '*' \;|\; range \;|\; '0..1' $$

$$ range \;::=\; integer \;'..' \;integer $$

### Allowed Multiplicity Values

| Multiplicity | Set Theory Interpretation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `0..1` | $\subseteq \{0, 1\}$ | Optional, at most one |
| `1` | $= \{1\}$ | Mandatory, exactly one |
| `1..*` | $\subseteq \mathbb{N}_{\geq 1}$ | At least one, many |
| `*` or `0..*` | $\subseteq \mathbb{N}_0$ | Zero or many (unbounded) |
| `n..m` | $\subseteq \{n, n+1, \dots, m\}$ | Bounded range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The asterisk `*` is a **shorthand for unbounded infinity** in UML — equivalent to $\mathbb{N}_0$ in set theory.

---

## 3. Relationship Constraints — Reading Directional Strength

The **strength** of a relationship determines **coupling** and **maintainability**:

$$ \text{Coupling Strength} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{Independence of classes}} $$

| Relationship | Coupling Level | Recommended Use |
|---|---|---|
| Dependency | Very Low | Method parameter, local variable |
| Association | Medium | Object reference held as field |
| Aggregation | Medium-High | Field holding a collection (weak) |
| Composition | High | Field holding a collection (strong) |
| Realization | Low | Contract implementation |
| Generalization | High | IS-A hierarchy (use sparingly) |

---

## 4. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it summarizes 90% of class diagram theory questions.**

| Concept | UML Notation | Real-world Cue | Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class | 3-compartment rectangle | Noun in problem statement | "Object blueprint" |
| Attribute | `visibility name : type` | Property / state of object | "Data member" |
| Operation | `visibility name(args) : return` | Verb in problem statement | "Method" |
| Public | `+` | Accessible everywhere | `public` |
| Private | `-` | Internal to class | `private` |
| Protected | `#` | Accessible to subclasses | `protected` |
| Abstract | *italics* | Incomplete; must be extended | `abstract` |
| Static | _underlined_ | Shared across all instances | `static` |
| Interface | `<<interface>>` or lollipop ◯ | Pure contract | "100% abstract" |
| Association | `——>` | General peer link | "uses-a" |
| Aggregation | `◇——` | Weak whole–part | "has-a (weak)" |
| Composition | `◆——` | Strong whole–part | "owns-a / part-of" |
| Generalization | `——▷` | IS-A hierarchy | "inherits-from" |
| Dependency | `- - ▷` | Temporary use | "depends-on" |
| Realization | `- - ▷` | Implements interface | "realizes" |
| Multiplicity | `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*` | Cardinality constraint | OCL grammar |
| Package | Folder shape | Logical grouping | Namespace |
| Note | Dog-eared rectangle | Free-form comment | Constraint `{...}` |

---

## 5. Real-World Engineering Utility

Class diagrams are not just academic — they are the **foundation of Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**:

- **Code Generation:** Tools like **Enterprise Architect**, **Rational Rose**, **StarUML** can auto-generate **Java, C++, Python, SQL** skeletons from a class diagram.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Tools like **Visual Paradigm** can reconstruct a class diagram from existing source code.
- **Database Design:** A class diagram maps directly to an **ER (Entity-Relationship) diagram** and hence to **relational schemas**.
- **API Documentation:** Class diagrams form the basis of **Swagger/OpenAPI** specifications for REST services.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Building a Class Diagram

## Sample Problem (KTU-Style)

> *"Design a class diagram for an **Online Course Management System**. The system has Students who enroll in Courses. Each Course is taught by an Instructor and contains multiple Modules. A Student can submit Assignments for each Module, and an Instructor grades them."*

### Step 1: Identify Nouns (Candidate Classes)

Reading the problem: **Student, Course, Instructor, Module, Assignment, System, Grade**.

### Step 2: Apply the Filter

| Noun | Decision | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Student | ✅ **Keep** | Tangible entity with identity |
| Course | ✅ **Keep** | Tangible entity |
| Instructor | ✅ **Keep** | Role-based entity |
| Module | ✅ **Keep** | Tangible sub-entity |
| Assignment | ✅ **Keep** | Event / document entity |
| Grade | ⚠️ Refine | Becomes an **attribute** of Assignment, not a class |
| System | ❌ **Discard** | Redundant — *the system IS the diagram* |

### Step 3: Identify Verbs → Operations

| Class | Verbs | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| Student | enroll, viewCourses | `+ enroll(c: Course) : void` |
| Course | addStudent, listModules | `+ addStudent(s: Student)` |
| Instructor | teach, gradeAssignment | `+ gradeAssignment(a: Assignment, g: Grade)` |
| Module | getContent | `+ getContent() : String` |
| Assignment | submit, getSubmission | `+ submit(content: String) : void` |

### Step 4: Identify Adjectives → Attributes

| Class | Attributes |
|---|---|
| Student | `- studentId : int`, `- name : String`, `- email : String` |
| Course | `- courseId : int`, `- title : String`, `- credits : int` |
| Instructor | `- instructorId : int`, `- name : String`, `- department : String` |
| Module | `- moduleId : int`, `- title : String`, `- contentURL : String` |
| Assignment | `- assignmentId : int`, `- maxMarks : int`, `- submissionDate : Date` |

### Step 5: Identify Relationships

| Source | Verb Phrase | Target | Relationship | Multiplicity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Course | "taught by" | Instructor | Association | `1..*` to `1` |
| Student | "enrolls in" | Course | Association | `1..*` to `1..*` |
| Course | "contains" | Module | **Composition** (◆) | `1` to `1..*` |
| Module | "has" | Assignment | **Aggregation** (◇) | `1` to `0..*` |
| Assignment | "graded by" | Instructor | Association | `1..*` to `1` |
| Student | "submits" | Assignment | Association | `1` to `0..*` |

### Step 6: Final Verification

Using the formula:

$$ \text{Number of Classes} = \mid \text{Filtered Nouns} \mid = 5 $$

$$ \text{Number of Relationships} = \mid \text{Verbs} \mid_{\text{filtered}} = 6 $$

$$ \text{Diagram Complexity} = \frac{\text{Relationships}}{\text{Classes}} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2 \quad (\text{Healthy range: } 0.8 \text{ to } 1.5) $$

> [!NOTE]
> A **Complexity Ratio** in the range **0.8 to 1.5** indicates a **well-balanced** object-oriented design. Values > 2.0 suggest **over-coupling**, and < 0.5 suggests **under-utilization** of OOP.

This validated class set and relationship map now form the basis for the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. The Complete Class Diagram (Mermaid)

Below is the **rendered Mermaid class diagram** for the *Online Course Management System* derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR

    class Student {
        - studentId : int
        - name : String
        - email : String
        + enroll(c : Course) void
        + viewCourses() Course[]
    }

    class Course {
        - courseId : int
        - title : String
        - credits : int
        + addStudent(s : Student) void
        + listModules() Module[]
    }

    class Instructor {
        - instructorId : int
        - name : String
        - department : String
        + teach(c : Course) void
        + gradeAssignment(a : Assignment) void
    }

    class Module {
        - moduleId : int
        - title : String
        - contentURL : String
        + getContent() String
    }

    class Assignment {
        - assignmentId : int
        - maxMarks : int
        - submissionDate : Date
        + submit(content : String) void
    }

    %% Relationships with multiplicities
    Student "1..*" --> "1..*" Course : enrolls in
    Course "1" --> "1..*" Module : contains
    Module "1" o-- "0..*" Assignment : has
    Course "1..*" --> "1" Instructor : taught by
    Assignment "0..*" --> "1" Instructor : graded by
    Student "1" --> "0..*" Assignment : submits
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - `Course "1" --> "1..*" Module` → One Course contains one or more Modules (**Composition** direction).
> - `Module "1" o-- "0..*" Assignment` → The hollow diamond `o--` denotes **Aggregation** in Mermaid's class diagram syntax.
> - `-->` is **Association**, `--▷` would be **Inheritance** (none used here as all classes are concrete).

---

## 2. Relationship Type Reference (Mermaid Notation Map)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction TB

    class Animal {
        <<abstract>>
        # name : String
        + eat() void
    }

    class Dog {
        + bark() void
    }

    class Vehicle {
        + move() void
    }

    class Engine {
        - horsepower : int
        + start() void
    }

    class Wheel {
        - size : int
    }

    class IPayable {
        <<interface>>
        + pay(amount : double) boolean
    }

    class Invoice {
        + pay(amount : double) boolean
    }

    class OrderProcessor {
        + process(o : Order) void
    }

    class Order {
        + orderId : int
    }

    %% Inheritance (Generalization)
    Animal <|-- Dog

    %% Composition
    Vehicle *-- Engine

    %% Aggregation
    Vehicle o-- Wheel

    %% Realization
    IPayable <|.. Invoice

    %% Dependency
    OrderProcessor ..> Order
```

### Diagram Key

| Mermaid Syntax | UML Relationship |
|---|---|
| `<\|--` | Generalization (Inheritance) |
| `*--` | Composition |
| `o--` | Aggregation |
| `-->` | Association |
| `<\|..` | Realization (interface implementation) |
| `..>` | Dependency |
| `<<abstract>>` | Abstract class stereotype |
| `<<interface>>` | Interface stereotype |

---

## 3. Visibility Modifier Legend

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class VisibilityDemo {
        + publicAttribute : int
        - privateAttribute : int
        # protectedAttribute : int
        ~ packageAttribute : int
        + publicMethod() void
        - privateMethod() void
        # protectedMethod() void
    }
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Mermaid, the **first character** of the member declaration determines visibility:
> - `+` Public
> - `-` Private
> - `#` Protected
> - `~` Package/Default

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Define a Class Diagram. List any four relationships used in a class diagram with their UML notations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Class Diagram** is a static structural UML diagram that depicts the **classes**, their **attributes**, **operations**, and the **static relationships** between the classes of a system. It is the most fundamental diagram in object-oriented modeling and is used during the analysis and design phases of software development.

**Four Relationships (½ mark each):**

| Relationship | UML Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Association | `——>` | "Uses-a" general peer relationship |
| Aggregation | `◇——>` | Weak "has-a" whole–part |
| Composition | `◆——>` | Strong "owns-a" whole–part |
| Generalization | `——▷` | "Is-a" inheritance hierarchy |
| Dependency | `- - ▷` | Temporary usage |
| Realization | `- - ▷` | Interface implementation |

*(Any four = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Differentiate between Aggregation and Composition with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol | **Empty (hollow) diamond** at whole side | **Filled (solid) diamond** at whole side |
| Relationship strength | Weak "has-a" | Strong "contains-a" / "owns-a" |
| Lifetime dependency | Part **can exist** without the whole | Part **cannot exist** without the whole |
| Ownership | Shared / optional | Exclusive |
| Example | `Department ◇— Professor` (Professor can belong to no department temporarily) | `House ◆— Room` (Rooms cannot exist without a House) |

**Example sentence (1 mark):** *A `Library` aggregates `Book` (a book can exist in multiple libraries over time), but a `Human` composes a `Heart` (a heart cannot exist without a human).*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of multiplicity in class diagrams with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a class diagram for a **Hospital Management System** with the following description: *"A Hospital has multiple Departments. Each Department has a Head Doctor and several Doctors. Patients visit Doctors and get admitted to Wards. Each Ward has multiple Beds."* Show all classes, attributes, operations, and relationships with proper multiplicities. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Multiplicity in Class Diagrams** (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Multiplicity specifies the **number of instances** of one class that can be associated with **one instance** of the related class. It is a cardinality constraint written at each end of an association line.

**Standard Notations (3 marks):**

| Notation | Meaning | Set-Theoretic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one | $\{1\}$ |
| `0..1` | Optional, at most one | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many (unbounded) | $\mathbb{N}_0$ |
| `1..*` | At least one, many | $\mathbb{N}_{\geq 1}$ |
| `n..m` | Bounded range | $\{n, n+1, \ldots, m\}$ |

**Worked Examples (3 marks):**

1. **One-to-One (`1 — 1`):** A `Person` is associated with exactly one `Passport`, and vice-versa.
   $$ \text{Person} \xrightarrow{1} \xleftrightarrow{1} \text{Passport} $$

2. **One-to-Many (`1 — 0..*`):** A `Department` has zero or more `Employees`.
   $$ \text{Department} \xrightarrow{1} \xleftrightarrow{0..*} \text{Employee} $$

3. **Many-to-Many (`* — *`):** A `Student` can enroll in many `Course`s, and a `Course` has many `Student`s.
   $$ \text{Student} \xrightarrow{*} \xleftrightarrow{*} \text{Course} $$

**Practical Significance:** Multiplicity helps in determining **foreign key cardinality** during database schema design and in implementing **container fields** (e.g., `List<Course>`) in code.

---

#### **Part (b) — Hospital Management System Class Diagram** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Classes Identified (1 mark):** `Hospital`, `Department`, `Doctor`, `HeadDoctor`, `Patient`, `Ward`, `Bed`.

**Step 2 — Attributes & Operations (3 marks):**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| Hospital | `- hospitalId : int`, `- name : String`, `- location : String` | `+ addDepartment(d : Department)` |
| Department | `- deptId : int`, `- name : String` | `+ addDoctor(d : Doctor)` |
| Doctor | `- doctorId : int`, `- name : String`, `- specialization : String` | `+ treat(p : Patient)` |
| Patient | `- patientId : int`, `- name : String`, `- age : int` | `+ getAdmitted(w : Ward)` |
| Ward | `- wardId : int`, `- wardType : String` | `+ assignBed(b : Bed)` |
| Bed | `- bedId : int`, `- isOccupied : boolean` | `+ occupy()` , `+ vacate()` |

**Step 3 — Mermaid Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR

    class Hospital {
        - hospitalId : int
        - name : String
        - location : String
        + addDepartment(d : Department) void
    }

    class Department {
        - deptId : int
        - name : String
        + addDoctor(d : Doctor) void
    }

    class Doctor {
        - doctorId : int
        - name : String
        - specialization : String
        + treat(p : Patient) void
    }

    class Patient {
        - patientId : int
        - name : String
        - age : int
        + getAdmitted(w : Ward) void
    }

    class Ward {
        - wardId : int
        - wardType : String
        + assignBed(b : Bed) void
    }

    class Bed {
        - bedId : int
        - isOccupied : boolean
        + occupy() void
        + vacate() void
    }

    Hospital "1" *-- "1..*" Department : contains
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Doctor : has
    Department "1" --> "1" Doctor : headed by
    Doctor "1" --> "0..*" Patient : treats
    Patient "1" --> "0..1" Ward : admitted to
    Ward "1" *-- "1..*" Bed : contains
```

**Relationship Justifications:**
- `Hospital ◆— Department` (**Composition**): Departments cannot exist without the hospital.
- `Department ◇— Doctor` (**Aggregation**): Doctors have an independent professional identity.
- `Patient — Ward` (**Association, 0..1**): A patient may not always be admitted.
- `Ward ◆— Bed` (**Composition**): Beds lose meaning if the ward is closed.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — **Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the various components (compartments) of a UML class. Discuss the different visibility modifiers used in class diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a class diagram for an **E-Commerce Shopping System**: *"A Customer can place multiple Orders. Each Order contains one or more Products. A Product belongs to a Category. The system processes Payments and ships Orders via Shipment."* Include classes, attributes, methods, multiplicities, and at least one inheritance and one composition relationship. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Components of a UML Class & Visibility Modifiers** (7 marks)

**Three Compartments of a Class (3 marks):**

A UML class is depicted as a **rectangle divided into three horizontal compartments**:

```
+----------------------------+
|         ClassName          |  ← Compartment 1: Class Name
+----------------------------+
| - attr1 : Type            |  ← Compartment 2: Attributes
| + attr2 : Type = default  |
+----------------------------+
| + method1(arg: Type): Rtn  |  ← Compartment 3: Operations
| - method2() : void        |
+----------------------------+
```

1. **Class Name Compartment:** Contains the class name (in **bold** for concrete, *italics* for abstract). Stereotypes like `<<interface>>` may appear above.
2. **Attribute Compartment:** Lists the **state (data members)** with visibility, name, type, and optional default value.
   Syntax: `visibility name : type [multiplicity] = default`
3. **Operation Compartment:** Lists the **behavior (methods/functions)** with visibility, name, parameters, and return type.
   Syntax: `visibility name(paramList) : returnType`

**Visibility Modifiers (3 marks):**

| Symbol | Visibility | Scope | OOP Equivalent |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| `+` | Public | All classes | `public` |
| `-` | Private | Same class only | `private` |
| `#` | Protected | Same class + subclasses | `protected` |
| `~` | Package | Same package | default |

**Special Notations (1 mark):**
- **Underlined members** → Static (class-level) members.
- **Italicized members** → Abstract members.
- **Derived attributes** → Prefixed with `/` (e.g., `/age : int` — computed from dateOfBirth).

---

#### **Part (b) — E-Commerce Class Diagram** (7 marks)

**Classes (1 mark):** `Customer`, `Order`, `Product`, `Category`, `Payment`, `Shipment`, `ElectronicProduct` (subclass).

**Attributes & Operations (2 marks):**

| Class | Key Members |
|---|---|
| `Customer` | `- customerId`, `- name`, `- email`; `+ placeOrder()` |
| `Order` | `- orderId`, `- orderDate`, `- totalAmount`; `+ calculateTotal()` |
| `Product` | `# productId : int`, `# name : String`, `# price : double`; `+ getDiscount()` |
| `ElectronicProduct` | `- warrantyMonths : int`; `+ getWarrantyInfo()` |
| `Category` | `- categoryId`, `- categoryName` |
| `Payment` | `- paymentId`, `- amount`, `- mode`; `+ process()` |
| `Shipment` | `- trackingId`, `- status`; `+ dispatch()` |

**Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR

    class Customer {
        - customerId : int
        - name : String
        - email : String
        + placeOrder(o : Order) void
    }

    class Order {
        - orderId : int
        - orderDate : Date
        - totalAmount : double
        + calculateTotal() double
    }

    class Product {
        <<abstract>>
        # productId : int
        # name : String
        # price : double
        + getDiscount() double
    }

    class ElectronicProduct {
        - warrantyMonths : int
        + getWarrantyInfo() String
    }

    class ClothingProduct {
        - size : String
        + getSize() String
    }

    class Category {
        - categoryId : int
        - categoryName : String
    }

    class Payment {
        - paymentId : int
        - amount : double
        - mode : String
        + process() boolean
    }

    class Shipment {
        - trackingId : String
        - status : String
        + dispatch() void
    }

    %% Inheritance
    Product <|-- ElectronicProduct
    Product <|-- ClothingProduct

    %% Composition
    Order "1" *-- "1..*" Product : contains

    %% Associations with multiplicity
    Customer "1" --> "0..*" Order : places
    Order "1" --> "1" Payment : paid via
    Order "1" --> "1" Shipment : shipped via
    Category "1" --> "1..*" Product : groups
```

**Why these relationships (1 mark):**
- **Inheritance (`Product <|-- ElectronicProduct`):** "ElectronicProduct **is-a** Product" — true generalization.
- **Composition (`Order ◆— Product`):** An Order-Line cannot logically exist once the parent Order is cancelled, hence strong ownership.
- **Association with multiplicity:** Captures realistic business rules (one customer → many orders; one order → one shipment).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Class Diagram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Aggregation vs. Composition (–2 marks typical):** The deciding rule is **lifetime dependency**, not just visual preference. Always state the justification in your answer text.
> 2. **Missing Multiplicity (–1 to –2 marks):** A class diagram without multiplicities is considered **incomplete** by KTU examiners. Always annotate `1`, `*`, `0..1`, etc., at both ends of every association.
> 3. **Wrong arrow direction in Inheritance (–1 mark):** The **hollow triangle points to the PARENT (superclass)**, not the child. Students frequently reverse this.
> 4. **Using Dashed line for Association (–1 mark):** Association is a **solid** line. Dashed lines are reserved for **Dependency** and **Realization** only.
> 5. **Omitting visibility modifiers (–1 mark):** Always use `+`, `-`, `#`, `~` for each attribute and operation. Bare names will be penalized.
> 6. **Too many classes (over-engineering):** KTU expects **5–8 well-justified classes** for a typical 7-mark diagram question. More than 10 usually means you have not filtered the nouns.
> 7. **No "justification sentence":** Always end with 1–2 lines explaining **why** a particular relationship (e.g., composition over aggregation) was chosen.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Class Diagram (Module 2)**

- ✅ Class Diagram is a **static structural** UML diagram.
- ✅ Three compartments: **Name | Attributes | Operations**.
- ✅ Visibility: `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- ✅ **Italicized** class name → abstract; **underlined** member → static.
- ✅ Six relationships: **Association, Aggregation, Composition, Generalization, Dependency, Realization**.
- ✅ **Composition (◆)** = strong ownership + lifetime dependency.
- ✅ **Aggregation (◇)** = weak ownership + independent lifetime.
- ✅ **Generalization (▷)** = hollow triangle pointing to parent.
- ✅ **Dependency** = dashed line, temporary usage (method parameter).
- ✅ **Realization** = dashed line with hollow triangle, class implements interface.
- ✅ **Multiplicity** is mandatory on both ends of an association.
- ✅ Common multiplicities: `1`, `*`, `0..1`, `1..*`, `n..m`.
- ✅ Class identification = **noun extraction + CRC filtering**.
- ✅ Complexity ratio (Relationships ÷ Classes) should lie in **[0.8, 1.5]**.
- ✅ Always include **a justification line** for every relationship choice in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **State Diagram (UML State Machine Diagram)**
> A *State Diagram* is a **Unified Modeling Language (UML) behavioral diagram** that depicts the **dynamic behavior of a single object** by illustrating the **sequence of states** it passes through during its lifetime, in response to a finite set of external/internal **events**. It captures the permissible state transitions, the events that trigger them, and the actions/activities performed on each transition or within each state.

According to the **OMG UML 2.5 Specification** (which KTU 2024 syllabus references), a State Diagram models a **discrete behavior** driven by a finite-state system of events, and is most often applied to objects that exhibit *interesting* lifecycle behavior — i.e., objects whose responses to events vary depending on their current state.

**Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Standard Terminology):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **State** | A condition or situation of an object during its lifecycle |
| **Transition** | A change from one state to another in response to an event |
| **Event** | A notable occurrence (internal/external) that may trigger a transition |
| **Guard Condition** | A boolean expression that must be true for a transition to fire |
| **Action** | An atomic, instantaneous computation attached to a transition |
| **Activity** | A non-atomic, time-consuming computation attached to a state |
| **Initial State** | The starting point of the modeled behavior (filled black circle) |
| **Final State** | The termination point of the modeled behavior (bull's-eye circle) |
| **Composite State** | A state containing nested substates |
| **Concurrent Substates** | Independent sub-state machines within a state |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Vending Machine

Imagine a **vending machine** you use every day on the KTU campus.

- When you walk up, the machine is in the **Idle** state, waiting patiently.
- The moment you insert a ₹10 coin, an **event** `coinInserted(10)` occurs, and the machine **transitions** to the *Credit = ₹10* state.
- Insert another ₹10, total credit becomes ₹20, and the machine may **transition** to a `Ready` state.
- Now press a button (e.g., *Coke*) → event `select(Coke)` fires → guard condition checks credit ≥ ₹20 → if true, machine dispenses and **transitions** back to *Idle*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice carefully: the machine's **response to the same event** (e.g., pressing a button) **depends entirely on its current state**. If no coin was inserted, the button press does nothing! This state-dependent behavior is precisely what a State Diagram captures — *the object has a memory of where it currently is*.

**This same concept applies to:**
- A **login session** in a web application (Anonymous → Authenticated → Expired)
- A **TCP connection** (LISTEN → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED → CLOSE_WAIT → CLOSED)
- An **order** in e-commerce (Placed → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered → Cancelled)

## 1.3 Why State Diagrams? (KTU Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Note:** Out of all UML diagrams, **State Diagrams are the most frequently asked in KTU ESE** for Software Engineering. They fall under **Behavioral Modeling** in Module 2 alongside Activity and Sequence diagrams. State diagrams are explicitly **NOT** flowcharts — they describe object lifecycle, not process flow.

State Diagrams shine when:

1. An object's behavior depends on its **current condition** (state-dependent responses).
2. The number of permissible states is **finite and manageable** (typically ≤ 20).
3. The system has **clearly defined events** that cause transitions.
4. We need to model **real-time or reactive systems** (protocols, controllers, UI workflows).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a State Diagram — Core Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 State Notation

In UML, a state is depicted as a **rounded rectangle**. The standard UML state syntax is:

```
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  stateName              │
│  entry / actionOnEntry  │
│  do / activity          │
│  exit / actionOnExit    │
└─────────────────────────┘
```

Where:
- **`entry/`** action executes **atomically** the moment the state is entered.
- **`do/`** activity executes *while* the object is in the state (may be interrupted).
- **`exit/`** action executes **atomically** the moment the state is exited.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** A state without sub-divisions (no entry/do/exit) can be drawn as a simple rounded rectangle with just the state name. This simplification is usually sufficient for 7-mark KTU questions.

### 2.1.2 Transition Notation

The standard UML transition syntax is:

$$ \text{event}[\text{guard}]/\text{action} $$

Where:
- **`event`** → the trigger that may cause the transition (e.g., `coinInserted`, `timeout`).
- **`[guard]`** → a boolean expression that **must evaluate to true** for the transition to fire (e.g., `[balance >= 20]`).
- **`/action`** → the atomic computation executed when the transition fires (e.g., `/dispenseCoke`, `/deductBalance`).

### 2.1.3 Special Pseudo-States

| Pseudo-State | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial** | ● (filled black circle) | Entry point — exactly one per state machine |
| **Final** | ◉ (bull's-eye) | Termination — 0 or more |
| **Decision** | ◇ (diamond) | Dynamic branching based on guards |
| **Fork / Join** | Thick horizontal/vertical bar | Splitting/merging concurrent regions |
| **History** | Ⓗ (H inside circle) | Remembers last active substate of a composite state |
| **Deep History** | Ⓗ* (H* inside circle) | Remembers last active state at any depth |

## 2.2 Two Fundamental Models — Mealy vs. Moore

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Concept:** Every state diagram is classified into one of these two machine types — this is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU ESE.

### 2.2.1 Moore Machine

In a **Moore Machine**, outputs (actions) are associated with **states** — the action depends *only* on the current state, not on the transition or input event.

$$ \text{Output}_t = f(\text{State}_{t}) $$

### 2.2.2 Mealy Machine

In a **Mealy Machine**, outputs (actions) are associated with **transitions** — the action depends on both the current state AND the input event.

$$ \text{Output}_t = f(\text{State}_{t},\ \text{Input}_{t}) $$

### 2.2.3 Comparative Analysis

| Property | Moore Machine | Mealy Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Output depends on | Current state only | Current state + Input event |
| Action placed at | Inside the state | On the transition arrow |
| Number of states | Usually more | Usually fewer |
| Output timing | Synchronous with state entry | Synchronous with transition firing |
| Implementation simplicity | Easier to debug | More compact |
| Real-world use | Vending machines, traffic lights | Communication protocols, controllers |
| Safety on input glitches | Output stable | May produce glitches |

## 2.3 Construction Methodology — Step-by-Step Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked *"Draw a state diagram for ..."*, examiners award marks for **states identified (3 marks)**, **transitions with events (3 marks)**, and **guards/actions (1 mark)**. Total 7 marks.

**Step 1 — Identify States**
List all distinguishable conditions the object can be in. Ask: *"What does the user/system observe?"*

**Step 2 — Identify Events**
List all internal/external occurrences that may change the object's state.

**Step 3 — Identify Transitions**
For each (state, event) pair, decide:
- Does the event cause a transition? If yes, to which state?
- Or is the event ignored in this state?

**Step 4 — Add Guards**
For ambiguous transitions from the same state on the same event, add guard conditions.

**Step 5 — Add Actions**
Specify the atomic computation on each transition (Mealy) or state (Moore).

**Step 6 — Identify Initial and Final States**
Mark the entry and exit points.

**Step 7 — Validate**
Verify reachability: *every state must be reachable from the initial state*, and *no dead states* unless intentional.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| # | Symbol / Notation | UML Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ● | Initial pseudo-state |
| 2 | ◉ | Final state |
| 3 | Ⓗ | Shallow history |
| 4 | Ⓗ* | Deep history |
| 5 | ▬▬▬ | Fork/Join bar |
| 6 | ◇ | Decision/junction |
| 7 | `event` | Simple transition trigger |
| 8 | `event[x>0]/y:=x-1` | Event + guard + action |
| 9 | `after(10s)` | Time-based event |
| 10 | `entry/ log()` | Action executed on state entry |
| 11 | `do/ heartbeat()` | Activity executed while in state |
| 12 | `exit/ cleanup()` | Action executed on state exit |
| 13 | `included sub1` | Inclusion of a submachine reference |
| 14 | `send signalA` | Self-transition with deferred trigger |

## 2.5 State Diagram vs. Other UML Behavioral Diagrams

| Aspect | State Diagram | Activity Diagram | Sequence Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focus | Object lifecycle states | Workflow of activities | Message exchange order |
| Granularity | Per-object | Per-process / business flow | Per-actor interaction |
| Time axis | Implicit (state-to-state) | Implicit (activity flow) | Explicit (vertical lifeline) |
| Best for | Reactive/real-time systems | Business processes | Multi-object collaboration |
| Parallelism | Fork/Join in concurrent regions | Fork/Join bars | Par (parallel) fragments |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: TCP Connection Lifecycle (Classic KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark Question):**
> *"Draw a UML state diagram for the TCP connection establishment and termination, showing all states, events, transitions, and key actions."*

### Step 1 — Identify States

From RFC 793, the principal states are:

$$\text{States} = \{ \text{CLOSED},\ \text{LISTEN},\ \text{SYN\_SENT},\ \text{SYN\_RECEIVED},\ \text{ESTABLISHED},\ \text{FIN\_WAIT\_1},\ \text{FIN\_WAIT\_2},\ \text{CLOSE\_WAIT},\ \text{CLOSING},\ \text{LAST\_ACK},\ \text{TIME\_WAIT} \}$$

Total = **11 states**. For a simplified KTU answer, we use 6 essential states.

### Step 2 — Identify Events

$$\text{Events} = \{ \text{open},\ \text{sendSYN},\ \text{receiveSYN},\ \text{receiveSYNACK},\ \text{sendACK},\ \text{close},\ \text{sendFIN},\ \text{receiveFIN},\ \text{receiveACK},\ \text{timeout}(2\text{MSL}) \}$$

### Step 3 — Define Transitions

For each event in each state, we apply the TCP rule book:

1. From **CLOSED**: on event `open` → transition to **LISTEN** (server) or **SYN_SENT** (client).
2. From **LISTEN**: on `receiveSYN` → transition to **SYN_RECEIVED**, action: `sendSYNACK`.
3. From **SYN_SENT**: on `receiveSYNACK` → transition to **ESTABLISHED**, action: `sendACK`.
4. From **ESTABLISHED**: on `close` → transition to **FIN_WAIT_1**, action: `sendFIN`.
5. From **FIN_WAIT_1**: on `receiveACK` → transition to **FIN_WAIT_2**.
6. From **FIN_WAIT_2**: on `receiveFIN` → transition to **TIME_WAIT**, action: `sendACK`.
7. From **TIME_WAIT**: on `timeout(2MSL)` → transition to **CLOSED**.

### Step 4 — Express Transitions Formally

For the client-side handshake, the transition logic is:

$$
\text{State}_{t+1} = \begin{cases}
\text{SYN\_SENT} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{CLOSED} \land \text{Event} = \text{open}_{\text{client}} \\
\text{ESTABLISHED} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{SYN\_SENT} \land \text{Event} = \text{receiveSYNACK} \\
\text{FIN\_WAIT\_1} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{ESTABLISHED} \land \text{Event} = \text{close} \\
\text{CLOSED} & \text{if } \text{State}_t = \text{TIME\_WAIT} \land \text{Event} = \text{timeout}(2\text{MSL}) \\
\text{State}_t & \text{otherwise (ignored event)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Validate

- **Reachability check:** CLOSED → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT → CLOSED ✓
- **Total transitions** drawn in our simplified model = **9**
- **Machine type:** This is a **Mealy machine** because actions (e.g., `sendSYNACK`) are attached to transitions, not states.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Microwave Oven Controller (Moore Machine)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A microwave oven has three modes: *Idle*, *Cooking*, and *DoorOpen*. Transitions: button press, door open, door close, timer expiry.

### Step 1 — Identify States

$$S = \{\text{Idle},\ \text{Cooking},\ \text{DoorOpen}\}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Events

$$E = \{\text{pressStart},\ \text{timerExpired},\ \text{doorOpened},\ \text{doorClosed}\}$$

### Step 3 — Identify Actions (Moore — placed inside states)

- **Idle** → `entry/ lampOff, magnetronOff`
- **Cooking** → `entry/ lampOn, magnetronOn` + `do/ countdownTimer`
- **DoorOpen** → `entry/ lampOn, magnetronOff`

### Step 4 — Transition Table

| Current State | Event | Next State | Guard | Action on Transition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle | `pressStart` | Cooking | `[time > 0]` | /none (Moore) |
| Idle | `doorOpened` | DoorOpen | — | — |
| Cooking | `timerExpired` | Idle | — | — |
| Cooking | `doorOpened` | DoorOpen | — | — |
| DoorOpen | `doorClosed` | Idle | — | — |

### Step 5 — Validation

This is a **pure Moore machine**: every action depends *only* on the state, never on the event. Notice how `lampOn` happens both in Cooking and DoorOpen — but since it is attached to each state's `entry/`, the output is deterministic from state alone.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — State Minimization Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Optional Extension (Moore's Algorithm):** When you have a redundant state diagram, you can minimize it. Marks are usually NOT awarded for this in 14-mark questions, but it shows examiner-level mastery.

**Given:** A deterministic finite automaton (DFA) with states $S$, input alphabet $\Sigma$, transition function $\delta$, and final states $F$.

**Goal:** Find the minimum equivalent DFA.

**Step 1 — Initialization (0th Partition)**

$$
P_0 = \{F,\ S \setminus F\}
$$

Separate accepting (final) from non-accepting states.

**Step 2 — Iterative Refinement**

For each iteration $i$, build partition $P_{i+1}$ by splitting any block $B \in P_i$ where two states $s_1, s_2 \in B$ transition to different blocks under the same input symbol.

$$
(s_1, s_2) \text{ are equivalent} \iff \forall a \in \Sigma:\ \delta(s_1, a) \equiv \delta(s_2, a) \pmod{P_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Termination**

The algorithm terminates when $P_{i+1} = P_i$. The number of blocks in the final partition equals the **minimum number of states**.

**Example:** For a DFA with states $\{A, B, C, D\}$ and final states $\{C\}$, we get:

$$
P_0 = \{\{C\},\ \{A, B, D\}\}
$$

After applying the refinement step:

$$
P_1 = \{\{C\},\ \{B, D\},\ \{A\}\}
$$

After one more iteration:

$$
P_2 = \{\{C\},\ \{D\},\ \{B\},\ \{A\}\} \quad \text{(no further splits)}
$$

The minimal DFA has **4 states** in this case (already minimal).

---

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls When Drawing State Diagrams

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall List (Loses 2-3 Marks per Mistake):**

1. **Using a flowchart instead of a state diagram.** A flowchart models procedural flow; a state diagram models object lifecycle. They are NOT interchangeable.
2. **Forgetting the initial pseudo-state (●).** Every state diagram must have exactly one initial state — no more, no less.
3. **Missing final states.** A state diagram that never terminates is incomplete.
4. **No transition labels.** Arrows without `event[guard]/action` labels earn only 50% credit.
5. **Confusing `entry/` with `do/`.** Entry is instantaneous; do is durative.
6. **Self-loop confusion.** A self-transition re-executes both `exit/` and `entry/` actions.
7. **Mixing Mealy and Moore without rationale.** Pick one style and stay consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Coffee Vending Machine

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block uses the **stateDiagram-v2** syntax with strict alphanumeric node identifiers and clean, plain-text labels. This renders directly in GitHub, VS Code, and most modern markdown viewers.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> HasCredit : coinInserted
    Idle --> Idle : coinInserted [noCoinSlot]
    HasCredit --> Idle : coinReturned
    HasCredit --> Selection : buttonPressed [balanceGTEQPrice]
    HasCredit --> Idle : buttonPressed [balanceLTPrice]
    Selection --> Dispensing : validProductSelected
    Selection --> Idle : timeout / refund
    Dispensing --> Idle : dispenseComplete
    Dispensing --> OutOfOrder : jamDetected
    OutOfOrder --> Idle : reset / maintenanceCleared
    Idle --> [*]
    OutOfOrder --> [*]
```

**Diagram Legend (KTU Board Style):**
- Each **rounded rectangle** is a state.
- Each **arrow** is a transition with the syntax `event [guard]`.
- `[*]` is the **initial/final pseudo-state**.
- Notice the **guard conditions** `[balanceGTEQPrice]` and `[balanceLTPrice]` on the same event — this is how UML handles mutually exclusive transitions.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — State Diagram Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Read SRS Document] --> B[Step 2: Identify Candidate States]
    B --> C{Step 3: Finite and Meaningful?}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Step 4: Enumerate External and Internal Events]
    D --> E[Step 5: Build State Transition Table]
    E --> F{Step 6: Guards Required?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 7: Add Boolean Guard Expressions]
    F -- No --> H[Step 8: Add Mealy or Moore Actions]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 9: Mark Initial and Final States]
    I --> J[Step 10: Validate Reachability and Liveness]
    J --> K{All States Reachable?}
    K -- No --> B
    K -- Yes --> L[Step 11: Render UML State Diagram]
    L --> M[Output: Final UML State Diagram]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#d4edda
    style M fill:#fff3cd
    style C fill:#f8d7da
    style F fill:#f8d7da
    style K fill:#f8d7da
```

**Reading the Architecture:**
- **Blue nodes** = Input stages (reading requirements).
- **Green node** = Validation gate (final QC).
- **Yellow node** = Final deliverable.
- **Pink diamonds** = Decision gates where you may need to loop back.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Mealy vs. Moore Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Moore["Moore Machine"]
        M1[Current State S_t] --> M2[State Register]
        M2 --> M3[Next State Logic delta]
        M3 --> M2
        M2 --> M4[Output Logic lambda of S_t only]
        M4 --> M5[Output Y_t]
    end

    subgraph Mealy["Mealy Machine"]
        L1[Current State S_t] --> L2[State Register]
        L2 --> L3[Next State Logic delta]
        L3 --> L2
        L1 --> L4[Output Logic lambda of S_t and Input X_t]
        L4 --> L5[Output Y_t]
        L2 --> L4
    end

    style Moore fill:#e3f2fd
    style Mealy fill:#fff8e1
```

**Key Visual Difference:**
- In the **Moore** diagram, the output logic depends **only on the state register output**.
- In the **Mealy** diagram, the output logic has **two input arrows** — one from the state register and one from the external input — making it event-aware.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Mealy and Moore machines with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Output depends on | Current state + Input | Current state only |
| Action placement | On transitions | Inside states (entry/do) |
| State count | Fewer states typically | More states typically |
| Output latency | Immediate (with transition) | One cycle delay |
| Example | UART receiver, TCP FSM | Traffic light controller, Elevator |

A **Mealy machine** outputs are functions of $(S_t, X_t)$ — i.e., the current state and the input event jointly determine the output. A **Moore machine** outputs are functions of $S_t$ only — the current state uniquely determines the output, regardless of input. Hence, for the same vending machine, a Moore representation has the action inside each state (e.g., `Dispensing` state with `entry/ motorOn`), while a Mealy representation attaches the action to the transition arrow (e.g., `Idle -- dispenseCoke / motorOn --> Idle`). **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List the main components of a UML state diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The main components of a UML state diagram are:

1. **Initial State** — A filled black circle marking the entry point.
2. **Final State** — A bull's-eye symbol marking the termination point.
3. **State** — A rounded rectangle representing a condition of the object.
4. **Transition** — A directed arrow connecting two states, labeled with `event[guard]/action`.
5. **Event** — A noteworthy occurrence that triggers transitions.
6. **Guard Condition** — A boolean expression in square brackets that gates transitions.
7. **Action** — Atomic computation performed on a transition or state entry/exit.
8. **Decision / Junction** — A diamond for dynamic branching.
9. **Fork / Join** — Thick bars for concurrent substate regions. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below, Question A and Question B are independent alternatives — your examiner will pick either one.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) — Draw the UML state diagram for an Online Order Processing System. Include states for *Placed*, *Payment Pending*, *Payment Confirmed*, *Shipped*, *Delivered*, *Cancelled*, and *Returned*. Show all events and transitions with guards and actions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — States identified:**
$$\{\text{Placed},\ \text{PaymentPending},\ \text{PaymentConfirmed},\ \text{Shipped},\ \text{Delivered},\ \text{Cancelled},\ \text{Returned}\}$$

**Step 2 — Events identified:**
$$\{\text{checkoutCompleted},\ \text{paymentInitiated},\ \text{paymentSuccess},\ \text{paymentFailure},\ \text{shipmentDispatched},\ \text{deliveryConfirmed},\ \text{cancellationRequested},\ \text{returnRequested},\ \text{returnApproved}\}$$

**Step 3 — Guards:**
- `paymentFailure` → can retry or cancel
- `cancellationRequested` → allowed only before shipment
- `returnRequested` → allowed within 7 days of delivery

**Step 4 — Sample transition rules:**

$$
\text{NextState} = \begin{cases}
\text{PaymentPending} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{Placed} \land \text{Event}=\text{checkoutCompleted} \\
\text{PaymentConfirmed} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{PaymentPending} \land \text{Event}=\text{paymentSuccess} \\
\text{Cancelled} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{PaymentPending} \land \text{Event}=\text{paymentFailure}[retries>3] \\
\text{Shipped} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{PaymentConfirmed} \land \text{Event}=\text{shipmentDispatched} \\
\text{Delivered} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{Shipped} \land \text{Event}=\text{deliveryConfirmed} \\
\text{Returned} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{Delivered} \land \text{Event}=\text{returnRequested}[\text{days}\leq 7] \\
\text{Cancelled} & \text{if CurrentState}=\text{Placed} \land \text{Event}=\text{cancellationRequested} \\
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Diagram rendering (described textually):**

```
[*] --> Placed
Placed --> PaymentPending : checkoutCompleted
Placed --> Cancelled : cancellationRequested
PaymentPending --> PaymentConfirmed : paymentSuccess
PaymentPending --> Cancelled : paymentFailure [retries > 3]
PaymentConfirmed --> Shipped : shipmentDispatched / notifyCustomer
Shipped --> Delivered : deliveryConfirmed
Delivered --> Returned : returnRequested [days <= 7] / refundInitiated
Delivered --> [*]
Returned --> [*]
Cancelled --> [*]
```

**Mark Distribution (KTU Examiner Allocation):**
- Correct identification of states → **2 marks**
- All events listed → **1 mark**
- Correct transitions drawn → **2 marks**
- Guards and actions → **1 mark**
- Initial and final states → **1 mark**
**Total: 7 marks**

---

**Part (b) — Explain the difference between a State Diagram and an Activity Diagram with a real-time example.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **State Diagram** models the lifecycle of a **single object**, emphasizing the states it passes through and the events that cause transitions. It is event-driven and memory-based — the object's response to an event depends on its current state.

An **Activity Diagram** models the **workflow of a process or business activity**, showing the flow of control between actions, decisions, parallel paths, and object flows. It is action-driven and focuses on *what activities occur and in what order*, not on which states exist.

**Real-Time Example — ATM Withdrawal:**

- **State Diagram (ATM Machine object):**
  States: `Idle` → `CardInserted` → `PINEntered` → `AmountSelected` → `Dispensing` → `Idle`
  Events: `cardInserted`, `pinEntered`, `amountSelected`, `cashDispensed`
  Each event triggers a transition only if the ATM is in the right state.

- **Activity Diagram (Withdrawal process):**
  Actions: Insert Card → Enter PIN → Verify PIN → Select Amount → Validate Balance → Dispense Cash → Print Receipt → Eject Card
  Decisions: PIN correct? Balance sufficient?
  Shows procedural flow, not state memory.

**Comparison Table (for the 7 marks):**

| Aspect | State Diagram | Activity Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Object states and transitions | Activities and workflow |
| Driven by | Events | Actions |
| Memory | Yes (object remembers state) | No inherent memory |
| Notation | Rounded rectangles, labeled arrows | Rounded rectangles + diamonds + bars |
| Used for | Reactive / real-time systems | Business processes |
| Concurrency | Fork/Join in substate regions | Fork/Join bars in swim lanes |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of each diagram → **2 marks**
- ATM example (state diagram portion) → **2 marks**
- Activity diagram portion + comparison → **3 marks**
**Total: 7 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Part (a) — Draw a state diagram for a Library Book Borrowing System. Include states: *Available*, *Reserved*, *Borrowed*, *Overdue*, and *Returned*. Show all transitions and events.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**States:** $\{$`Available`, `Reserved`, `Borrowed`, `Overdue`, `Returned`$\}$

**Events:** $\{$`reserveBook`, `cancelReservation`, `borrowBook`, `returnBook`, `dueDatePassed`, `payFine`$\}$

**Transitions:**

1. `Available --reserveBook--> Reserved` (action: `notifyUser`)
2. `Reserved --borrowBook--> Borrowed` (guard: `[userId valid]`, action: `setDueDate`)
3. `Reserved --cancelReservation--> Available`
4. `Borrowed --returnBook--> Available` (action: `calculateFine`)
5. `Borrowed --dueDatePassed--> Overdue`
6. `Overdue --returnBook--> Available` (action: `collectFine`)
7. `Overdue --payFine--> Available`

**Diagram Description:**

```
[*] --> Available
Available --> Reserved : reserveBook / notifyUser
Reserved --> Borrowed : borrowBook [userIdValid] / setDueDate
Reserved --> Available : cancelReservation
Borrowed --> Available : returnBook / calculateFine
Borrowed --> Overdue : dueDatePassed
Overdue --> Available : returnBook / collectFine
Overdue --> Available : payFine
Available --> [*]
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct states → **2 marks**
- All events → **1 mark**
- All transitions → **3 marks**
- Initial/final state + arrows → **1 mark**
**Total: 7 marks**

---

**Part (b) — Explain with a neat diagram the concept of composite states and concurrent substates. Why are they useful in modeling complex systems?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **composite state** is a state that contains one or more **substates**, allowing hierarchical decomposition of complex behaviors. The outer state is the *containing* state, and the inner rectangles are *substates*. The notation is a rounded rectangle enclosing a dashed-line boundary and additional state rectangles.

A **concurrent substate** region is a composite state partitioned by **dashed lines** into multiple regions, each containing its own independent state machine. All regions execute concurrently when the composite state is active.

**Example — Airplane Flight Phase (composite state with concurrent substates):**

The state `InFlight` is composite and contains two concurrent regions:

- **Region 1 (Engine Status):** substates `EnginesOn` ↔ `EnginesIdle`
- **Region 2 (Cabin Status):** substates `CabinPressurized` ↔ `CabinDepressurized`

When the airplane enters `InFlight`, *both* region-1 and region-2 state machines begin executing simultaneously and independently.

**Benefits in Modeling Complex Systems:**

1. **Reduces visual clutter** — a state with 30 inner states can be hidden inside one composite.
2. **Promotes reuse** — the same submachine can be referenced from multiple composite states.
3. **Models true concurrency** — concurrent regions capture independent parallel behaviors.
4. **Encapsulation** — local details of a state are hidden from the parent diagram.
5. **Maintainability** — changes to a substate diagram do not ripple through the parent model.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of composite states → **2 marks**
- Definition of concurrent substates → **2 marks**
- Airplane example → **2 marks**
- Benefits → **1 mark**
**Total: 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — How Marks are Deducted:**
> - **Drawing an Activity Diagram or Flowchart instead of a State Diagram** → Lose 5 marks (full marks).
> - **Forgetting the initial pseudo-state (●)** → Lose 1 mark.
> - **No event labels on transitions** → Lose 2 marks.
> - **Confusing UML syntax (e.g., writing `→` instead of `-->` in mermaid)** → No marks, re-draw.
> - **More than 1 initial state** → Lose 1 mark (UML forbids this).
> - **Using `end` as a node name in any diagram** → Parsing fails, 0 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A State Diagram models the **state-dependent behavior of a single object** over its lifetime, in response to events.
- **Core Components:** States, Transitions, Events, Guards, Actions, Initial/Final pseudo-states.
- **Transition syntax:** `event [guard] / action`
- **State syntax:** `stateName entry/do/exit actions`
- **Two machine types:** **Moore** (output depends on state only) and **Mealy** (output depends on state + input).
- **Composite states** encapsulate complex behavior hierarchically.
- **Concurrent substates** model parallelism inside a single state.
- **Initial state:** exactly **one** per diagram (filled black circle ●).
- **Final state:** 0 or more (bull's-eye ◉).
- **Reachability check** is mandatory — every state must be reachable from the initial state.
- **Use Case:** Real-time systems, protocols, object lifecycles, UI workflows, controllers.
- **KTU-Favourite examples:** TCP connection, ATM, vending machine, login session, library book, elevator, traffic light.
- **State Diagram ≠ Flowchart ≠ Activity Diagram.** Each serves a distinct purpose.
- **Notation placement rule:** Mealy puts actions on transitions; Moore puts actions inside states.
- **Self-transition caveat:** A self-transition re-fires both `exit/` and `entry/` actions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Use Case Diagram** is a behavioural diagram in the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** that graphically depicts the functional requirements of a system from an external observer's perspective. It captures the dynamic behaviour of a system by modelling the interactions between **Actors** (external entities) and the **System** (under design) through **Use Cases** (functional capabilities).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PECST411 syllabus, a Use Case Diagram belongs to the family of UML diagrams used during the **Requirement Analysis and Design Phase** of the **Unified Software Development Process (USDP)**. It is a *high-level* behavioural diagram (not a procedural flowchart) and answers the question: *"What does the system do for each type of user?"* — without specifying *how* it does it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Use Case Diagrams were originally introduced by **Ivar Jacobson** (1992) as part of the **Objectory Method**, later adopted by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** under the UML specification (current standard: UML 2.5.1). KTU expects students to know the term **Jacobson's Use Cases** as a possible 3-mark short-answer question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu Analogy" 🍽️**

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. As a customer:
- **You** = the **Actor**
- The **restaurant** = the **System**
- Things you can do there (*Place Order, Request Bill, Make Reservation*) = the **Use Cases**
- The walls of the restaurant that separate you from the kitchen = the **System Boundary**

A Use Case Diagram is essentially the **"menu of services"** the system offers to each category of user. It does NOT tell you *how* the chef cooks (that's internal design); it only tells you *what* you can request.

**Another Analogy — ATM Machine:**
For an ATM system, a **Customer** actor can perform use cases like *Withdraw Cash, Check Balance, Transfer Funds, Print Statement*. A **Bank Employee** (another actor) might perform *Refill Cash, Audit Transactions*. The diagram captures these without revealing internal processing logic.

### 1.3 Why Use Case Diagrams Matter in KTU Software Engineering

- They form the **starting point** of most KTU-labelled UML practical and theory questions.
- They bridge **user requirements (SRS)** with **system design (OOAD)**.
- They are heavily tested in the 14-mark Part B questions where students must draw a diagram for a given problem statement.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted in Bold:** A well-designed Use Case Diagram typically contains between **5 to 15 use cases** for a single system. More than **20 use cases** in one diagram is considered a **code smell** (poor granularity) and is generally discouraged in the industry.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Core Building Blocks of a Use Case Diagram

Every KTU Use Case Diagram question expects students to correctly identify and draw these four elements:

#### 🔹 2.1.1 Actor
- **Definition:** An external entity (human user, another system, hardware device, or time trigger) that interacts with the system.
- **Notation in UML:** A **stick figure** (for human actors) OR a rectangle with the stereotype `<<actor>>` (for non-human actors).
- **Types:**
  - **Primary Actor:** Initiates the interaction (e.g., Customer placing an order).
  - **Secondary Actor:** Responds to system requests (e.g., Payment Gateway service).
  - **Concrete Actor:** Real, instantiable role.
  - **Abstract Actor:** Generalized role (parent in inheritance hierarchy).
- **Identification Heuristic (KTU Board Favourite):** Look for **nouns outside the system boundary** that have a goal inside the system.

#### 🔹 2.1.2 Use Case
- **Definition:** A functionally cohesive unit of behaviour that provides a measurable result of value to an actor.
- **Notation:** An **ellipse (oval)** containing a verb-noun phrase (e.g., *Withdraw Cash*, *Validate User*).
- **Naming Rules:**
  - Must start with a **strong verb** in present tense.
  - Should describe the **outcome**, not the mechanism.
  - ✅ Correct: *Generate Invoice*   ❌ Wrong: *Invoice Generation Module*
- **Initiator Rule (Jacobson):** Every use case MUST be initiated by at least one actor.

#### 🔹 2.1.3 System Boundary
- **Definition:** A rectangle that visually separates the system under development from the external environment.
- **Notation:** A solid-line rectangle labelled with the **system name** (e.g., *<<ATM System>>*).
- **Placement Rule:** All use cases reside **INSIDE** the boundary; all actors reside **OUTSIDE** the boundary.

#### 🔹 2.1.4 Relationships
The four UML-recognised relationships between use cases/actors are tested extensively in KTU exams:

| Relationship | Notation | Semantic Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|--------------|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Association** | Plain solid line | "An actor participates in a use case" | Customer ↔ Place Order |
| **Include** (`<<include>>`) | Dashed arrow + stereotype | Mandatory reuse of another use case | "Place Order **always requires** Validate User" |
| **Extend** (`<<extend>>`) | Dashed arrow + stereotype | Optional, conditional behaviour injection | "Apply Coupon **may extend** Checkout" |
| **Generalization** | Hollow triangle arrow | Inheritance between actors OR use cases | "Premium Customer *is-a* Customer" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Distinction:** 
> - `<<include>>` → the **base use case cannot exist without** the included one (mandatory, unconditional).
> - `<<extend>>` → the **extension is optional** and occurs only under specific conditions/extension points.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Standard Notation | When to Use | Marks Weightage Hint |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Actor (Human) | Stick figure | End-user role | 1 mark each (Part A) |
| 2 | Actor (System) | Rectangle + `<<actor>>` | External software/hardware | 1 mark each (Part A) |
| 3 | Use Case | Ellipse + verb-noun label | Functional requirement | 1 mark each (Part A) |
| 4 | System Boundary | Rectangle + system name | Scope definition | Often part of 14-mark Q |
| 5 | Association | Solid line | Actor ↔ Use Case link | Mandatory in every diagram |
| 6 | `<<include>>` | Dashed arrow + label | Mandatory reuse, no conditions | Tested in scenario questions |
| 7 | `<<extend>>` | Dashed arrow + label | Optional behaviour, conditional | Tested in scenario questions |
| 8 | Generalization | Hollow triangle | Common "is-a" hierarchy | Tested in inheritance diagrams |
| 9 | Use Case Description | Tabular / narrative doc | Detailed textual spec | Out of scope for diagram marks |

### 2.3 Why & How — The Operational Logic Behind Each Step

**Why do we draw Use Case Diagrams FIRST?**
Because in the **Rational Unified Process (RUP)** and **Agile User Story** methodologies, use cases are the primary vehicle for capturing functional requirements. They provide the foundation upon which sequence diagrams, class diagrams, and activity diagrams are built.

**How to identify use cases from a problem statement (KTU Exam Strategy):**
1. Identify the **system boundary** (the noun representing the software).
2. List all **external actors** by asking "Who uses this?" and "What external systems does it talk to?"
3. For each actor, identify their **goals** → these become use cases.
4. Look for **shared behaviour** → extract to `<<include>>`.
5. Look for **optional/variant behaviour** → extract to `<<extend>>`.
6. Identify **common roles** → use **Generalization**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Use Case Diagrams are used in:
- **Banking:** ATM, Loan Processing Systems
- **Healthcare:** Hospital Management, Telemedicine Apps
- **E-Commerce:** Amazon/Flipkart order workflows
- **Transportation:** IRCTC Reservation, Uber Booking
- **IoT & Embedded:** Smart Home Automation, Industrial SCADA

They serve as a **contract** between stakeholders (customers, developers, testers) and are the basis for deriving **test cases** in software testing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Since Use Case Diagrams are a **conceptual, graphical** topic rather than a heavily mathematical one, this section follows the **Analytical Matrix format** — systematic breakdowns of scenario-based problems with exhaustive step-by-step reasoning, exactly as a KTU examiner expects.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Procedure to Construct a Use Case Diagram

Below is the **canonical 7-step procedure** that fetches full marks in any KTU 14-mark "Draw a Use Case Diagram for..." question.

---

#### **Step 1 — Identify the System & Its Boundary**
*Logic:* The system boundary defines *what is in scope*. Every noun in the problem statement that names a software product (e.g., "Library Management System", "Online Shopping Portal") becomes the system.

*Conversion Logic:* Draw a large rectangle. Label it with the system name (and optionally a stereotype like `<<Library Management System>>`).

---

#### **Step 2 — Identify External Actors**
*Logic:* Scan the problem statement for the word "user", "customer", "admin", "system", "device", or any external role. Each such role becomes an actor.

*Conversion Logic:* Place stick figures *outside* the rectangle, one for each distinct role. If a role is non-human (e.g., "Bank Server"), draw a rectangle with `<<actor>>`.

---

#### **Step 3 — Identify Use Cases (Functional Goals)**
*Logic:* For each actor, ask *"What does this actor want to accomplish with the system?"* The answers become use cases (verb-noun phrases).

*Conversion Logic:* Draw ellipses *inside* the boundary for each goal. Avoid technical/implementation phrases.

---

#### **Step 4 — Establish Associations**
*Logic:* Connect each actor to the use cases they directly interact with.

*Conversion Logic:* Draw solid lines between actor stick figures and use case ellipses. No arrows needed for plain associations.

---

#### **Step 5 — Detect & Apply `<<include>>` Relationships**
*Logic:* Scan use cases. If two or more use cases share a **mandatory sub-task**, extract that sub-task as a separate use case and link it with `<<include>>`.

*Conversion Logic:* Draw a **dashed arrow** from the *base use case* pointing *toward* the *included use case*. Label the arrow with `<<include>>`.

---

#### **Step 6 — Detect & Apply `<<extend>>` Relationships**
*Logic:* If a use case has **optional, conditional behaviour** that triggers only in certain scenarios, extract it as an extension.

*Conversion Logic:* Draw a **dashed arrow** from the *extension use case* pointing *toward* the *base use case* (note the **reversed direction** vs include). Label with `<<extend>>`. Optionally specify the **extension point**.

---

#### **Step 7 — Detect Generalization Hierarchies**
*Logic:* If two actors or two use cases share a common "is-a" relationship, draw a generalization arrow.

*Conversion Logic:* Draw a **solid line with a hollow (open) triangle arrowhead** pointing from the *child* toward the *parent*.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — ATM System (Canonical KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:** *"Design a Use Case Diagram for an ATM System. The system allows customers to withdraw cash, check balance, transfer funds, and view mini-statement. The bank admin can refill cash and view transaction reports. Every transaction must authenticate the user via PIN. If the balance is insufficient, the system displays an error. Apply coupon logic during promotional periods."*

#### Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown:

**Step 1 — System Boundary:** `<<ATM System>>`

**Step 2 — Actors Identified:**
- Customer (primary, human)
- Bank Admin (primary, human)
- Bank Server (secondary, external system — for balance verification)

**Step 3 — Use Cases Identified (verb-noun form):**
- Withdraw Cash
- Check Balance
- Transfer Funds
- View Mini-Statement
- Authenticate User (PIN)
- Refill Cash (admin)
- View Transaction Reports (admin)
- Validate Sufficient Balance
- Apply Promotional Offer

**Step 4 — Associations (actor ↔ use case):**
- Customer ↔ Withdraw Cash, Check Balance, Transfer Funds, View Mini-Statement
- Bank Admin ↔ Refill Cash, View Transaction Reports
- Bank Server ↔ Validate Sufficient Balance

**Step 5 — `<<include>>` Links (mandatory reuse):**
- Withdraw Cash `<<include>>` Authenticate User
- Transfer Funds `<<include>>` Authenticate User
- Withdraw Cash `<<include>>` Validate Sufficient Balance

**Step 6 — `<<extend>>` Links (optional/conditional):**
- Apply Promotional Offer `<<extend>>` Withdraw Cash *(only during promotional periods)*

**Step 7 — Generalizations:**
- Premium Customer `is-a` Customer (specialization if added)

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Library Management System

| Element | Identified | Type |
|---------|------------|------|
| Actors | Student, Librarian, Email Notification System | 3 actors |
| Use Cases | Issue Book, Return Book, Search Catalog, Pay Fine, Register Member, Send Due Reminder | 6 use cases |
| `<<include>>` | Issue Book `<<include>>` Validate Membership | 1 mandatory link |
| `<<extend>>` | Pay Fine `<<extend>>` Return Book *(only if overdue)* | 1 conditional link |
| Generalization | Premium Student `is-a` Student | 1 hierarchy |

### 3.4 Common Mistakes Analysis Matrix (Board Examiner Perspective)

| # | Mistake | Marks Lost | Correct Approach |
|---|---------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Drawing actors **inside** the system boundary | 1–2 | Always **outside** |
| 2 | Naming use cases as nouns ("Book Issue") | 1 | Use **verb-noun** ("Issue Book") |
| 3 | Confusing `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrow directions | 2 | Include: base → included. Extend: extension → base |
| 4 | Using solid lines for `<<include>>`/`<<extend>>` | 1 | Must be **dashed** with stereotype label |
| 5 | Adding implementation details inside use cases | 2 | Keep it **black-box** (functional only) |
| 6 | No system boundary drawn | 1–2 | Mandatory for full marks |
| 7 | Multiple unrelated use cases for one diagram | 1 | Group by system scope |
