## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams use the `flowchart` syntax to represent **Use Case Diagrams** because Mermaid does not have a native UML Use Case template. The conventions follow UML 2.5.1 visual standards: **ellipses** for use cases, **rectangles with actor label** for actors, **dashed arrows with stereotypes** for include/extend, and **solid arrows with hollow heads** for generalization. Stick-figure actors are represented by labelled rectangles due to Mermaid's rendering limits.

### 4.1 Architecture Flow 1 — ATM System Use Case Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% --- External Actors ---
    Customer(["Customer (Actor)"])
    Admin(["Bank Admin (Actor)"])
    BankServer["Bank Server (System Actor)"]

    %% --- System Boundary ---
    subgraph ATM ["<< ATM System >>"]
        direction TB
        UC_Withdraw(["Withdraw Cash"])
        UC_Check(["Check Balance"])
        UC_Transfer(["Transfer Funds"])
        UC_Statement(["View Mini-Statement"])
        UC_Auth(["Authenticate User (PIN)"])
        UC_Refill(["Refill Cash"])
        UC_Reports(["View Transaction Reports"])
        UC_Validate(["Validate Sufficient Balance"])
        UC_Promo(["Apply Promotional Offer"])
    end

    %% --- Associations ---
    Customer --- UC_Withdraw
    Customer --- UC_Check
    Customer --- UC_Transfer
    Customer --- UC_Statement
    Admin --- UC_Refill
    Admin --- UC_Reports
    BankServer --- UC_Validate

    %% --- Include Relationships (mandatory reuse) ---
    UC_Withdraw -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
    UC_Transfer -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
    UC_Withdraw -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Validate

    %% --- Extend Relationship (optional behaviour) ---
    UC_Promo -.->|"<<extend>>"| UC_Withdraw

    %% --- Style Definitions ---
    classDef actor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef usecase fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px
    classDef boundary fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#000,stroke-dasharray:5

    class Customer,Admin,BankServer actor
    class UC_Withdraw,UC_Check,UC_Transfer,UC_Statement,UC_Auth,UC_Refill,UC_Reports,UC_Validate,UC_Promo usecase
    class ATM boundary
```

### 4.2 Architecture Flow 2 — Library Management System Use Case Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% --- Actors ---
    Student(["Student"])
    Librarian(["Librarian"])
    EmailSys["Email Notification System"]

    %% --- System Boundary ---
    subgraph Library ["<< Library Management System >>"]
        direction TB
        UC_Search(["Search Catalog"])
        UC_Issue(["Issue Book"])
        UC_Return(["Return Book"])
        UC_Register(["Register Member"])
        UC_Fine(["Pay Fine"])
        UC_Remind(["Send Due Reminder"])
        UC_Validate(["Validate Membership"])
    end

    %% --- Associations ---
    Student --- UC_Search
    Student --- UC_Issue
    Student --- UC_Return
    Student --- UC_Fine
    Librarian --- UC_Register
    Librarian --- UC_Issue
    EmailSys --- UC_Remind

    %% --- Include (mandatory) ---
    UC_Issue -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Validate

    %% --- Extend (conditional) ---
    UC_Fine -.->|"<<extend>>"| UC_Return
    UC_Remind -.->|"<<extend>>"| UC_Return

    classDef actor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef usecase fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px
    classDef boundary fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#000,stroke-dasharray:5

    class Student,Librarian,EmailSys actor
    class UC_Search,UC_Issue,UC_Return,UC_Register,UC_Fine,UC_Remind,UC_Validate usecase
    class Library boundary
```

### 4.3 Architecture Flow 3 — Conceptual Relationship Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Step 1: Identify System & Boundary] --> B[Step 2: Identify External Actors]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Use Cases Verb-Noun]
    C --> D[Step 4: Draw Associations]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply include for Mandatory Reuse]
    D --> F[Step 6: Apply extend for Optional Behaviour]
    D --> G[Step 7: Apply Generalization for is-a]
    E --> H[Final Use Case Diagram]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    classDef step fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px
    classDef final fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G step
    class H final
```

### 4.4 Visual Legend Table (For Reference During Drawing)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| 🧍 Stick figure | Human actor |
| ▭ Rectangle with `<<actor>>` | Non-human actor |
| ⬭ Ellipse | Use case |
| ▭ Large rectangle | System boundary |
| ─── Solid line | Association |
- - -> Dashed arrow | `<<include>>` or `<<extend>>` |
| ▷ Hollow triangle | Generalization |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**  
*Define the term "Use Case" in UML. List any two notations used in a Use Case Diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Use Case** in UML represents a functionally cohesive unit of behaviour performed by the system that yields a result of measurable value to an actor. It captures a specific goal an actor wants to achieve with the system (3 marks).

Two notations used in a Use Case Diagram are:
1. **Ellipse (Oval)** — represents a use case, labelled with a verb-noun phrase (1 mark).
2. **Stick Figure** — represents an external actor who interacts with the system (1 mark).

*(Other acceptable notations: System boundary rectangle, dashed arrow with `<<include>>`/`<<extend>>`, hollow triangle for generalization.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**  
*Differentiate between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships in UML Use Case Diagrams.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Direction** | Base use case → Included use case (1 mark) | Extension use case → Base use case (1 mark) |
| **Nature** | Mandatory, unconditional reuse (0.5 mark) | Optional, conditional behaviour (0.5 mark) |

*Example:* `Place Order <<include>> Validate User` (always needed).  
`Apply Discount <<extend>> Place Order` (only on promo days).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** (14 Marks)

> *"Design a Use Case Diagram for an Online Shopping System. The system allows customers to register, login, search products, add items to cart, place orders, make payments, and view order history. The admin can add/update/delete products and manage inventory. Every order must authenticate the customer and verify payment. Loyalty points are awarded only to registered members during special promotions."*

**Sub-parts:**  
**(a)** Identify the actors, use cases, and system boundary. List any 5 use cases. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Draw the complete Use Case Diagram with proper notation including `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`, and generalization if any. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Identification of Components (7 marks):**

**Actors (2 marks):**
- **Customer** — primary human actor
- **Admin** — primary human actor
- **Payment Gateway** — secondary system actor

**System Boundary (1 mark):**  
`<<Online Shopping System>>`

**Use Cases — 5 out of the following (2 marks):**
- Register
- Login (Authenticate User)
- Search Products
- Add to Cart
- Place Order
- Make Payment
- View Order History
- Manage Inventory
- Award Loyalty Points

**Relationships Identified (2 marks):**
- **Association:** Customer ↔ Login, Search, Place Order, etc.
- **`<<include>>`:** Place Order `<<include>>` Authenticate User; Place Order `<<include>>` Verify Payment
- **`<<extend>>`:** Award Loyalty Points `<<extend>>` Place Order (only during promotions, for registered members)
- **Generalization:** Registered Customer `is-a` Customer

---

**(b) Use Case Diagram (7 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Customer(["Customer"])
    Admin(["Admin"])
    Gateway["Payment Gateway (System)"]

    subgraph Shop ["<< Online Shopping System >>"]
        direction TB
        UC_Register(["Register"])
        UC_Login(["Login (Authenticate User)"])
        UC_Search(["Search Products"])
        UC_Cart(["Add to Cart"])
        UC_Order(["Place Order"])
        UC_Pay(["Make Payment"])
        UC_History(["View Order History"])
        UC_Inventory(["Manage Inventory"])
        UC_Loyalty(["Award Loyalty Points"])
        UC_VerifyPay(["Verify Payment"])
    end

    Customer --- UC_Register
    Customer --- UC_Login
    Customer --- UC_Search
    Customer --- UC_Cart
    Customer --- UC_Order
    Customer --- UC_History
    Admin --- UC_Inventory
    Gateway --- UC_VerifyPay

    UC_Order -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Login
    UC_Order -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_VerifyPay
    UC_Loyalty -.->|"<<extend>>"| UC_Order

    classDef actor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef usecase fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px
    class Customer,Admin,Gateway actor
    class UC_Register,UC_Login,UC_Search,UC_Cart,UC_Order,UC_Pay,UC_History,UC_Inventory,UC_Loyalty,UC_VerifyPay usecase
```

*(Note: In the actual answer script, students must hand-draw the diagram. Mermaid is shown here for clarity of structure.)*

**Award of Marks for the Diagram (7):**
- Correct system boundary — **1 mark**
- All actors correctly placed outside — **1 mark**
- Minimum 6 use cases correctly drawn as ellipses — **2 marks**
- At least one `<<include>>` drawn correctly (dashed arrow direction) — **1 mark**
- At least one `<<extend>>` drawn correctly (reversed direction) — **1 mark**
- Neatness, labels, and legend — **1 mark**

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`** (14 Marks)

> *"Design a Use Case Diagram for a Hospital Management System. Patients can book appointments, view reports, and pay bills online. Doctors can view patient history, update prescriptions, and request lab tests. Receptionists schedule appointments and manage patient registration. Lab technicians upload test results. Every appointment booking must verify doctor availability. SMS reminders are sent to patients one day before the appointment (optional)."*

**Sub-parts:**  
**(a)** Identify and list all actors with a brief description of each. Also identify the use cases and any relationships (include/extend/generalization). **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Draw the complete Use Case Diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Component Identification (7 marks):**

**Actors with descriptions (3 marks):**
- **Patient** — primary actor; books appointments and views reports.
- **Doctor** — primary actor; views history and updates prescriptions.
- **Receptionist** — primary actor; manages registration and scheduling.
- **Lab Technician** — primary actor; uploads test results.
- **SMS Gateway** — secondary system actor; sends reminders.
- **Insurance System** (optional) — secondary actor; processes bills.

**Use Cases (2 marks):**  
Book Appointment, View Medical Report, Pay Bill, View Patient History, Update Prescription, Request Lab Test, Upload Test Result, Schedule Appointment, Register Patient, Verify Doctor Availability, Send SMS Reminder.

**Relationships (2 marks):**
- `<<include>>`: Book Appointment `<<include>>` Verify Doctor Availability
- `<<extend>>`: Send SMS Reminder `<<extend>>` Book Appointment (one day before)
- Generalization: Senior Doctor `is-a` Doctor

---

**(b) Use Case Diagram (7 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Patient(["Patient"])
    Doctor(["Doctor"])
    Receptionist(["Receptionist"])
    LabTech(["Lab Technician"])
    SMSGateway["SMS Gateway"]
    Insurance["Insurance System"]

    subgraph Hospital ["<< Hospital Management System >>"]
        direction TB
        UC_Book(["Book Appointment"])
        UC_ViewReport(["View Medical Report"])
        UC_PayBill(["Pay Bill"])
        UC_History(["View Patient History"])
        UC_Prescription(["Update Prescription"])
        UC_Lab(["Request Lab Test"])
        UC_Upload(["Upload Test Result"])
        UC_Schedule(["Schedule Appointment"])
        UC_Register(["Register Patient"])
        UC_Verify(["Verify Doctor Availability"])
        UC_SMS(["Send SMS Reminder"])
    end

    Patient --- UC_Book
    Patient --- UC_ViewReport
    Patient --- UC_PayBill
    Doctor --- UC_History
    Doctor --- UC_Prescription
    Doctor --- UC_Lab
    Receptionist --- UC_Schedule
    Receptionist --- UC_Register
    LabTech --- UC_Upload
    SMSGateway --- UC_SMS
    Insurance --- UC_PayBill

    UC_Book -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Verify
    UC_SMS -.->|"<<extend>>"| UC_Book

    classDef actor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef usecase fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px
    class Patient,Doctor,Receptionist,LabTech,SMSGateway,Insurance actor
    class UC_Book,UC_ViewReport,UC_PayBill,UC_History,UC_Prescription,UC_Lab,UC_Upload,UC_Schedule,UC_Register,UC_Verify,UC_SMS usecase
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks 🚨**
> 
> 1. **Direction of `<<include>>` vs `<<extend>>`** (Loss: **2 marks**): Most students draw `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrows in the wrong direction. **Mnemonic trick:** *Include goes IN (base → included). Extend is EXTRA (extension → base).*
> 
> 2. **Drawing Use Cases as Nouns** (Loss: **1 mark**): Always use **Verb + Noun** ("Place Order", NOT "Order Placement").
> 
> 3. **Placing Actors Inside the System Boundary** (Loss: **2 marks**): Actors are ALWAYS **outside** the boundary rectangle.
> 
> 4. **Confusing Generalization with Dependency** (Loss: **1 mark**): Generalization uses a **hollow triangle** arrowhead, not a plain arrow.
> 
> 5. **Missing System Boundary Rectangle** (Loss: **1–2 marks**): Without the boundary, the diagram is incomplete per UML 2.5.1 standards.
> 
> 6. **Not labelling the stereotype `<<include>>` or `<<extend>>`** (Loss: **1 mark**): UML requires the stereotype text label on dashed arrows.
> 
> 7. **Adding internal classes/sequence logic** (Loss: **2–3 marks**): Use Case Diagrams are **functional black-box views** — never include internal design details.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Frequency KTU Board Points — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ Use Case Diagrams belong to **UML behavioural diagrams** (along with Sequence, Activity, State Machine, Communication).
- ✅ Originated by **Ivar Jacobson** (Objectory Method, 1992); standardized by **OMG** under **UML 2.5.1**.
- ✅ **Four components:** Actor, Use Case, System Boundary, Relationships.
- ✅ **Actor notation:** Stick figure (human) OR Rectangle with `<<actor>>` stereotype (non-human/system).
- ✅ **Use case notation:** Ellipse with verb-noun label.
- ✅ **System boundary:** Solid rectangle; use cases inside, actors outside.
- ✅ **`<<include>>`:** Mandatory reuse; arrow from base to included; dashed line.
- ✅ **`<<extend>>`:** Optional/conditional; arrow from extension to base; dashed line with `<<extend>>` stereotype.
- ✅ **Generalization:** Hollow triangle arrowhead; solid line; represents "is-a".
- ✅ **Naming convention:** Verb + Noun for use cases ("Issue Book", not "Book Issue").
- ✅ **Limit:** A single diagram typically has **5–15 use cases**; >20 is a granularity smell.
- ✅ **Use case diagrams do NOT show:** internal logic, sequence, class structure, or state transitions.
- ✅ **Use case diagrams DO show:** actors' goals, system scope, and functional requirements at a glance.
- ✅ Use cases form the basis for **Test Case derivation** in software testing (each use case ≈ 1 happy path + multiple alternative paths).
- ✅ In KTU exams, always draw a **legend**, **label all arrows**, and **maintain neatness** for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "AUSE"**
> **A**ctor — **U**se Case — **S**ystem Boundary — **E**llipse (use case shape)
> These are the 4 pillars of every KTU Use Case Diagram question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequence Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sequence Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sequence Diagram** is a **Unified Modeling Language (UML) interaction diagram** that depicts the **time-ordered flow of messages** exchanged among a set of **objects, actors, and components** participating in a specific use-case scenario of a software system. The vertical dimension represents **time progression (top to bottom)**, while the horizontal dimension represents the **structural collaboration** between participating entities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus):** "A sequence diagram shows object interactions arranged in time sequence. It depicts the objects and classes involved in the scenario and the sequence of messages exchanged between the objects needed to carry out the functionality of the scenario."

The four pillars of a sequence diagram, as per the **OMG UML 2.5 specification**, are:
1. **Participants** (Actors, Objects, Components)
2. **Lifelines** (Vertical dashed lines representing existence over time)
3. **Messages** (Horizontal arrows representing communication)
4. **Combined Fragments** (Control structures like `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`)

> [!NOTE]
> Sequence diagrams are classified under the **Behavioral View** of UML modeling and belong to the category of **Interaction Diagrams**, alongside **Communication Diagrams**, **Timing Diagrams**, and **Interaction Overview Diagrams**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **WhatsApp chat conversation** between you and a pizza delivery agent. The conversation unfolds as a vertical stream:

| Real World | Sequence Diagram Mapping |
|------------|-------------------------|
| Person sending message | **Actor** (initiator) |
| Person receiving message | **Object** (responder) |
| Vertical scrolling timeline | **Lifeline** |
| Each chat bubble | **Message** |
| Reply (with grey tick) | **Return message** |
| "If raining, cancel order" condition | **`alt` combined fragment** |

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner to remember:** A sequence diagram is essentially a **"screenshot of a conversation"** frozen in time, where every message is plotted in the exact order it is sent, and the lifeline of each participant visually drops as time passes.

## 1.3 Purpose in Software Engineering

- **Visualizing Use-Case Realization:** Shows *how* a use case is executed internally.
- **Documenting Object Collaboration:** Reveals which object delegates work to which.
- **Identifying Responsibilities:** Clarifies methods and operations of each class.
- **Detecting Race Conditions:** Highlights message ordering issues.
- **Detailing Service-Oriented Interactions:** Used heavily in API design documentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notation Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy: The Core Components

### A. Participants (Top of the Diagram)

Participants are entities drawn at the **top horizontal axis** of the diagram.

- **Actor:** A stick figure (☻) representing an external user or system.
- **Object:** A rectangle with `ObjectName : ClassName` (e.g., `acc : Account`).
- **Boundary/Entity/Control:** Stereotypes used in Robustness Analysis.
- **Self:** An object calling its own operation.

### B. Lifeline (Vertical Dashed Line)

Each participant has a **lifeline** — a vertical dashed line extending downward representing the **time during which the object exists**. Conventionally, the bottom is **unbounded** unless explicitly destroyed (denoted by a **cross ×**).

### C. Activation Bar (Focus of Control)

A **thin vertical rectangle** overlaid on the lifeline. It indicates the **time period during which an object is actively executing a method**. Nested activation bars represent recursive or chained calls.

### D. Messages (Horizontal Arrows)

The most critical elements. A message is the **unit of communication** between two objects.

## 2.2 Message Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Message Type | Notation | Semantics | KTU Keyword |
|---|--------------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Synchronous** | `→` solid line, filled arrowhead | Sender **blocks** and waits for reply | `call` |
| 2 | **Asynchronous** | `→` solid line, open arrowhead | Sender **does not wait**; continues execution | `send` |
| 3 | **Return** | `⇢` dashed line, open arrowhead | Control returns to caller | `return` |
| 4 | **Self-Message** | `↺` loop arrow on same lifeline | Object invokes its own method | `this.method()` |
| 5 | **Create** | `→` dashed line, open arrowhead | New object instantiation | `<<create>>` |
| 6 | **Destroy** | `×` cross at lifeline end | Object is deallocated | `<<destroy>>` |
| 7 | **Found Message** | From an entry point (filled circle) | Message origin unknown | `<<found>>` |
| 8 | **Lost Message** | Arrow ending at `×` outside diagram | Message destination unknown | `<<lost>>` |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU valuation pitfall: confusing the **solid line with filled triangle** (synchronous) versus the **solid line with open arrowhead** (asynchronous). Examiners allot dedicated marks for drawing the **correct arrowhead geometry**.

## 2.3 Combined Fragments (Control Structures)

Combined fragments allow representation of **loops, branches, parallelism, and optional behaviors** in a sequence diagram. Each fragment is enclosed in a **rectangle with a pentagonal operator tag** in the top-left corner.

| Operator | Keyword | Purpose | Example Use Case |
|----------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| `alt` | Alternatives | **if-else** conditional | Valid vs invalid login |
| `opt` | Option | **Single if** (no else) | Send SMS if balance low |
| `loop` | Loop | **while/for** iteration | Retry login 3 times |
| `par` | Parallel | **Concurrent** processing | Validate + Log simultaneously |
| `strict` | Strict | **Strict sequencing** | A→B→C in that exact order |
| `seq` | Weak Sequencing | Default ordering; fragments internally sequential | Multiple independent blocks |
| `neg` | Negative | **Invalid** scenario | Failed transaction trace |
| `critical` | Critical Region | **Atomic** region (no interleaving) | Money transfer |
| `ignore` / `consider` | Filtering | Show **only relevant** messages | Abstract away noise |

> [!NOTE]
> The **guard condition** is written in square brackets, e.g., `[balance > 0]`, and is placed adjacent to the incoming message of the fragment.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Notation Cheat Sheet (Replaces Formula Sheet)

Since Sequence Diagrams are a **visual modeling language** rather than an analytical/mathematical topic, the conventional "Formula Sheet" is replaced by a **Notation Cheat Sheet** for fast board-exam reference.

| Symbol | Visual | Meaning |
|--------|--------|---------|
| `actor` | Stick figure (☻) | External entity |
| `object` | Rectangle `Name : Class` | System object |
| Lifeline | Vertical dashed line | Object existence over time |
| Activation | Thin vertical rectangle | Method execution period |
| `→` filled | Synchronous message | Blocking call |
| `→` open | Asynchronous message | Non-blocking call |
| `⇢` open dashed | Return message | Return of control |
| `↺` self-loop | Self-message | Internal call |
| `×` | Cross at end | Object destruction |
| Pentagon tag | `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par` | Combined fragment |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Systematic Construction Procedure (Algorithmic Walkthrough)

A KTU examination answer for "draw a sequence diagram" follows a **standard 6-step construction protocol**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step procedure using a **canonical ATM Withdrawal Scenario** as the worked example.

### Step 1: Identify the Use Case and Scope

> Pin down the specific scenario. For our case: *"A customer withdraws cash from an ATM."*

- **Use Case Name:** `Withdraw Cash`
- **Precondition:** Customer has a valid card and PIN.
- **Postcondition:** Cash is dispensed, account is debited.

### Step 2: Identify the Participating Objects

List the entities (Actor + Objects) that participate. Order them horizontally to convey spatial collaboration:

$$
\text{Participants} = \{ \text{User}, \text{ATM}, \text{Account}, \text{Bank} \}
$$

### Step 3: Establish the Lifelines

Draw vertical dashed lifelines from each participant at the top, descending downwards as time progresses. Time $t_0$ is at the top; time $t_n$ at the bottom.

### Step 4: Activate in Order

For each object, mark an **activation bar** at the moment it begins executing a method. The bar's vertical length equals the method's execution duration.

### Step 5: Plot Messages in Chronological Order

Starting from $t_0$, plot each message as a horizontal arrow from sender to receiver, top to bottom:

| Order ($t_i$) | Sender | Receiver | Message | Type |
|---------------|--------|----------|---------|------|
| $t_1$ | User | ATM | `insertCard(cardNo)` | Sync |
| $t_2$ | ATM | User | `requestPIN()` | Sync |
| $t_3$ | User | ATM | `enterPIN(pin)` | Sync |
| $t_4$ | ATM | Account | `validatePIN(pin)` | Sync |
| $t_5$ | Account | Bank | `verify(cardNo, pin)` | Sync |
| $t_6$ | Bank | Account | `pinStatus` | Return |
| $t_7$ | ATM | User | `selectTransaction()` | Sync |
| $t_8$ | User | ATM | `withdraw(amount)` | Sync |
| $t_9$ | ATM | Account | `debit(amount)` | Sync |
| $t_{10}$ | Account | Bank | `updateLedger()` | Sync |
| $t_{11}$ | ATM | ATM | `dispenseCash()` | Self-Message |
| $t_{12}$ | ATM | User | `ejectCard()` | Sync |
| $t_{13}$ | ATM | User | `cash` | Return |

### Step 6: Embed Combined Fragments

For conditional flows (e.g., insufficient balance), wrap the relevant messages in an `alt` fragment:

- **Guard 1:** `[balance \geq amount]` — successful path.
- **Guard 2:** `[balance < amount]` — failure path (`neg`-like branch).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ordering property must hold: for any two messages $m_i$ and $m_j$ drawn on the same lifeline, if $i < j$ then $m_i$ appears **strictly above** $m_j$. This is the **Happens-Before** constraint in UML.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Component-to-Question Mapping

This matrix is a KTU-exam-prep aid. For every component, we map the **typical question phrasing** a board examiner uses.

| Component | Typical KTU Question Phrasing | Marks Weightage |
|-----------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| Actor & Objects | "List the participants in ..." | 2 marks |
| Lifeline | "Explain lifeline with diagram" | 2 marks |
| Activation Bar | "Define focus of control / activation" | 2 marks |
| Message Types | "Differentiate sync vs async messages" | 3-4 marks |
| Combined Fragments | "Explain `alt`/`opt`/`loop` with example" | 5-7 marks |
| Full Diagram | "Draw sequence diagram for X scenario" | 7-10 marks |
| Guard Conditions | "What is a guard? Show with example" | 2 marks |
| Self-Message | "Show how an object calls its own method" | 2 marks |

## 3.3 Mathematical Notation: Happens-Before Relation

Formally, the **partial order** of message exchanges in a sequence diagram can be expressed as:

$$
\forall \, m_i, m_j \in M : \text{if } t(m_i) < t(m_j) \text{ then } m_i \rightarrow m_j
$$

Where:
- $M$ = set of all messages in the diagram.
- $t(m_i)$ = timestamp of message $m_i$ (implicit, read top-to-bottom).
- $\rightarrow$ = "happens-before" (transitive, anti-symmetric).

The **transitivity** property is satisfied if:

$$
(m_i \rightarrow m_j) \land (m_j \rightarrow m_k) \Rightarrow (m_i \rightarrow m_k)
$$

And the **liveness property** ensures no message is lost (every send has a corresponding receive on the same lifeline or another):

$$
\forall \, m_i^{\text{send}} \; \exists \, m_i^{\text{receive}} : m_i^{\text{send}} \rightarrow m_i^{\text{receive}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — ATM Withdrawal Use Case

Below is a **fully compilable Mermaid sequence diagram** illustrating the canonical ATM withdrawal scenario with an `alt` combined fragment for the insufficient balance case. The node ID alpha rule is respected (all participant IDs are alphanumeric and pre-fixed with letters).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor U as Customer
    participant A as ATM
    participant AC as Account
    participant B as BankServer

    U->>A: insertCard(cardNumber)
    activate A
    A->>U: requestPIN()
    U->>A: enterPIN(pin)
    A->>AC: validatePIN(pin)
    activate AC
    AC->>B: verifyCredentials(cardNo, pin)
    B-->>AC: pinStatus (valid)
    deactivate AC
    A->>U: selectTransaction()

    alt balance is sufficient
        U->>A: withdraw(amount)
        A->>AC: debit(amount)
        activate AC
        AC->>B: updateLedger(amount)
        B-->>AC: ack
        deactivate AC
        A->>A: dispenseCash()
        A-->>U: cash and receipt
    else balance is insufficient
        A-->>U: displayInsufficientBalance
        A-->>U: ejectCard
    end

    A->>U: ejectCard
    deactivate A
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **abstract functional architecture** of a sequence diagram — showing how the visual notation layers are constructed from source.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Use Case Specification] --> B1[Identify Participants]
    B1 --> C1[Draw Lifelines]
    C1 --> D1[Plot Messages Top to Bottom]
    D1 --> E1[Add Activation Bars]
    E1 --> F1{Need Control Flow?}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[Add Combined Fragments]
    F1 -- No --> H1[Validate Happens-Before]
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Final Sequence Diagram]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style I1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style F1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

## 4.3 Component-to-Notation Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Actor: stick figure] --> P1[Object: Rectangle Name Class]
    P1 --> P2[Lifeline: Dashed vertical]
    P2 --> P3[Activation: Thin rectangle]
    P3 --> P4[Sync Message: Solid arrow filled]
    P3 --> P5[Async Message: Solid arrow open]
    P3 --> P6[Return: Dashed arrow open]
    P3 --> P7[Self Message: Loop arrow]
    P2 --> P8[Combined Fragment: Pentagon tag]

    style A fill:#ffe6e6
    style P4 fill:#cce5ff
    style P5 fill:#cce5ff
    style P6 fill:#cce5ff
    style P7 fill:#fff4cc
    style P8 fill:#e6ccff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define a sequence diagram. List any four components of a sequence diagram.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sequence diagram** is a UML behavioral diagram that depicts the **time-ordered interaction** between objects participating in a use-case scenario, emphasizing the **sequence of messages** exchanged from top to bottom along each object's lifeline.

The four key components are:
1. **Actor / Object** — participants placed horizontally at the top.
2. **Lifeline** — vertical dashed line representing the existence of an object over time.
3. **Activation Bar (Focus of Control)** — thin rectangle showing active method execution.
4. **Messages** — horizontal arrows indicating communication (sync, async, return, self).

> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing 4 components with 0.5 each.)*

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous messages with a neat diagram.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Synchronous Message | Asynchronous Message |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Arrow | Solid line, **filled triangle** arrowhead | Solid line, **open (stick)** arrowhead |
| Sender behavior | **Blocks** until reply is received | **Does not block**; continues execution |
| Use case | Procedure call, RPC | Fire-and-forget, event notification |
| Return | Explicit (dashed) | Implicit (no immediate return) |
| Stack | New frame pushed on sender's stack | No new frame; event posted to queue |

**Neat sketch:** Draw a horizontal solid line with a **filled triangle** labeled *"sync"*; beside it draw a solid line with an **open V** labeled *"async"*.

> *(Award 1 mark for table, 1 mark for arrowhead distinction, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions have an internal choice. The model below presents **Choice A** and **Choice B** as independent alternatives, each carrying sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Draw a sequence diagram for the **"Online Book Ordering System"** where a Customer searches for a book, adds it to the cart, makes payment, and receives order confirmation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Participants identified:**
- `Customer` (actor)
- `UI` (Web Interface)
- `OrderController` (controller)
- `Inventory` (entity)
- `PaymentGateway` (external system)
- `Order` (entity)

**Sequence of messages (top-to-bottom):**

$$
M = \{ m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_{12} \}
$$

| # | From | To | Message |
|---|------|----|---------|
| 1 | Customer | UI | `searchBook(title)` |
| 2 | UI | Inventory | `query(title)` |
| 3 | Inventory | UI | `bookList` |
| 4 | Customer | UI | `addToCart(bookId)` |
| 5 | UI | OrderController | `createOrder(items)` |
| 6 | OrderController | Order | `<<create>>` |
| 7 | OrderController | UI | `redirectToPayment` |
| 8 | Customer | UI | `makePayment(cardInfo)` |
| 9 | UI | PaymentGateway | `processPayment(amount)` |
| 10 | PaymentGateway | UI | `paymentStatus = success` |
| 11 | UI | OrderController | `confirmOrder(orderId)` |
| 12 | OrderController | Customer | `orderConfirmation` |

**Sketch (textual):**

```
Customer -> UI : searchBook
UI -> Inventory : query
Inventory --> UI : bookList
Customer -> UI : addToCart
UI -> OrderController : createOrder
OrderController -> Order : <<create>>
Customer -> UI : makePayment
UI -> PaymentGateway : processPayment
PaymentGateway --> UI : success
UI -> OrderController : confirmOrder
OrderController --> Customer : confirmation
```

> *(Award 3 marks for participant identification, 2 marks for message ordering, 2 marks for correct arrowhead notation.)*

**(b)** Explain the following combined fragments with examples: `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **`alt` (Alternatives):** Models **if-else** branching. Example: If `paymentStatus == SUCCESS`, deliver order; **else** show error.
2. **`opt` (Option):** Models a **single if without else**. Example: *Optionally* send an email confirmation after order placement.
3. **`loop` (Loop):** Models **iteration**. Example: *Loop* retry payment up to 3 times if gateway fails.
4. **`par` (Parallel):** Models **concurrent** execution. Example: *Parallel* send SMS notification and email notification after order placement.

**Syntax template:**

```
alt [guard1]
    Sender -> Receiver : message1
else [guard2]
    Sender -> Receiver : message2
end
```

> *(Award 1.5 marks per fragment definition + 0.25 marks for example = 7 marks total.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the various components of a UML sequence diagram with a neat labeled diagram. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The major components are:

1. **Actor (☻):** External user/system triggering the flow.
2. **Object (Rectangle):** System entity, notation `objectName : ClassName`.
3. **Lifeline (Dashed vertical line):** Object's existence over time.
4. **Activation Bar (Thin rectangle on lifeline):** Focus of control — duration of method execution.
5. **Synchronous Message (→ with filled triangle):** Blocking call.
6. **Asynchronous Message (→ with open V):** Non-blocking call.
7. **Return Message (- -⇢):** Dashed arrow returning control.
8. **Self-Message (↺):** Object calls its own method.
9. **Combined Fragment (Rectangle with pentagon tag):** Control structures.
10. **Guard Condition `[...]`:** Boolean condition on a fragment.
11. **Object Destruction (×):** End of an object's lifeline.

**Labeled diagram (ASCII representation):**

```
   ┌───┐     ┌────────┐
   │ U │     │ ATM    │
   └─┬─┘     └───┬────┘
     │           │
     │ ────────> │  insertCard   (synchronous)
     │           │
     │           ║  (activation)
     │ <──────── │  requestPIN   (return)
     │           ║
     │ ────────> │  enterPIN
     │           ║
     │           ╳  (destroyed at end)
```

> *(Award 5 marks for the diagram with labels, 2 marks for textual explanation.)*

**(b)** Construct a sequence diagram for the **"Student Course Registration System"** where a student logs in, browses courses, registers, and receives a confirmation. Show the use of `alt` for "seat available vs not available". (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Participants:** `Student`, `LoginUI`, `CourseCatalog`, `RegistrationSystem`, `Database`.

**Step 1 — Authentication phase:**
- `Student → LoginUI : enterCredentials(username, password)`
- `LoginUI → Database : validate(user, pass)`
- `Database --> LoginUI : authToken`

**Step 2 — Browse phase:**
- `Student → CourseCatalog : viewCourses(semester)`
- `CourseCatalog --> Student : courseList`

**Step 3 — Registration with `alt` fragment:**

```
alt [seat available]
    Student -> RegistrationSystem : register(courseId)
    RegistrationSystem -> Database : enroll(studentId, courseId)
    Database --> RegistrationSystem : success
    RegistrationSystem --> Student : confirmation
else [seat not available]
    RegistrationSystem --> Student : waitlistMessage
end
```

> *(Award 2 marks for participants, 3 marks for `alt` fragment construction, 2 marks for correct message types and returns.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Based on KTU Valuation Key Trends):**
>
> 1. **Wrong Arrowheads — 1 to 2 marks lost.** Examiners specifically check the **filled vs open triangle** distinction. Drawing all arrows as plain lines is a **major** error.
> 2. **Missing Time Order — 1 mark lost.** A sequence diagram that is not strictly top-to-bottom is invalid. Always read like a timeline.
> 3. **Confusing Class Diagram with Sequence Diagram — 3 marks lost.** If the student draws static relationships (inheritance, association) instead of time-ordered messages, full marks are withheld.
> 4. **Omitting the `alt` fragment label — 1 mark lost.** Every combined fragment's pentagon tag **must** be labeled (`alt`, `opt`, `loop`, etc.). A blank tag is incorrect.
> 5. **No return messages — 0.5 to 1 mark lost.** Although not mandatory, KTU examiners prefer explicit dashed return arrows for clarity.
> 6. **Activation bar missing on receiver — 0.5 mark lost.** Every synchronous message should result in an activation bar on the receiver's lifeline.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- A **Sequence Diagram** = **Time-ordered interaction** between objects.
- **Time flows top to bottom**, *not* left to right (common confusion).
- The **horizontal axis** = participants; the **vertical axis** = time.
- **Lifeline = dashed vertical line.** **Activation = thin rectangle.**
- **Synchronous message = filled triangle arrowhead.** **Asynchronous = open V arrowhead.** **Return = dashed open arrow.**
- A **self-message** is drawn as a **U-shaped loop** on the same lifeline.
- **Combined fragments** are enclosed in rectangles with a **pentagon operator tag** in the top-left.
- The `alt` fragment models **if-else**, `opt` models a **single if**, `loop` models **iteration**, and `par` models **concurrency**.
- A **guard condition** is written in **square brackets**, e.g., `[balance > 0]`.
- The **happens-before relation** is implicitly established by vertical ordering of messages.
- Sequence diagrams belong to the **Behavioral / Interaction** category of UML 2.5 diagrams.
- **Object destruction** is marked with a **cross (×)** at the end of the lifeline.
- Sequence diagrams are best suited for **modeling a single scenario** of a use case; use multiple diagrams for multiple scenarios.
- **Do NOT confuse** a sequence diagram with a **collaboration/communication diagram** — the former emphasizes **time**, the latter emphasizes **object links**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies: Voice Mail System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Voice Mail System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voice Mail System (VMS):** A Voice Mail System is a **computer-based, telephone-integrated messaging application** that allows a caller to leave a recorded audio message for a called party when the called party is unavailable, and allows the called party to retrieve, manage, store, forward, or delete those messages at a later time via a telephone interface (DTMF/touch-tone or speech-driven).

In the context of **KTU Software Engineering (PECST411)**, the *Voice Mail System* is presented as a **pedagogical case study** (popularized by Roger S. Pressman in *Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach*) to demonstrate **object-oriented analysis and design (OOAD)** techniques, specifically:

- Identification of **actors** and **use cases**
- Construction of a **System Context Diagram (SCD)**
- Development of a **Use-Case Diagram**
- Creation of an **Analysis Class Diagram**
- Elaboration of **Use-Case Descriptions** using narrative templates

The VMS is intentionally chosen because it is **small enough to be understood in one sitting**, yet **rich enough** to exhibit concurrency, real-time constraints, multiple actors, exception paths, and data persistence — all the hallmarks of a real-world software product.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Digital Receptionist Notebook"**
> Imagine you call a colleague, Mr. X, but he is in a meeting. Instead of the line going *beep… beep… dead*, imagine a polite, automated **receptionist (the VMS)** picks up the phone. The receptionist:
> 1. Plays a personalized greeting in Mr. X's voice: *"Hi, this is X, please leave a message."*
> 2. Records whatever you say for up to **2 minutes**.
> 3. Files that recording inside Mr. X's private **locker (mailbox)**.
> 4. When Mr. X returns, the receptionist says *"You have 3 new messages"* the moment he dials in.
> 5. Mr. X can now **listen, replay, save, delete, or forward** each message using only the phone keypad.

That automated receptionist, the locker, the greeting, the notification, and the controls are exactly what the *Voice Mail System* software implements. From a Software Engineering perspective, we are not building the receptionist — we are building the **software brain** that runs her.

## 1.3 Why This Case Study is in Your Syllabus

| Reason | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| **Demonstrates Requirement Elicitation** | Forces students to think about *what* the system must do before *how* it does it. |
| **Multi-actor Scenario** | At least 3 actors (Caller, Subscriber, Administrator) — perfect for use-case practice. |
| **Real-time Constraints** | Recording, playback, and DTMF decoding must happen within human-perceptible latency. |
| **Persistent Storage** | Messages, greetings, and passwords must be stored reliably. |
| **Exception Handling** | Wrong password, full mailbox, phone line drop, no message to play. |
| **Industry Relevance** | Every enterprise PBX, IVR, and call-center system descends from this architecture. |

## 1.4 Core Vocabulary (KTU Board-Examiner Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Subscriber (Mailbox Owner):** A registered user who *owns* a mailbox and can retrieve messages.
> - **Caller:** Any external party who dials into the system and may leave a message.
> - **Administrator:** A privileged user who configures the system (creates mailboxes, sets parameters).
> - **Mailbox:** A logical storage container unique to a subscriber, holding greetings, messages, and password.
> - **DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency):** The "touch-tones" generated by a telephone keypad; the primary input modality.
> - **Greeting:** A pre-recorded audio message played to a caller when a mailbox is reached.
> - **Distribution List:** A named group of subscribers to whom a message can be sent in one operation.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Requirements of the VMS

A complete Pressman-style VMS must support the following functional capabilities. These are the **anchor points** for any 14-mark KTU question.

### 2.1.1 Mailbox Management
- Each subscriber has a **unique numeric mailbox number** (e.g., 4–10 digits).
- The administrator can **create, suspend, or delete** a mailbox.
- Each mailbox stores: a **password**, a **greeting**, a **recorded name**, and a list of **messages**.

### 2.1.2 Call Answering Workflow
1. An incoming call is routed to the VMS.
2. The system plays a **system-level greeting** (default) or the called subscriber's **personal greeting** if configured.
3. The system prompts the caller (or the subscriber-on-login) for input via DTMF.
4. If the caller is a **subscriber logging in**, the system requests a **password** and authenticates.
5. If the caller is a **guest**, the system offers to **leave a message**.

### 2.1.3 Message Operations
Once authenticated, a subscriber can perform:

| Operation | Description | Typical DTMF Key |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Listen** | Play the current message | `1` |
| **Save** | Retain message in mailbox | `2` |
| **Delete** | Remove message permanently | `3` |
| **Forward** | Send to another subscriber's mailbox | `4` |
| **Replay** | Replay the current message from the start | `5` |
| **Skip** | Move to the next message | `6` |

### 2.1.4 Notification
- A "**message waiting**" indicator (lamp on the phone, or stutter dial-tone) is activated when a new message arrives.
- Many implementations also support **out-dial notification** — the VMS calls a pre-set number to alert the subscriber.

## 2.2 Non-Functional Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for distinguishing **functional** from **non-functional** requirements. Memorize this table.

| Non-Functional Attribute | Specification for VMS |
| --- | --- |
| **Performance / Latency** | DTMF response time $\leq 200$ ms; greeting playback begins within $1$ s of call answer. |
| **Throughput** | Support at least **N concurrent calls** (e.g., 1000 simultaneous sessions). |
| **Availability** | **99.99 %** uptime (≈ 52 min/year downtime). |
| **Storage Capacity** | Each mailbox must hold a configurable number of messages (default **20**) and a total duration (e.g., **30 minutes** of audio). |
| **Security** | Password-based authentication; passwords **never echoed back** in DTMF confirmation prompts. |
| **Reliability** | No message loss on power failure — use journaling or UPS-backed disk writes. |
| **Maintainability** | Modular OO design; new features (e.g., speech recognition) added without modifying existing modules (Open/Closed Principle). |

## 2.3 The Three Actors of the VMS

> [!NOTE]
> An **actor** in UML is a role played by an *external entity* (human or another system) that interacts with the system under consideration.

1. **Subscriber** — the mailbox owner. Authenticates, retrieves, manages messages, sets greetings, defines distribution lists.
2. **Caller (Guest / Non-Subscriber)** — leaves a message for a subscriber, may also be a subscriber of *another* mailbox dialing in.
3. **Administrator** — configures the VMS: creates/suspends mailboxes, sets system-wide parameters (max message length, retention policy, etc.).

A *fourth* actor is sometimes identified: the **Telephone Network (PSTN) / PBX**, treated as a system-level actor that delivers dialed digits and audio streams.

## 2.4 The Five Core Use Cases (Pressman Canonical List)

The KTU board paper expects the student to enumerate use cases. The canonical Pressman list is:

1. **Set Up Subscriber** (Administrator)
2. **Access Subscriber Functions** (Subscriber logs in)
3. **Record Greeting** (Subscriber)
4. **Retrieve Messages** (Subscriber)
5. **Leave Message** (Caller)

Some authors extend this with:
6. **Forward Message**
7. **Create / Manage Distribution List**
8. **Send Message to Distribution List**
9. **Delete Message** / **Save Message**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield "Cheat Sheet" — Voice Mail System

| Concept | Key Term / Formula / Pattern | KTU Significance |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **System Boundary** | Rectangular box enclosing use cases; actors lie *outside* the boundary. | Drawing rule in use-case diagram. |
| **Use-Case Naming** | Verb + Object (e.g., *Retrieve Messages*, *Leave Message*). | 1 mark question every year. |
| **Association** | Solid line between actor and use case (no arrowhead unless directional). | UML notation. |
| **«include»** | Dashed arrow from base to required use case; required use case is *always* invoked. | A14: 14-mark questions on use-case relationships. |
| **«extend»** | Dashed arrow from extending to base use case; extension triggers only under a condition. | A common point of confusion — see §3.3. |
| **Authentication** | Password check performed *once* and reused — therefore modeled as an «include» from multiple base use cases. | Reduces duplication. |
| **Concurrency** | Multiple callers can use the system simultaneously — implies multi-threaded server design. | Real-time constraint. |
| **Persistence** | Mailbox state (greetings, messages, password) must survive reboot → RDBMS or file store. | Software architecture question. |
| **Latency budget** | $\text{Total response time} = \text{DTMF decode} + \text{DB lookup} + \text{Audio I/O}$ | Sometimes asked. |
| **Mailbox full** | Exception flow when `message_count == max_messages`. | Exception handling. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The VMS pattern underpins:
- **PBX systems** in offices (Avaya, Cisco Unified Communications Manager).
- **Cloud IVR** platforms (Amazon Connect, Genesys).
- **Unified Communications** (Microsoft Teams voicemail, Zoom Phone).
- **Call-centre ACD (Automatic Call Distribution)** systems.

Understanding the VMS case study is therefore not academic — it is the **conceptual skeleton** of every modern telephony product.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Models

In this section we walk through the *engineering* of the VMS case study in the order a developer/analyst would. We will build, step by step, the **System Context Diagram**, the **Use-Case Diagram**, the **Use-Case Descriptions** (narrative templates), and the **Analysis Class Diagram**. Every transition is justified.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Establish the System Context (Level-0 DFD)

The *System Context Diagram* shows the VMS as a single process and its external entities.

### 3.1.1 Analytical Reasoning

A system context diagram has exactly **one process** (the system itself), surrounded by **external entities** (actors and adjacent systems) and **data flows**. The purpose is to fix the *boundary* of what is in scope and what is not.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 3.1.2.1** — Identify all external entities interacting with the VMS:
- Caller (guest)
- Subscriber (mailbox owner)
- Administrator
- Telephone Network (PSTN / PBX)

**Step 3.1.2.2** — Identify the data flows (inputs and outputs):

| Source Entity | Data Flow | Destination | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Caller | DTMF digits, voice audio | VMS | Caller interacts with the system. |
| VMS | Voice prompts, greeting audio | Caller | System plays prompts. |
| Subscriber | DTMF digits, voice | VMS | Subscriber logs in and manages mailbox. |
| VMS | Voice menu, stored messages | Subscriber | System delivers messages and menu. |
| Administrator | Configuration commands | VMS | Sets up mailboxes. |
| VMS | Status / logs | Administrator | Reports configuration results. |
| PSTN | Incoming call signaling, audio | VMS | Carries the physical call. |
| VMS | Message-waiting indicator | PSTN | Toggles lamp / dial-tone. |

**Step 3.1.2.3** — Verify the closure property: every arrow leaving the system must be matched by an arrow entering the system for the same conceptual data item. This guarantees no information is "lost".

## 3.2 Step 2 — Identify Actors and Use Cases (the OOAD Heart)

### 3.2.1 Algorithm to Extract Actors

1. List all external entities from the system context diagram.
2. For each, ask: *"Does this entity initiate an action, or merely respond?"*
3. If the entity *initiates* an action, it is an **actor**.
4. If the entity is *purely passive* (e.g., a database), it is **not** an actor.

Applying the algorithm:
- Caller → actor (initiates *Leave Message*).
- Subscriber → actor (initiates *Retrieve Messages*, *Record Greeting*, *Forward Message*, etc.).
- Administrator → actor (initiates *Set Up Subscriber*).
- Telephone Network → it is a *system-level* actor; KTU question papers often ask whether this counts. **Answer: Yes**, it can be modeled as an actor.

### 3.2.2 Algorithm to Extract Use Cases

For each actor, enumerate the *goals* they want the system to achieve. Each goal is a candidate use case. Use cases are **goal-oriented**, not action-oriented; they represent a *complete* interaction with a measurable result.

| Actor | Goal | Use Case Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Administrator | Configure a new subscriber's mailbox | *Set Up Subscriber* |
| Subscriber | Gain access to my mailbox | *Access Subscriber Functions* |
| Subscriber | Record a personal greeting | *Record Greeting* |
| Subscriber | Listen to messages left for me | *Retrieve Messages* |
| Subscriber | Send a stored message to another subscriber | *Forward Message* |
| Subscriber | Broadcast a message to many subscribers | *Send to Distribution List* |
| Subscriber | Manage named groups of recipients | *Create / Modify Distribution List* |
| Caller | Leave a voice message | *Leave Message* |

## 3.3 Step 3 — Apply «include» and «extend» Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important sub-topic** for KTU 14-mark questions on case studies. The examiner will deliberately interleave include and extend to test understanding.

### 3.3.1 «include» — Mandatory Re-Use

The use case *Access Subscriber Functions* is invoked by almost every subscriber-initiated use case (Retrieve, Forward, Record Greeting, etc.). Rather than duplicating the authentication flow, we model it once as **Authenticate Subscriber** and «include» it from the others.

> **Rule of «include»:** The base use case **always** calls the included use case. The included use case **cannot** stand alone — it exists only to be invoked.

### 3.3.2 «extend» — Conditional Re-Use

Consider the use case *Forward Message*. Normally the subscriber forwards a message, but if the recipient's mailbox is *full*, the system might **extend** the base behavior with a "Notify Sender of Mailbox-Full" sub-flow. This is modeled as:

> **Rule of «extend»:** The extension runs **only** if a *trigger condition* is met. The base use case is *meaningful on its own* without the extension.

### 3.3.3 Quick Decision Table for Students

| Question to ask | If **Yes** | If **No** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Is the sub-flow **always** executed by the base? | «include» | «extend» (or pure refactoring) |
| Does the sub-flow make sense on its own? | «extend» candidate | «include» |
| Are you merely **factoring out** duplication? | «include» | — |

## 3.4 Step 4 — Write a Use-Case Description (Narrative Template)

A formal KTU answer must include a *narrative* description. The standard template is:

> **Use-Case Name:** *Retrieve Messages*
> **Actor:** Subscriber
> **Preconditions:**
> 1. The subscriber's mailbox exists and is active.
> 2. The system is in the main menu state.
> **Postconditions:**
> 1. The selected message is played to the subscriber.
> 2. The message's *new* flag is cleared.
> **Main Flow:**
> 1. The subscriber selects "Retrieve Messages" from the main menu.
> 2. The system requests the password («include» Authenticate Subscriber).
> 3. The subscriber enters the password.
> 4. The system validates the password.
> 5. The system retrieves all *new* messages sorted by arrival time.
> 6. The system announces the number of new messages and the total duration.
> 7. The subscriber selects the first message (or presses skip).
> 8. The system plays the message.
> 9. The subscriber chooses Save, Delete, or Forward.
> **Alternate / Exception Flows:**
> - **3a. Wrong password:** system plays "Invalid password", returns to step 2. After 3 failures, system disconnects.
> - **5a. No new messages:** system announces "You have no new messages" and returns to main menu.
> - **9a. Forward selected:** «extend» *Forward Message* sub-flow.
> - **9b. Mailbox full during save:** system announces "Mailbox full" and offers delete or skip.

> [!NOTE]
> The narrative template is **text**, not a diagram. KTU boards award 1–2 marks for the pre/post-condition discipline alone.

## 3.5 Step 5 — Derive the Analysis Class Diagram

The final modeling step converts use cases into **analysis classes**. A standard OOAD technique is to:

1. Underline all **nouns** in the use-case narrative.
2. Filter nouns to find candidate classes.
3. Underline all **verbs** to find candidate operations.

### 3.5.1 Noun Extraction (Manual Walkthrough)

From the narrative of *Retrieve Messages*, the nouns are:

> *subscriber, mailbox, system, password, message, menu, arrival time, number, duration, sender, greeting, distribution list, administrator*

After eliminating **redundant** nouns (e.g., *menu* is part of the *System*), **vague** nouns (e.g., *system*), and **attributes** (e.g., *arrival time* is an attribute of *Message*), we arrive at the candidate classes:

- **Subscriber**
- **Mailbox**
- **Message**
- **DistributionList**
- **Greeting**
- **Administrator**

### 3.5.2 Verb Extraction — Methods on Classes

| Verb | Class | Method |
| --- | --- | --- |
| authenticate | Mailbox | `authenticate(pwd)` |
| retrieve | Mailbox | `getNewMessages()` |
| play | Message | `play()` |
| save | Message | `save()` |
| delete | Message | `delete()` |
| forward | Message | `forward(Mailbox target)` |
| record | Greeting | `record(audio)` |

### 3.5.3 Class Diagram (Mermaid Form)

See §4.3 for the rendered diagram.

## 3.6 Step 6 — Identify Collaborations and Stereotypes

Analysis classes fall into three stereotype buckets (Jacobson):

| Stereotype | Role in VMS | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Boundary** | Interfaces with actors | *TelephoneInterface*, *AdminConsole* |
| **Control** | Coordinates use-case flow | *CallController*, *MessageController* |
| **Entity** | Stores persistent data | *Mailbox*, *Message*, *Greeting* |

Mapping the VMS to MVC-like triad:

- **TelephoneInterface** (boundary) ↔ **Subscriber / Caller**
- **CallController** (control) ↔ orchestrates "Leave Message" and "Retrieve Messages"
- **Mailbox / Message / Greeting** (entity) ↔ persistent storage

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

All diagrams are rendered in **Mermaid** and follow the syntactic safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown formatting inside node labels, and no reserved keyword collisions.

## 4.1 System Context Diagram (Level-0 DFD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    callerNode[Caller]
    subscriberNode[Subscriber]
    adminNode[Administrator]
    pstnNode[PSTN Telephone Network]
    vmsNode{{Voice Mail System VMS}}

    callerNode -- DTMF digits and voice --> vmsNode
    vmsNode -- Voice prompts and greetings --> callerNode
    subscriberNode -- DTMF and voice --> vmsNode
    vmsNode -- Audio messages and menu --> subscriberNode
    adminNode -- Configuration commands --> vmsNode
    vmsNode -- Logs and status --> adminNode
    pstnNode -- Call signaling and audio --> vmsNode
    vmsNode -- Message waiting indicator --> pstnNode
```

## 4.2 Use-Case Diagram (Pressman Canonical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subscriberActor[Subscriber]
    callerActor[Caller]
    adminActor[Administrator]

    subgraph VMS_Boundary [VMS System Boundary]
        ucSetUp(Set Up Subscriber)
        ucAccess(Access Subscriber Functions)
        ucRecord(Record Greeting)
        ucRetrieve(Retrieve Messages)
        ucForward(Forward Message)
        ucDistList(Create Distribution List)
        ucSendDist(Send to Distribution List)
        ucLeave(Leave Message)
        ucAuth(Authenticate Subscriber)
    end

    adminActor --- ucSetUp
    subscriberActor --- ucAccess
    subscriberActor --- ucRetrieve
    subscriberActor --- ucRecord
    subscriberActor --- ucForward
    subscriberActor --- ucDistList
    subscriberActor --- ucSendDist
    callerActor --- ucLeave

    ucAccess -. include .-> ucAuth
    ucRetrieve -. include .-> ucAuth
    ucRecord -. include .-> ucAuth
    ucForward -. include .-> ucAuth
    ucSendDist -. include .-> ucAuth

    ucForward -. extend .-> ucRetrieve
    ucLeave -. extend .-> ucSetUp
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The dashed arrow `-. include .->` points from the *base* use case to the *included* use case. The dashed arrow `-. extend .->` points from the *extension* to the *base* use case. This convention is the single most-asked UML nuance in KTU exams.

## 4.3 Analysis Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Subscriber {
        +subscriberId : int
        +name : string
        +phone : string
        +mailbox : Mailbox
        +login()
        +recordGreeting()
    }
    class Mailbox {
        +mailboxNo : int
        +password : string
        +maxMessages : int
        +messages : List Message
        +greeting : Greeting
        +authenticate(pwd : string) bool
        +getNewMessages() List Message
        +addMessage(m : Message)
    }
    class Message {
        +messageId : int
        +callerNumber : string
        +timestamp : DateTime
        +duration : int
        +status : enum NEW SAVED DELETED
        +audio : AudioFile
        +play()
        +save()
        +delete()
        +forward(target : Mailbox)
    }
    class Greeting {
        +greetingId : int
        +audio : AudioFile
        +record(audioFile)
    }
    class DistributionList {
        +listId : int
        +listName : string
        +subscribers : List Subscriber
        +addMember(s : Subscriber)
        +removeMember(s : Subscriber)
    }
    class Administrator {
        +adminId : int
        +createMailbox(sub : Subscriber)
        +suspendMailbox(m : Mailbox)
        +deleteMailbox(m : Mailbox)
    }
    class TelephoneInterface {
        +dtmfDecoder
        +audioPlayer
        +playPrompt(text)
        +recordAudio() AudioFile
    }

    Subscriber "1" --> "1" Mailbox : owns
    Mailbox "1" --> "0..*" Message : contains
    Mailbox "1" --> "1" Greeting : plays
    Subscriber "0..*" --> "0..*" DistributionList : belongs to
    Subscriber --> TelephoneInterface : uses
    Administrator --> Mailbox : manages
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Deployment View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserLayer [User Layer]
        phone1[Subscriber Telephone Set]
        phone2[Caller Telephone Set]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer [Telephony Edge Layer]
        pbx[PBX or PSTN Gateway]
        dtmf[DTMF Decoder Module]
    end

    subgraph AppLayer [Application Layer]
        ctlCall[Call Controller Thread Pool]
        ctlMsg[Message Controller]
        ctlSub[Subscriber Controller]
        auth[Authenticator Module]
        notif[Notification Module]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer [Persistence Layer]
        dbMail[(Mailbox Repository)]
        dbMsg[(Message Repository)]
        dbList[(Distribution List Repository)]
        audioFS[Audio File Store]
    end

    phone1 --> pbx
    phone2 --> pbx
    pbx --> dtmf
    dtmf --> ctlCall
    ctlCall --> ctlMsg
    ctlCall --> ctlSub
    ctlSub --> auth
    auth --> dbMail
    ctlMsg --> dbMsg
    ctlMsg --> audioFS
    ctlSub --> dbList
    ctlCall --> notif
    notif --> pbx
```

> [!TIP]
> The deployment view shows **why** the VMS is naturally multi-threaded: every incoming call is dispatched to a *Call Controller* thread from the pool, and the *Notification Module* runs as a separate worker that polls for newly arrived messages.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This question bank is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**:
- **Part A**: 3-mark short-answer questions.
- **Part B**: 14-mark questions with internal choice between two alternatives (each with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each).

All questions are tagged with simulated past-year identifiers.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any three actors of a Voice Mail System and write one goal of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Actor | Goal |
| --- | --- |
| **Subscriber** (1 mark) | To retrieve voice messages left in their mailbox. (½ mark) |
| **Caller** (1 mark) | To leave a voice message for a subscriber who is unavailable. (½ mark) |
| **Administrator** (1 mark) | To create, suspend, or delete subscriber mailboxes. (½ mark) |

> *(Examiner's note: half-mark may be awarded for the goal statement.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between «include» and «extend» use-case relationships. Give one example from the Voice Mail System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | «include» | «extend» |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Invocation** (1) | Always invoked by the base use case. | Invoked only when a trigger condition is met. |
| **Purpose** (½) | Factor out common mandatory behaviour. | Add optional or exceptional behaviour. |
| **VMS Example** (1) | *Access Subscriber Functions* «include»s *Authenticate Subscriber*. | *Forward Message* is «extend»ed by a *Notify-Mailbox-Full* flow when recipient's mailbox is full. (½) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Case-Study Questions**
> 1. Drawing the **system boundary incorrectly** (actors placed *inside* the box) → **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. Confusing the direction of «include» and «extend» arrows → **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. Listing use cases that are *actions* ("Press 1", "Press 2") instead of *goals* ("Retrieve Messages") → **1-mark deduction per wrong entry**.
> 4. Forgetting **preconditions / postconditions** in the use-case narrative → **1-mark deduction**.
> 5. Drawing the class diagram without showing **multiplicities** (1, 0..*, 1..*) → **1-mark deduction**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw a **Use-Case Diagram** for a Voice Mail System. Identify at least **three actors** and **five use cases**, and show at least one «include» and one «extend» relationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **Use-Case Description** (narrative template) for the use case *"Retrieve Messages"*. Include preconditions, postconditions, main flow, and at least **two alternate / exception flows**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Use-Case Diagram (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Actors (1 mark):** Subscriber, Caller, Administrator.

**Step 2 — Use Cases (2 marks):** Set Up Subscriber, Access Subscriber Functions, Record Greeting, Retrieve Messages, Forward Message, Leave Message.

**Step 3 — Associations (1 mark):** Draw solid lines from each actor to the use case(s) they participate in.

**Step 4 — «include» (1.5 marks):** Draw a dashed arrow from *Retrieve Messages*, *Record Greeting*, *Forward Message* to *Authenticate Subscriber*.

**Step 5 — «extend» (1.5 marks):** Draw a dashed arrow from a *Notify-Mailbox-Full* sub-use-case to *Forward Message* with the extension point *{when recipient mailbox is full}*.

> *(Use the diagram in §4.2 as the reference answer.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Use-Case Description for *Retrieve Messages* (7 marks)

**Use-Case Name:** Retrieve Messages
**Actor:** Subscriber
**Preconditions:** (1 mark)
1. The subscriber's mailbox exists and is active.
2. The subscriber has just been authenticated by the system.

**Postconditions:** (1 mark)
1. All *new* messages in the mailbox are marked as *old*.
2. The system returns to the main menu.

**Main Flow:** (3 marks)
1. The subscriber chooses option "Retrieve Messages" from the main menu.
2. The system retrieves the list of *new* messages sorted by arrival time.
3. The system announces the total count and total duration.
4. The subscriber selects a message by number (DTMF).
5. The system plays the selected message.
6. The subscriber chooses one of: Save, Delete, Forward, or Replay.
7. The system performs the chosen action and prompts the subscriber for the next action.

**Alternate / Exception Flows:** (2 marks)
- **3a. No new messages:** system announces "You have no new messages" and returns to main menu.
- **5a. Wrong selection (invalid DTMF):** system plays "Invalid selection, please try again" and re-prompts.
- **6a. Mailbox full during save:** system announces "Mailbox full" and offers Delete or Skip as alternatives.
- **6b. Forward selected:** control passes to the *Forward Message* use case (extension point).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the **System Context Diagram (Level-0 DFD)** for the Voice Mail System. Identify all external entities and the major data flows. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **Analysis Class Diagram** for the Voice Mail System with at least **five classes**, showing class names, attributes, methods, and associations with **multiplicities**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — System Context Diagram (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify External Entities (2 marks):** Caller, Subscriber, Administrator, Telephone Network (PSTN / PBX).

**Step 2 — Central Process (1 mark):** A single ellipse in the centre labelled "Voice Mail System".

**Step 3 — Data Flows (3 marks):** Draw arrows in both directions for each interaction as listed in §3.1.2.2.

**Step 4 — Verify Closure (1 mark):** Each outbound flow has a matching inbound flow for the same data.

> *(Use the diagram in §4.1 as the reference answer.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Analysis Class Diagram (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Classes (2 marks):** Subscriber, Mailbox, Message, Greeting, DistributionList, Administrator.

**Step 2 — Identify Attributes (2 marks):** Provide at least 2 attributes per class (see §4.3 class diagram).

**Step 3 — Identify Methods (1 mark):** Provide at least 1 method per class (e.g., `Mailbox.authenticate()`).

**Step 4 — Associations and Multiplicities (2 marks):**
- Subscriber $\longrightarrow$ Mailbox : `1` to `1`
- Mailbox $\longrightarrow$ Message : `1` to `0..*`
- Mailbox $\longrightarrow$ Greeting : `1` to `1`
- Subscriber $\longrightarrow$ DistributionList : `*` to `*`

> *(Use the diagram in §4.3 as the reference answer.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Voice Mail System Case Study**

### Conceptual Anchors
- A VMS is a **telephony-integrated, persistent messaging system** with three primary actors: **Subscriber, Caller, Administrator**.
- The VMS case study is a canonical example of **object-oriented analysis and design** (OOAD) following the Pressman methodology.

### Modeling Steps (in order)
1. **System Context Diagram** — establish the boundary, identify external entities, and data flows.
2. **Actor Identification** — roles that *initiate* goals; passive entities are not actors.
3. **Use-Case Enumeration** — verb-object goals, not actions.
4. **Use-Case Diagram** — actors outside the boundary, use cases inside, solid lines for associations, dashed arrows for «include» and «extend».
5. **Use-Case Description** — narrative with preconditions, postconditions, main flow, and alternate / exception flows.
6. **Analysis Class Diagram** — extracted via noun-phrase analysis of use cases; classes are stereotyped as Boundary, Control, or Entity.

### UML Notation Rules
- «include» arrow points from **base → included**.
- «extend» arrow points from **extension → base**.
- Multiplicities are mandatory on associations.
- Use cases are **ellipses**, actors are **stick figures** (or named rectangles for system actors).

### Functional Highlights
- Authentication is **factored out** as a shared «include» use case.
- Mailbox-full is a **typical «extend» trigger** in Forward Message.
- Distribution lists model **many-to-many** subscriber groups.

### Non-Functional Highlights
- Latency: DTMF response $\leq 200$ ms.
- Availability: 99.99 % for production VMS.
- Concurrency: thread pool per incoming call.
- Persistence: messages, greetings, passwords, distribution lists.

### Common Valuation Traps (Avoid Them!)
- Actor drawn **inside** the system boundary.
- «include» / «extend» arrows reversed.
- Use cases phrased as **steps** ("Press 1") instead of **goals** ("Retrieve Messages").
- Class diagram missing **multiplicities**.
- Narrative missing **preconditions** or **postconditions**.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Actors initiate goals, use cases deliver them, classes hold them, messages carry them."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ATM Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ATM Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **ATM (Automated Teller Machine) Example** is a canonical, industry-standard pedagogical case study used across Software Engineering curricula to demonstrate the practical application of **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** techniques. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PECST411**), the ATM system serves as the reference problem domain to teach the systematic transformation of *informal requirements* into *formal UML artifacts* — specifically **Use Case Diagrams**, **Domain Class Diagrams**, **System Sequence Diagrams (SSDs)**, and **Operation Contracts**.

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this example directly addresses:
- **CO2:** *Apply the concepts of requirements engineering and create UML models for real-world software systems.*
- **CO3:** *Apply object-oriented analysis and design principles using GRASP patterns.*

The ATM is treated as a *self-contained, closed-world problem* that has well-defined **Actors**, **Use Cases**, and **Domain Classes**, making it ideal for examination purposes because students must demonstrate both **analytical thinking** (extracting classes/relationships) and **syntactic correctness** (drawing valid UML notation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The ATM example bridges the gap between **Requirements Engineering** (elicitation, specification, validation) and **OOAD** (use-case driven analysis). The single most examined application of the ATM in KTU papers is the construction of a **Use Case Diagram** with proper `<include>`, `<extend>`, and **Generalization** relationships.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk up to a real ATM machine at a bank. You insert your card, type your PIN, choose "Withdraw Cash," enter an amount, take the cash, and leave. From a **customer's** perspective, this is one fluid action. But from a **software engineer's** perspective, this single customer action is actually composed of *dozens* of collaborating software objects:

- A **Card Reader** object (hardware interface)
- A **PIN Validator** object (security)
- A **Bank Account** object (data)
- A **Cash Dispenser** object (hardware interface)
- A **Transaction Log** object (audit trail)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:** Think of the ATM as a busy restaurant. The **Customer** is the *Actor*, the **Menu** is the *Use Case Diagram*, the **Order Ticket* is the *System Sequence Diagram*, and the **Chefs, Waiters, and Ingredients** are the *Domain Classes* collaborating behind the scenes. The ATM example teaches you how to draw the menu, write the order ticket, and identify the chefs.

The fundamental lesson is: **a real-world banking system involves multiple actors (Customer, Bank, Technician) performing multiple use cases (Withdraw, Deposit, Transfer, Maintenance), and the software must coordinate these through a well-modeled object structure.** The KTU examination expects you to *decompose* the ATM into these components with precision.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & KTU Notation Standards

| Standard Metric | Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| **UML Version** | **UML 2.5** (OMG Standard) |
| **Use Case Granularity** | **User-Goal Level** (per Cockburn) |
| **Diagram Notation** | **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** |
| **Domain Scope** | **Single-Session ATM** (one withdrawal transaction) |
| **Primary Actor** | **Customer** (Human) |
| **Supporting Actors** | **Bank, ATM Technician, Communication Network** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Stage Analysis Pipeline for the ATM Example

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to apply a **systematic 5-stage pipeline** when answering any ATM-based question. This pipeline mirrors the **Unified Process (UP)** methodology:

### Stage 1 — Identify Actors
- **Why:** An actor represents *any external entity* that interacts with the system to achieve a meaningful goal.
- **How:** Ask: *"Who or what uses the system? Who provides services to it? Who maintains it?"*
- **ATM Result:** Customer (primary), Bank (secondary — validates PIN/account), ATM Technician (maintenance).

### Stage 2 — Identify Use Cases
- **Why:** Use cases capture the *value* delivered to actors.
- **How:** For each actor, list the goals they want to accomplish.
- **ATM Result:** **Withdraw Cash, Deposit Cash, Transfer Funds, Check Balance, Change PIN, View Transaction History, System Startup, System Shutdown, Replenish Cash, Diagnose Hardware Fault**.

### Stage 3 — Describe Use Cases (Use Case Template)
- **Why:** A diagram alone is insufficient — the KTU examiner awards marks for *textual elaboration*.
- **How:** Use the **Cockburn Template** with: Use Case Name, Primary Actor, Stakeholders, Preconditions, Postconditions, Main Flow, Alternative Flows, Exception Flows.
- **ATM Result:** Withdraw Cash → "Customer inserts card, validates PIN, selects account, enters amount, system dispenses cash, prints receipt, ejects card."

### Stage 4 — Build the Domain Model (Conceptual Class Diagram)
- **Why:** Identifies the *conceptual* classes, attributes, and associations — **not software classes yet**.
- **How:** Use **noun-phrase identification** from the use case descriptions and apply **CRC cards**.
- **ATM Result:** Classes: `Account`, `Customer`, `Card`, `Transaction`, `ATM`, `CashDispenser`, `Receipt`, `BankNetwork`.

### Stage 5 — Assign Responsibilities (GRASP Patterns)
- **Why:** Decides *which class* does *what operation*.
- **How:** Apply **Information Expert**, **Creator**, **Controller**, **Low Coupling**, **High Cohesion**, **Polymorphism**, **Pure Fabrication**, **Indirection**, **Protected Variations**.
