- **ATM Result:** `withdraw()` → Information Expert is `Account`; `dispenseCash()` → Controller is `ATMController`.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | KTU Notation Rule | Weight in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Actor Notation** | Stick figure (human) or rectangle with `<<actor>>` stereotype (non-human) | **High** |
| **2** | **Use Case Notation** | Ellipse with verb-noun phrase (e.g., "Withdraw Cash") | **High** |
| **3** | **System Boundary** | Rectangle enclosing all use cases, labeled with system name | **High** |
| **4** | **Association** | Solid line between Actor and Use Case (no arrows unless directional) | **High** |
| **5** | **`<include>`** | Dashed arrow `---->` with `<<include>>` — *mandatory* reuse | **High** |
| **6** | **`<extend>`** | Dashed arrow `---->` with `<<extend>>` — *optional/conditional* extension | **High** |
| **7** | **Generalization** | Solid line with hollow triangle arrowhead between use cases or actors | **Medium** |
| **8** | **Domain Class** | Rectangle with **3 compartments** (Name, Attributes, Methods) | **High** |
| **9** | **Multiplicity** | Format `1`, `*`, `1..*`, `0..1`, `n..m` placed at association ends | **High** |
| **10** | **System Sequence Diagram (SSD)** | Single system object as a box on top, actor on left, arrows represent *messages* | **Medium** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same 5-stage pipeline is used in industry for *banking software, hospital information systems, e-commerce platforms, and embedded systems*. The ATM is the training wheels — once you master it, every enterprise system follows the same shape.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Problem Statement to Full Use Case Diagram

> **Scenario (typical KTU 14-mark question):** *"Consider an ATM system. A customer can withdraw cash, deposit cash, check balance, transfer funds, and change PIN. All transactions require PIN validation. The system maintains a transaction log. A technician can perform maintenance. Replenishing cash is an extension of the maintenance use case. Model this as a UML use case diagram."*

### Step 1 — Actor Identification (Heuristic Application)

We apply the **WHO/WHAT/MAINTAINS** heuristic systematically.

$$
\text{Actors}_{\text{ATM}} = \{\text{Customer},\ \text{Bank},\ \text{ATM Technician}\}
$$

- **Customer** → uses the ATM to perform banking tasks.
- **Bank** → authorizes transactions, validates accounts.
- **ATM Technician** → services hardware, replenishes cash.

### Step 2 — Use Case Extraction

Each verb-noun phrase from the problem statement maps to a use case:

$$
\text{UseCases}_{\text{ATM}} = \{\text{Withdraw Cash},\ \text{Deposit Cash},\ \text{Check Balance},\ \text{Transfer Funds},\ \text{Change PIN},\ \text{View Transaction History},\ \text{Validate PIN},\ \text{Log Transaction},\ \text{Perform Maintenance},\ \text{Replenish Cash}\}
$$

### Step 3 — Relationship Inference (the critical reasoning step)

We now ask: *"Are any of these use cases *reused* or *extended* by others?"*

$$
\text{Rule}_{\text{include}}: \text{If UC}_A \text{ is mandatory for } \text{UC}_B \text{, then } B \xrightarrow{<<include>>} A
$$

Applying this:
- **Withdraw Cash** ← includes → **Validate PIN** (every withdrawal requires PIN)
- **Deposit Cash** ← includes → **Validate PIN**
- **Transfer Funds** ← includes → **Validate PIN**
- **Change PIN** ← includes → **Validate PIN**

$$
\text{Rule}_{\text{extend}}: \text{If } \text{UC}_X \text{ optionally adds behavior to } \text{UC}_Y \text{ under a condition, then } X \xrightarrow{<<extend>>} Y
$$

Applying this:
- **Replenish Cash** ← extends → **Perform Maintenance** (only if cash level is low)
- **Print Receipt** ← extends → **Withdraw Cash** (optional)

### Step 4 — System Boundary & Layout

The system boundary is a **rectangle** with the label **"ATM System"**. All use cases go inside, all actors stay outside.

### Step 5 — Final Validation

We verify that:
1. Every actor has at least one association to a use case.
2. Every `<include>` points from a *concrete* use case to a *concrete* reusable use case.
3. Every `<extend>` points from the *extension* to the *base* use case.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: ATM Use Case Detailed Specification

| Use Case | Primary Actor | Precondition | Main Flow | Postcondition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Withdraw Cash** | Customer | Card inserted, valid PIN | Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select Account → Enter Amount → Dispense Cash → Print Receipt → Eject Card | Cash dispensed, balance reduced, log updated |
| **Deposit Cash** | Customer | Card inserted, valid PIN | Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select Account → Insert Envelope/Cash → Confirm Amount → Update Balance | Balance increased, log updated |
| **Check Balance** | Customer | Card inserted, valid PIN | Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select Account → Display Balance | Balance displayed on screen |
| **Transfer Funds** | Customer | Card inserted, valid PIN, sufficient funds | Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select "Transfer" → Enter Target Account → Enter Amount → Confirm | Funds moved, both accounts updated, log updated |
| **Change PIN** | Customer | Card inserted, valid PIN | Insert Card → Enter PIN → Select "Change PIN" → Enter New PIN → Confirm | PIN updated in bank database |
| **Validate PIN** | System | Card inserted | Read card → Prompt for PIN → Compare with database → Return boolean | PIN validated/denied |
| **Log Transaction** | System | Transaction completed | Create log entry (timestamp, type, amount, account) → Append to log file | Transaction recorded |
| **Replenish Cash** | ATM Technician | Maintenance mode active | Open vault → Load cash cassettes → Update cash level → Close vault | ATM cash level updated |

## 3.3 Derivation: Domain Class Diagram for ATM

### Step 1 — Noun-Phrase Extraction

From the use case flows, we extract the *nouns* that represent *domain concepts* (excluding actors, which are external):

$$
\text{Domain Classes} = \{\text{ATM},\ \text{Card},\ \text{Account},\ \text{Customer},\ \text{Transaction},\ \text{CashDispenser},\ \text{Receipt},\ \text{BankDatabase},\ \text{CashCassette}\}
$$

### Step 2 — Attribute Identification (per class)

| Class | Key Attributes | Methods (Responsibilities) |
|---|---|---|
| **Account** | `accountNumber: String`, `balance: double`, `accountType: String` | `debit(amount)`, `credit(amount)`, `getBalance()` |
| **Customer** | `customerID: String`, `name: String`, `pin: String` | `authenticate(pin)` |
| **ATM** | `atmID: String`, `location: String`, `cashOnHand: double` | `startSession()`, `endSession()` |
| **Card** | `cardNumber: String`, `expiryDate: Date`, `customer: Customer` | `validate()`, `eject()` |
| **Transaction** | `transactionID: String`, `timestamp: DateTime`, `amount: double`, `type: String` | `commit()`, `rollback()` |
| **CashDispenser** | `cassettes: List<CashCassette>`, `maxCapacity: int` | `dispense(amount)`, `checkLevel()` |

### Step 3 — Association & Multiplicity

$$
\text{Customer} \xrightarrow{1 \ \text{to} \ *} \text{Account}
$$

$$
\text{ATM} \xrightarrow{1 \ \text{to} \ 1} \text{CashDispenser}
$$

$$
\text{Account} \xrightarrow{1 \ \text{to} \ *} \text{Transaction}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply GRASP Information Expert Principle

> *"Assign responsibility to the class that has the information needed to fulfill it."*

For the operation `withdraw(amount: double)`:
- The class with the `balance` attribute is **`Account`**.
- Therefore, `withdraw()` is assigned to **`Account`**.

$$
\text{Responsibility}(\text{withdraw}) \rightarrow \text{Information Expert} = \text{Account}
$$

This is mathematically represented as:

$$
\text{Class}_{\text{responsible}} = \arg\max_{c \in \text{Classes}} \text{Information}(c, \text{operation})
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use Case Diagram for the ATM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ATM_SYS["ATM System Boundary"]
        UC1(["Withdraw Cash"])
        UC2(["Deposit Cash"])
        UC3(["Check Balance"])
        UC4(["Transfer Funds"])
        UC5(["Change PIN"])
        UC6(["View Transaction History"])
        UC7(["Validate PIN"])
        UC8(["Log Transaction"])
        UC9(["Print Receipt"])
        UC10(["Perform Maintenance"])
        UC11(["Replenish Cash"])
        UC12(["Diagnose Hardware"])
    end

    ACT1(("Customer"))
    ACT2(("Bank"))
    ACT3(("ATM Technician"))

    ACT1 --- UC1
    ACT1 --- UC2
    ACT1 --- UC3
    ACT1 --- UC4
    ACT1 --- UC5
    ACT1 --- UC6

    ACT2 --- UC7
    ACT3 --- UC10
    ACT3 --- UC11
    ACT3 --- UC12

    UC1 -.->|includes| UC7
    UC1 -.->|includes| UC8
    UC2 -.->|includes| UC7
    UC2 -.->|includes| UC8
    UC3 -.->|includes| UC7
    UC4 -.->|includes| UC7
    UC4 -.->|includes| UC8
    UC5 -.->|includes| UC7
    UC6 -.->|includes| UC7
    UC9 -.->|extends| UC1
    UC11 -.->|extends| UC10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid lines represent *association* (actor–use case). The dashed arrows represent `<include>` and `<extend>` dependencies. Every line and arrow is **mandatory for full KTU marks**.

## 4.2 Domain Class Diagram for the ATM

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Customer {
        -customerID : String
        -name : String
        -pin : String
        -accounts : List
        +authenticate(pin : String) Boolean
    }

    class Account {
        -accountNumber : String
        -balance : double
        -accountType : String
        +debit(amount : double) Boolean
        +credit(amount : double) void
        +getBalance() double
    }

    class ATM {
        -atmID : String
        -location : String
        -cashOnHand : double
        -status : String
        +startSession() void
        +endSession() void
    }

    class Card {
        -cardNumber : String
        -expiryDate : Date
        -isLocked : Boolean
        +validate() Boolean
    }

    class Transaction {
        -transactionID : String
        -timestamp : DateTime
        -amount : double
        -type : String
        +commit() void
        +rollback() void
    }

    class CashDispenser {
        -cassettes : List
        -maxCapacity : int
        +dispense(amount : double) Boolean
        +checkLevel() int
    }

    class Receipt {
        -receiptID : String
        -timestamp : DateTime
        -amount : double
        +print() void
    }

    Customer "1" --> "*" Account : owns
    Customer "1" --> "1" Card : carries
    ATM "1" --> "1" CashDispenser : contains
    ATM "1" --> "*" Transaction : processes
    Account "1" --> "*" Transaction : records
    Transaction "1" --> "0..1" Receipt : generates
```

## 4.3 System Sequence Diagram (SSD) — Withdraw Cash Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ACT as Customer
    participant SYS as ATM System
    participant BN as Bank Network
    participant CD as Cash Dispenser

    ACT->>SYS: insertCard(cardNumber)
    SYS->>ACT: promptForPIN()
    ACT->>SYS: enterPIN(pin)
    SYS->>BN: validatePIN(cardNumber, pin)
    BN-->>SYS: pinValid (true/false)
    alt pinValid = true
        SYS->>ACT: showMenu()
        ACT->>SYS: selectWithdraw()
        SYS->>ACT: promptForAmount()
        ACT->>SYS: enterAmount(amount)
        SYS->>BN: debitAccount(accountNumber, amount)
        BN-->>SYS: debitSuccess
        SYS->>CD: dispenseCash(amount)
        CD-->>SYS: cashDispensed
        SYS->>ACT: ejectCard()
        SYS->>ACT: dispenseReceipt()
    else pinValid = false
        SYS->>ACT: displayError(Invalid PIN)
        SYS->>ACT: ejectCard()
    end
```

## 4.4 State Machine Diagram — ATM Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OutOfService
    OutOfService --> Idle : startUp()
    Idle --> CardInserted : insertCard()
    CardInserted --> Authenticating : enterPIN()
    Authenticating --> Idle : pinInvalid / ejectCard()
    Authenticating --> MenuShown : pinValid
    MenuShown --> PerformingTransaction : selectOperation()
    PerformingTransaction --> PrintingReceipt : transactionComplete
    PrintingReceipt --> Idle : receiptPrinted / ejectCard()
    PerformingTransaction --> OutOfService : hardwareError
    Idle --> OutOfService : shutDown()
    OutOfService --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Use Case" and "Actor" with respect to the ATM example. List the primary actor and three use cases.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Use Case:** A use case is a *sequence of actions performed by the system* that yields an *observable result of value* to a particular actor. It represents a *complete, meaningful goal* a user wants to accomplish.
- **Actor:** An actor is an *external entity* (human, hardware device, or another system) that interacts with the system to achieve a goal.
- **Primary Actor of ATM:** The **Customer**.
- **Three Use Cases:** **Withdraw Cash, Deposit Cash, Check Balance**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A *common mistake* is to confuse **"Include"** with **"Extend."** Remember: **Include = Mandatory** (the base use case *cannot* work without it, e.g., Validate PIN). **Extend = Optional/Conditional** (adds behavior only when a condition holds, e.g., Print Receipt). Confusing these two will cost you 2-3 marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between `<include>` and `<extend>` relationships using the ATM example.

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `<include>` | `<extend>` |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | From base UC → reused UC | From extension UC → base UC |
| **Nature** | Mandatory reuse | Optional, conditional reuse |
| **ATM Example** | Withdraw Cash `<include>` Validate PIN | Print Receipt `<extend>` Withdraw Cash |
| **When Triggered** | Always executed when base UC runs | Only executed when extension condition is true |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> *"Consider an ATM system. The ATM allows a Customer to withdraw cash, deposit cash, check balance, transfer funds between accounts, and change PIN. The system must validate the PIN for every transaction. The system maintains a transaction log for audit purposes. An ATM Technician can perform maintenance and replenish cash. The maintenance use case is extended by replenishing cash when the cash level falls below a threshold. Draw the complete Use Case Diagram and write the detailed use case description for 'Withdraw Cash'."*

#### Part (a) — Use Case Diagram (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (Diagram + Explanation):**

The diagram should include:
1. **System boundary** rectangle labeled **"ATM System"** (1 mark)
2. **Three actors** outside the boundary: **Customer** (stick figure), **Bank** (rectangle with `<<actor>>`), **ATM Technician** (stick figure) (1 mark)
3. **Six use cases** inside boundary: Withdraw Cash, Deposit Cash, Check Balance, Transfer Funds, Change PIN, Perform Maintenance, Replenish Cash, Log Transaction, Validate PIN (2 marks)
4. **Include relationships:** Withdraw Cash, Deposit Cash, Check Balance, Transfer Funds, Change PIN → Validate PIN; All transaction use cases → Log Transaction (2 marks)
5. **Extend relationship:** Replenish Cash → Perform Maintenance (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Use Case Description for "Withdraw Cash" (7 Marks)

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Use Case Name** | Withdraw Cash |
| **Primary Actor** | Customer |
| **Stakeholders** | Customer (wants cash), Bank (wants accurate ledger) |
| **Preconditions** | ATM is operational; Customer has a valid card; Cash is available |
| **Postconditions** | Customer's account is debited; Cash is dispensed; Transaction is logged |
| **Main Flow** | 1. Customer inserts card<br>2. System reads card and prompts for PIN<br>3. Customer enters PIN<br>4. System validates PIN with Bank<br>5. System displays account menu<br>6. Customer selects "Withdraw Cash"<br>7. System prompts for amount<br>8. Customer enters amount<br>9. System checks balance and daily limit<br>10. System debits account via Bank Network<br>11. Cash Dispenser dispenses cash<br>12. System prints receipt<br>13. System ejects card |
| **Alternative Flow 1** | *Insufficient funds* — at step 9, if balance < amount, display error, eject card |
| **Alternative Flow 2** | *Invalid PIN* — at step 4, after 3 failed attempts, card is retained |
| **Exception Flow** | *Hardware failure* — if cash dispenser jams, transaction is rolled back, account is re-credited, error is logged |

**Mark Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Pre/Postconditions (1 mark)
- Main Flow with 8+ steps (3 marks)
- 2 Alternative Flows (2 marks)
- 1 Exception Flow (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> *"Design the Domain Class Diagram for an ATM system. Identify at least 6 domain classes with their attributes and methods. Show the relationships between them with proper multiplicities. Justify the application of GRASP 'Information Expert' pattern for the withdraw operation."*

#### Part (a) — Domain Class Diagram (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The diagram should contain:
1. **Six classes** with proper 3-compartment notation: `Customer`, `Account`, `Card`, `ATM`, `Transaction`, `CashDispenser` (2 marks)
2. **Attributes** for each class (data types specified) (2 marks)
3. **Methods** for each class (responsibilities defined) (2 marks)
4. **Multiplicities** on associations (1 mark)

**Key Associations:**
- `Customer` `1` —— `*` `Account` (one customer, many accounts)
- `Customer` `1` —— `1` `Card` (one customer, one active card)
- `ATM` `1` —— `*` `Transaction` (one ATM, many transactions)
- `Account` `1` —— `*` `Transaction` (one account, many transactions)

#### Part (b) — GRASP Information Expert Justification (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Information Expert pattern** is a GRASP pattern that guides the assignment of responsibilities: *"Assign the responsibility to the class that has the information necessary to fulfill it."*

For the `withdraw(amount: double)` operation, we apply the pattern systematically:

**Step 1 — Identify the responsibility:** The system must "withdraw cash" from a customer's account.

**Step 2 — Identify the information needed:**
- The current `balance` of the account
- The `accountNumber` to identify the source
- The `amount` parameter to be withdrawn

**Step 3 — Identify which class holds this information:** The class `Account` has the attributes `accountNumber` and `balance` as private members. Therefore, **`Account` is the Information Expert**.

**Step 4 — Assign the responsibility:**

$$
\text{Class.withdraw(amount)} \leftarrow \text{Account.withdraw(amount: double): Boolean}
$$

**Justification:** The `Account` class has the *intrinsic information* (balance, account number, transaction history) required to perform the withdrawal. It is the only class that knows whether sufficient funds exist. Therefore, the method `withdraw()` should be defined in `Account`.

**Implementation Sketch (Pseudocode — not a programming requirement):**

```
Account.withdraw(amount):
    IF this.balance >= amount THEN
        this.balance = this.balance - amount
        Transaction t = new Transaction("WITHDRAWAL", amount, NOW)
        this.transactions.add(t)
        RETURN true
    ELSE
        RETURN false
    END IF
```

**Mark Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct statement of Information Expert principle (2 marks)
- Identification of Account as expert (1 mark)
- Reasoning for why (2 marks)
- Coherent justification + alternative considered (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse UML and ER Diagrams.** Class diagrams use *3-compartment rectangles* with **methods** in the bottom compartment — ER diagrams do not. Mixing them = 3-mark penalty.
> 2. **Do NOT use crow's foot notation in UML class diagrams.** UML uses *multiplicity numbers* (1, *, 0..1, n..m).
> 3. **For Use Case Diagrams, ALWAYS draw the system boundary rectangle** — omitting it costs 1 full mark.
> 4. **For `<include>` and `<extend>`, the arrow direction matters.** The arrow goes **from the base use case toward the reused use case for include**, and **from the extension use case toward the base use case for extend**. Reversed arrows = 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Do NOT name use cases as single nouns** (e.g., "PIN"). Use **verb-noun phrases** (e.g., "Validate PIN"). Single-noun use cases = 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — ATM Example: 1-Page Rapid Revision**

- ✅ **ATM** is a *closed-world case study* used to demonstrate **Use Case Modeling** and **OOAD**.
- ✅ **Primary Actor:** Customer. **Secondary Actors:** Bank, ATM Technician.
- ✅ **Use Case Naming:** Always **verb-noun** format (e.g., *Withdraw Cash*, *Check Balance*).
- ✅ **System Boundary:** Mandatory rectangle enclosing all use cases.
- ✅ **`<include>`** = Mandatory reuse. Direction: **Base UC → Reused UC**.
- ✅ **`<extend>`** = Optional, conditional extension. Direction: **Extension UC → Base UC**.
- ✅ **Generalization** = "is-a" relationship (e.g., `Premium Customer` is-a `Customer`).
- ✅ **Domain Class Diagram** uses **3-compartment notation** (Name, Attributes, Methods).
- ✅ **Multiplicities** in UML: `1`, `*`, `1..*`, `0..1`, `n..m`.
- ✅ **GRASP Information Expert** → Class with the *most information* about a responsibility.
- ✅ **GRASP Controller** → First object *behind the UI* that handles system events (e.g., `ATMController`).
- ✅ **SSD** uses *one system-level object* (the black box) — not internal classes.
- ✅ **State Machine Diagram** is for *one object's lifecycle* (e.g., ATM's states: Idle → CardInserted → Authenticating → MenuShown).
- ✅ **Cockburn Use Case Template** has: Name, Primary Actor, Pre/Postconditions, Main Flow, Alternative Flows, Exception Flows.
- ✅ **Noun-Phrase Analysis** is the standard *heuristic* for finding domain classes.
- ✅ **Cardinal Rule of OOAD:** *Objects do not exist in isolation* — they collaborate to fulfill use cases.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Pattern - Model View Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Pattern - Model View Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Model View Controller (MVC)** is a compound architectural design pattern originally formulated by **Trygve Reenskaug** in **1979** at Xerox PARC. It is classified under the category of **Architectural Patterns** in software engineering and is frequently cited in the *Gang of Four (GoF)* canon as a foundational structure for building interactive, user-facing software systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST411 – Module 2):**
> *Model–View–Controller (MVC) is an architectural pattern that decouples an application's data (Model), presentation (View), and user-input handling logic (Controller) into three interconnected components, thereby enforcing the **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** principle.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, MVC is treated as a **structural/architectural pattern** that addresses the recurring design problem of *“How to modularize a user-interface software so that the business logic, display logic, and input logic can evolve independently?”*

### 1.2 The Three Components — Quick Look

| Component | Responsibility | Key Word to Remember |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | Encapsulates application **data** and **business rules** | *“Knowledge”* |
| **View** | Renders the **presentation** of the Model to the user | *“Look”* |
| **Controller** | Receives **user input**, translates it into Model/View operations | *“Glue / Brain”* |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Office

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Daily Newspaper**
> - **The Editor (Model):** Holds the verified facts, articles, and data. He does *not* decide how the paper looks, only *what is true*.
> - **The Layout Designer (View):** Takes the editor's data and arranges it into a beautiful front page. He does *not* verify facts.
> - **The Reporter / Phone Operator (Controller):** Receives a phone call from a reader, decides which article to fetch (Model), and instructs the designer which section to re-layout (View).

If the newspaper switches from broadsheet to tabloid size, only the **View** changes. If a new source of news arrives via Twitter, only the **Model** changes. The **Controller** continues to route requests. *This is the essence of MVC — independent evolution.*

### 1.4 Why MVC? — The Driving Problem

Before MVC, monolithic UI code (often called *Smart-UI* or *Spaghetti Code*) entangled data, display, and input in a single block. KTU examiners frequently test *why* MVC emerged:

1. **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) violation** in legacy UI code.
2. **Code duplication** — same business logic written in many screens.
3. **Testing difficulty** — UI tightly coupled with database.
4. **Parallel development blocked** — designers, coders, and DBAs stepped on each other.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Keyword Hook:** Always write the phrase *"separation of concerns"* in any MVC answer — it is the single most rewarding **3-mark buzzword** in the KTU valuation script.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in MVC Discussions

| Metric | Symbol / Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of triads in MVC** | **3** | Model, View, Controller |
| **Number of direct bidirectional links** | **3** | Model↔View, Model↔Controller, View↔Controller (classic MVC) |
| **Gang of Four catalog size** | **23** patterns | MVC is referenced but is *larger* than a GoF design pattern |
| **Origin Year of MVC** | **1979** | Trygve Reenskaug, Xerox PARC, Smalltalk-80 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Each Component

#### 2.1.1 The Model
- Holds the **domain state** (e.g., `Student`, `Order`, `Invoice`).
- Implements **business rules** (e.g., `isEligibleForDiscount()`).
- Is **completely ignorant** of HTML, Swing, JSON, or any UI technology.
- Notifies registered Views when its state changes (using the **Observer pattern** internally).

#### 2.1.2 The View
- A *passive* presentation layer (Classic MVC) or an *active* updater (Web MVC 2).
- Subscribes to the Model.
- **Contains no business logic** — only formatting, rendering, and template logic.
- May exist in **multiple representations** of the same Model (e.g., Bar Chart + Table + Pie Chart for the same sales data).

#### 2.1.3 The Controller
- The *only* entry point for user actions (clicks, key-presses, HTTP requests).
- Translates UI events into method calls on the Model.
- Selects the appropriate View to render the updated state.
- Holds **no display logic and no business logic** — pure *mediator*.

### 2.2 The Underlying GoF Patterns Inside MVC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MVC is a *compound* pattern. KTU expects you to know the smaller patterns it composes.

| Interaction | Underlying GoF Pattern | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Model ↔ View | **Observer** | Model is the *Subject*, Views are *Observers* |
| View ↔ Controller | **Strategy** | View is configured with a Controller strategy |
| Controller ↔ Model | **Mediator / Command** | Controller mediates UI events into Model ops |

### 2.3 MVC Variant Forms (Highly Tested)

| Variant | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Classic MVC (Smalltalk)** | View actively polls/observes Model; Controller is input-only | Smalltalk-80, Java Swing |
| **Model 1 (JSP-centric)** | View & Controller merged in JSP page | Early Java EE |
| **Model 2 MVC (MVC 2)** | Front Controller + Dispatcher + pure MVC | **Struts, Spring MVC** |
| **HMVC (Hierarchical MVC)** | Nested triads, parent-child MVC | JavaScript frameworks |
| **MVVM** | ViewModel replaces Controller (data-binding) | WPF, Angular, Vue |
| **MVP** | Presenter replaces Controller; View is *dumb* | GWT, ASP.NET Web Forms |

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since MVC is an *architectural* concept, KTU questions often include metric-based numericals. Use these standard formulas:

$$
\text{Separation Factor (SF)} = \frac{\text{Number of Independent Concerns}}{N_{\text{components}}} = \frac{3}{3} = 1
$$

$$
\text{Coupling}(C) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{k_{ij}}{n(n-1)}
$$

$$
\text{Cohesion of Component} = \frac{\text{Methods serving single purpose}}{\text{Total methods in component}}
$$

$$
\text{Testability Index} \propto \frac{1}{1 + \text{Inter-component Coupling}}
$$

$$
\text{Number of bidirectional links in classical MVC} = 3 \quad (\text{Model} \leftrightarrow \text{View},\ \text{Model} \leftrightarrow \text{Controller},\ \text{View} \leftrightarrow \text{Controller})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pipe Rule:** In any KTU answer sheet, never use the symbol `|` inside a table cell. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value and set-builder notation to prevent markdown rendering break.

| Coupling Type | Formula Notation | MVC Compliance |
|---|---|---|
| Content coupling | $C_c \mid_{direct\;access}$ | **Forbidden** — strict layered access only |
| Stamp coupling | $C_s \mid_{shared\;object}$ | Allowed via *DTO/VO* |
| Data coupling | $C_d \mid_{primitive\;args}$ | **Preferred** in MVC |
| No coupling | $C_0$ | Achieved between Model and View (via events) |

### 2.5 Advantages vs. Disadvantages (Board-Favourite Table)

| # | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Separation of Concerns** — three roles, three files | Increased **number of files/classes** |
| 2 | **Parallel development** — designer + coder + DBA | Steeper **learning curve** |
| 3 | **High testability** — Model unit-tested without UI | **Over-engineering** for tiny apps (Hello-World syndrome) |
| 4 | **Multiple Views** for same Model | Tight timing in event-notification can cause *race conditions* |
| 5 | **Reusable Model** across web, mobile, desktop | Initial development cost is higher |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spring MVC, ASP.NET MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django (MTV variant), Express + EJS, Laravel** — all are industrial deployments of MVC.
- **Android** uses a *modified* MVC (closer to MVP) via Activities/Fragments.
- **Embedded UI frameworks** (Qt, GTK) embed MVC principles.
- **Robotics and SCADA** dashboards use MVC to separate sensor-data Model from HMI View.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Trace & Derivation Matrix

### 3.1 The Canonical Request-Response Flow

The following derivation walks through the **complete lifecycle** of a user request in a **Web Model-2 MVC** architecture (e.g., Spring MVC). Each step is broken out so that KTU students can reproduce it in any 14-mark question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup Notation.** Let
> $U$ = User (Browser) ,
> $C$ = Controller Servlet ,
> $D$ = Dispatcher (Front Controller) ,
> $M$ = Model (POJO / Service Layer) ,
> $V$ = View (JSP / Thymeleaf) ,
> $H$ = Handler Mapping .

#### Step 1 — Initial Request Submission

$$
U \xrightarrow{\;\text{HTTP GET /save?x=10}\;} D
$$

The user submits a request. The **Dispatcher (Front Controller)** — which is *itself* an MVC sub-pattern — is the single ingress point.

#### Step 2 — Request Delegation via Handler Mapping

$$
D \xrightarrow{\;\text{consult}\;} H
$$

$$
H \xrightarrow{\;\text{return Controller } C\;} D
$$

The Dispatcher asks the **Handler Mapping**: *“Which Controller handles /save?”* The handler returns the appropriate Controller bean $C$.

#### Step 3 — Controller Invokes Model Layer

$$
D \xrightarrow{\;\text{invoke}\;} C
$$

$$
C \xrightarrow{\;\text{call}\;} M.\text{process}(x=10)
$$

The Controller $C$ receives the request, parses parameters, and **calls the Model** $M$. **Crucially**, the Controller never touches the database directly; it delegates to Model-layer services.

#### Step 4 — Model Executes Business Logic

$$
M.\text{process}(10) = \text{BusinessResult}
$$

The Model mutates its internal state, applies rules, persists via DAO/Repository, and returns a **result object** (often a *ModelAndView* in Spring).

#### Step 5 — Controller Returns Logical View Name

$$
C \xrightarrow{\;\text{logicalViewName = "success"}\;} D
$$

The Controller **does not pick the View directly**. It returns a *logical name*. This is a key MVC invariant.

#### Step 6 — View Resolver Maps Logical → Physical View

$$
D \xrightarrow{\;\text{consult}\;} \text{ViewResolver}
$$

$$
\text{ViewResolver}(\text{"success"}) = V.\text{jsp}
$$

The Dispatcher consults the **ViewResolver** to map `"success"` → `/WEB-INF/views/success.jsp`.

#### Step 7 — Model Data Passed to View

$$
D \xrightarrow{\;\text{modelAttributes = } \{x:10,\ \text{status:"OK"\}\;} V
$$

#### Step 8 — View Renders Response

$$
V.\text{render}(\text{modelAttributes}) = \text{HTML Response}
$$

#### Step 9 — Response Dispatched to Browser

$$
V \xrightarrow{\;\text{HTTP 200}\;} U
$$

### 3.2 Consolidated Equation of the Lifecycle

$$
U \to D \to H \to C \to M \to C \to D \to V \to U
$$

This nine-step pipeline is the **single most important derivation** for a 14-mark KTU MVC question. Memorize it.

### 3.3 Analytical Trace — Worked Numerical on Coupling

> [!TIP]
> KTU sometimes asks: *“Given N components, what is the minimum number of MVC triads?”* The answer uses set partitioning.

Suppose a system has **N = 12** distinct responsibilities. How many MVC triads are required?

$$
N_{\text{triads}} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{3} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{12}{3} \right\rceil = 4
$$

If responsibilities grow to **N = 13**:

$$
N_{\text{triads}} = \left\lceil \frac{13}{3} \right\rceil = 5
$$

And the **leftover responsibilities** $= 13 - (4 \times 3) = 1$ go into a partial triad (typically absorbed into the Controller of the nearest triad).

### 3.4 Coupling Reduction Proof

Let $C_{\text{before}}$ be the coupling in a monolithic UI (Smart-UI), and $C_{\text{after}}$ be the coupling after MVC application.

$$
C_{\text{before}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} k_{ij} \quad \text{where } k_{ij} \in \{0,1\}
$$

For $N = 3$ components in MVC, allowed links are exactly **3** (Model↔View, Model↔Controller, View↔Controller):

$$
C_{\text{after}} = \sum_{(i,j) \in L_{\text{allowed}}} k_{ij} = 3
$$

$$
\text{Reduction Ratio} = \frac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} = \frac{N(N-1) - 3}{N(N-1)}
$$

For $N = 4$ mixed components in monolith:

$$
\text{Reduction Ratio} = \frac{4(3) - 3}{4(3)} = \frac{9}{12} = 0.75 \;\; (75\%)
$$

Hence MVC reduces intra-system coupling by **~75%** in a 4-component baseline.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** with strictly alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`). No markdown formatting (`**`, `*`, tables) is placed inside node labels.

### 4.1 High-Level MVC Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[User Browser Client]
    ctrlA[Controller C Input Handler]
    modelA[Model M Domain Logic And State]
    viewA[View V Presentation Template]
    dataA[(Database Or DAO Layer)]

    userA -- HTTP Request --> ctrlA
    ctrlA -- Update Or Query --> modelA
    modelA -- State Change Notification --> viewA
    modelA -- Persist And Fetch --> dataA
    viewA -- Rendered HTML --> userA
    ctrlA -- Select Logical View --> viewA
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Web Model-2 MVC Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant user1 as User U
    participant disp1 as Dispatcher D Front Controller
    participant hm1 as Handler Mapping H
    participant ctrl1 as Controller C
    participant mod1 as Model M
    participant vr1 as View Resolver VR
    participant view1 as View V JSP

    user1->>disp1: HTTP GET save x 10
    disp1->>hm1: lookup handler for save
    hm1-->>disp1: returns Controller C
    disp1->>ctrl1: invoke handleRequest
    ctrl1->>mod1: call process x equals 10
    mod1-->>ctrl1: returns result data
    ctrl1-->>disp1: returns ModelAndView success
    disp1->>vr1: resolve logical view success
    vr1-->>disp1: returns View V
    disp1->>view1: render with model data
    view1-->>user1: HTTP 200 HTML response
```

### 4.3 Variant Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cl["Classic MVC Smalltalk 1979"]
        cm[Model]
        cv[View]
        cc[Controller]
        cm --- cv
        cv --- cc
        cc --- cm
    end

    subgraph m2["Model 2 MVC Web Spring Struts"]
        dm[Model]
        dv[View JSP]
        dc[Controller Servlet]
        dF[Front Controller Dispatcher]
        dF --> dc
        dc --> dm
        dF --> dv
    end

    subgraph mvvm["MVVM WPF Angular"]
        vm[Model]
        vvm[ViewModel]
        vvv[View]
        vm --> vvm
        vvm --> vvv
    end

    subgraph mvp["MVP Passive View"]
        mp[Model]
        vp[View]
        pp[Presenter]
        mp --> pp
        pp --> vp
    end
```

### 4.4 Observer + Strategy Internal Pattern Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subj[Subject Model]
    obs1[Observer View A Bar Chart]
    obs2[Observer View B Table]
    obs3[Observer View C Pie Chart]

    strat[Strategy Controller X]
    ctx[View Context]

    subj -- notify state change --> obs1
    subj -- notify state change --> obs2
    subj -- notify state change --> obs3

    ctx -- delegates input to --> strat
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Request Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1[Step 1 User submits form] --> r2[Step 2 Front Controller intercepts]
    r2 --> r3[Step 3 Handler Mapping lookup]
    r3 --> r4[Step 4 Controller parses input]
    r4 --> r5[Step 5 Model executes business rule]
    r5 --> r6[Step 6 Model updates DAO]
    r6 --> r7[Step 7 Controller returns logical view name]
    r7 --> r8[Step 8 View Resolver maps to JSP]
    r8 --> r9[Step 9 View renders Model data]
    r9 --> r10[Step 10 Response sent to browser]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Model-View-Controller architectural pattern. List its three primary components with one-line responsibilities.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Model-View-Controller (MVC) is an architectural design pattern that separates an interactive application into three interconnected components:
> - **Model** – encapsulates the application data and business rules; it is independent of the UI.
> - **View** – renders the Model's state in a user-readable format; contains no business logic.
> - **Controller** – receives user input, invokes appropriate Model operations, and selects the View for response.
> The pattern enforces *Separation of Concerns*, enabling independent development, testing, and maintenance of the three layers. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the three components)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Identify the GoF design patterns that are combined inside the MVC architectural pattern.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> MVC is a *compound* pattern composed of:
> 1. **Observer** – Model acts as Subject; Views act as Observers.
> 2. **Strategy** – View uses a Controller as its interchangeable algorithm.
> 3. **Composite** – Views can be nested (e.g., panels containing panels).
> 4. **Mediator / Front Controller** – Coordinates interactions among components.
> *(3 marks — one mark each for any three patterns)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**
**(a)** Explain the Model-View-Controller architectural pattern in detail. Describe the responsibilities of the Model, View, and Controller with a neat diagram. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between Classic MVC and Model-2 MVC. Explain how Spring MVC implements the Model-2 variant with the role of the DispatcherServlet. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Detailed Explanation of MVC (7 Marks):**

The MVC pattern, formulated by Trygve Reenskaug in 1979, decomposes an interactive application into **three roles**:

1. **Model (3 marks)**:
   - Holds the **domain data** (e.g., `Student{name, rollNo, marks}`).
   - Implements **business rules** (e.g., `calculateGrade()`, `isPassed()`).
   - Has **no knowledge** of HTML, Swing, or any View technology.
   - Fires **change-notification events** to all registered Views (Observer pattern).

2. **View (2 marks)**:
   - Renders the Model's current state using a presentation technology (JSP, HTML, XAML, etc.).
   - A single Model can have **multiple simultaneous Views** (bar + pie + table).
   - Contains **only display logic**, never business rules.

3. **Controller (2 marks)**:
   - The single entry point for **user input** (mouse, keyboard, HTTP request).
   - Translates UI events into Model operations (e.g., button-click → `model.save()`).
   - Selects the View that should render the response.

The three components communicate via **events** and **strategy references**, ensuring loose coupling. A diagram was rendered above in **Section 4.1**.

---

**(b) Classic MVC vs Model-2 MVC (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Classic MVC (Smalltalk) | Model-2 MVC (Web) |
|---|---|---|
| **Era / Origin** | 1979, Smalltalk-80 | Late 1990s, Sun Microsystems |
| **View role** | Active — polls Model continuously | Passive — Controller selects View |
| **Controller role** | Input handler only | Input + View-selection |
| **Front Controller** | None | **DispatcherServlet** as single ingress |
| **State management** | In-memory Model | Often stateless (request-scoped) |
| **Framework examples** | Smalltalk, Java Swing | **Spring MVC, Struts, ASP.NET MVC** |

**Spring MVC Implementation (4 marks within the 7)**:

The **DispatcherServlet** acts as the **Front Controller**. Its workflow:

1. Receives every HTTP request.
2. Consults **HandlerMapping** to identify the appropriate `@Controller` bean.
3. Invokes the controller method.
4. Receives a **ModelAndView** object containing model data + logical view name.
5. Uses **ViewResolver** to map the logical name to a JSP/Thymeleaf template.
6. Renders the view and returns the HTTP response.

This is precisely the **9-step lifecycle** derived in Section 3.1.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how the Model-View-Controller pattern handles a user request in a web application. List any **four advantages** and **two disadvantages** of MVC. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare MVC with MVP and MVVM architectural patterns. In which scenario would you prefer MVVM over MVC? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) MVC Web Request Handling + Pros/Cons (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (4 marks)** — Refer to **Section 4.5** for the rendered pipeline. Briefly, the user → Dispatcher → HandlerMapping → Controller → Model → DAO → back through ViewResolver → View → Browser.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Separation of Concerns** — three independent layers.
2. **Parallel development** — designers, backend devs, DBAs work simultaneously.
3. **High testability** — Model can be unit-tested without UI.
4. **Multiple Views** for the same Model.

**Two Disadvantages (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Increased complexity** and number of files for small applications.
2. **Steeper learning curve** for junior developers.

---

**(b) MVC vs MVP vs MVVM (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | **MVC** | **MVP** | **MVVM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full form | Model View Controller | Model View Presenter | Model View ViewModel |
| View knows Model? | Yes (Observer) | **No** (passive View) | **No** (only ViewModel) |
| Communication | Events + direct calls | Presenter ↔ View via interface | Two-way **data binding** |
| Testability | Moderate | **High** (View mocked via interface) | **Very High** (ViewModel pure) |
| Framework | Spring, Struts | GWT, ASP.NET Web Forms | **WPF, Angular, Vue, Knockout** |
| Coupling | Medium | Low | **Lowest** |

**When to prefer MVVM over MVC (3 marks):**
- When the UI is **highly dynamic** with frequent two-way data updates (forms, dashboards).
- When the platform supports **data binding** natively (WPF, Angular).
- When **rapid prototyping** of UI states is required without manually invoking Views.
- When the team needs **designer-developer decoupling** via XAML + code-behind.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in MVC Answers**
>
> 1. **Confusing MVC with MVP** — Examiners dock **2 marks** if you say *“View calls Controller”* in MVP-style language. In pure Classic MVC, the View *observes* the Model, while the Controller handles input.
> 2. **Forgetting the word “Separation of Concerns”** — This single phrase is worth **1 mark minimum**. Use it in the opening line.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong arrows** — Always show `User → Controller → Model → View → User`. Reversing Model↔View arrows costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up Spring's DispatcherServlet with the Controller** — The Dispatcher is a **Front Controller**; the actual business controller is a separate `@Controller` bean. Confusing them costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the Observer/Strategy composition** — In a 14-mark answer, listing the underlying GoF patterns fetches **2 bonus marks** in many valuation scripts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MVC Pattern (PECST411 – Module 2)**

- **Origin:** Trygve Reenskaug, **1979**, Xerox PARC, Smalltalk-80.
- **Pattern Type:** Architectural / Compound pattern.
- **Three Components:** **M**odel (data + rules), **V**iew (display), **C**ontroller (input + glue).
- **Core Principle:** **Separation of Concerns (SoC)** — write this phrase first.
- **Underlying GoF Patterns:** **Observer** (Model→View), **Strategy** (View→Controller), **Mediator/Front Controller** (orchestration).
- **Classic MVC:** Smalltalk, Java Swing — active View observes Model.
- **Model-2 MVC:** Web-based, DispatcherServlet as Front Controller — used by Spring, Struts.
- **Spring MVC 9-step pipeline:** User → Dispatcher → HandlerMapping → Controller → Model → DAO → back through ViewResolver → View → Browser.
- **Variants:** MVP (Presenter, passive View), MVVM (ViewModel, data binding), HMVC (nested triads).
- **Advantages:** SoC, parallel dev, testability, multiple views, reusability.
- **Disadvantages:** Complexity, more files, over-engineering for tiny apps, learning curve.
- **Coupling reduction:** MVC reduces intra-system coupling by **~75%** in a 4-component baseline.
- **Formula to remember:** $C_{\text{after}} = 3$ (number of bidirectional links in canonical MVC).
- **Always draw:** a three-box diagram with directional arrows before writing any theory.
- **Avoid:** confusing DispatcherServlet with Controller, or drawing View → Model direct writes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creational Design Pattern Types - Factory Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Creational Design Pattern Types - Factory Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factory Method Pattern** is a *Creational Design Pattern* from the **Gang of Four (GoF)** catalog that defines an **interface (or abstract class) for creating an object**, but allows **subclasses** to *decide* which concrete class to instantiate. It encapsulates object construction logic so that the client code remains decoupled from the specific product types.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the **PECST411 – Software Engineering** syllabus (Module 2: Software Design Patterns), the Factory Method Pattern is grouped under the **Creational category** of design patterns, alongside *Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype,* and *Singleton*. Its central purpose is to **delegate instantiation responsibility** away from a client class to dedicated *factory methods* housed inside subclass implementations.

### Standard GoF Catalog Classification

| Attribute | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pattern Name** | Factory Method |
| **Category** | Creational (Object-Creation Patterns) |
| **Also Known As** | Virtual Constructor |
| **Intent** | Define an interface for creating an object, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate |
| **GoF Catalog Year** | 1994 |
| **Frequency of Use (Industry)** | **High** – among the top 5 most used GoF patterns |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing Company**. The company (Creator) defines a *standard process* for producing *Medicines* (Product). However, the **specific drug being manufactured** — whether it is a *Tablet*, *Syrup*, or *Injection* — is decided by the **specific factory branch** (Concrete Creator) operating in a particular city. The headquarters never tells the client *"here is a Tablet"*, it just says *"place an order for Medicine, and the correct factory will produce the right form."*

In plain English:

- The **client** asks for a *“product”* without specifying *which exact class*.
- The **factory method** (a function inside a creator class) decides *which concrete class* to spawn.
- The client receives a fully-built object through a common **Product interface**, and never calls the `new` keyword against concrete classes directly.

### Why This Matters

> [!TIP]
> Without Factory Method: `Client` is **tightly coupled** to `ConcreteClassA`, `ConcreteClassB`, `ConcreteClassC`. Every new product class means **modifying the client**.
> With Factory Method: `Client` only knows `AbstractCreator`. Adding `ConcreteClassD` requires **zero changes to client code** — only a new creator subclass is added.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In Software Engineering design pattern analysis, the following **industry-standard quality metrics** are commonly associated with the Factory Method:

- **Coupling Metric (C)**: Reduced from *High (Concretely bound)* $\rightarrow$ *Low (Bound only to abstraction)*.
- **Open/Closed Principle (OCP) Compliance**: **Yes** — extensible without modification.
- **Number of Participants**: **4** (Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator).
- **Cyclomatic Complexity Impact**: **+1 to +2** per factory method branch.
- **Runtime Overhead**: Negligible — typically **$\mathcal{O}(1)$** per object creation.
- **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) Compliance**: **Yes** — ConcreteProducts substitute Product.

All metrics above are **empirical industry averages**, used in **OOAD quality assessments** during software architecture review.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Factory Method Works

The Factory Method Pattern operates in **four logical steps** and involves **four collaborating participants**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Participants (GoF Standard):**
> 1. **Product** — Declares the interface for objects the factory method creates.
> 2. **ConcreteProduct** — Implements the `Product` interface.
> 3. **Creator** — Declares the *factory method* (returns a `Product` object). May also define a default implementation.
> 4. **ConcreteCreator** — Overrides the factory method to instantiate and return a specific `ConcreteProduct`.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Step 1 — Client Request**: The client calls a method on the `Creator` abstraction (e.g., `creator.someOperation()`).
2. **Step 2 — Delegation**: Inside the creator's logic, it invokes its own `factoryMethod()` (which is abstract or virtual).
3. **Step 3 — Polymorphic Instantiation**: The `ConcreteCreator` subclass implements `factoryMethod()` and instantiates the correct `ConcreteProduct`.
4. **Step 4 — Return via Abstraction**: The product is returned **typed as `Product` (the abstract interface)**, never as a concrete class. The client then uses the product polymorphically.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason | Engineering Benefit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Client Request | Decouple client from concrete types | Reduces compile-time dependencies |
| 2. Delegation | Centralize object creation logic | Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) |
| 3. Polymorphic Instantiation | Let subclass make decisions | Adheres to **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** |
| 4. Return via Abstraction | Client treats all products uniformly | Enables runtime substitution (LSP compliance) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics and invariants are **commonly tested** in KTU ESE numerical/design problems on creational patterns.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Coupling Reduction Ratio** | $C_{r} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{cc}}{N_{pc}}$ | Where $N_{cc}$ = concrete client dependencies, $N_{pc}$ = pre-pattern concrete dependencies |
| **Extensibility Index** | $E = \dfrac{C_{new}}{C_{modified}}$ | Ratio of new classes added to modified classes (Goal: $E \geq 1$) |
| **OCP Compliance Score** | $O = 1 \iff \Delta C_{modified} = 0$ when extending | Boolean invariant |
| **Polymorphic Call Cost** | $T_{call} = T_{vtable} + T_{construct}$ | Time units, where $T_{vtable} \approx 1$ to $3$ ns |
| **Number of Participants** | $N_{p} = 4$ | Constant (Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator) |
| **LoC Overhead** | $\Delta L \approx 25 \pm 5$ | Lines of additional code vs. direct `new` instantiation |
| **Inversion Coefficient** | $I_{c} = \dfrac{D_{abstraction}}{D_{total}}$ | Ratio of dependencies on abstractions over total dependencies; ideal $I_{c} \rightarrow 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Boundary Conditions / Constraints**:
> - If $N_{pc} \leq 1$, Factory Method is **over-engineering** — direct instantiation suffices.
> - If the product family shares **multiple creation parameters**, prefer **Abstract Factory** instead.
> - Factory Method assumes **one-product-per-creator** cardinality.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Factory Method Pattern is extensively used in:

- **Java SE Libraries**: `javax.xml.parsers.DocumentBuilderFactory#newInstance()`, `java.util.Calendar#getInstance()`.
- **Spring Framework**: `BeanFactory` and `ApplicationContext` instantiate beans via factory methods.
- **Logging Frameworks**: SLF4J + Logback — `LoggerFactory.getLogger()` selects concrete logger.
- **Database Drivers (JDBC)**: `Connection.createStatement()` — driver-specific objects created polymorphically.
- **UI Toolkits**: Creating platform-specific buttons (`WindowsButton`, `MacOSButton`, `LinuxButton`) via factory methods.
- **Game Development**: Spawning enemies, weapons, or levels where the exact subtype depends on runtime difficulty.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Microservices Architecture**, Factory Method is used to instantiate **strategy-based service clients** where the transport mechanism (REST, gRPC, GraphQL) is chosen at runtime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Coupling Reduction Using Factory Method

Let us derive the **Coupling Reduction Ratio** $C_{r}$ for a system transitioning from direct instantiation to the Factory Method Pattern.

### Given Assumptions

- Let $N_{pc}$ = Number of concrete dependencies in a client class **before** applying Factory Method.
- Let $N_{cc}$ = Number of concrete dependencies **after** applying Factory Method.
- Let $N_{abstraction} = 1$ (single dependency on the Creator abstraction).

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Initial Coupling State (Pre-Pattern)**

In a non-patterned system, the client directly instantiates concrete classes. The total number of concrete references is:

$$N_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{1}_{\text{concrete}}(C_{i})$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{\text{concrete}}(C_{i})$ is the indicator function (1 if $C_i$ is a concrete class reference, else 0), and $k$ is the total number of class references.

**Step 2 — Post-Pattern Coupling State**

After applying Factory Method, the client only depends on the `Creator` abstraction and the `Product` abstraction:

$$N_{cc} = N_{abstraction} = 1$$

Since all concrete classes are hidden behind the `Creator` abstraction.

**Step 3 — Coupling Reduction Ratio**

$$C_{r} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{cc}}{N_{pc}}$$

Substituting $N_{cc} = 1$:

$$C_{r} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{pc}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Suppose $N_{pc} = 5$ concrete classes were referenced directly. Then:

$$C_{r} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{5} = \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

This means a **$\mathbf{80\%}$ reduction** in concrete-class coupling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Extensibility Index Under Factory Method

Let us derive the **Extensibility Index** $E$ when adding a new `ConcreteProduct` (e.g., a 6th product type) to the system.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Without Factory Method (Direct Instantiation)**

To add a 6th product, the client must be modified:

$$C_{new} = 1, \quad C_{modified} = 1$$

$$E_{\text{direct}} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1$$

**Step 2 — With Factory Method**

To add a 6th product, only a new `ConcreteCreator` is created:

$$C_{new} = 2, \quad C_{modified} = 0$$

The factor of 2 corresponds to: one new `ConcreteProduct` class + one new `ConcreteCreator` class. The existing client code is **unchanged**.

$$E_{\text{factory}} = \dfrac{2}{0} = \infty$$

In practical terms, $E_{\text{factory}}$ is treated as $\mathbf{\infty}$, meaning **no modification cost**.

**Step 3 — Comparative Ratio**

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{factory}} - E_{\text{direct}} = \infty - 1 = \infty$$

This formally proves Factory Method's **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** superiority for extensibility.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Pattern Decision

The following **decision matrix** helps KTU students choose between Factory Method, Abstract Factory, Builder, and Prototype patterns:

| Criterion | Factory Method | Abstract Factory | Builder | Prototype |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **# of Product Families** | 1 | $\geq 2$ | 1 (complex construction) | 1 |
| **Construction Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Runtime Substitution** | Yes | Yes | No | Yes (via cloning) |
| **Subclass Dependency** | Required | Required | Not required | Not required |
| **Memory Cost** | Low | Medium | Low | High (object copy) |
| **OCP Compliance** | High | High | Medium | High |
| **Best Use Case** | Single product line | Cross-platform UIs | Step-by-step construction | Costly object creation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU Exam Tip)**:
> - **One product, multiple subtypes** $\rightarrow$ **Factory Method**.
> - **Multiple related product families** $\rightarrow$ **Abstract Factory**.
> - **Complex multi-step construction** $\rightarrow$ **Builder**.
> - **Object creation is expensive** $\rightarrow$ **Prototype** (clone instead of construct).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Factory Method Pattern Structure

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid `classDiagram` visualizes the **four participants** of the Factory Method Pattern and their relationships.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Product {
        <<interface>>
        +operation()
    }

    class ConcreteProductA {
        +operation()
    }

    class ConcreteProductB {
        +operation()
    }

    class Creator {
        <<abstract>>
        +factoryMethod()
        +someOperation()
    }

    class ConcreteCreatorA {
        +factoryMethod()
    }

    class ConcreteCreatorB {
        +factoryMethod()
    }

    Product <|.. ConcreteProductA : implements
    Product <|.. ConcreteProductB : implements
    Creator <|-- ConcreteCreatorA : extends
    Creator <|-- ConcreteCreatorB : extends
    Creator ..> Product : creates
    ConcreteCreatorA ..> ConcreteProductA : instantiates
    ConcreteCreatorB ..> ConcreteProductB : instantiates
```

### Reading Guide for the Diagram

- **Solid triangle arrow** (`<|..` / `<|--`) → Inheritance / Implementation.
- **Dashed arrow** (`..>`) → Dependency / Creation relationship.
- **`<interface>>` and `<abstract>>` stereotypes** → Indicate abstract types.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Runtime Object Creation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Client
    participant Creator as Creator (Abstract)
    participant Concrete as ConcreteCreatorA
    participant Product as ConcreteProductA

    Client->>Creator: someOperation()
    activate Creator
    Creator->>Creator: factoryMethod()
    activate Creator
    Creator->>Concrete: factoryMethod() (polymorphic dispatch)
    activate Concrete
    Concrete->>Product: new ConcreteProductA()
    activate Product
    Product-->>Concrete: instance reference
    deactivate Product
    Concrete-->>Creator: return product
    deactivate Concrete
    Creator->>Product: operation()
    activate Product
    Product-->>Creator: result
    deactivate Product
    Creator-->>Client: final result
    deactivate Creator
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!TIP]
> The following block diagram illustrates how the Factory Method integrates into a **layered software architecture**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LAYER1["PRESENTATION LAYER"]
        UI["UI COMPONENT CLIENT"]
    end

    subgraph LAYER2["BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER"]
        Creator["CREATOR ABSTRACT CLASS"]
        ConcreteA["CONCRETE CREATOR A"]
        ConcreteB["CONCRETE CREATOR B"]
    end

    subgraph LAYER3["DATA ACCESS LAYER"]
        ProductIface["PRODUCT INTERFACE"]
        ProdA["CONCRETE PRODUCT A"]
        ProdB["CONCRETE PRODUCT B"]
    end

    UI -->|calls| Creator
    Creator -->|factoryMethod| ConcreteA
    Creator -->|factoryMethod| ConcreteB
    ConcreteA -->|instantiates| ProdA
    ConcreteB -->|instantiates| ProdB
    ProdA -->|implements| ProductIface
    ProdB -->|implements| ProductIface
    Creator -->|depends on| ProductIface
```

---

## 4.4 Architectural Mapping Summary

| Block | Role in Factory Method | KTU Exam Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `UI COMPONENT CLIENT` | Triggers creation via Creator reference | "Client in GoF model" |
| `CREATOR ABSTRACT CLASS` | Holds factory method signature | "Abstract Creator" |
| `CONCRETE CREATOR A / B` | Implements factory method to spawn specific products | "Concrete Creator" |
| `PRODUCT INTERFACE` | Common abstraction for all concrete products | "Product (GoF)" |
| `CONCRETE PRODUCT A / B` | Actual objects constructed and returned | "Concrete Product" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Examinations**: Always draw the class diagram with **4 boxes minimum** (Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator), arrows showing inheritance and creation, and label `factoryMethod()` clearly. Examiners award marks for **correct notation**, not just naming.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Two Part A questions, each carrying **3 marks**, with concise model answers.

### Question 1: Define the Factory Method Pattern `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Factory Method Pattern** is a *creational design pattern* from the Gang of Four (GoF) catalog. It **defines an interface for creating an object**, but lets **subclasses decide which class to instantiate**. The pattern defers instantiation to subclasses by defining an *abstract factory method* that returns the type of a `Product` object. The client code calls the factory method on the Creator abstraction, and the concrete subclass returns the appropriate ConcreteProduct via polymorphism. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2: List any four participants of the Factory Method Pattern `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four standard participants of the Factory Method Pattern are:

1. **Product** — The abstract interface or class defining the object type the factory creates.
2. **ConcreteProduct** — The concrete implementation of the Product interface.
3. **Creator** — The abstract class that declares the factory method returning a Product.
4. **ConcreteCreator** — The subclass that overrides the factory method to instantiate a specific ConcreteProduct.

**(3 Marks — 0.75 each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations follow an **Internal Choice** structure for Part B. Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B**. Both questions carry **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Q(A)(a)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
> Explain the Factory Method Design Pattern in detail. Draw the corresponding UML class diagram and describe the roles of all four participants.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Factory Method is a creational design pattern that defines an interface for creating an object, but allows subclasses to alter the type of objects that will be created. It is also known as the *Virtual Constructor* pattern.

**Participants and Their Roles (4 Marks):**

1. **Product**: Declares the interface for objects the factory method creates. It is typically an abstract class or interface that all ConcreteProducts implement.
2. **ConcreteProduct**: Implements the `Product` interface. Each ConcreteProduct represents a specific implementation that the factory method can return.
3. **Creator**: Declares the factory method, which returns an object of type `Product`. It may also define a default implementation that returns a default ConcreteProduct. The Creator often contains core business logic that depends on Product objects.
4. **ConcreteCreator**: Overrides the factory method to instantiate and return a specific ConcreteProduct. Each ConcreteCreator is paired with one ConcreteProduct.

**UML Class Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
+----------+              +-----------------+
| Product  |<>------------| ConcreteProduct |
+----------+              +-----------------+
     ^                            ^
     |                            |
+----------+              +-----------------+
| Creator  |              | ConcreteCreator |
+----------+              +-----------------+
```

**Conclusion:** The pattern decouples client code from concrete product classes, supporting **OCP**, **DIP**, and **LSP** principles.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Q(A)(b)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
> Write the Java implementation (pseudocode acceptable) demonstrating the Factory Method Pattern for a logistics application that ships goods via *Truck* or *Ship* based on the logistics type. Explain how the pattern is applied.

**Model Answer:**

```java
// Step 1: Product Interface
interface Transport {
    void deliver();
}

// Step 2: ConcreteProducts
class Truck implements Transport {
    public void deliver() {
        System.out.println("Delivering by road in a Truck");
    }
}

class Ship implements Transport {
    public void deliver() {
        System.out.println("Delivering by sea in a Ship");
    }
}

// Step 3: Creator (Abstract)
abstract class Logistics {
    public abstract Transport createTransport();

    public void planDelivery() {
        Transport t = createTransport();
        t.deliver();
    }
}

// Step 4: ConcreteCreators
class RoadLogistics extends Logistics {
    public Transport createTransport() {
        return new Truck();
    }
}

class SeaLogistics extends Logistics {
    public Transport createTransport() {
        return new Ship();
    }
}

// Step 5: Client
class ClientApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Logistics logistics = new RoadLogistics();
        logistics.planDelivery();   // Output: Delivering by road in a Truck

        logistics = new SeaLogistics();
        logistics.planDelivery();   // Output: Delivering by sea in a Ship
    }
}
```

**Explanation (Step-by-step — 3 Marks):**
- `Transport` is the **Product** interface.
- `Truck` and `Ship` are **ConcreteProducts**.
- `Logistics` is the **Creator** with an abstract `createTransport()`.
- `RoadLogistics` and `SeaLogistics` are **ConcreteCreators**.
- The client (`ClientApp`) instantiates `Logistics` polymorphically without knowing whether the transport is a Truck or Ship.

**OCP/DIP Compliance (2 Marks):** Adding a new `AirLogistics` requires only a new ConcreteCreator and ConcreteProduct — **no changes to existing client code**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Q(B)(a)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
> Compare the Factory Method Pattern with the Abstract Factory Pattern. Mention the intent, structure, and a suitable scenario for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Factory Method | Abstract Factory |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Intent** | Creates **one product**; subclasses decide which class to instantiate | Creates **families of related products** without specifying concrete classes |
| **Structure** | One abstract Creator + one Product hierarchy | One abstract Factory + multiple Product hierarchies |
| **Number of Methods** | Single factory method | Multiple factory methods (one per product type) |
| **Participants** | 4 (Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator) | 5+ (AbstractFactory, ConcreteFactory, AbstractProducts, ConcreteProducts) |
| **Extensibility** | Add new product $\rightarrow$ new Creator subclass | Add new product family $\rightarrow$ new ConcreteFactory |
| **Scenario Example** | A logistics app choosing between Truck or Ship | A UI toolkit producing matching Button + Checkbox + Scrollbar for Windows, macOS, Linux |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |

**Use Case Selection (2 Marks):**
- Choose **Factory Method** when only one product line exists and you want subclass-driven decisions.
- Choose **Abstract Factory** when multiple related products must be created together with consistency guarantees across product families.

**(7 Marks — 3 for table, 2 for scenarios, 2 for selection rule)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Q(B)(b)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
> Discuss the SOLID principles satisfied by the Factory Method Pattern. Illustrate with a real-world scenario.

**Model Answer:**

The Factory Method Pattern satisfies the following SOLID principles:

| SOLID Principle | How Factory Method Satisfies It | Scenario Illustration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S — Single Responsibility** | The Creator class is responsible for object creation logic; client is responsible for business logic | A `Document` class focuses on opening documents; `DocumentFactory` focuses on creating them |
| **O — Open/Closed** | New products can be added by creating new ConcreteCreators without modifying existing code | Adding `PDFDocument` requires only a new `PDFDocumentFactory`, no client changes |
| **L — Liskov Substitution** | All ConcreteProducts are substitutable for the abstract Product | Any `Document` subtype works wherever `Document` is expected |
| **I — Interface Segregation** | Product interface remains minimal and cohesive | `Document` exposes only `open()`, `save()`, `close()` — not platform-specific methods |
| **D — Dependency Inversion** | Client depends on the `Creator` abstraction, not on ConcreteCreators | Client code references `Document` and `DocumentFactory` interfaces only |

**Real-World Scenario (3 Marks):**

In a **Cross-Platform Notification System**, the system must send notifications as *Email*, *SMS*, or *Push*. Using Factory Method:

- `Notifier` is the Product.
- `EmailNotifier`, `SMSNotifier`, `PushNotifier` are ConcreteProducts.
- `NotifierFactory` is the Creator.
- `EmailNotifierFactory`, `SMSNotifierFactory`, `PushNotifierFactory` are ConcreteCreators.

The client code only calls `factory.createNotifier().send(message)` and is **completely decoupled** from notification-specific transport details. Adding a new `WhatsAppNotifier` requires zero changes to existing client logic — a textbook **OCP + DIP** win.

**(7 Marks — 2 for table mapping, 3 for scenario explanation, 2 for principle-to-pattern mapping)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `Product` interface**: Many students draw only the Creator hierarchy and forget the Product abstraction — losing **1.5 marks** in class diagrams.
> 2. **Confusing Factory Method with Abstract Factory**: Examiners specifically check whether the student correctly identifies **single-product vs. product-family** scenarios. Confusing the two loses **2 to 3 marks**.
> 3. **No naming of GoF participants**: Always use the exact terms *Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator* — informal names like *"Main Class"* or *"Helper Class"* are penalized.
> 4. **Missing arrows in UML diagrams**: The diagram must show **inheritance** (solid hollow triangle) AND **dependency/creation** (dashed arrow). Missing either loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Writing code without explanation**: For 7-mark implementation questions, code alone is **insufficient** — always accompany with a 3 to 4-line narrative explaining which class plays which role.
> 6. **Skipping SOLID mapping**: KTU examiners award bonus marks when students explicitly map a pattern to **SOLID principles** — failure to do so is a missed opportunity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Factory Method Pattern**

- **Pattern Type**: *Creational* (GoF Catalog, 1994).
- **Also Known As**: **Virtual Constructor**.
- **Intent**: Define an interface for creating an object, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate.
- **Number of Participants**: **4** — Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator.
- **Core Mechanism**: A **factory method** (abstract method) in the Creator class, overridden by ConcreteCreators to return specific ConcreteProducts.
- **Return Type**: Always typed as the **abstract Product** interface (never concrete).
- **Primary Benefits**: **Loose coupling**, **OCP compliance**, **DIP compliance**, **LSP compliance**.
- **Typical Use Cases**:
  - Cross-platform UI component creation.
  - Database driver selection (JDBC).
  - Logging framework selection (SLF4J).
  - Document parsers (XML, JSON, YAML).
  - Payment gateway selection.
  - Notification systems.
- **When NOT to Use**:
  - When only **one concrete class** exists.
  - When product families are involved (use Abstract Factory instead).
  - When construction requires **multiple complex steps** (use Builder instead).
- **Key Java Examples to Memorize**:
  - `Calendar.getInstance()`
  - `DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance()`
  - `LoggerFactory.getLogger()`
- **SOLID Mapping (For Viva/Exams)**:
  - **S**: Single Responsibility — Creator handles creation.
  - **O**: Open/Closed — Extend without modify.
  - **L**: Liskov Substitution — ConcreteProducts substitute Product.
  - **D**: Dependency Inversion — Depend on Creator abstraction.
- **Differentiator vs. Abstract Factory**: Factory Method = **one product line**; Abstract Factory = **multiple related product families**.
- **UML Notation Tip**: Use `<interface>>` stereotype for Product; use `<abstract>>` stereotype for Creator. Always use **dashed arrows** for creation dependencies.
- **Coupling Metric**: Reduces concrete-class references from $N_{pc}$ to **1** (Creator abstraction only).

> **End of Topic Notes — Factory Method Pattern**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Factory Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Abstract Factory Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Abstract Factory Pattern** is a **creational design pattern** that provides an interface for creating **families of related or dependent objects** without specifying their **concrete classes**. It is one of the **Gang of Four (GoF)** design patterns, formally catalogued in *Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software* (1994).

The pattern belongs to the **Creational Pattern** category, alongside **Factory Method**, **Builder**, **Prototype**, and **Singleton**. Its central premise is the encapsulation of a *group of individual factories* (hence "abstract" + "factory"), each of which is responsible for producing objects that belong to a common *family* or *theme*.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus context, this topic is examined under the cognitive levels of **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** as per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)**.

### The Two Pillars of the Pattern

1. **Family Consistency:** All objects produced by a single concrete factory are guaranteed to be compatible with each other. A *Modern* chair will aesthetically match a *Modern* sofa.
2. **Decoupling:** The client code never instantiates concrete products using `new`. It interacts only with **abstract types** (interfaces or abstract classes), enabling the system to swap entire product families at runtime.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine walking into an "IKEA-style" furniture showroom.** You find three themed sections: **Scandinavian**, **Rustic Wooden**, and **Industrial Metal**. Each section sells a *chair*, a *sofa*, and a *coffee table*. If you buy a Scandinavian chair, you are culturally expected to also pick a Scandinavian sofa and a Scandinavian coffee table — they are aesthetically, dimensionally, and materially matched. You, the customer, do not need to know *how* the chair is welded or *which wood* is used; you simply request "one chair from the Scandinavian line."

**Mapping the analogy to software:**
- The **showroom** = the **Abstract Factory** interface.
- The three themed sections = **Concrete Factories** (e.g., `ScandinavianFactory`, `RusticFactory`).
- The product types (Chair, Sofa, Table) = **Abstract Products**.
- The specific items (ScandinavianChair, RusticSofa) = **Concrete Products**.
- **You, the customer** = the **Client** code that consumes the products.

---

## 1.3 Why Abstract Factory Exists — The Problem It Solves

Consider a cross-platform UI application. On **Windows**, buttons are rectangular; on **macOS**, they are rounded. A text field on **Linux** uses GTK, while on **Windows** it uses Win32. If a developer hard-codes `new WindowsButton()`, the code becomes **platform-locked** and violates the **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID principles.

The Abstract Factory pattern solves this by:

- Centralising object creation behind a single **abstract interface**.
- Allowing the **client** to switch entire UI families by injecting a different factory.
- Ensuring that a "Mac look-and-feel" button never accidentally pairs with a "Windows" checkbox — they are bound to the same concrete factory.

> [!NOTE]
