3. **Continuous deployment** culture in web development matches the iterative nature of prototyping.
4. Requirements evolve frequently due to changing user trends — prototyping accommodates this.

**Extreme Prototyping:**

Extreme Prototyping is a specialized prototyping methodology used primarily for **web applications**. It consists of three sequential stages:

1. **Static Prototype (HTML Mockup):** A set of static HTML pages is created that represent the screens of the final application. There is no functionality — only the look and feel.
2. **Simulated Prototype (Interactive Screens):** JavaScript or similar client-side scripting is added to simulate navigation, form submissions, and user interactions. Data is faked or hardcoded; no real database exists.
3. **Fully Functional Prototype (Services Layer):** Real backend services, databases, authentication, and business logic are implemented. The simulated screens are connected to the live backend, completing the system.

> *(Distribution: 2 marks for suitability reasons + 5 marks for Extreme Prototyping explanation = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Defining Prototype as "a small version of the software"** — This is incomplete. A prototype is a *working model* built for *learning and feedback*. Always mention *iteration* and *user feedback*.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the feedback loop arrow** — Examiners explicitly check for the cyclic arrow from User Evaluation back to Design. Without it, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Incremental and Evolutionary Prototyping** — Evolutionary = one prototype that grows. Incremental = multiple prototypes combined.
> 4. **Omitting the word "Throwaway"** — In Throwaway Prototyping questions, students often forget to state that the prototype is *discarded*.
> 5. **Mixing up Spiral and Prototype** — Spiral emphasizes *risk analysis* at every cycle. Prototype emphasizes *requirement clarification*. Never interchange.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — A textual answer without a flowchart loses 2 marks easily in Part B.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Prototype Model** = iterative SDLC model focused on building a working model early for user feedback.
- [x] **Four phases:** Requirements → Quick Design → Build → User Evaluation (looped).
- [x] **Most useful when** requirements are unclear, user is non-technical, or project is UI-driven.
- [x] **Four types:** Throwaway, Evolutionary, Incremental, Extreme.
- [x] **Throwaway prototyping** = prototype is discarded; real system built from scratch.
- [x] **Evolutionary prototyping** = prototype grows into the final system.
- [x] **Extreme prototyping** = used for web apps; three stages (static → simulated → functional).
- [x] **Main advantage** = early and continuous user involvement reduces risk.
- [x] **Main disadvantage** = scope creep and risk of poor internal architecture.
- [x] **Always draw** the feedback loop arrow in your diagram — it is the visual signature of this model.
- [x] **Convergence condition:** $\delta_n = \delta_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)^n \leq \epsilon$ — number of iterations $n = \ln(\epsilon/\delta_0) / \ln(1 - \alpha)$.
- [x] **Avoid confusion:** Spiral ≠ Prototype; Prototype ≠ Incremental Model.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Builder Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Builder Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Builder Pattern (Gang of Four, Creational Pattern)**
> The **Builder Method** is a creational design pattern that separates the **construction of a complex object** from its **representation**, allowing the same construction process to create different representations. It encapsulates object creation logic by using a step-by-step approach, with a **Director** orchestrating the construction sequence and a **Builder** providing the interface for assembling individual parts.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for PECST411, this topic falls under **Module 2 – Software Design**, specifically within the sub-unit dealing with **Design Patterns**. The Builder Method is officially catalogued as one of the **five (5) Creational Design Patterns** from the GoF (Gang of Four) catalogue, alongside Abstract Factory, Factory Method, Prototype, and Singleton.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Product** | The complex object that is being constructed (e.g., a House, a Computer, a Meal). |
| **Builder (Abstract)** | An interface or abstract class that declares the construction steps (e.g., `buildWalls()`, `buildRoof()`). |
| **Concrete Builder** | The specific implementation that constructs and assembles parts of the product, defining and keeping track of the representation. |
| **Director** | The class that orchestrates the construction by calling builder methods in a specific order. |
| **Client** | The module that instantiates the Director and the Concrete Builder to obtain a product. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Custom Burger" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Ordering a Custom Burger at a Restaurant**
> Imagine walking into a fast-food chain. You do not say *"Give me a burger"*; instead, you interact with a **cashier (Director)** who knows the recipe. Behind the counter, a **chef (Builder)** assembles the burger step by step: bottom bun → patty → cheese → lettuce → sauce → top bun. If you want a *Veg Burger*, the same Director and the same step-by-step process is used, but the **Concrete Chef (ConcreteBuilder)** picks different ingredients. If you want a *Chicken Burger*, you reuse the **same process** but a **different Concrete Chef**.

This analogy maps directly to the Builder Pattern:
- **You (Client)** = Final consumer
- **Cashier (Director)** = The class that drives the build sequence
- **Recipe (Builder interface)** = Abstract construction steps
- **Different Chefs (ConcreteBuilder)** = Different representations of the final product

## 1.3 Why Was the Builder Pattern Invented?

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Problem Solved:**
> When a class has **too many constructor parameters** (e.g., 10+ optional parameters), constructors become unwieldy (the **Telescoping Constructor Anti-Pattern**). The Builder Pattern solves this by allowing you to set only the parameters you care about, step by step, and produce the final object with a single `build()` call.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this pattern is also covered as a solution to the **"Object Construction Complexity"** problem encountered during the transition from **Analysis to Design** phases of the SDLC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Builder Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Intent (GoF Original):**
> *"Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation so that the same construction process can create different representations."* — Erich Gamma et al.

The pattern is engineered to address **four (4) explicit software design problems** identified in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Constructor Explosion:** A class with 10 optional fields would require $2^{10} = 1024$ constructors.
2. **Inconsistent State Risk:** Telescoping constructors often leave objects in a partially initialized state.
3. **Immutability Violation:** Java/C++ style setters break immutability.
4. **Parameter Confusion:** Calling `new Car("Red", "V8", true, false)` is unreadable; named setters like `setColor("Red")` are self-documenting.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Builder Pattern operates through **four (4) participant roles** executing **five (5) sequential phases**:

| Phase | Role | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Instantiate** | Client | Creates a `ConcreteBuilder` object. |
| **2. Instantiate** | Client | Creates a `Director`, passing the builder. |
| **3. Construct** | Director | Invokes builder methods in a fixed sequence. |
| **4. Assemble** | ConcreteBuilder | Builds and aggregates the parts internally. |
| **5. Retrieve** | Client | Calls `getResult()` to obtain the final immutable `Product`. |

## 2.3 Formal UML Participants (KTU Board Exam Standard)

The **Kerala University B.Tech valuation scheme** expects students to remember the following **4 cardinal participants** by name and role:

| Participant | Type | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| `Builder` | Abstract Class/Interface | Declares all construction steps (`buildPartA`, `buildPartB`). |
| `ConcreteBuilder` | Concrete Class | Implements the steps; maintains the internal representation; provides `getResult()`. |
| `Director` | Concrete Class | Knows the construction algorithm; calls builder steps in a strict order. |
| `Product` | Concrete Class | The complex object under construction; typically immutable post-`build()`. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Builder Pattern at a Glance (Exam-Ready Table)**

| Aspect | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Pattern Category** | Creational (GoF) |
| **Number of Participants** | 4 (Builder, ConcreteBuilder, Director, Product) |
| **Pattern Intent** | Encapsulate step-by-step construction |
| **Use When** | Object has many optional/configurable parts |
| **Avoid When** | Object has 2–3 simple parameters |
| **Key Benefit** | Single Responsibility Principle + Immutability |
| **Key Drawback** | Increased number of classes; verbose code |
| **Related Patterns** | Abstract Factory (both build complex objects), Composite (recursive building) |
| **JDK Example** | `java.lang.StringBuilder`, `java.util.stream.Stream.Builder`, `ProcessBuilder` |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Beyond Software

The Builder pattern's philosophy is used in:
- **Test Data Builders** in automated testing frameworks.
- **SQL Query Builders** (e.g., `QueryBuilder` in Hibernate Criteria API).
- **Configuration Object Construction** in frameworks like Spring (`HttpClient.newBuilder()`).
- **Document Object Construction** in PDF/HTML generation libraries (e.g., iText PDFBuilder).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Construction Logic Walkthrough — A Java-Based Analytical Matrix**
> The following analytical matrix deconstructs the **House Building Example**, which is the canonical KTU textbook illustration. Every transition is explicitly explained, with no skipped steps.

## 3.1 The Product Class

The **Product** is the *raw material* the builder will eventually create. It is typically a **plain data holder** (POJO — Plain Old Java Object).

$$
\text{Product} = \{ \text{parts} \mid \text{parts} = \langle p_1, p_2, p_3, \dots, p_n \rangle \}
$$

In this model, the Product `House` contains a set of `parts` (walls, roof, doors, windows). The list grows as the builder adds parts.

## 3.2 The Abstract Builder

The **Abstract Builder** is the **contract** that every concrete builder must honor. It declares the construction steps but does not implement them.

$$
\forall \text{ method } m \in \text{Builder} : m \text{ is abstract} \lor m \text{ returns Builder (for chaining)}
$$

The abstract builder in this case is `HouseBuilder`, declaring four (4) abstract steps: `buildWalls()`, `buildRoof()`, `buildDoors()`, `buildWindows()`. It also declares the `getResult()` method, which is *concrete* in most implementations, not abstract, since the return type is known (`House`).

## 3.3 The Concrete Builder — Deriving the Internal State

The **Concrete Builder** is where the *actual* construction logic lives. There can be **N** concrete builders for **N** representations.

Let $B_c$ represent a concrete builder. Its internal state is:

$$
\text{State}(B_c) = \{ \text{house} \mid \text{house} = \text{House} \}
$$

When `buildWalls()` is called, the builder performs:

$$
\text{State}(B_c).\text{house}.\text{parts}.\text{add}(\text{"Walls"})
$$

This is a **mutating** operation on the internal product. After all four (4) steps, the internal `house` object is fully populated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Decision (KTU 2024):**
> In the *Modern Fluent Builder* variant (popular in Java/Spring), the abstract methods return `Builder` instead of `void`. This enables **method chaining**:
> `builder.buildWalls().buildRoof().buildDoors().buildWindows().build()`
> This is the *fluent interface* style taught in the KTU 2024 PECST411 syllabus.

## 3.4 The Director — The Algorithm

The **Director** does NOT know *how* the parts are built; it only knows the **order** in which to call the builder methods. This is the **key decoupling point** of the pattern.

Let $D$ be the Director, and $S$ be the construction sequence:

$$
S = \langle \text{buildFoundation}, \text{buildStructure}, \text{buildRoof}, \text{buildInterior} \rangle
$$

The Director's `constructHouse()` method executes this sequence in a strict linear order. If the order is wrong (e.g., roof before walls), the result is logically incorrect. **The Director enforces the invariant.**

## 3.5 The Client — Triggering the Build

The **Client** wires everything together. The pseudocode execution is:

1. Instantiate concrete builder: `HouseBuilder builder = new ConcreteHouseBuilder()`
2. Instantiate director: `CivilEngineer director = new CivilEngineer(builder)`
3. Trigger construction: `director.constructHouse()`
4. Retrieve product: `House house = builder.getResult()`

The Director's `constructHouse()` internally calls each `buildXxx()` method of the builder, which appends a part to the internal list.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Builder vs. Other Creational Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Comparison Matrix (Mandatory KTU Comparison Table)**

| Pattern | Construction Style | When to Choose | Number of Methods | Immutability Support |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Builder** | Step-by-step, then build | Many optional parts, complex assembly | Many small setters | **Yes (high)** |
| **Factory Method** | One-shot object creation | Single, simple product, polymorphic creation | One factory method | Optional |
| **Abstract Factory** | Family of related objects | Cross-platform UI / Theme families | One per product type | Optional |
| **Prototype** | Clone an existing object | Creation cost is high | One `clone()` method | Optional |
| **Singleton** | Single global instance | Only one instance ever needed | One static accessor | **Yes (high)** |

## 3.7 Mathematical Representation of the Construction Algorithm

Let $P$ be the Product, $B$ the Builder, and $D$ the Director. The construction operation can be modeled as:

$$
P = B.\text{buildWalls} \circ B.\text{buildRoof} \circ B.\text{buildDoors} \circ B.\text{buildWindows}
$$

The Director $D$ provides the **operator composition**:

$$
D.\text{constructHouse} = m_1 \bullet m_2 \bullet m_3 \bullet m_4
$$

where each $m_i$ is a builder method invocation. The final `getResult()` returns $P$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Builder Pattern Structure

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +main(args)
    }
    class Director {
        -builder Builder
        +construct(builder)
    }
    class Builder {
        <<interface>>
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +getResult()
    }
    class ConcreteBuilderA {
        -product Product
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +getResult()
    }
    class ConcreteBuilderB {
        -product Product
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +getResult()
    }
    class Product {
        -parts List
        +addPart()
        +listParts()
    }

    Client --> Director : creates
    Client --> Builder : instantiates
    Director --> Builder : uses
    Builder <|.. ConcreteBuilderA : implements
    Builder <|.. ConcreteBuilderB : implements
    ConcreteBuilderA --> Product : builds
    ConcreteBuilderB --> Product : builds
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Construction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant D as Director
    participant B as ConcreteBuilder
    participant P as Product

    C->>B: new ConcreteBuilder()
    C->>D: new Director(builder)
    C->>D: constructHouse()
    D->>B: buildFoundation()
    B->>P: addPart("Foundation")
    D->>B: buildStructure()
    B->>P: addPart("Structure")
    D->>B: buildRoof()
    B->>P: addPart("Roof")
    D->>B: buildInterior()
    B->>P: addPart("Interior")
    D-->>C: construction complete
    C->>B: getResult()
    B-->>C: returns fully built Product
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Module] --> B[Director Class]
    B --> C[Abstract Builder Interface]
    C --> D[Concrete Builder Class A]
    C --> E[Concrete Builder Class B]
    D --> F[Product Object Variant 1]
    E --> G[Product Object Variant 2]
    H[Construction Sequence Algorithm] -.controls.-> B
    I[Part Assembly Logic] -.implemented in.-> D
    I -.implemented in.-> E
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- The **Client** initiates the workflow but stays decoupled from concrete classes.
- The **Director** is the *only* component that knows the **order of construction**.
- The **Abstract Builder** is the *contract*; it never gets instantiated directly.
- The **Concrete Builders** are the only places where *business logic of part assembly* lives.
- The **Products** are returned as fully assembled, ready-to-use objects.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]
**Define the Builder Design Pattern. List any two situations where the Builder Pattern is preferred over the Factory Pattern.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Builder Pattern** is a creational design pattern that separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing the same construction process to create different representations.

**Two situations where Builder is preferred over Factory:**

1. **Many Optional Parameters:** When the object has numerous optional fields (e.g., a `Computer` with optional GPU, optional sound card, optional Wi-Fi), the Factory would require many overloaded constructors, while the Builder allows step-by-step configuration.

2. **Immutability Requirement:** When the final object must be immutable (e.g., a configuration record), the Builder can construct the object in a mutable intermediate state and return a fully immutable product via `build()`.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for two situations = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Identify the four (4) participants in the Builder Pattern and state the role of the Director class.**

**Model Answer:**

| Participant | Role |
|---|---|
| **Builder** | Abstract interface declaring construction steps. |
| **ConcreteBuilder** | Concrete class that implements the steps and assembles the product. |
| **Director** | Class that orchestrates the construction algorithm. |
| **Product** | The complex object that is being constructed. |

**Role of the Director:** The Director is responsible for **executing the construction steps in a predefined sequence**. It calls the builder's methods in the correct order to assemble a valid product, decoupling the client from the assembly logic. *(1 mark for list + 2 marks for Director role = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2 typically offers an internal choice between two questions, each worth 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the intent and structure of the Builder Design Pattern with a suitable UML class diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a Builder Pattern solution for constructing a `Computer` object with the following parts: `CPU`, `RAM`, `Storage`, `GraphicsCard`, and `PowerSupply`. Show the Java code for the `Computer` (Product), `ComputerBuilder` (Abstract), `GamingComputerBuilder` (Concrete), and `ComputerEngineer` (Director). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Intent:** The intent of the Builder Pattern is to **abstract the construction of a complex object so that the same construction process can produce different representations**. It addresses the *telescoping constructor* problem and supports immutable object construction.

**Structure (UML):**
The pattern has four (4) participants:

1. **Builder (Abstract):** Declares abstract methods `buildCPU()`, `buildRAM()`, `buildStorage()`, `buildGraphicsCard()`, `buildPowerSupply()`, and a concrete `getComputer()`.
2. **ConcreteBuilder (GamingComputerBuilder):** Implements all `buildXxx()` methods. Maintains an internal `Computer` instance. Populates parts in each method.
3. **Director (ComputerEngineer):** Has a `constructComputer()` method that calls builder methods in order: `buildCPU → buildRAM → buildStorage → buildGraphicsCard → buildPowerSupply`.
4. **Product (Computer):** POJO with fields `cpu, ram, storage, graphicsCard, powerSupply` and a `showConfiguration()` method.

**UML Diagram (Verbal Description for Exam):**

| Computer (Product) | ComputerBuilder (Abstract) | GamingComputerBuilder (Concrete) | ComputerEngineer (Director) |
|---|---|---|---|
| -cpu: String | +buildCPU(): void | -computer: Computer | -builder: ComputerBuilder |
| -ram: String | +buildRAM(): void | +buildCPU(): void | +constructComputer(): void |
| -storage: String | +buildStorage(): void | +buildRAM(): void | +getComputer(): Computer |
| -graphicsCard: String | +buildGraphicsCard(): void | +buildStorage(): void | |
| -powerSupply: String | +buildPowerSupply(): void | +buildGraphicsCard(): void | |
| +showConfig() | +getComputer(): Computer | +buildPowerSupply(): void | |
| | | +getComputer(): Computer | |

*(3 marks for intent + 4 marks for structure/UML = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Java Implementation Outline:**

**Step 1: The Product (Computer)**
```
public class Computer {
    private String cpu, ram, storage, graphicsCard, powerSupply;
    public void setCpu(String c) { this.cpu = c; }
    public void setRam(String r) { this.ram = r; }
    public void setStorage(String s) { this.storage = s; }
    public void setGraphicsCard(String g) { this.graphicsCard = g; }
    public void setPowerSupply(String p) { this.powerSupply = p; }
    public void showConfiguration() {
        System.out.println("CPU: " + cpu);
        System.out.println("RAM: " + ram);
        System.out.println("Storage: " + storage);
        System.out.println("GPU: " + graphicsCard);
        System.out.println("PSU: " + powerSupply);
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Abstract Builder (ComputerBuilder)**
```
public abstract class ComputerBuilder {
    protected Computer computer = new Computer();
    public abstract void buildCPU();
    public abstract void buildRAM();
    public abstract void buildStorage();
    public abstract void buildGraphicsCard();
    public abstract void buildPowerSupply();
    public Computer getComputer() { return computer; }
}
```

**Step 3: Concrete Builder (GamingComputerBuilder)**
```
public class GamingComputerBuilder extends ComputerBuilder {
    public void buildCPU() { computer.setCpu("Intel i9-13900K"); }
    public void buildRAM() { computer.setRam("32GB DDR5"); }
    public void buildStorage() { computer.setStorage("2TB NVMe SSD"); }
    public void buildGraphicsCard() { computer.setGraphicsCard("RTX 4090"); }
    public void buildPowerSupply() { computer.setPowerSupply("1000W 80+ Platinum"); }
}
```

**Step 4: Director (ComputerEngineer)**
```
public class ComputerEngineer {
    private ComputerBuilder builder;
    public ComputerEngineer(ComputerBuilder b) { this.builder = b; }
    public void constructComputer() {
        builder.buildCPU();
        builder.buildRAM();
        builder.buildStorage();
        builder.buildGraphicsCard();
        builder.buildPowerSupply();
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Client (main)**
```
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ComputerBuilder builder = new GamingComputerBuilder();
        ComputerEngineer engineer = new ComputerEngineer(builder);
        engineer.constructComputer();
        Computer pc = builder.getComputer();
        pc.showConfiguration();
    }
}
```

*(2 marks for Product + 2 marks for Builder hierarchy + 2 marks for Director + 1 mark for Client main = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the Builder Pattern with the Abstract Factory Pattern. Highlight at least three (3) differences with suitable use-case scenarios. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a scenario where you need to build different representations of a `User` object (e.g., `AdminUser`, `GuestUser`, `PremiumUser`). Apply the Builder Pattern and demonstrate with a class diagram and Java skeleton code. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Comparison Table (Mandatory KTU Format):**

| Aspect | Builder Pattern | Abstract Factory Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Step-by-step construction of a *single* complex object. | Construction of *families* of related objects. |
| **Number of Products** | Usually one product, multiple representations. | Multiple products, grouped by family. |
| **Construction Style** | Sequential, with explicit `build()` finalization. | One-shot creation per product in the family. |
| **Client Interaction** | Client obtains product after the build sequence. | Client obtains a factory, then calls factory methods. |
| **Use Case** | Building a `Computer` with 5 configurable parts. | Building a cross-platform UI (Button, Checkbox, Scrollbar). |
| **JDK Example** | `StringBuilder` | `DocumentBuilderFactory` (XML) |

**Three (3) Key Differences:**

1. **Granularity:** Builder builds one complex object incrementally; Abstract Factory creates families of related objects all at once.

2. **Immutability:** Builder produces immutable products; Abstract Factory products are typically mutable.

3. **Use Case:** Builder → HTML/PDF document generation. Abstract Factory → Theme-based UI toolkit (Material, Cupertino, Fluent).

*(3 marks for table + 4 marks for three differences with use-cases = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Class Diagram (Verbal Representation):**

| Class | Type | Key Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `User` | Product | `setName`, `setEmail`, `setRole`, `showDetails` |
| `UserBuilder` | Abstract Builder | `buildName`, `buildEmail`, `buildRole`, `getUser` |
| `AdminUserBuilder` | Concrete Builder | Implements all build methods for admin context |
| `GuestUserBuilder` | Concrete Builder | Implements all build methods for guest context |
| `PremiumUserBuilder` | Concrete Builder | Implements all build methods for premium context |
| `UserDirector` | Director | `constructUser()` |
| `Client` | Client | `main()` |

**Java Skeleton Code (Key Methods Only):**

```
public class User {
    private String name, email, role;
    public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
    public void setEmail(String e) { this.email = e; }
    public void setRole(String r) { this.role = r; }
    public void showDetails() {
        System.out.println(name + " | " + email + " | " + role);
    }
}

public abstract class UserBuilder {
    protected User user = new User();
    public abstract void buildName();
    public abstract void buildEmail();
    public abstract void buildRole();
    public User getUser() { return user; }
}

public class AdminUserBuilder extends UserBuilder {
    public void buildName() { user.setName("AdminUser"); }
    public void buildEmail() { user.setEmail("admin@ktu.in"); }
    public void buildRole() { user.setRole("ADMIN"); }
}

public class UserDirector {
    private UserBuilder builder;
    public UserDirector(UserBuilder b) { this.builder = b; }
    public void constructUser() {
        builder.buildName();
        builder.buildEmail();
        builder.buildRole();
    }
}

public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        UserBuilder builder = new AdminUserBuilder();
        UserDirector director = new UserDirector(builder);
        director.constructUser();
        User u = builder.getUser();
        u.showDetails();
    }
}
```

*(2 marks for class diagram + 3 marks for code structure + 2 marks for client invocation = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Director with Builder:** The Director does NOT build parts — it only *orchestrates* calls. Writing `builder.buildCPU()` inside the Builder class is correct; writing the construction logic inside the Director (i.e., `director.cpu = "i9"`) is incorrect and costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting `getResult()`:** Many students omit the `getResult()` / `getComputer()` method, breaking the chain of responsibility. This costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Singleton-Builder Confusion:** Builder is NOT a Singleton. Each `build()` typically creates a NEW product. Writing a private constructor in the Builder class will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Mixing Builder with Prototype:** Builder constructs from scratch; Prototype clones an existing object. Examiners often test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the `Product` class:** Drawing only Builder and Director without a `Product` is incomplete. Always include the Product in your diagram.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- **Pattern Type:** Creational (GoF).
- **Number of Participants:** 4 — Builder, ConcreteBuilder, Director, Product.
- **Key Intent:** Separate complex object construction from its representation.
- **When to Use:** Object has $\geq 4$ optional/configurable parameters.
- **Key JDK Examples to Memorize:** `StringBuilder`, `ProcessBuilder`, `Stream.Builder`, `Locale.Builder`.
- **Director's Job:** Orchestrate the sequence — not implement logic.
- **Builder's Job:** Implement part assembly — not control order.
- **Pattern Benefit:** Immutability, readability, no telescoping constructors.
- **Pattern Drawback:** Verbose; increases class count by 1 (Builder) + N (ConcreteBuilders).
- **Compare With:** Abstract Factory (family) vs Prototype (clone) vs Factory Method (one-shot).
- **Fluent Style:** Modern Builders return `this` from each setter for method chaining.
- **Director Optional:** In fluent Builders, the Director is often absorbed into the Builder itself (e.g., `Lombok @Builder`).
- **UML Rule to Remember:** Director aggregates Builder; ConcreteBuilder implements Builder and creates Product.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw 4 boxes (Builder, ConcreteBuilder, Director, Product) and 4 arrows. Missing any one of these loses 2 marks on a 14-mark question.
- **One-Line Definition to Memorize:** *"Builder constructs complex objects step by step, with a Director controlling the order and a Builder implementing the assembly."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Design Pattern and its Types - Adapter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (Gang of Four — GoF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Design Patterns** are a category of design patterns (one of the three GoF classifications, alongside **Creational** and **Behavioral**) that deal with the composition of classes and objects to form larger structures. They simplify the structure by identifying relationships between entities — usually leveraging **inheritance** and **composition** to obtain new functionality.

The **Adapter Pattern** is a **Structural Design Pattern** that converts the interface of a class into another interface the **client expects**. It allows classes with incompatible interfaces to work together by wrapping its own interface around that of an already existing class. The Adapter is also known as a **Wrapper**.

> [!NOTE]
> **GoF Canonical Definition:**
> *"Convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect. Adapter lets classes work together that couldn't otherwise because of incompatible interfaces."*

### Classification of Structural Patterns (KTU Syllabus Context)

| S.No | Pattern | One-Line Intent |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Adapter** | Converts one interface into another expected by the client |
| 2 | **Bridge** | Decouples an abstraction from its implementation |
| 3 | **Composite** | Composes objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies |
| 4 | **Decorator** | Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically |
| 5 | **Facade** | Provides a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem |
| 6 | **Flyweight** | Uses sharing to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently |
| 7 | **Proxy** | Provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### Analogy 1: The Universal Travel Adapter (Electrical)

Imagine you are travelling from **India** to the **United Kingdom**.
- Your laptop charger has a **Type-D Indian plug** (round, 3-pin).
- The wall socket in London is a **Type-G British socket** (rectangular, 3-pin).
- These two interfaces are **physically incompatible**.

You don't manufacture a new charger. You simply buy a small **travel adapter** (a plastic block) that:
1. **Accepts** your Indian plug on one side.
2. **Exposes** a British plug on the other side.

The laptop **client** expects a "power source" through a British socket. The travel adapter **translates** the electrical interface without altering the laptop or the wall socket.

### Analogy 2: The Language Translator

- A **Japanese businessman** speaks only Japanese.
- A **French client** speaks only French.
- Neither can communicate directly.

A **human translator** sits between them, listening to Japanese and speaking French. The translator doesn't change either person's mind — they simply **convert the message format** from one protocol to another.

In both cases:
- **Client** → expects `InterfaceA`
- **Adaptee** (existing class) → only provides `InterfaceB`
- **Adapter** → bridges the gap

---

## 1.3 Intent & Motivation Summary

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **Target** | The domain-specific interface that the **client** uses directly |
| **Client** | Collaborates with objects conforming to the Target interface |
| **Adaptee** | The existing class that needs adapting (has a useful behaviour but incompatible interface) |
| **Adapter** | The bridge — implements the Target interface and holds a reference to the Adaptee |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Key Identifiers of the Adapter Pattern:**
> 1. The presence of an **already-existing class** (Adaptee) that is being reused.
> 2. The introduction of a **new class** (Adapter) that conforms to the client's expected interface while internally delegating calls to the Adaptee.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How the Adapter Works

The pattern operates through **delegation** (object adapter) or **inheritance** (class adapter). The following logical steps describe the runtime flow:

1. **Identification of Incompatibility:** The client is written against a clean domain interface (Target), but the third-party / legacy library (Adaptee) exposes a different method signature set.

2. **Adapter Instantiation:** The client instantiates the Adapter and passes/holds a reference to the Adaptee (composition).

3. **Request Translation:** When the client calls `adapter.request()`, the Adapter internally translates this call into one or more calls on the Adaptee using `adaptee.specificRequest()`.

4. **Return Normalization:** Any data returned by the Adaptee is optionally re-formatted by the Adapter before returning to the client, preserving the Target contract.

5. **Transparency:** From the client's perspective, it only knows about `Target` — the Adapter is fully polymorphic with respect to the Target.

---

## 2.2 Types of Adapter Pattern

### Type A: Object Adapter (Composition-based) — *Most Common*

- Uses **object composition** (HAS-A relationship).
- The Adapter **holds a reference** to an Adaptee instance.
- Works in **single-inheritance languages** (Java, C#).
- **More flexible** because it can adapt any subclass of Adaptee.

### Type B: Class Adapter (Inheritance-based) — *Less Common*

- Uses **multiple inheritance** (IS-A relationship to BOTH Target and Adaptee).
- Only possible in languages like **C++** that support multiple inheritance.
- **Less flexible** because it requires the Adaptee to be a concrete class, not an interface.
- Can **override** Adaptee's behaviour since it is a subclass.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Object Adapter | Class Adapter |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Mechanism | **Composition** (HAS-A) | **Inheritance** (IS-A) |
| UML Relationship | Adaptee ←—— Adapter (association) | Adaptee ◇— Adapter (generalization) |
| Adaptee Scope | Can adapt Adaptee **and its subclasses** | Can adapt only the **specific Adaptee class** |
| Language Support | Any OO language | Only C++ (multiple inheritance) |
| Flexibility | **Higher** | Lower |
| Overriding Behaviour | Cannot override Adaptee methods | Can override Adaptee methods |
| Typical Use | Reusing an unrelated class with similar behaviour | Reusing an Adaptee where IS-A relationship genuinely fits |

---

## 2.4 Applicability — When to Use Adapter (KTU-Favourite Bullets)

Use the Adapter pattern when:

- You want to **use an existing class**, but its interface does not match the one you need.
- You want to create a **reusable class** that cooperates with unrelated classes having incompatible interfaces.
- You need to use **several existing subclasses**, but it's impractical to adapt their interface by subclassing every one (use **object adapter**).
- Most **third-party library integrations** require it (e.g., JDBC drivers, SLF4J loggers, payment gateway SDKs).

---

## 2.5 Consequences (Trade-Off Analysis)

| Consequence | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| **+ Reusability** | Allows reuse of existing functionality without rewriting |
| **+ Decoupling** | Client is decoupled from the Adaptee's concrete implementation |
| **+ Open/Closed Principle** | New Adapters can be introduced without modifying the client |
| **− Class Explosion** | Each Adaptee variant may require its own Adapter subclass |
| **− Indirection Overhead** | One extra method call layer introduces minor runtime cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Adapter is the *de facto* pattern used in almost every enterprise software system — from **JDBC** (Java Database Connectivity) adapting vendor-specific drivers to the standard `java.sql.Connection` interface, to **SLF4J** adapting multiple logging backends. Mastering this pattern is essential for any KTU Software Engineering paper.

---

## 2.6 Related Patterns Comparison (Exam Favourite)

| Pattern | Difference from Adapter |
|---------|-------------------------|
| **Bridge** | Bridge is designed **upfront**; Adapter is **retro-fitted** to existing code. |
| **Decorator** | Decorator **adds** new behaviour without changing the interface; Adapter **changes** the interface. |
| **Proxy** | Proxy keeps the **same interface** as the real object; Adapter **changes** the interface. |
| **Facade** | Facade simplifies a **complex subsystem** with one interface; Adapter maps to a **specific expected interface**. |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & UML Blueprint

## 3.1 Worked Example: A Media Player Application

This is a classic KTU/textbook example. The business requirement is:

> *"Design a media player that currently plays MP3 files. In the future, it should also play VLC and MP4 files without modifying the existing client code."*

### Problem Statement Analysis

- The `AudioPlayer` client works with an `MediaPlayer` interface that exposes `play(String audioType, String fileName)`.
- The third-party classes (`VlcPlayer`, `Mp4Player`) expose a different signature: `playVlc(String fileName)` and `playMp4(String fileName)`.
- **Incompatibility identified.**

---

### Step 1: Define the Target Interface (Expected by Client)

```java
public interface MediaPlayer {
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName);
}
```

### Step 2: Define the Adaptee Interfaces and Concrete Classes

```java
public interface AdvancedMediaPlayer {
    public void playVlc(String fileName);
    public void playMp4(String fileName);
}

public class VlcPlayer implements AdvancedMediaPlayer {
    @Override
    public void playVlc(String fileName) {
        System.out.println("Playing vlc file. Name: " + fileName);
    }
    @Override
    public void playMp4(String fileName) {
        // Do nothing
    }
}

public class Mp4Player implements AdvancedMediaPlayer {
    @Override
    public void playVlc(String fileName) {
        // Do nothing
    }
    @Override
    public void playMp4(String fileName) {
        System.out.println("Playing mp4 file. Name: " + fileName);
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Create the Object Adapter (The Bridge)

```java
public class MediaAdapter implements MediaPlayer {

    AdvancedMediaPlayer advancedMusicPlayer;

    public MediaAdapter(String audioType){
        if(audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("vlc") ){
            advancedMusicPlayer = new VlcPlayer();
        } else if(audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4")){
            advancedMusicPlayer = new Mp4Player();
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName) {
        if(audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("vlc")){
            advancedMusicPlayer.playVlc(fileName);
        } else if(audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4")){
            advancedMusicPlayer.playMp4(fileName);
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Client Code Using the Adapter

```java
public class AudioPlayer implements MediaPlayer {
    MediaAdapter mediaAdapter;

    @Override
    public void play(String audioType, String fileName) {
        // Inbuilt support
        if(audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp3")){
            System.out.println("Playing mp3 file. Name: " + fileName);
        }
        // Delegation via Adapter
        else if(audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("vlc")
             || audioType.equalsIgnoreCase("mp4")){
            mediaAdapter = new MediaAdapter(audioType);
            mediaAdapter.play(audioType, fileName);
        }
        else{
            System.out.println("Invalid media. " + audioType + " format not supported");
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 5: Derivation of the Request Flow

When `AudioPlayer.play("mp4", "movie.mp4")` is invoked:

$$
\text{Flow} = \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{call}} \text{MediaPlayer} \xrightarrow{\text{delegate}} \text{MediaAdapter} \xrightarrow{\text{delegate}} \text{AdvancedMediaPlayer}
$$

Mathematically, the **transformation function** the Adapter performs is:

$$
f_{\text{adapter}}(\text{request}) = \text{Translate}\bigl(\text{play}(T, F)\bigr) \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{playVlc}(F), & \text{if } T = \text{"vlc"} \\[4pt] \text{playMp4}(F), & \text{if } T = \text{"mp4"} \\[4pt] \text{null}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Each branch maps a **single Target method call** to a **specific Adaptee method call** — this is the essence of the translation.

---

## 3.2 Class Adapter (C++ Sketch — For Understanding Only)

In C++, multiple inheritance lets the Adapter inherit from **both** Target and Adaptee:

```cpp
class ClassAdapter : public MediaPlayer, private VlcPlayer {
public:
    void play(string audioType, string fileName) override {
        if(audioType == "vlc"){
            playVlc(fileName); // Direct inheritance access
        }
    }
};
```

Because the Adapter **IS-A** VlcPlayer, it can directly invoke Adaptee methods without delegation — a structural difference from the object adapter.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Evaluation Criterion | Object Adapter | Class Adapter |
|----------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Reuses subclassed Adaptees | ✅ Yes (via composition polymorphism) | ❌ No (binding is static at compile time) |
| Adds new Adaptee at runtime | ✅ Possible | ❌ Not possible |
| Overrides Adaptee behaviour | ❌ Requires wrapper | ✅ Possible |
| Coupling | Looser (composition) | Tighter (inheritance) |
| KTU Examiner Preference | ⭐ **More commonly asked** | Rarely asked unless C++ context |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram — Object Adapter (Composition)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Client {
        +main() void
    }
    class MediaPlayer {
        <<interface>>
        +play(String audioType, String fileName) void
    }
    class MediaAdapter {
        -AdvancedMediaPlayer advancedMusicPlayer
        +MediaAdapter(String audioType)
        +play(String audioType, String fileName) void
    }
    class AdvancedMediaPlayer {
        <<interface>>
        +playVlc(String fileName) void
        +playMp4(String fileName) void
    }
    class VlcPlayer {
        +playVlc(String fileName) void
        +playMp4(String fileName) void
    }
    class Mp4Player {
        +playVlc(String fileName) void
        +playMp4(String fileName) void
    }
    class AudioPlayer {
        -MediaAdapter mediaAdapter
        +play(String audioType, String fileName) void
    }

    Client --> MediaPlayer : uses
    AudioPlayer ..|> MediaPlayer : implements
    MediaAdapter ..|> MediaPlayer : implements
    MediaAdapter --> AdvancedMediaPlayer : delegates to
    VlcPlayer ..|> AdvancedMediaPlayer : implements
    Mp4Player ..|> AdvancedMediaPlayer : implements
```

**Diagram Interpretation Notes (for Examiner):**
- `AudioPlayer` and `MediaAdapter` both realize the **Target** (`MediaPlayer`).
- `MediaAdapter` holds a **reference** to `AdvancedMediaPlayer` (the Adaptee interface) — this is the composition arrow.
- `VlcPlayer` and `Mp4Player` are concrete Adaptees.

---

## 4.2 UML Class Diagram — Class Adapter (Multiple Inheritance)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class MediaPlayer {
        <<interface>>
        +play(String audioType, String fileName) void
    }
    class AdvancedMediaPlayer {
        <<interface>>
        +playVlc(String fileName) void
        +playMp4(String fileName) void
    }
    class VlcPlayer {
        +playVlc(String fileName) void
        +playMp4(String fileName) void
    }
    class ClassMediaAdapter {
        +play(String audioType, String fileName) void
    }

    ClassMediaAdapter ..|> MediaPlayer : implements
    ClassMediaAdapter --|> VlcPlayer : inherits
    VlcPlayer ..|> AdvancedMediaPlayer : implements
```

**Diagram Interpretation Notes:**
- `ClassMediaAdapter` inherits from BOTH `MediaPlayer` and `VlcPlayer`.
- Only one Adaptee (`VlcPlayer`) can be adapted at a time because C++ does not permit dynamic swapping of inherited base classes.

---

## 4.3 Runtime Sequence — Object Adapter Call Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant clientSys as Client System
    participant audPlayer as AudioPlayer
    participant medAdapt as MediaAdapter
    participant advMedia as AdvancedMediaPlayer (VlcPlayer)

    clientSys->>audPlayer: play("vlc", "song.vlc")
    activate audPlayer
    audPlayer->>medAdapt: new MediaAdapter("vlc")
    activate medAdapt
    medAdapt->>advMedia: new VlcPlayer()
    activate advMedia
    advMedia-->>medAdapt: instance ready
    deactivate advMedia
    medAdapt-->>audPlayer: adapter ready
    deactivate medAdapt
    audPlayer->>medAdapt: play("vlc", "song.vlc")
    activate medAdapt
    medAdapt->>advMedia: playVlc("song.vlc")
    activate advMedia
    advMedia-->>medAdapt: success
    deactivate advMedia
    medAdapt-->>audPlayer: success
    deactivate medAdapt
    audPlayer-->>clientSys: success
    deactivate audPlayer
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**
- Steps **1–8**: Object construction (Adapter instantiates Adaptee internally).
- Steps **9–14**: The actual request translation chain.
- This diagram is **the exact answer KTU examiners expect** when asked to "draw the sequence diagram of the Adapter pattern."

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Structural Design Patterns. List any four patterns belonging to this category.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Structural Design Patterns** are design patterns that ease the design by identifying a simple way to realize relationships between entities. They deal with class and object composition, using inheritance and composition to build larger structures.

**Four Structural Patterns:**
1. **Adapter Pattern** – Converts the interface of a class into another interface clients expect.
2. **Facade Pattern** – Provides a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem.
3. **Decorator Pattern** – Attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically.
4. **Composite Pattern** – Composes objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies.

*(Any four from the seven GoF Structural patterns are acceptable. 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for listing.)*

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners give **1 mark for the definition** and **0.5 mark each** for naming correctly. Memorize all seven patterns to be safe.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Object Adapter and Class Adapter.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No | Parameter | Object Adapter | Class Adapter |
|------|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Mechanism | Uses **composition** (HAS-A relationship) | Uses **multiple inheritance** (IS-A relationship) |
| 2 | Flexibility | Can adapt Adaptee **and its subclasses** at runtime | Can adapt only the **specific Adaptee class** |
| 3 | Language | Any OO language | Only languages supporting multiple inheritance (e.g., C++) |
| 4 | Overriding | Cannot override Adaptee behaviour | Can override Adaptee methods |

*(2 marks for tabular distinction + 1 mark for conclusion: Object Adapter is preferred in most modern languages like Java.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Tag Applied]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Adapter Pattern with Real-World Scenario

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the intent and motivation of the **Adapter Design Pattern**. Describe a real-world scenario where the Adapter pattern is essential. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design and implement an Adapter pattern for the following scenario: A stock market monitoring application currently displays data in **XML format**. The system needs to be extended to support **JSON format** analytics from a third-party analytics provider without modifying the existing client code. Provide the **UML class diagram** and explain how the Adapter bridges the two interfaces. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Intent:** The Adapter pattern's intent is to **convert the interface of a class into another interface that the client expects**, allowing otherwise incompatible classes to collaborate.

**Motivation:** Sometimes a class designed for reuse (Adaptee) has the right functionality but the wrong interface for the client's domain. Rather than rewriting the class — which violates the **Open/Closed Principle** — we introduce an **Adapter** that translates requests.

**Real-World Scenario:**
- **Travel Adapter (Power Plug):** An Indian laptop charger (Type-D plug) cannot be plugged into a UK wall socket (Type-G). A travel adapter bridges this incompatibility.
- **Language Translator:** A Japanese speaker and French speaker need a translator to communicate.
- **Software Example:** A legacy `XMLStockDataProvider` returns XML strings, but the modern `AnalyticsDashboard` client expects a JSON-formatted object. An `AnalyticsAdapter` wraps the XML provider and exposes JSON.

*(3 marks for intent + 2 marks for motivation + 2 marks for real-world scenario.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify Participants:**

| Participant | Role in Scenario |
|-------------|------------------|
| `AnalyticsTool` (Target Interface) | Interface expected by the client (e.g., dashboard) |
| `AnalyticsAdapter` (Adapter) | Bridges XMLProvider output to JSON-expected format |
| `XMLStockDataProvider` (Adaptee) | Existing class returning XML data |
| `AnalyticsClient` (Client) | The dashboard application using AnalyticsTool |

**Step 2 — UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class AnalyticsTool {
        <<interface>>
        +getJsonAnalytics(String stockSymbol) String
    }
    class AnalyticsAdapter {
        -XMLStockDataProvider xmlProvider
        +AnalyticsAdapter(XMLStockDataProvider p)
        +getJsonAnalytics(String stockSymbol) String
    }
    class XMLStockDataProvider {
        +getXMLData(String stockSymbol) String
    }
    class AnalyticsClient {
        +displayData(String symbol) void
    }

    AnalyticsClient --> AnalyticsTool : depends on
    AnalyticsAdapter ..|> AnalyticsTool : implements
    AnalyticsAdapter --> XMLStockDataProvider : wraps
```

**Step 3 — Implementation Sketch (Java-style Pseudocode):**

```java
// Target — what the client expects
public interface AnalyticsTool {
    String getJsonAnalytics(String stockSymbol);
}

// Adaptee — legacy XML provider (already exists)
public class XMLStockDataProvider {
    public String getXMLData(String stockSymbol) {
        return "<stock><symbol>" + stockSymbol + "</symbol></stock>";
    }
}

// Adapter — bridges XML to JSON
public class AnalyticsAdapter implements AnalyticsTool {
    private XMLStockDataProvider xmlProvider;

    public AnalyticsAdapter(XMLStockDataProvider provider) {
        this.xmlProvider = provider;
    }

    @Override
    public String getJsonAnalytics(String stockSymbol) {
        String xml = xmlProvider.getXMLData(stockSymbol);
        // Translation logic: parse XML, convert to JSON
        String json = "{\"symbol\":\"" + stockSymbol + "\",\"data\":\"" + xml + "\"}";
        return json;
    }
}

// Client — untouched by the adapter
public class AnalyticsClient {
    public void displayData(AnalyticsTool tool, String symbol) {
        System.out.println(tool.getJsonAnalytics(symbol));
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Explanation of the Bridge Mechanism:**

The client's `displayData()` method receives any `AnalyticsTool`. When passed an `AnalyticsAdapter`, the call `getJsonAnalytics(symbol)`:
1. Internally invokes `xmlProvider.getXMLData(symbol)`.
2. Parses the returned XML string.
3. Converts it into a JSON-formatted string.
4. Returns it to the client.

**The client never knows the XML provider exists** — this is the *Open/Closed Principle* in action: **closed for modification, open for extension**.

*(3 marks for UML diagram + 2 marks for code + 2 marks for bridging explanation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Object Adapter vs Class Adapter Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Object Adapter** and **Class Adapter** in detail. State the conditions under which each is preferable with justified examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a payment gateway system that integrates three different payment processors: **PayPal**, **Stripe**, and **Razorpay**. Each processor has its own SDK with unique method names. Design an **Object Adapter-based architecture** that allows a single `PaymentProcessor` client to work with all three. Provide the class diagram and explain how the **Strategy-like flexibility** combined with **Adapter** enables this. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Aspect | Object Adapter | Class Adapter |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| **Composition** | HAS-A Adaptee | IS-A Adaptee (via inheritance) |
| **Language Support** | Java, C#, most modern languages | C++ only |
| **Runtime Binding** | Yes — Adaptee type can be chosen at runtime | No — fixed at compile time |
| **Subclass Adaptation** | Works with Adaptee and all its subclasses | Works only with the specific Adaptee |
| **Behaviour Override** | Cannot override Adaptee methods | Can override Adaptee methods |

**When to Prefer Object Adapter:**
- The Adaptee is an **interface** with multiple concrete implementations.
- You want **runtime polymorphism** in choosing the Adaptee.
- Working in Java/C# where multiple inheritance is unavailable.

**When to Prefer Class Adapter:**
- You genuinely need to **override** Adaptee behaviour for customization.
- Working in C++ and the Adaptee hierarchy is stable.

**Justified Example:** Adapting a `Logger` interface to multiple backends (Console, File, Remote) — object adapter wins because each backend is a subclass of a `BaseLogger` interface.

*(7 marks distributed across comparison, justification, and examples.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Architecture Components:**

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| `PaymentProcessor` (Target) | Unified interface with `processPayment(double amount)` |
| `PayPalAdapter`, `StripeAdapter`, `RazorpayAdapter` (Concrete Adapters) | Each wraps its respective SDK |
| `PayPalSDK`, `StripeSDK`, `RazorpaySDK` (Adaptees) | Third-party APIs with incompatible signatures |
| `CheckoutClient` (Client) | Uses `PaymentProcessor` polymorphically |

**Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction TB
    class PaymentProcessor {
        <<interface>>
        +processPayment(double amount) void
    }
    class PayPalAdapter {
        -PayPalSDK paypal
        +processPayment(double amount) void
    }
    class StripeAdapter {
        -StripeSDK stripe
        +processPayment(double amount) void
    }
    class RazorpayAdapter {
        -RazorpaySDK razorpay
        +processPayment(double amount) void
    }
    class PayPalSDK {
        +makePayment(String currency, double amt) String
    }
    class StripeSDK {
        +charge(double amt, String token) String
    }
    class RazorpaySDK {
        +createPayment(double amt) String
    }
    class CheckoutClient {
        -PaymentProcessor processor
        +setProcessor(PaymentProcessor p) void
        +checkout(double amount) void
    }

    PayPalAdapter ..|> PaymentProcessor : implements
    StripeAdapter ..|> PaymentProcessor : implements
    RazorpayAdapter ..|> PaymentProcessor : implements
    PayPalAdapter --> PayPalSDK : adapts
    StripeAdapter --> StripeSDK : adapts
    RazorpayAdapter --> RazorpaySDK : adapts
    CheckoutClient --> PaymentProcessor : uses
```

**Explanation of Adapter + Strategy Flexibility:**

Although Adapter itself is structural, the *runtime swapping* of adapters (`setProcessor()`) effectively behaves like the **Strategy Pattern**. The `CheckoutClient`:
1. Does not depend on any concrete SDK class.
2. Can swap adapters at runtime based on user choice.
3. All three adapters conform to the **same** `PaymentProcessor` interface.

**Method Signature Translation Example:**
- `PayPalAdapter.processPayment(100.0)` → calls `paypal.makePayment("USD", 100.0)`.
- `StripeAdapter.processPayment(100.0)` → calls `stripe.charge(100.0, "tok_xyz")`.
- `RazorpayAdapter.processPayment(100.0)` → calls `razorpay.createPayment(100.0)`.

The Adapter's `processPayment()` method acts as the **translation layer** between the unified signature and each vendor-specific signature.

*(3 marks for class diagram + 2 marks for explanation + 2 marks for adapter-strategy combination.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to name the participants** (Target, Client, Adaptee, Adapter) in your answer costs you **at least 2 marks**. Always label them explicitly.
> 2. **Drawing only one diagram and skipping the other** (Class Adapter vs Object Adapter) when the question asks to *compare* costs you the comparison marks — always provide **both UML diagrams** if asked.
> 3. **Confusing Decorator with Adapter** is the most common error. Remember: Decorator **keeps the interface intact** and adds behaviour; Adapter **changes the interface** to match the client's expectation.
> 4. **Not stating the Open/Closed Principle benefit** explicitly when justifying the Adapter pattern loses you the application-engineering marks — examiners look for design-principle awareness.
> 5. **Writing Java code for Class Adapter** is **incorrect** — Java doesn't support multiple inheritance. If asked about Class Adapter, default to C++.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Adapter Pattern** is a **Structural Design Pattern** from the **Gang of Four (GoF)** catalogue.
- [x] It converts the interface of an existing class (Adaptee) into another interface (Target) that the client expects.
- [x] **Two Types:** **Object Adapter** (composition — Java/C# friendly) and **Class Adapter** (multiple inheritance — C++ only).
- [x] **Object Adapter** uses a **HAS-A** relationship; **Class Adapter** uses an **IS-A** relationship.
- [x] Object Adapter can adapt **any subclass** of the Adaptee type; Class Adapter is **locked to one specific Adaptee**.
- [x] **Participants (4):** Client, Target, Adapter, Adaptee.
- [x] **Common Real-World Use Cases:** Travel power adapters, language translators, JDBC drivers, SLF4J, payment gateway SDKs, legacy XML-to-JSON converters.
- [x] **Key Design Principle Honoured:** **Open/Closed Principle** — client is closed for modification, open for extension via new Adapters.
- [x] **Adapter vs Decorator:** Adapter **changes** the interface; Decorator **preserves** the interface.
- [x] **Adapter vs Bridge:** Adapter is **retro-fitted** to existing code; Bridge is **planned up-front**.
- [x] **Adapter vs Facade:** Adapter converts a **single class interface**; Facade simplifies an **entire subsystem**.
- [x] For KTU exam answers, always: **(1)** State intent → **(2)** Draw UML → **(3)** Label participants → **(4)** Provide code → **(5)** State consequence/trade-off.
- [x] **Memorize the seven Structural Patterns** by name — Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Flyweight, Proxy.
- [x] For Part B answers, prefer **Object Adapter** examples since KTU Java-centric syllabus dominates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Bridge Pattern** is a **Structural Design Pattern** from the *Gang of Four (GoF)* catalogue that **decouples an abstraction from its implementation** so that the two can vary independently. Instead of using inheritance to bind abstraction and implementation, the pattern uses **composition (object aggregation)** to delegate implementation work to a separate hierarchy. This allows the abstraction and its implementation to be developed, extended, and modified without mutual dependency.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Bridge pattern is classified as an **Object Structural Pattern** (as opposed to *Class Structural Patterns* like Adapter-With-Classes), because it uses **object composition** rather than class inheritance to combine separable abstractions and implementations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GoF Classification:** Structural Pattern
> **Alias:** Handle / Body Pattern
> **Category:** Object Structural Pattern (uses composition)
> **Intent:** Decouple abstraction from implementation so both can vary independently.

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Remote Control"

Imagine you have a **Smart Remote Control** (the *Abstraction*) that can operate different brands of televisions: Sony, Samsung, LG (the *Implementations*).

- **Problem (Without Bridge):** You would need `SonyRemote`, `SamsungRemote`, `LGRemote` — and if you add a new feature (e.g., voice control), you must create `SonyVoiceRemote`, `SamsungVoiceRemote`, `LGVoiceRemote`… a **combinatorial explosion** of remote classes.
- **Solution (With Bridge):** You build **one universal Remote** that holds a reference to a `TV` object (the *Implementor*). The Remote just calls `tv.turnOn()`, `tv.setChannel()` etc., and the actual TV object does the work. The Remote doesn't care if it's a Sony or Samsung — it only knows it has a `TV`.

The **Remote = Abstraction hierarchy** | The **TV = Implementor hierarchy** | The **plug/cable that connects them = the "Bridge"** that lets the Remote talk to any TV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bridge"?** The pattern is named after the *bridge* that connects the abstraction hierarchy to the implementation hierarchy through a reference field. This reference acts as the literal "bridge" between the two object trees.

---

## When Does KTU Expect You to Use the Bridge Pattern? (Recognition Triggers)

The KTU Board Examiner expects the Bridge pattern whenever **two independent dimensions of variation** exist:

| Trigger Condition | Real-World Symptom |
|---|---|
| Abstraction and Implementation should evolve independently | Changing TV internals breaks the Remote code |
| Class explosion due to combinatorial inheritance | `NxM` classes for N types × M features |
| Runtime binding of implementation required | Remote should work with any TV brand plugged in |
| Implementation details must be hidden from clients | Clients should not see "Sony" or "Samsung" code |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions *"two orthogonal axes of change"* or *"decoupling abstraction from implementation"*, the answer is the **Bridge Pattern**.

---

## Participants of the Bridge Pattern (Canonical GoF Roles)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires you to name the four canonical participants:

1. **Abstraction** — Defines the abstract interface; maintains a reference to the Implementor.
2. **Refined Abstraction** — Extends Abstraction; provides variant abstractions.
3. **Implementor** — Defines the interface for implementation classes (does NOT have to match Abstraction's interface).
4. **Concrete Implementor** — Provides the concrete implementation of the Implementor interface.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Problem Analysis Without Bridge

Consider a *Shape Drawing Application* with two **independent dimensions**:

- **Dimension A (Abstraction):** Shapes — `Circle`, `Square`, `Triangle`
- **Dimension B (Implementation):** Renderers — `VectorRenderer`, `RasterRenderer`

### Naive Inheritance Approach (Anti-Pattern)

To support every shape with every renderer using **inheritance**, you would need:

$$
N_{classes} = N_{abstractions} \times N_{implementations} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ classes}
$$

Class list: `VectorCircle`, `RasterCircle`, `VectorSquare`, `RasterSquare`, `VectorTriangle`, `RasterTriangle`.

**Drawbacks:**
- **Combinatorial Explosion** — Adding one new shape or renderer requires creating $N$ new classes.
- **Tight Coupling** — The shape hierarchy is permanently bound to rendering code.
- **Violates Open/Closed Principle** — Adding features requires modifying multiple classes.
- **Compile-time binding** — Cannot switch renderers at runtime.

---

## 2.2 The "How" — Bridge Pattern Mechanism

The Bridge pattern **separates the two hierarchies** and uses **composition** to connect them:

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Create the Implementor hierarchy (Implementation side).**
   Define an interface `Renderer` with method `renderShape(String shape)`. Concrete classes: `VectorRenderer`, `RasterRenderer`.

2. **Create the Abstraction hierarchy (Abstraction side).**
   Define an abstract class `Shape` that holds a **protected reference** `protected Renderer renderer;` to a `Renderer` object. Subclasses: `Circle`, `Square`.

3. **Inject the Implementor via the constructor (Bridge construction).**
   Each `Shape` subclass receives a `Renderer` object in its constructor: `this.renderer = renderer;`.

4. **Delegate rendering work through the bridge.**
   The `Shape.draw()` method calls `renderer.renderShape("Circle")` — the shape doesn't know *how* it is rendered, only *that* it is rendered.

5. **Client wires the bridge at runtime.**
   The client creates a `VectorRenderer` and passes it to a new `Circle`. The same `Circle` could later be given a `RasterRenderer` without changing `Circle`'s code.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Context |
|---|---------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Class count **without** Bridge | $C_{naive} = A \times I$ | $A$ = #Abstractions, $I$ = #Implementations |
| 2 | Class count **with** Bridge | $C_{bridge} = A + I + 1$ | (+1 for the Implementor interface) |
| 3 | Reduction factor | $R = \dfrac{A \times I}{A + I + 1}$ | Higher $R$ = more benefit |
| 4 | Coupling type | Composition (HAS-A) | "Abstraction HAS-A Implementor" |
| 5 | Binding time | Runtime (dynamic) | Implementation is injected post-instantiation |
| 6 | Inheritance used for | Each hierarchy separately | NOT across abstraction↔implementation |
| 7 | OOP Principle satisfied | Open/Closed, Single Responsibility | Adding new types needs no modification of existing code |
| 8 | Pattern category | Structural (Object Structural) | Uses object composition |
| 9 | GoF name | Bridge (Alias: Handle/Body) | GoF 1994 catalogue |
| 10 | Distinguishing keyword | "Decouple" / "Two independent dimensions" | Exam recognition trigger |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The phrase *"decouple abstraction from implementation"* appearing in a 14-mark question is the **strongest signal** that the expected answer is the Bridge pattern. Do not confuse it with **Adapter** (which makes incompatible interfaces work together after the fact) or **Strategy** (which encapsulates interchangeable algorithms — but doesn't separate a *type hierarchy* from its *implementation hierarchy*).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bridge pattern is heavily used in:

- **Java AWT** — `Component` (abstraction) ↔ `ComponentPeer` (implementor) bridges Java GUI to platform-specific peers.
- **JDBC** — `DriverManager` abstraction bridges to vendor-specific `Driver` implementations.
- **Device Drivers** — OS abstraction layer bridges to vendor-specific hardware.
- **GUI Frameworks** — Window abstraction bridges to platform-specific windowing systems (X11, Win32, Cocoa).
- **Cryptography Libraries** — `Cipher` abstraction bridges to provider-specific implementations (AES, RSA providers).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Application Question Hook:** If asked *"Give a real-world example of the Bridge pattern"*, **AWT Peer classes** or **JDBC DriverManager** are the safest, most-credited answers in KTU valuation keys.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Class Explosion Problem (Quantitative Proof)

Let us **mathematically derive** why the Bridge pattern is necessary. Assume:
- $A$ = number of independent **abstraction types** (e.g., Shapes)
- $I$ = number of independent **implementation types** (e.g., Renderers)

### Step 1: Naive Inheritance Count

In the naive approach, every combination must be its own subclass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{naive} &= \underbrace{A \times I}_{\text{every } (a,i) \text{ pair is a class}} \\
&= A \cdot I
\end{aligned}
$$

For $A = 3, \; I = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{naive} &= 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ classes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Bridge Pattern Class Count

In the Bridge pattern, the two hierarchies are independent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{bridge} &= \underbrace{A}_{\text{abstraction classes}} + \underbrace{I}_{\text{concrete implementors}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{Implementor interface}} \\
&= A + I + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $A = 3, \; I = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{bridge} &= 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 \text{ classes (interface case)} \\
C_{bridge}^{\text{abstract}} &= 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ (if Implementor is an abstract class)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: The "Real" Benefit — Decoupling, Not Raw Count

The class count is **often similar** for small $A, I$, but the **structural** benefit is what matters. The true metric is the **modification cost** when adding a new shape or renderer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New Shape Cost (Naive)} &= I \text{ new classes} \\
\text{New Shape Cost (Bridge)} &= 1 \text{ new class} \\
\text{Savings} &= I - 1 \text{ classes avoided}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the math:** Every time you add a new shape in the naive approach, you must duplicate it for every renderer. With Bridge, you add **one** shape class that delegates rendering to the injected `Renderer`. This is the *Open/Closed Principle* made concrete.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the Bridge Pattern (UML-to-Code Derivation)

Below is the **exhaustive, line-by-line derivation** of the Bridge pattern from a Shape/Renderer problem.

### Step A — Define the Implementor Interface

```java
// IMPLEMENTOR HIERARCHY (the "body" or implementation side)
public interface Renderer {
    void renderCircle(double radius);
    void renderSquare(double side);
}
```

*Conversion logic:* The `Renderer` interface declares the **primitive operations**. It deliberately does **not** match the Shape interface — this asymmetry is the signature of the Bridge pattern (Adapter would have matched interfaces; Strategy would have a single method).

### Step B — Define Concrete Implementors

```java
public class VectorRenderer implements Renderer {
    public void renderCircle(double radius) {
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle of radius " + radius + " using VECTORS");
    }
    public void renderSquare(double side) {
        System.out.println("Drawing Square of side " + side + " using VECTORS");
    }
}

public class RasterRenderer implements Renderer {
    public void renderCircle(double radius) {
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle of radius " + radius + " using PIXELS");
    }
    public void renderSquare(double side) {
        System.out.println("Drawing Square of side " + side + " using PIXELS");
    }
}
```

*Conversion logic:* Two concrete implementations exist; they can grow independently of the Shape hierarchy.

### Step C — Define the Abstraction

```java
// ABSTRACTION HIERARCHY (the "handle" side)
public abstract class Shape {
    protected Renderer renderer;   // <-- THIS REFERENCE IS THE "BRIDGE"

    public Shape(Renderer renderer) {
        this.renderer = renderer;  // Composition: HAS-A
    }

    public abstract void draw();
    public abstract void resize(double factor);
}
```

*Conversion logic:* `Shape` holds a `Renderer` reference — the **literal bridge** between the two hierarchies. It is `protected` so subclasses can access it.

### Step D — Define Refined Abstractions

```java
public class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;

    public Circle(Renderer renderer, double radius) {
        super(renderer);
        this.radius = radius;
    }

    public void draw() {
        renderer.renderCircle(radius);   // Delegates to the bridge
    }

    public void resize(double factor) {
        radius *= factor;
    }
}

public class Square extends Shape {
    private double side;

    public Square(Renderer renderer, double side) {
        super(renderer);
        this.side = side;
    }

    public void draw() {
        renderer.renderSquare(side);     // Delegates to the bridge
    }

    public void resize(double factor) {
        side *= factor;
    }
}
```

*Conversion logic:* Each `RefinedAbstraction` (Circle, Square) **delegates** rendering work to the injected `Renderer` — the shape knows nothing about vectors or pixels.

### Step E — Client Wires the Bridge at Runtime

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Renderer vector = new VectorRenderer();
        Renderer raster = new RasterRenderer();

        Shape circle = new Circle(vector, 5.0);
        circle.draw();   // Output: Drawing Circle of radius 5.0 using VECTORS

        // Runtime bridge swap:
        circle = new Circle(raster, 5.0);
        circle.draw();   // Output: Drawing Circle of radius 5.0 using PIXELS
    }
}
```

*Conversion logic:* The same `Circle` object can be **re-bridged** to a different `Renderer` at runtime — this is **dynamic binding of implementation**, a property impossible in the naive inheritance approach.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Bridge vs Other Patterns

| Criterion | **Bridge** | **Adapter** | **Strategy** | **Decorator** |
|----------|-----------|------------|--------------|---------------|
| Intent | Decouple abstraction from implementation | Make incompatible interfaces compatible | Encapsulate interchangeable algorithms | Add behaviour dynamically |
| When applied | **At design time** (upfront) | After the fact (post-design) | At design time | At runtime |
| Structure | Two parallel hierarchies | One class wrapping another | One algorithm interface, many implementations | One wrapper class stacking responsibilities |
| Inheritance? | Each hierarchy uses inheritance | Single class + composition | Single hierarchy (algorithms) | Single class + composition |
| Distinct phrase | "Two independent dimensions" | "Reuse an existing incompatible class" | "Choose algorithm at runtime" | "Add responsibility without subclassing" |
| KTU typical question | "Design a system with N shapes and M renderers" | "Integrate a third-party library" | "Choose sorting algorithm at runtime" | "Add scrollbars to windows dynamically" |

---

## 3.4 Applicability Checklist (Decision Matrix)

Use this matrix to decide **when to apply Bridge** in exam answers:

| Condition Met? | If YES → Use Bridge |
|---|---|
| Are there two independent dimensions of variation? | ✅ |
| Do you want runtime implementation switching? | ✅ |
| Do you want to hide implementation from clients? | ✅ |
| Is class explosion (combinatorial inheritance) a concern? | ✅ |
| Do abstraction and implementation need to evolve independently? | ✅ |

If **all five** are YES, the Bridge pattern is the correct choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical GoF UML Class Diagram (Bridge Pattern)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Abstraction {
        -implementor : Implementor
        +operation()
    }
    class RefinedAbstraction {
        +operation()
    }
    class Implementor {
        <<interface>>
        +operationImpl()
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorA {
        +operationImpl()
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorB {
        +operationImpl()
    }
    class Client {
        +main()
    }

    RefinedAbstraction --|> Abstraction
    Abstraction o-- Implementor : bridge (composition)
    ConcreteImplementorA ..|> Implementor
    ConcreteImplementorB ..|> Implementor
    Client ..> Abstraction : uses
    Client ..> Implementor : instantiates
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `RefinedAbstraction` **inherits** from `Abstraction` (Refinement IS-A Abstraction).
- `Abstraction` **holds a reference** (diamond `o--`) to `Implementor` — the **bridge itself**.
- `ConcreteImplementorA` and `B` **realize** the `Implementor` interface (dashed arrow `..|>`).
