- `Client` instantiates both hierarchies and **plugs** them together.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Shape/Renderer Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Code] --> B[Choose Renderer Implementation]
    B --> C{Renderer Type?}
    C -->|Vector| D[VectorRenderer Instance]
    C -->|Raster| E[RasterRenderer Instance]
    D --> F[Pass Renderer into Shape Constructor]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Shape Object with Bridge Reference]
    G --> H[Call Shape.draw Method]
    H --> I[Shape Delegates to Renderer]
    I --> J[Renderer Renders Geometric Primitives]
    J --> K[Output: Vector Lines or Pixel Grid]
```

**Reading the flowchart:**
1. The **Client** decides which implementation to use (Vector or Raster).
2. The chosen `Renderer` is **injected** into the `Shape` constructor — this is the bridge being built.
3. When `Shape.draw()` is called, control **flows across the bridge** to the `Renderer`.
4. The `Renderer` does the actual drawing, completely independent of the Shape's class hierarchy.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Runtime Bridge Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Shape as Shape Abstraction
    participant BridgeRef as Renderer Reference (Bridge)
    participant ConcreteImpl as Concrete Implementor

    Client->>Shape: new Circle(renderer, 5.0)
    Shape->>BridgeRef: store renderer reference
    Client->>Shape: draw()
    Shape->>BridgeRef: renderCircle(5.0)
    BridgeRef->>ConcreteImpl: renderCircle(5.0)
    ConcreteImpl-->>BridgeRef: rendering complete
    BridgeRef-->>Shape: returns
    Shape-->>Client: draw complete
```

**Reading the sequence:**
- The `Shape` never knows what `ConcreteImpl` it's talking to — only that it conforms to `Renderer`.
- The **vertical arrows crossing the abstraction/implementation boundary** in the diagram are literally the "bridge" the pattern is named after.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Bridge Design Pattern. List its four canonical participants.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Bridge pattern is a **Structural Design Pattern** that decouples an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently. It uses **object composition (a reference field)** instead of inheritance to bind the abstraction to its implementation, allowing both to be extended without mutual modification.

**Four Canonical Participants (1.5 marks):**
1. **Abstraction** — defines the high-level control interface and holds a reference to the Implementor.
2. **Refined Abstraction** — extends Abstraction to provide variant behaviours.
3. **Implementor** — declares the interface for implementation classes (not required to match Abstraction's interface).
4. **Concrete Implementor** — provides the concrete implementation behind the Implementor interface.

*(0.5 marks reserved for correct classification as Structural Pattern and mention of "object composition".)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Bridge pattern and the Adapter pattern in one line each. State one scenario where Bridge is preferred over Adapter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Pattern | One-line Definition |
|---------|---------------------|
| **Bridge** | Decouples abstraction from implementation so both can vary independently; applied **at design time**. |
| **Adapter** | Converts the interface of an existing class into another interface the client expects; applied **after the fact** to integrate incompatible code. |

**Preferred scenario (1 mark):**
When developing a system from scratch that has **two independent dimensions of variation** (e.g., Shapes × Renderers, UI Themes × Platforms), the Bridge pattern is preferred because it prevents the combinatorial class explosion that would result from inheritance. Adapter is used only when you must integrate an **existing, incompatible third-party class** that you cannot redesign.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **intent, structure, and participants** of the Bridge design pattern with a suitable **UML class diagram**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a **Document Management System** that needs to support:
- Document types: `Report`, `Spreadsheet`, `Presentation`
- Persistence formats: `FileSystemStorage`, `DatabaseStorage`, `CloudStorage`

Design the system using the Bridge pattern. Show the **complete Java class structure** and explain how the pattern resolves the combinatorial class explosion problem. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Intent (2 marks):**
The Bridge pattern **decouples an abstraction from its implementation** so that the two can vary independently. It is used when both the abstraction (e.g., document type) and the implementation (e.g., storage mechanism) have multiple variants and we want to avoid a permanent binding between them.

**Structure (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Document {
        -storage : Storage
        +Document(Storage s)
        +open()
        +save()
    }
    class Report {
        +open()
        +save()
    }
    class Spreadsheet {
        +open()
        +save()
    }
    class Presentation {
        +open()
        +save()
    }
    class Storage {
        <<interface>>
        +saveData(byte[])
        +loadData()
    }
    class FileSystemStorage {
        +saveData(byte[])
        +loadData()
    }
    class DatabaseStorage {
        +saveData(byte[])
        +loadData()
    }
    class CloudStorage {
        +saveData(byte[])
        +loadData()
    }

    Report --|> Document
    Spreadsheet --|> Document
    Presentation --|> Document
    Document o-- Storage : bridge
    FileSystemStorage ..|> Storage
    DatabaseStorage ..|> Storage
    CloudStorage ..|> Storage
```

**Participants (2 marks):**
- **Abstraction:** `Document` (abstract class with reference to `Storage`)
- **RefinedAbstraction:** `Report`, `Spreadsheet`, `Presentation`
- **Implementor:** `Storage` (interface)
- **ConcreteImplementor:** `FileSystemStorage`, `DatabaseStorage`, `CloudStorage`

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Naive count (1 mark):**
Without Bridge: $3 \text{ document types} \times 3 \text{ storage types} = 9 \text{ classes}$ (`FileSystemReport`, `DatabaseReport`, `CloudReport`, `FileSystemSpreadsheet`, …).

**With Bridge count (1 mark):**
$3 + 3 + 1 = 7 \text{ classes}$ (3 documents + 3 storages + 1 Storage interface).

**Java Implementation (5 marks):**

```java
// 1. Implementor Interface
public interface Storage {
    void saveData(byte[] data, String filename);
    byte[] loadData(String filename);
}

// 2. Concrete Implementors
public class FileSystemStorage implements Storage {
    public void saveData(byte[] data, String filename) {
        System.out.println("Saving " + filename + " to local file system.");
    }
    public byte[] loadData(String filename) {
        System.out.println("Loading " + filename + " from local file system.");
        return new byte[0];
    }
}

public class DatabaseStorage implements Storage {
    public void saveData(byte[] data, String filename) {
        System.out.println("Saving " + filename + " to relational database.");
    }
    public byte[] loadData(String filename) {
        System.out.println("Loading " + filename + " from database.");
        return new byte[0];
    }
}

public class CloudStorage implements Storage {
    public void saveData(byte[] data, String filename) {
        System.out.println("Uploading " + filename + " to cloud (S3-compatible).");
    }
    public byte[] loadData(String filename) {
        System.out.println("Downloading " + filename + " from cloud.");
        return new byte[0];
    }
}

// 3. Abstraction
public abstract class Document {
    protected Storage storage;   // The BRIDGE reference
    protected String filename;

    public Document(Storage storage, String filename) {
        this.storage = storage;
        this.filename = filename;
    }

    public abstract void save(byte[] data);
    public abstract void open();
}

// 4. Refined Abstractions
public class Report extends Document {
    public Report(Storage storage, String filename) { super(storage, filename); }
    public void save(byte[] data) { storage.saveData(data, filename); }
    public void open() { storage.loadData(filename); }
}

public class Spreadsheet extends Document {
    public Spreadsheet(Storage storage, String filename) { super(storage, filename); }
    public void save(byte[] data) { storage.saveData(data, filename); }
    public void open() { storage.loadData(filename); }
}

public class Presentation extends Document {
    public Presentation(Storage storage, String filename) { super(storage, filename); }
    public void save(byte[] data) { storage.saveData(data, filename); }
    public void open() { storage.loadData(filename); }
}

// 5. Client
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Storage cloud = new CloudStorage();
        Document report = new Report(cloud, "Q4_Financials.pdf");
        report.save("Annual report data".getBytes());
        // The same Report class could be re-bridged to DatabaseStorage at runtime
    }
}
```

**Explanation of how Bridge solves the class explosion (the "Why"):**
By separating the *Document* hierarchy from the *Storage* hierarchy and connecting them via a **runtime-injected reference field**, the system grows **linearly** ($3 + 3 + 1 = 7$ classes) instead of **multiplicatively** ($3 \times 3 = 9$ classes). Adding a new document type (e.g., `PDFDocument`) requires **only one new class** — it automatically works with all three storage types. Similarly, adding a new storage type (e.g., `RedisCacheStorage`) requires **only one new class** — it automatically works with all document types.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **motivation** behind the Bridge pattern. Why does the Bridge pattern prefer **composition over inheritance**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A banking application must support:
- Account types: `SavingsAccount`, `CurrentAccount`, `FixedDepositAccount`
- Interest calculation strategies: `SimpleInterest`, `CompoundInterest`, `NoInterest`

Critically analyze whether the **Bridge pattern** is the most appropriate design choice here, or whether the **Strategy pattern** would be a better fit. Justify with reference to the design intent of both patterns. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Motivation (3 marks):**
When a system has **two (or more) independent dimensions of variation**, the natural instinct is to use inheritance to capture all combinations. For example, with $A$ abstraction types and $I$ implementations, naive inheritance requires $A \times I$ classes. Adding a new variation in **either** dimension forces multiplication in the other dimension. This:
- Causes a **combinatorial explosion** of classes
- **Couples** abstraction permanently to implementation
- Makes **runtime switching** impossible
- Violates the **Open/Closed Principle**

The Bridge pattern solves this by **splitting** the two dimensions into **separate class hierarchies** and **bridging** them through a **reference field** (composition) at runtime.

**Why Composition over Inheritance (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Inheritance (Anti-Pattern) | Composition (Bridge) |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Binding time | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| Coupling | Tight — subclass locked to parent | Loose — reference can be swapped |
| Flexibility | Cannot change parent at runtime | Can swap implementation object |
| Class growth | $A \times I$ (multiplicative) | $A + I + 1$ (additive) |
| Reusability | Reuse limited by inheritance chain | Implementor can be reused across many abstractions |
| OOP principle | "IS-A" relationship | "HAS-A" relationship |

**Additional rationale (2 marks within 4):**
- Gang of Four principle: *"Favor object composition over class inheritance."*
- Composition lets the **abstraction hierarchy** and **implementation hierarchy** evolve independently — modifying `VectorRenderer` does not require recompiling `Circle` or `Square`.
- Composition enables **dependency injection**, which is essential for unit testing (you can inject a mock renderer).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Critical Analysis (5 marks):**

In this banking scenario, the two dimensions are:
- **Account type** (Savings, Current, FD) — these have **distinct behaviours** beyond just interest calculation: withdrawal rules, minimum balance, maturity handling, etc.
- **Interest strategy** (Simple, Compound, None) — these are **interchangeable algorithms** that compute a value.

This is the **classic confusion point** between Bridge and Strategy. Let us dissect:

| Dimension | Account Type | Interest Strategy |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| Nature | A **type hierarchy** with multiple methods and fields (withdraw, deposit, maturity) | A **single-method algorithm** (compute interest) |
| How many methods? | Multiple (open, close, debit, credit, calculateMaturity…) | One (calculateInterest) |
| Identity semantics | IS-A Account | HAS-A calculation method |

**Verdict: Strategy is more appropriate here** (3 marks for the verdict + 2 marks for justification).

**Justification:**
- The interest dimension is a **single behaviour** (an algorithm), not a parallel hierarchy. Strategy's intent is to *"define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable."* This matches exactly.
- Bridge is intended for cases where **both dimensions are full type hierarchies** with their own internal structure. Account type has methods like `debit()`, `credit()`, `maturityCheck()` that are orthogonal to interest calculation — using Bridge would force these to be defined on the `Implementor`, which is semantically wrong.
- The proper design is: `Account` is the abstraction, with subclasses `SavingsAccount`, `CurrentAccount`, `FixedDepositAccount`; and **interest calculation is a Strategy** field inside each account, not a parallel Bridge hierarchy.

**However**, if the question had been *"Account type AND Audit Logging mechanism (Console, File, Database)"*, then **Bridge would be correct** because logging is a **parallel hierarchy** of mechanisms, not a single algorithm.

**When Bridge IS appropriate here (2 marks):**
If we extended the problem to also include **Notification mechanism** (Email, SMS, Push), then we would have **two parallel implementation hierarchies** (Interest + Notification), and combining Bridge + Strategy would be valid. But for a single algorithm like interest, **Strategy alone** is the cleaner solution.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Bridge Pattern Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bridge with Adapter** — A Bridge pattern decouples **abstractions from implementations at design time**; an Adapter **makes existing incompatible interfaces work together** after the fact. If you write "Adapter" instead of "Bridge" for a "two-dimensions" question, you lose **at least 3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Drawing the UML diagram with the wrong arrow types** — The bridge relationship is **composition (filled or hollow diamond `o--` or `*--`)**, NOT inheritance. The Implementor should be an **interface** with a **dashed realization arrow `..|>`** to its ConcreteImplementors. Wrong arrows = **2 marks lost**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Implementor interface** — Many students write only the Abstraction hierarchy and the ConcreteImplementor classes, omitting the `Implementor` interface. This loses **1.5 marks**.
>
> 4. **Writing "Bridge uses inheritance"** — The Bridge pattern **uses composition** to connect the two hierarchies. Inheritance is used **within** each hierarchy (RefinedAbstraction extends Abstraction), but the **bridge itself is composition**. Misstating this loses **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the alias "Handle/Body"** — KTU examiners often award **0.5 marks** for the GoF alias; missing it is a free-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Confusing Bridge with Strategy** — If a question explicitly says "**two independent dimensions**" or "**decouple abstraction from implementation**", it is Bridge. If it says "**interchangeable algorithms**" or "**choose behaviour at runtime**", it is Strategy. Mixing them up costs **heavily** in 14-mark answers.
>
> 7. **Not giving a real-world example** — KTU 2024 Part B questions on design patterns **always expect at least one real-world example** (AWT, JDBC, Device Drivers). Skipping this loses **1 mark**.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Bridge is a **Structural (Object Structural)** GoF pattern — alias **Handle/Body**.
- ✅ **Intent:** Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so both can vary independently.
- ✅ **Mechanism:** Two parallel class hierarchies connected by a **composition reference** (the "bridge").
- ✅ **Four participants:** Abstraction, RefinedAbstraction, Implementor, ConcreteImplementor.
- ✅ **Composition over inheritance** — the bridge is a `protected` reference field in the Abstraction.
- ✅ **Runtime binding** — the implementation can be swapped after object construction.
- ✅ **Class count formula:** Naive $= A \times I$; Bridge $= A + I + 1$.
- ✅ **Recognition keywords:** "two independent dimensions", "decouple", "combinatorial explosion", "orthogonal axes".
- ✅ **Real-world examples to memorize:** Java AWT `Component`/`ComponentPeer`, JDBC `DriverManager`/`Driver`, Device Drivers, GUI toolkits (Window/WindowImpl).
- ✅ **Distinguish from:** Adapter (post-hoc compatibility, single class wrapping) and Strategy (single algorithm interface, no parallel hierarchy).
- ✅ **Design Principle satisfied:** Open/Closed Principle (extend without modifying existing code).
- ✅ **GoF Principle followed:** *"Favor object composition over class inheritance."*
- ✅ **When NOT to use Bridge:** When there is only **one dimension of variation** (use Strategy instead), or when implementation is **fixed at compile time** and never varies (over-engineering).
- ✅ **UML arrow cheat-sheet:** `o--` = composition (bridge), `--|>` = inheritance (refinement), `..|>` = realization (interface implementation).
- ✅ **Mandatory exam phrases to use:** "decouple abstraction from implementation", "composition over inheritance", "two independent dimensions of variation", "runtime binding of implementation".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proxy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proxy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Proxy Pattern (Gang of Four – Structural Pattern):**  
> *"The Proxy design pattern provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object in order to control access to it. The Proxy object has the same interface as the real object and acts on its behalf, adding an additional level of indirection to support distributed, controlled, lazy, or protected access."*

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education)**, the Proxy pattern is classified under **Structural Design Patterns** because it composes objects into larger structures while preserving interface compatibility, adding **behavioral control** through object composition rather than inheritance.

**Standard GoF Vocabulary used in KTU answers:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Subject** | The common interface implemented by both RealSubject and Proxy |
| **RealSubject** | The actual object that does the real business work |
| **Proxy** | The surrogate that holds a reference to RealSubject and controls access |
| **Client** | The code that uses the Subject interface (unaware of whether it gets a real or proxy object) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍽️

Imagine you walk into a **5-star hotel**. You do **not** go directly to the chef (the RealSubject) to get your food. Instead:

- You place an order with the **waiter** (the **Proxy**).
- The waiter takes your order, checks the menu, validates your table reservation, maybe adds a complimentary drink — then **forwards** the request to the kitchen.
- The chef prepares the food; the waiter delivers it back to you.

The waiter is a **surrogate** for the chef. From your point of view, you are interacting with a single entity that "serves food." You don't care whether it is the chef himself or someone representing him. That indirection gives the hotel **control** — over access (non-guests can't enter the kitchen), over ordering (waiters can prioritize), and over caching (waiters can memorize frequent orders).

In software terms:

- The **Chef** = RealSubject (expensive, protected, remote, or resource-heavy object)
- The **Waiter** = Proxy (holds a reference to the Chef, intercepts calls, adds value)
- The **Customer** = Client (talks only to the Subject interface)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight for KTU Answers:**  
> The Proxy and RealSubject share the **same interface**, so the Client is **completely transparent** about the indirection. This is the essence of the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** — any place that accepts a Subject must accept a Proxy without code change.

---

## 1.3 Why Use a Proxy? (KTU High-Yield Bullet Points)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> A Proxy is introduced **only when a direct reference to the real object is impractical, unsafe, or inefficient**. The four canonical "Gang of Four" justifications are:

1. **Remote Proxy** – The RealSubject lives in a different address space (different JVM, different machine). The Proxy hides the network marshalling/unmarshalling complexity. *Example: RMI stubs in Java, gRPC clients.*
2. **Virtual Proxy** – The RealSubject is **expensive to create** (loads large images, opens DB connections). The Proxy creates it **on-demand** (lazy initialization). *Example: Lazy-loading large image thumbnails in a UI.*
3. **Protection Proxy** – The RealSubject must be accessed only by **authorized callers**. The Proxy checks credentials/roles before forwarding. *Example: Spring Security AOP around service methods.*
4. **Smart Reference / Smart Proxy** – The Proxy adds housekeeping such as **reference counting, locking, caching, logging, or transaction management**. *Example: Hibernate's lazy-loading proxies for entity associations.*

---

## 1.4 Subject Standards & Boundaries (Quantified for KTU)

- **Identity Boundary:** A Proxy **must** implement *every* method of the Subject interface (LSP contract).
- **Lifecycle Boundary:** A Proxy *may* be created eagerly (for Protection/Smart) or lazily (for Virtual).
- **Performance Metric (typical):** A Virtual Proxy reduces memory footprint by **30–70%** in image-heavy UIs by deferring image decoding until the bitmap is actually painted.
- **Network Metric (typical):** A Remote Proxy adds **1 to 3 round-trip latencies** but saves the cost of duplicating business logic on the client.

---

## 1.5 Quick Syllabus Snapshot for KTU 2024

| KTU Module 2 Sub-Topic | Proxy Pattern Coverage |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| Structural Patterns | ✅ Listed as a core pattern |
| UML Class Diagram | ✅ Mandatory to draw |
| Participants | ✅ Must list all 4 |
| Collaborations | ✅ Must describe call sequence |
| Use-case justification | ✅ Must give a real example |
| Comparison with Decorator | ✅ Frequently asked in 14-mark Q |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Proxy Pattern – Operational Anatomy

The Proxy pattern works on the principle of **"same interface, controlled indirection."** Let us break it into seven precise logical steps:

1. **Define a Subject interface** that declares the operations the Client wants to perform. This is the *contract* that both RealSubject and Proxy must honour.
2. **Implement RealSubject** with the actual business logic. This object may be expensive, remote, protected, or all three.
3. **Implement Proxy** with the *same* Subject interface. Internally, the Proxy holds a reference (typically `private RealSubject realSubject;`) to the RealSubject instance.
4. **Client invokes a method on the Proxy**, believing it is the real object (interface transparency).
5. **Proxy intercepts the call** and decides *what to do*:
   - *For Virtual Proxy:* Check if realSubject is null; if so, create it (lazy instantiation).
   - *For Protection Proxy:* Check caller's credentials/role.
   - *For Remote Proxy:* Marshal arguments, send over network, wait, unmarshal result.
   - *For Smart Proxy:* Add reference counting, lock acquisition, logging, or caching.
6. **Proxy forwards the call** to RealSubject (`realSubject.request()`).
7. **Result is returned to the Client**, possibly after the Proxy performs additional post-processing (e.g., cache the result, release a lock).

---

## 2.2 Why This Works — The Design Rationale

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Why" behind each step:**

- **Same interface (step 1):** Without a shared interface, the Client would have to know whether it holds a Proxy or a RealSubject, breaking the *Open/Closed Principle*.
- **Lazy creation (step 5a):** Building a RealSubject upfront may consume memory, file handles, or DB connections that the Client may never actually use.
- **Access control (step 5b):** RealSubjects often contain privileged operations; the Proxy becomes a *gatekeeper*, encapsulating the security policy in **one** place.
- **Marshalling (step 5c):** The Client should not be polluted with `ObjectOutputStream`/`ObjectInputStream` plumbing; the Proxy encapsulates remote communication.
- **Post-processing (step 7):** Cross-cutting concerns (logging, caching, transaction commit) are best applied **around** the call, not **inside** it. This is the **AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming)** philosophy.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Proxy Use-Case |
|--------------------|----------------|
| **Distributed Systems** | RMI stubs, gRPC stubs, REST client SDKs are all Remote Proxies |
| **Web Development** | Nginx reverse proxy in front of Tomcat servers (Reverse Proxy) |
| **ORM Frameworks** | Hibernate lazy-loads associations via bytecode-generated proxies |
| **GUI Frameworks** | JavaFX/Swing use Virtual Proxies for image placeholders |
| **Cybersecurity** | Firewalls act as Protection Proxies between internal and external networks |
| **CDN Networks** | Edge servers are Caching Proxies that shield origin servers |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The Proxy pattern is a **qualitative** pattern — it has no algebraic formulas. However, KTU examiners often expect **quantified reasoning** about its impact. The following table summarizes the **decision metrics** a Proxy affects.

| Metric | Without Proxy | With Proxy | Trade-off Note |
|--------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| **Memory Footprint (M)** | M_real loaded eagerly | M_proxy + (lazy) M_real | Saves memory if RealSubject never used |
| **Latency (L)** | L_local only | L_proxy + L_marshal + L_network + L_unmarshal | Adds 1 to 3 hops |
| **First-Response Time (T₁)** | High (full init) | Low (proxy-only init) | RealSubject init cost deferred to first use |
| **Security Surface (S)** | S_real = full | S_proxy = filtered | Protection proxy reduces attack surface |
| **Throughput (Θ)** | Θ_real | Θ_proxy ≈ Θ_real × (1 − α) | α = overhead fraction, typically 0.02 to 0.10 |
| **Coupling (C)** | Client → RealSubject (tight) | Client → Subject (loose) | Reduces coupling by 1 level of indirection |

**Key Engineering Equation — Effective Latency:**

$$
L_{\text{effective}} = L_{\text{local}} + L_{\text{marshal}} + L_{\text{network}} + L_{\text{unmarshal}}
$$

> Where each component is measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. For a Remote Proxy over LAN: $L_{\text{network}} \approx 0.5\text{ ms}$; over WAN: $L_{\text{network}} \approx 20\text{ ms}$ to $200\text{ ms}$.

**Lazy-Loading Memory Savings Equation:**

$$
M_{\text{saved}} = M_{\text{real}} - M_{\text{proxy}} \quad \text{when} \quad M_{\text{real}} \gg M_{\text{proxy}}
$$

For image-heavy UIs, $M_{\text{real}} \approx 4\text{ MB}$ per image vs. $M_{\text{proxy}} \approx 64\text{ bytes}$; savings are of order $10^5$ per image.

**Reference Counting (Smart Proxy) Equation:**

$$
\text{Live Instances} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{refcount}_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{release}_j
$$

When $\text{Live Instances} = 0$, the Proxy deallocates the RealSubject (deterministic cleanup).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive UML Class Structure (Step-by-Step Derivation)

The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to **derive** the class diagram step-by-step, justifying each relationship. Below is the complete analytical walkthrough.

### Step 1 — Identify the Abstract Contract

The pattern starts with an **interface** (or abstract class) that captures the *uniform* operations both RealSubject and Proxy must expose. We declare this as `Subject` with method `request()`.

### Step 2 — Implement the Real Object

`RealSubject` implements `Subject` and contains the *actual* business logic. Its `request()` method performs the real work — for example, loading a 4 MB image from disk.

### Step 3 — Implement the Surrogate

`Proxy` also implements `Subject` (this is **non-negotiable** for transparency). Inside, it declares a *reference* to a `RealSubject`:

$$
\text{Proxy} \;\; \text{has-a} \;\; \text{RealSubject} \quad \text{(composition, not inheritance)}
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Client

`Client` depends only on the `Subject` interface. It invokes `subject.request()` polymorphically.

### Step 5 — Draw the Relationships

The relationships that emerge are:

- **Client → Subject**: Association (Client holds a reference to a Subject).
- **Proxy → Subject**: Realization (implements the interface).
- **RealSubject → Subject**: Realization (implements the interface).
- **Proxy → RealSubject**: Association (Proxy holds a reference to the real object).

### Step 6 — Translate to Class Diagram Notation

In standard UML:

- **Realization** = dashed hollow triangle arrow (------▷).
- **Association** = solid line (possibly with multiplicity).
- **Composition** (when Proxy owns and destroys RealSubject) = solid line with filled diamond (◆).

The derived diagram is:

```
                  <<interface>>
                    Subject
                   + request()
                        △
                        ‖ (realization)
                        ‖
              ┌─────────┴─────────┐
              │                   │
        RealSubject            Proxy
       + request()           - realSubject : RealSubject
       (real work)           + request()
                              + getRealSubject()
```

### Step 7 — Verify LSP Compliance

For every method $m$ in Subject, both `RealSubject.m()` and `Proxy.m()` must have:

- Identical signature.
- Compatible pre-conditions (Proxy may **weaken** pre-conditions, never strengthen).
- Compatible post-conditions (Proxy may **strengthen** post-conditions, never weaken).
- Same exception contract (Proxy may translate but not add new exceptions).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Substitution verification (formal):**  
> For all clients $C$, and for all programs $P$ using Subject:  
> If $P$ behaves correctly with a RealSubject instance, then $P$ must also behave correctly when the RealSubject is replaced by a Proxy. This is the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** in formal notation.

$$
\forall C, \forall m \in \text{Subject}: \;\; C.m(\text{RealSubject}) \equiv_{\text{observable}} C.m(\text{Proxy})
$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Sequence Derivation (Call Flow)

Below is the **exhaustive sequence of calls** when a Virtual Proxy is used:

1. Client calls `proxy.request()`.
2. Proxy checks: `if (realSubject == null)`.
3. If true, Proxy instantiates: `realSubject = new RealSubject();` ← *expensive step deferred*.
4. Proxy may perform *pre-processing*: e.g., `log("Access at " + System.nanoTime());`.
5. Proxy invokes: `realSubject.request();`.
6. RealSubject does the real work and returns result `r`.
7. Proxy may perform *post-processing*: e.g., `cache.put("request", r);`.
8. Proxy returns `r` to Client.

Total operations in flow: **8 distinct steps**, of which **Step 3** is the only one with non-trivial cost on first invocation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All Five Proxy Variants Compared

The following matrix is **examination-critical**. KTU frequently asks to *"Compare types of proxies"* for 7 marks.

| Aspect | Virtual Proxy | Remote Proxy | Protection Proxy | Smart Proxy | Caching Proxy |
|--------|---------------|--------------|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Primary Intent** | Lazy creation | Hide network | Enforce access | Add housekeeping | Memoize results |
| **RealSubject Lifetime** | Deferred until first use | Persistent on remote JVM | Persistent | Persistent | Persistent |
| **Where Logic Lives** | Client side | Client side (stub) | Client or server | Client side | Client side |
| **Performance Cost** | Low (one-time init) | Medium (network) | Low | Low to medium | Low (after warm-up) |
| **Memory Profile** | Small | Small | Small | Small | Grows with cache |
| **Example** | Image placeholder | RMI / gRPC stub | Spring Security | Reference counter | HTTP cache, CDN |
| **Failure Mode** | First-call latency spike | Network timeout | AuthorizationException | Resource leak if not released | Stale cache |
| **KTU 2024 Status** | Most-asked variant | Frequently paired with RMI | Often asked in 14-mark | Less frequent | Less frequent |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Java-Style Skeleton (Pseudocode, Conceptual)

> [!WARNING]  
> KTU does **not** require compilable code, but examiners reward students who show a **clean skeleton**. The following is *conceptual* pseudo-code that illustrates the pattern; it is *not* a runnable program.

**Subject Interface (conceptual):**

```
interface Subject {
    Object request();
}
```

**RealSubject Implementation (conceptual):**

```
class RealSubject implements Subject {
    public Object request() {
        // actual expensive work
        return computeResult();
    }
}
```

**Proxy Implementation (conceptual):**

```
class Proxy implements Subject {
    private RealSubject realSubject;  // null until first use
    
    public Object request() {
        if (realSubject == null) {
            realSubject = new RealSubject();   // lazy init
        }
        log("Pre-processing checkpoint");
        Object result = realSubject.request();
        log("Post-processing checkpoint");
        return result;
    }
}
```

**Client (conceptual):**

```
class Client {
    public void doWork() {
        Subject s = new Proxy();   // Client never sees RealSubject
        s.request();               // virtual call dispatched to Proxy
    }
}
```

**Analysis of the above:**

- The Client **does not import** `RealSubject`. It only knows `Subject`.
- The Proxy **defers** creation until the first `request()`.
- Adding logging to all calls requires changing **one class** (Proxy) — the Proxy centralizes the cross-cutting concern.

---

## 3.5 Comparison: Proxy vs Decorator (Frequently Asked)

| Criterion | Proxy | Decorator |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| **Primary Purpose** | **Control access** to the wrapped object | **Add responsibilities/behavior** to the wrapped object |
| **Relationship to wrapped object** | Often creates/owns the RealSubject (lazy) | Always **receives** the wrapped object from outside (composition by reference) |
| **Awareness of identity** | Proxy may hide the *existence* of the RealSubject | Decorator usually delegates explicitly to the constructor-injected object |
| **Typical use** | Security, lazy loading, remote stubs | UI scrollbars, borders, dynamic streams |
| **Number of decorators** | Usually **one** Proxy | Can **stack** multiple Decorators |
| **Interface compliance** | Identical interface, mandatory | Identical interface, mandatory |
| **KTU marking hint** | "Control access" — emphasize gatekeeping | "Add behavior" — emphasize composition chain |

**Mnemonic for KTU Answers:**  
> *"**Proxy decides IF you can call.**  
> **Decorator decides WHAT happens when you call.**"*

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram (Proxy Pattern Architecture)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ISubject {
        <<interface>>
        +request() Object
    }
    class RealSubject {
        +request() Object
        -heavyData Data
        +loadFromDisk() void
    }
    class ProxyObj {
        -realSubject RealSubject
        -callerRole String
        +request() Object
        +checkAccess() boolean
        +lazyInit() void
    }
    class ClientCode {
        +main(String args) void
        -subject ISubject
    }

    ISubject <|.. RealSubject : realizes
    ISubject <|.. ProxyObj : realizes
    ProxyObj o-- RealSubject : holds reference
    ClientCode --> ISubject : depends on
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram reading guide for KTU students:**  
> - `ISubject` is an interface (shown by `<<interface>>` stereotype and italicized name).  
> - `RealSubject` and `ProxyObj` both **realize** `ISubject` (dashed arrow with hollow triangle).  
> - `ProxyObj` has a **composition** relationship with `RealSubject` (filled diamond `o--`).  
> - `ClientCode` depends only on `ISubject`, not on either concrete class — this is **Dependency Inversion** in action.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram (Lazy Virtual Proxy Call Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CL as Client
    participant PX as Proxy
    participant RS as RealSubject
    participant LG as Logger

    CL->>PX: request()
    Note over PX: Check if realSubject is null

    alt First call (realSubject is null)
        PX->>PX: realSubject = new RealSubject()
        PX->>LG: log("Lazy init at " + time)
    else Subsequent call (realSubject exists)
        Note over PX: Skip initialization
    end

    PX->>LG: log("Pre-processing")
    PX->>RS: request()
    RS-->>PX: return result
    PX->>LG: log("Post-processing")
    PX-->>CL: return result
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU reading strategy:** When asked *"Explain the dynamic behavior of the Proxy pattern"*, refer to this sequence. The `alt` block highlights **lazy initialization**, which is the most distinctive feature of a Virtual Proxy.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Five Proxy Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Application] --> B{Subject Interface}

    B --> C1[Virtual Proxy]
    B --> C2[Remote Proxy]
    B --> C3[Protection Proxy]
    B --> C4[Smart Proxy]
    B --> C5[Caching Proxy]

    C1 --> D1[RealSubject - lazy create on first use]
    C2 --> D2[RealSubject on remote JVM]
    C3 --> D3[RealSubject behind security gate]
    C4 --> D4[RealSubject with refcount and lock]
    C5 --> D5[RealSubject with result cache store]

    D1 --> E[Shared Business Logic]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style E fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **How to read this in an exam:** All five Proxy variants wrap the **same** `RealSubject` business logic (the green box at the bottom). What differs is the **pre- and post-processing** logic at the Proxy level. This is why KTU expects you to identify the **variant** from the use-case description.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Proxy Variant to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Why do you need a Proxy?} -->|Object is expensive to create| Q2[Use Virtual Proxy]
    Q1 -->|Object is on another machine| Q3[Use Remote Proxy]
    Q1 -->|Object needs access control| Q4[Use Protection Proxy]
    Q1 -->|Object needs bookkeeping| Q5[Use Smart Proxy]
    Q1 -->|Object results are reused| Q6[Use Caching Proxy]

    Q2 --> R1[Defer construction until first method call]
    Q3 --> R2[Encapsulate network marshalling in stub]
    Q4 --> R3[Validate credentials before delegation]
    Q5 --> R4[Add reference count and lock around call]
    Q6 --> R5[Store result in map keyed by arguments]

    style Q1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style R1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style R2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style R3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style R4 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style R5 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
```

---

## 4.5 Real-World Mapping — Proxy Variants in Industry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Industry["Industry Use-Cases"]
        H1[Java RMI Stub]
        H2[Hibernate Lazy Fetch]
        H3[Spring Security AOP]
        H4[Nginx Reverse Proxy]
        H5[CDN Edge Cache]
    end

    subgraph Pattern["Proxy Variant"]
        P1[Remote Proxy]
        P2[Virtual Proxy]
        P3[Protection Proxy]
        P4[Reverse Protection Proxy]
        P5[Caching Proxy]
    end

    H1 --- P1
    H2 --- P2
    H3 --- P3
    H4 --- P4
    H5 --- P5
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU 2024:** The question *"Give a real-world example of Proxy pattern"* is worth **2 to 3 marks**. Map your example to the variant. For instance: *"Nginx in front of Tomcat is a Protection + Caching Proxy because it filters malicious requests and serves cached responses."*

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 — Definition Type `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Define the Proxy design pattern. List any two situations where it is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Proxy design pattern** is a *structural pattern* from the Gang of Four catalog that provides a **surrogate or placeholder** for another object to control access to it.

The Proxy and the RealSubject **share the same interface**, so the client remains unaware of the indirection. The Proxy intercepts calls and may perform additional operations such as **lazy initialization, access checks, marshalling, or caching** before forwarding the call to the RealSubject.

**Two situations of application (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Virtual Proxy** — when an object is expensive to create (e.g., large image, DB connection). The Proxy defers creation until the object is actually needed, reducing memory and startup time.

2. **Protection Proxy** — when access to an object must be restricted to authorized users. The Proxy validates credentials or roles before forwarding the request, centralizing the security policy.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 0.75 marks per situation with example. Total = **3 marks**.

---

### Question A2 — Comparison Type `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Proxy and Adapter patterns in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proxy | Adapter |
|--------|-------|---------|
| **Purpose** | Controls **access** to an object with the same interface | Converts the **interface** of an existing class to a different one the client expects |
| **Relationship** | Implements the **same** interface as the RealSubject | Implements a **different** interface than the Adaptee |
| **Wrapping Logic** | Adds behavior **around** the call (pre/post processing) | Translates calls from one interface to another |

**Two crisp points (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Proxy preserves the interface; Adapter changes it.** A Proxy and its RealSubject expose identical methods to the Client; an Adapter exposes a different interface from the Adaptee it wraps.

2. **Proxy adds a level of indirection for control; Adapter adds a level of indirection for compatibility.** Proxy decides *if* and *when* to forward; Adapter decides *how* to translate the call.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Drawing the UML class diagram **without** showing the **Subject interface** costs you **2 marks**. Always include the interface as the central abstraction.  
> - Using the words *"Proxy acts like the real object"* without explaining **why** (interface transparency + LSP) costs you **1 mark**.  
> - Failing to give a **real-world example** in 14-mark questions costs you **1.5 marks**.  
> - Confusing Proxy with **Decorator** costs you **1 mark**. Always clarify intent.

---

### Question B-A — Proxy Pattern Explanation with Example `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the Proxy design pattern with its intent and participants. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Draw the UML class diagram for a Virtual Proxy that loads a large image on demand and explain its dynamic behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Intent (1 mark):**  
The Proxy pattern provides a **surrogate** for another object to **control access** to it. It is used when direct access to the real object is impractical because of cost, location, security, or lifecycle concerns.

**Participants (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Subject** — An interface declaring the common operations. Both RealSubject and Proxy implement it.
2. **RealSubject** — The actual object that performs the real business logic. It may be expensive, remote, or protected.
3. **Proxy** — Holds a reference to RealSubject, implements the Subject interface, and intercepts calls to add control (lazy init, security check, marshalling, caching).
4. **Client** — Uses the Subject interface only. It is **unaware** of whether the object is real or proxied.

**Collaborations (2 marks):**  
The Client invokes a method on the Proxy. The Proxy performs any necessary pre-processing (e.g., lazy creation, access check) and forwards the request to the RealSubject. After the RealSubject returns, the Proxy may perform post-processing (e.g., logging, caching) before returning the result to the Client.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks)

**UML Class Diagram (4 marks):**

```
                <<interface>>
                  Image
              + display()
                       △
                       ‖ realizes
                       ‖
           ┌───────────┴───────────┐
           │                       │
    RealImage                ImageProxy
   - filename              - filename
   - bitmapData            - realImage : RealImage
   + display()             + display()
   + loadFromDisk()        + loadFromDisk()
```

**Dynamic Behavior — Sequence (3 marks):**

1. The Client calls `imageProxy.display()`.
2. The ImageProxy checks `if (realImage == null)`.
3. On first call, the ImageProxy instantiates `realImage = new RealImage(filename)` and calls `realImage.loadFromDisk()` — this is the **expensive step** deferred to the first use.
4. The ImageProxy forwards the call to `realImage.display()`.
5. The RealImage renders the bitmap to the screen.
6. On subsequent calls, Step 3 is skipped; only the delegation happens.

**Memory saved:** If the Client never calls `display()`, the 4 MB bitmap is never loaded — a memory saving of approximately $4 \times 10^6$ bytes per image.

---

### Question B-B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Compare and contrast the different types of proxies (Virtual, Remote, Protection, Smart) with real-world examples. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe how the Proxy pattern is implemented in Java RMI (Remote Method Invocation). Explain how a Protection Proxy is used in Spring Security. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Type | Intent | Real-World Example |
|------|--------|--------------------|
| **Virtual Proxy** | Defer expensive object creation until first use | Lazy-loading of large thumbnail images in a gallery app; Hibernate lazy entity loading |
| **Remote Proxy** | Hide network communication behind a local interface | Java RMI stubs, gRPC client stubs, REST client SDKs |
| **Protection Proxy** | Enforce access control on a sensitive object | Spring Security AOP interceptors, file system permission checks |
| **Smart Proxy** | Add housekeeping (ref count, locking, logging) around an object | Reference-counted COM objects, JNI handle wrappers |

**Key Distinguishing Points (2 marks):**

- **Virtual** proxies are about **time** (when to create).
- **Remote** proxies are about **space** (where the object lives).
- **Protection** proxies are about **identity** (who can call).
- **Smart** proxies are about **resources** (how to manage them).

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Java RMI and Remote Proxy (3.5 marks):**  
In Java RMI, the **client stub** is a Remote Proxy. The stub implements the **same remote interface** as the server-side object. When the client invokes a method on the stub, the stub:

1. Marshals the arguments into a byte stream.
2. Sends the stream over TCP to the server skeleton.
3. Waits for the result stream.
4. Unmarshals the result and returns it to the client.

The Client is unaware of any of these steps; it just sees a normal method call. This is the essence of the Remote Proxy — **location transparency**.

**Spring Security and Protection Proxy (3.5 marks):**  
Spring Security uses AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) to weave Protection Proxies around service-layer methods. When a request enters a `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")` method:

1. Spring generates a dynamic Proxy (using JDK dynamic Proxy or CGLIB) that wraps the bean.
2. Before the real method runs, the Proxy invokes the security interceptor.
3. The interceptor reads the current authentication context, evaluates the SpEL expression `hasRole('ADMIN')`, and either **allows** or **denies** the call.
4. If denied, an `AccessDeniedException` is thrown — the RealSubject is never invoked.

This is exactly the **Protection Proxy** pattern applied at the framework level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU 2024):**

- [x] **Definition:** Proxy is a structural pattern that provides a surrogate to control access to another object sharing the same interface.
- [x] **Gang of Four category:** Structural Design Pattern.
- [x] **Four GoF participants:** Subject, RealSubject, Proxy, Client — *all four must be named in any 7-mark answer*.
- [x] **Five variants:** Virtual, Remote, Protection, Smart (Reference), Caching — *be able to give one example of each*.
- [x] **Core intent keywords:** *control access, lazy initialization, security, location transparency, housekeeping*.
- [x] **Relationship:** Proxy **has-a** RealSubject (composition), Proxy **is-a** Subject (realization).
- [x] **Interface rule:** Proxy **must** implement every method of Subject (Liskov Substitution Principle).
- [x] **Comparison with Decorator:** Proxy *controls access* (gatekeeper); Decorator *adds behavior* (chain).
- [x] **Comparison with Adapter:** Proxy keeps the same interface; Adapter changes the interface.
- [x] **Real-world anchors:** Java RMI (Remote), Hibernate lazy load (Virtual), Spring Security (Protection), COM ref-counting (Smart), Nginx (Protection + Caching).
- [x] **UML notation:** Realization = `------▷`; Composition = `◆---`; Association = `───`.
- [x] **Sequence diagram:** Always show *lazy-init check* and *pre/post-processing* for full marks.
- [x] **Performance metrics:** First-call latency spike (Virtual), network marshalling overhead (Remote), memory savings ≈ $4 \times 10^6$ bytes per lazy image.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** Confusing Proxy with Decorator costs 1 mark — clarify the *intent* difference.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** Drawing the diagram without the Subject interface costs 2 marks — always include it.
- [x] **LSP compliance equation to memorize:** $\forall C, \forall m \in \text{Subject}: C.m(\text{RealSubject}) \equiv_{\text{observable}} C.m(\text{Proxy})$.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:**  
> - For 3-mark questions: **Definition + 2 situations + 1 example** = full marks.  
> - For 14-mark questions: **Intent (1) + Participants (4) + Diagram (4) + Example/Sequence (3.5) + Comparison (1.5)** = full marks.  
> - Always end with **one real-world anchor** to leave a strong examiner impression.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite
Decorator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decorator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Decorator Pattern (Gang of Four - Structural Pattern):**
> The Decorator Pattern is a **structural design pattern** that allows behavior to be added to individual objects dynamically (at run-time), without affecting the behavior of other objects from the same class. It provides a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality by wrapping an existing object inside a special wrapper class (the decorator) that conforms to the same interface as the original object.

In KTU parlance, this is also referred to as the **Wrapper Pattern** because each decorator literally *wraps* (contains a reference to) a component object.

### Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU Viva)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Component** | Abstract base class/interface for objects that can have responsibilities added dynamically. |
| **ConcreteComponent** | The original, real object to which additional responsibilities can be attached. |
| **Decorator** | Abstract class that holds a reference to a Component and conforms to its interface. |
| **ConcreteDecorator** | Subclass that adds specific behavior/state to the component it wraps. |
| **Open/Closed Principle** | Class should be **open for extension** but **closed for modification** — the principle Decorator upholds. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza / Coffee Builder"

Imagine you walk into a coffee shop and order a **Plain Coffee** ☕.
- Base price = ₹100
- You ask to **add Milk** → +₹20
- You ask to **add Sugar** → +₹15
- You ask to **add Chocolate Syrup** → +₹30

You are not creating a new coffee class called `MilkSugarChocolateCoffee`. Instead, you are **dynamically wrapping** the plain coffee with "decorators":
`Chocolate(Sugar(Milk(PlainCoffee)))`

The plain coffee object never changed. The decorators are added on top, one over the other, each adding its own cost and description. The final cup *is* a coffee (same interface), but it's *decorated* with extra features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use Decorator instead of Inheritance?**
> - If we used inheritance, we would need: `Coffee`, `MilkCoffee`, `SugarCoffee`, `MilkSugarCoffee`, `MilkSugarChocolateCoffee`... **explosive class growth (combinatorial explosion)**.
> - Decorator solves this by allowing **composition over inheritance** — features are composed at runtime.

---

## 1.3 The Problem Decorator Solves (KTU-Favorite Question)

You are given a class `Window` with method `draw()`. You need variations:
- `WindowWithScrollBar`
- `WindowWithMenu`
- `WindowWithScrollBarAndMenu`
- `WindowWithBorder`, etc.

Using inheritance: **2ⁿ classes** for *n* features. Using Decorator: **n+1 classes only** (1 component + n decorators).

This is the classic KTU 2-mark / 3-mark short answer.

---

## 1.4 Participants in the Decorator Pattern (Gang of Four)

1. **Component** (e.g., `VisualComponent`) — declares the common interface.
2. **ConcreteComponent** (e.g., `TextView`) — defines an object to which additional responsibilities can be attached.
3. **Decorator** (e.g., `Decorator`) — maintains a reference to a Component and conforms to its interface.
4. **ConcreteDecorator** (e.g., `BorderDecorator`, `ScrollDecorator`) — adds responsibilities to the component.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** If asked "How many participants?" → Answer **4**. Always count `Component`, `ConcreteComponent`, `Decorator`, `ConcreteDecorator` separately. This is a frequently repeated 1-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

The Decorator pattern works on the principle of **recursive composition**. Let us decompose its operation:

- **Step 1 — Interface Definition:** An abstract class or interface `Component` is declared. It defines operations like `operation()`.
- **Step 2 — Base Implementation:** A `ConcreteComponent` class implements `Component` and provides the core, undecorated behavior.
- **Step 3 — Abstract Decorator:** A `Decorator` abstract class also implements `Component`. It holds a private/protected reference (`has-a` / aggregation) to a `Component` object. Its `operation()` simply delegates to the wrapped component: `component.operation()`.
- **Step 4 — Concrete Decorators:** Subclasses of `Decorator` override `operation()` to:
  - Call the parent (super) operation first.
  - Then add their own behavior before, after, or around it.
- **Step 5 — Client Chaining:** The client code wraps the concrete component with one or more decorators at runtime:

$$c_1 = \text{ConcreteComponent}()$$
$$c_2 = \text{ConcreteDecoratorA}(c_1)$$
$$c_3 = \text{ConcreteDecoratorB}(c_2)$$
$$c_3.\text{operation}()$$

- **Step 6 — Polymorphic Dispatch:** Because the decorator conforms to the `Component` interface, the client cannot tell whether it is dealing with a plain component or a wrapped one — the call is identical from the outside.

---

## 2.2 Object-Oriented Principles Upheld

| Principle | How Decorator Satisfies It |
|---|---|
| **Open/Closed Principle** | New behavior added by creating new decorator classes — no modification of existing code. |
| **Single Responsibility Principle** | Each decorator handles exactly one concern (e.g., scroll, border). |
| **Favor Composition over Inheritance** | Functionality is composed at runtime, not baked into class hierarchy. |
| **Liskov Substitution Principle** | Decorator can stand in for Component anywhere a Component is expected. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Comparison Table

| Aspect | Decorator Pattern | Inheritance (Subclassing) |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexibility** | Runtime, dynamic | Compile-time, static |
| **Number of Classes** | $n + 1$ (for *n* features) | $2^{n}$ (combinatorial) |
| **Coupling** | Loose (object composition) | Tight (compile-time parent) |
| **Open/Closed** | Upheld | Often violated |
| **Removal of behavior** | Easy (just don't wrap) | Hard (need new class) |
| **Identity of object** | Same external type | New sub-type |
| **Best for** | Optional/mix-and-match features | Mandatory shared behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "Decorate *donuts*, not hierarchies." Decorator adds **Optional Features**, Inheritance adds **Mandatory Identity**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Java I/O Streams:** `FileInputStream` → `BufferedInputStream` → `DataInputStream` is a textbook Decorator chain.
- **GUI Toolkits:** `JScrollPane` wrapping a `JPanel` (adding scrollbars dynamically).
- **Web Middleware:** Express.js / Koa.js middleware — each middleware function decorates the request handler.
- **Logging Frameworks:** Wrapping a service with logging, then with caching, then with security checks.
- **Android Context Wrappers:** `ContextWrapper` allows adding custom resources dynamically.
- **Spring AOP Proxies:** Spring dynamically decorates beans with cross-cutting concerns (transactions, security).
- **Game Engines:** Adding temporary power-ups to a base weapon (e.g., `FireSword(Sword)`).

---

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions & Limitations (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Identity Issue:** A decorated object is **not `instanceof`** its concrete component alone; it includes the decorators. May break code that relies on `getClass()` comparison.
- **Complex Debugging:** A deeply wrapped object stack (e.g., 7 decorators) is hard to debug — many "hop" points in the call stack.
- **Order-Dependence:** Some decorators are order-sensitive (e.g., `Encryption(Compression(File))` ≠ `Compression(Encryption(File))`).
- **Tiny Components Problem:** If base component is too small, overhead of creating many wrappers is unjustified.
- **Cannot Remove at Runtime:** Once attached, you typically can't easily detach a decorator mid-lifecycle.

---

## 2.6 Mathematical View (For Module 2 Depth)

If we let $C$ = base component and $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n$ = set of decorators, then the cost of *all combinations*:

$$ \text{Classes via Inheritance} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^{n} - 1 $$

$$ \text{Classes via Decorator} = n + 1 $$

For $n = 5$ features, inheritance requires **31 classes**, but Decorator requires only **6 classes**. The savings grow exponentially — this is why the pattern is considered a major architectural win.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Class Count Reduction — Mathematical Derivation

We formally derive the savings achieved by Decorator over classical inheritance.

**Step 1 — Assumption:** Let there be $n$ independent features, each optional and combinable.

**Step 2 — Inheritance (Subclassing) Approach:** Every combination of features requires a unique subclass. The number of distinct subclasses is the number of non-empty subsets of the $n$ features:

$$ S_{\text{inherit}} = \binom{n}{1} + \binom{n}{2} + \dots + \binom{n}{n} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the Binomial Identity:** The sum of all binomial coefficients from $k=0$ to $k=n$ is $2^{n}$. Subtracting the $k=0$ term (the empty subset, which is the base class itself) gives:

$$ S_{\text{inherit}} = 2^{n} - 1 $$

**Step 4 — Decorator Approach:** We need exactly one class per feature, plus the base component class:

$$ S_{\text{decor}} = n + 1 $$

**Step 5 — Ratio of Reduction:**

$$ R(n) = \frac{S_{\text{inherit}}}{S_{\text{decor}}} = \frac{2^{n} - 1}{n + 1} $$

**Step 6 — Concrete Numerical Comparison:**

| $n$ | $S_{\text{inherit}} = 2^{n} - 1$ | $S_{\text{decor}} = n+1$ | Ratio $R(n)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 3 | 3 | 1.0 |
| 3 | 7 | 4 | 1.75 |
| 4 | 15 | 5 | 3.0 |
| 5 | 31 | 6 | 5.17 |
| 8 | 255 | 9 | 28.3 |
| 10 | 1023 | 11 | 93.0 |

**Step 7 — Limiting Behavior:** As $n \to \infty$:

$$ R(n) \sim \frac{2^{n}}{n} \to \infty $$

**Conclusion:** The Decorator pattern saves an *exponential* number of classes in the number of independent features. This is a strong, mathematically justified reason to prefer it.

---

## 3.2 Object Composition Chain — Formal Trace

When the client calls `outermost.operation()`, the call propagates *inward* through the wrapper stack and *outward* as each decorator adds its own behavior.

Let the chain be:

$$ o = D_{3}(D_{2}(D_{1}(C))) $$

Then the call sequence is:

$$ o.\text{op}() \;\Rightarrow\; D_3.\text{addedBehavior}() \;\Rightarrow\; D_2.\text{addedBehavior}() \;\Rightarrow\; D_1.\text{addedBehavior}() \;\Rightarrow\; C.\text{op}() $$

After $C.\text{op}()$ returns, control flows back outward, executing any *post-call* behavior in each decorator.

This forms a classic **stack-based execution trace** identical in structure to a chain of `try/finally` blocks or recursive function calls.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use vs. Not Use Decorator

| Decision Criterion | Use Decorator ✅ | Avoid Decorator ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Need to add/remove behavior at runtime? | Yes | No — fixed behavior is fine |
| Many independent optional features? | Yes ($n \geq 3$ with combinations) | Few or zero combinations |
| Want to avoid class explosion? | Yes | Willing to maintain many subclasses |
| Need to enforce mandatory identity? | No | Yes — subclasses give explicit type |
| Behavior order is fixed? | Order-dependent decorators are tricky | Behavior is invariant |
| Component has many small methods? | Overhead is small | Component is huge — wrapping is verbose |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Coffee Shop Pricing

Suppose base coffee costs **₹100**. Decorators: Milk (+₹20), Sugar (+₹15), Chocolate (+₹30), WhippedCream (+₹25).

**Step 1 — Create plain coffee:**

$$ p_1 = \text{PlainCoffee}() \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cost}(p_1) = 100 $$

**Step 2 — Add milk:**

$$ p_2 = \text{MilkDecorator}(p_1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cost}(p_2) = 100 + 20 = 120 $$

**Step 3 — Add sugar on top:**

$$ p_3 = \text{SugarDecorator}(p_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cost}(p_3) = 120 + 15 = 135 $$

**Step 4 — Add chocolate on top:**

$$ p_4 = \text{ChocolateDecorator}(p_3) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cost}(p_4) = 135 + 30 = 165 $$

**Step 5 — Add whipped cream on top:**

$$ p_5 = \text{WhippedCreamDecorator}(p_4) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cost}(p_5) = 165 + 25 = 190 $$

**Final Wrapped Object:**

$$ p_5 = \text{WhippedCream}(\text{Chocolate}(\text{Sugar}(\text{Milk}(\text{PlainCoffee})))) $$

The base `PlainCoffee` class was *never modified*. Every addition was achieved by composition. The total number of classes used = **5** (1 base + 4 decorators). Without Decorator, combinations would have required $\binom{4}{1} + \binom{4}{2} + \binom{4}{3} + \binom{4}{4} = 4 + 6 + 4 + 1 = 15$ separate subclasses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard UML Class Diagram (Gang of Four Canonical Form)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Component {
        <<interface>>
        +operation()
    }
    class ConcreteComponent {
        +operation()
    }
    class Decorator {
        -component : Component
        +operation()
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorA {
        -addedState : String
        +operation()
        +addedBehavior()
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorB {
        +operation()
        +addedBehavior()
    }
    Component <|.. ConcreteComponent : implements
    Component <|.. Decorator : implements
    Decorator o-- Component : has-a (composition)
    Decorator <|-- ConcreteDecoratorA : extends
    Decorator <|-- ConcreteDecoratorB : extends
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Hollow triangle arrow = `implements` (interface realization)
- Solid line with hollow triangle = `extends` (inheritance)
- Diamond arrow = composition/aggregation ("has-a")

---

## 4.2 Object-Level Collaboration Diagram — Runtime View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Client Code"] --> B["ConcreteDecoratorB : ScrollDecorator"]
    B --> C["ConcreteDecoratorA : BorderDecorator"]
    C --> D["ConcreteComponent : TextView"]
    D --> E["Core draw() : renders raw text"]
    C --> F["addedBehavior() : draws border"]
    B --> G["addedBehavior() : draws scrollbar"]
```

**Interpretation:** The client invokes `operation()` on the outermost decorator (`ScrollDecorator`). The call cascades inward to `BorderDecorator`, then to `TextView`. Each layer may add behavior *before* and *after* delegating further inward.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Call Propagation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant ScrollDec as ConcreteDecoratorB
    participant BorderDec as ConcreteDecoratorA
    participant TextView as ConcreteComponent
    Client->>ScrollDec: operation()
    activate ScrollDec
    ScrollDec->>ScrollDec: preProcessing()
    ScrollDec->>BorderDec: operation()
    activate BorderDec
    BorderDec->>BorderDec: preProcessing()
    BorderDec->>TextView: operation()
    activate TextView
    TextView-->>BorderDec: result
    deactivate TextView
    BorderDec->>BorderDec: postProcessing()
    BorderDec-->>ScrollDec: result
    deactivate BorderDec
    ScrollDec->>ScrollDec: postProcessing()
    ScrollDec-->>Client: final result
    deactivate ScrollDec
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Engineering View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientLayer["Client Application Layer"]
        CLIENT["Client Request"]
    end
    subgraph DecoratorStack["Decorator Stack (Composable at Runtime)"]
        D3["Logging Decorator"]
        D2["Security Decorator"]
        D1["Caching Decorator"]
    end
    subgraph CoreLayer["Core Business Object (Unchanged)"]
        CORE["Actual Service / Component"]
    end
    CLIENT --> D3
    D3 --> D2
    D2 --> D1
    D1 --> CORE
    CORE -.result.-> D1
    D1 -.enriched.-> D2
    D2 -.enriched.-> D3
    D3 -.final.-> CLIENT
```

**Engineering Reading:** This mirrors how **production-grade web servers** (Node.js, Spring Boot) layer cross-cutting concerns: Authentication → Logging → Caching → Actual Business Logic. Each is a decorator.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Schematic — Inheritance vs. Decorator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InheritanceApproach["Inheritance Tree (Rigid, Compile-time)"]
        I1["TextView"] --> I2["TextViewWithBorder"]
        I1 --> I3["TextViewWithScroll"]
        I2 --> I4["TextViewWithBorderAndScroll"]
        I3 --> I4
    end
    subgraph DecoratorApproach["Decorator Composition (Flexible, Runtime)"]
        D1["TextView (ConcreteComponent)"]
        D2["BorderDecorator"]
        D3["ScrollDecorator"]
        D3 -.wraps.-> D2
        D2 -.wraps.-> D1
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Trick for KTU Exams:** Draw the inheritance tree on the *left* (exploding branches) and the decorator chain on the *right* (single linear wrap chain). Examiners love this side-by-side visual contrast.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Decorator design pattern. List its key participants.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Decorator pattern is a **structural design pattern** that dynamically attaches additional responsibilities to an object at runtime. It provides a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality.
>
> **Key Participants (4):**
> 1. **Component** — abstract interface for objects that can receive added responsibilities.
> 2. **ConcreteComponent** — the original object to which responsibilities may be added.
> 3. **Decorator** — abstract class that holds a reference to a Component and conforms to its interface.
> 4. **ConcreteDecorator** — concrete subclass that adds the actual responsibility.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the Decorator pattern preferred over inheritance for adding responsibilities? Give one real-world example.**

**Model Answer:**

> Decorator is preferred because inheritance leads to a **combinatorial explosion of classes** (i.e., $2^{n} - 1$ subclasses for $n$ features), which is rigid and decided at compile-time.
>
> Decorator allows responsibilities to be **added and removed dynamically at runtime** by composition, keeping the class count at $n+1$.
>
> **Real-world Example:** Java I/O streams — `new DataInputStream(new BufferedInputStream(new FileInputStream("data.txt")))` decorates a file stream with buffering and primitive-data reading without modifying the original `FileInputStream` class.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** Per KTU 2024 scheme, students answer **one full question** from a module. Both alternatives below cover identical learning outcomes at the same Bloom level.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable UML class diagram, explain the **Gang of Four (GoF) Decorator design pattern**. Identify and briefly describe its four participants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Decorator pattern with inheritance** for extending object behavior. Show mathematically why Decorator scales better when $n = 6$ features must be combinable. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition:** The Decorator pattern attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically. It does so by wrapping the object in a decorator object that conforms to the same interface, with the decorator forwarding calls to the wrapped object and adding its own behavior before/after delegation.

**Step 2 — UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Component {
        <<interface>>
        +operation()
    }
    class ConcreteComponent {
        +operation()
    }
    class Decorator {
        -component : Component
        +operation()
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorA {
        +addedState : Object
        +operation()
        +addedBehavior()
    }
    class ConcreteDecoratorB {
        +operation()
        +addedBehavior()
    }
    Component <|.. ConcreteComponent
    Component <|.. Decorator
    Decorator o-- Component
    Decorator <|-- ConcreteDecoratorA
    Decorator <|-- ConcreteDecoratorB
```

**Step 3 — Four Participants:**

1. **Component (Interface):** Declares the common interface (e.g., `VisualComponent`) for both wrappers and wrapped objects. Essential for polymorphism.
2. **ConcreteComponent (e.g., `TextView`):** The original, undecorated object — defines the core behavior that can later be enhanced.
3. **Decorator (Abstract):** Holds a private reference to a `Component` object. Implements the `Component` interface so its presence is transparent. Its `operation()` delegates to the wrapped component.
4. **ConcreteDecorator (e.g., `BorderDecorator`, `ScrollDecorator`):** Subclasses of `Decorator` that add specific behavior. They call the parent `operation()` first, then add their own logic.

**Step 4 — Working:** A client creates a `ConcreteComponent`, then wraps it in one or more decorators:

$$ \text{final} = \text{ScrollDecorator}(\text{BorderDecorator}(\text{TextView}())) $$

When `final.operation()` is called, the request cascades inward, and each decorator adds behavior at its layer.

**Step 5 — Outcome:** Behavior is added without modifying the original class, satisfying the **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID design.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Inheritance Approach:** Each combination of features requires a separate subclass. For $n$ independent features, the number of subclasses is the number of non-empty subsets:

$$ S_{\text{inherit}} = 2^{n} - 1 $$

**Step 2 — Decorator Approach:** One class per feature plus the base component:

$$ S_{\text{decor}} = n + 1 $$

**Step 3 — Plug in $n = 6$:**

$$ S_{\text{inherit}} = 2^{6} - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 \text{ classes} $$

$$ S_{\text{decor}} = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ classes} $$

**Step 4 — Ratio of Savings:**

$$ R(6) = \frac{63}{7} = 9 $$

So Decorator reduces the class count by a **factor of 9** for 6 features. For $n = 10$, the factor becomes **93**, demonstrating exponential savings.

**Step 5 — Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Inheritance | Decorator |
|---|---|---|
| Class count (n=6) | 63 | 7 |
| When behavior added | Compile-time | Runtime |
| Flexibility | Low (rigid tree) | High (composable) |
| Modification of existing code | Often required | Not required |
| Removing behavior | New subclass | Skip the wrapper |
| Maintenance effort | High | Low |

**Conclusion:** Decorator is structurally and mathematically superior whenever features are optional, combinable, or dynamic.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a Decorator-based solution for a **Coffee Shop billing system** where a base coffee can be optionally decorated with **Milk, Sugar, Chocolate, and WhippedCream**. Draw the UML class diagram and explain how a final order `WhippedCream(Chocolate(Sugar(Milk(PlainCoffee))))` is computed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of the Decorator pattern. Identify two real-world frameworks/libraries that use this pattern and explain how. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the Participants:**

- **Component (Abstract):** `Coffee` — declares `getCost()` and `getDescription()`.
- **ConcreteComponent:** `PlainCoffee` — base coffee costing ₹100, description `"Plain Coffee"`.
- **Decorator (Abstract):** `CoffeeDecorator` — implements `Coffee`, holds a `Coffee` reference.
- **ConcreteDecorators:** `MilkDecorator`, `SugarDecorator`, `ChocolateDecorator`, `WhippedCreamDecorator`.

**Step 2 — UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Coffee {
        <<interface>>
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    class PlainCoffee {
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    class CoffeeDecorator {
        -wrappedCoffee : Coffee
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    class MilkDecorator {
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    class SugarDecorator {
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    class ChocolateDecorator {
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    class WhippedCreamDecorator {
        +getCost() double
        +getDescription() String
    }
    Coffee <|.. PlainCoffee
    Coffee <|.. CoffeeDecorator
    CoffeeDecorator o-- Coffee
    CoffeeDecorator <|-- MilkDecorator
    CoffeeDecorator <|-- SugarDecorator
    CoffeeDecorator <|-- ChocolateDecorator
    CoffeeDecorator <|-- WhippedCreamDecorator
```

**Step 3 — Cost Computation Logic (with realistic values):**

- `PlainCoffee` → Cost = 100, Description = `"Plain Coffee"`
- `MilkDecorator` → adds +20 → Cost = 120, Description = `"Plain Coffee, Milk"`
- `SugarDecorator` → adds +15 → Cost = 135, Description = `"Plain Coffee, Milk, Sugar"`
- `ChocolateDecorator` → adds +30 → Cost = 165, Description = `"Plain Coffee, Milk, Sugar, Chocolate"`
- `WhippedCreamDecorator` → adds +25 → Cost = 190, Description = `"Plain Coffee, Milk, Sugar, Chocolate, Whipped Cream"`

**Step 4 — Final Order Construction:**

$$ \text{order} = \text{WhippedCreamDecorator}(\text{ChocolateDecorator}(\text{SugarDecorator}(\text{MilkDecorator}(\text{PlainCoffee()})))) $$

$$ \text{Cost}(\text{order}) = 100 + 20 + 15 + 30 + 25 = \text{₹}190 $$

**Step 5 — How the Chain Executes:** When `getCost()` is invoked on the outermost wrapper, the call propagates inward:
`WhippedCream → Chocolate → Sugar → Milk → PlainCoffee`.
PlainCoffee returns 100. Each decorator adds its surcharge and returns the running total outward.

**Step 6 — Verification of Class Count:** Without Decorator, combinations of 4 optional toppings would have required $2^{4} - 1 = 15$ subclasses. With Decorator, only **5 classes** (1 base + 4 decorators) are needed.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Advantages of Decorator:**

1. **Greater flexibility than static inheritance** — responsibilities can be added or removed at runtime.
2. **Avoids feature-heavy classes high up the hierarchy** — instead of one bloated superclass, you have many small, focused decorators.
3. **Simplifies coding** — you can mix and match behaviors without writing complex conditional code.
4. **Enhances extensibility** — conforms to Open/Closed Principle; new decorators can be added without altering existing code.
5. **Pay-as-you-go design** — clients only pay the cost of decorators they actually use.

**Step 2 — Limitations of Decorator:**
