1. **Lots of small classes** — can overwhelm developers unfamiliar with the pattern, making systems harder to learn.
2. **Identity problem** — a decorated object is not `instanceof` the original concrete type; can break type-sensitive code.
3. **Debugging complexity** — many wrapper layers create long stack traces.
4. **Order sensitivity** — some decorators are order-dependent (e.g., encryption must occur before compression in some pipelines).
5. **Configuration overhead** — composing decorators requires careful client-side assembly.

**Step 3 — Real-World Framework #1: Java I/O Streams**

Java's `java.io` package is the canonical Decorator example. The abstract class `FilterInputStream` is the Decorator base. `BufferedInputStream`, `DataInputStream`, and `CheckedInputStream` are ConcreteDecorators that wrap an `InputStream` (the Component) and add buffering, primitive-type reading, and checksum computation, respectively. Chains like:

$$ \text{new DataInputStream(new BufferedInputStream(new FileInputStream("a.dat")))} $$

are textbook Decorator stacks.

**Step 4 — Real-World Framework #2: Express.js Middleware (Node.js)**

In Express.js, middleware functions are decorators over the HTTP request handler. Each middleware receives `req`, `res`, and `next`, performs a cross-cutting concern (authentication, logging, body parsing), and then calls `next()` to delegate to the next decorator in the chain. The actual route handler is the innermost "ConcreteComponent."

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Decorator is a foundational pattern enabling modular, layered, runtime-configurable software — a hallmark of modern frameworks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Component" interface** — many students draw only Decorator → ConcreteComponent; you *must* show the abstract `Component` because polymorphism is the entire point. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing Decorator with Adapter** — Adapter changes *interface*; Decorator keeps the *same interface* and adds *behavior*. Examiners specifically test this. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> 3. **Writing "extends Component" for Decorator** — Decorator **implements** Component and **has-a** Component. Two distinct relationships. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Omitting aggregation arrow** — the `Decorator o-- Component` composition arrow is the most-graded UML element. Always draw it explicitly. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Failing to mention Open/Closed Principle** — if asked "why use Decorator," always cite this principle. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Pattern Category:** GoF **Structural** pattern, also called **Wrapper**.
- **Core Intent:** Attach additional responsibilities to an object **dynamically**; provide a flexible alternative to subclassing.
- **Participants (4):** `Component`, `ConcreteComponent`, `Decorator`, `ConcreteDecorator`.
- **Key Relationships:** Decorator **implements** Component, Decorator **has-a** Component (composition/aggregation).
- **Open/Closed Principle:** Class is open for extension (new decorators) but closed for modification (existing classes untouched).
- **Class Count Math:** Inheritance → $2^{n} - 1$ classes; Decorator → $n + 1$ classes. Exponential savings.
- **Composition over Inheritance:** Decorator exemplifies this GoF/SOLID guideline.
- **Polymorphism Power:** Client treats decorated and undecorated objects identically through the `Component` interface.
- **Dynamic Configuration:** Decorators can be added or removed at runtime — not possible with inheritance.
- **Famous Use-Case:** **Java I/O streams** (`BufferedInputStream` wrapping `FileInputStream`) — memorize this for viva.
- **Confusable Pattern:** **Adapter** changes interface; **Decorator** keeps the same interface and adds behavior. Don't mix them up.
- **Order Matters:** Some decorator chains are order-sensitive (e.g., `Encryption(Compression(File))` ≠ `Compression(Encryption(File))`).
- **Identity Caveat:** A decorated object is not strictly `instanceof` its inner concrete component — keep this in mind for `equals()` / `getClass()` checks.
- **Modern Uses:** Middleware (Express.js, Koa.js), Spring AOP proxies, Android `ContextWrapper`, GUI toolkits, game engine weapon upgrades.
- **Syllabus Tag:** This topic falls under **Module 2 — Structural Design Patterns** of the PECST411 Software Engineering course, KTU 2024 scheme.
- **Examiner Loves:** UML class diagram + a worked numerical example (e.g., coffee, pizza, or window). Always include both in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Façade
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Façade

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Façade Pattern (GoF Structural Pattern):**
> *"Provide a unified, higher-level interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. The Façade pattern defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use."*
> — Gang of Four (Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides), 1994.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** Software Engineering syllabus, the Façade pattern is classified under **Module 2: Design Patterns — Structural Patterns**. It is one of the seven classical GoF structural patterns and addresses the principle of **"least knowledge"** (also called the *Law of Demeter*).

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| **Pattern Category** | Structural (GoF) |
| **Pattern Type** | Object-Level Wrapper / Interface Simplifier |
| **Also Known As** | *Wrapper* (in a loose sense), *Fronting Interface* |
| **Primary Intent** | Decouple client from subsystem complexity |
| **SOLID Principle Supported** | **D** (Dependency Inversion) and **I** (Interface Segregation indirectly) |
| **Standard KTU Reference** | GoF Book, Chapter 4 (Structural Patterns) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Concierge"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition (Memorize this for viva!):**

Imagine you walk into a **5-star hotel**. You (the *Client*) want breakfast, a cab to the airport, laundry, and a spa booking. You do **not** walk into the kitchen, call the cab company, find the laundry staff, and chase the spa therapist individually. Instead, you walk to the **Concierge Desk** (the *Façade*).

The concierge has a **single phone number / single desk** for you. Behind the scenes, the concierge coordinates with:
- The Kitchen
- The Cab Vendor
- The Laundry Department
- The Spa Center

You **only know one interface** (`concierge.order("breakfast+taxi")`) instead of **four subsystems**.

**Mapping:**
- **You** → Client
- **Concierge Desk** → Façade object
- **Kitchen / Cab / Laundry / Spa** → Subsystem classes
- **Single counter** → Unified higher-level interface

---

## 1.3 Why KTU Includes Façade in Module 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECST411 Module 2):**
> The module targets *"applying design patterns to improve object-oriented design quality and reduce coupling."* Façade is a **favourite 14-mark question** because:
> 1. It is asked as a definition + UML diagram question.
> 2. It is asked comparatively (Façade vs Adapter vs Mediator).
> 3. It maps directly to **system integration** use cases in real KTU lab assignments.

---

## 1.4 Problem Statement Façade Solves

When a client must interact with **many interrelated classes** of a complex subsystem, two problems arise:
1. **Tight coupling** — Client knows every subsystem class.
2. **Complexity leakage** — Business logic of the subsystem pollutes the client code.

Façade wraps the subsystem behind a **single, simple, high-level API**, allowing the client to remain clean and loosely coupled.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Participants (GoF Canonical Roles)

The Façade pattern formally defines **two participants**:

### 2.1.1 Façade
- Knows which subsystem classes are responsible for a request.
- Delegates client requests to appropriate subsystem objects.
- Optionally, performs **additional functionality** (e.g., input validation, ordering of calls) before/after delegation.

### 2.1.2 Subsystem Classes
- Implement subsystem functionality.
- Handle work assigned by the Façade.
- **Have no knowledge of the Façade** (i.e., they do not hold a reference to the Façade). This keeps subsystems reusable and independent.

### 2.1.3 Client (Implied role)
- Communicates with subsystem via the Façade only.
- Avoids direct references to subsystem classes where possible.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" and "How" behind each step:**

1. **Client invokes a high-level method on the Façade.**
   *Why:* Reduces the number of method calls the client must know.
2. **Façade validates input / orders the request internally.**
   *Why:* Centralizes cross-cutting logic (logging, transaction start, auth).
3. **Façade delegates the call to one or more subsystem objects.**
   *How:* Holds `private` references to subsystem instances (often composed at construction).
4. **Subsystems perform their specialised work.**
   *Why:* Single-responsibility is preserved within the subsystem.
5. **Results propagate back through the Façade to the Client.**
   *Why:* Maintains a clean unidirectional dependency (Client → Façade → Subsystem).

---

## 2.3 When to Use Façade — Applicability Matrix

| Scenario | Use Façade? | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Complex subsystem with many interdependent classes | ✅ Yes | Façade simplifies the public API |
| Subsystem is layered (e.g., DAO, Service, Util) | ✅ Yes | Each layer acts as a Façade to the one below |
| Client needs only a subset of subsystem features | ✅ Yes | Façade exposes only the needed subset |
| Subsystem is simple (1–2 classes) | ❌ No | Adds unnecessary indirection |
| Client needs full access to every subsystem feature | ❌ No | Façade becomes a pass-through with no value |
| You need to convert interfaces (legacy ↔ new) | ❌ No | That is the **Adapter** pattern's job |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula-Style Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim for KTU ESE.**

| Concept | Formal Statement | Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Pattern Type** | Structural (GoF, 1994) | "Structural pattern" |
| **Intent** | Provide unified higher-level interface | "Simplify subsystem usage" |
| **Number of Participants** | **2** (Façade + Subsystem) | Mention explicitly |
| **Knowledge Direction** | Client → Façade → Subsystem (one-way) | "One-way dependency" |
| **Subsystem Awareness** | Subsystems do **NOT** know the Façade | "Decoupled subsystems" |
| **SOLID Mapping** | DIP + ISP support | Name the principle |
| **Related Pattern** | Often implemented as **Singleton** | "Singleton Façade" |
| **Anti-Pattern Risk** | *God Façade* — façade doing too much | Avoid in design answers |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Façade is heavily used in:
- **JDBC API** — `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` present a façade over low-level DB driver calls.
- **Web Frameworks** — Spring's `RestTemplate` / `WebClient` façade over HTTP client libraries.
- **Compilers** — High-level API façade over lexer, parser, optimizer, code generator.
- **Microservices** — API Gateway acts as a façade over many backend services.
- **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** — OS exposes a uniform façade over diverse hardware drivers.

This bridges Module 2 (Design Patterns) with Module 4 (Architectural Styles) — examiners love cross-module questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Sample Code & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Java Implementation (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A "Home Theater" subsystem has 4 components — Amplifier, DVD Player, Projector, Lights. Without a Façade, the client would call all four sequentially with verbose code.

### 3.1.1 Subsystem Classes (Step 1 — These exist independently)

```java
// Subsystem 1: Amplifier
class Amplifier {
    public void on()              { System.out.println("Amplifier ON"); }
    public void off()             { System.out.println("Amplifier OFF"); }
    public void setVolume(int v)  { System.out.println("Volume set to " + v); }
}

// Subsystem 2: DVDPlayer
class DVDPlayer {
    public void on()      { System.out.println("DVD Player ON"); }
    public void play(String movie) { System.out.println("Playing: " + movie); }
    public void off()     { System.out.println("DVD Player OFF"); }
}

// Subsystem 3: Projector
class Projector {
    public void on()  { System.out.println("Projector ON"); }
    public void off() { System.out.println("Projector OFF"); }
}

// Subsystem 4: Lights
class TheaterLights {
    public void dim(int level) { System.out.println("Lights dimmed to " + level + "%"); }
}
```

**Explanation:** Each subsystem has its own granular API. The subsystems **do not know** that a Façade exists — they are decoupled and reusable.

### 3.1.2 The Façade Class (Step 2 — Unified Interface)

```java
class HomeTheaterFacade {
    private Amplifier amp;
    private DVDPlayer dvd;
    private Projector projector;
    private TheaterLights lights;

    public HomeTheaterFacade(Amplifier amp, DVDPlayer dvd,
                             Projector projector, TheaterLights lights) {
        this.amp = amp;
        this.dvd = dvd;
        this.projector = projector;
        this.lights = lights;
    }

    // High-level method 1: Watch a movie
    public void watchMovie(String movie) {
        System.out.println("Get ready to watch a movie...");
        lights.dim(10);
        projector.on();
        amp.on();
        amp.setVolume(5);
        dvd.on();
        dvd.play(movie);
    }

    // High-level method 2: End movie
    public void endMovie() {
        System.out.println("Shutting movie theater down...");
        lights.dim(100);
        amp.off();
        dvd.off();
        projector.off();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The Façade encapsulates the **sequence** and **coordination** logic. The client now calls just **2 methods** instead of **8+**.

### 3.1.3 The Client Code (Step 3 — Simplified)

```java
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Amplifier amp       = new Amplifier();
        DVDPlayer dvd       = new DVDPlayer();
        Projector projector = new Projector();
        TheaterLights lights= new TheaterLights();

        HomeTheaterFacade homeTheater =
            new HomeTheaterFacade(amp, dvd, projector, lights);

        // Client uses ONLY the Façade — clean, simple, decoupled
        homeTheater.watchMovie("Inception");
        homeTheater.endMovie();
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Coupling Reduction

Let us **mathematically quantify** the coupling reduction achieved by Façade — a unique value-add for KTU exams.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{before}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m_i} \text{Refs}(C_j \rightarrow S_i) \\[4pt]
\text{where } n &= \text{number of subsystem classes} \\
m_i &= \text{number of methods exposed by class } S_i
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{after}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{p} \text{Refs}(C \rightarrow F_k) \\[4pt]
\text{where } p &= \text{number of high-level methods in Façade}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CouplingReduction} = \frac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%\;}
$$

**Worked Example (Home Theater):**
- Before: 4 subsystem classes × ~3 methods each ≈ **12 references**.
- After: 2 Façade methods ≈ **2 references**.
- Reduction = (12 − 2) / 12 × 100% = **83.33%** coupling reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Writing this formula in a 14-mark answer earns you **bonus marks** because it shows engineering rigour, not just textbook recall.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Design Decision | Façade Choice | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Singleton Façade | `private static final` instance | One global access point to subsystem |
| Abstract Façade | Abstract class + concrete subclasses | When multiple subsystems share a façade |
| Parameter Object | Pass a `Request` DTO to façade method | Reduces method-explosion in Façade |
| Layered Façade | Each layer is a façade for the next | Maps to 3-tier architecture |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical GoF UML Class Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +operation()
    }
    class FacadeClass {
        -subsystemA: SubsystemA
        -subsystemB: SubsystemB
        -subsystemC: SubsystemC
        +highLevelOp1()
        +highLevelOp2()
    }
    class SubsystemA {
        +methodA1()
        +methodA2()
    }
    class SubsystemB {
        +methodB1()
    }
    class SubsystemC {
        +methodC1()
        +methodC2()
        +methodC3()
    }

    Client --> FacadeClass : uses
    FacadeClass --> SubsystemA : delegates
    FacadeClass --> SubsystemB : delegates
    FacadeClass --> SubsystemC : delegates
```

**Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU answer sheets):**
- **Solid line + arrow** = Association (Client knows Façade; Façade knows Subsystems).
- **Filled diamond** = Composition (Façade owns Subsystem instances — strong lifecycle tie).
- **No arrow from Subsystems back to Façade** = One-way dependency (subsystems unaware).

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Movie Watching Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant F as HomeTheaterFacade
    participant L as TheaterLights
    participant P as Projector
    participant A as Amplifier
    participant D as DVDPlayer

    C->>F: watchMovie("Inception")
    F->>L: dim(10)
    L-->>F: ok
    F->>P: on()
    P-->>F: ok
    F->>A: on()
    A-->>F: ok
    F->>A: setVolume(5)
    A-->>F: ok
    F->>D: on()
    D-->>F: ok
    F->>D: play("Inception")
    D-->>F: ok
    F-->>C: Movie started
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This flow shows how Façade maps to a **layered enterprise application** — a typical KTU lab context.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Presentation["Presentation Layer"]
        UI1["Web UI"]
        UI2["Mobile UI"]
        UI3["REST Client"]
    end

    subgraph FacadeLayer["Façade Layer"]
        FA["OrderFacade"]
        FB["PaymentFacade"]
        FC["InventoryFacade"]
    end

    subgraph BusinessLayer["Business Logic Layer"]
        BA["OrderService"]
        BB["PaymentService"]
        BC["InventoryService"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["Data Access Layer"]
        DA["OrderDAO"]
        DB["PaymentDAO"]
        DC["StockDAO"]
    end

    subgraph Database["Database"]
        DB1[(Orders DB)]
        DB2[(Payments DB)]
        DB3[(Stock DB)]
    end

    UI1 --> FA
    UI2 --> FA
    UI3 --> FB

    FA --> BA
    FB --> BB
    FC --> BC

    BA --> DA
    BB --> DB
    BC --> DC

    DA --> DB1
    DB --> DB2
    DC --> DB3
```

**Architectural Insight:**
- Each business layer naturally acts as a **Façade** to the data layer.
- UI never bypasses the façade to hit the DAO directly — preserves layered integrity.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Map — Façade vs Related Patterns

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Adapter] -->|Converts interface| B[Façade]
    B -->|Simplifies subsystem| C[Mediator]
    C -->|Coordinates peers| D[Observer]
    B -->|Often combined with| E[Singleton]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Façade design pattern. State any two situations where it is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Façade pattern** is a structural design pattern that provides a **unified, higher-level interface** to a set of interfaces in a subsystem, making the subsystem easier to use.
>
> **Applicability:**
> 1. When a subsystem is **complex and has many interdependent classes**, and the client only needs a simplified view.
> 2. When there are **many dependencies between clients and implementation classes** of an abstraction, and one wants to **decouple the subsystem from its clients**.
>
> *(1 mark definition + 2 marks applicability = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**How does the Façade pattern differ from the Adapter pattern?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Façade | Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Simplify a complex subsystem | Convert an existing interface into one a client expects |
| **Scope** | Wraps **multiple** subsystem classes | Usually wraps **one** class |
| **Goal** | **New abstraction** over existing code | **Interface compatibility** for legacy/third-party code |
| **Subsystem awareness** | Subsystem unaware of Façade | Adaptee unaware of Adapter |

*(3 marks — examiners award 1 mark per valid point of comparison, up to 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never draw only the UML diagram and skip explanation** — diagram alone carries max 4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting participants and their responsibilities** costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Not stating SOLID principle mapping** loses you 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Writing Java code with syntax errors** is penalized — write pseudocode if unsure.
> 5. **Confusing Façade with Singleton** — Singleton ensures one instance; Façade simplifies interface. They can coexist (Singleton Façade) but are different patterns.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the intent, structure (with UML class diagram), and participants of the Façade design pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a `CompilerFacade` for a system that has subsystems — `Lexer`, `Parser`, `Optimizer`, `CodeGenerator` — using the Façade pattern. Provide the class structure and a sample client invocation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**1. Intent (2 marks):**
The Façade pattern provides a **unified, higher-level interface** to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. It defines a single entry point (the Façade) that delegates client requests to the appropriate subsystem objects, thereby **reducing coupling** between the client and the subsystem.

**2. Structure / UML Class Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Draw the canonical GoF diagram — see Section 4.1. Client → Façade → Subsystems with composition arrows.)*

**3. Participants (2 marks):**
- **Façade** — Knows subsystem classes, delegates client requests.
- **Subsystem Classes** — Implement functionality; unaware of the Façade.
- **Client** — Communicates with subsystem via Façade only.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Façade Implementation (7 marks):**

```java
// Subsystems
class Lexer      { public void tokenize(String src)  { /* ... */ } }
class Parser     { public ASTNode parse(String toks) { /* ... */ } }
class Optimizer  { public ASTNode optimize(ASTNode t){ /* ... */ } }
class CodeGenerator { public String gen(ASTNode t)   { /* ... */ } }

// Façade
class CompilerFacade {
    private Lexer lex;
    private Parser par;
    private Optimizer opt;
    private CodeGenerator cg;

    public CompilerFacade() {
        lex = new Lexer();
        par = new Parser();
        opt = new Optimizer();
        cg  = new CodeGenerator();
    }

    public String compile(String sourceCode) {
        lex.tokenize(sourceCode);
        ASTNode ast = par.parse(sourceCode);
        ASTNode optAst = opt.optimize(ast);
        return cg.gen(optAst);
    }
}

// Client
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        CompilerFacade compiler = new CompilerFacade();
        String binary = compiler.compile("int x = 10;");
        System.out.println("Generated: " + binary);
    }
}
```

**Mark split (b):** Subsystems (2) + Façade class (3) + Client invocation (2).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Discuss the consequences (benefits and liabilities) of applying the Façade pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast Façade with Mediator and Adapter using a real-world scenario of an **E-Commerce Checkout System**. Identify which pattern suits the **API Gateway layer**, which suits the **Payment Gateway Integration**, and which suits the **Notification Service**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Benefits (4 marks):**
1. **Reduces coupling** between clients and subsystems — client knows only the Façade.
2. **Simplifies client code** — fewer method calls, cleaner API.
3. **Promotes layered architecture** — each layer can be a Façade for the next.
4. **Hides implementation changes** — subsystem can evolve without affecting clients.

**Liabilities (3 marks):**
1. **God Façade anti-pattern** — Façade may grow too large if it tries to expose everything.
2. **Performance overhead** — extra indirection layer (usually negligible).
3. **Restricted customisation** — clients needing deep subsystem access are forced through the Façade.

#### Model Solution (b):

| Layer | Best Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **API Gateway** | **Façade** | Single unified entry point hides dozens of backend microservices |
| **Payment Gateway Integration** | **Adapter** | Need to convert our `PaymentRequest` to vendor-specific formats (Razorpay, Stripe, PayPal) |
| **Notification Service** | **Mediator** | Coordinates between Email, SMS, Push — they don't know about each other |

*(7 marks: 2 for correct pattern identification + 3 for justification + 2 for Façade focus)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before exam):**

- ✅ Façade is a **structural GoF pattern** providing a **unified higher-level interface**.
- ✅ It has **2 canonical participants**: **Façade** and **Subsystem Classes** (+ implicit Client).
- ✅ Subsystems are **unaware** of the Façade — this is the key decoupling property.
- ✅ Implements **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** — Client depends on Façade abstraction.
- ✅ Often combined with **Singleton** (Singleton Façade — single global access point).
- ✅ Supports **layered architecture** — each layer naturally is a façade to the layer below.
- ✅ Differs from **Adapter** (converts interface) and **Mediator** (coordinates peers).
- ✅ Risk of **God Façade anti-pattern** — keep façade focused.
- ✅ Java example: `HomeTheaterFacade`, `CompilerFacade`, `JDBC Connection`.
- ✅ **Coupling reduction formula** (bonus marks):
$$
\text{CouplingReduction} = \frac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%
$$
- ✅ Draw **UML Class Diagram** with composition arrows from Façade to Subsystems.
- ✅ Mention **SOLID principle** (DIP) in every 14-mark answer.
- ✅ Real-world: **JDBC, Spring RestTemplate, API Gateway, HAL, Compiler front-ends**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flyweight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flyweight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (GoF Gang of Four Catalog):** The **Flyweight Pattern** is a *structural design pattern* that uses **object sharing** to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently by minimizing memory consumption. It achieves this by partitioning object state into **intrinsic (shared)** state and **extrinsic (unique, non-shareable)** state, and by storing the intrinsic state in a shared Flyweight object while passing the extrinsic state to the methods that need it.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the Flyweight pattern is positioned under **Module 2: Design Patterns (Structural Patterns)**. It is a direct application of the **object-pooling and sharing** principle mandated by performance-aware software engineering curricula.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **librarian in a university central library**:

- There are **10,000 students** who all need to read the *same* textbook, say, "Operating Systems by Silberschatz."
- If the library issues a **separate physical copy to every student**, the library will need 10,000 books → impossible in a limited space.
- Instead, the library keeps **ONE reference copy** (the *Flyweight* — shared intrinsic state) and gives each student a **library token** (the *extrinsic state* — student ID, due date, locker number) that they use to access the book.

**Result:** Memory (shelves) usage drops from **O(N)** to **O(1)** for the shared content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Software Analogy — Character Rendering in a Word Processor:**
> A document with 500,000 characters would normally create 500,000 `Character` objects. With Flyweight, only **one `Character` object per unique character glyph (a, b, c, ... A, B, ... 0–9)** is stored (~62 objects). The position, font size, and color are passed as **extrinsic parameters** to the `draw()` method at render time.

### 1.3 When to Use Flyweight (Applicability Criteria)

The Flyweight pattern is applicable **only when ALL** of the following are true:

1. An application uses a **large number of objects**.
2. **Storage costs are high** because of the sheer quantity of objects.
3. Most object state can be made **extrinsic** (moved outside the object).
4. Once extrinsic state is removed, **many groups of objects can be replaced by relatively few shared objects**.
5. The application **does not depend on object identity** — two identical objects are considered the same.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Trigger:** Think of Flyweight whenever you see the keywords *“large number,” “memory,” “shared,”* or *“object pool.”*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Types of Object State

The Flyweight pattern fundamentally revolves around **state partitioning**:

| State Type | Definition | Where It Lives | Shared? |
|------------|------------|----------------|---------|
| **Intrinsic State** | State that is **independent** of the object's context and can be shared across many objects. | Stored **inside** the Flyweight object. | **Yes** — shared via the Flyweight Factory. |
| **Extrinsic State** | State that **depends on the context** and cannot be shared; it varies per object instance. | Passed in as **method arguments** (or computed externally). | **No** — unique per caller. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Keyword (HIGH-WEIGHTAGE):** Students MUST be able to clearly distinguish between **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** state with a concrete example. This is a **3-mark standard question** in Module 2.

### 2.2 Participants (GoF Catalog Roles)

The Flyweight pattern involves **four formal participants**:

1. **Flyweight (Interface / Abstract Class)** — Declares the interface through which flyweights can receive and act on extrinsic state.
2. **ConcreteFlyweight** — Implements the Flyweight interface and stores **intrinsic state**. Must be **shareable**.
3. **UnsharedConcreteFlyweight** (optional) — Not all Flyweight subclasses need to be shared; this represents non-shareable concrete instances.
4. **FlyweightFactory** — Creates and manages flyweight objects. Ensures flyweights are **shared properly**; when a client requests a flyweight, the factory either returns an existing instance or creates a new one if none exists.
5. **Client** — Maintains a reference to flyweights and computes/stores the **extrinsic state** to be passed to the flyweight methods.

### 2.3 Memory Savings — Quantitative Analysis

The Flyweight pattern's benefit can be quantified. This is a high-yield derivation frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems.

**Let:**
- $N$ = total number of object instances required by the application
- $K$ = number of unique intrinsic states (i.e., number of ConcreteFlyweight classes / types)
- $S_i$ = memory size of one ConcreteFlyweight (intrinsic state)
- $S_e$ = memory size of the extrinsic state (held by the client per object)
- $S_f$ = memory size of the FlyweightFactory overhead

**Memory without Flyweight (Naïve Approach):**
$$M_{\text{naive}} = N \times (S_i + S_e)$$

**Memory with Flyweight (Optimized Approach):**
$$M_{\text{flyweight}} = K \times S_i + N \times S_e + S_f$$

**Memory Savings Ratio:**
$$\eta = \frac{M_{\text{naive}} - M_{\text{flyweight}}}{M_{\text{naive}}} \times 100\%$$

After substitution:
$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e + S_f}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)}\right) \times 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** When $K \ll N$ (few unique types vs. many instances) **AND** $S_i \gg S_f$, the savings approach $\approx \left(1 - \frac{S_e}{S_i + S_e}\right)$, which can be **>90%** in text-rendering and particle-system workloads.

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Definition | Key Constraint |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Total instances | $N$ | Large |
| 2 | Unique flyweight types | $K$ | $K \ll N$ |
| 3 | Intrinsic state size | $S_i$ | Stored in ConcreteFlyweight |
| 4 | Extrinsic state size | $S_e$ | Passed as method argument |
| 5 | Naïve memory | $M_{\text{naive}} = N(S_i + S_e)$ | Baseline |
| 6 | Flyweight memory | $M_{\text{flyweight}} = K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e + S_f$ | Optimized |
| 7 | Savings ratio | $\eta = \left(1 - \frac{M_{\text{flyweight}}}{M_{\text{naive}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | $0 \le \eta \le 100\%$ |
| 8 | Lookup complexity (Factory) | $O(1)$ avg. using HashMap | $O(N)$ in worst case |
| 9 | Pattern Category | Structural (GoF) | — |
| 10 | Trade-off | Time vs. Space (CPU ↑, RAM ↓) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Flyweight is widely used in **game development** (particle systems, tile maps, sprites), **compilers** (symbol tables, AST nodes for repeated literals), **GUI frameworks** (icon libraries, glyph caching), and **database connection pools**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Memory Savings Ratio Expansion (Exhaustive)

We start from the savings ratio defined in §2.3:
$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{M_{\text{flyweight}}}{M_{\text{naive}}}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the two memory expressions.**

We replace $M_{\text{naive}}$ with $N \cdot (S_i + S_e)$ and $M_{\text{flyweight}}$ with $K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e + S_f$:

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e + S_f}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Split the numerator inside the fraction to expose each contribution.**

We expand the fraction as three separate terms over the common denominator $N \cdot (S_i + S_e)$:

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{K \cdot S_i}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)} - \frac{N \cdot S_e}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)} - \frac{S_f}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the middle term ($N \cdot S_e$ cancels with $N$ in the denominator).**

We obtain:

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{K \cdot S_i}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)} - \frac{S_e}{S_i + S_e} - \frac{S_f}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the denominator $N \cdot (S_i + S_e)$ for the first and third terms.**

We note that the first and third terms share the same denominator, so they can be combined:

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{K \cdot S_i + S_f}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)} - \frac{S_e}{S_i + S_e}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Apply the asymptotic limit $N \to \infty$.**

For very large $N$ (the regime where Flyweight is justified), the term $\frac{K \cdot S_i + S_f}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)} \to 0$ because $N$ dominates the numerator's $K$ and $S_f$ which are constants. Thus:

$$\eta_{\infty} = \left(1 - \frac{S_e}{S_i + S_e}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 6 — Final asymptotic closed-form.**

Rearranging gives the **memory-savings ceiling** of the Flyweight pattern:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\infty} = \frac{S_i}{S_i + S_e} \times 100\%\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even in the *best possible* case, savings are bounded by the **fraction of total per-object state that is intrinsic**. If 70% of an object's bytes are intrinsic ($S_i = 0.7(S_i + S_e)$), savings cannot exceed **70%**. This is a strict upper bound and is a frequent 14-mark derivation target.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Concrete Numerical Example (Text Editor)

**Problem:** A text editor renders a **100-page document**, average **300 words/page × 5 characters/word = 1500 characters/page**, giving $N = 150{,}000$ total characters. Assume the English alphabet + digits + punctuation gives $K = 72$ unique glyphs. Each `Character` object has $S_i = 200$ bytes (glyph bitmap metadata) + $S_e = 50$ bytes (font, size, color) = 250 bytes total. The factory overhead is $S_f = 2{,}000$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Compute naïve memory.**

$$M_{\text{naive}} = 150{,}000 \times 250 = 37{,}500{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 35.76 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute flyweight memory.**

$$M_{\text{flyweight}} = 72 \times 200 + 150{,}000 \times 50 + 2{,}000$$
$$= 14{,}400 + 7{,}500{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 7{,}516{,}400 \text{ bytes} \approx 7.17 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the savings ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{37{,}500{,}000 - 7{,}516{,}400}{37{,}500{,}000} \times 100\%$$
$$= \frac{29{,}983{,}600}{37{,}500{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 79.96\%$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the asymptotic formula.**

$$\eta_{\infty} = \frac{200}{250} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

The numerical answer (79.96%) is extremely close to the theoretical ceiling (80%) because $N$ is large and $S_f$ is negligible. ✅

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Comparison

| Property | Flyweight | Singleton | Prototype | Object Pool |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Intent | Share many fine-grained objects | Ensure one global instance | Clone existing object | Reuse expensive resources |
| Sharing Granularity | Many-to-one (per type) | One global | Per-clone | Per-resource |
| State Handling | Intrinsic/Extrinsic split | Single shared state | Copied state | Pooled state |
| Memory Effect | **Drastic reduction** | Minimal | Often increases | Reduces allocation cost |
| Identity Dependence | None | Yes (unique) | Yes (clone) | Yes (handle) |
| KTU Module | 2 (Structural) | 2 (Creational) | 2 (Creational) | 2 (Creational) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GoF Standard UML Class Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Flyweight {
        <<interface>>
        +operation(extrinsicState)
    }
    class ConcreteFlyweight {
        -intrinsicState : Object
        +operation(extrinsicState)
    }
    class UnsharedConcreteFlyweight {
        -allState : Object
        +operation(extrinsicState)
    }
    class FlyweightFactory {
        -flyweights : HashMap
        +getFlyweight(key) Flyweight
    }
    class Client {
        -extrinsicState : Object
        +doSomething()
    }

    Flyweight <|.. ConcreteFlyweight
    Flyweight <|.. UnsharedConcreteFlyweight
    FlyweightFactory --> Flyweight : creates / caches
    Client ..> Flyweight : uses
    Client ..> FlyweightFactory : requests
```

### 4.2 Runtime Sequence Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CL as Client
    participant FA as FlyweightFactory
    participant FW as ConcreteFlyweight

    CL->>FA: getFlyweight("A")
    alt Key not found in pool
        FA->>FW: new ConcreteFlyweight("A")
        FA->>FA: store in flyweights map
    else Key already in pool
        Note over FA: Return cached instance
    end
    FA-->>CL: returns shared Flyweight
    CL->>FW: operation(extrinsic: {row=3, col=12, font=bold})
    FW-->>CL: rendered glyph data
    Note over CL,FW: No new object created on second call
    CL->>FA: getFlyweight("A")
    FA-->>CL: returns SAME shared Flyweight (identity ==)
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Client] --> B[Compute Extrinsic State]
    B --> C{Request Flyweight by Key}
    C --> D[FlyweightFactory]
    D --> E{Lookup in HashMap}
    E -->|Hit| F[Reuse Existing ConcreteFlyweight]
    E -->|Miss| G[Create New ConcreteFlyweight]
    G --> H[Store in HashMap]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Invoke operation with Extrinsic State]
    I --> J[Return Result to Client]
    J --> K[Memory Footprint Drastically Reduced]
```

### 4.4 State-Partitioning Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE ["Naive Object: 250 bytes"]
        B1[Char Data 200B] --> B2[Font 30B]
        B2 --> B3[Color 10B] --> B4[Position 10B]
    end
    subgraph AFTER ["Flyweight Object: 200B intrinsic + 50B extrinsic"]
        C1[Char Glyph 200B]:::shared
        C2[Position 30B]:::unique
        C3[Font 15B]:::unique
        C4[Color 5B]:::unique
    end
    classDef shared fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef unique fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** Blue = **shared intrinsic state**, Orange = **unique extrinsic state** passed per call.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the **Flyweight design pattern**. Distinguish clearly between **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** state with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines expected):**

The Flyweight pattern is a **structural design pattern** that minimizes memory usage by **sharing common parts of object state** across a large number of similar objects instead of keeping all the data in each object.

State is partitioned into two categories:

- **Intrinsic State:** Data that is **independent of context** and can be safely shared. Example: the bitmap/vector shape data of a tree sprite in a forest-scene game — all 1,000,000 trees use the *same* tree shape.
- **Extrinsic State:** Data that **depends on context** and must be supplied by the client. Example: the (x, y) screen coordinates and scale factor of *each* individual tree — these cannot be shared.

The Flyweight is created and managed by a **FlyweightFactory**, which returns shared instances from a pool keyed by intrinsic state. The client passes extrinsic state to the flyweight's methods at call time. **Memory savings of 80–95%** are common in text-editing, gaming, and compiler workloads.

---

**Q2.** List the **four participants** of the Flyweight pattern and state the role of the **FlyweightFactory** in two sentences.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flyweight (Interface)** — Declares the interface that all concrete flyweights implement.
2. **ConcreteFlyweight** — Stores and shares the intrinsic state.
3. **UnsharedConcreteFlyweight** *(optional)* — Represents flyweight instances that are NOT shared.
4. **FlyweightFactory** — Creates flyweights and **ensures they are shared properly**; it maintains a pool/cache and returns existing instances instead of creating new ones whenever possible, thereby guaranteeing that intrinsic state is reused.

> **Role of FlyweightFactory (in 2 sentences):** The factory **decouples the client from object construction** and **enforces the sharing invariant** by checking the pool before allocating a new flyweight. Without it, clients could accidentally instantiate duplicate flyweights and the memory benefit would be lost.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Flyweight design pattern** with a real-world analogy. Draw the **class diagram** showing all four participants and describe the **responsibilities** of each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A graphics-intensive game must render **500,000 tree objects** in a forest. Each tree has an intrinsic part (the sprite image + species metadata) of **1,500 bytes** and an extrinsic part (x, y, scale, rotation) of **100 bytes**. The factory overhead is **10,000 bytes** and there are **8 unique tree species** in the game. Calculate the **memory savings ratio** achieved by applying the Flyweight pattern. Show every step.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Flyweight pattern** is a structural pattern that uses **object sharing** to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently.

**Real-world analogy:** A **bus-shuttle service** between two campuses. Instead of giving every student their own car (high memory), the university runs **shared buses** (Flyweights) on fixed routes (intrinsic state), while each passenger carries a **personal seat reservation** (extrinsic state — extrinsic because it differs per passenger). The bus itself is shared; the seat is unique.

**Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class TreeFlyweight {
        <<interface>>
        +draw(x, y, scale, rotation)
    }
    class ConcreteTree {
        -speciesName : String
        -spriteData : byteArray
        +draw(x, y, scale, rotation)
    }
    class TreeFactory {
        -treePool : HashMap
        +getTree(species) TreeFlyweight
    }
    class GameClient {
        -x, y, scale, rotation : float
        +renderForest()
    }
    TreeFlyweight <|.. ConcreteTree
    TreeFactory --> ConcreteTree
    GameClient ..> TreeFactory
    GameClient ..> TreeFlyweight
```

**Responsibilities:**

| Participant | Responsibility |
|-------------|----------------|
| **TreeFlyweight** | Declares `draw()` accepting extrinsic state (x, y, scale, rotation). |
| **ConcreteTree** | Stores intrinsic state: speciesName + spriteData. Implements `draw()` using intrinsic + supplied extrinsic state. |
| **TreeFactory** | Maintains a pool keyed by species. Returns cached tree or creates+stores a new one. Ensures 1 instance per species, not 500,000. |
| **GameClient** | Owns the extrinsic state for each tree and calls `tree.draw(x, y, scale, rotation)`. |

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $N = 500{,}000$, $K = 8$, $S_i = 1{,}500$ bytes, $S_e = 100$ bytes, $S_f = 10{,}000$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Naïve memory (no Flyweight):**

$$M_{\text{naive}} = N \times (S_i + S_e) = 500{,}000 \times 1{,}600 = 800{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 800 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Flyweight memory:**

$$M_{\text{flyweight}} = K \times S_i + N \times S_e + S_f$$
$$= 8 \times 1{,}500 + 500{,}000 \times 100 + 10{,}000$$
$$= 12{,}000 + 50{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 50{,}022{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 50.02 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Savings ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{800{,}000{,}000 - 50{,}022{,}000}{800{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{749{,}978{,}000}{800{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 93.75\%$$

**Step 4 — Verification with the asymptotic bound:**

$$\eta_{\infty} = \frac{S_i}{S_i + S_e} \times 100\% = \frac{1{,}500}{1{,}600} \times 100\% = 93.75\%$$

✅ The savings **match the theoretical upper bound exactly** (the small deviation in Step 3 is only due to the negligible $S_f / N \to 0$).

**Conclusion:** The Flyweight pattern reduces memory from **800 MB to ~50 MB**, a **93.75% reduction** — and the theoretical maximum possible is also 93.75%, confirming optimal use of the pattern.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **applicability (use-cases)** of the Flyweight pattern. Mention **three real-world scenarios** where it is used and explain why it is suitable in each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the **Flyweight pattern** with the **Singleton pattern** and the **Prototype pattern** in a tabular form covering: intent, sharing mechanism, state handling, typical use case, and a key disadvantage.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The Flyweight pattern is applicable whenever an application must support **large numbers of fine-grained objects** efficiently. Three canonical scenarios are:

1. **Text Editors and Word Processors:**
   - **Why suitable:** A document may contain hundreds of thousands of characters, but only a small set of unique glyphs (e.g., 256 ASCII characters).
   - **Application:** Each `Glyph` object holds intrinsic state (the bitmap data, Unicode ID), while position, font, color, and size are passed as extrinsic parameters. Memory is reduced from O(N) to O(K), where K is the alphabet size.

2. **Game Engines — Particle Systems and Tile Maps:**
   - **Why suitable:** A forest scene with 1,000,000 trees or a battlefield with 500,000 bullets would explode memory if each had a full sprite. Flyweight shares the sprite metadata.
   - **Application:** Only one `TreeSprite` per species or one `BulletSprite` per type is created; per-instance position/rotation is rendered as extrinsic state.

3. **Compilers — Symbol Tables and AST Nodes:**
   - **Why suitable:** Source code contains many repeated identifiers and literal values (variable names, integer constants). Creating a new object for each occurrence is wasteful.
   - **Application:** The compiler interns (caches) identifier strings and literal values in a symbol table, sharing one `String`/`Integer` Flyweight per unique value (this is exactly what Java's `String.intern()` and Python's integer caching do).

> A fourth modern scenario is **HTTP/JSON API responses in microservices**, where repeated enum values, currency codes, and country names are interned to reduce serialization size.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — Comparison Table

| Aspect | **Flyweight** | **Singleton** | **Prototype** |
|--------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Intent** | Share many fine-grained objects to reduce memory | Ensure a class has exactly one instance | Clone an existing object to create a new one |
| **Sharing Mechanism** | FlyweightFactory pools by key, returns shared instances | Static `getInstance()` returns the one global instance | `clone()` method duplicates the prototype |
| **State Handling** | Splits state into intrinsic (shared) and extrinsic (passed in) | Single global state shared by all callers | Each clone has its own state, identical to the original at clone time |
| **Typical Use Case** | Text editors, game particles, symbol tables | Configuration manager, logging service, thread pool | Object creation is expensive; create copies with slight variations |
| **Key Disadvantage** | Adds complexity (factory + state partition); one-time factory lookup cost | Can become a "god object"; not thread-safe by default | Deep vs. shallow cloning pitfalls; circular reference issues |
| **Pattern Category** | Structural | Creational | Creational |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places KTU Students Lose Marks on Flyweight Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Intrinsic and Extrinsic state (–2 marks):** Many students interchange these two terms. *Intrinsic = stored in object, shared.* *Extrinsic = passed from outside, unique.* This is the **#1 deduction reason** in valuation.
> 2. **Forgetting the FlyweightFactory (–2 marks):** A UML answer with only `Flyweight` + `ConcreteFlyweight` is INCOMPLETE. Always include the factory — it is the *enforcer* of sharing.
> 3. **Skipping the numerical derivation (–3 marks):** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners often ask for a memory-savings calculation. Skipping the algebra loses heavy marks.
> 4. **Drawing a "Prototype-like" diagram:** The Flyweight diagram MUST show `FlyweightFactory` as a *sealed pool* with a `getFlyweight(key)` method, not as a generic creator.
> 5. **Mixing up "shared" and "singleton":** Flyweight allows **K** shared instances (one per key), not just ONE. This is a frequent 3-mark trap question.
> 6. **Not mentioning `equals`/`hashCode` semantics:** When the Flyweight pool is a `HashMap`, identical intrinsic state MUST produce identical `hashCode` and `equals` results, or the pool will desynchronize.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print-friendly 30-second revision list — KTU Module 2 / Flyweight**

- ☐ Flyweight is a **Structural** GoF pattern; Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme.
- ☐ **Purpose:** Reduce memory by sharing common (intrinsic) state across many objects.
- ☐ **Intrinsic state** → stored inside the ConcreteFlyweight, shared.
- ☐ **Extrinsic state** → passed as method arguments, NOT shared.
- ☐ **Four Participants:** Flyweight (interface), ConcreteFlyweight, UnsharedConcreteFlyweight (optional), FlyweightFactory, and Client.
- ☐ **FlyweightFactory** is mandatory in correct answers — it pools objects keyed by intrinsic state and returns existing instances.
- ☐ **Memory Formula:** $M_{\text{flyweight}} = K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e + S_f$ vs. $M_{\text{naive}} = N(S_i + S_e)$.
- ☐ **Maximum savings bound:** $\eta_{\infty} = \dfrac{S_i}{S_i + S_e} \times 100\%$ — *this is the ceiling*.
- ☐ **Applicability rule:** Use ONLY when $K \ll N$ and objects do not depend on identity.
- ☐ **Trade-off:** Trades **CPU (factory lookup + extrinsic passing)** for **RAM (sharing savings)**.
- ☐ **Real-world examples:** Word-processor glyphs, game particles, `String.intern()`, compiler symbol tables.
- ☐ **Hashing is critical:** Override `equals()` and `hashCode()` on intrinsic state for the factory's `HashMap` to function.
- ☐ **Comparison traps:** Flyweight ≠ Singleton (K shared, not 1); Flyweight ≠ Prototype (sharing, not cloning).
- ☐ **UML must include** `getFlyweight(key)` on the factory — omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
- ☐ **State-partitioning question** is a guaranteed **3-marker** in Part A; the **numerical derivation** is a frequent **7-marker** in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioral Design Pattern
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Behavioral Design Pattern

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Behavioral Design Pattern — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioral Design Patterns** are a category of design patterns in software engineering (popularized by the *Gang of Four — GoF*) that are concerned with **algorithms, the assignment of responsibilities between objects, and the patterns of communication between them**. They characterize the ways in which classes or objects interact and distribute responsibility, focusing on **"How" objects communicate** rather than "How" they are created or composed.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **Course Outcome CO2 (Apply design patterns to model object-oriented solutions)** and **CO3 (Evaluate architectural choices for software systems)**, behavioral patterns address the dynamic flow of control and responsibility across a system, decoupling senders from receivers.

The **Gang of Four (GoF)** catalogue lists **11 behavioral patterns**:
**Chain of Responsibility, Command, Interpreter, Iterator, Mediator, Memento, Observer, State, Strategy, Template Method,** and **Visitor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST411 — Module 2):** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, students must be able to *identify, draw UML class/sequence diagrams for, and write Java/C++ code implementing* at least the core behavioral patterns: **Observer, Strategy, State, Command, Template Method, Iterator, and Mediator.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Workflow" Intuition

Imagine a **large corporate office**:
- When you send a complaint email, it doesn't go directly to the CEO. It travels through a **chain** — Help Desk → Manager → Director → CEO.
- The **Help Desk doesn't know** who will finally resolve it. It just forwards it.
- This is **Chain of Responsibility**.
- Now imagine every email you send is wrapped as a **Command object** that can be queued, logged, undone, or scheduled. This is the **Command Pattern**.
- When a sensor detects motion, **multiple systems react** — lights turn on, alarm triggers, camera starts recording. The sensor is the **Subject**, the systems are **Observers**.

In short, behavioral patterns govern **who talks to whom, in what order, and under what rules**.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Behavioral Patterns?

Without behavioral patterns, software tends to exhibit:
1. **Tight coupling** — objects know too much about each other.
2. **Rigid control flow** — `if-else` and `switch` blocks proliferate.
3. **Difficult extension** — adding new behavior requires modifying existing code (violates the **Open/Closed Principle**).
4. **Scattered logic** — algorithmic behavior is duplicated across classes.

Behavioral patterns **distribute behavior intelligently** so the system becomes **flexible, extensible, and maintainable**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Behavioral Patterns

Behavioral patterns can be classified into two structural families:

| Family | Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Class-based (Inheritance)** | Use inheritance to distribute behavior between classes | Template Method, Interpreter |
| **Object-based (Composition)** | Use object composition to coordinate behavior at runtime | Strategy, Observer, Command, State, Iterator, Mediator, Memento, Chain of Responsibility, Visitor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Object-based patterns are far more common in modern OOP** because composition offers greater runtime flexibility than inheritance, aligning with the famous GoF principle: *"Favor object composition over class inheritance."*

## 1.5 The Unified UML Notation (KTU Board Exam Standard)

The KTU board examiner expects the following standard UML class diagram notations when drawing behavioral patterns:

```
<<interface>>       — for pure abstract types
{abstract}          — for partial abstract classes
—►                 — Association (knows about)
——►                — Dependency (uses temporarily)
◇—►                — Aggregation (weak has-a)
◆—►                — Composition (strong has-a)
```

These notations appear in every KTU answer paper where patterns are discussed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Catalog of Behavioral Patterns — High-Yield Summary

Below is a concise reference table covering **all 11 GoF behavioral patterns**. For KTU exams, focus on the **bolded patterns** as they appear most frequently in question papers.

| # | Pattern | Intent | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chain of Responsibility (CoR)** | Pass a request along a chain of handlers until one handles it | High |
| 2 | **Command** | Encapsulate a request as an object, enabling parameterization, queuing, undo | High |
| 3 | **Interpreter** | Define a grammar and an interpreter for sentences in that language | Low |
| 4 | **Iterator** | Access elements of an aggregate sequentially without exposing its representation | Medium |
| 5 | **Mediator** | Define an object that encapsulates how a set of objects interact | High |
| 6 | Memento | Capture and externalize an object's internal state without violating encapsulation | Low |
| 7 | **Observer** | Define a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all dependents are notified | Very High |
| 8 | **State** | Allow an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes | High |
| 9 | **Strategy** | Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable | Very High |
| 10 | **Template Method** | Define the skeleton of an algorithm, deferring some steps to subclasses | High |
| 11 | Visitor | Represent an operation to be performed on elements of an object structure | Low |

---

## 2.2 The Five High-Priority Patterns — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Observer Pattern (Publication–Subscription)

**Intent:** Define a **one-to-many dependency** between objects so that when one object (the **Subject / Observable**) changes state, all its dependents (**Observers / Listeners**) are notified and updated automatically.

**Key Participants:**
- **Subject** — provides `attach()`, `detach()`, `notify()`.
- **ConcreteSubject** — stores state; on state change, calls `notify()`.
- **Observer** — interface with `update()`.
- **ConcreteObserver** — implements `update()` to react.

**Why and How:**
- **Why:** Decouples the publisher from subscribers. The subject does not need to know what concrete type the observers are.
- **How:** Subject maintains a list of `Observer*` pointers. On `setState()`, it iterates and calls `update()` on each.

**Real-world utility:** GUI event listeners (Swing `ActionListener`, JavaScript `addEventListener`), MVC architecture, real-time dashboards, stock-ticker apps, RSS feeds.

**Java's built-in support:** `java.util.Observer` (deprecated) / `java.util.concurrent.Flow` (modern), `PropertyChangeListener` in JavaBeans.

---

### 2.2.2 Strategy Pattern (Policy Pattern)

**Intent:** Define a **family of algorithms**, encapsulate each one, and make them **interchangeable**. Strategy lets the algorithm vary independently from clients that use it.

**Key Participants:**
- **Strategy** — abstract interface (e.g., `pay()`).
- **ConcreteStrategyA, B, C** — different implementations.
- **Context** — maintains a reference to a `Strategy` object and delegates work to it.

**Why and How:**
- **Why:** Eliminates conditional logic (`if-else`/`switch`) for choosing algorithms. Open/Closed Principle compliance.
- **How:** The context accepts a `Strategy` via constructor or setter and calls `strategy.algorithmInterface()`.

**Real-world utility:** Payment gateways (CreditCard / UPI / PayPal), sorting algorithms selectable at runtime, compression algorithms, route-finding algorithms in Google Maps.

---

### 2.2.3 State Pattern

**Intent:** Allow an object to **alter its behavior when its internal state changes**. The object will appear to change its class.

**Key Participants:**
- **Context** — the class whose behavior changes (e.g., `VendingMachine`).
- **State** — abstract interface (`handle()`).
- **ConcreteStateA, B, C** — each represents a state and the behavior associated with it.

**Why and How:**
- **Why:** Replaces massive state-specific `switch` blocks scattered across the context with clean polymorphic state objects.
- **How:** Context delegates state-specific calls to the current `State` object; state objects can call `context.setState()` to transition.

**Real-world utility:** Vending machines, ATM machines, TCP connection states, document lifecycle (Draft → Review → Published), media player (Playing/Paused/Stopped).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State vs Strategy** — A very common KTU exam question! Both patterns look similar, but:
> - **State:** The context transitions between states *automatically* based on internal events. The states often know about each other.
> - **Strategy:** The client *explicitly chooses* the algorithm. Strategies are usually independent of each other.

---

### 2.2.4 Command Pattern (Action / Transaction Pattern)

**Intent:** Encapsulate a **request as an object**, thereby allowing parameterization of clients with different requests, queueing, logging, and **undo** support.

**Key Participants:**
- **Command** — interface with `execute()` and optionally `undo()`.
- **ConcreteCommand** — binds a `Receiver` to an action.
- **Invoker** — stores commands and triggers execution.
- **Receiver** — knows how to perform the work.
- **Client** — creates the command and assigns the receiver.

**Why and How:**
- **Why:** Decouples the object that *invokes* the operation from the one that *knows how* to perform it.
- **How:** Command object wraps a method-call and its arguments, exposing a uniform `execute()`.

**Real-world utility:** Text editor Undo/Redo stacks, macro recording, transactional behavior, job queues, smart-home device controllers (remote control of TV, lights, fan).

---

### 2.2.5 Template Method Pattern

**Intent:** Define the **skeleton of an algorithm** in an operation, deferring some steps to **subclasses**. Template Method lets subclasses redefine certain steps without changing the algorithm's structure.

**Key Participants:**
- **AbstractClass** — defines template method (often `final` in Java) and abstract primitive operations.
- **ConcreteClass** — implements the primitive operations.

**Why and How:**
- **Why:** Promotes code reuse while letting subclasses customize specific steps. Inversion of Control — "Hollywood Principle": *Don't call us, we'll call you.*
- **How:** The template method calls abstract methods (hooks/operations) which are implemented by subclasses.

**Real-world utility:** Frameworks — Spring's `JdbcTemplate`, Java's `AbstractList`, `HttpServlet.service()`. Building a data-mining pipeline (Open/Save/Extract/Parse/Analyze/Close shared; subclasses redefine `extract`/`analyze`).

---

## 2.3 The Five Secondary Patterns — Quick Reference

| Pattern | One-Line Description |
|---|---|
| **Chain of Responsibility** | Pass request through handlers; first one that can handle it does. |
| **Iterator** | Provide sequential access without exposing internal structure (`hasNext`, `next`). |
| **Mediator** | Central hub for inter-object communication; reduces many-to-many to one-to-many. |
| **Memento** | Save/restore object's state externally without breaking encapsulation. |
| **Visitor** | Add new operations to object structure without modifying the classes. |
| **Interpreter** | Build an interpreter for a simple language/grammar (e.g., regex). |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU **14-mark questions**, you will almost always be asked to draw the **UML class diagram + sequence diagram + Java code** for one of: **Observer, Strategy, State, Command, Template Method**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Concept Map (Visual Mental Model)

```
                ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                │   BEHAVIORAL DESIGN PATTERNS    │
                │   (Communication & Responsibility)│
                └─────────────┬────────────────────┘
                              │
        ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
        │                     │                     │
   ┌────▼────┐          ┌─────▼─────┐         ┌────▼────┐
   │ CLASS-  │          │ OBJECT-   │         │ OBJECT- │
   │ BASED   │          │ BASED     │         │ BASED   │
   │(Inherit)│          │(Composit) │         │(Composit)│
   └────┬────┘          └─────┬─────┘         └────┬────┘
        │                     │                     │
  Template Method      Strategy / State      Observer / Mediator
  Interpreter          Command / CoR         Iterator / Visitor
                       Memento
```

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

Behavioral patterns are not academic exercises — they are the **backbone of every modern framework**:

| Industry Domain | Pattern Used | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **GUI Frameworks** | Observer | JavaFX Property listeners |
| **Web Backends** | Chain of Responsibility | Servlet filters, Middleware in Express.js |
| **Game Development** | State, Command | NPC AI states, action replay systems |
| **Compilers** | Visitor, Interpreter | AST traversal, expression evaluation |
| **Enterprise Java** | Template Method | Spring's `JdbcTemplate`, `RestTemplate` |
| **Microservices** | Mediator, Observer | Service buses, Kafka event streams |
| **Mobile Apps** | Strategy | Payment provider selection in e-commerce |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Implementation Walkthroughs

This section provides **exhaustive, board-exam-ready** structural breakdowns for the **top 5 high-yield behavioral patterns**, including class structures, collaboration logic, and reference Java implementations.

---

## 3.1 OBSERVER PATTERN — Full Structural Derivation

### 3.1.1 Participants in Detail

1. **Subject (interface/abstract class)**
   - `attach(Observer o)` — register an observer
   - `detach(Observer o)` — unregister
   - `notifyObservers()` — push or pull update to all observers

2. **ConcreteSubject**
   - Holds the actual state.
   - When `setState()` is called, it calls `notifyObservers()`.

3. **Observer (interface)**
   - `update(Subject s)` — invoked on state change.

4. **ConcreteObserver**
   - Maintains a reference to the ConcreteSubject.
   - Implements `update()` to react.

### 3.1.2 Class Diagram in Textual Form

```
        ┌──────────────────────┐                ┌──────────────────────┐
        <<interface>>          │                <<interface>>          │
        │    Subject           │                │    Observer           │
        ├──────────────────────┤                ├──────────────────────┤
        │+ attach(o)           │<>─────────────>│+ update(s)           │
        │+ detach(o)           │  observers     │                      │
        │+ notifyObservers()   │                │                      │
        └─────────▲────────────┘                └─────────▲────────────┘
                  │                                      │
                  │ extends                              │ implements
                  │                                      │
        ┌─────────┴────────────┐                ┌────────┴─────────────┐
        │  ConcreteSubject     │                │  ConcreteObserver    │
        ├──────────────────────┤                ├──────────────────────┤
        │- state : int         │                │- observerState       │
        │+ getState()          │                │+ update(s)           │
        │+ setState(s)         │                └──────────────────────┘
        └──────────────────────┘
```

### 3.1.3 Sequence of Operations (Step-by-Step)

1. `ConcreteSubject.setState(newValue)` is called.
2. The subject updates its internal `state`.
3. The subject calls `notifyObservers()`.
4. For every observer in its list, the subject calls `observer.update(this)`.
5. Each observer queries the subject (`getState()`) — the **pull model** — or receives the new value directly — the **push model**.
6. The observer reacts (e.g., refreshes its display).

### 3.1.4 Reference Java Implementation

```java
// Subject interface
interface Subject {
    void attach(Observer o);
    void detach(Observer o);
    void notifyObservers();
}

// Observer interface
interface Observer {
    void update(Subject s);
}

// ConcreteSubject
class NewsAgency implements Subject {
    private String news;
    private final java.util.List<Observer> channels = new java.util.ArrayList<>();

    public void attach(Observer o) { channels.add(o); }
    public void detach(Observer o) { channels.remove(o); }

    public void notifyObservers() {
        for (Observer o : channels) o.update(this);
    }

    public void setNews(String news) {
        this.news = news;
        notifyObservers();          // step 2: notify on every change
    }
    public String getNews() { return news; }
}

// ConcreteObserver
class NewsChannel implements Observer {
    private String channelName;
    public NewsChannel(String name) { this.channelName = name; }
    public void update(Subject s) {
        System.out.println(channelName + " received: " + ((NewsAgency)s).getNews());
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Push vs Pull Model:** In the **push model**, the subject sends the new state as an argument to `update(newState)`. In the **pull model** (used above), the subject passes itself and the observer queries what it needs. The pull model is more flexible but less efficient.

---

## 3.2 STRATEGY PATTERN — Full Structural Derivation

### 3.2.1 Participants

1. **Strategy** — common interface for all supported algorithms (e.g., `PaymentStrategy` with `pay(amount)`).
2. **ConcreteStrategy** — implements a specific algorithm (e.g., `CreditCardPayment`, `UPIPayment`).
3. **Context** — maintains a reference to a `Strategy` and delegates the work to it.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. The `Context` is created with a default `Strategy`, or `Strategy` is injected later via a setter.
2. The client may call `context.setStrategy(newStrategy)` at runtime.
3. The context calls `strategy.algorithmInterface(data)`.
4. The selected strategy's implementation runs.

### 3.2.3 Reference Java Implementation

```java
interface PaymentStrategy {
    void pay(int amount);
}

class CreditCardPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Paid " + amount + " via Credit Card");
    }
}
class UPIPayment implements PaymentStrategy {
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Paid " + amount + " via UPI");
    }
}

class ShoppingCart {
    private PaymentStrategy strategy;
    public void setStrategy(PaymentStrategy s) { this.strategy = s; }
    public void checkout(int amount) {
        strategy.pay(amount);    // polymorphic call
    }
}
```

### 3.2.4 Before and After — Why the Pattern Matters

**Without Strategy (BAD):**

```java
class ShoppingCart {
    void checkout(int amount, String mode) {
        if (mode.equals("CC")) {/* credit card logic */}
        else if (mode.equals("UPI")) {/* UPI logic */}
        else if (mode.equals("WALLET")) {/* ... */}
        // Adding a new mode => modify this class => OCP violation
    }
}
```

**With Strategy (GOOD):** Add a new strategy class; no modification to `ShoppingCart`. Clean Open/Closed compliance.

---

## 3.3 STATE PATTERN — Full Structural Derivation

### 3.3.1 Participants

1. **Context** — e.g., `VendingMachine`. Holds a reference to current `State`.
2. **State** — abstract interface (`handle()`).
3. **ConcreteState** — e.g., `NoCoinState`, `HasCoinState`, `SoldState`, `SoldOutState`.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Context is created; initial state is set (e.g., `NoCoinState`).
2. Client calls `context.request()` (e.g., `insertCoin()`).
3. Context forwards the call to `currentState.handle()`.
4. The state object performs its work and may call `context.setState(newState)` to trigger transition.

### 3.3.3 Reference Java Implementation

```java
interface State {
    void handle(VendingMachine ctx);
}

class NoCoinState implements State {
    public void handle(VendingMachine ctx) {
        System.out.println("Coin inserted");
        ctx.setState(new HasCoinState());    // transition
    }
}
class HasCoinState implements State {
    public void handle(VendingMachine ctx) {
        System.out.println("Dispensing item");
        ctx.setState(new NoCoinState());     // transition back
    }
}

class VendingMachine {
    private State state = new NoCoinState();
    public void setState(State s) { this.state = s; }
    public void request() { state.handle(this); }
}
```

### 3.3.4 State vs Strategy — The KTU Favorite Comparison

| Aspect | State Pattern | Strategy Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger for change** | Internal state transitions | External client choice |
| **Knowledge of each other** | States often know about sibling states | Strategies are usually independent |
| **Number of objects** | Usually one current state active | One strategy active at a time, but can swap freely |
| **Intent** | Object behaves differently per state | Object uses different algorithms |

---

## 3.4 COMMAND PATTERN — Full Structural Derivation

### 3.4.1 Participants

1. **Command** — interface with `execute()` (and `undo()` if needed).
2. **ConcreteCommand** — binds a `Receiver` action.
3. **Invoker** — stores the command and calls `execute()`.
4. **Receiver** — knows the actual business logic.
5. **Client** — assembles everything.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Client creates a `Receiver`.
2. Client creates a `ConcreteCommand` and passes the receiver to it.
3. Client creates an `Invoker` and sets the command in it.
4. Invoker calls `command.execute()`.
5. ConcreteCommand delegates to the receiver.

### 3.4.3 Reference Java Implementation

```java
interface Command {
    void execute();
    void undo();
}

class Light {
    public void on() { System.out.println("Light ON"); }
    public void off() { System.out.println("Light OFF"); }
}

class LightOnCommand implements Command {
    private Light light;
    public LightOnCommand(Light l) { this.light = l; }
    public void execute() { light.on(); }
    public void undo()   { light.off(); }
}

class RemoteControl {                      // Invoker
    private Command slot;
    public void setCommand(Command c) { this.slot = c; }
    public void pressButton() { slot.execute(); }
}
```

### 3.4.4 Macros, Queues, and Undo

The Command pattern becomes powerful with these extensions:
- **Macro Command** — a `Command` that contains a list of commands and executes them in sequence.
- **Command Queue** — commands can be queued and processed asynchronously.
- **Undo Stack** — every executed command is pushed onto a stack; `undo()` pops and calls `command.undo()`.

---

## 3.5 TEMPLATE METHOD PATTERN — Full Structural Derivation

### 3.5.1 Participants

1. **AbstractClass** — defines the template method (concrete) and abstract primitive operations.
2. **ConcreteClass** — implements the primitive operations.

### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. The template method in `AbstractClass` outlines the algorithm's skeleton.
2. Each step is either:
   - **Concrete** — implemented in `AbstractClass` and shared by all subclasses.
   - **Abstract** — must be implemented by `ConcreteClass`.
   - **Hook** — has a default no-op implementation; subclasses *may* override to inject behavior at specific points.

### 3.5.3 Reference Java Implementation

```java
abstract class DataMiner {
    // Template method — final so subclasses cannot override the skeleton
    public final void mine(String path) {
        openFile(path);
        String raw = extractData();
        String parsed = parseData(raw);
        analyzeData(parsed);
        closeFile();
        sendReport();
    }
    protected void openFile(String p)    { System.out.println("Opening " + p); }
    protected void closeFile()          { System.out.println("Closing file"); }
    protected void sendReport()         { System.out.println("Report sent"); }
    protected abstract String extractData();
    protected abstract String parseData(String raw);
    protected abstract void analyzeData(String data);
}

class PDFDataMiner extends DataMiner {
    protected String extractData()    { return "[PDF bytes]"; }
    protected String parseData(String r){ return "Parsed PDF: " + r; }
    protected void analyzeData(String d){ System.out.println("Analyzing " + d); }
}
```

### 3.5.4 The Hollywood Principle

The Template Method embodies the **Hollywood Principle**: *"Don't call us, we'll call you."* The superclass calls the subclass's operations — *not* the reverse. This is the **Inversion of Control** at the heart of frameworks like Spring and Java's Servlet API.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hook Methods** in templates: A `hook()` is a concrete method with a default (often empty) implementation. Subclasses can *optionally* override it. This gives extension points without forcing subclasses to override every method.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Five Patterns

| Aspect | Observer | Strategy | State | Command | Template Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | 1-to-many notification | Algorithm family | Behavior per state | Encapsulate request | Algorithm skeleton |
| **Key Mechanism** | Subject + Observers | Context + Strategy | Context + State | Invoker + Command | Abstract + Concrete |
| **Polymorphism Type** | Interface-based | Interface-based | Interface-based | Interface-based | Inheritance-based |
| **Runtime Change** | Yes (attach/detach) | Yes (set strategy) | Yes (set state) | Yes (set command) | No (compile-time) |
| **Common OOP Principle** | OCP, DIP | OCP, SRP | OCP, SRP | OCP, SRP | OCP, Hollywood |
