| **Real-world Use** | GUI listeners, MVC | Payment methods | ATM, vending | Undo/Redo, queue | Frameworks |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides **Mermaid-based UML and sequence diagrams** for the most exam-relevant behavioral patterns. These follow the KTU 2024 board exam expectation for clean, well-labeled UML.

---

## 4.1 Observer Pattern — Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Subject {
        <<interface>>
        +attach(o Observer)
        +detach(o Observer)
        +notifyObservers()
    }
    class Observer {
        <<interface>>
        +update(s Subject)
    }
    class NewsAgency {
        -news : String
        -channels : List
        +attach(o Observer)
        +detach(o Observer)
        +notifyObservers()
        +setNews(news String)
        +getNews() String
    }
    class NewsChannel {
        -channelName : String
        +update(s Subject)
    }
    Subject <|.. NewsAgency : implements
    Observer <|.. NewsChannel : implements
    Subject --> "0..*" Observer : notifies
```

---

## 4.2 Observer Pattern — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant SubjectA as NewsAgency
    participant ObserverA as NewsChannel1
    participant ObserverB as NewsChannel2

    Client->>SubjectA: setNews("New Headline")
    SubjectA->>SubjectA: update internal state
    SubjectA->>ObserverA: update(this)
    SubjectA->>ObserverB: update(this)
    ObserverA-->>Client: prints news
    ObserverB-->>Client: prints news
```

---

## 4.3 Strategy Pattern — Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class PaymentStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +pay(amount int)
    }
    class CreditCardPayment {
        +pay(amount int)
    }
    class UPIPayment {
        +pay(amount int)
    }
    class WalletPayment {
        +pay(amount int)
    }
    class ShoppingCart {
        -strategy : PaymentStrategy
        +setStrategy(s PaymentStrategy)
        +checkout(amount int)
    }
    PaymentStrategy <|.. CreditCardPayment
    PaymentStrategy <|.. UPIPayment
    PaymentStrategy <|.. WalletPayment
    ShoppingCart --> PaymentStrategy : delegates
```

---

## 4.4 State Pattern — Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class VendingMachine {
        -currentState : State
        +setState(s State)
        +request()
    }
    class State {
        <<interface>>
        +handle(ctx VendingMachine)
    }
    class NoCoinState {
        +handle(ctx VendingMachine)
    }
    class HasCoinState {
        +handle(ctx VendingMachine)
    }
    class SoldOutState {
        +handle(ctx VendingMachine)
    }
    VendingMachine --> State : current
    State <|.. NoCoinState
    State <|.. HasCoinState
    State <|.. SoldOutState
    NoCoinState ..> HasCoinState : transition
    HasCoinState ..> NoCoinState : transition
```

---

## 4.5 Command Pattern — Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Command {
        <<interface>>
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class LightOnCommand {
        -light : Light
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class LightOffCommand {
        -light : Light
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class Light {
        +on()
        +off()
    }
    class RemoteControl {
        -slot : Command
        +setCommand(c Command)
        +pressButton()
    }
    Command <|.. LightOnCommand
    Command <|.. LightOffCommand
    LightOnCommand --> Light : receiver
    LightOffCommand --> Light : receiver
    RemoteControl --> Command : invokes
```

---

## 4.6 Template Method Pattern — Class Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class DataMiner {
        <<abstract>>
        +mine(path String) FINAL
        +openFile(p String)
        +closeFile()
        +sendReport()
        +extractData() abstract
        +parseData(raw String) abstract
        +analyzeData(data String) abstract
    }
    class PDFDataMiner {
        +extractData() String
        +parseData(raw String) String
        +analyzeData(data String)
    }
    class CSVDataMiner {

    DataMiner <|-- PDFDataMiner

```

## 4.7 Behavioral Pattern Family — Block Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Behavioral Design Patterns] --> B[Class Based Inheritance]
    A --> C[Object Based Composition]

    B --> B1[Template Method]
    B --> B2[Interpreter]

    C --> D[Behavioral via Encapsulation]
    C --> E[Behavioral via Distribution]
    C --> F[Behavioral via Notification]

    D --> D1[Strategy]
    D --> D2[State]

    E --> E1[Command]
    E --> E2[Mediator]
    E --> E3[Chain of Responsibility]
    E --> E4[Memento]

    F --> F1[Observer]
    F --> F2[Iterator]
    F --> F3[Visitor]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Behavioral Design Patterns. List any four behavioral patterns from the GoF catalogue with their intent.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Behavioral Design Patterns are concerned with the **algorithms, assignment of responsibilities, and patterns of communication** between objects. They focus on **how classes and objects interact and distribute responsibility**, rather than on object creation or structure.

**Four patterns with intent (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. **Observer** — Defines a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified.
2. **Strategy** — Encapsulates a family of interchangeable algorithms.
3. **Command** — Encapsulates a request as an object, enabling parameterization, queuing, and undo.
4. **State** — Allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between State Pattern and Strategy Pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | State Pattern | Strategy Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Internal state transitions | External client choice |
| **Inter-state Knowledge** | States often know sibling states | Strategies are usually independent |
| **Primary Intent** | Behavior changes with object state | Algorithm is selectable at runtime |
| **Example** | Vending machine states | Payment method selection |

> State encapsulates **behavior based on internal condition**; Strategy encapsulates **a family of algorithms** chosen by the client.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions for KTU ESE 2024:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, has sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**, and the student must answer **one full question** (a + b).

---

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Observer Design Pattern** with a real-world scenario. Draw the **UML class diagram** and explain its participants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a **Java program** to implement the Observer pattern for a stock-price monitoring system where multiple investor clients are notified when a stock's price changes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Real-world scenario (1 mark):**
Consider a **stock ticker** application. A `Stock` (Subject) maintains the current price. Multiple `Investor` objects (Observers) register themselves. When the stock price changes, every registered investor is automatically notified. The stock does not know or care *who* the investors are.

**Participants (3 marks):**

1. **Subject (interface)** — declares `attach()`, `detach()`, `notify()`.
2. **ConcreteSubject (`Stock`)** — maintains the price; calls `notify()` whenever `setPrice()` is invoked.
3. **Observer (interface)** — declares `update(Subject s)`.
4. **ConcreteObserver (`Investor`)** — implements `update()` to react to the new price.

**UML Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```
        <<interface>>              <<interface>>
        ┌──────────────┐          ┌──────────────┐
        │   Subject    │          │   Observer   │
        ├──────────────┤          ├──────────────┤
        │+attach()     │<>───────>│+update()     │
        │+detach()     │ notifies │              │
        │+notify()     │          │              │
        └──────┬───────┘          └──────┬───────┘
               │                         │
        ┌──────┴───────┐          ┌──────┴───────┐
        │     Stock    │          │   Investor   │
        ├──────────────┤          ├──────────────┤
        │-price        │          │-name         │
        │-observers    │          │+update()     │
        │+setPrice()   │          └──────────────┘
        │+getPrice()   │
        └──────────────┘
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

// Observer interface
interface Observer {
    void update(float price);
}

// Subject interface
interface Subject {
    void attach(Observer o);
    void detach(Observer o);
    void notifyObservers();
}

// ConcreteSubject
class Stock implements Subject {
    private String symbol;
    private float price;
    private final List<Observer> investors = new ArrayList<>();

    public Stock(String symbol, float price) {
        this.symbol = symbol; this.price = price;
    }
    public void attach(Observer o) { investors.add(o); }
    public void detach(Observer o) { investors.remove(o); }
    public void notifyObservers() {
        for (Observer o : investors) o.update(price);
    }
    public void setPrice(float newPrice) {
        this.price = newPrice;
        System.out.println("\n[" + symbol + "] Price changed to ₹" + price);
        notifyObservers();
    }
    public String getSymbol() { return symbol; }
}

// ConcreteObserver
class Investor implements Observer {
    private final String name;
    public Investor(String name) { this.name = name; }
    public void update(float price) {
        System.out.println("  -> " + name + " notified: New price = ₹" + price);
    }
}

// Client
public class StockApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Stock reliance = new Stock("RELIANCE", 2500.00f);
        Investor inv1 = new Investor("Alice");
        Investor inv2 = new Investor("Bob");
        reliance.attach(inv1);
        reliance.attach(inv2);
        reliance.setPrice(2520.50f);
        reliance.setPrice(2575.00f);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
[RELIANCE] Price changed to ₹2520.5
  -> Alice notified: New price = ₹2520.5
  -> Bob notified: New price = ₹2520.5

[RELIANCE] Price changed to ₹2575.0
  -> Alice notified: New price = ₹2575.0
  -> Bob notified: New price = ₹2575.0
```

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Strategy Design Pattern** with a real-world scenario. Draw the **UML class diagram** and list its participants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a **Java program** to implement a `Payment` system that uses Strategy pattern to support Credit Card, UPI, and Net Banking payment methods. The method should be selectable at runtime. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Real-world scenario (1 mark):**
An **e-commerce checkout** allows the user to choose among Credit Card, UPI, or Net Banking. The selection happens at runtime. Using the Strategy pattern, we avoid large `if-else` blocks and allow easy addition of new payment options.

**Participants (2 marks):**
- **Strategy** (`PaymentStrategy`) — interface with `pay(int amount)`.
- **ConcreteStrategy** — `CreditCardStrategy`, `UPIStrategy`, `NetBankingStrategy`.
- **Context** — `ShoppingCart` that holds and uses a `PaymentStrategy`.

**UML Class Diagram (4 marks):**

```
   <<interface>>            ┌────────────────────┐
  ┌────────────────┐       │  ShoppingCart      │
  │ PaymentStrategy│       ├────────────────────┤
  ├────────────────┤       │ -strategy :        │
  │ +pay(amount)   │<──◇───│   PaymentStrategy  │
  └────────┬───────┘       │ +setStrategy(s)    │
           │               │ +checkout(amount)  │
           │               └────────────────────┘
           │
   ┌───────┴──────────────────────────┐
   │                                  │
┌──┴───────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────┴───────────┐
│CreditCard    │  │   UPI      │  │ NetBanking     │
│Strategy      │  │ Strategy   │  │ Strategy       │
├──────────────┤  ├────────────┤  ├────────────────┤
│+pay(amount)  │  │+pay(amount)│  │+pay(amount)    │
└──────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────────┘
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

```java
// Strategy interface
interface PaymentStrategy {
    void pay(int amount);
}

// Concrete strategies
class CreditCardStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
    private final String cardNumber;
    public CreditCardStrategy(String cardNumber) { this.cardNumber = cardNumber; }
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Paid ₹" + amount + " using Credit Card ending " 
                           + cardNumber.substring(cardNumber.length() - 4));
    }
}
class UPIStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
    private final String upiId;
    public UPIStrategy(String upiId) { this.upiId = upiId; }
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Paid ₹" + amount + " via UPI ID: " + upiId);
    }
}
class NetBankingStrategy implements PaymentStrategy {
    private final String bankName;
    public NetBankingStrategy(String bankName) { this.bankName = bankName; }
    public void pay(int amount) {
        System.out.println("Paid ₹" + amount + " via Net Banking (" + bankName + ")");
    }
}

// Context
class ShoppingCart {
    private PaymentStrategy strategy;
    public void setStrategy(PaymentStrategy strategy) { this.strategy = strategy; }
    public void checkout(int amount) {
        if (strategy == null) {
            System.out.println("Please select a payment method first.");
            return;
        }
        strategy.pay(amount);
    }
}

// Client
public class ECommerceApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ShoppingCart cart = new ShoppingCart();
        cart.setStrategy(new CreditCardStrategy("4111111111111234"));
        cart.checkout(2500);
        cart.setStrategy(new UPIStrategy("user@oksbi"));
        cart.checkout(800);
        cart.setStrategy(new NetBankingStrategy("HDFC"));
        cart.checkout(1500);
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the `<<interface>>` stereotype** in UML — costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing State and Strategy** — If asked to differentiate, students often just say "both use polymorphism" without giving the *trigger* difference. Always mention **internal vs external** triggers.
> 3. **Drawing only class diagrams** — KTU often awards marks for both **class diagram AND sequence diagram**. Try to include a sequence diagram for at least one pattern.
> 4. **Missing the Context class** in Strategy/State — examiners check whether you understand the *role* of the context.
> 5. **Omitting `notify()` mechanism in Observer** — you must show how the subject iterates through its observer list.
> 6. **Java code without `main()`** — if asked for a runnable program, your code should be complete and compilable.
> 7. **Not stating the OOP principle (OCP/DIP/Hollywood)** the pattern enforces — KTU increasingly tests conceptual mapping between pattern and SOLID principle.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 📌 Core Concepts
- Behavioral patterns deal with **communication and responsibility** between objects.
- GoF catalogue has **11 behavioral patterns** (Chain of Responsibility, Command, Interpreter, Iterator, Mediator, Memento, Observer, State, Strategy, Template Method, Visitor).
- Two main families: **Class-based (inheritance)** and **Object-based (composition)**.

### 📌 Top 5 Exam Patterns

1. **Observer**
   - 1-to-many dependency
   - Subject/Observer interfaces; `attach`, `detach`, `notify`
   - Real-world: GUI listeners, MVC, stock tickers
   - Push vs Pull model

2. **Strategy**
   - Family of interchangeable algorithms
   - Context holds a `Strategy` reference
   - Eliminates `if-else` for algorithm selection
   - Real-world: Payment methods, sorting algorithms

3. **State**
   - Object behavior changes with internal state
   - Each state is a separate class implementing a `State` interface
   - States may transition via `context.setState()`
   - Real-world: ATM, vending machine, TCP connection

4. **Command**
   - Encapsulates request as an object
   - Supports undo, queuing, logging, macro
   - Participants: Command, ConcreteCommand, Invoker, Receiver, Client
   - Real-world: Text editor Undo/Redo, remote controls

5. **Template Method**
   - Defines algorithm skeleton in superclass
   - Subclasses override specific steps
   - Hollywood Principle / Inversion of Control
   - Hook methods for optional extension
   - Real-world: Spring `JdbcTemplate`, `HttpServlet`

### 📌 Critical Comparisons (Most Repeated KTU Questions)
- **State vs Strategy** — internal trigger vs external choice
- **Observer vs Mediator** — one-to-many notification vs central hub
- **Command vs Strategy** — encapsulate *request* vs encapsulate *algorithm*
- **Template Method vs Strategy** — inheritance-based skeleton vs composition-based algorithm

### 📌 UML Drawing Rules (KTU Standard)
- Always use `<<interface>>` for abstract types
- Show multiplicity (e.g., `0..*`, `1`)
- Use correct arrow types (—► association, ◇—► aggregation, ◆—► composition)
- Include method signatures in class boxes

### 📌 OOP Principles at Play
- **OCP (Open/Closed Principle)** — Open to extension, closed to modification
- **DIP (Dependency Inversion Principle)** — Depend on abstractions
- **SRP (Single Responsibility Principle)** — One reason to change
- **Hollywood Principle** — Don't call us, we'll call you (Template Method)
- **Favor composition over inheritance** — applies to most behavioral patterns

### 📌 Quick Memorization Mnemonics
- Patterns in alphabetical order: **C, C, I, I, M, M, O, S, S, T, V** — "Can Cats Inquire Into Many Mysterious Operational State Transitions Vaguely?"
- Observer = **PUB-SUB**
- Strategy = **Pick-and-choose algorithm**
- State = **Mood-based behavior**
- Command = **Action as object**
- Template = **Recipe with optional steps**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coding Guidelines - Code Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coding Guidelines - Code Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Coding Guidelines & Code Review

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Coding Guidelines?

A **Coding Guideline** (also called a **Coding Standard** or **Programming Style Guide**) is a formally documented set of rules, conventions, and best practices that govern how source code is written, formatted, named, commented, and organized within a software project. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, coding guidelines are an *engineering discipline artifact* that ensures uniformity, readability, maintainability, and reduce defect injection during the implementation phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST411, Module 3):**
> *Coding guidelines are a set of rules and recommendations that programmers must follow while writing code. They cover naming conventions, indentation, commenting, file organization, error handling, and language-specific constructs to produce consistent, defect-resistant code.*

Mathematically, we can abstract the goal of coding guidelines as:

$$Q_c = f(R, C, M, D)$$

Where:
- $Q_c$ = Code Quality
- $R$ = Readability
- $C$ = Consistency across the team
- $M$ = Maintainability index
- $D$ = Defect density (lower is better)

### 1.2 What is Code Review?

A **Code Review** is a systematic, quality-assurance activity in which one or more persons (other than the author) examine the source code of a software system to identify bugs, enforce coding standards, find security vulnerabilities, and verify that the implementation matches the design specification. The IEEE 1028 standard formally defines this as a *Software Review* — an evaluation of a software element for discrepancies, errors, and deviations from standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal IEEE 1028 Definition:**
> *A software review is a systematic examination of a software product performed by a team of qualified personnel to evaluate its adequacy and identify discrepancies from specifications, standards, or other criteria.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Editorial Desk

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (for a B.Tech Student's Intuition):**
> 
> Imagine you are writing an article for the college magazine. Before it gets printed, the **editor** reads it line by line. The editor doesn't just check grammar — they check:
> 1. **Style consistency** (Have you used "USA" everywhere, or "U.S.A." in some places and "America" in others?) — this is the **coding guideline** part.
> 2. **Factual errors** (Did you claim Kerala's capital is Chennai by mistake?) — this is the **defect detection** part of code review.
> 3. **Plagiarism** (Did you copy a paragraph from Wikipedia without citation?) — this is the **IP / license compliance** part.
> 4. **Logical flow** (Does your argument make sense, or did paragraph 3 contradict paragraph 1?) — this is the **design-vs-implementation consistency** part.
> 
> **Coding guidelines** are the *rulebook* the editor hands out before you start writing. **Code review** is the *actual reading session* where the editor enforces the rulebook AND catches new problems that the rulebook didn't anticipate.

### 1.4 Why Do KTU 2024 Scheme Students Need Both?

In the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, the following Course Outcomes (COs) directly map to this topic:

| KTU CO | Course Outcome Statement | Mapped to Topic |
|--------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **CO3** | Apply coding standards and conduct systematic code reviews to improve software quality. | Coding Guidelines + Code Review |
| **CO4** | Estimate defect density and review effort using industry-standard metrics. | Code Review Metrics |

### 1.5 Categories of Coding Guidelines (Overview)

For KTU 2024, coding guidelines are categorized into **five core domains**:

1. **Naming Conventions** — how variables, functions, classes, constants are named.
2. **Formatting & Indentation** — whitespace, brace placement, line length.
3. **Commenting & Documentation** — header blocks, inline comments, Javadoc/Doxygen.
4. **Error Handling & Defensive Programming** — try/catch, return codes, assertions.
5. **Language-Specific Best Practices** — language idioms, forbidden constructs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Coding Guideline Document

A well-engineered coding guideline document (as required in the KTU lab assessment of PECST411) contains the following structured sections:

### A. Naming Conventions
- **Variables** → camelCase (e.g., `employeeName`, `totalAmount`)
- **Constants** → UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (e.g., `MAX_RETRY_COUNT = 3`)
- **Classes** → PascalCase (e.g., `CustomerOrder`, `PaymentProcessor`)
- **Functions/Methods** → camelCase with verb prefix (e.g., `calculateTax()`, `getUserById()`)
- **Private members** → underscore prefix in some languages (e.g., `_internalState`)

### B. Indentation & Formatting
- Use **spaces, not tabs** (typically 2 or 4 spaces).
- Maximum line length: **80 or 120 characters**.
- One statement per line.
- Brace style: **K&R style** (opening brace on same line) or **Allman style** (on new line) — pick ONE and apply consistently.

### C. Commenting Rules
- **Header comment** for every file: author, date, purpose, version.
- **Function-level comments**: pre/post-conditions, parameters, return values.
- **Inline comments** only for non-obvious logic — never restate the code.
- Avoid commented-out code in committed source.

### D. Error Handling Rules
- Never silently swallow exceptions.
- Validate all external inputs at the boundary.
- Use `finally` blocks (or language equivalent) for resource cleanup.
- Prefer specific exception types over generic ones.

### E. Language-Specific Best Practices
- Avoid `goto`, `eval()`, deep inheritance.
- Use `const` / `final` for variables that should not be reassigned.
- Prefer composition over inheritance (the "S.O.L.I.D." first letter).

## 2.2 Why Coding Guidelines Matter — The Engineering Rationale

The "**Why**" behind each rule, in KTU-mapped language:

| Rule | Engineering "Why" |
|------|-------------------|
| Consistent naming | A new developer can predict what a variable is without reading the body → reduces **code comprehension time**. |
| Meaningful names | `x` vs `customerAge` — the latter conveys *semantic intent* → reduces bug injection during modification. |
| Short functions (< 50 LOC) | Functions doing one thing have lower **cyclomatic complexity** → fewer paths to test. |
| Mandatory comments | Six months later, the author forgets → comments are a *time-machine* for future maintainers. |
| No magic numbers | `if (x > 3.14159)` vs `if (x > MAX_DISCOUNT_RATE)` → magic numbers are bugs waiting to happen. |

## 2.3 Code Review — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Definitions of Key Code Review Terms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Highlighted Vocabulary (you will see this in exam questions):**

- **Reviewer / Inspector** — The qualified person who examines the code.
- **Author / Producer** — The developer who wrote the code.
- **Defect** — Any deficiency in the code (bug, style violation, security issue, performance anti-pattern).
- **Inspection** — The most formal type of review (per IEEE 1028 / Fagan Inspection).
- **Walkthrough** — A semi-formal, author-led review session.
- **Pass** — One cycle of inspection in a multi-pass Fagan inspection.
- **Inspection Rate** — Lines of code reviewed per hour.
- **Defect Density** — Defects per KLOC (thousand lines of code).

### 2.3.2 Types of Code Review (IEEE 1028 Classification)

| Type | Formality | Led By | Goal | When Used |
|------|-----------|--------|------|-----------|
| **Informal Review** | Low | Peer | Quick feedback | Daily, IDE-based |
| **Walkthrough** | Medium | Author | Knowledge transfer | After module completion |
| **Technical Review** | Medium-High | Technical expert | Verify design conformance | Before integration |
| **Inspection (Fagan)** | Highest | Moderator | Defect removal | Critical modules, regulated domains |
| **Pair Programming** | Continuous | Two developers | Real-time review | Agile / XP teams |

### 2.3.3 The Fagan Inspection Process (Most Likely KTU Exam Topic)

The Fagan Inspection is the **gold standard** of code review and is heavily tested in KTU exams. It has **6 stages**:

1. **Planning** — Moderator selects materials, forms the inspection team, distributes the code.
2. **Overview (Optional)** — Author presents the design intent to the team.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the code alone, marks potential defects.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — Team meets, defects are logged; NO solutions are discussed.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes the defects.
6. **Follow-up** — Moderator verifies all defects were addressed; product is re-inspected if needed.

### 2.3.4 Roles in a Fagan Inspection

| Role | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| **Moderator** | Coordinates, leads the meeting, NOT the author. |
| **Author** | Writes the code, explains intent, performs rework. |
| **Reader** | Walks the team through the code line-by-line. |
| **Recorder** | Logs every defect raised. |
| **Inspectors** | Domain/technical experts who find defects. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Code Review Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme examinable. Memorize the metric names, the formulas, and the units.**

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Meaning |
|---|--------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Inspection Rate (IR)** | $IR = \dfrac{L}{E}$ | LOC/hour | Lines inspected per unit time. |
| 2 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{N_d}{L} \times 1000$ | Defects/KLOC | Defects per thousand lines. |
| 3 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{\text{found}}}{D_{\text{found}} + D_{\text{shipped}}} \times 100\%$ | Percent % | How many defects we caught before release. |
| 4 | **Review Effort (RE)** | $RE = \dfrac{E}{L}$ | Person-hours/KLOC | Effort to review 1 KLOC. |
| 5 | **Code Coverage** | $CC = \dfrac{L_{\text{covered}}}{L_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percent % | Lines exercised by tests. |
| 6 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Integer | Number of independent paths. |
| 7 | **Halstead Volume** | $V = N \times \log_2(\eta)$ | Bits | Program size in information theory. |
| 8 | **Maintainability Index** | $MI = 171 - 5.2 \ln V - 0.23 V(G) - 16.2 \ln LOC$ | 0–100 scale | Higher = more maintainable. |
| 9 | **Average Fix Time** | $AFT = \dfrac{T_{\text{fix}}}{N_d}$ | Hours/defect | Speed of defect resolution. |
| 10 | **Review Yield** | $RY = \dfrac{D_{\text{inspection}}}{L}$ | Defects/KLOC | Defects found per KLOC inspected. |

Where in the formulas above:
- $L$ = Lines of code inspected
- $E$ = Total review effort (in hours)
- $N_d$ = Number of defects found
- $D_{\text{found}}$ = Defects caught before release
- $D_{\text{shipped}}$ = Defects found post-release
- $E$, $N$ = Edges and nodes in the control flow graph
- $P$ = Number of connected components
- $N$ = Halstead program length (operators + operands)
- $\eta$ = Halstead vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Never write `|x|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the column. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Metrics

In industry, these metrics directly feed into:
- **CMMI Level 4/5 process improvement** — required by DOD contracts.
- **ISO/IEC 25010 quality model** — Maintainability sub-characteristics.
- **GitHub / GitLab merge-request analytics** — modern equivalents of Fagan inspections, automatically computing review yield per pull request.
- **Medical device software (FDA 21 CFR Part 820)** — inspections are legally mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Defect Density Calculation

### Problem Setup
A team develops a C++ module containing **2,800 lines of code**. During a Fagan inspection, the inspectors raise **42 issues**, of which **38 are confirmed as defects** (the remaining 4 were false positives raised as questions). After the fix pass, the QA team later discovers **3 additional defects** that escaped inspection.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert lines of code to KLOC.**

$$L_{\text{KLOC}} = \dfrac{L}{1000} = \dfrac{2800}{1000} = 2.8 \text{ KLOC}$$

**Step 2 — Confirm defects found during inspection.**

We count only the **38 confirmed defects**, not the 42 issues. The 4 false positives are recorded as *questions* and excluded from defect count.

$$D_{\text{inspection}} = 38$$

**Step 3 — Identify total defects (caught + escaped).**

$$D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{inspection}} + D_{\text{escaped}} = 38 + 3 = 41$$

**Step 4 — Compute Defect Density.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD &= \dfrac{D_{\text{total}}}{L_{\text{KLOC}}} \\
   &= \dfrac{41}{2.8} \\
   &= 14.64 \text{ defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Defect Removal Efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \dfrac{D_{\text{inspection}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\
    &= \dfrac{38}{41} \times 100\% \\
    &= 92.68\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The team has a Defect Density of **14.64 defects/KLOC** and a **DRE of 92.68%**. Industry benchmarks suggest DRE ≥ 95% is excellent, 85–95% is acceptable. Our team is just below the "good" threshold — they should improve their review checklist to catch the 3 escaped defects (likely the boundary-condition issues that inspection checklists should always include).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe) Walkthrough

### Problem Setup
Given the following pseudocode control-flow snippet:

```
1  function Check(x, y):
2      if (x > 0) AND (y > 0):
3          print("Both positive")
4      else if (x < 0):
5          if (y == 0):
6              print("Neg/Zero")
7          else:
8              print("Neg/NonZero")
9      else:
10         print("Other")
11     return
```

### Step 1 — Identify the predicate (decision) nodes.

The decision points are:
- Line 2: `(x > 0) AND (y > 0)` → 2 conditions → **2 decision nodes**
- Line 4: `x < 0` → **1 decision node**
- Line 5: `y == 0` → **1 decision node**

**Total predicates $P = 4$.**

### Step 2 — Apply the McCabe formula for a single function.

For a single connected component ($P_{\text{components}} = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P + 1 \\
     &= 4 + 1 \\
     &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb:** Cyclomatic complexity $V(G) \leq 10$ is generally considered maintainable. $V(G) > 20$ is untestable and must be refactored. Our value of **5 is healthy**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inspection Rate & Effort Estimation

### Problem Setup
A 5,000-LOC Java module is reviewed by 3 inspectors, each spending 4 hours. The total combined effort is 12 person-hours. During review, they find 25 defects. After release, customers report 2 more defects.

### Step 1 — Inspection Rate.

$$
IR = \dfrac{L}{E} = \dfrac{5000}{12} = 416.67 \text{ LOC/hour}
$$

### Step 2 — Review Effort per KLOC.

$$
RE = \dfrac{E}{L_{\text{KLOC}}} = \dfrac{12}{5} = 2.4 \text{ person-hours/KLOC}
$$

### Step 3 — Review Yield.

$$
RY = \dfrac{D_{\text{inspection}}}{L_{\text{KLOC}}} = \dfrac{25}{5} = 5 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

### Step 4 — DRE.

$$
DRE = \dfrac{25}{25 + 2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{25}{27} \times 100\% = 92.59\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coding Guideline Violation Categories (KTU Table)

For the KTU 2024 lab exam, students often must **categorize** a given code snippet's violations. Use this matrix:

| Violation Category | Symptom in Code | Fix |
|--------------------|-----------------|-----|
| **Magic Number** | `if (age > 18)` | Replace with `if (age > LEGAL_AGE)` |
| **Inconsistent Naming** | `EmpName` vs `emp_name` | Pick ONE standard and stick to it |
| **Long Method** | Function with 200 LOC | Split into helper methods |
| **Deep Nesting** | 6 levels of `if` inside `if` | Use early-return / guard clauses |
| **Dead Code** | Unreachable `return` after a `throw` | Delete it |
| **Missing Null Check** | `customer.getAddress().getCity()` | Add null guards (or use Optional) |
| **Hard-coded Path** | `C:\Users\admin\file.txt` | Use config / env variable |
| **Ignored Exception** | `catch (Exception e) { }` | Log or rethrow |
| **God Class** | Class with 50 methods | Split by Single Responsibility |
| **Commented-out Code** | `// x = 5; // old logic` | Delete; rely on Git history |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Code Review Effectiveness Across Methods

| Method | Defects Found/KLOC | Cost (relative) | Best Use Case |
|--------|-------------------:|:---------------:|---------------|
| Fagan Inspection | **30 – 50** | 5× | Safety-critical (aerospace, medical) |
| Technical Review | **15 – 25** | 3× | Architectural compliance |
| Walkthrough | **8 – 15** | 2× | Knowledge transfer |
| Pair Programming | **10 – 20** | 4× | Agile/XP, complex logic |
| Peer Review (light) | **5 – 10** | 1× | Daily pull-request flow |
| No Review | **0 (in this step)** | 0 | N/A — defects caught in testing, at 10× cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU examiner's favorite):** The earlier a defect is caught, the cheaper it is. A defect caught in **code review costs 1×**, in **unit testing costs 5×**, in **system testing costs 10×**, and in **production it costs 100×** (the well-known *Boehm curve*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fagan Inspection Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Code Module Ready] --> B[Planning: Moderator forms team]
    B --> C[Optional: Overview by Author]
    C --> D[Individual Preparation: Inspectors study code]
    D --> E[Inspection Meeting: Log defects]
    E --> F{Defects Found?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Rework: Author fixes defects]
    G --> H[Follow-up: Moderator verifies fixes]
    H --> I{All Defects Resolved?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Sign-off: Module Approved]
    F -- No --> J
    J --> K[End: Code enters next phase]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The flow has **one decision diamond** at F (Defects Found?) and one at I (All Defects Resolved?).
- The **loop** between G, H, and I represents the *rework cycle* — this loop terminates only when the moderator confirms all defects are addressed.

## 4.2 Code Review Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Author: Write Code] --> B[Self Review vs Coding Guidelines]
    B --> C[Static Analysis Tool: Linter]
    C --> D[Submit Pull Request]
    D --> E[Peer Reviewer: 2+ developers]
    E --> F{Defects Found?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Author: Address Review Comments]
    G --> E
    F -- No --> H[Merge to Main Branch]
    H --> I[Automated CI: Tests + Coverage]
    I --> J[Code Enters QA]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Coding Guidelines to Code Review Checkpoints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU Coding Guideline Categories] --> B[Naming Conventions]
    A --> C[Formatting & Indentation]
    A --> D[Commenting Rules]
    A --> E[Error Handling]
    A --> F[Language Best Practices]
    B --> G[Reviewer checks: Are names meaningful and consistent?]
    C --> H[Reviewer checks: Is indentation uniform?]
    D --> I[Reviewer checks: Are comments present and non-redundant?]
    E --> J[Reviewer checks: Are exceptions handled?]
    F --> K[Reviewer checks: No forbidden constructs?]
    G --> L[Decision: Pass / Fail]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.4 Code Review Metrics Dashboard (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Inputs]
        L[Lines of Code]
        E[Effort in Hours]
        D[Defects Found]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation]
        IR[Inspection Rate = L / E]
        DD[Defect Density = D x 1000 / L]
        DRE[DRE = D_caught / D_total x 100]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS[Outputs]
        Q[Quality Indicator]
        P[Process Improvement Action]
    end
    L --> IR
    E --> IR
    L --> DD
    D --> DD
    D --> DRE
    IR --> Q
    DD --> Q
    DRE --> P
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked to "draw" in the exam, a block-level flow diagram like the above is **fully acceptable** and earns full marks, provided you label each block and show the inputs/outputs. Mermaid-style flowcharts translate well to hand-drawn boxes-and-arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define coding guidelines. List any four important elements that should be included in a coding guideline document.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **coding guideline** is a formally documented set of rules, conventions, and best practices that programmers follow while writing source code to ensure consistency, readability, and maintainability across a software project.

**Four elements (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Naming Conventions** — Rules for naming variables, functions, classes, and constants (e.g., camelCase for variables, PascalCase for classes).
2. **Indentation & Formatting** — Whitespace usage, brace style, and line-length limits.
3. **Commenting & Documentation** — Header comments, function-level documentation, and inline comment rules.
4. **Error Handling** — Rules for exception handling, input validation, and resource cleanup.

*(Valid alternative elements: file organization, language-specific best practices, magic-number restrictions, complexity limits.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a Fagan Inspection? List its six stages in order.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Fagan Inspection** is a formal, systematic, and structured code review technique defined by Michael Fagan in 1976, designed to identify defects in software artifacts through a multi-stage process led by a trained moderator.

**Six stages in order (2 marks — ~0.33 each):**

1. **Planning** — Moderator selects code, forms the inspection team, distributes materials.
2. **Overview** *(optional)* — Author explains design intent to the team.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector reviews the code independently and notes potential defects.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — Team meets, defects are logged by the recorder; **no solutions are discussed**.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes the defects identified.
6. **Follow-up** — Moderator verifies all defects were addressed and signs off.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PECST411, Module 3]**

*(a)* Explain the different types of code reviews as classified by **IEEE 1028**, with their relative formality and use cases. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A 4,500-LOC Python module undergoes Fagan inspection by 4 inspectors, each spending 5 hours. They find 32 defects. After release, 3 additional defects are reported by customers. Calculate the **Inspection Rate**, **Defect Density**, and **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**. Interpret whether the DRE is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Types of Code Review (7 marks — 1 mark per type + 2 marks for comparison table)

The **IEEE 1028 standard** classifies software reviews into five types, ordered from most informal to most formal:

1. **Informal Review (1 mark):**
   - A peer-to-peer, ad-hoc check with no documented process.
   - Used daily; examples: over-the-shoulder code review, IDE-based "inspect" tools.
   - Lowest cost, lowest formality, catches obvious bugs.

2. **Walkthrough (1 mark):**
   - Author-led session where the developer explains the code logic to peers.
   - Goal: knowledge transfer + defect discovery.
   - Medium formality, useful for new team members.

3. **Technical Review (1 mark):**
   - Led by a technical expert (not the author), focuses on technical correctness, design conformance, and adherence to standards.
   - Used before integration or before merging into a release branch.

4. **Inspection / Fagan Inspection (1 mark):**
   - The most formal review type, with defined roles (moderator, reader, recorder, inspectors).
   - Uses checklists, entry/exit criteria, and a multi-stage process.
   - Mandatory in safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical, defense).

5. **Pair Programming (1 mark, alternative accepted):**
   - Continuous real-time review where two developers write code together (XP/Agile).
   - One writes (driver), one reviews (navigator); roles rotate frequently.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Type | Formality | Goal | Cost | Use Case |
|------|-----------|------|------|----------|
| Informal | Low | Quick feedback | Minimal | Daily PR reviews |
| Walkthrough | Medium | Knowledge transfer | Low | Onboarding new modules |
| Technical | Medium-High | Standards compliance | Medium | Pre-integration |
| Inspection | Highest | Defect removal | High | Safety-critical |
| Pair Programming | Continuous | Real-time review | High | Agile teams |

---

#### (b) Metric Calculation (7 marks — 1 for setup, 2 each for the three metrics, 2 for interpretation)

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
- $L = 4500$ LOC
- $N_{\text{inspectors}} = 4$
- $t = 5$ hours each
- $D_{\text{inspection}} = 32$
- $D_{\text{escaped}} = 3$

**Step 2 — Total effort and KLOC (1 mark):**

$$E = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ person-hours}$$

$$L_{\text{KLOC}} = \dfrac{4500}{1000} = 4.5 \text{ KLOC}$$

**Step 3 — Inspection Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= \dfrac{L}{E} = \dfrac{4500}{20} \\
   &= 225 \text{ LOC/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Defect Density (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{total}} &= 32 + 3 = 35 \\
DD &= \dfrac{35}{4.5} = 7.78 \text{ defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — DRE (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \dfrac{32}{32 + 3} \times 100\% \\
    &= \dfrac{32}{35} \times 100\% \\
    &= 91.43\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (within the 7 marks):**
Industry benchmarks:
- DRE ≥ 95% → **excellent**
- 85% ≤ DRE < 95% → **acceptable but improvable**
- DRE < 85% → **poor — process needs intervention**

Our value of **91.43%** falls in the *acceptable* band. The team should improve its **review checklist** to specifically cover boundary conditions and error-handling paths to push DRE above 95%.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative for Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECST411]**

*(a)* List and explain the **roles** defined in a Fagan Inspection. Why is the moderator required to be different from the author? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* For a software module, the following metrics are observed: **Halstead Volume $V = 4500$**, **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G) = 12$**, **Lines of Code $= 1500$**. Calculate the **Maintainability Index (MI)** and interpret whether the module needs refactoring. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Roles in a Fagan Inspection (7 marks)

**Five roles (1.2 marks each):**

1. **Moderator:**
   - Coordinates the entire inspection process.
   - Selects the team, schedules meetings, ensures the process is followed.
   - Decides when the product passes/fails inspection.
   - **Must NOT be the author** — to maintain objectivity. The author is emotionally attached to the code and may unconsciously defend it or skip thorough checking.

2. **Author / Producer:**
   - The developer who wrote the code.
   - Explains the design intent during the optional Overview.
   - Performs the **Rework** stage by fixing the defects found.

3. **Reader:**
   - Walks the inspection team through the code line-by-line during the Inspection Meeting.
   - Often a different person from the author to provide a fresh perspective.

4. **Recorder / Scribe:**
   - Logs every defect, question, and decision raised during the meeting.
   - Ensures nothing is lost in oral discussion.
   - Produces the **inspection log** used during the Follow-up stage.

5. **Inspectors / Reviewers:**
   - Domain experts and technical specialists.
   - Individually prepare by studying the code and listing potential defects.
   - Raise defects during the Inspection Meeting.

**Why moderator ≠ author (1 mark bonus/clarification):**
Objectivity is the cornerstone of inspection. A moderator who is also the author cannot neutrally assess the code's quality. Splitting the role ensures a fresh, unbiased review.

---

#### (b) Maintainability Index Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall the formula (1 mark):**

$$MI = 171 - 5.2 \ln V - 0.23 V(G) - 16.2 \ln LOC$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**
- $V = 4500$
- $V(G) = 12$
- $LOC = 1500$

**Step 3 — Compute natural logs (2 marks):**

$$
\ln 4500 = 8.4118
$$

$$
\ln 1500 = 7.3132
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term (1 mark):**

$$
5.2 \times 8.4118 = 43.7414
$$

$$
0.23 \times 12 = 2.76
$$

$$
16.2 \times 7.3132 = 118.4743
$$

**Step 5 — Final MI (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MI &= 171 - 43.7414 - 2.76 - 118.4743 \\
   &= 171 - 164.9757 \\
   &= 6.0243
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintainability Index Scale:**
> - 85–100: Highly maintainable
> - 65–85: Moderately maintainable
> - **Below 65: Difficult to maintain — refactor recommended**
> 
> Our value of **MI ≈ 6** is extremely low. The module is **almost impossible to maintain** and **must be refactored** or rewritten. Typical causes: too much complexity, too large a volume of code, poor modularization.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DRE with Defect Density (DD).** DRE is a *percentage*; DD is *defects per KLOC*. Writing 92 instead of 92% in DRE is a 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert LOC to KLOC.** Defect density = defects per *thousand* lines. Using raw LOC gives a numerically tiny answer that will be marked wrong.
> 
> 3. **Stating the inspection meeting "discusses solutions."** WRONG. The **inspection meeting only logs defects**. Solutions are addressed in the **Rework** stage by the author. This is a classic 1–2 mark trap question.
> 
> 4. **Confusing cyclomatic complexity formula.** It is $V(G) = P + 1$ for a single function or $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ for a graph. Using the wrong one loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Not writing units in metric answers.** Always write "LOC/hour", "defects/KLOC", "person-hours/KLOC". Missing units = partial mark loss.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to interpret the numerical result.** Calculation alone is not enough — KTU 2024 Scheme requires you to **state what the value means** for the project (acceptable / needs improvement / refactor).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Coding Guidelines** = *the rulebook*; **Code Review** = *the inspection* that enforces the rulebook.
- ✅ Five core coding-guideline categories: **Naming, Formatting, Commenting, Error Handling, Language Best Practices**.
- ✅ **Fagan Inspection** has exactly **6 stages** (Planning → Overview → Individual Prep → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up).
- ✅ In the Inspection Meeting, **only defects are logged** — never solutions.
- ✅ **Five IEEE 1028 review types:** Informal, Walkthrough, Technical Review, Inspection, Pair Programming.
- ✅ **Five roles in Fagan Inspection:** Moderator, Author, Reader, Recorder, Inspectors. **Moderator ≠ Author.**
- ✅ **Defect Density** = $D_{\text{total}} / KLOC$.
- ✅ **DRE** = $D_{\text{caught}} / D_{\text{total}} \times 100\%$. ≥ 95% = excellent; 85–95% = acceptable; < 85% = poor.
- ✅ **Inspection Rate** = $L / E$ in LOC/hour.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = P + 1$. Target: $V(G) \leq 10$.
- ✅ **Maintainability Index** $MI = 171 - 5.2 \ln V - 0.23 V(G) - 16.2 \ln LOC$. Below 65 → refactor.
- ✅ **Always** convert LOC to KLOC, **always** state units, **always** interpret the result.
- ✅ **Earlier defect detection = exponentially cheaper fix** (Boehm cost-curve).
- ✅ Static analysis tools (linters like ESLint, Pylint, Checkstyle) **automate** the coding-guideline check portion of a review.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Code Walkthrough — Formal Definition
A **Code Walkthrough** is an informal, semi-structured group code review technique in which the author of the code (or a designated presenter) guides a small team of peers through the source code line-by-line. The objective is to discover errors, clarify algorithm logic, identify deviations from coding standards, and exchange programming knowledge among team members. Walkthroughs are typically led by the author and emphasize learning, knowledge transfer, and consensus building rather than formal fault-detection metrics.

### 1.2 Code Inspection — Formal Definition
A **Code Inspection** is a formal, systematic, and highly structured static analysis technique originally proposed by Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976 (commonly known as **Fagan Inspection**). It is a methodical examination of the source code by trained participants using a well-defined process, strict roles, checklists, and explicit entry/exit criteria. The primary objective is defect detection (not correction) and producing measurable quality metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Walkthrough and Inspection are classified under **Static Verification & Validation (V&V) Techniques** in Module 3. Both are part of *Software Reviews*, but they differ sharply in formality, structure, and statistical rigor.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two Editors:**
> 
> - **Code Walkthrough ≈ A book club reading session.** A small group of friends gathers, and the *author* reads aloud their own story chapter. Everyone listens, comments freely, gives suggestions, and learns together. The atmosphere is relaxed, conversational, and focused on understanding. The author is also the presenter.
> 
> - **Code Inspection ≈ A formal newspaper editorial review board.** Editors follow a strict checklist, each has a defined role (proofreader, fact-checker, language editor), defects are logged on a tracking sheet, and a moderator enforces the rules. The author does **not** present — a separate *reader* walks the team through the manuscript. The goal is purely to find and log defects.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Thresholds
- **Inspection Rate (Michael Fagan's Empirical Standard):** Roughly **100–150 Lines of Code (LOC) per hour** is considered the optimal sustainable inspection rate.
- **Defect Density Goal:** Industry-target post-inspection defect density is typically **≤ 0.5 defects per KLOC** for mature inspection processes.
- **Inspection Effectiveness (E):** 

$$E = \frac{\text{Defects Found During Inspection}}{\text{Total Defects in the Module (Found in All Phases)}} \times 100\%$$

A well-run Fagan inspection is empirically cited as finding **60%–90%** of all defects in a single pass, often outperforming unit testing alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Code Walkthrough — Operational Breakdown

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Informal / Semi-structured static review |
| **Conductor** | The **author** of the code (also acts as the presenter) |
| **Participants** | 2–5 peers; cross-functional mix encouraged (designers, testers, coders) |
| **Preparation** | Attendees pre-read the code; no formal checklists required |
| **Focus** | Algorithm logic, design intent, alternative approaches, learning |
| **Output** | Informal notes; no formal defect log; can generate rework items |
| **Tone** | Conversational, collaborative, low psychological friction |

#### Step-by-Step Process of a Walkthrough
1. **Pre-Distribution:** Author distributes the source listing and design documentation to participants **at least 2 days before** the session.
2. **Individual Review:** Each participant reads the code independently, jotting down questions and observations.
3. **The Session (typically 1–2 hours):** The author walks the group through the code, explaining logic, decisions, and tricky constructs.
4. **Discussion & Brainstorming:** Participants raise questions, suggest improvements, and propose alternative algorithms.
5. **Action Items:** The author captures agreed-upon issues to be addressed after the meeting.

#### Why and How Walkthroughs Work
- The **author-as-presenter** model triggers self-explanation, which itself reveals hidden assumptions.
- The **small group** setting leverages *N-version reading* — multiple cognitive paths reduce the chance of an error surviving.
- Low formality encourages **early and frequent** application, especially on critical or complex modules.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Walkthroughs are best for **early lifecycle** code and for **knowledge transfer across teams**, not for achieving statistically auditable quality metrics.

### 2.2 Code Inspection (Fagan Inspection) — Operational Breakdown

Michael Fagan's landmark 1976 IBM paper defined a **6-stage** process with strict role separation, the cornerstone of formal verification in classical software engineering.

#### The Six Stages of Code Inspection

1. **Planning** — The **moderator** organizes the inspection: forms the team, distributes the code and supporting documents, sets the meeting time, and confirms *entry criteria* (code compiles, conforms to standards, has been desk-checked).
2. **Overview** (Optional) — The **author** presents the design intent and algorithm approach so inspectors understand the *intent*, not just the code.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the code independently (typically 90–120 LOC per hour), using **checklists** tailored to the language and module type. This is where most defects are actually found.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — The **reader** (paraphrasing the code logic) walks the team through it, the **recorder** logs every defect on a **Defect Log Sheet**, and the **moderator** enforces discipline (no debate on solutions, focus on identification only).
5. **Rework** — The **author** fixes all logged defects off-line.
6. **Follow-up** — The **moderator** verifies that all rework was completed correctly and that *exit criteria* (re-inspection of changed code, updated defect logs) are satisfied.

#### The Three Core Roles (Most Critical for KTU)

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Facilitates, enforces process, controls pace, decides re-inspection. *Not the author.* |
| **Reader** | Paraphrases the code in plain language during the meeting. *Not the author.* |
| **Recorder** | Logs every defect verbosely on the official Defect Log. *Not the author.* |
| **Author** | Answers questions, explains intent, performs rework afterward. *Cannot be moderator/reader/recorder.* |
| **Inspector** | Domain/standards expert who finds defects in individual preparation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why role separation matters:** Fagan's empirical insight was that when the author *also* moderates or presents, ego and familiarity biases cause defects to be explained away. Forcing the author to *listen* rather than *talk* dramatically increases defect yield.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Definition / Use |
|---|---|
| **Inspection Rate** $IR = \frac{LOC_{\text{reviewed}}}{T_{\text{hours}}}$ | Optimal range **100–150 LOC/hour** (Fagan) |
| **Defect Density** $DD = \frac{D_{\text{found}}}{KLOC}$ | Target $\leq 0.5$ per KLOC post-process |
| **Inspection Effectiveness** $E = \frac{D_{\text{inspection}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Typical range **60%–90%** |
| **Effort (person-hours)** $E_{total} = n_{\text{inspectors}} \times T_{\text{prep}} + T_{\text{meeting}}$ | Used to compute ROI of inspection |
| **Yield** $Y = \frac{D_{\text{found}}}{T_{\text{inspection-effort}}}$ | Defects found per person-hour |
| **Cost of Defect Removal** | **Cheaper in inspection than in testing**; cheapest in requirements review |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World
- Used at **NASA, Microsoft, IBM, and Bosch** as a mandatory gate before unit test.
- Significantly reduces **field-defect escape rate**, especially for safety-critical systems (avionics, medical devices, automotive — ISO 26262 mandates formal reviews).
- Combined with **Cleanroom Software Engineering**, inspection alone can push reliability above **0.999**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Inspection Effort & Defect Discovery

> **Scenario (Worked Analytical Example for KTU Board Exam):**
> A team of **4 inspectors** examines a module of **600 LOC**. Each inspector spends **3 hours** in individual preparation. The inspection meeting lasts **2 hours**. During the meeting and preparation, **18 unique defects** are found. Subsequent unit testing finds **4 more** defects that escaped inspection.
> Compute: *(a)* Total inspection effort, *(b)* Inspection Rate, *(c)* Defect Density, *(d)* Inspection Effectiveness.

---

#### Step 1 — Total Inspection Effort $E_{total}$

The total effort combines all inspectors' preparation plus the meeting time.

$$E_{total} = (n_{\text{inspectors}} \times T_{\text{prep}}) + T_{\text{meeting}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Since *all 4 inspectors* prepare in parallel, we multiply, then add the joint meeting effort (attended by all 4 once).

$$E_{total} = (4 \times 3) + 2$$

$$E_{total} = 12 + 2 = 14 \text{ person-hours}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Inspection Rate $IR$

The rate at which the team processed code.

$$IR = \frac{LOC_{\text{reviewed}}}{T_{\text{prep}} \times n_{\text{inspectors}}}$$

**Conversion logic:** We measure rate against total *preparation* effort, not meeting time, because preparation is where the actual reading happens.

$$IR = \frac{600}{4 \times 3} = \frac{600}{12} = 50 \, LOC/\text{person-hour}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A rate of **50 LOC/hr** is *lower* than the optimal Fagan band of 100–150. This implies the code is **dense, complex, or unfamiliar** — the team is being appropriately careful.

---

#### Step 3 — Defect Density $DD$

How many defects per thousand lines of code were found during inspection.

$$DD = \frac{D_{\text{found}}}{KLOC} = \frac{18}{0.6}$$

**Conversion logic:** $600 \, LOC = 0.6 \, KLOC$, so we divide defects by KLOC to get a normalized rate.

$$DD = 30 \text{ defects/KLOC}$$

This is a *high* defect density, indicating the module was either written hastily or represents complex domain logic.

---

#### Step 4 — Inspection Effectiveness $E$

The fraction of *total* defects (inspection + downstream) caught by inspection.

$$E = \frac{D_{\text{inspection}}}{D_{\text{inspection}} + D_{\text{escaped}}} \times 100\%$$

**Conversion logic:** Total defects in the module = 18 (caught) + 4 (escaped to test) = 22. The inspection caught 18 of them.

$$E = \frac{18}{18 + 4} \times 100\%$$

$$E = \frac{18}{22} \times 100\% \approx 81.82\%$$

This sits **inside the expected Fagan range of 60%–90%**, confirming the process is well-tuned.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Walkthrough vs. Inspection

| Dimension | **Code Walkthrough** | **Code Inspection (Fagan)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Informal / Semi-structured | Highly Formal |
| **Leader** | Author | Moderator (≠ Author) |
| **Number of Participants** | 2–5 | 3–6 (typical 4) |
| **Preparation Time** | Light | Heavy (90–120 LOC/hr) |
| **Use of Checklists** | Optional | Mandatory |
| **Defect Logging** | Informal notes | Formal Defect Log Sheet |
| **Entry/Exit Criteria** | Loose | Strict & enforced |
| **Primary Goal** | Learning + defect discovery | Defect discovery + metrics |
| **Statistical Rigor** | None | High (Fagan metrics) |
| **Best Suited For** | Early code, training, exploration | Production code, regulated domains |
| **Psychological Safety** | High | Moderate (process-driven) |
| **Tone of Meeting** | Conversational | Disciplined, time-boxed |
| **Cost of Rework** | Low | Higher (but ROI positive) |
| **Etymology** | IEEE Std 1028-2008 "Walkthrough" | Fagan 1976, IBM |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If a 14-mark question asks for "differences between walkthrough and inspection," the *role separation (author ≠ moderator)* and the *use of formal checklists + defect logs* are the two highest-scoring points to make.

---

### 3.3 Cost–Benefit Justification (Why Inspection Pays Off)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boehm's Rule (widely tested in industry):** The cost of fixing a defect grows roughly **10×** with each lifecycle stage (requirements → design → code → test → production). An inspection finding a requirements defect at *1×* saves a *100×* post-deployment cost.

$$C_{\text{avoided}} = C_{\text{fix-at-stage}} \times 10^{(n_{\text{stages-skipped}})}$$

If a defect caught in code inspection would have cost **₹10,000** to fix post-release, and inspection cost **₹1,000** to find it, the **ROI is 10:1** — a standard reported industry figure for mature inspection programs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Fagan Inspection Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Planning] --> B[Stage 2: Overview optional]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Individual Preparation with Checklists]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Inspection Meeting Reader Recorder Moderator]
    D --> E{Defects Found?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Stage 5: Rework by Author]
    F --> G[Stage 6: Follow-up Verification by Moderator]
    E -- No --> G
    G --> H[Exit Achieved Re-inspect if Major Changes]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Role Interaction Map in Fagan Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph before[Before Meeting]
        M1[Moderator] -->|distributes code| I1[Inspector 1]
        M1 -->|distributes code| I2[Inspector 2]
        M1 -->|distributes code| R1[Reader]
        M1 -->|distributes code| RC1[Recorder]
        M1 -->|sets entry criteria| A1[Author]
    end

    subgraph meeting[During Meeting]
        R1 -->|paraphrases code| ALL[All Participants]
        ALL -->|raise issues| RC1
        RC1 -->|logs defects| LOG[Defect Log Sheet]
        M1 -->|enforces process| ALL
    end

    subgraph after[After Meeting]
        LOG -->|worklist| A1
        A1 -->|rework| A1
        A1 -->|submits fix| M1
        M1 -->|verifies| OUT[Follow-up Sign-off]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Walkthrough Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Author Prepares Code and Design Docs] --> start2[Distribute 2 Days Before]
    start2 --> start3[Participants Read Independently]
    start3 --> start4[Author Walks Through Code Line by Line]
    start4 --> start5[Group Asks Questions and Brainstorms]
    start5 --> start6[Author Captures Action Items]
    start6 --> start7[Author Performs Rework and Learning]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Where Reviews Fit in the V-Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REQ[Requirements Specification] --> RR[Requirements Review Informal Walkthrough]
    RR --> DES[System Design]
    DES --> DR[Design Inspection Fagan Style]
    DR --> CD[Detailed Code]
    CD --> CI[Code Inspection Fagan]
    CI --> UT[Unit Testing Dynamic]
    UT --> IT[Integration Testing]
    IT --> ST[System Testing]
    ST --> AT[Acceptance Testing]
    CI -.feeds metrics.-> QM[Quality Metrics Repository]
    DR -.feeds metrics.-> QM
    RR -.feeds metrics.-> QM
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently use the V-Model to ask "At which stage would you apply inspection vs. walkthrough?" — the safe answer is **inspection at code & design**, **walkthrough at requirements & exploratory review**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Code Inspection. Who is the Moderator and why is the Author prohibited from acting as Moderator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Code Inspection is a formal, systematic static analysis technique introduced by **Michael Fagan (1976)** in which a trained team examines source code against predefined checklists to identify defects, logging them on a formal defect sheet. The **Moderator** is the team leader who plans the inspection, distributes materials, controls the pace of the meeting, enforces discipline, and performs follow-up verification. The **Author is prohibited from being the Moderator** because role separation eliminates author bias — when the author leads, they tend to *justify* rather than *expose* defects, drastically reducing detection effectiveness (Fagan's empirical studies show effectiveness dropping from ~85% to below 50% without role separation).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any four differences between Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each, any four):**
1. **Formality:** Walkthrough is informal; Inspection is highly formal with strict process.
2. **Leader:** Walkthrough is led by the **Author**; Inspection is led by a **Moderator** (≠ Author).
3. **Checklists:** Walkthroughs use no formal checklist; Inspections mandate **language/standard-specific checklists**.
4. **Defect Logging:** Walkthroughs produce informal notes; Inspections produce a formal **Defect Log Sheet** with measurable metrics.
5. **Statistical Output:** Walkthroughs yield no metrics; Inspections yield **defect density, inspection rate, effectiveness**.

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **not** confuse "walkthrough" with "informal test execution" — it is a **static review**, not a test.
> - Always mention **Michael Fagan / Fagan Inspection** for full credit in inspection questions.
> - Skipping the **role table** (Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Author) loses at least **3 marks** in any 14-mark inspection question.
> - In comparison questions, structure your answer as a **table** for easy tick-marking by the examiner.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **six stages of Fagan's Code Inspection process** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Discuss the **roles and responsibilities** of participants in a Fagan inspection. Why is role separation important? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Stages of Fagan Inspection (7 marks):**
1. **Planning** (1 mark) — The Moderator selects the inspection team (typically 4 members), distributes the source code (≤ 200 LOC for first inspection), design documents, and any relevant standards. Entry criteria are verified: code must compile, conform to coding standards, and have been desk-checked.
2. **Overview** (0.5 mark) — The Author presents the design intent, algorithm approach, and data structures. This is optional and is skipped for experienced teams or routine modules.
3. **Individual Preparation** (1.5 marks) — Each inspector studies the code independently at a rate of **100–150 LOC/hour**, using checklists (e.g., "uninitialized variables," "off-by-one errors," "language-specific pitfalls"). This is the stage where the **majority of defects are discovered**.
4. **Inspection Meeting** (1.5 marks) — A time-boxed (typically 2 hours) session. The **Reader** paraphrases the code, inspectors raise issues, the **Recorder** logs each defect verbosely (location, type, severity) on the Defect Log. The Moderator enforces *no solution debate* — only identification.
5. **Rework** (1 mark) — The Author fixes all logged defects off-line. For major logical changes, the moderator may mandate a **re-inspection** of modified sections.
6. **Follow-up** (1.5 marks) — The Moderator verifies that all rework is complete and correct, ensures the Defect Log is updated, and confirms that exit criteria are met. A re-inspection is scheduled if rework exceeded threshold (typically >5% LOC change).

**(b) Roles and Responsibilities (7 marks):**

| Role | Responsibility | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Plans inspection, distributes code, leads meeting, enforces rules, verifies rework, decides re-inspection | 2 |
| **Reader** | Paraphrases code logic in plain language during meeting, never explains, only reads | 1 |
| **Recorder** | Logs every defect (location, description, severity) on the Defect Log Sheet | 1 |
| **Author** | Provides overview, answers clarifying questions, performs all rework after meeting | 1 |
| **Inspectors** | Domain experts who prepare individually using checklists and raise issues in meeting | 1 |
| **Why role separation?** (1 mark) | Fagan's empirical data shows separating author from moderator/reader eliminates "explainer bias" and increases defect detection from ~50% to **~85%** of total defects. It also enables measurable, repeatable quality metrics independent of personality. | 1 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**
*(a)* What is a **Code Walkthrough**? Describe its **process, participants, and outcomes** in detail. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Compare and contrast **Code Walkthrough vs. Code Inspection** along **eight** distinct parameters. State one scenario where each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Code Walkthrough (7 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An informal, semi-structured group code review where the Author presents the code to a small group of peers to expose defects, clarify logic, and share knowledge.
- **Process (3 marks):**
  1. Author pre-distributes code ≥ 2 days before the session.
  2. Participants review independently, marking questions.
  3. Author walks the team through the code line-by-line, explaining intent and logic.
  4. Group raises questions, suggests alternative algorithms, identifies defects.
  5. Author captures action items; no formal defect log required.
  6. Author performs rework and shares learning with team.
- **Participants (2 marks):** Author (presenter), 2–5 peers (mix of designers, coders, testers), occasionally a scribe for notes. **No formal moderator.**
- **Outcomes (1 mark):** Defects discovered, alternative design ideas, knowledge transfer across the team, shared ownership of code.

**(b) Comparison (7 marks — 0.75 mark per row for 8 rows = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for the two scenarios):**

| Parameter | Walkthrough | Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| Formality | Informal | Formal |
| Leader | Author | Moderator (≠ Author) |
| Checklists | Not mandatory | Mandatory |
| Defect log | Informal notes | Formal log sheet |
| Preparation | Light | Heavy (100–150 LOC/hr) |
| Goal | Learning + defects | Defects + metrics |
| Tone | Conversational | Disciplined |
| Best for | Early / exploratory code | Production / regulated code |

**Appropriate Scenarios (1 mark):**
- **Walkthrough best for:** A junior team exploring a brand-new algorithm for the first time — focus is on shared understanding.
- **Inspection best for:** A safety-critical module in a medical device firmware (IEC 62304 / ISO 26262) — focus is on auditable defect removal and metric reporting.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **Code Walkthrough** is **informal**, led by the **Author**, focuses on **learning and clarification**, no formal checklist or defect log.
- [x] **Code Inspection (Fagan)** is **formal**, led by a **Moderator ≠ Author**, uses **checklists** and produces a **Defect Log**.
- [x] Always credit **Michael Fagan (1976, IBM)** when defining inspection.
- [x] The **6 stages** are: Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up.
- [x] **5 core roles:** Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Author, Inspectors.
- [x] **Inspection Rate** target: **100–150 LOC/hour** (Fagan).
- [x] **Inspection Effectiveness:** typically **60%–90%** of all defects.
- [x] **Defect Density target:** $\leq 0.5$ defects/KLOC for mature processes.
- [x] **Entry Criteria:** code compiles, conforms to standards, desk-checked.
- [x] **Exit Criteria:** all defects addressed, rework verified, defect log updated.
- [x] Walkthroughs are best at **requirements/early design**; Inspections are best at **detailed design and code**.
- [x] **Boehm's 10× rule:** fixing a defect 2 stages later costs ~10× more.
- [x] Inspection ROI: industry-standard **10:1** (₹1 spent on inspection saves ₹10 in rework).
- [x] The **Author can NEVER be the Moderator, Reader, or Recorder** — role separation is non-negotiable.
- [x] Inspection is a **static verification** technique — do **not** confuse it with testing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Debugging and its Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Debugging and its Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Code Debugging** is the systematic, controlled process of locating, analyzing, and removing **defects (bugs)** from a software artifact. According to the IEEE Standard 729-1983 (Glossary of Software Engineering Terminology), debugging is the process of *determining the exact nature and location of the suspected fault in a computer program and fixing it*.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education), debugging is treated as a **Verification & Validation (V&V)** sub-activity that directly supports the maintenance phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

### 1.1.1 Standardized Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Precise Definition |
|---|---|
| **Error** | A human mistake made during any phase of software development. |
| **Defect / Bug / Fault** | The manifestation of an error captured in software artifacts (requirements, design, code). |
| **Failure** | The inability of a software system to perform its required function under specified conditions. |
| **Debugging** | The process of finding the **root cause** of a defect and removing it. |
| **Fault Localization** | A sub-task of debugging that narrows down the location of the fault within a code base. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):** *Testing reveals failures, but debugging finds the faults that caused them.* A test case may pass (no failure) even if the code contains latent defects that have not yet been triggered.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you wake up and the bathroom tap is **leaking a single drop every second**. You call a plumber:

1. The plumber **observes** the symptom (dripping water on the floor).
2. He **hypothesizes** possible causes: worn washer, cracked pipe, loose nut, high water pressure.
3. He **isolates** the most likely cause by testing one hypothesis at a time.
4. He **fixes** the root cause and **verifies** the drip has stopped.

**Software debugging is exactly this workflow**, but applied to source code. The leaking tap = the failing test case. The plumber = the developer. The root cause (e.g., worn washer) = the actual faulty line of code.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Students: "OH-IS-FV"** — **O**bserve, **H**ypothesize, **I**solate, **S**olve, **F**ix, **V**erify. This is the universal debugging loop used by professional engineers.

## 1.3 Why Debugging is a Critical Engineering Skill

> [!WARNING]
> Industry data (IBM Systems Sciences Institute, NIST reports) consistently indicates that **debugging and related corrective maintenance consume 50%–80%** of total software development lifetime cost. KTU examiners often frame a 14-mark question around this economic reality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Debugging Process (IEEE Standard Workflow)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates familiarity with the **systematic debugging cycle**. The following six steps form the canonical procedure:

1. **Reproduce the Failure** — Establish a deterministic, repeatable test case that triggers the failure. Without reproducibility, debugging becomes guesswork.
2. **Localize the Fault** — Narrow down the *module*, *function*, and eventually the *line* responsible.
3. **Analyze the Root Cause** — Determine *why* that line produces wrong behavior (logic flaw, off-by-one, type mismatch, race condition).
4. **Apply a Fix** — Modify the code, configuration, or data to eliminate the root cause.
