  * Major flaws in the *control hierarchy* are exposed before significant work is done on utilities.

**4. Steps to Execute Top-Down:**
1. Unit-test the main module.
2. Replace all direct sub-modules with stubs.
3. Integrate one stub at a time (depth-first or breadth-first).
4. After each integration, run **regression tests** on already-integrated paths.
5. Remove stubs as real modules become unit-tested.

---

### (b) Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration Testing

**1. Definition:**
The Sandwich strategy integrates the system **simultaneously from the top down and from the bottom up**, converging at a predefined *target layer* (usually the business logic or middle-tier).

**2. Diagram (Recreated):**

```
TOP-DOWN  ───►  [Top Layers]   ◄─── stubs injected downward
                    │
              ┌─────┴─────┐
              │  TARGET    │  ◄─── Test this layer first
              │  (Middle)  │
              └─────┬─────┘
                    │
BOTTOM-UP ───►  [Bottom Layers] ◄─── drivers injected upward
```

**3. Workflow:**
1. The top team begins integrating from the *UI/controllers* using stubs.
2. The bottom team begins integrating from the *database/utilities* using drivers.
3. Both teams meet at the *middle layer* (e.g., business logic).
4. The middle layer is then integrated and tested comprehensively.
5. Regression is run continuously at every convergence point.

**4. Real-World Scenario — Three-Tier Web Application:**

In a typical *React/Angular frontend + Spring Boot REST API + PostgreSQL database* architecture:
* **Top-Down layer:** UI components → Mocked API responses.
* **Bottom-Up layer:** Database queries + DAO methods → Driven by JUnit test harnesses.
* **Target middle layer:** REST controllers and service beans — these are integrated once both teams converge.
* This is the *most efficient* approach because the top and bottom teams can work **in parallel**, drastically reducing project calendar time.

**5. Advantages:**
* Parallel development → faster delivery.
* Best of both worlds — early architectural validation *and* early utility validation.
* Suits large, distributed, geographically-separated teams.

**6. Disadvantages:**
* Higher cost (both stubs *and* drivers must be built).
* The *target middle layer* must be very stable — if it changes, both teams are impacted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Big-Bang** integration testing approach. List three of its major drawbacks and suggest an alternative strategy that mitigates each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Smoke Testing** and **Regression Testing**. Explain the relationship between them and integration testing. With a suitable example, describe how a CI/CD pipeline performs these tests automatically. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### (a) Big-Bang Integration — Drawbacks & Alternatives

**Definition:**
In the *Big-Bang* approach, all (or most) individually unit-tested modules are integrated *simultaneously* in a single step, and the entire combined system is tested as one monolithic unit.

**Major Drawbacks & Alternatives:**

| Drawback | Description | Alternative Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Late Defect Detection** | Interface bugs are only found *after* full assembly; debugging is expensive. | Use **Incremental (Top-Down or Bottom-Up)** integration — faults surface as soon as a new module is added. |
| **2. Difficulty in Fault Isolation** | When the whole system fails, isolating *which* module caused the failure is nearly impossible. | Use **Bottom-Up with cluster testing** — small clusters are tested independently before merging. |
| **3. Critical Modules Tested Too Late** | High-risk modules (e.g., payment processing) are only validated at the very end, risking project deadline overruns. | Use **Risk-Based Incremental Integration** — integrate the highest-risk modules first regardless of position in the call tree. |
| **4. No Working System Until the End** | Stakeholders have no demonstrable product until all modules are integrated. | Use **Sandwich Integration** — the target middle layer can be demoed early. |

**Conclusion:**
Big-Bang is **only recommended for very small systems** (≤ 5 modules) where the cost of scaffolding exceeds the benefit of incremental testing. For any production-grade project, *incremental* is the KTU-accepted best practice.

---

### (b) Smoke, Regression, and CI/CD Automation

**1. Smoke Testing:**
A *shallow, wide-coverage* test suite that verifies the **critical paths** of an application work end-to-end. It answers the question: *"Does the build run at all?"* — analogous to turning on a new machine and checking for smoke.

**2. Regression Testing:**
A *re-execution of a representative subset* of previously passed tests to ensure that new changes (new modules, bug fixes, refactoring) have not broken existing functionality.

**3. Relationship with Integration Testing:**
* After **every** integration step, **regression** is run to ensure no existing integration has been broken.
* Before detailed integration begins, **smoke** is run on the daily build to confirm the build is *stable enough* to be integrated further.
* Together, smoke and regression form the **safety net** of integration testing.

**4. Example — CI/CD Pipeline (Jenkins):**

Consider a Node.js + MongoDB e-commerce application.

| Stage | Action | Test Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Developer pushes code to GitHub | Trigger webhook | — |
| 2. Jenkins pulls code | `git pull` | — |
| 3. Compile / Build | `npm install && npm run build` | — |
| 4. Run Unit Tests | `npm test` | Unit |
| 5. Start MongoDB container | `docker run -d mongo` | Setup |
| 6. Deploy app to test server | `npm start` | — |
| 7. **Run Smoke Suite** | Hit `/`, `/login`, `/products` endpoints | **Smoke** |
| 8. **Run Integration Suite** | Test DB connectivity, API contracts | **Integration** |
| 9. **Run Regression Suite** | Re-execute the last 5000 test cases | **Regression** |
| 10. Report pass/fail | Notify Slack channel | — |

If smoke fails, the pipeline halts immediately — no deeper tests are run. If integration passes but regression fails, the change is *rejected* and the developer is notified.

**Conclusion:**
In modern KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations, the expectation is that students understand that **integration testing is no longer a manual, late-stage activity** — it is an **automated, continuous, and feedback-driven** practice embedded in CI/CD pipelines.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Integration Testing** tests the *interactions* between modules, not the modules themselves.
- **Top-Down** uses **stubs**; **Bottom-Up** uses **drivers**; **Sandwich** uses both.
- **Big-Bang** integrates everything at once — KTU 2024 generally marks it as a *poor* strategy for large systems.
- **Incremental** integration is the industry standard (and KTU's preferred answer).
- **Stubs** simulate *callees*; **Drivers** simulate *callers*.
- **Regression Testing** = re-running old tests after every new integration.
- **Smoke Testing** = shallow wide-coverage build-validity test (run *before* integration).
- **Continuous Integration (CI)** = automated build + test on every commit (Jenkins, GitHub Actions).
- **Three Tiers of Integration:** Low-Order (small clusters) → Medium-Order (subsystems) → High-Order (full system).
- **Defect Density (IDD)** and **DRE** are key numerical metrics KTU may ask.
- Always mention **advantages AND disadvantages** in long answers — KTU marking scheme explicitly allocates marks for both.
- In a hybrid architecture (UI + API + DB), always recommend **Sandwich Integration**.
- For a hierarchical architecture with a clear main controller, recommend **Top-Down**.
- For a utility-heavy system (OS, firmware), recommend **Bottom-Up**.
- **Regression is not optional** — every integration step is incomplete without it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Testing and its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Testing and its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — System Testing and its Types

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Testing** is the level of software testing where a **complete, integrated system** is tested as a whole. It is performed on the **entire system** in the context of either functional requirements (Functional Testing) or non-functional requirements (Performance, Security, Usability). System testing verifies that the system complies with its specified requirements, business processes, and end-user expectations.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board)** and IEEE Standard 829 adopted by KTU's Software Engineering framework, system testing is a **black-box testing** technique performed on a fully integrated product, usually after integration testing and before acceptance testing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Final Inspection" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy**: Imagine an automotive company that has manufactured every part of a car — the engine, brakes, gear system, electronics, AC, and chassis have all been built and individually tested (Unit Testing). Then the parts are connected together and tested as sub-assemblies (Integration Testing). Finally, the **whole car** is taken to a test track to test **braking on wet roads, hill-start assist, fuel efficiency, crash-worthiness, AC cooling, dashboard indicator correctness, etc.** That final, holistic track test of the complete car is exactly what **System Testing** is for a software product.

The key intuition:
- **Unit Test** = testing a single spark plug.
- **Integration Test** = testing whether the spark plug works inside the engine.
- **System Test** = testing whether the **whole car** drives safely from point A to point B under varied conditions.

### 1.3 Position of System Testing in the Testing Pyramid

System testing sits at the **second-highest level** in the standard software testing hierarchy (after acceptance testing and before integration testing). It is typically conducted by an **independent testing team** rather than the developer, to maintain objectivity.

### 1.4 Why is System Testing Mandatory in KTU Context?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs)** for Software Engineering explicitly require students to:
> - **CO3**: *Apply appropriate testing strategies and techniques to ensure software quality.*
> - **CO4**: *Design test cases and execute various levels of testing including unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing.*

Therefore, an in-depth understanding of system testing is **frequently assessed** in both University (ESE) and Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) components.

### 1.5 Key Stakeholders in System Testing

| Stakeholder | Role in System Testing |
|-------------|------------------------|
| Independent Test Team | Designs and executes test cases |
| Project Manager | Approves test plan and resources |
| System Architect | Provides design specifications |
| Configuration Manager | Manages test environment versions |
| End User Representative | Provides UAT and feedback |
| Quality Assurance Lead | Validates compliance with standards |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Goal of System Testing

The primary goals of system testing can be summarized as:

1. **Verify compliance** with functional, business, and technical requirements.
2. **Validate** the system in the operational environment.
3. **Reduce risks** associated with real-world deployment.
4. **Verify non-functional attributes** (performance, security, usability, reliability).
5. **Establish confidence** in the system's quality before user acceptance.

## 2.2 Major Types of System Testing

System testing is broadly classified into two categories:

### A. Functional Testing Types
Functional testing verifies that each function of the software operates as specified in the requirements document.

### B. Non-Functional Testing Types
Non-functional testing verifies how well the system performs under various conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Both **Functional** and **Non-Functional** testing types are assessable, with non-functional types (performance, security, usability) typically carrying **higher marks (10–14 marks)** in Part B.

## 2.3 Comprehensive List of System Testing Types

The following table presents the **complete taxonomy** of system testing types expected by KTU examiners:

### I. Functional Testing Sub-Types

| # | Testing Type | Purpose & Description |
|---|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Functional Testing** | Verifies each function works as per SRS (Software Requirement Specification). |
| 2 | **Smoke Testing** | A preliminary test to check the **critical functionalities** of a build before detailed testing. Also called *Build Verification Testing*. |
| 3 | **Sanity Testing** | Narrow regression test focused on one or a few areas of functionality after minor changes. |
| 4 | **Regression Testing** | Re-execution of selected test cases to ensure that **modifications (bug fixes or new features) do not break existing functionality**. |
| 5 | **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | Final validation by the business client/end-users. Two sub-forms: **Alpha Testing** (in-house) and **Beta Testing** (at customer site). |
| 6 | **Recovery Testing** | Verifies the system's ability to **recover from crashes, hardware failures, or network drops**. |
| 7 | **Configuration Testing** | Tests software behavior under different hardware/software configurations (OS, browser, DB). |

### II. Non-Functional Testing Sub-Types

| # | Testing Type | Purpose & Description |
|---|--------------|----------------------|
| 8 | **Performance Testing** | Determines the system's responsiveness, throughput, and stability under a workload. |
| 9 | **Load Testing** | Tests system behavior under **expected normal and peak load** conditions. |
| 10 | **Stress Testing** | Tests system behavior under **extreme or beyond-capacity** load (negative testing). |
| 11 | **Volume Testing** | Tests the system with **large volumes of data** (also called flood testing). |
| 12 | **Scalability Testing** | Measures the system's ability to **scale up/out** when load increases. |
| 13 | **Security Testing** | Ensures system is protected from **unauthorized access, SQL injection, XSS, malware, data breaches**. |
| 14 | **Usability Testing** | Validates the **ease of use, learnability, and user-friendliness** of the UI. |
| 15 | **Compatibility Testing** | Verifies compatibility across different OS, browsers, devices, and networks. |
| 16 | **Installation Testing** | Validates the **installation, upgrade, and uninstallation** procedures. |
| 17 | **Reliability Testing** | Checks the system's ability to perform **failure-free operations** for a specified time in a specified environment. |
| 18 | **Compliance Testing** | Verifies adherence to **regulatory standards** (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS). |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **frequently asked in KTU University Exam Part A questions (3 marks)** and must be memorized.

### 2.4.1 Test Coverage Metrics

Let $S_{exe}$ be the number of statements executed, $S_{tot}$ be the total statements, $B_{exe}$ be the executed branches, and $B_{tot}$ be the total branches.

$$
\text{Statement Coverage (SC)} = \frac{S_{exe}}{S_{tot}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Branch Coverage (BC)} = \frac{B_{exe}}{B_{tot}} \times 100\%
$$

### 2.4.2 Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric)

Let $G$ be the control flow graph, $E$ be the number of edges, $N$ be the number of nodes, and $P$ be the number of connected components (usually $P = 1$).

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

An alternative form using predicate nodes:

$$
V(G) = P + 1
$$

where $P$ is the count of **predicate nodes** (decision points) in the flow graph.

### 2.4.3 Defect Density

Let $D$ be the number of defects found, and $Size$ be the code size in KLOC (thousand lines of code).

$$
\text{Defect Density (DD)} = \frac{D}{Size \;\; (\text{KLOC})}
$$

> **Industry Standard**: A defect density below **1 per KLOC** is considered excellent; **>5 per KLOC** indicates poor quality.

### 2.4.4 Test Effectiveness Ratio

Let $D_t$ be defects found in testing, $D_p$ be defects found post-release, and $D_{tot}$ be total defects.

$$
\text{Test Effectiveness (TE)} = \frac{D_t}{D_t + D_p} \times 100\%
$$

### 2.4.5 Mean Time To Failure (Reliability Metric)

Let $\sum T_i$ be the total operational time and $N_f$ be the number of failures observed.

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\sum T_i}{N_f}
$$

### 2.4.6 Halstead Software Science Metrics

Let $\eta_1$ be the number of distinct operators, $\eta_2$ be the number of distinct operands, $N_1$ be the total operators, and $N_2$ be the total operands.

$$
\text{Program Vocabulary} = \eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2
$$

$$
\text{Program Length} = N = N_1 + N_2
$$

$$
\text{Estimated Length} = \hat{N} = \eta_1 \log_2 \eta_1 + \eta_2 \log_2 \eta_2
$$

## 2.5 Formula Cheat Sheet (Compact Table)

| Metric | Formula | Typical Target Value |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| Statement Coverage | $\frac{S_{exe}}{S_{tot}} \times 100$ | **≥ 80%** |
| Branch Coverage | $\frac{B_{exe}}{B_{tot}} \times 100$ | **≥ 75%** |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | **≤ 10 per module** |
| Defect Density | $\frac{D}{\text{KLOC}}$ | **≤ 1** |
| Test Effectiveness | $\frac{D_t}{D_t + D_p} \times 100$ | **≥ 95%** |
| MTTF | $\frac{\sum T_i}{N_f}$ | Higher is better |
| Path Coverage | $\frac{P_{exe}}{P_{tot}} \times 100$ | **≥ 60%** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

System testing metrics are extensively used in the following engineering fields:

- **Aerospace**: NASA uses MTTF and reliability testing to certify flight-control software.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262)**: Defect density thresholds for functional safety compliance.
- **Medical Devices (FDA)**: Cyclomatic complexity limits for embedded medical software.
- **Banking & Finance**: Security and performance testing for high-frequency trading platforms.
- **E-Commerce**: Load and stress testing for sale-day traffic spikes (e.g., Amazon's Big Billion Days).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity of a Sample Program

### Problem
Given the following pseudo-code, draw the control flow graph (CFG) and compute the cyclomatic complexity using all three methods.

```
1. START
2. INPUT a, b
3. IF a > b THEN
4.      result = a - b
5. ELSE
6.      result = b - a
7. ENDIF
8. IF result > 10 THEN
9.      PRINT "Large Difference"
10. ELSE
11.     PRINT "Small Difference"
12. ENDIF
13. STOP
```

### Step 1: Identify Nodes

Each statement/branch becomes a node:

- Node 1: START
- Node 2: IF (a > b)
- Node 3: result = a - b
- Node 4: result = b - a
- Node 5: Join node (after first IF-ELSE)
- Node 6: IF (result > 10)
- Node 7: PRINT "Large Difference"
- Node 8: PRINT "Small Difference"
- Node 9: STOP

Therefore, total nodes:

$$
N = 9
$$

### Step 2: Identify Edges

- 1 → 2
- 2 → 3 (TRUE branch)
- 2 → 4 (FALSE branch)
- 3 → 5
- 4 → 5
- 5 → 6
- 6 → 7 (TRUE)
- 6 → 8 (FALSE)
- 7 → 9
- 8 → 9

Therefore, total edges:

$$
E = 10
$$

### Step 3: Count Predicate Nodes

The IF statements at lines 3 and 8 each contribute one predicate node.

$$
P = 2
$$

Number of connected components (the graph is connected, so):

$$
\pi = 1
$$

### Step 4: Method 1 — Using Edge-Node Formula

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2\pi
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V(G) = 10 - 9 + 2(1) = 3
$$

### Step 5: Method 2 — Using Predicate Nodes

$$
V(G) = P + 1
$$

Substituting:

$$
V(G) = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

### Step 6: Method 3 — Using Regions (Closed Areas + 1)

The CFG contains **2 closed regions** (loops formed by the IF-ELSE structures) plus the **1 outer region**.

$$
V(G) = R + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

### Result and Interpretation

All three methods yield **V(G) = 3**, confirming correctness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: The module requires at least **3 independent test paths** for **100% path coverage**. Since 3 ≤ 10, the module complexity is acceptable.

### Independent Test Paths

$$
\text{Path 1: } 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7 \to 9
$$

$$
\text{Path 2: } 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 6 \to 8 \to 9
$$

$$
\text{Path 3: } 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7 \to 9
$$

(Note: A 4th path $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 8 \to 9$ exists but is dependent on the first three.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Defect Density Calculation

### Problem
A software project is **45,000 lines of code**. During system testing, the QA team reported **27 defects**. Compute the defect density and interpret the result against industry benchmarks.

### Step 1: Identify Given Values

$$
D = 27 \quad \text{defects}
$$

$$
\text{Size} = 45{,}000 \quad \text{LOC}
$$

### Step 2: Convert LOC to KLOC

$$
\text{KLOC} = \frac{45{,}000}{1{,}000} = 45
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Defect Density Formula

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{D}{\text{KLOC}}
$$

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{27}{45}
$$

$$
\text{DD} = 0.6 \;\; \text{defects/KLOC}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation Against Industry Standards

| Defect Density (DD) | Quality Classification |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| **DD ≤ 1** | Excellent (Industry-leading quality) |
| 1 < DD ≤ 3 | Good |
| 3 < DD ≤ 5 | Average |
| **DD > 5** | Poor (Re-engineering recommended) |

> **Verdict**: DD = **0.6** → **Excellent Quality**, well below the threshold of 1.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Statement and Branch Coverage

### Problem
A Java module has **120 total statements** and **30 branches**. A test suite executed **96 statements** and **24 branches**. Compute the statement coverage and branch coverage.

### Step 1: Statement Coverage Calculation

$$
\text{SC} = \frac{S_{exe}}{S_{tot}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{SC} = \frac{96}{120} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

### Step 2: Branch Coverage Calculation

$$
\text{BC} = \frac{B_{exe}}{B_{tot}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{BC} = \frac{24}{30} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

Both metrics meet the typical **KTU/Industry benchmark of ≥ 80%**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Test Effectiveness Ratio

### Problem
During system testing, **85 defects** were identified. After release, the customer reported **5 more defects**. Compute the Test Effectiveness Ratio.

### Step 1: Identify Values

$$
D_t = 85, \quad D_p = 5
$$

$$
D_{tot} = D_t + D_p = 85 + 5 = 90
$$

### Step 2: Apply Test Effectiveness Formula

$$
\text{TE} = \frac{D_t}{D_{tot}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{TE} = \frac{85}{90} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{TE} = 94.44\%
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> A Test Effectiveness of **94.44%** is considered **Good**, but the target in safety-critical systems (avionics, medical) is **≥ 99%**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — MTTF Calculation for Reliability Testing

### Problem
A web server is tested continuously for **720 hours** and experiences **9 failures**. Compute the MTTF.

### Step 1: Identify Values

$$
\sum T_i = 720 \quad \text{hours}, \quad N_f = 9
$$

### Step 2: Apply MTTF Formula

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\sum T_i}{N_f}
$$

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{720}{9} = 80 \quad \text{hours/failure}
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

> The system, on average, runs **80 hours without a failure** — a moderate reliability metric suitable for an internal business application but below the **≥ 1000 hours** benchmark for mission-critical systems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Testing Type

| Trigger Condition | Recommended System Test |
|-------------------|-------------------------|
| New feature added | **Regression Testing** |
| Customer demands speed | **Performance / Load Testing** |
| Handles sensitive data | **Security Testing** |
| Multi-OS / cross-browser | **Compatibility Testing** |
| Users complain about UI | **Usability Testing** |
| First build received from dev | **Smoke Testing** |
| Minor bug fix applied | **Sanity Testing** |
| Migrated to new DB | **Recovery + Volume Testing** |
| Pre-release to client | **User Acceptance Testing** |
| App deployed to many users | **Scalability + Stress Testing** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — System Testing Position in the V-Model

The **V-Model** (Verification & Validation Model) is the standard reference framework used by KTU examiners when describing testing levels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef phaseStyle fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef testStyle fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef coreStyle fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff

    req["User Requirements"]:::phaseStyle
    sys["System Requirements"]:::phaseStyle
    arch["High-Level Design"]:::phaseStyle
    mod["Module Design"]:::phaseStyle
    code["Implementation Coding"]:::phaseStyle

    utest["Unit Testing"]:::testStyle
    itest["Integration Testing"]:::testStyle
    stest["SYSTEM TESTING"]:::coreStyle
    atest["Acceptance Testing"]:::testStyle

    req --> sys --> arch --> mod --> code
    utest --> itest --> stest --> atest
    mod -.-> utest
    arch -.-> itest
    sys -.-> stest
    req -.-> atest
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Each development phase on the left has a corresponding test phase on the right. **System Testing** maps directly to the **System Requirements** specification.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Verification and Validation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Definition | Confirms that the **product is built right** (conforms to specs). | Confirms that the **right product is built** (meets user needs). |
| Question Asked | *Are we building the product correctly?* | *Are we building the correct product?* |
| Type | **Static** (no code execution). | **Dynamic** (code is executed). |
| Methods | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections. | System testing, UAT. |
| Stage | Early in SDLC. | Late in SDLC. |

> **Memory Trick (KTU Examiner Tip)**: **Verification = Specification-driven. Validation = User-driven.**

---

### Q2. Define System Testing. List any FOUR types of non-functional system testing. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition**: System testing is the level of testing that evaluates the **complete, integrated software system** against the specified requirements. It is a **black-box testing** method conducted in an environment that closely mirrors production.

**Four Non-Functional Types**:
1. **Performance Testing** — verifies responsiveness and stability under workload.
2. **Security Testing** — detects vulnerabilities and unauthorized access loopholes.
3. **Usability Testing** — evaluates ease-of-use from the end-user's perspective.
4. **Compatibility Testing** — checks behavior across OS, browsers, and devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> - Students commonly **confuse Regression Testing with Re-testing** — Re-testing is for the *same* bug after fix; Regression is for *other* features that may have broken.
> - **Load vs Stress**: Many students mix these up. Load = expected peak. Stress = beyond peak.
> - **Smoke vs Sanity**: Smoke tests the *entire system critically*; Sanity tests *only the changed area*.
> - Always **draw a diagram** (V-Model or taxonomy tree) — KTU examiners award **+2 bonus marks** for diagrams.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full System Testing Types

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

#### Q3 (a) Explain the different types of functional system testing in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

Functional testing validates the software against the **functional requirements** specified in the Software Requirements Specification (SRS). The major types are:

**1. Functional Testing**
It tests each function of the software against the SRS. Inputs are provided, outputs are compared, and actual behavior is validated against expected behavior. Example: Verifying that a *login form with valid credentials logs in successfully*.

**2. Smoke Testing**
Also called *Build Verification Testing*. When a new build is received, a small set of critical test cases are executed to confirm that the build is stable enough for further detailed testing. If smoke fails, the build is **rejected** and sent back to the dev team.

**3. Sanity Testing**
A narrow regression test executed after receiving a software build with **minor changes or bug fixes**. It checks only the specific functionality impacted by the change, not the whole system.

**4. Regression Testing**
Re-execution of selected test cases on a modified program to ensure that **no new defects have been introduced** into previously working functionality. Strategies include:
- **Re-test all** — re-execute every test case (expensive).
- **Selective regression** — choose only relevant test cases.
- **Prioritization** — order test cases by failure likelihood/criticality.

**5. Recovery Testing**
Verifies the system's ability to **recover from crashes** (e.g., power failure, network drop, hardware failure) and resume operations within an acceptable timeframe. Example: Disconnecting network mid-transaction and verifying that the transaction is **rolled back** correctly.

**6. User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**
Performed by the **end user or client** to validate the system against business requirements.
- **Alpha Testing**: Conducted at the developer's site, in a controlled environment, with developers observing.
- **Beta Testing**: Conducted at the customer's site with real users in the real environment; no developers present.

**7. Configuration Testing**
Tests software behavior under various hardware/software configurations: different OS (Windows, Linux, macOS), browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge), database versions, etc.

---

#### Q3 (b) Explain the various non-functional system testing types with real-world examples. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

Non-functional testing checks **how well** the system performs, rather than what it does.

**1. Performance Testing**
Measures response time, throughput, and resource utilization under workload. Sub-types:
- **Load Testing**: Simulates **expected peak user load** (e.g., 5000 concurrent users on Flipkart during Big Billion Days).
- **Stress Testing**: Pushes load **beyond capacity** to determine the breakpoint (e.g., 50,000 concurrent users to find failure point).
- **Volume Testing**: Floods the system with **huge data volumes** (e.g., inserting 10 million records into a database).
- **Scalability Testing**: Determines if the system can **scale up** (add resources) or **scale out** (add servers) gracefully.

**2. Security Testing**
Identifies vulnerabilities such as **SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), CSRF, weak authentication, data leakage**. Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Nessus.

**3. Usability Testing**
Evaluates **ease of learning, efficiency of use, memorability, error-handling**, and user satisfaction. Conducted with representative end-users.

**4. Compatibility Testing**
Verifies software behavior across **different OS, browsers, devices, screen resolutions, and network speeds**.

**5. Installation Testing**
Validates the **smooth installation, upgrade, and uninstallation** processes. Critical for desktop and mobile apps.

**6. Reliability Testing**
Measures the system's ability to perform **failure-free operations** for a specified period. The **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** is a key metric.

**7. Compliance Testing**
Ensures adherence to **regulatory standards** like GDPR (data privacy), HIPAA (healthcare), PCI-DSS (payment).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Q4 (a) What is Regression Testing? Explain its strategies and challenges in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Definition**:
> Regression Testing is the re-execution of selected test cases to ensure that **modifications (bug fixes, enhancements, configuration changes)** have not introduced new defects or broken previously working features.

**When is Regression Testing Performed?**

| Trigger Event | Example |
|---------------|---------|
| Bug fix | Developer fixes login crash. |
| New feature added | Cart feature added to e-commerce site. |
| Code optimization | Refactoring of payment module. |
| Environment change | Migration from MySQL 5.7 to MySQL 8.0. |

**Strategies of Regression Testing**:

1. **Re-test All Strategy**: Re-execute the entire test suite after every change.
   - *Pros*: Maximum coverage.
   - *Cons*: Expensive, time-consuming, requires full automation.

2. **Selective Regression Testing**: Choose only those test cases that **cover the affected modules**.
   - Tools: Use **code coverage analysis** to identify impacted areas.

3. **Test Case Prioritization**: Rank test cases by:
   - Code coverage priority.
   - Fault-detection history.
   - Customer-reported severity.

4. **Hybrid Strategy**: Combine selective + prioritized.

**Challenges**:
- Test suite becomes **too large** to re-execute manually.
- High **maintenance overhead** for test cases.
- **Time constraints** in tight release cycles.
- Difficulty in identifying **true affected area**.

---

#### Q4 (b) Compare Load, Stress, and Volume Testing with appropriate diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing | Volume Testing |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Goal** | Verify performance under expected load | Test behavior at extremes | Test with huge data volume |
| **Load Applied** | Normal and peak | Beyond capacity | Massive data sets |
| **Type** | Quantitative | Negative testing | Database scaling |
| **Metrics** | Response time, throughput | Breakpoint, recovery | Memory, DB queries/sec |
| **Example** | 10,000 users on IRCTC | 100,000 users on IRCTC | 1 TB of data inserted |
| **Tool** | JMeter, LoadRunner | JMeter, Gatling | BigData tools, SQL scripts |

**Comparison Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef box fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    L["LOAD TEST"]:::box
    S["STRESS TEST"]:::box
    V["VOLUME TEST"]:::box

    L --> L1["Normal peak users"]:::box
    S --> S1["Beyond capacity"]:::box
    V --> V1["Huge data flood"]:::box

    L1 --> R1["Check response time and throughput"]:::box
    S1 --> R2["Find failure point and recovery"]:::box
    V1 --> R3["Find memory leak, DB slowdown"]:::box
```

**Conclusion**: All three are **performance testing sub-types**, but each addresses a different "dimension" — Load = users, Stress = extremes, Volume = data.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Exam Points — Memorize These**:

- **System Testing** is the **third level** of testing (after Unit and Integration, before Acceptance).
- **System Testing is a Black-Box testing technique** — no internal code knowledge required.
- **Smoke Testing** = *Build Verification Testing* — rejects unstable builds fast.
- **Sanity Testing** = *Narrow regression after minor change* — focuses on affected area only.
- **Regression Testing** = *Re-execution after modification* — ensures no new defects.
- **Re-Testing** ≠ **Regression Testing** — Re-testing is for the **same bug** after fix; Regression is for **other features** that may have broken.
- **Alpha Testing** = at developer's site, developer present, controlled environment.
- **Beta Testing** = at customer's site, no developer, real users, real environment.
- **Load Testing** = normal/peak user load.
- **Stress Testing** = beyond capacity (negative testing).
- **Volume Testing** = large volume of data.
- **Security Testing** = finds vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10 awareness).
- **Usability Testing** = user-friendliness check.
- **Recovery Testing** = system's ability to recover after crash.
- **Installation Testing** = tests install / upgrade / uninstall procedures.
- **Compatibility Testing** = cross-OS / cross-browser verification.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G) = E - N + 2P$** — minimum number of test paths.
- **Defect Density DD = $D$/KLOC** — industry target ≤ 1.
- **Test Effectiveness TE = $D_t / (D_t + D_p) \times 100$** — target ≥ 95%.
- **Statement Coverage = $S_{exe}/S_{tot} \times 100$** — target ≥ 80%.
- **Branch Coverage = $B_{exe}/B_{tot} \times 100$** — target ≥ 75%.
- **MTTF = $\sum T_i / N_f$** — higher is better.
- **V-Model**: System Testing corresponds to the **System Requirements** phase.
- **Independent Test Team** performs system testing (not the developer) for objectivity.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy**:
> 1. Always **draw the V-Model** while explaining any testing level — gives +1 to +2 marks.
> 2. **Tabulate comparisons** (Load vs Stress vs Volume, Alpha vs Beta, Smoke vs Sanity) — examiners love clarity.
> 3. Mention **ISTQB** and **IEEE 829** standards once in long answers — adds academic depth.
> 4. Always end Part B questions with a **conclusion line** like *"Hence, system testing is critical for ensuring compliance with functional and non-functional requirements before UAT."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing and White Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Black Box Testing and White Box Testing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Software Testing?

> [!NOTE]
> **Software Testing (KTU 2024 Definition):** Software testing is a systematic, planned activity employed to evaluate and verify that a software system meets its specified requirements, detects defects, identifies gaps between expected and actual behavior, and ensures the product is fit for use under the **V-Model of Verification & Validation (V&V)**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, testing is positioned as a *Verification & Validation (V&V)* activity within the broader **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**. Testing techniques are broadly classified as:

- **Dynamic Testing** (executed on running code) — includes Black-Box and White-Box.
- **Static Testing** (reviews, walkthroughs, inspections) — not executed.

This module focuses on the two pillars of **dynamic functional & structural testing**.

---

### 1.2 Black-Box Testing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black-Box Testing (Behavioural / Functional / Specification-Based Testing):** A test design technique in which test cases are derived from the **functional specification / requirements document** *without* any reference to the internal structure, design, or implementation of the program. The tester is concerned **only with inputs and outputs**, treating the program as a closed black box whose internal logic is invisible.

**Key Properties of Black-Box Testing (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Driver of test design:** External specifications (SRS, use cases, user stories).
- **Tester knowledge required:** Domain expertise, requirement traceability.
- **Independent of:** Programming language, internal code paths, control flow.
- **Used to find:** Missing functions, incorrect outputs, interface errors, data-access errors, performance anomalies, initialization/termination faults.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Mystery Restaurant

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (For First-Time Learners):**
> Imagine a customer ordering food at a restaurant. The customer (tester) hands over a *menu* (specification) and a *list of orders* (test cases). The kitchen (program) prepares the dish. The customer **does not see the chef's recipe, the cooking utensils, or the oven temperature** — they only check whether the dish delivered is the one ordered, well-presented, at the right temperature, and tasty.
>
> - If the wrong dish arrives → **Black-Box Failure (Functional Defect)**.
> - The customer can never tell *why* it failed inside the kitchen — they only report *what* they observed.
>
> This is exactly how **Black-Box Testing** works. Testers have a *specification* (menu) and they *exercise inputs* (orders) to verify *outputs* (food). The internal logic of the system remains opaque.

---

### 1.4 White-Box Testing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **White-Box Testing (Structural / Glass-Box / Open-Box Testing):** A test design technique in which test cases are derived from the **internal structure, control flow, data flow, and code logic** of the program. The tester has *full visibility* into the source code and uses this knowledge to design tests that exercise specific internal paths, branches, and conditions.

**Key Properties of White-Box Testing (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Driver of test design:** Source code, design documents, flowgraphs.
- **Tester knowledge required:** Programming, control structures, code architecture.
- **Independent of:** External specifications.
- **Used to find:** Unreachable code, unused variables, infinite loops, faulty logic, missing branches, dead code, uninitialized variables.

---

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The Open-Engine Mechanic

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (For First-Time Learners):**
> Take the same restaurant, but now you are a **mechanic** inspecting the *kitchen machinery*. You open the oven door, check the burners, the gas pipeline, the thermostat, and the electrical wiring. You are not eating the food — you are verifying that the *machinery* functions as designed.
>
> - If the gas line is broken → **White-Box Failure (Structural Defect)**, even if the food came out correct.
> - The mechanic *must* know how the equipment is built to test it.
>
> This mirrors **White-Box Testing**: the tester inspects the *internal mechanics* (code structure) of the system.

---

### 1.6 The Two Testing Paradigms — A Side-by-Side Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics used in KTU Examination:**

| **Attribute** | **Black-Box Testing** | **White-Box Testing** |
|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Synonyms** | Functional, Behavioural, Closed-Box | Structural, Glass-Box, Open-Box |
| **Knowledge of Internals** | **None** | **Complete** |
| **Basis of Test Cases** | Requirements, SRS, Use Cases | Source Code, Control Flowgraph |
| **Performed By** | Independent Testers, End Users | Developers, White-Box Testers |
| **Phase in SDLC** | System Testing, Acceptance Testing | Unit Testing, Integration Testing |
| **Coverage Criteria** | Equivalence, Boundary, Decision Table | Statement, Branch, Condition, Path |
| **Automation Tools** | Selenium, QTP, JMeter | JUnit, NUnit, CodeCover, Emma |
| **Cost Metric** | Lower for design, Higher for execution | Higher for design, Lower for execution |

---

### 1.7 KTU 2024 Syllabus Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 of PECST411 (Software Engineering):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly maps the following concepts to this module:
>
> - **Black-Box Techniques:** Equivalence Class Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, Decision Table Testing, Cause-Effect Graphing, Error Guessing.
> - **White-Box Techniques:** Statement Coverage, Branch / Decision Coverage, Condition Coverage, Multiple Condition Coverage, Cyclomatic Complexity, Basis Path Testing, Control Structure Testing.
> - **Comparison & Application Context:** When to apply which technique in the **V-Model**.

This module is **directly tested** in both **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** descriptive exams and **End Semester Examination (ESE)** as 14-mark analytical questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Black-Box Testing Techniques — Exhaustive Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A black-box technique that divides the input domain of the program into **equivalence classes** (or partitions) of data, from which a *small but representative* set of test cases can be derived. The underlying assumption is that if one test in a class passes, all members of that class will pass (and vice versa).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Identify input conditions** from the specification (e.g., "age must be between 18 and 60").
2. **Partition each condition** into:
   - **Valid Equivalence Class (VEC):** Set of inputs that are *valid* per specification.
   - **Invalid Equivalence Classes (IECs):** Sets of inputs that are *invalid* (one per invalid scenario).
3. **Select one representative value** from each class.
4. **Form a test case** using that representative value.

**Numerical Example (KTU-Frequent):**
> *"A program accepts an integer representing exam marks from 0 to 100. Design test cases using ECP."*
> - Valid EC: $0 \le \text{marks} \le 100$ → one representative test, e.g., `marks = 65`.
> - Invalid EC #1: $\text{marks} < 0$ → e.g., `marks = -5`.
> - Invalid EC #2: $\text{marks} > 100$ → e.g., `marks = 150`.

So **3 test cases** are derived from potentially infinite inputs.

---

### 2.1.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A black-box technique that focuses on the **boundaries of equivalence classes** because defects are most frequently observed at the *edges* of input ranges. It tests the values *at, just above, and just below* each boundary.

**Standard BVA Rule (KTU High-Yield):** For an input range $[a, b]$:
- Test exactly at $a$ and $b$ (on-boundary).
- Test just below $a$ (i.e., $a - 1$) and just above $b$ (i.e., $b + 1$) (off-boundary).
- Optionally: nominal midpoint.

**Numerical Example:**
> *"Marks range: 0 to 100."*
> - On-boundary: $0$ and $100$.
> - Off-boundary (just below): $-1$.
> - Off-boundary (just above): $101$.
> - Nominal: $50$.
>
> Total: **4 to 5 test cases per boundary** (often 3 in basic BVA: $a, b, a-1, b+1$).

---

### 2.1.3 Decision Table Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A black-box technique that captures combinations of input conditions and their corresponding actions in a tabular form. Each column represents a unique combination (rule), and each row represents a condition or action. Useful when the system has **complex business rules** with multiple logical relationships.

**Structure of a Decision Table:**

| **Condition 1** | **Condition 2** | **Action** |
|------------------|------------------|------------|
| True             | True             | Action A   |
| True             | False            | Action B   |
| False            | True             | Action C   |
| False            | False            | Action D   |

For $n$ conditions, the maximum number of rules = $2^n$. (KTU high-frequency formula.)

---

### 2.1.4 Cause-Effect Graphing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A black-box technique that converts input conditions (*causes*) and output actions (*effects*) into a **Boolean graph**, which is then converted to a **decision table**. It explicitly models logical *dependencies* (AND, OR, NOT, identity) between causes and effects, eliminating infeasible combinations.

**Notation (Standard):**
- $\bigcirc$ → Identity (cause ↔ effect).
- $\sim$ → NOT (negation).
- $\vee$ → OR (any of).
- $\wedge$ → AND (all of).

---

### 2.1.5 Error Guessing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A black-box technique based on the tester's *intuition, skill, and experience* to anticipate where defects are likely to occur (e.g., division by zero, null input, empty string, leap year edge case). No formal derivation — purely *heuristic*.

---

## 2.2 White-Box Testing Techniques — Exhaustive Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Statement Coverage (SC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The percentage of executable statements in the program that have been exercised by a test suite. It is the *weakest* structural coverage criterion.

**Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
\text{Statement Coverage (SC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Number of Executable Statements}} \times 100\%
$$

---

### 2.2.2 Branch Coverage (BC) / Decision Coverage (DC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The percentage of decision outcomes (True/False branches) that have been exercised. Stronger than SC — achieving **100% branch coverage guarantees 100% statement coverage** (in structured code) but not vice versa.

**Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
\text{Branch Coverage (BC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Number of Branches}} \times 100\%
$$

---

### 2.2.3 Condition Coverage (CC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The percentage of *atomic boolean sub-expressions* (not whole decisions) that have evaluated to both True and False. Required for *compound* conditions such as `(A > 5) \&\& (B < 10)`.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{Condition Coverage (CC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Condition Outcomes Tested}}{\text{Total Number of Condition Outcomes}} \times 100\%
$$

---

### 2.2.4 Multiple Condition Coverage (MCC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A criterion requiring every *possible combination* of truth values of the atomic conditions in a compound expression to be tested. It is the *strongest* practical condition-based criterion.

**Formula:** For $n$ atomic sub-conditions, MCC requires $2^n$ test cases per compound expression.

---

### 2.2.5 Cyclomatic Complexity (CC-M)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A software metric developed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)** that quantifies the *number of linearly independent paths* through a program's source code. It is the single most important white-box metric in KTU exams.

**Three Equivalent Formulas (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — must memorize all three):**

$$
M = E - N + 2P \quad \text{(Graph-based)}
$$

$$
M = \pi - s + 2 \quad \text{(Predicates-based)}
$$

$$
M = \text{Number of regions in the flowgraph}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the control flowgraph.
- $N$ = number of nodes in the control flowgraph.
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically $P = 1$).
- $\pi$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes.
- $s$ = number of exit / sink nodes.

**Interpretation Table (KTU Exam Favorite):**

| **Cyclomatic Complexity $M$** | **Risk Category** | **Testability** |
|-------------------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| $1 \le M \le 10$              | Simple, low risk  | Highly testable  |
| $11 \le M \le 20$             | Moderate risk     | Testable         |
| $21 \le M \le 50$             | Complex, high risk| Hard to test     |
| $M > 50$                      | Untestable        | Refactor required|

---

### 2.2.6 Basis Path Testing (BPT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A white-box technique proposed by **McCabe** that derives a *basis set* of linearly independent paths through a program — exactly equal in number to the **cyclomatic complexity $M$**. It guarantees that every statement, branch, and condition is exercised *at least once* in the minimal set.

**Procedure (6 Steps — KTU High-Yield):**

1. Draw the **control flowgraph (CFG)** using standard notation.
2. Compute the **cyclomatic complexity** $M$ (using any of the three formulas).
3. Determine the **basis set** of $M$ linearly independent paths.
4. Design a test case for each basis path.
5. Execute, compare actual vs. expected output.
6. Repeat until all paths are covered.

---

### 2.2.7 Control Structure Testing (CST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A broader white-box testing approach that targets specific control-flow constructs:
> - **Condition Testing** — atomic sub-expression coverage.
> - **Loop Testing** — simple, nested, concatenated, unstructured loops; tests zero, one, two, $m$, $m-1$, $m+1$ iterations.
> - **Data Flow Testing** — verifies definitions and uses of variables along paths.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Cheat Sheet — Black-Box & White-Box Metrics**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units** | **Standard Range / Threshold** |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------|----------------------------------|
| **Statement Coverage** | $SC = \dfrac{S_{\text{exec}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | $\ge 80\%$ in industry; $100\%$ in KTU ideal |
| **Branch Coverage** | $BC = \dfrac{B_{\text{exec}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | $100\%$ target |
| **Condition Coverage** | $CC = \dfrac{C_{\text{out\_exec}}}{C_{\text{out\_total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | $100\%$ target |
| **Multiple Condition Coverage** | $\text{Test cases} = 2^n$ per compound | Count | $n$ = number of atomic conditions |
| **Decision Table Rules** | $\text{Max rules} = 2^n$ | Count | $n$ = number of conditions |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (Graph)** | $M = E - N + 2P$ | Scalar (integer) | Ideal: $M \le 10$ |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (Predicate)** | $M = \pi - s + 2$ | Scalar (integer) | Ideal: $M \le 10$ |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (Region)** | $M = R$ (regions in CFG) | Scalar (integer) | Ideal: $M \le 10$ |
| **Basis Path Test Cases** | $N_{\text{tests}} = M$ | Count | Equals complexity |
| **Boundary Value Test Cases** | $4$ per boundary (basic) or $5$ (extended) | Count | Always test on/just-out-of bounds |
| **Equivalence Classes** | $\text{1 valid} + k$ invalid | Count | $k$ = number of invalid scenarios |

**Important Convention for Absolute Value in LaTeX (per KTU typesetting):**
Use `\vert a \vert` or `\mid a \mid` — **never** the raw vertical pipe `|a|` inside a markdown table cell.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application of Black-Box & White-Box Testing:**

- **Aerospace & Avionics (DO-178C Standard):** Mandates **Modified Condition / Decision Coverage (MC/DC = 100%)** for safety-critical flight software — built directly on white-box condition coverage.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304 / FDA):** Requires both black-box (use-case based) and white-box (structural) evidence in the **Software Verification & Validation Report**.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** ASIL-D systems require near **100% branch + condition coverage** plus requirement traceability.
- **Banking & FinTech (PCI-DSS):** Black-box penetration testing is mandatory for production systems; white-box used internally for unit regression.
- **Agile & DevOps (KTU 2024 Industry Alignment):** White-box unit tests are integrated into **CI/CD pipelines** (Jenkins, GitHub Actions); black-box tests (Selenium, Cypress) are used for **end-to-end acceptance** in staging.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivational Walkthrough — Cyclomatic Complexity on a Sample Program

This is the **single most important calculation** in KTU Module 3. We will solve it **step-by-step**, applying all three McCabe formulas to verify the result.

### 3.1.1 The Source Code (KTU-Frequent Example)

```c
1:  void process(int a, int b) {
2:      int sum = 0;
3:      if (a > 0 && b > 0) {
4:          sum = a + b;
5:          if (sum > 100) {
6:              printf("High\n");
7:          } else {
8:              printf("Low\n");
9:          }
10:     } else {
11:         sum = a - b;
12:         printf("Negative branch\n");
13:     }
14: }
```

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Draw the Control Flowgraph (CFG)

**Mapping of source lines to nodes:**
- **N1** → Lines 1–2 (entry + initialization)
- **N2** → Line 3 (compound decision: `a>0 && b>0`)
- **N3** → Line 4 (assignment in True branch)
- **N4** → Line 5 (inner decision: `sum > 100`)
- **N5** → Line 6 (True-inner action)
- **N6** → Line 7 (False-inner action) → N7
- **N7** → Line 8–9 (False-inner print)
- **N8** → Line 10–12 (outer else)
- **N9** → Line 13 (exit)

**Edges (E) — labelled explicitly:**
- E1: N1 → N2
- E2: N2 → N3 (compound = True)
- E3: N2 → N8 (compound = False)
- E4: N3 → N4
- E5: N4 → N5 (sum > 100 = True)
- E6: N4 → N6 (sum > 100 = False)
- E7: N5 → N7
- E8: N6 → N7
- E9: N7 → N9
- E10: N8 → N9
- E11: N9 → Exit (sink)

**Counts:**
- Nodes $N = 9$
- Edges $E = 11$
- Predicate nodes $\pi = 2$ (line 3 and line 5)
- Exit nodes $s = 1$
- Connected components $P = 1$

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Apply Formula 1: Graph-Based

$$
M_1 = E - N + 2P
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
M_1 = 11 - 9 + 2(1)
$$

$$
M_1 = 11 - 9 + 2 = 4
$$

**Interpretation:** $M_1 = 4$ linearly independent paths.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Apply Formula 2: Predicate-Based

$$
M_2 = \pi - s + 2
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
M_2 = 2 - 1 + 2 = 3
$$

⚠️ **Discrepancy!** The two formulas give different values because in **Formula 2**, the compound `&&` is counted as **one** predicate, but it is *logically* two conditions. The graph-based formula is *exact*; the predicate formula may need adjustment for compound predicates. KTU accepts the **graph-based** answer as canonical: $M = 4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always show *both* calculations. If they differ, write a one-line explanation that the graph-based result is exact, and the predicate formula treats compound predicates as a single decision node.

---

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Formula 3: Region Counting

Counting the closed regions in the CFG (including the outer infinite region):

- **Region R1** — True branch of outer decision (encloses N3, N4, N5, N6, N7).
- **Region R2** — True branch of inner decision (encloses N5).
- **Region R3** — False branch of inner decision (encloses N6).
- **Region R4** — Outer infinite region (the rest of the graph).

$$
M_3 = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 = 4
$$

**Consistent with graph-based result.** ✔

---

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Derive the Basis Set of 4 Paths

| **Path #** | **Route** | **Condition Tested** |
|------------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Path 1** | N1 → N2 → N3 → N4 → N5 → N7 → N9 | Both decisions True; inner = True |
| **Path 2** | N1 → N2 → N3 → N4 → N6 → N7 → N9 | Both decisions True; inner = False |
| **Path 3** | N1 → N2 → N8 → N9 | Outer = False |
| **Path 4** | N1 → N2 → N3 → N4 → N5 → N7 → N9 (loop back) | *(alternate execution trace)* |

These are the **four linearly independent paths**. To verify linear independence, ensure no path can be formed as a *concatenation* of others. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivational Walkthrough — Equivalence Partitioning & BVA on a Loan-Approval System

> *"A loan system accepts applicant age in years. Eligibility condition: 21 ≤ age ≤ 60."*

### Step 1 — Equivalence Classes
- **VEC:** $21 \le \text{age} \le 60$ → Representative: $\text{age} = 35$
- **IEC-1:** $\text{age} < 21$ → Representative: $\text{age} = 15$
- **IEC-2:** $\text{age} > 60$ → Representative: $\text{age} = 70$

**Total test cases via ECP: 3.**

### Step 2 — Boundary Values (BVA)
- On-lower: $21$
- On-upper: $60$
- Just-below-lower: $20$
- Just-above-upper: $61$
- Nominal: $40$

**Total test cases via BVA: 5.**

### Step 3 — Decision Table (for multi-condition business rules)

Consider a refined rule:
- **C1:** Age in $[21, 60]$?
- **C2:** Salary $\ge 25{,}000$ INR/month?
- **C3:** Existing loans? (Yes/No)

$$
\text{Max Rules} = 2^3 = 8
$$

The full decision table is constructed with 8 columns, but **infeasible combinations** (e.g., Age < 21 AND Salary ≥ 25000 AND Existing loans = No) may be pruned — a standard KTU exam sub-question.

---

## 3.3 Coverage Analysis — Worked Example

> *"Given a program with 80 executable statements, 25 decision branches, and a test suite that executes 76 statements and 22 branches, compute SC and BC."*

### Statement Coverage

$$
SC = \frac{76}{80} \times 100\% = 95\%
$$

### Branch Coverage

$$
BC = \frac{22}{25} \times 100\% = 88\%
$$

### Comparison Statement (for 14-mark answer)

> 100% branch coverage **implies** 100% statement coverage *in well-structured code*, because every branch in structured code must contain at least one statement. However, 100% statement coverage does **not** guarantee 100% branch coverage, because the *False* side of an `if` may never be tested. Hence, **branch coverage is the stronger criterion**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply Which Technique

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to answer the KTU question: "Compare BBT and WBT" (very frequent 14-marker).**

| **Dimension** | **Black-Box** | **White-Box** |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| **SDLC Phase** | System, Acceptance | Unit, Integration |
| **Granularity** | External behaviour | Internal logic |
| **Spec dependency** | Specification-heavy | Code-heavy |
| **Tester persona** | End user, QA | Developer, code-aware tester |
| **Automation** | Selenium, JMeter, QTP | JUnit, PyTest, Cobertura |
| **Defect types found** | Missing features, wrong outputs | Dead code, unhandled paths |
| **Coverage metric** | EC, BVA rules, decision table rows | SC%, BC%, CC%, $M$ |
| **Cost of test design** | Moderate (spec analysis) | High (code inspection) |
| **Cost of execution** | High (system setup) | Low (unit-level) |
| **Independence** | High (independent testers) | Low (developer bias risk) |
| **Limitation** | Cannot find hidden defects in unused code | Misses missing requirements |
| **Combinable with** | Each other, in **integration** phase | Each other, in **unit** phase |
| **KTU exam tag** | "Behavioural / specification-based" | "Structural / glass-box" |

---

## 3.5 The V-Model Mapping (KTU 2024 Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where each testing technique is applied in the V-Model:**

| **V-Model Phase (Left Side — Development)** | **Corresponding V-Model Phase (Right Side — Test)** | **Preferred Technique** |
|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|

| Architectural Design | Integration Testing | **Mixed** (mostly White-Box for components) |

| Coding | — | — |

This table is a **KTU-favourite 14-mark sub-question** ("Explain V-Model with mapping of testing techniques").

**Interpretation:** Strength ordering of coverage criteria — each higher level **subsumes** the lower levels. **Path coverage is theoretically the strongest but practically infeasible** (infinite loops) — hence MCC is the practical KTU answer for safety-critical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "100% branch coverage = 100% path coverage" — this is **false**. Path coverage requires exercising *every possible path*, which is exponential and often impossible due to loops.
