# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Format:** Short-answer, two-sentence-to-one-paragraph responses. KTU-typical in CIE descriptive exams and as sub-parts of 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **Q:** Differentiate between **Black-Box Testing** and **White-Box Testing** in terms of *tester knowledge* and *test case derivation*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Black-Box Testing** is a *specification-based* technique where the tester has **no knowledge of the internal code structure**; test cases are derived purely from the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)**, use cases, and external behaviour.
- **White-Box Testing** is a *structure-based* technique where the tester has **complete visibility into the internal code**; test cases are derived from **source code, control flowgraphs, and data flow analysis**.
- The two paradigms are *complementary* and are usually applied at different SDLC phases — Black-Box at System/Acceptance, White-Box at Unit/Integration.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> **Q:** State the **three McCabe formulas** for computing cyclomatic complexity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three mathematically equivalent formulas proposed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)** are:

1. **Graph-based:** $M = E - N + 2P$, where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components.
2. **Predicate-based:** $M = \pi - s + 2$, where $\pi$ = predicate nodes, $s$ = exit nodes.
3. **Region-based:** $M = R$, where $R$ = number of enclosed regions in the flowgraph (including the outer region).

These formulas yield the *number of linearly independent paths*, which is the minimum number of test cases required for **Basis Path Testing**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Pattern)

> **Format:** Each 14-mark question has internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU-ESE standard.

---

### **Question 1A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### **Part (a)** — Black-Box Testing on a Specification (7 marks)

> **Q:** A university admission system accepts a student's *percentage* in the range **60 ≤ percentage ≤ 95** for eligibility to engineering admission. Design test cases using:
> (i) **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)**, and
> (ii) **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Equivalence Class Partitioning (3.5 marks):**

We identify one **Valid Equivalence Class (VEC)** and two **Invalid Equivalence Classes (IECs)**:

| **Class** | **Range** | **Representative Value** | **Expected Outcome** |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| VEC (valid) | $60 \le \text{percentage} \le 95$ | $75$ | Eligible |
| IEC-1 (invalid, too low) | $\text{percentage} < 60$ | $50$ | Not eligible |
| IEC-2 (invalid, too high) | $\text{percentage} > 95$ | $98$ | Invalid input (error) |

**Test Cases:**

| **TC #** | **Input** | **Expected Output** |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| TC1 | $\text{percentage} = 75$ | Eligible (admission granted) |
| TC2 | $\text{percentage} = 50$ | Not eligible |
| TC3 | $\text{percentage} = 98$ | Error: invalid input |

**(ii) Boundary Value Analysis (3.5 marks):**

For the range $[60, 95]$, BVA selects the *on-boundary*, *off-boundary* (just below/above), and *nominal* values:

| **TC #** | **Input Value** | **Position** | **Expected Output** |
|----------|------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| TC4 | $60$ | On-lower boundary | Eligible |
| TC5 | $95$ | On-upper boundary | Eligible |
| TC6 | $59$ | Just below lower | Not eligible |
| TC7 | $96$ | Just above upper | Invalid input |
| TC8 | $78$ | Nominal midpoint | Eligible |

**Total test cases derived:** 3 (ECP) + 5 (BVA) = **8 unique test cases**, providing robust coverage of the eligibility rule.

---

#### **Part (b)** — White-Box Testing Cyclomatic Complexity (7 marks)

> **Q:** Compute the **cyclomatic complexity** of the following C program using *all three McCabe formulas*, and identify the **minimum number of basis path test cases**.

```c
1:  int grade(int marks) {
2:      if (marks >= 90)
3:          return 'A';
4:      else if (marks >= 75)
5:          return 'B';
6:      else if (marks >= 60)
7:          return 'C';
8:      else
9:          return 'F';
10: }
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Nodes and Edges in CFG:**

- **N1** = entry (line 1)
- **N2** = decision `marks >= 90` (line 2)
- **N3** = return 'A' (line 3)
- **N4** = decision `marks >= 75` (line 4)
- **N5** = return 'B' (line 5)
- **N6** = decision `marks >= 60` (line 6)
- **N7** = return 'C' (line 7)
- **N8** = return 'F' (line 9)
- **N9** = exit (line 10)

**Edges (E):** N1→N2, N2→N3, N2→N4, N4→N5, N4→N6, N6→N7, N6→N8, N3→N9, N5→N9, N7→N9, N8→N9.

Therefore, $N = 9$ nodes and $E = 11$ edges.

**Step 2 — Apply Formula 1 (Graph-based):**

$$
M_1 = E - N + 2P = 11 - 9 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Formula 2 (Predicate-based):**

There are $\pi = 3$ predicate nodes (lines 2, 4, 6) and $s = 1$ exit node.

$$
M_2 = \pi - s + 2 = 3 - 1 + 2 = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Formula 3 (Region-based):**

The CFG has **4 regions**: three enclosed triangular regions (one for each True branch: A, B, C) and one outer infinite region (the F branch returning to the exit). Thus $R = 4$.

$$
M_3 = 4
$$

**Step 5 — All three formulas agree:** $M = 4$.

**Step 6 — Minimum Basis Path Test Cases:** $N_{\text{tests}} = M = 4$.

**Step 7 — Basis Path Set:**

| **Path #** | **Route** | **Test Input** | **Expected Output** |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Path 1 | N1→N2→N3→N9 | $95$ | 'A' |
| Path 2 | N1→N2→N4→N5→N9 | $80$ | 'B' |
| Path 3 | N1→N2→N4→N6→N7→N9 | $65$ | 'C' |
| Path 4 | N1→N2→N4→N6→N8→N9 | $40$ | 'F' |

Each path is *linearly independent* — no path can be expressed as a concatenation of others. The minimum test suite has **4 test cases** to cover all statements and branches.

---

### **Question 1B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### **Part (a)** — Decision Table Testing on Insurance Premium (7 marks)

> **Q:** An **insurance premium calculator** uses the following business rules:
> - **Rule 1:** If the applicant is *male* and *age > 30*, premium = ₹5000.
> - **Rule 2:** If the applicant is *male* and *age ≤ 30*, premium = ₹3000.
> - **Rule 3:** If the applicant is *female* and *age > 25*, premium = ₹3500.
> - **Rule 4:** If the applicant is *female* and *age ≤ 25*, premium = ₹2000.
>
> Construct a **Decision Table**, identify the number of rules, and provide a test case per rule.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Conditions (C1, C2):**
- C1: Gender = Male? (Yes/No)
- C2: Age > (threshold)? (Yes/No) — *threshold differs by gender; we use a unified Boolean for the table: high-age* (Yes/No)

To keep the table manageable, we use **two separate tables** — one for male, one for female — which is industry practice when rules are gender-dependent.

**Step 2 — Decision Table for Male Applicants (sub-table 1):**

| **Conditions** | **Rule M1** | **Rule M2** |
|----------------|---------------|---------------|
| Age > 30       | Yes           | No            |
| **Action**     |               |               |
| Premium        | ₹5000         | ₹3000         |

**Decision Table for Female Applicants (sub-table 2):**

| **Conditions** | **Rule F1** | **Rule F2** |
|----------------|---------------|---------------|
| Age > 25       | Yes           | No            |
| **Action**     |               |               |
| Premium        | ₹3500         | ₹2000         |

**Step 3 — Combined Decision Table (using binary flags):**

| **#** | **C1: Male?** | **C2: High Age?** | **Action: Premium (₹)** |
|--------|---------------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| 1      | Yes           | Yes                | 5000                      |
| 2      | Yes           | No                 | 3000                      |
| 3      | No            | Yes                | 3500                      |
| 4      | No            | No                 | 2000                      |

**Step 4 — Number of Rules:** $2^n = 2^2 = 4$ rules.

**Step 5 — Test Cases:**

| **TC #** | **Gender** | **Age** | **Expected Premium** |
|----------|------------|---------|-----------------------|
| TC1      | Male       | 35      | ₹5000                |
| TC2      | Male       | 25      | ₹3000                |
| TC3      | Female     | 30      | ₹3500                |
| TC4      | Female     | 22      | ₹2000                |

These four test cases cover **all 4 rules**, ensuring every business rule is verified at least once.

---

#### **Part (b)** — White-Box Condition Coverage & MCC (7 marks)

> **Q:** For the compound Boolean expression `if (X > 10 && Y < 5 || Z == 0)`, design test cases that achieve:
> (i) **Condition Coverage (CC)**
> (ii) **Multiple Condition Coverage (MCC)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Atomic Conditions:**

- **C1:** $X > 10$
- **C2:** $Y < 5$
- **C3:** $Z == 0$

The expression is logically `(C1 AND C2) OR C3`. For MCC, we consider the *full* 3-atom compound.

**Step 2 — Condition Coverage (CC):**

CC requires each atomic condition to evaluate to **both True and False** at least once.

| **TC #** | **X** | **Y** | **Z** | **C1 (X>10)** | **C2 (Y<5)** | **C3 (Z==0)** | **Whole Expression** |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|----------------|---------------|----------------|----------------------|
| TC1      | 15    | 3     | 7     | T              | T             | F              | T                    |
| TC2      | 5     | 8     | 0     | F              | F             | T              | T (via C3)           |

**Coverage achieved:**
- C1: T (TC1), F (TC2) ✔
- C2: T (TC1), F (TC2) ✔
- C3: F (TC1), T (TC2) ✔

**CC achieved with 2 test cases.**

**Step 3 — Multiple Condition Coverage (MCC):**

MCC requires every combination of truth values of the atomic conditions, regardless of short-circuit evaluation. For 3 atomic conditions, we need $2^3 = 8$ test cases.

| **TC #** | **C1** | **C2** | **C3** | **X** | **Y** | **Z** | **Expression Value** |
|----------|--------|--------|--------|-------|-------|-------|----------------------|
| TC1      | T      | T      | T      | 15    | 3     | 0     | T                    |
| TC2      | T      | T      | F      | 15    | 3     | 7     | T                    |
| TC3      | T      | F      | T      | 15    | 8     | 0     | T                    |
| TC4      | T      | F      | F      | 15    | 8     | 7     | F                    |
| TC5      | F      | T      | T      | 5     | 3     | 0     | T                    |
| TC6      | F      | T      | F      | 5     | 3     | 7     | F                    |
| TC7      | F      | F      | T      | 5     | 8     | 0     | T                    |
| TC8      | F      | F      | F      | 5     | 8     | 7     | F                    |

**MCC achieved with 8 test cases.** Note that CC only needed 2 — a classic demonstration that **MCC is much stronger but more expensive** to satisfy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Full Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Branch Coverage" with "Path Coverage."** Branch = 2 outcomes per decision; Path = every permutation. Saying "100% branch = 100% path" will cost 2 marks.
> 2. **In ECP/BVA questions, always list the *representative values* explicitly** — do not just write "test invalid ranges." The examiner checks for *specific numbers*.
> 3. **In Cyclomatic Complexity, show the substitution step** ($E$, $N$, $\pi$, $s$ all written out) — not just the final answer. Marks are awarded for working.
> 4. **For Decision Tables, mark infeasible rules** with "—" or "INFEASIBLE" if any rule is logically impossible. Ignoring infeasibility loses a mark.
> 5. **Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside a table cell** — it breaks markdown rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation.
> 6. **Always state the *interpretation* of $M$** at the end of a cyclomatic complexity problem ("$M = 4$ means 4 linearly independent paths and 4 minimum test cases for Basis Path Testing"). The interpretation line is mandatory for the last 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE Preparation**

### **Core Definitions (Memorize Verbatim)**

- **Black-Box Testing** = *specification-based, no internal visibility*.
- **White-Box Testing** = *structure-based, full internal visibility*.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** = *number of linearly independent paths* (McCabe, 1976).

### **Formulas (Must Memorize All)**

- **Statement Coverage:** $SC = \dfrac{S_{\text{exec}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$
- **Branch Coverage:** $BC = \dfrac{B_{\text{exec}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$
- **Condition Coverage:** $CC = \dfrac{C_{\text{out\_exec}}}{C_{\text{out\_total}}} \times 100\%$
- **Multiple Condition Coverage:** $2^n$ test cases for $n$ atomic conditions.
- **Decision Table Rules:** $2^n$ for $n$ conditions.
- **McCabe Graph:** $M = E - N + 2P$
- **McCabe Predicate:** $M = \pi - s + 2$
- **McCabe Region:** $M = R$
- **Basis Path Test Cases:** $N_{\text{tests}} = M$

### **Black-Box Techniques (5 — Name and One-Line Use)**

1. **Equivalence Partitioning (ECP)** — Group inputs, pick 1 per group.
2. **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** — Test on/just-in/just-out of edges.
3. **Decision Table Testing** — Tabulate rules for compound logic.
4. **Cause-Effect Graphing** — Boolean graph of cause-effect dependencies.
5. **Error Guessing** — Heuristic, experience-driven.

### **White-Box Techniques (6 — Name and One-Line Use)**

1. **Statement Coverage** — % statements executed.
2. **Branch Coverage** — % decision outcomes executed.
3. **Condition Coverage** — % atomic sub-conditions tested T/F.
4. **Multiple Condition Coverage** — All $2^n$ truth-value combinations.
5. **Cyclomatic Complexity** — McCabe metric, $M \le 10$ ideal.
6. **Basis Path Testing** — $M$ linearly independent paths.
7. **Control Structure Testing** — Loops, data flow, conditions.

### **Subsumption Hierarchy (Don't Confuse)**

$$
SC \subset BC \subset CC \subset MCC \subset \text{Path Coverage}
$$

### **McCabe Risk Bands (Memorize Table)**

- $1 \le M \le 10$ → Simple.
- $11 \le M \le 20$ → Moderate.
- $21 \le M \le 50$ → Complex.
- $M > 50$ → Untestable.

### **V-Model Mapping (Frequently Asked)**

- Unit Test = **White-Box** (SC, BC).
- Integration Test = **Mixed**.
- System Test = **Black-Box** (ECP, BVA).
- Acceptance Test = **Black-Box** (User Acceptance).

### **Critical Pitfalls to Avoid in ESE**

- **Don't say** "100% branch = 100% path" — *false*.
- **Don't say** "Cyclomatic = number of paths" — it's *linearly independent* paths.
- **Don't skip** the *interpretation* of $M$.
- **Don't forget** to state the *expected output* per test case.

### **Industry Standards to Name-Drop (Boosts Marks)**

- **DO-178C** (Aerospace — MC/DC).
- **ISO 26262** (Automotive — ASIL).
- **IEC 62304** (Medical).
- **Selenium, JUnit, PyTest, Cobertura** (Tools).

### **Final Exam Strategy**

- For 3-mark Q: 1 definition + 1 distinguishing point + 1 example.
- For 7-mark sub-part: Diagram + 3–5 steps + numerical derivation.
- For 14-mark Q: Always answer *both* sub-parts; use tables and CFG diagrams; close with the *interpretation* line.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Black Box Testing and White Box Testing**
> **Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme, PECST411 — Software Engineering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regression Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Testing** is a type of software testing that ensures previously developed and tested software still performs correctly after a change, modification, or integration with new code. It verifies that recent code changes have not adversely affected existing features and that the system continues to meet its specified functional and non-functional requirements.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECST411 syllabus**, regression testing is defined as:

> *"Regression testing is the process of testing the modified software to ensure that the changes made (whether in code, configuration, or environment) have not introduced new defects or re-activated old ones in the unchanged portions of the system."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The House Renovation Problem**
> 
> Imagine you have a beautifully painted house with working electricity, plumbing, and air conditioning. Now you decide to renovate the kitchen. After the renovation, you don't just check whether the kitchen works — you ALSO check whether the bathroom still drains properly, whether the lights in the hallway still turn on, and whether the AC in the bedroom still cools. Why? Because the workers might have accidentally knocked out a wire, blocked a pipe, or shifted a duct.
> 
> **Regression testing is exactly this concept applied to software.** Every time developers add a feature, fix a bug, refactor code, or upgrade a library, there is a risk that perfectly working functionality might break. Regression testing re-runs existing tests on the modified software to catch these unintended "side effects."

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Test Suite** | A collection of test cases to be executed in a specific cycle | Reused during regression |
| **Re-test All** | Re-executing the entire existing test suite | Most thorough but costly |
| **Test Selection** | Choosing a subset of relevant tests for regression | Reduces time/cost |
| **Test Prioritization** | Ordering tests to maximize early defect detection | Improves efficiency |
| **Smoke Testing** | A quick, shallow test to verify build stability | Often precedes regression |
| **Sentry Test** | A test that detects specific defect-prone areas | Used in selective regression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The two highest-weightage concepts in this topic are:
> 1. **Regression Test Selection (RTS) Techniques** — *Coverage-based, Minimization-based, Safe Reduction*
> 2. **Regression Test Prioritization (RTP) Techniques** — *Total/Additional/Average Percentage of Fault Detected (APFD)*

### 1.4 Why Regression Testing is Critical in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Butterfly Effect" in Software**
> 
> A seemingly harmless **1-line code change** in a Java import statement or a database query can break **dozens of dependent modules**. In large-scale projects, the change impact graph can span thousands of files. Without systematic regression testing, teams risk shipping software where old, previously-fixed bugs mysteriously reappear — these are called **regression defects** or **re-introduced defects**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 When is Regression Testing Performed?

Regression testing is triggered by a wide variety of changes in the software lifecycle:

1. **New Functional Requirements Added** — New modules/features must not break old ones.
2. **Bug Fixes** — Patches may unintentionally alter adjacent logic.
3. **Performance Optimization** — Speed improvements may alter execution paths.
4. **Environment Changes** — OS upgrade, database migration, library update.
5. **Configuration Changes** — Tweaking deployment parameters or feature flags.
6. **Code Refactoring** — Internal restructuring without changing external behavior.
7. **Patch Updates** — Third-party dependency upgrades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:** *Re-testing vs Regression Testing*
> 
> - **Re-testing** = Re-executing a specific test case that failed earlier to verify the bug fix. (Specific, targeted)
> - **Regression testing** = Re-executing a broad test suite to ensure no new defects appear elsewhere. (Broad, comprehensive)

### 2.2 Phases / Lifecycle of Regression Testing

The operational workflow follows a structured sequence:

1. **Detect Change Identification** — Identify what code/config/environment changed.
2. **Impact Analysis** — Determine which modules/features may be affected.
3. **Test Case Selection** — Pick relevant test cases from the existing repository.
4. **Test Case Prioritization** — Order them for maximum early fault detection.
5. **Test Suite Construction** — Build the optimized regression test suite.
6. **Execution** — Run the suite on the modified build.
7. **Defect Reporting & Feedback** — Log failures, feed back to developers.
8. **Re-execution Loop** — After fixes, re-run until clean.

### 2.3 Regression Test Selection (RTS) Techniques

#### 2.3.1 Coverage-Based Selection
Selects tests that exercise the **changed code** or code that **depends on changed code**.

$$T_{selected} = \{ t \in T \mid t \text{ covers at least one program element in } C_{modified} \}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Full test suite
- $t$ = Individual test case
- $C_{modified}$ = Set of modified program elements

#### 2.3.2 Minimization-Based Selection
Removes redundant tests while maintaining the same **code coverage**.

$$T_{min} = T_{selected} \setminus \{ t \mid \exists t' \text{ such that } t \text{ is subsumed by } t' \}$$

#### 2.3.3 Safe Reduction Selection
A subset that is **guaranteed** to detect all faults that the full suite would have detected, given the change.

$$\forall f \in F_{relevant}: f \text{ detected by } T_{safe} \iff f \text{ detected by } T_{full}$$

### 2.4 Regression Test Prioritization (RTP) Techniques

Test prioritization reorders tests so that **higher-priority tests (likely to reveal faults) run first**.

#### 2.4.1 The APFD Metric (Average Percentage of Faults Detected)

The most critical formula for KTU exam problems:

$$APFD = 1 - \frac{TF_1 + TF_2 + \dots + TF_m}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$$

Where:
- $n$ = Total number of test cases in the prioritized suite
- $m$ = Total number of faults detected by the test suite
- $TF_i$ = Position of the **first test case** that detects fault $i$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range:** APFD values range from **0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%)**. Higher APFD = better prioritization strategy. The ideal APFD is 1.0, meaning all faults are detected by the very first test.

#### 2.4.2 Alternate Compact Form

$$APFD = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (n - TF_i + 1)}{\binom{n}{2}} \times \frac{1}{\binom{n}{2}/\binom{n}{2}}$$

Simplified standardized form widely used in literature:

$$APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case | Range / Units |
|-------------------|------------|----------|---------------|
| **APFD Metric** | $APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$ | Measuring prioritization quality | 0 to 1 |
| **Test Reduction Ratio** | $TRR = \frac{\vert T_{full}\vert - \vert T_{selected}\vert}{\vert T_{full}\vert} \times 100\%$ | Reduction in test suite size | 0% to 100% |
| **Fault Detection Ratio** | $FDR = \frac{F_{detected}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Effectiveness of regression suite | 0% to 100% |
| **Code Coverage by Tests** | $CC = \frac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Identifying untested code | 0% to 100% |
| **Safe Selection Coverage** | $S_{safe} = T \cap C_{modified}$ | RTS theoretical guarantee | Subset |
| **Defect Density** | $DD = \frac{N_{defects}}{KLOC}$ | Quality metric for releases | per KLOC |
| **Cost per Defect** | $C_{defect} = \frac{C_{total}}{N_{defects}}$ | ROI of testing | Currency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Exam:** Always state units, boundary conditions, and the *interpretation* of each metric in your answer. Examiners reward contextual reasoning.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

| Industry | Application of Regression Testing |
|----------|----------------------------------|
| **Banking Software** | Every interest rate rule change must be re-validated against 1000s of loan scenarios |
| **Avionics** | DO-178C mandates exhaustive regression before certification |
| **E-Commerce** | Cart/checkout changes must not break product search, payment gateways |
| **Healthcare (FDA)** | Medical device software requires regression on every patch |
| **Telecom** | 5G stack upgrades trigger full regression of network functions |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing APFD (Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A regression test suite contains **$n = 10$ test cases**. When prioritized, the first test cases that detect each of the **$m = 5$ faults** are located at positions:
> - Fault 1: $TF_1 = 2$
> - Fault 2: $TF_2 = 3$
> - Fault 3: $TF_3 = 5$
> - Fault 4: $TF_4 = 7$
> - Fault 5: $TF_5 = 9$
>
> Calculate the APFD metric and comment on the effectiveness of this prioritization strategy.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.
- $n = 10$
- $m = 5$
- $\sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i = 2 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 9$

**Step 2:** Calculate the summation.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} TF_i = 2 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 9 = 26$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the APFD formula.
$$APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$$

$$APFD = 1 - \frac{26}{10 \times 5} + \frac{1}{2 \times 10}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator terms.
$$\frac{26}{50} = 0.52$$

$$\frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

**Step 5:** Final arithmetic.
$$APFD = 1 - 0.52 + 0.05 = 0.53$$

**Step 6:** Convert to percentage for clarity.
$$APFD_{percent} = 0.53 \times 100\% = 53\%$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation.
> [!NOTE]
> An APFD of **0.53 (53%)** indicates a **moderately effective** prioritization. The maximum possible APFD is 1.0 (100%), which would mean all faults are detected by the first test case. This result suggests that faults are being detected gradually throughout the suite rather than concentrated at the beginning. A better prioritization strategy would reorder the test cases so faults are detected in earlier positions (i.e., lower $TF_i$ values), which would yield a higher APFD.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Test Suite Reduction Calculation

> **Problem Statement:**
> A project originally had a regression test suite of **800 test cases**. After applying coverage-based test selection, only **320 test cases** were found to exercise the modified code modules. Calculate:
> 1. The Test Reduction Ratio (TRR)
> 2. The percentage of test cases retained
> 3. Comment on the trade-off between cost and fault detection

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
$$\vert T_{full}\vert = 800$$

$$\vert T_{selected}\vert = 320$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Test Reduction Ratio formula.
$$TRR = \frac{\vert T_{full}\vert - \vert T_{selected}\vert}{\vert T_{full}\vert} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$TRR = \frac{800 - 320}{800} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator.
$$TRR = \frac{480}{800} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation.
$$TRR = 0.60 \times 100\% = 60\%$$

**Step 6:** Calculate retention percentage.
$$\text{Retention} = \frac{\vert T_{selected}\vert}{\vert T_{full}\vert} \times 100\% = \frac{320}{800} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**Step 7:** Trade-off analysis.
> [!NOTE]
> By selecting only **40%** of the original suite, the team saves **60% of regression testing effort, time, and cost**. However, this comes with a *theoretical risk* — if the selection criteria is imperfect, some faults may go undetected. **Coverage-based selection** is preferred over random selection because it provides a structural guarantee: any test that touches modified code will be included.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Comparative APFD for Two Prioritization Strategies

> **Problem Statement:**
> Given a test suite with **$n = 6$ test cases** and **$m = 3$ faults**, compare two prioritization strategies:
> - **Strategy A:** $TF_1 = 1, \ TF_2 = 4, \ TF_3 = 6$
> - **Strategy B:** $TF_1 = 2, \ TF_2 = 3, \ TF_3 = 5$
>
> Determine which strategy is better.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute sum for Strategy A.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} TF_i^{(A)} = 1 + 4 + 6 = 11$$

**Step 2:** Compute APFD for Strategy A.
$$APFD_A = 1 - \frac{11}{6 \times 3} + \frac{1}{2 \times 6}$$

$$APFD_A = 1 - \frac{11}{18} + \frac{1}{12}$$

$$APFD_A = 1 - 0.6111 + 0.0833$$

$$APFD_A = 0.4722$$

**Step 3:** Compute sum for Strategy B.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} TF_i^{(B)} = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10$$

**Step 4:** Compute APFD for Strategy B.
$$APFD_B = 1 - \frac{10}{6 \times 3} + \frac{1}{2 \times 6}$$

$$APFD_B = 1 - \frac{10}{18} + \frac{1}{12}$$

$$APFD_B = 1 - 0.5556 + 0.0833$$

$$APFD_B = 0.5278$$

**Step 5:** Comparison.
$$APFD_B = 0.5278 > APFD_A = 0.4722$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy B is better** because it has a higher APFD value. This means Strategy B detects faults earlier in the test execution sequence, providing **faster feedback** to developers. In time-constrained regression cycles, Strategy B would be preferred.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Derivation of Test Reduction Trade-off

> **Problem Statement:**
> A regression suite has **1000 tests** with average execution time **2 minutes/test**. The suite currently detects **40 faults**. A proposed reduction strategy removes **300 redundant tests** that share 100% coverage with other tests, but it is estimated that **5 faults** may be missed. Calculate:
> 1. New fault detection capability (in %)
> 2. Time saved in hours
> 3. Average time per fault detected (before and after)

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Original suite metrics.
$$N_{tests}^{orig} = 1000, \quad T_{avg} = 2 \text{ min}, \quad F_{orig} = 40$$

**Step 2:** New suite metrics.
$$N_{tests}^{new} = 1000 - 300 = 700$$

$$F_{new} = 40 - 5 = 35$$

**Step 3:** Original time.
$$T_{orig} = 1000 \times 2 = 2000 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4:** New time.
$$T_{new} = 700 \times 2 = 1400 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5:** Time saved in hours.
$$\Delta T = 2000 - 1400 = 600 \text{ minutes} = \frac{600}{60} = 10 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6:** Fault detection capability after reduction.
$$FDR_{new} = \frac{35}{40} \times 100\% = 87.5\%$$

**Step 7:** Time per fault (original).
$$T_{fault}^{orig} = \frac{2000}{40} = 50 \text{ min/fault}$$

**Step 8:** Time per fault (new).
$$T_{fault}^{new} = \frac{1400}{35} = 40 \text{ min/fault}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction strategy saves **10 hours** while losing only **12.5% of fault detection** — a generally favorable trade-off. The new strategy is more **time-efficient per fault detected**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Regression Testing Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Code Change Detected] --> B[Impact Analysis]
    B --> C[Test Suite Selection]
    C --> D[Test Prioritization]
    D --> E[Build Regression Suite]
    E --> F[Execute Tests]
    F --> G{Tests Pass?}
    G -- No --> H[Report Defects]
    H --> I[Developer Fix]
    I --> F
    G -- Yes --> J[Sign-off Build]
    J --> K[Archive Suite Results]
```

### 4.2 Regression Test Selection Techniques Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RTS Techniques] --> B[Coverage-Based]
    A --> C[Minimization-Based]
    A --> D[Safe Reduction]
    B --> B1[Includes tests for modified code]
    C --> C1[Removes redundant tests]
    D --> D1[Provably detects all faults from full suite]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Regression Testing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        SRC[Source Code Repository]
        TC[Test Case Repository]
        CHG[Change Log]
    end
    subgraph Processing
        IA[Impact Analyzer]
        TSE[Test Selector Engine]
        TPE[Test Prioritization Engine]
        SU[Suite Builder]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        RTS[Optimized Regression Suite]
        EXE[Test Executor]
        REP[Defect Report]
    end
    SRC --> IA
    CHG --> IA
    IA --> TSE
    TC --> TSE
    TSE --> TPE
    TPE --> SU
    SU --> RTS
    RTS --> EXE
    EXE --> REP
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Apply Which RTS Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B{Budget Available?}
    B -- Unlimited --> C[Re-test All]
    B -- Limited --> D{Fault Coverage Critical?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Safe Reduction]
    D -- No --> F{Suite Size a Concern?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Minimization-Based]
    F -- No --> H[Coverage-Based]
```

### 4.5 Re-testing vs Regression Testing — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Software Testing] --> B[Re-testing]
    A --> C[Regression Testing]
    B --> B1[Targeted to specific failed test]
    B --> B2[Verifies bug fix]
    C --> C1[Broad test suite re-execution]
    C --> C2[Detects side-effects of changes]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Regression Testing. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Regression testing is a type of software testing conducted to verify that recent code changes, enhancements, or bug fixes have not adversely affected the existing functionality of the software. It involves re-executing a subset of the existing test cases (or the entire test suite) against the modified build to ensure that previously working features continue to behave as expected. The primary goal is to detect **regression defects** — defects that are unintentionally introduced into previously tested code as a side-effect of changes. Regression testing is critical in iterative development models like Agile and DevOps, where frequent code commits make such testing a continuous activity.

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between Re-testing and Regression Testing. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Re-testing | Regression Testing |
|--------|------------|---------------------|
| **Purpose** | Verify that a specific defect has been fixed | Verify that no new defects are introduced |
| **Scope** | Targeted at specific failed test cases | Broad, covers entire affected functionality |
| **Trigger** | After bug fix | After any change (feature, fix, refactor) |
| **Test Selection** | Only the previously failing tests | Selected or full test suite |
| **Priority** | Higher than regression (must run first) | Runs after re-testing confirms fix |
| **Example** | Re-running login test after fixing password bug | Running all module tests after a database schema change |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: Regression Test Selection and Prioritization [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain in detail the various Regression Test Selection (RTS) techniques with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Regression Test Selection (RTS) refers to the process of selecting a subset of test cases from an existing test suite to re-run on a modified version of the software. The objective is to **reduce testing effort and time** while still detecting most of the faults that the full suite would detect. RTS techniques are categorized as follows:

**1. Coverage-Based Selection:**
This technique selects all tests that exercise the modified code or code that depends on the modified code. Tools like **ATAC, DejaVu, and TestTube** use control-flow and data-flow coverage information to perform selection.

> **Example:** If a function `calculateTax()` is modified, all tests that call this function (directly or transitively) are selected.

**2. Minimization-Based Selection:**
This technique removes **redundant tests** that exercise the same code paths without losing coverage. It uses concepts like test subsumption — if test $T_i$ covers everything $T_j$ covers, then $T_j$ is removed.

> **Example:** If $T_1$ and $T_2$ both test the same login flow with identical assertions, $T_2$ may be minimized out.

**3. Safe Reduction Selection:**
This is the **strongest** form — it selects only those tests that are **guaranteed** to detect all faults detectable by the full suite. It uses formal program dependency graphs.

> **Example:** Using program slicing techniques to extract the minimal set of tests that touch the data dependencies of modified variables.

**4. Random Selection:**
Tests are selected randomly. Cheapest but **least reliable**; not recommended for safety-critical systems.

**Summary:** The trade-off across these techniques is **safety vs. effort**. Safe reduction is most reliable but expensive; random selection is cheapest but risky.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]: A regression test suite has 8 test cases and 4 faults. The prioritized positions of fault detection are TF1=2, TF2=4, TF3=5, TF4=7. Calculate the APFD metric and interpret the result. Suggest one improvement strategy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $n = 8$ test cases
- $m = 4$ faults
- $TF_1 = 2, \ TF_2 = 4, \ TF_3 = 5, \ TF_4 = 7$

**Step 1:** Compute the summation.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} TF_i = 2 + 4 + 5 + 7 = 18$$

**Step 2:** Apply the APFD formula.
$$APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$APFD = 1 - \frac{18}{8 \times 4} + \frac{1}{2 \times 8}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.
$$APFD = 1 - \frac{18}{32} + \frac{1}{16}$$

$$APFD = 1 - 0.5625 + 0.0625$$

**Step 5:** Final result.
$$APFD = 0.50 \quad \text{or} \quad 50\%$$

**Interpretation:** An APFD of **50%** indicates **moderate effectiveness** of the prioritization strategy. While some faults are detected early (TF=2), others (TF=7) are detected late, suggesting non-optimal ordering.

**Improvement Strategy:** Apply **fault-history-based prioritization**, where test cases that have detected the most faults in past regression cycles are moved to the front of the suite. Alternatively, use **code-coverage-based prioritization**, giving higher priority to tests covering recently modified branches.

---

#### **Question B: Regression Testing Tools and Strategies [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]: Discuss the various strategies for performing regression testing. Explain the role of automation in regression testing.**

**Model Answer:**

Regression testing strategies are categorized along a **spectrum of cost vs. thoroughness**:

**1. Re-test All Strategy:**
- Re-run the entire existing test suite on the modified build.
- **Pros:** Most thorough; guarantees no regression is missed.
- **Cons:** Extremely time-consuming and expensive; impractical for large projects.
- **When used:** Major releases, critical safety systems (aerospace, medical).

**2. Selective Regression Strategy:**
- Select only those test cases relevant to the change.
- Uses **dependency analysis** to identify affected modules.
- **Pros:** Faster; cost-effective.
- **Cons:** Requires accurate impact analysis; may miss unexpected couplings.

**3. Test Prioritization Strategy:**
- Reorder tests so high-value tests run first.
- Based on **fault history, code coverage, or risk**.
- **Pros:** Maximizes early fault detection.
- **Cons:** Does not reduce suite size; only changes execution order.

**4. Hybrid Strategy:**
- Combines **selection + prioritization**.
- Most widely used in industry.

**5. Progressive Regression Strategy:**
- Used when software evolves in stages (e.g., database migration).
- New tests are added incrementally; old tests may be deprecated.

**Role of Automation:**
Automation is **critical** in regression testing because:
- Manual regression is **error-prone and slow**.
- Automation tools (Selenium, JUnit, TestNG, QTP/UFT, Cypress) enable **continuous regression** in CI/CD pipelines.
- Automated regression runs **overnight or on every commit** (in DevOps).
- Automation supports **parallel execution**, drastically reducing wall-clock time.
- Tools provide **automatic reporting and traceability** for KTU audits and compliance.

**Recommended Tools:**
- **Selenium** (Web UI)
- **JUnit/TestNG** (Java unit tests)
- **Postman** (API regression)
- **Jenkins** (CI/CD orchestration)

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]: A project has 1200 regression test cases taking 3 minutes each. A selective regression strategy reduces this to 480 tests, but 6 out of 45 originally detected faults may be missed. Calculate the original regression time, new time, time saved, and the fault detection percentage after reduction.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $N_{orig} = 1200$ tests
- $T_{avg} = 3$ min/test
- $N_{new} = 480$ tests
- $F_{orig} = 45$ faults detected
- $F_{lost} = 6$ faults (potentially missed)

**Step 1:** Original regression time.
$$T_{orig} = 1200 \times 3 = 3600 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{orig}^{hrs} = \frac{3600}{60} = 60 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** New regression time.
$$T_{new} = 480 \times 3 = 1440 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{new}^{hrs} = \frac{1440}{60} = 24 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3:** Time saved.
$$\Delta T = 3600 - 1440 = 2160 \text{ minutes} = 36 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4:** Faults detected after reduction.
$$F_{new} = 45 - 6 = 39 \text{ faults}$$

**Step 5:** Fault detection percentage.
$$FDR = \frac{39}{45} \times 100\% = 86.67\%$$

**Step 6:** Test Reduction Ratio.
$$TRR = \frac{1200 - 480}{1200} \times 100\% = 60\%$$

**Conclusion:** The selective regression strategy saves **36 hours (60% reduction in effort)** while retaining **86.67% of fault detection capability** — a strong trade-off, especially suitable for iterative CI/CD pipelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **APFD Formula Mistake:** Students often forget the $+\frac{1}{2n}$ correction term. Marks are lost if the formula is incomplete.
> 2. **Confusing APFD with APPC:** APFD measures *fault* detection; APPC measures *coverage* progression. They are NOT the same.
> 3. **Re-testing vs Regression Testing Confusion:** Examiners specifically test this distinction. Don't interchange the two terms.
> 4. **Unit Confusion in APFD:** Always specify whether the answer is in decimal (0.53) or percentage (53%). Both are acceptable, but be consistent.
> 5. **Skipping Impact Analysis:** In long-form answers, students jump directly to test selection without explaining the *change identification* phase. Always mention this step.
> 6. **Ignoring the "Why":** Pure formula calculation without interpretation loses 1-2 marks. Always add a concluding analytical sentence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Regression Testing (Module 3):**

- [x] Regression testing = re-running tests to ensure no new defects after a change.
- [x] Triggered by bug fixes, new features, refactoring, environment changes, patches.
- [x] **Re-testing ≠ Regression Testing** — Re-testing is targeted; regression is broad.
- [x] **RTS techniques:** Coverage-based, Minimization-based, Safe Reduction, Random.
- [x] **RTP techniques:** Fault-history-based, Code-coverage-based, Risk-based, APFD-driven.
- [x] **APFD formula:** $APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$
- [x] APFD ranges from **0 to 1**; higher = better.
- [x] **TRR formula:** Reduction in suite size as a percentage.
- [x] **Safe Reduction** = theoretical guarantee of detecting all relevant faults.
- [x] **Automation tools:** Selenium, JUnit, TestNG, Jenkins, Postman.
- [x] Smoke testing **precedes** regression; sanity testing is a subset of regression.
- [x] In Agile/DevOps, regression is **continuous** and integrated into CI/CD.
- [x] The phrase "**side-effects**" is the KTU-favored keyword for regression defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for RTS Categories: "CMS-R"**
> - **C**overage-based
> - **M**inimization-based
> - **S**afe reduction
> - **R**andom

> **End of Module 3 — Regression Testing Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PECST411 Software Engineering Syllabus*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of DevOps and Code Management - Code Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of DevOps and Code Management - Code Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Management** in DevOps refers to the systematic, automated, and collaborative practice of **storing, versioning, branching, reviewing, merging, and maintaining source code** across distributed teams using version control systems (VCS), repository hosting platforms, and code review workflows integrated with the **CI/CD pipeline**.

It is the **backbone of the DevOps "Plan" and "Code" phases** of the **DASA DevOps lifecycle** and the **CALMS framework (Culture, Automation, Lean, Measurement, Sharing)**. Code management ensures that every line of code is **traceable, auditable, and reversible**, which directly supports **Continuous Integration (CI)** and **Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition to Memorize:** *Code Management is the disciplined engineering practice of using Version Control Systems (VCS) like Git, along with repository management, branching strategies, and peer-reviewed pull requests, to enable rapid, conflict-free, and traceable software delivery.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Docs of Engineers"

Imagine you and 5 friends are writing a **single 500-page novel** together. Without a system, you'd send Word files over WhatsApp — resulting in **conflicting versions, lost edits, and chaos**. 

**Code Management is the Google Docs equivalent for software engineers:**

- **Git (VCS)** = The "version history" feature that lets you roll back to *any* previous version.
- **Repository (Repo)** = The shared Google Doc where everyone's edits live.
- **Branch** = A "private copy" where you experiment without disturbing the main story.
- **Pull Request (PR)** = A polite request saying *"Hey team, please review my chapter changes before I add them to the main novel."*
- **Merge** = Your reviewed chapter getting officially stitched into the novel.
- **Conflict** = Two authors editing the *same sentence* — Git forces a human to decide whose version wins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric to Remember:** Industry standard is the **"Trunk-Based Development"** or **"Git Flow"** branching model, with PRs reviewed by **at least 2 peers** and CI pipelines triggered on **every commit**. The **DORA Four Key Metrics** measure DevOps performance:
> 1. **Deployment Frequency** — How often code reaches production
> 2. **Lead Time for Changes** — Commit-to-production time
> 3. **Change Failure Rate** — % of deployments causing failures
> 4. **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** — Time to fix production failures
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Code Management

### Pillar 1: Version Control Systems (VCS)

A VCS tracks changes to files over time, allowing you to **recall specific versions later**.

| **Type** | **Examples** | **Architecture** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Local VCS** | RCS | Single machine database | Fast, simple | Single point of failure |
| **Centralized VCS (CVCS)** | SVN, Perforce, CVS | Single central server + clients | Easy collaboration, admin control | Server failure = no work; no offline commits |
| **Distributed VCS (DVCS)** | **Git**, Mercurial, Bazaar | Every user has a full mirror of the repo | Offline work, multiple backups, fast branching | Steeper learning curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** *"Differentiate between Centralized and Distributed Version Control Systems."* Always highlight that **Git is a DVCS** — every clone is a **full backup**, branching is **lightweight (just a pointer)**, and operations are **local-first**.

### Pillar 2: Repository Hosting & Collaboration Platforms

Modern code management lives on cloud-based platforms that wrap Git with extra features:

- **Pull Requests / Merge Requests** — Code review workflow
- **Issue Tracking** — Bug and feature tickets
- **CI/CD Integrations** — GitHub Actions, GitLab CI
- **Access Control** — Role-based permissions
- **Code Scanning** — Security and quality analysis (e.g., SonarQube, CodeQL)

Major platforms: **GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps Repos**.

## 2.2 Git — The Heart of Code Management

Git is a **distributed, content-addressable, snapshot-based VCS** created by Linus Torvalds in 2005.

### Git's Three States (The Core Concept)

Files in Git live in one of **three states**:

1. **Modified** — You've changed the file but haven't committed it to your local database yet.
2. **Staged** — You've marked a modified file to go into your next commit snapshot (added to the **Index/Staging Area**).
3. **Committed** — The data is safely stored in your **local Git database** (the `.git` directory).

### The Three Areas of Git

1. **Working Directory** → Single checkout of one version of the project (from the local database).
2. **Staging Area (Index)** → A file that stores information about what will go into your next commit.
3. **Git Directory (Repository)** → The `.git` folder that stores the metadata and object database.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Git Workflow:** `Working Directory → git add → Staging Area → git commit → Git Directory`

### Git Object Model — The Deep Theory

Git stores everything as **four object types**, all addressed by **SHA-1 hashes**:

- **Blob** — Stores file content (no filename).
- **Tree** — Stores directory structure (points to blobs and other trees).
- **Commit** — Points to a tree, parent commit(s), author, committer, and message.
- **Tag** — A named pointer to a specific commit.

Each commit is **immutable** and forms a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — a chain of history that can never loop back.

## 2.3 Branching Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

| **Strategy** | **Best For** | **Main Branches** | **Release Cycle** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Git Flow** | Scheduled, versioned releases (e.g., mobile apps) | `main`, `develop`, `feature/*`, `release/*`, `hotfix/*` | Long-lived, versioned |
| **GitHub Flow** | Continuous deployment web apps | `main`, `feature/*` | Lightweight, deploy-from-`main` |
| **GitLab Flow** | Production + multiple environments | `main`, `production`, `feature/*` | Environment-tracking branches |
| **Trunk-Based Development** | Mature DevOps, Google/Amazon style | Single `trunk`/`main`, short-lived feature branches (< 1 day) | Continuous, multiple deploys/day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantic Versioning (SemVer) Standard:** `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (e.g., `2.7.3`)
> - **MAJOR** ↑ when you make **incompatible API changes**
> - **MINOR** ↑ when you add **backward-compatible functionality**
> - **PATCH** ↑ when you make **backward-compatible bug fixes**
> - Pre-release tags: `1.0.0-alpha`, `2.1.0-beta.1`, `3.0.0-rc.2`

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commit Hash | $H = \text{SHA-1}(\text{content})$ | 40-char unique ID of a commit | Tracing exact code state |
| Branch Pointer | $B \rightarrow H_c$ | A label pointing to a commit | Parallel development |
| Merge Commit | $M = \text{merge}(C_1, C_2)$ | Joins two histories with 2 parents | Combining feature branches |
| Rebase | $C' = \text{replay}(C, \text{onto} \rightarrow B_{base})$ | Re-applies commits on top of another base | Linear history |
| DORA Lead Time | $LT = T_{deploy} - T_{commit}$ | Commit → Production time | DevOps performance metric |
| DORA MTTR | $MTTR = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{recovered,i} - T_{failed,i})$ | Mean recovery time | Operational resilience |
| Change Failure Rate | $CFR = \frac{\text{Failed deployments}}{\text{Total deployments}} \times 100\%$ | % of bad releases | Quality indicator |
| Commit Frequency | $CF = \frac{\text{Commits}}{\text{Time period}}$ | Team velocity proxy | Productivity metric |
| Code Churn | $CC = \frac{\text{Lines added} + \text{Lines deleted}}{\text{Total LOC}}$ | Rework ratio | Stability measure |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In **modern software companies**, code management is not optional — it is **organizational infrastructure**. It enables:
- **Parallel development** by thousands of engineers (e.g., Google has **90,000+ developers** in monorepos using Trunk-Based Development with custom tools like Piper).
- **Audit trails** for regulatory compliance (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR).
- **Disaster recovery** — every developer has a full backup of the source.
- **A/B testing and feature flags** enabled by branch isolation.
- **Open-source collaboration** at planetary scale (e.g., Linux kernel, Kubernetes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Git Commit Hash Computation

A Git commit hash is derived from a structured payload — let us derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1:** Gather the commit metadata.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Metadata} = \{ \text{parent hash},\ \text{author},\ \text{committer},\ \text{tree hash},\ \text{message} \}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Construct the commit object header.
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{header}} = \text{"commit "} \Vert \ \text{size} \Vert \ \text{NULL}
\end{aligned}$$
where $\text{size}$ is the byte-length of the object content.

**Step 3:** Append the structured content.
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{body}} = \text{tree } H_{\text{tree}} + \text{parent } H_{\text{parent}} + \text{author } T_{\text{auth}} + \text{committer } T_{\text{com}} + \text{NULL} + \text{message}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Concatenate header and body.
$$\begin{aligned}
P = H_{\text{header}} + C_{\text{body}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the SHA-1 hash of the payload.
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{commit}} = \text{SHA-1}(P) = \text{fingerprint of the ENTIRE commit}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Implication:** Changing even **one byte** in the message or tree will produce a **completely different hash**. This is what makes Git **tamper-evident** and **cryptographically verifiable**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Merge vs. Rebase vs. Squash

| **Aspect** | **Merge** | **Rebase** | **Squash Merge** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **History Shape** | Non-linear DAG (preserves topology) | Linear (rewrites history) | Linear, 1 commit per feature |
| **Commits Preserved** | All original commits | All commits replayed | All combined into one |
| **Conflict Resolution** | Once, at merge point | May repeat conflicts on each replay | Once, at squash time |
| **Traceability** | High (see exact branch origin) | Low (commits get new SHAs) | Low (loses intermediate work) |
| **Safe for shared branches?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (rewrites public history) | ✅ Yes |
| **KTU Recommendation** | Default for shared branches | Use only for **local cleanup** before PR | Best for "feature X done" merges |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Conflict Resolution Algorithm

When two branches modify the **same lines** of a file, Git cannot auto-merge. Here is the resolution algorithm:

**Step 1:** Identify conflicting files.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Conflicting files} = \text{git status} \rightarrow \text{Unmerged paths}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Open the file and locate conflict markers.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Markers} = \{ \text{<<<<<<< HEAD},\ \text{=======},\ \text{>>>>>>> branch-name} \}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Manually edit the file to produce the **correct, integrated version** $V_{\text{final}}$.

**Step 4:** Stage the resolved file.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{git add } \rightarrow \text{Marks file as resolved}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Complete the merge with a commit.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{git commit} \rightarrow \text{Finalizes merge with conflict resolution message}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Verify clean state.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{git status} = \text{"working tree clean"} \iff \text{Resolution complete}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: DORA Lead Time Calculation

For a team with $n$ recent deployments, the **average lead time** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
LT_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{\text{deploy},i} - T_{\text{commit},i})
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Example (for KTU numerical):**

Suppose 4 commits were deployed with the following timestamps:

| **Commit #** | **Commit Time** | **Deploy Time** | **Lead Time (hours)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 09:00 | 14:00 | $5$ |
| 2 | 11:00 | 14:00 | $3$ |
| 3 | 12:30 | 14:00 | $1.5$ |
| 4 | 13:45 | 14:00 | $0.25$ |

Compute the average lead time:

$$\begin{aligned}
LT_{\text{avg}} & = \frac{1}{4} \left( 5 + 3 + 1.5 + 0.25 \right) \\[6pt]
& = \frac{1}{4} ( 9.75 ) \\[6pt]
& = 2.4375 \text{ hours} \approx 2 \text{ h } 26 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Elite performers (per DORA reports):** Lead time $\lt 1$ hour. **Medium performers:** 1 week–1 month. Code management practices directly drive this metric.

## 3.5 Branching Strategy Decision Tree (Analytical)

Given a project profile $(P)$, choose branching strategy $S$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S(P) = \begin{cases}
\text{Git Flow} & \text{if } P.\text{release} = \text{versioned \& infrequent} \\
\text{GitHub Flow} & \text{if } P.\text{deploy} = \text{continuous \& web-based} \\
\text{Trunk-Based} & \text{if } P.\text{team size} \geq 50 \text{ \& CI maturity} = \text{high} \\
\text{GitLab Flow} & \text{if } P.\text{environments} \geq 3 \text{ (dev, stage, prod)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **decision matrix** exam-friendly derivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Areas and Three States of Git

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Working Directory] -- git add --> B[Staging Area Index]
    B -- git commit --> C[Git Directory Local Repo]
    C -- git checkout --> A
    C -- git push --> D[Remote Repository Origin]
    D -- git pull fetch --> A

    A1[File State: Modified] --- A
    B1[File State: Staged] --- B
    C1[File State: Committed] --- C

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520
    style B1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520
    style C1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520
```

## 4.2 Git Flow Branching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    main[main branch - production releases]
    develop[develop branch - integration line]
    feature1[feature user-auth]
    feature2[feature payment]
    release1[release v1.0]
    hotfix1[hotfix v1.0.1]
    tag1[v1.0 tag]
    tag2[v1.0.1 tag]

    main --- develop
    develop --- feature1
    develop --- feature2
    develop --- release1
    release1 --- main
    release1 --- tag1
    main --- hotfix1
    hotfix1 --- main
    hotfix1 --- tag2
    hotfix1 --- develop

    style main fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    style develop fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFF
    style feature1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style feature2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style release1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
    style hotfix1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style tag1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
    style tag2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Code Management in DevOps

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLAN["PLAN PHASE"]
        P1[Issue Tracking - Jira]
        P2[User Stories - Product Backlog]
    end

    subgraph CODE["CODE PHASE - Code Management Core"]
        C1[Developer Local Repo - Git]
        C2[Staging Area]
        C3[Commit with Semantic Message]
        C4[Push to Remote - GitHub]
        C5[Pull Request - PR Open]
        C6[Peer Code Review - 2 Reviewers]
        C7[Static Code Analysis - SonarQube]
```

## 4.5 Pull Request Review Workflow State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft: Developer opens Draft PR
    Draft --> OpenReady: Mark as Ready for Review
    OpenReady --> InReview: Reviewer assigned
    InReview --> ChangesRequested: Reviewer requests changes
    ChangesRequested --> InReview: Developer pushes new commits
    InReview --> Approved: All reviewers approve
    Approved --> Merging: All checks green CI
    Merging --> Merged: Squash or Merge Commit
    Merging --> ConflictDetected: Merge conflicts found
    ConflictDetected --> InReview: Developer resolves locally
    Merged --> [*]: Branch can be deleted
    Approved --> Closed: PR abandoned
    Closed --> [*]

    state Approved: Checks Pass + Reviews Approved
    state Merging: Auto or Manual Merge
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Explain the concept of a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS) and list its advantages over Centralized VCS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Distributed Version Control System (DVCS)** like **Git** does not rely on a single central server. Instead, **every developer maintains a full local copy** of the entire repository, including its complete history.

**Three Key Advantages:**

1. **Offline Work Capability (1 mark):** Developers can commit, branch, browse history, and diff locally without network connectivity. Operations become **near-instantaneous** since they happen on the local disk.

2. **No Single Point of Failure (1 mark):** Every clone is a **complete backup**. If the central server crashes, any developer can push their local repo to restore it. In CVCS (e.g., SVN), a server crash halts all work.

3. **Fast, Cheap Branching (1 mark):** Creating a branch in Git is just a **40-character file write** (a pointer), making it ideal for **feature branching workflows** and experimentation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only *"DVCS is better than CVCS"* without giving **specific technical reasons** (offline, no SPOF, cheap branching). Always list **3 concrete advantages with explanations** for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**  
*What is Semantic Versioning? Explain the meaning of version number 2.7.3-beta.1.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** is a standardized versioning scheme specified as `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (1 mark).

- **MAJOR (2)** ↑ when you make **incompatible API changes**
- **MINOR (7)** ↑ when you add **backward-compatible features**
- **PATCH (3)** ↑ when you make **backward-compatible bug fixes**
- **Pre-release label (`-beta.1`)** (1 mark) indicates this is **not yet production-ready** — `beta` is the pre-release identifier, and `1` is the pre-release sequence number. Production systems should **not** depend on `2.7.3-beta.1`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Don't confuse `beta` with a `MINOR` bump. The `2.7.3-beta.1` actually means **"third patch of the seventh minor release of the second major version, first beta pre-release."** State this clearly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — Question A Choice]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Git Flow branching model** in detail. Discuss the roles of `master`, `develop`, `feature`, `release`, and `hotfix` branches. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **pull request workflow** in detail. How does it integrate with **Continuous Integration** to ensure code quality? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Git Flow** is a strict branching model designed by Vincent Driessen for projects that ship **versioned releases** (1 mark).

**The 5 branch types:**

1. **`master` (or `main`)** — Contains **production-ready, tagged releases only** (1 mark). Every commit is a released version. Hotfixes merge here.

2. **`develop`** — The **integration branch** where all completed features are merged. It reflects the **latest delivered development changes** for the next release (1 mark).

3. **`feature/*`** — Branched from `develop`, used for new feature development. Merged back into `develop` via a PR when complete (1 mark). Naming: `feature/user-auth`, `feature/payment-gateway`.

4. **`release/*`** — Branched from `develop` when features are frozen for a release. Used for **bug fixes, version bumping, and release preparation** (1 mark). Once stable, it merges into **both** `master` (with a version tag) and back into `develop`.

5. **`hotfix/*`** — Branched directly from `master` for **urgent production bug fixes** (1 mark). Merges into both `master` (with a new patch tag) and `develop` to keep both branches consistent.

**Workflow sequence (refer to the Git Flow diagram in Section 4.2):**
$$\text{develop} \xrightarrow{\text{feature}} \text{develop} \xrightarrow{\text{release}} \text{master} + \text{develop} \xrightarrow{\text{hotfix}} \text{master} + \text{develop}$$

**Drawback (1 mark):** Heavy and complex for **continuous-deployment** web apps; better suited for **scheduled, versioned releases** like mobile apps or libraries.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Pull Request (PR) Workflow — Step by Step (5 marks):**

1. **Open the PR:** Developer pushes a feature branch and opens a PR from `feature/x` → `main`/`develop` on GitHub/GitLab (1 mark).

2. **Automated CI Checks Trigger:** As soon as the PR is opened, **CI pipelines** run automatically — **linting, unit tests, code coverage, security scans, and build verification** (1 mark).

3. **Reviewer Assignment:** The PR is assigned to one or more peers. Reviewers can:
   - **Approve** ✅
   - **Request Changes** ✏️
   - **Comment** 💬 (1 mark)

4. **Iterative Refinement:** Developer pushes more commits to the same branch; CI re-runs; reviewers re-evaluate. The PR becomes a **collaborative conversation thread** (1 mark).

5. **Merge with Strategy:** Once all checks pass and approvals are given, the PR is merged using **merge commit, squash, or rebase** strategy. The feature branch is **deleted** (1 mark).

**Integration with Continuous Integration (2 marks):**

- **Every commit to the PR** triggers CI — this is called **PR-level CI** or **branch build**, distinct from the main-branch build.
- CI **blocks merging** if any check fails (a **required status check**).
- Tools: **GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI, Travis CI**.
- This **prevents broken code from reaching `main`**, preserving the **trunk's deployability** — a core principle of Trunk-Based Development and the CALMS framework's *Automation* pillar.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Loses 2-3 marks):** Students often describe PR workflow **without** linking it to CI automation. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the **"DevOps integration" angle** — always mention **automated checks blocking the merge**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Question B Choice]**

**(a)** What is a **Version Control System**? Compare and contrast **Centralized VCS (CVCS)** and **Distributed VCS (DVCS)** with a tabular comparison covering architecture, examples, advantages, and disadvantages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **DORA Four Key Metrics** used to measure DevOps performance. Compute the **average Lead Time for Changes** for a team whose last 5 deployments had lead times of 6 hours, 4 hours, 3 hours, 5 hours, and 2 hours. Comment on the team's performance. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

A **Version Control System (VCS)** is software that **tracks and manages changes** to files over time, allowing developers to **recall specific versions later** and **collaborate without overwriting each other's work** (1 mark).

**Comparison Table (6 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **CVCS (e.g., SVN)** | **DVCS (e.g., Git)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Single central server, multiple working copies | Every user has a full repository clone with history |
| **Examples** | Subversion, Perforce, CVS | Git, Mercurial, Bazaar |
| **Network Dependency** | Required for almost all operations | Optional — most operations are local |
| **Performance** | Slower — network round-trips | Very fast — local operations |
| **Branching** | Heavy, server-side, often avoided | Lightweight, local, encouraged |
| **Backup / Recovery** | Server crash = history loss; manual backup | Every clone is a full backup |
| **Offline Commits** | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Fully supported |
| **Disadvantages** | Single point of failure; bottlenecks | Steeper learning curve; larger disk usage |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** DVCS (Git) is the **de facto industry standard** for modern DevOps due to its offline capability, cheap branching, and resilience. CVCS remains in use in **legacy enterprise systems** with strict centralized access policies.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**DORA Four Key Metrics (3 marks):**

DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) defines four metrics that measure software delivery performance:

1. **Deployment Frequency** — How often an organization successfully releases to production (1 mark).
2. **Lead Time for Changes** — Time from code commit to code successfully running in production (1 mark).
3. **Change Failure Rate** — Percentage of deployments causing a failure in production (0.5 mark).
4. **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** — Time it takes to restore service after a production incident (0.5 mark).

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given 5 lead times: 6, 4, 3, 5, 2 hours.

$$\begin{aligned}
LT_{\text{avg}} & = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} LT_i \\[6pt]
& = \frac{1}{5} (6 + 4 + 3 + 5 + 2) \\[6pt]
& = \frac{1}{5} (20) \\[6pt]
& = 4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Performance Commentary (1 mark):**

According to the **DORA 2023 report benchmarks:**
- **Elite performers:** Lead time $\lt 1$ hour
- **High performers:** 1 hour – 1 day
- **Medium performers:** 1 day – 1 week
- **Low performers:** $\gt 1$ month

With an **average lead time of 4 hours**, this team falls in the **"High Performer"** category — they can ship a change from commit to production in less than a day. To reach elite, they should **automate testing further, reduce manual approval gates, and adopt Trunk-Based Development with smaller, more frequent commits.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Loses 2 marks):** Students often forget to **categorize the team** based on the computed value. Always tie the **numerical answer to a DORA performance tier** for full marks. Also, **show the formula before substituting values** — KTU evaluators deduct marks for "bare numbers without derivation."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **Code Management = VCS + Repos + PRs + Branching Strategy** — all integrated with **CI/CD**.
- ✅ **Git is a DVCS** — Linus Torvalds, 2005, **content-addressable, snapshot-based**.
- ✅ **Three States:** Modified → Staged → Committed. **Three Areas:** Working Dir → Staging Area → Git Directory.
- ✅ **Git Object Model:** Blob, Tree, Commit, Tag. All addressed by **SHA-1 hashes**.
- ✅ **Branching Strategies:** Git Flow (versioned releases), GitHub Flow (web CD), Trunk-Based (elite DevOps), GitLab Flow (multi-env).
- ✅ **Semantic Versioning:** `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH[-prerelease]`. Bump MAJOR for breaking changes.
- ✅ **PR Workflow:** Open → CI Auto-Checks → Peer Review → Approve → Merge → Delete Branch.
- ✅ **DORA Metrics:** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, MTTR.
- ✅ **Lead Time Formula:** $LT_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum (T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{commit}})$.
- ✅ **Merge vs Rebase:** Merge preserves history (safe for shared branches); Rebase rewrites it (use locally only).
- ✅ **Conflict Markers:** `<<<<<<< HEAD`, `=======`, `>>>>>>> branch`. Resolve manually, then `git add` and `git commit`.
- ✅ **DVCS Advantages:** Offline work, no single point of failure, cheap branching.
- ✅ **CALMS Framework's "Sharing" + "Automation" pillars** are most directly enabled by code management.
- ✅ **Conventional Commits Format:** `<type>(<scope>): <subject>` — e.g., `feat(auth): add OAuth2 login`.

**🔥 Must-Remember One-Liners for KTU Viva:**

> *"Code management is the *Plan* and *Code* phase of the DevOps infinity loop — without it, CI is impossible."*
>
> *"Git is not a version control system; it is a **content-addressable filesystem** with a VCS interface."* — Scott Chacon
>
> *"Trunk-based development + feature flags + continuous integration = the elite DevOps trifecta."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DevOps Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** DevOps Automation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **DevOps Automation** is the strategic application of technology, tooling, and predefined workflows to programmatically execute, monitor, and manage the repetitive tasks across the software development lifecycle (SDLC), thereby eliminating manual intervention during integration, testing, deployment, and infrastructure provisioning phases. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Software Engineering syllabus, DevOps Automation represents the engineering discipline that bridges the traditional gap between **Software Development (Dev)** and **IT Operations (Ops)** by enforcing continuous, automated feedback loops. It transforms manual, error-prone processes into reproducible, script-driven pipelines governed by version control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST411 Module 3):** DevOps Automation refers to the use of automated tools and processes to enable Continuous Integration (CI), Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD), Continuous Testing, Continuous Monitoring, and Infrastructure as Code (IaC), ensuring rapid, reliable, and consistent software releases. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory" Model Imagine a modern **car manufacturing assembly line** versus a traditional **artisan workshop**: - **Traditional Model (Manual DevOps):** Each mechanic hand-crafts a car, manually tightens every bolt, personally test-drives it, and personally delivers it to the customer. Mistakes are common, speed is slow, and quality varies by mechanic. - **DevOps Automation Model (Smart Factory):** Robotic arms (automated scripts) assemble parts, automated sensors (monitoring tools) check quality at every station, conveyor belts (CI/CD pipelines) move the car between stages, and a central dashboard (observability platform) shows the live status of every vehicle in production. In this analogy: - **The Assembly Line** = The CI/CD Pipeline - **The Robotic Arms** = Build & Test Automation Scripts - **The Quality Sensors** = Automated Testing Frameworks - **The Conveyor Belts** = Deployment Automation - **The Factory Blueprint** = Infrastructure as Code (IaC) - **The Central Dashboard** = Continuous Monitoring Tools > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** DevOps Automation is NOT about replacing human engineers — it is about replacing **repetitive human tasks** so engineers can focus on creative, high-value problem-solving. ## 1.3 Core Pillars of DevOps Automation The **5 Pillars of DevOps Automation** that KTU examiners frequently test: | Pillar | Primary Function | Common Tools | |---|---|---| | **Continuous Integration (CI)** | Automated code merging & build verification | Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions | | **Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD)** | Automated release to staging/production | Spinnaker, ArgoCD, Octopus Deploy | | **Continuous Testing (CT)** | Automated test execution (unit, integration, regression) | Selenium, JUnit, pytest, Postman | | **Continuous Monitoring (CM)** | Real-time observability of apps & infrastructure | Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack | | **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** | Programmatic provisioning of infrastructure | Terraform, Ansible, Pulumi, CloudFormation | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Exams — "I-MTCD" (Imagine My Tests Can Deploy):** **I**aC → **M**onitoring → **T**esting → **C**I → **D** — the logical flow from infrastructure setup to deployment. ## 1.4 Why DevOps Automation Matters — Engineering Justification The **DORA Metrics** (defined by Google's DevOps Research and Assessment team) provide the gold standard for measuring automation success: - **Deployment Frequency** — How often code reaches production - **Lead Time for Changes** — Time from commit to production - **Change Failure Rate** — Percentage of deployments causing failures - **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** — Time to restore service after an incident High-performing DevOps teams achieve **deployment frequencies of multiple times per day** compared to traditional teams that may deploy **monthly or quarterly**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The DevOps Lifecycle — Phased Automation Breakdown DevOps Automation is not a single tool but a **layered ecosystem** of automated processes. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps it to the **infinity loop (∞)** model, which represents the non-linear, continuous nature of DevOps. ### Phase 1: Plan & Code (PLAN) - **Automation Action:** Issue tracking, backlog automation, code linting - **Tools:** Jira, Git, SonarQube - **Output:** Versioned source code in a repository ### Phase 2: Build & Integrate (CI) - **Automation Action:** Trigger automated build on every `git push` - **Process Logic:** 1. Developer pushes code to remote repository 2. Webhook triggers the CI server 3. Build tool compiles the source 4. Artifact (JAR/Docker image) is produced - **Tools:** Maven, Gradle, Jenkins, GitHub Actions ### Phase 3: Test Automatically (CT) - **Automation Action:** Execute pre-defined test suites without human clicks - **Test Pyramid Structure:** - **Unit Tests** (70%) — Test individual functions/methods - **Integration Tests** (20%) — Test module interactions - **End-to-End Tests** (10%) — Test full user workflows - **Tools:** JUnit, pytest, Selenium, Cypress, JMeter ### Phase 4: Release & Deploy (CD) - **Automation Action:** Promote artifacts through environments (Dev → Staging → Production) - **Deployment Strategies:** - **Blue-Green Deployment** — Two identical production environments; switch traffic instantly - **Canary Release** — Roll out to a small percentage of users first - **Rolling Update** — Gradually replace old instances with new ones - **Recreate (Big Bang)** — Tear down old, spin up new ### Phase 5: Operate & Monitor (CM) - **Automation Action:** Auto-scaling, self-healing, log aggregation - **Feedback Loop:** Monitoring data feeds back to PLAN phase -
