**Tools:** Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, PagerDuty ## 2.2 CI/CD Pipeline — The Heart of DevOps Automation The **CI/CD Pipeline** is the executable workflow that automates code movement from developer workstation to production server. ### Conceptual Pipeline Stages (Exhaustive List) $$\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Build} \rightarrow \text{Test} \rightarrow \text{Analyze} \rightarrow \text{Deploy} \rightarrow \text{Monitor}$$ ### Detailed Stage Definitions 1. **Source Stage** — VCS polling or webhook trigger 2. **Build Stage** — Compile code, resolve dependencies, generate artifacts 3. **Test Stage** — Run automated unit, integration, and security tests 4. **Code Quality Analysis** — Static code analysis (SAST), code coverage checks 5. **Artifact Storage** — Push built artifacts to a repository (e.g., Nexus, Artifactory) 6. **Deploy to Staging** — Auto-deploy to pre-production environment 7. **Deploy to Production** — Controlled rollout (canary/blue-green) ## 2.3 Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — Deep Dive **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** is the practice of managing and provisioning computing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files, rather than physical hardware configuration or interactive configuration tools. ### IaC Approaches — Two Paradigms | Approach | Description | Example Tool | State Management | |---|---|---|---| | **Declarative (Functional)** | Define *what* the end state should be | Terraform, CloudFormation | Tool tracks current vs desired state | | **Imperative (Procedural)** | Define *how* to achieve the end state step-by-step | Ansible, Chef, Puppet | User writes explicit execution steps | ### IaC Workflow $$\text{Write Code} \rightarrow \text{Version Control} \rightarrow \text{Review} \rightarrow \text{Plan (Dry Run)} \rightarrow \text{Apply} \rightarrow \text{Verify State}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for DevOps Automation While DevOps is largely process-driven, several quantitative formulas appear in KTU exams. Below is the consolidated cheat sheet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Rule:** This table uses `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe character `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX Expression | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Deployment Frequency (DF)** | $DF = \frac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}}$ | Number of deployments per time unit | deployments / day | | 2 | **Change Failure Rate (CFR)** | $CFR = \frac{N_{failed\_deploys}}{N_{total\_deploys}} \times 100$ | Percentage of deployments causing failures | Percentage (%) | | 3 | **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{recovered,i} - T_{failed,i})}{n}$ | Average downtime per incident | Minutes / Hours | | 4 | **Lead Time for Changes (LTC)** | $LTC = T_{production} - T_{commit}$ | Time from code commit to production deployment | Hours | | 5 | **Test Automation Coverage (TAC)** | $TAC = \frac{N_{automated\_tests}}{N_{total\_tests}} \times 100$ | Proportion of test cases automated | Percentage (%) | | 6 | **Pipeline Success Rate (PSR)** | $PSR = \frac{N_{successful\_runs}}{N_{total\_runs}} \times 100$ | Pipeline reliability indicator | Percentage (%) | | 7 | **Build Duration (BD)** | $BD = T_{end} - T_{start}$ | Total time taken for a build job | Minutes | | 8 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \frac{T_{total\_uptime}}{N_{failures}}$ | System reliability metric | Hours | | 9 | **Resource Utilization Efficiency (RUE)** | $RUE = \frac{U_{actual}}{U_{capacity}} \times 100$ | Infrastructure usage efficiency | Percentage (%) | | 10 | **Rollback Time (RT)** | $RT = T_{restore} - T_{incident\_detect}$ | Time to revert a bad deployment | Seconds | ### Boundary Conditions & Assumptions - **CFR** assumes all failures are detected and recorded. $0 \leq CFR \leq 100$ - **MTTR** assumes uniform incident severity. For skewed data, use median - **PSR** is meaningful only when $N_{total\_runs} \geq 30$ (statistical significance) - **TAC** ideal target is **80–90%** as per industry benchmarks (Google, Amazon) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility DevOps Automation principles power every major technology sector: - **FinTech (PayPal, Stripe):** Deploy 50+ times per day with automated rollback - **E-Commerce (Amazon):** 23,000+ deployments per day (per Amazon engineering blogs) - **Healthcare (Epic Systems):** Automated compliance scanning for HIPAA - **Telecommunications (Ericsson):** Network function automation via IaC - **Aerospace (NASA):** Automated testing of flight control software # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — DORA Performance Score Calculation The KTU 2024 syllabus frequently asks students to **derive composite DevOps performance scores** from raw metrics. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. ### Problem Setup A software company recorded the following data for a quarter (90 days): - Total deployments: **450** - Total failed deployments: **18** - Total downtime incidents: **6** - Total downtime duration: **720 minutes** - Total uptime: **129,600 minutes** (90 days × 24 hr × 60 min) **Required:** Calculate DF, CFR, MTTR, MTBF, and PSR (assuming 450 successful runs out of 468 total runs). --- ### Step 1: Deployment Frequency (DF) $$DF = \frac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}}$$ Substituting the values: $$DF = \frac{450 \text{ deployments}}{90 \text{ days}}$$ $$DF = 5.0 \text{ deployments/day}$$ **Interpretation:** The team deploys **5 times per day**, qualifying as a **High Performer** per DORA benchmarks. --- ### Step 2: Change Failure Rate (CFR) $$CFR = \frac{N_{failed\_deploys}}{N_{total\_deploys}} \times 100$$ Substituting: $$CFR = \frac{18}{450} \times 100$$ $$CFR = 0.04 \times 100$$ $$CFR = 4.0\%$$ **Interpretation:** Only **4%** of deployments cause failures, which is **Elite performer** range (DORA elite: 0–15%). --- ### Step 3: Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) $$MTTR = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{recovered,i} - T_{failed,i})}{n}$$ Given the total downtime is 720 minutes across 6 incidents: $$MTTR = \frac{720 \text{ minutes}}{6 \text{ incidents}}$$ $$MTTR = 120 \text{ minutes/incident}$$ $$MTTR = 2 \text{ hours/incident}$$ **Interpretation:** Average recovery time is **2 hours**, which is within the **High Performer** threshold (DORA high: < 1 day). --- ### Step 4: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) $$MTBF = \frac{T_{total\_uptime}}{N_{failures}}$$ Substituting: $$MTBF = \frac{129{,}600 \text{ minutes}}{6 \text{ failures}}$$ $$MTBF = 21{,}600 \text{ minutes/failure}$$ Converting to hours: $$MTBF = \frac{21{,}600}{60} = 360 \text{ hours/failure}$$ Converting to days: $$MTBF = \frac{360}{24} = 15 \text{ days/failure}$$ **Interpretation:** On average, a failure occurs every **15 days**. --- ### Step 5: Pipeline Success Rate (PSR) $$PSR = \frac{N_{successful\_runs}}{N_{total\_runs}} \times 100$$ $$PSR = \frac{450}{468} \times 100$$ $$PSR = 0.9615 \times 100$$ $$PSR \approx 96.15\%$$ **Interpretation:** Pipeline is highly reliable; target for elite teams is **> 95%**. --- ### Step 6: Composite DORA Performance Classification | Metric | Calculated Value | DORA Elite Range | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | DF | 5.0 / day | On-demand / multiple per day | Elite | | CFR | 4.0% | 0% – 15% | Elite | | MTTR | 2 hours | < 1 hour | High | | MTBF | 15 days | > 30 days | Medium | **Final Verdict:** The team is an **Elite DevOps Performer** overall, with MTTR being the bottleneck for improvement. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection by Automation Phase The following matrix is a KTU exam favorite. It maps automation phases to appropriate tool categories with rationale. | Automation Phase | Tool Category | Specific Tool | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Source Code Management | VCS | Git, GitHub, GitLab | Distributed version control enables branching strategies | | Build Automation | Build Tools | Maven, Gradle, npm | Dependency management + compilation | | Continuous Integration | CI Servers | Jenkins, GitLab CI | Webhook-based pipeline execution | | Unit Testing | Test Frameworks | JUnit, pytest, Mocha | Language-specific test execution | | Integration Testing | API Testing | Postman, REST Assured | Service contract validation | | UI Testing | Browser Automation | Selenium, Cypress | End-to-end user flow simulation | | Performance Testing | Load Testers | JMeter, Gatling | Stress & volume testing | | Code Quality | SAST | SonarQube, Checkstyle | Static analysis for vulnerabilities | | Containerization | Container Engines | Docker, Podman | Environment consistency | | Orchestration | Container Orchestrators | Kubernetes, Docker Swarm | Auto-scaling & self-healing | | IaC (Declarative) | Provisioning | Terraform, Pulumi | Multi-cloud state management | | IaC (Imperative) | Configuration | Ansible, Chef, Puppet | OS & application configuration | | Artifact Storage | Repositories | Nexus, Artifactory | Versioned binary storage | | Deployment | CD Platforms | Spinnaker, ArgoCD | GitOps-based deployment | | Monitoring | Metrics | Prometheus, Datadog | Time-series data collection | | Visualization | Dashboards | Grafana, Kibana | Real-time observability | | Incident Response | Alerting | PagerDuty, Opsgenie | On-call rotation automation | | Logging | Log Aggregation | ELK Stack, Splunk | Centralized log analysis | ## 3.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Deployment Strategy A frequent KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Given scenario X, which deployment strategy should be adopted?"* ``` START │ ├── Is zero-downtime critical? ──── NO ──→ Recreate Strategy │ │ │ YES │ │ ├── Is the user base large? ──── NO ──→ Blue-Green Deployment │ │ │ YES │ │ ├── Is the change risky? ──── NO ──→ Rolling Update │ │ │ YES │ │ └───── Canary Release (gradual rollout to 1%, 5%, 25%, 100%) ``` **Decision Rule (formalized):** $$\text{Strategy} = \begin{cases} \text{Canary} & \text{if } Risk_{change} = High \land Users_{base} \geq 10^6 \\ \text{Blue-Green} & \text{if } Downtime_{tol} = 0 \land Cost_{infra} \leq Budget \\ \text{Rolling} & \text{if } Resource_{constraint} = True \\ \text{Recreate} & \text{if } Downtime_{tol} > 0 \land Cost_{min} = Priority \end{cases}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The DevOps Infinity Loop — High-Level Architecture This diagram represents the **continuous, non-linear nature** of DevOps Automation as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["PLAN
Issue Tracking
Backlog Management"] nodeB["CODE
Version Control
Git Branching"] nodeC["BUILD
Compile + Dependencies
Artifact Creation"] nodeD["TEST
Automated Test Suites
SAST and DAST"] nodeE["RELEASE
Version Tagging
Artifact Promotion"] nodeF["DEPLOY
IaC Provisioning
Canary or Blue-Green"] nodeG["OPERATE
Auto-scaling
Self-healing Systems"] nodeH["MONITOR
Metrics and Logs
Alerting Feedback"] nodeA -->|User Stories| nodeB nodeB -->|Git Push Trigger| nodeC nodeC -->|Artifact Ready| nodeD nodeD -->|Quality Gate Pass| nodeE nodeE -->|Version Approved| nodeF nodeF -->|Live Traffic| nodeG nodeG -->|Telemetry Data| nodeH nodeH -->|Insights and Alerts| nodeA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Each node represents a phase with its primary automation activities. The arrows depict the **automated trigger flow** — no human gatekeeping between phases. The feedback loop from MONITOR back to PLAN is the **continuous improvement engine**. ## 4.2 CI/CD Pipeline — Detailed Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB dev1["Developer Workstation"] repo["Git Repository
GitHub or GitLab"] webhook["Webhook Trigger"] ciserver["CI Server
Jenkins"] build["Build Stage
Maven or Gradle"] unittest["Unit Tests
JUnit and pytest"] integtest["Integration Tests
Postman and Selenium"] sast["SAST Scan
SonarQube"] artifact["Artifact Repository
Nexus or Artifactory"] staging["Staging Environment
Docker Container"] prod["Production
Kubernetes Cluster"] monitor["Monitoring
Prometheus and Grafana"] dev1 -->|git push| repo repo -->|webhook fires| webhook webhook -->|trigger build| ciserver ciserver --> build build --> unittest unittest -->|all green| integtest integtest -->|contracts valid| sast sast -->|no critical issues| artifact artifact -->|deploy image| staging staging -->|smoke tests pass| prod prod -->|expose metrics| monitor monitor -->|alerts| dev1 ``` ## 4.3 Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Workflow ```mermaid graph LR auth["Author
Write HCL or YAML"] ver["Version Control
Git Commit"] rev["Peer Review
Pull Request"] plan["Terraform Plan
Dry Run Diff"] approve["Manual Approval
Optional Gate"] apply["Terraform Apply
Provision Resources"] state["State File
terraform.tfstate"] verify["Verify State
Drift Detection"] auth --> ver ver --> rev rev --> plan plan --> approve approve --> apply apply --> state state --> verify verify -->|drift detected| auth ``` ## 4.4 Deployment Strategy Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Blue-Green Deployment" bg1["Blue Environment
Current Production v1.0"] bg2["Green Environment
New Version v2.0"] bg3["Load Balancer
Switches Traffic"] bg1 --- bg3 bg2 --- bg3 end subgraph "Canary Release" cn1["Stable Pool
99 percent Users on v1.0"] cn2["Canary Pool
1 percent Users on v2.0"] cn3["Metrics Collector
Compares Error Rates"] cn1 --- cn3 cn2 --- cn3 end subgraph "Rolling Update" rl1["Instance 1 v1.0"] rl2["Instance 2 v1.0"] rl3["Instance 3 v2.0"] rl4["Instance 4 v2.0"] rl1 --> rl2 rl2 --> rl3 rl3 --> rl4 end ``` ## 4.5 Monitoring & Feedback Loop — Observability Stack ```mermaid graph TD app1["Application Server"] app2["Database Server"] app3["Container Cluster"] agent["Exporters
Node and cAdvisor"] prom["Prometheus
Time Series DB"] graf["Grafana
Visualization"] alert["Alertmanager
PagerDuty"] logstash["Logstash
Log Pipeline"] es["Elasticsearch
Search Engine"] kibana["Kibana
Log Dashboard"] app1 --> agent app2 --> agent app3 --> agent agent --> prom prom --> graf prom --> alert app1 -->|stdout| logstash app2 -->|stdout| logstash app3 -->|stdout| logstash logstash --> es es --> kibana ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define DevOps Automation. List any four automation tools used in CI/CD pipelines. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** DevOps Automation is the practice of using technology and tooling to programmatically execute repetitive SDLC tasks — including integration, testing, deployment, and infrastructure provisioning — without manual intervention. **Four CI/CD Automation Tools:** 1. **Jenkins** — Open-source CI server for build automation 2. **GitHub Actions** — Native CI/CD within GitHub repositories 3. **Ansible** — Configuration management and deployment automation 4. **Terraform** — Declarative infrastructure provisioning > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** Always define the term first, then enumerate tools with their specific automation role. Avoid generic statements like "DevOps is a culture" — keep answers tool-and-process focused for 3-mark questions. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Continuous Integration (CI) | Continuous Delivery (CD) | |---|---|---| | **Primary Focus** | Merging code changes frequently | Ensuring code is always deployable | | **Trigger** | Developer `git push` | Successful CI build completion | | **Automation Scope** | Build + Unit + Integration tests | Build + Test + Deploy to staging | | **Manual Gate** | None | Manual approval before production | | **Output** | Verified artifact | Production-ready artifact | | **Frequency** | Multiple times per day | Daily or per feature completion | **Key Distinction:** CI is about **integration automation**; CD is about **delivery automation**. In **Continuous Deployment** (the hyphen-less variant), production deployment is also fully automated with no manual gate. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): DevOps Automation Architecture and Pipeline Design `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the DevOps lifecycle with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment in detail. **Model Answer:** #### DevOps Lifecycle Overview The DevOps lifecycle is represented as a **continuous infinity loop (∞)** symbolizing the iterative, non-terminating nature of modern software delivery. The eight phases are: 1. **PLAN** — Define features, user stories, sprint goals 2. **CODE** — Develop using version-controlled repositories 3. **BUILD** — Compile source, resolve dependencies, create artifacts 4. **TEST** — Execute automated test suites 5. **RELEASE** — Tag, version, and approve artifacts 6. **DEPLOY** — Provision infrastructure and release to environments 7. **OPERATE** — Manage production workloads with auto-scaling 8. **MONITOR** — Collect metrics, logs, and traces for observability #### Role of Continuous Integration (CI) CI automates the **merging, building, and testing** of code from multiple contributors. Key activities: - **Automated Triggers:** Every `git push` activates a webhook - **Build Verification:** Compilation must succeed before tests run - **Fast Feedback:** Developers receive test results within minutes - **Quality Gates:** Static analysis (SonarQube) blocks bad code - **Artifact Creation:** Produces immutable, versioned binaries **Benefits:** Reduces integration conflicts, catches bugs early, improves code quality. #### Role of Continuous Deployment (CD) CD automates the **release of validated artifacts to production environments**. Key activities: - **Environment Promotion:** Dev → Staging → Production - **Deployment Strategies:** Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling updates - **Automated Rollback:** Failure detection triggers instant revert - **GitOps Integration:** Git repository is the single source of truth **Benefits:** Faster time-to-market, reduced manual errors, higher release confidence. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss Infrastructure as Code (IaC). Compare declarative and imperative IaC approaches with suitable examples. **Model Answer:** #### Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — Definition IaC is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through **machine-readable definition files** (HCL, YAML, JSON) rather than manual console clicks or imperative scripts. #### Declarative (Functional) Approach In the declarative approach, the engineer specifies the **desired end state**, and the IaC tool figures out *how* to achieve it. **Example (Terraform HCL):** ```hcl resource "aws_instance" "web_server" { ami = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0" instance_type = "t2.micro" tags = { Name = "WebServer" Environment = "Production" } } ``` **Key Characteristics:** - Tool maintains a **state file** (`terraform.tfstate`) tracking current vs. desired state - **Idempotent** — Running the same code multiple times yields the same result - Self-healing — Tool detects drift and reconciles automatically **Tools:** Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, Pulumi #### Imperative (Procedural) Approach In the imperative approach, the engineer writes **explicit step-by-step instructions** describing *how* to reach the desired state. **Example (Ansible YAML Playbook):** ```yaml - name: Install and start Nginx hosts: web_servers tasks: - name: Install Nginx package apt: name: nginx state: present - name: Start Nginx service service: name: nginx state: started ``` **Key Characteristics:** - Execution order is critical and explicitly defined - No built-in state tracking — user must ensure idempotency - More flexible for complex orchestration logic **Tools:** Ansible, Chef, Puppet, SaltStack #### Comparative Analysis | Parameter | Declarative (Terraform) | Imperative (Ansible) | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Desired end state | Execution steps | | **State Management** | Built-in state file | External/manual | | **Idempotency** | Automatic | Manual via modules | | **Learning Curve** | Moderate | Lower (YAML-based) | | **Best For** | Cloud provisioning | Configuration management | | **Drift Detection** | Yes (terraform plan) | No (must re-run) | **Conclusion:** Declarative IaC is preferred for **infrastructure provisioning** (VMs, networks, databases), while imperative IaC excels at **configuration management** (software installation, OS tuning). Many enterprises use both in tandem — Terraform for provisioning + Ansible for configuration. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: DevOps Metrics, Monitoring, and Deployment Strategies `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four key DORA metrics for measuring DevOps performance. A team made 200 deployments in 60 days, with 10 failures and total downtime of 600 minutes. Calculate the Deployment Frequency, Change Failure Rate, and MTTR. **Model Answer:** #### The Four DORA Metrics (Google DevOps Research and Assessment) 1. **Deployment Frequency (DF):** How often an organization releases changes to production. Higher is better. 2. **Lead Time for Changes (LTC):** Time elapsed from code commit to successful production deployment. Lower is better. 3. **Change Failure Rate (CFR):** Percentage of deployments causing a failure in production. Lower is better. 4. **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR):** Average time to restore service after a production incident. Lower is better. **Performance Classification:** | Metric | Elite | High | Medium | Low | |---|---|---|---|---| | DF | On-demand (multiple/day) | Weekly to monthly | Monthly to semi-annually | > 6 months | | LTC | < 1 hour | 1 day – 1 week | 1 week – 1 month | > 6 months | | CFR | 0% – 15% | 16% – 30% | 31% – 45% | > 45% | | MTTR | < 1 hour | < 1 day | < 1 week | > 1 month | #### Calculations **Given Data:** - $N_{deployments} = 200$ - $T_{period} = 60 \text{ days}$ - $N_{failed\_deploys} = 10$ - $T_{total\_downtime} = 600 \text{ minutes}$ - $N_{incidents} = 10$ (assumed equal to failed deployments) **Step 1: Deployment Frequency** $$DF = \frac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}} = \frac{200}{60}$$ $$DF \approx 3.33 \text{ deployments/day}$$ This is an **Elite Performer** level (multiple deployments per day). **Step 2: Change Failure Rate** $$CFR = \frac{N_{failed\_deploys}}{N_{total\_deploys}} \times 100 = \frac{10}{200} \times 100$$ $$CFR = 5.0\%$$ This is **Elite** (range: 0% – 15%). **Step 3: MTTR** $$MTTR = \frac{T_{total\_downtime}}{N_{incidents}} = \frac{600}{10}$$ $$MTTR = 60 \text{ minutes/incident} = 1 \text{ hour/incident}$$ This is **Elite** (range: < 1 hour). **Conclusion:** The team is an **Elite DevOps Performer** on all three calculated metrics. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Blue-Green Deployment, Canary Release, and Rolling Update strategies. Under what conditions is each strategy most appropriate? **Model Answer:** #### Strategy 1: Blue-Green Deployment **Mechanism:** Maintain two identical production environments — **Blue** (current live) and **Green** (new version). Deploy new code to Green, test it, then switch the load balancer to route 100% traffic to Green. Blue becomes the new standby. **Advantages:** - Instant rollback by switching load balancer back to Blue - Zero downtime during deployment - Full production testing on Green before cutover **Disadvantages:** - Requires **2x infrastructure cost** (both environments running) - Database schema changes are complex - Switchover is binary — no gradual validation **Best Suited For:** - Mission-critical applications requiring zero downtime - Short release windows (e.g., banking transaction systems) - Scenarios where instant rollback is mandatory #### Strategy 2: Canary Release **Mechanism:** Deploy the new version to a **small subset of users** (1% → 5% → 25% → 100%) while the majority continues using the old version. Monitor error rates, latency, and business KPIs at each stage before progressing. **Advantages:** - Risk mitigation through gradual exposure - Real-user feedback before full rollout - A/B testing capability built-in **Disadvantages:** - Complex traffic routing logic required - Longer total deployment time - Session affinity issues for stateful applications **Best Suited For:** - Large user bases (millions of users) - High-risk changes (algorithm updates, ML model rollouts) - Feature experiments requiring metric comparison #### Strategy 3: Rolling Update **Mechanism:** Gradually replace instances of the old version with the new version in **batches**. E.g., with 10 instances, replace 2 at a time until all 10 are updated. **Advantages:** - No extra infrastructure cost (reuses existing capacity) - Continuous availability throughout deployment - Native support in Kubernetes (`Deployment` resource) **Disadvantages:** - Slower than Blue-Green - Mixed-version coexistence may cause compatibility bugs - Rollback is gradual, not instant **Best Suited For:** - Resource-constrained environments - Stateless microservices - Kubernetes-native applications #### Comparative Summary | Parameter | Blue-Green | Canary | Rolling Update | |---|---|---|---| | **Downtime** | Zero | Zero | Zero | | **Infrastructure Cost** | 2x | 1.1x – 1.5x | 1x | | **Rollback Speed** | Instant (seconds) | Fast (minutes) | Gradual (minutes to hours) | | **Risk Exposure** | Full at switchover | Gradual | Incremental batches | | **Complexity** | Medium | High | Low | | **Best Use Case** | Critical apps, zero-downtime SLA | Large-scale, risky changes | Kubernetes, stateless services | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing "Continuous Delivery" with "Continuous Deployment":** CD (with delivery) has a manual approval gate before production; CD (with deployment) is fully automated. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation. > 2. **Writing only tool names without describing their role:** Listing "Jenkins, Docker, Kubernetes" without explaining *what each automates* loses half the marks. > 3. **Skipping numerical calculations in metric questions:** If the question provides numbers, you MUST show the formula, substitution, and final answer with units. Skipping steps costs 2–3 marks. > 4. **Drawing vague diagrams:** Pipeline diagrams must show specific tools, stages, and arrow labels. Generic boxes labeled "Build" and "Test" without context lose marks. > 5. **Ignoring the feedback loop:** DevOps is a closed loop. Any lifecycle diagram without the MONITOR → PLAN feedback arrow is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DevOps Automation** ### Core Concepts (Must Memorize) - ✅ **DevOps Automation** = Programmatic execution of SDLC tasks without manual intervention - ✅ **CI (Continuous Integration)** = Automated merge + build + test on every code change - ✅ **CD (Continuous Delivery)** = Automated deployment up to staging with manual production gate - ✅ **CD (Continuous Deployment)** = Fully automated deployment to production, no manual gate - ✅ **CT (Continuous Testing)** = Automated test execution (unit, integration, E2E, performance) - ✅ **CM (Continuous Monitoring)** = Real-time observability via metrics, logs, and traces - ✅ **IaC (Infrastructure as Code)** = Machine-readable infrastructure definitions ### Key Formulas (Must Know for Calculations) - ✅ $DF = \frac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}}$ - ✅ $CFR = \frac{N_{failed}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ - ✅ $MTTR = \frac{\sum T_{downtime}}{N_{incidents}}$ - ✅ $MTBF = \frac{T_{uptime}}{N_{failures}}$ - ✅ $PSR = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ - ✅ $TAC = \frac{N_{automated}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ ### Essential Tools (Map to Phases) - ✅ **Version Control:** Git, GitHub, GitLab - ✅ **CI Servers:** Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI - ✅ **Build Tools:** Maven, Gradle, npm, pip - ✅ **Testing:** JUnit, Selenium, pytest, JMeter, Postman - ✅ **Containers:** Docker, Podman - ✅ **Orchestration:** Kubernetes, Docker Swarm - ✅ **IaC — Declarative:** Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi - ✅ **IaC — Imperative:** Ansible, Chef, Puppet - ✅ **Monitoring:** Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack, Datadog ### Deployment Strategies (Know Trade-offs) - ✅ **Blue-Green:** Zero downtime, 2x cost, instant rollback - ✅ **Canary:** Gradual rollout, low risk, requires traffic routing logic - ✅ **Rolling Update:** Incremental replacement, cost-efficient, slower rollback - ✅ **Recreate:** Simplest, has downtime, lowest cost ### DORA Performance Benchmarks (Elite Tier) - ✅ **DF:** Multiple per day (on-demand) - ✅ **LTC:** < 1 hour - ✅ **CFR:** 0% – 15% - ✅ **MTTR:** < 1 hour ### Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns - ✅ "Explain DevOps lifecycle with diagram" → Draw ∞-loop with all 8 phases + feedback arrow - ✅ "Compare CI vs CD" → Use comparison table with 6+ parameters - ✅ "Calculate DORA metrics from given data" → Show all 3–4 formulas with substitution - ✅ "Discuss IaC with declarative/imperative examples" → Include HCL and YAML snippets - ✅ "Compare deployment strategies" → Cover Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling, Recreate ### Quick Mnemonics - ✅ **CI/CD/CT/CM/IaC Pillars:** "**I**nfrastructure → **M**onitor → **T**est → **C**I → **D**eploy" (I-MTCD) - ✅ **Pipeline Stages:** "**S**ource → **B**uild → **T**est → **A**nalyze → **D**eploy → **M**onitor" (SBTADM) - ✅ **DORA Metrics:** "**D**eployment, **L**ead time, **C**hange failure, **M**TTR" (DLCM)

topics.title: Continuous Integration, Delivery, and Deployment (CI/CD/CD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Continuous Integration, Delivery, and Deployment (CI/CD/CD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Continuous Integration (CI)

**Continuous Integration (CI)** is a software development practice in which members of a team integrate their work frequently — usually each person integrates at least **daily** — leading to multiple integrations per day. Each integration is verified by an **automated build** and **automated test sequence** to detect integration errors as quickly as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** CI is the practice of merging all developer working copies to a shared mainline several times a day, accompanied by automated builds and tests to validate every change.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a busy restaurant where multiple chefs are preparing different parts of a dish — one chef grills the meat, another prepares the sauce, a third chops vegetables. Without **CI**, each chef cooks in isolation, and at the end of the day, the head chef tries to combine everything — leading to disasters (overcooked meat, cold sauce, mismatched portions).

With **CI**, every 30 minutes, the chefs bring their work-in-progress to a **central testing station** (the automated build server). A small tasting is performed. If anything tastes wrong, the chef gets instant feedback. This ensures that the final dish, when assembled, is harmonious. In software terms, every code commit is **immediately compiled, tested, and validated**.

## 1.2 Continuous Delivery (CD)

**Continuous Delivery** is a software engineering approach in which teams produce software in short cycles, ensuring that the software can be **reliably released at any time**. It aims at building, testing, and releasing software with **greater speed and frequency**. The key distinction is that the **release to production is a manual business decision**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Continuous Delivery, the artifact is *ready* for production deployment, but a human clicks the "deploy" button. The pipeline ends at a *staging* or *pre-production* environment.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Packaged Courier"

Think of an e-commerce company that pre-packages your order, prints the label, weighs it, and parks the courier at the dispatch bay — fully ready to leave. However, the dispatch manager waits for your confirmation call before releasing the van. The package **can be sent instantly**, but the human-controlled "go-live" decision remains.

## 1.3 Continuous Deployment (CD)

**Continuous Deployment** goes one step further than Continuous Delivery. Every change that passes all stages of the production pipeline is **released to customers automatically — without explicit human approval**. There is no "release day"; every successful commit becomes a production update.

> [!WARNING]
> Continuous Deployment requires a **highly mature test automation suite**, robust monitoring, and feature flags. Without them, a single bad commit can break production for all users.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Fully Automatic Vending Machine"

A vending machine accepts money, dispenses the product, returns change, and even self-diagnoses issues — **all without a human operator pressing any button** after the customer pays. Each validated transaction is auto-finalized. Similarly, in Continuous Deployment, each validated commit auto-finalizes as a production release.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars — At a Glance

| Practice | Build & Test | Deploy to Staging | Deploy to Production | Human Gate? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Integration** | Automatic | No | No | N/A |
| **Continuous Delivery** | Automatic | Automatic | **Manual** | Yes |
| **Continuous Deployment** | Automatic | Automatic | **Automatic** | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **"I-B" (Integration Builds), "D-Delivery-Decide" (Delivery waits for decision), "D-Deployment-Do" (Deployment does it automatically).**

## 1.5 Historical Context & Industry Relevance

The term **Continuous Integration** was introduced by **Grady Booch** in his 1991 method, although he did not advocate integrating multiple times a day. The practice was later refined by **Kent Beck** (Extreme Programming, 1996) and **Martin Fowler**, who published the seminal article *"Continuous Integration"* in 2000, formalizing the modern definition used in KTU curricula.

Modern industry tools include **Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, CircleCI, Travis CI, Azure DevOps, and Bamboo**, which form the backbone of **DevOps pipelines** in 78% of Fortune 500 companies (as of 2024 industry surveys).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline Architecture

A CI/CD pipeline is a series of automated steps that move code from a developer's workstation to production. The **canonical pipeline** consists of the following stages:

### Stage 1: Source Code Commit (Version Control Trigger)
- Developer pushes code to a shared repository (Git, SVN, Mercurial).
- A **webhook** triggers the CI server.
- **Key Metric:** Time-to-trigger latency should be **< 5 seconds**.

### Stage 2: Automated Build (Compilation)
- The CI server pulls the latest code and compiles it.
- Dependency resolution (Maven, npm, pip) occurs here.
- **Key Metric:** Build duration is tracked; builds exceeding **15 minutes** are considered a "KTU Anti-Pattern" indicating excessive monolithic compilation.

### Stage 3: Automated Unit Testing
- Unit tests are executed against the freshly built artifact.
- Coverage threshold: industry standard is **≥ 80%** code coverage.
- Failing tests **halt the pipeline** and notify the developer via email/Slack.

### Stage 4: Static Code Analysis
- Tools like **SonarQube, ESLint, PMD** scan for code smells, security vulnerabilities, and style violations.
- Quality gates enforce thresholds (e.g., **technical debt ratio < 5%**).

### Stage 5: Integration & Functional Testing
- Multi-service integration tests, API contract tests, and end-to-end (E2E) tests run.
- Tools: **Selenium, Cypress, Postman/Newman, JUnit, TestNG**.

### Stage 6: Artifact Storage
- A versioned, immutable artifact (JAR, WAR, Docker image, npm package) is stored in a repository like **Nexus, Artifactory, or Docker Hub**.
- **Key Tag:** Semantic versioning (`v1.4.2`).

### Stage 7: Deployment to Staging (Delivery) / Production (Deployment)
- Blue-Green, Canary, or Rolling deployment strategies are used.

## 2.2 Core Theoretical Principles (Martin Fowler's CI Tenets)

1. **Maintain a single source repository** — All code lives in one version-controlled location.
2. **Automate the build** — A single command must produce a deployable artifact.
3. **Make the build self-testing** — Tests must run automatically on every build.
4. **Everyone commits to the mainline every day** — Frequent integration reduces merge hell.
5. **Every commit builds on an integration machine** — Triggered via webhooks, not manually.
6. **Keep the build fast** — Aiming for the **10-minute build rule**.
7. **Test in a clone of the production environment** — Environmental parity.
8. **Make it easy to get the latest deliverables** — Artifacts accessible to QA, PMs.
9. **Everyone can see what's happening** — Transparency via dashboards.
10. **Automate deployment** — Reduces manual errors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Description | Standard / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum \text{Downtime}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | Average time to restore service after an outage. | < 1 hour (industry best) |
| 2 | **Deployment Frequency (DF)** | $\text{DF} = \frac{\text{Deployments}}{\text{Time Period}}$ | Number of deployments per day/week. | Daily to multiple per day |
| 3 | **Change Lead Time (CLT)** | $\text{CLT} = t_{\text{deploy}} - t_{\text{commit}}$ | Time from code commit to production deploy. | < 1 day (Elite performers) |
| 4 | **Change Failure Rate (CFR)** | $\text{CFR} = \frac{\text{Failed Deployments}}{\text{Total Deployments}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of changes causing production failures. | 0% – 15% (Elite) |
| 5 | **Pipeline Success Rate (PSR)** | $\text{PSR} = \frac{\text{Successful Builds}}{\text{Total Builds}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability of the CI pipeline. | ≥ 90% (target) |
| 6 | **Code Coverage (CC)** | $\text{CC} = \frac{\text{Lines Covered by Tests}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of code exercised by automated tests. | ≥ 80% |
| 7 | **Build Duration (BD)** | Empirical (timed) | Time taken for automated build to complete. | ≤ 10 minutes (Fowler's rule) |
| 8 | **Test Execution Ratio (TER)** | $\text{TER} = \frac{\text{Automated Tests}}{\text{Manual Tests}}$ | Ratio of automated vs manual test execution. | ≥ 4:1 (mature pipeline) |
| 9 | **Rollback Time (RBT)** | Empirical (timed) | Time to revert a faulty release. | ≤ 5 minutes (with blue-green) |
| 10 | **Lead Time for Changes** | $\text{LT} = t_{\text{production}} - t_{\text{first commit}}$ | Holistic throughput metric (DORA). | < 1 hour (Elite) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DORA Metrics** (MTTR, DF, CLT, CFR) are the **four key DevOps performance indicators** defined by Google's DevOps Research and Assessment team. KTU examiners frequently test these four in Part B questions.

## 2.4 The Why — Engineering Value Proposition

| Practice | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **CI** | Reduces integration risk, detects bugs within minutes (vs. weeks), eliminates "merge hell." |
| **Continuous Delivery** | Decouples deploy from release, reduces last-minute panic, enables business-driven release timing. |
| **Continuous Deployment** | Eliminates human error in release, accelerates feedback loops, enables true A/B experimentation. |
| **Combined CI/CD/CD** | Establishes a **reproducible, auditable, and reversible** deployment process — critical for SRE and regulated industries (BFSI, healthcare). |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing MTTR for a Sample CI/CD Pipeline

This is a frequently tested **calculation-type question** in KTU examinations. We will demonstrate the complete derivation.

### Problem Statement

A software team recorded the following production outages last quarter (Q1):

| Incident # | Downtime Duration (hours) | Triggered By |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.5 | Failed Deployment |
| 2 | 0.75 | Failed Deployment |
| 3 | 4.0 | Infrastructure |
| 4 | 1.25 | Failed Deployment |
| 5 | 0.5 | Infrastructure |
| 6 | 3.0 | Failed Deployment |

Calculate the **MTTR**, **Change Failure Rate (CFR)**, and **Deployment Frequency (DF)** given that there were **120 total deployments** during the quarter.

---

### Derivation of MTTR

The general formula for Mean Time to Recovery is:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{\text{down},i}}{n}
$$

Where $t_{\text{down},i}$ is the downtime of the $i$-th incident and $n$ is the total number of incidents.

**Step 1: Sum the downtimes of all 6 incidents.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t_{\text{down}} &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6 \\
&= 2.5 + 0.75 + 4.0 + 1.25 + 0.5 + 3.0 \\
&= 12.0 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Count the number of incidents.**

$$
n = 6
$$

**Step 3: Compute the MTTR by dividing the total downtime by the number of incidents.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{12.0}{6} \\
&= 2.0 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the team takes **2 hours** to recover from any production incident. This falls in the **"High" performer band** of the DORA report (acceptable for KTU credit).

---

### Derivation of Change Failure Rate (CFR)

The general formula is:

$$
\text{CFR} = \frac{D_{\text{failed}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1: Count the failed deployments from the table.**

Failed deployments correspond to incidents triggered by "Failed Deployment": incidents 1, 2, 4, and 6.

$$
D_{\text{failed}} = 4
$$

**Step 2: Identify the total deployments.**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 120
$$

**Step 3: Compute the CFR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFR} &= \frac{4}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.0333... \times 100\% \\
&= 3.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A CFR of **3.33%** places the team in the **Elite performer** band (0%–15%), which is the highest tier in the DORA framework.

---

### Derivation of Deployment Frequency (DF)

$$
\text{DF} = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{period}}}
$$

**Step 1: Identify the time period.**

A quarter = 3 months ≈ **90 days**.

**Step 2: Compute the daily deployment frequency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DF}_{\text{daily}} &= \frac{120}{90} \\
&= 1.33 \text{ deployments/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Express the monthly rate for examiner convenience.**

$$
\text{DF}_{\text{monthly}} = \frac{120}{3} = 40 \text{ deployments/month}
$$

**Interpretation:** The team deploys roughly **1.33 times per day**, satisfying the KTU benchmark for a "mature" CI/CD practice.

---

### Derivation of Change Lead Time (CLT)

Assume the team's average commit-to-deploy window is **6 hours**.

$$
\text{CLT} = t_{\text{deploy}} - t_{\text{commit}} = 6 \text{ hours}
$$

This is well within the **"Elite" threshold** of less than 1 day.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Deployment Strategies

| Strategy | Risk Level | Rollback Speed | Cost | KTU Exam Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Big Bang** | High | Slow (≥ hours) | Low | Legacy systems |
| **Rolling Update** | Medium | Medium (minutes) | Low | Stateless services |
| **Blue-Green** | Low | Instant (seconds) | High (2x infra) | High-availability apps |
| **Canary** | Very Low | Fast (minutes) | Medium | User-facing, experimental |
| **Feature Flags** | Very Low | Instant (config flip) | Low | Continuous Deployment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blue-Green** is the strategy that **Continuous Deployment** relies upon most heavily, because it allows instant rollback by simply re-routing traffic back to the green (old) environment if the blue (new) fails.

## 3.3 Maturity Model (KTU Theoretical Framework)

The **CI/CD Maturity Model** is a 5-stage progression. KTU questions often ask students to "identify the maturity level" of a given scenario.

| Level | Description | Marking Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 — Manual** | No automation, manual builds, manual deploys. | "Initial / Ad-hoc" |
| **Level 2 — Continuous Integration** | Automated builds + tests, but manual deploys. | "Build automation exists" |
| **Level 3 — Continuous Delivery** | Automated deploys to staging; production is manual. | "Artifact is always deployable" |
| **Level 4 — Continuous Deployment** | Fully automated deploys to production. | "No human gate" |
| **Level 5 — Continuous Verification** | AI-driven monitoring, auto-rollback on anomaly. | "Self-healing pipeline" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End CI/CD/CD Pipeline Architecture

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **canonical pipeline** from developer commit to production release, including the human decision gate that distinguishes Delivery from Deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Commits Code] --> B[Git Repository]
    B --> C{Webhook Trigger}
    C --> D[CI Server Pulls Code]
    D --> E[Automated Build and Compile]
    E --> F{Build Success?}
    F -->|No| G[Notify Developer Failure]
    G --> A
    F -->|Yes| H[Run Unit Tests]
    H --> I{All Tests Pass?}
    I -->|No| G
    I -->|Yes| J[Static Code Analysis]
    J --> K{Quality Gate Met?}
    K -->|No| G
    K -->|Yes| L[Integration and E2E Tests]
    L --> M{Integration OK?}
    M -->|No| G
    M -->|Yes| N[Store Artifact in Nexus or Artifactory]
    N --> O[Deploy to Staging Environment]
    O --> P[Smoke and UAT Tests in Staging]
    P --> Q{Staging OK?}
    Q -->|No| G
    Q -->|Yes| R{Delivery or Deployment Mode}
    R -->|Delivery| S[Manual Business Approval]
    S --> T[Production Deploy]
    R -->|Deployment| T
    T --> U[Monitor Production Metrics]
    U --> V{Anomaly Detected?}
    V -->|Yes| W[Auto Rollback via Blue-Green]
    V -->|No| X[Release Complete]
    W --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The diamond nodes (`{}`) are decision points.
- The path `R → T` (skipping the manual gate) represents **Continuous Deployment**.
- The path `R → S → T` represents **Continuous Delivery**.
- The feedback loops back to `Developer Commits Code` represent the iterative nature of CI.

---

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: CI vs. CD (Delivery) vs. CD (Deployment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CI[Continuous Integration Scope]
        CI1[Code Commit] --> CI2[Build] --> CI3[Unit Tests] --> CI4[Integration Tests]
    end
    subgraph DEL[Continuous Delivery Scope]
        DEL1[Staging Deploy] --> DEL2[UAT] --> DEL3[Manual Approval]
    end
    subgraph DEP[Continuous Deployment Scope]
        DEP1[Production Deploy] --> DEP2[Monitoring] --> DEP3[Auto Rollback]
    end
    CI4 --> DEL1
    DEL3 --> DEP1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **scope of CI ends at integration testing**.
- **Continuous Delivery extends the pipeline to include staging + a manual gate**.
- **Continuous Deployment extends it further to include fully automated production deploy and self-healing rollback**.

---

## 4.3 Feedback Loop Schematic (KTU High-Yield Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan] --> P2[Code] --> P3[Build] --> P4[Test] --> P5[Release] --> P6[Deploy] --> P7[Operate] --> P8[Monitor]
    P8 -->|Feedback| P1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is the **DevOps Infinite Loop**, the conceptual bedrock of CI/CD/CD.
- The `Monitor → Plan` feedback arc represents **continuous improvement** — production telemetry informs the next planning cycle.
- KTU examiners often use this to test whether students can distinguish **CI/CD practices from the broader DevOps philosophy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Continuous Delivery | Continuous Deployment |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Software is always kept in a deployable state; release to production is a **manual business decision**. | Every change passing automated tests is **automatically deployed** to production without human intervention. |
| **Human Gate** | Yes — manual approval before production release. | No — fully automated. |
| **Example** | An e-commerce site that is auto-built and tested, but a release manager clicks "Deploy to Production" on Friday afternoon. | A SaaS platform (e.g., Google Docs) where every validated commit becomes live for users within minutes. |
| **Risk Profile** | Lower (human checkpoint). | Higher (requires mature test suite and monitoring). |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for the example distinction.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three benefits of adopting Continuous Integration in a software project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Early Bug Detection:** Integration errors and regressions are detected within minutes of being introduced, rather than weeks later during system testing.
2. **Reduced Integration Risk:** Frequent small commits avoid the "merge hell" associated with long-lived feature branches, lowering the cost of integration.
3. **Improved Code Quality:** Automated unit tests and static code analysis enforce quality gates on every commit, ensuring consistent standards.
4. *(Optional third variation — bonus point in KTU valuation)* **Faster Feedback Loops:** Developers receive immediate validation of their changes, accelerating learning and reducing context switching.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically split into two 7-mark sub-questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of a typical CI/CD pipeline with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each stage in ensuring software quality.

**Model Answer:**

A **CI/CD pipeline** is an automated workflow that takes source code from a developer's machine, validates it through a series of quality gates, and delivers it to end users. The architecture consists of the following stages:

1. **Source Stage (Version Control Trigger):**
   The developer pushes code to a shared Git repository (e.g., GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket). A **webhook** configured on the repository notifies the CI server (e.g., Jenkins) of the new commit.

   *Role:* Initiates the pipeline and ensures traceability of every change.

2. **Build Stage (Compilation):**
   The CI server checks out the latest code and compiles it using build tools (Maven for Java, Gradle, npm for Node.js, etc.). Dependencies are resolved automatically.

   *Role:* Produces a runnable artifact (JAR, WAR, Docker image) and catches compilation errors early.

3. **Test Stage (Automated Testing):**
   - **Unit Tests** validate individual functions/methods.
   - **Integration Tests** validate interactions between modules.
   - **Static Code Analysis** (SonarQube) detects code smells, security vulnerabilities, and style violations.

   *Role:* Enforces functional correctness and code quality. Coverage threshold ≥ 80%.

4. **Artifact Storage Stage:**
   The validated artifact is versioned (e.g., `v1.4.2`) and stored in a binary repository (Nexus, Artifactory) for traceability and reuse.

5. **Deployment Stage (Staging → Production):**
   - **Continuous Delivery:** Deploys to a staging environment; production release requires manual approval.
   - **Continuous Deployment:** Auto-promotes to production if all tests pass.

6. **Monitoring and Feedback Stage:**
   Production metrics (latency, error rates, CPU usage) are collected using tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic. Failures trigger auto-rollback.

*Diagram: Refer to the pipeline architecture in Section 4.1 above.*

**Quality Assurance Role:** Each stage acts as a **filter**, progressively eliminating defects. The pipeline is only as strong as its weakest gate, so quality must be enforced at every stage — not just at the end. *(7 marks: 1 for overview + 2 for stages + 2 for QA role + 2 for diagram explanation.)*

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** A development team had 200 deployments in the last quarter. Out of these, 12 caused production failures. The total downtime due to these failures was 36 hours spread across 8 incidents. Compute the **Deployment Frequency (DF), Change Failure Rate (CFR), and Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)**. Also classify the team's performance using the DORA metrics framework.

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Total deployments: $D_{\text{total}} = 200$
- Failed deployments: $D_{\text{failed}} = 12$
- Total downtime: $\sum t_{\text{down}} = 36$ hours
- Number of incidents: $n = 8$
- Quarter duration: $T = 90$ days (3 months)

---

**Step 1: Deployment Frequency (DF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DF} &= \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{T} \\
&= \frac{200}{90} \\
&\approx 2.22 \text{ deployments/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2: Change Failure Rate (CFR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFR} &= \frac{D_{\text{failed}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{12}{200} \times 100\% \\
&= 6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{\sum t_{\text{down}}}{n} \\
&= \frac{36}{8} \\
&= 4.5 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: DORA Classification**

| Metric | Value | DORA Band | KTU Grade |
|---|---|---|---|
| DF | 2.22/day | **High** | 2 marks |
| CFR | 6% | **Elite** | 2 marks |
| MTTR | 4.5 hours | **High** | 2 marks |
| CLT | Assume 12 hrs (given) | **High** | 1 mark |

**Conclusion:** The team is a **"High Performer"** in DevOps maturity — close to Elite but with room for improvement in MTTR (could be reduced via better observability and automated rollback). *(7 marks: 1 per metric + 1 for classification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **Blue-Green Deployment** and **Canary Deployment** strategies. Mention one real-world scenario where each is most appropriate.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Blue-Green Deployment | Canary Deployment |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Two identical environments (Blue = old, Green = new) coexist; traffic is switched atomically. | New version is released to a small subset of users (e.g., 5%) first, then gradually rolled out. |
| **Rollback Speed** | Instant (route traffic back to Blue). | Fast (stop canary, route back to stable). |
| **Risk Exposure** | All users affected if Green fails (atomic switch). | Only canary users affected initially. |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | High — requires 2x environment. | Medium — scaled gradually. |
| **Suitability** | Mission-critical apps needing zero-downtime. | Apps with large user bases for staged rollouts. |
| **Real-World Scenario** | Banking transaction systems, healthcare apps. | Social media platforms (Facebook feature rollouts). |

*Exam Tip (7 marks: 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for real-world scenarios, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **Continuous Maturity Model** in DevOps. List any **four metrics** used to assess CI/CD pipeline effectiveness and justify their importance.

**Model Answer:**

The **Continuous Maturity Model** is a multi-stage framework that assesses how deeply an organization has adopted DevOps practices. The five levels are:

1. **Level 1 — Initial/Ad-hoc:** No automation; manual builds and deployments.
2. **Level 2 — Continuous Integration:** Automated builds and tests triggered on commit.
3. **Level 3 — Continuous Delivery:** Automated deployment to staging; manual production release.
4. **Level 4 — Continuous Deployment:** Fully automated production deployments.
5. **Level 5 — Continuous Verification:** AI-driven monitoring, anomaly detection, and self-healing.

---

**Four Key Metrics for CI/CD Effectiveness:**

1. **Deployment Frequency (DF):** Measures how often the team deploys to production. High DF indicates agility and low-risk release practices. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR):** Measures resilience — how quickly the team recovers from failures. Low MTTR indicates strong observability and rollback capability. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Change Failure Rate (CFR):** Measures quality of releases. Low CFR indicates robust test suites and quality gates. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Change Lead Time (CLT):** Measures velocity — time from commit to production. Low CLT indicates a streamlined pipeline. *(1.5 marks)*

**Justification:** These four metrics (the **DORA Four Keys**) collectively capture the **Speed, Stability, and Quality** of a DevOps pipeline, providing a holistic view of CI/CD effectiveness. *(1 mark for the holistic conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Delivery with Deployment:** Many students interchange these terms. Always clarify: Delivery = manual gate; Deployment = no gate.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in MTTR:** Always specify hours/minutes — losing 0.5 marks for missing units is common.
> 3. **Mixing CI with DevOps:** CI is a *practice* under the broader *philosophy* of DevOps. Examiners deduct marks for treating them as synonyms.
> 4. **Missing the "Why":** Merely listing pipeline stages without explaining their QA role loses 2-3 marks in Part B answers.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** A neatly labeled pipeline diagram fetches **2 easy marks** in any Part B question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):**

- ✅ **CI = Build + Test Automation**; **CD = Delivery (manual) vs Deployment (auto)**.
- ✅ CI was popularized by **Martin Fowler (2000)**; evolved from **Kent Beck's XP (1996)**.
- ✅ The **DORA Four Keys** are: **DF, CLT, CFR, MTTR** — must be memorized for numerical questions.
- ✅ **Elite DORA Benchmarks:** DF = on-demand/multiple per day; CLT < 1 day; CFR 0-15%; MTTR < 1 hour.
- ✅ **Blue-Green = 2x infra, instant rollback**; **Canary = gradual rollout, lower risk**.
- ✅ **Build Duration Rule:** Aim for ≤ 10 minutes (Fowler).
- ✅ **Code Coverage Threshold:** ≥ 80% for production-grade pipelines.
- ✅ **Pipeline Stages (in order):** Commit → Build → Unit Test → Static Analysis → Integration Test → Artifact Store → Deploy.
- ✅ **MTTR Formula:** $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum t_{\text{down}}}{n}$; **CFR Formula:** $\text{CFR} = \frac{D_{\text{failed}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Maturity Levels (5):** Initial → CI → Delivery → Deployment → Continuous Verification.
- ✅ **Tools to Remember:** Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, SonarQube, Nexus, Docker, Kubernetes.
- ✅ **DevOps ≠ CI/CD:** DevOps is a culture; CI/CD is a set of technical practices within it.
- ✅ **Feature Flags** are critical for safe Continuous Deployment — they decouple deploy from release.
- ✅ KTU Examiners love asking the difference between **Continuous Delivery vs Continuous Deployment** — memorize the human-gate distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study - Netflix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Netflix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the "Netflix Case Study" in Software Engineering?

In the context of KTU's **PECST411 – Software Engineering** syllabus, the **Netflix Case Study** is a comprehensive, real-world, industrial benchmark used to illustrate how a **large-scale, distributed, cloud-native software system** is designed, developed, deployed, tested, and evolved using modern Software Engineering principles. It is studied because Netflix openly publishes (via its *Tech Blog*) its engineering trade-offs, architectural failures (e.g., the **2011 AWS DB crash**), and the remedies that shaped today's distributed-systems vocabulary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> The Netflix Case Study is a **post-mortem-driven, architecture-centric, DevOps-validated case study** that demonstrates the practical application of the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**, **architectural patterns (Microservices, Strangler Fig)**, **quality assurance (Chaos Engineering)**, and **continuous delivery pipelines** in a globally distributed, **high-availability (99.99% uptime)** streaming platform serving over **260+ million** subscribers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Netflix = Global Restaurant Chain" Analogy

Imagine a global restaurant chain like McDonald's. It cannot:
- Cook every burger in one giant kitchen (single point of failure).
- Send frozen patties from one country to another (high latency).
- Never experiment with new menu items (no innovation).

Instead, it builds **small, specialized local kitchens (microservices)** that each handle one thing — fries, drinks, patties. The central brand ensures consistent quality. It uses a **delivery fleet (CDN / Open Connect)** to bring food (video) close to customers. It deliberately **breaks its own kitchens occasionally (Chaos Monkey)** to make sure staff know how to recover. It **A/B tests new recipes (A/B testing framework)** before rolling them out globally.

| Netflix Component | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| Microservices | Specialized small kitchens |
| AWS Cloud | Real estate / kitchen infrastructure |
| Chaos Monkey | Fire drills |
| Open Connect CDN | Local delivery trucks |
| A/B Testing | Menu experiments |
| Spinnaker | Central operations manager |
| Eureka | Phone directory of all kitchens |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Netflix is not just a video app — it is one of the most cited **software engineering case studies** in academia because it survived a catastrophic failure in 2011 and emerged with an entirely new architectural paradigm (cloud-native microservices).

### 1.3 Why KTU Asks This Question

- It maps directly to **Module 3** learning outcomes covering **Architectural Design, Quality Assurance, and Software Evolution**.
- It tests a student's ability to **map theoretical SE concepts** to **industry practice**.
- It is a **favourite 14-mark KTU ESE question** because it integrates 4–5 syllabus topics into one answer.

---

## 1.4 High-Yield Terminology (Bold Constants & Standards)

- **AWS (Amazon Web Services)** — Netflix's sole cloud provider since **2016** (post-2011 incident).
- **Microservices** — Netflix runs **>700** independently deployable services.
- **Hystrix** — Latency & fault-tolerance library (now in maintenance mode, replaced by **Resilience4j** concepts).
- **Chaos Monkey** — First member of the **Simian Army**, randomly terminates EC2 instances in production.
- **Open Connect** — Netflix's proprietary **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** with appliances in **1,000+ ISP locations** worldwide.
- **99.99% Availability Target** — equivalent to **\leq 52.6 minutes** of downtime per year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Mapping the SDLC Phases to Netflix's Engineering Practices

Below is a **conceptual mapping** that KTU examiners love to see in answers. Each row links an SDLC phase to Netflix's actual engineering practice.

| SDLC Phase | Netflix's Approach | Key Tools / Artifacts |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirement Engineering** | Data-driven requirement gathering via telemetry, user behaviour analytics, A/B test hypotheses. | **A/B Testing Framework, Keystone Pipeline (data)**, user-event streams. |
| **System / Architectural Design** | **Microservices architecture**, event-driven design, polyglot persistence. | **Eureka, Zuul, Ribbon, Hystrix, Archaius, Cassandra, MySQL, DynamoDB, S3.** |
| **Implementation (Coding)** | Polyglot programming — **Java (primary backend), Python, Node.js, Kotlin, Go, Ruby**. Strict **code-review** culture, internal open-source model. | **Netflix OSS (Open Source Software)**, *Plex* (internal build system). |
| **Testing / Quality Assurance** | **Chaos Engineering, automated unit/integration tests, canary releases.** | **Chaos Monkey, Latency Monkey, Doctor Monkey, Janitor Monkey, Security Monkey, ChAP (Chaos Automation Platform).** |
| **Deployment** | **Continuous Delivery** with progressive rollout. | **Spinnaker** (multi-cloud CD tool open-sourced by Netflix), **Asgard** (deprecated predecessor), **Aminator** (AMI baker). |
| **Maintenance / Evolution** | Live-site monitoring, customer-impact-driven prioritisation, *Freedom & Responsibility* culture. | **Atlas** (telemetry), **Lumen** (dashboard), **EDOS** (event-driven ops). |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Architectural Decision

### 2.2.1 Why Microservices?
- **Monolith** had become a **deployment bottleneck** (one change required the whole system to redeploy).
- Different services had **different scaling needs** (login vs. video encoding).
- Failure in one component should not cascade (the **bulkhead pattern**).

### 2.2.2 Why AWS instead of a private data centre?
- After the **August 2011 EBS outage**, Netflix deliberately chose to **"go all-in"** on AWS to:
  - Achieve **horizontal scalability** on demand.
  - Avoid a **single point of failure**.
  - Enable **global regional deployment** (US, EU, APAC).

### 2.2.3 Why Chaos Engineering?
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** "If you can't fail in production, you can't be confident in production." Netflix intentionally injects failures to ensure their services are **resilient by design**, not by accident.

### 2.2.4 Why a Custom CDN (Open Connect)?
- **Third-party CDNs** could not match the cost-efficiency at Netflix's streaming scale (peak **15 GB/s** per Open Connect appliance).
- Embedding appliances inside ISPs reduces **last-mile latency** and **transit costs**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (The "Cheat Sheet")

Although the Netflix case study is qualitative, KTU examiners often expect numerical intuition. Below are the key formulas a student should memorise.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | % | Netflix targets **99.99%** ("four nines"). |
| Allowed Downtime per Year | $T_{\text{down}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes | For $A = 0.9999$, $T_{\text{down}} \approx 52.56$ min/year. |
| Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Higher is better; Netflix optimises for **large MTBF** via redundancy. |
| Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | minutes | Netflix pushes MTTR to **sub-minute** using automated rollback. |
| Little's Law (Queueing) | $L = \lambda \times W$ | requests | $L$ = requests in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = time in system. Used in **capacity planning**. |
| Replication Factor (Cassandra) | $N = R + W - 1$ constraint for $R$ reads + $W$ writes across $N$ replicas | count | Netflix tunes $R, W$ per workload (e.g., $R=1, W=2, N=3$). |
| Throughput per OC Appliance | $B_{\text{peak}} = 15 \text{ GB/s}$ per appliance | GB/s | Used to size **ISP peering capacity**. |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, **absolute value** should be written as $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the table pipe syntax. This is the same principle the system enforces.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Netflix case study is the de-facto **reference architecture** taught in:
- **Distributed Systems** courses (CMU, MIT, IIT).
- **Cloud Computing** modules.
- **Site Reliability Engineering (SRE)** training.
- **DevOps** certifications (AWS SA, CKA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students should remember that **Netflix OSS** (open-source projects released by Netflix) — including **Hystrix, Eureka, Zuul, Ribbon, Spinnaker, Atlas** — is the **practical implementation** of textbook Software Engineering patterns. Linking these to module concepts guarantees high marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from Availability SLA

**Question:** Netflix commits to a 99.99% availability SLA. Calculate the **maximum permissible annual downtime** in minutes and seconds.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We are given availability $A = 99.99\% = 0.9999$.

The fraction of downtime in a year is:
$$
D = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001
$$

Total minutes in a non-leap year:
$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

Annual permissible downtime:
$$
T_{\text{down}} = D \times T_{\text{year}}
$$

Substituting the numbers:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{down}} &= 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 52.56 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to seconds:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{down, sec}} &= 52.56 \times 60 \\
&= 3{,}153.6 \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 52 \text{ minutes and } 33.6 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Netflix is allowed only **~52.56 minutes of total downtime per year across all services combined**. This is the engineering rationale behind aggressive **automated rollback, chaos engineering, and multi-region failover**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Cassandra Read/Write Tuning

**Question:** Netflix's "Viewing History" service stores user watch events in **Apache Cassandra** with 3 replicas across 3 AWS availability zones. The team decides on a **write-heavy** access pattern. If they set $W = 2$ (writes must be acknowledged by 2 replicas), what is the minimum $R$ to satisfy **strong consistency**?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Cassandra uses a **tunable consistency** model. For **strong consistency**, the following condition must hold:
$$
R + W > N
$$

Given $N = 3$ and $W = 2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R + W &> N \\
R + 2 &> 3 \\
R &> 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R$ is a positive integer, the **minimum $R$** is:
$$
R = 2
$$

**Conclusion:** A read must also consult **2 out of 3 replicas** to guarantee the most recent write is read. Netflix balances this against latency — for a less critical query, they may set $R = 1$ (eventual consistency) for speed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode → Mitigation Mapping

This matrix is a **must-draw** in any KTU 14-mark answer on the Netflix case study. It shows the **"Why"** and **"How"** of Netflix's resilience design.

| Failure Mode | Consequence Without Mitigation | Netflix's Mitigation | Software Engineering Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EC2 instance crash** | Single service down, manual recovery | **Chaos Monkey** kills random instances; auto-scaling replaces them. | **Fault Tolerance, Self-Healing Systems** |
| **Database latency spike** | Cascading timeouts (DOMino effect) | **Hystrix** circuit breaker opens, fast-fails. | **Bulkhead Pattern, Circuit Breaker** |
| **Region-wide AWS outage** | Total service unavailability | **Active-Active multi-region** deployment (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1). | **Geographic Redundancy, Disaster Recovery** |
| **Bad code in production** | Customer-facing regression | **Canary deployment** + automated rollback via **Spinnaker**. | **Continuous Delivery, Progressive Rollout** |
| **SSL/TLS certificate expiry** | Trust failures | **Security Monkey** + automated cert rotation (**Lemur**). | **Configuration Management, Ops Automation** |
| **Memory leak in service** | Gradual OOM crash | **Doctor Monkey** detects abnormal health, triggers restart. | **Health Checking, Observability** |
| **Orphaned resources (e.g., unused AMIs)** | Cost bloat | **Janitor Monkey** cleans up unused resources. | **Resource Optimisation** |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough — The Strangler Fig Pattern

Netflix famously used the **Strangler Fig Pattern** to migrate from a monolithic DVD-rental application to cloud-native microservices. This pattern is a **key KTU exam topic**.

**Analogy:** A strangler fig tree grows around a host tree, gradually replacing it. The host (monolith) is decommissioned only when the fig (microservices) is strong enough.

### Step-by-Step Migration Logic

1. **Identify a bounded context** in the monolith (e.g., *User Authentication*).
2. **Build a new microservice** that handles the *User Authentication* logic.
3. **Place an API Gateway (Zuul)** in front of both old and new code.
4. **Route a small % of traffic** (e.g., 1%) to the new microservice via **A/B testing**.
5. **Compare metrics** (latency, error rate) between monolith and microservice.
6. **Incrementally shift traffic** in batches: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%.
7. **Decommission the monolith module** once traffic is fully on the microservice.
8. **Repeat for the next bounded context** (e.g., *Recommendations*, *Billing*).

This pattern allowed Netflix to migrate **without a "Big Bang" cutover**, ensuring **zero customer downtime** during the **7-year** cloud migration (2008–2015).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Netflix High-Level Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid

    clientApp["Client Apps Web iOS Android TV"] --> cdn["Open Connect CDN ISP Level"]
    cdn --> zl["Zuul API Gateway Edge Proxy"]
    zl --> eureka["Eureka Service Registry"]

    ribbon --> ms2["Microservice 2 Recommendations"]

    ribbon --> ms4["Microservice 4 Billing"]

    ms2 --> hystrix
    ms3 --> hystrix
    ms4 --> hystrix
    hystrix --> cassandra["Cassandra NoSQL Wide Column"]
    hystrix --> mysql["MySQL RDBMS"]
    hystrix --> dynamo["DynamoDB Key Value"]
    hystrix --> s3["S3 Object Storage"]

    chaos -. kills .-> ms2

    spinnaker --> ms3
    spinnaker --> ms4
```
