```mermaid
    atlas --> ms2
    atlas --> ms3
    atlas --> ms4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote real-time request flow. Dashed arrows denote background automation (chaos, deployment, monitoring). This diagram is a **safe Mermaid render** — all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes to avoid reserved-keyword collisions.

## 4.2 The Netflix Chaos Engineering Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Steady State Hypothesis Define normal metric baseline"] --> stepB["Blast Radius Choose small scope e.g. 1 instance"]
    stepB --> stepC["Inject Failure Chaos Monkey kills instance"]
    stepC --> stepD["Observe Metrics Atlas Dashboard"]
    stepD --> stepE{"Did system recover automatically"}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF["Expand Blast Radius More instances regions"]
    stepE -- No  --> stepG["Fix Bug Open Ticket Improve Code"]
    stepG --> stepH["Run Test in Staging"]
    stepH --> stepC
    stepF --> stepI["Document Learnings Share with team"]
    stepI --> stepA
```

## 4.3 Continuous Delivery Pipeline (Spinnaker Stage Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1["Developer Push Code to Git"] --> p2["Jenkins CI Build and Unit Test"]
    p2 --> p3["Bake AMI Aminator Amazon Machine Image"]
    p3 --> p4["Deploy to Staging"]
    p4 --> p5["Run Integration and Load Tests"]
    p5 --> p6["Canary Deploy 1 percent of production fleet"]
    p6 --> p7{"Canary Metrics Healthy"}
    p7 -- Yes --> p8["Progressive Rollout 10 percent 50 percent 100 percent"]
    p7 -- No  --> p9["Automated Rollback via Spinnaker"]
    p8 --> p10["Full Production Release"]
    p10 --> p11["Live Site Monitoring Atlas Lumen"]
```

## 4.4 The Strangler Fig Migration Sequence (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["Phase 1 Monolith Handles 100 percent"] --> phase2["Phase 2 Strangler Routes 1 percent to New Microservice"]
    phase2 --> phase3["Phase 3 Canary Analysis 10 percent"]
    phase3 --> phase4["Phase 4 Traffic Shifted 100 percent"]
    phase4 --> phase5["Phase 5 Decommission Monolith Module"]
    phase5 --> phase6["Phase 6 Repeat for Next Bounded Context"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain the members of Netflix's "Simian Army". Why did Netflix introduce them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Simian Army** is a suite of failure-injection tools Netflix runs in **production** to verify the resilience of its AWS-based microservices. The key members are:

1. **Chaos Monkey** — randomly terminates EC2 instances to test auto-recovery.
2. **Latency Monkey** — induces artificial network latency between services.
3. **Doctor Monkey** — detects unhealthy instances (high CPU, memory leak) and heals them.
4. **Janitor Monkey** — searches for and cleans up unused resources (cost optimisation).
5. **Security Monkey** — finds security violations (open security groups, unrotated SSL certs).
6. **10-18 Monkey** — detects configuration and instance-group inconsistencies across regions.

**Why introduced:** After the 2011 AWS outage, Netflix's engineering leadership, led by **Adrian Cockcroft**, embraced a culture of *"failure as a feature"*. The Simian Army enforces the principle that **resilience must be continuously tested in production**, not assumed from design documents.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**What is the "Strangler Fig Pattern" as applied by Netflix? State ONE advantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Strangler Fig Pattern** is a **software migration strategy** where a new system (microservices) is built *around* an old legacy system (monolith) by gradually replacing one bounded context at a time, instead of performing a "big-bang" rewrite.

**Process:**
1. Place a routing/facade layer in front of the old system.
2. Redirect a small percentage of requests to the new microservice.
3. Compare and verify behaviour.
4. Increase redirection progressively until 100% of traffic is on the new service.
5. Decommission the old module.

**Advantage:** It enables **incremental, low-risk migration** with **zero customer-visible downtime**, allowing the legacy system to coexist with the new system throughout the transition.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice. You are expected to spend **~25–30 minutes** on it. The answer must combine **diagrams, definitions, justifications, and trade-offs**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **"Explain the Software Engineering practices adopted by Netflix with reference to its migration from a monolithic architecture to a cloud-native microservices architecture on AWS. Highlight the role of the Strangler Fig Pattern, Chaos Engineering, and Continuous Delivery in ensuring scalability and reliability."**

#### **Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Architecture Migration & Strangler Fig**

1. **Background (2 marks):** Netflix started as a **monolithic Java application** in 2008. By 2009, scaling issues and the 2011 EBS outage forced a re-architecture.
2. **Why Microservices (2 marks):** Independent deployment, polyglot persistence, fault isolation via **bulkhead pattern**.
3. **Strangler Fig Application (3 marks):** Step-by-step migration of bounded contexts (Auth → Recommendations → Billing) using **Zuul** as the gateway. Draw the migration diagram (refer to Section 4.4).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Chaos Engineering & Continuous Delivery**

1. **Chaos Engineering (3 marks):** Define the **principles** (steady-state hypothesis, blast radius, continuous experimentation). Mention **Chaos Monkey, ChAP, FIT (Failure Injection Testing)**. Explain how it uncovered latent bugs in circuit-breaker logic.
2. **Continuous Delivery with Spinnaker (3 marks):** Describe the **pipeline**: Git → Jenkins → AMI bake → Canary → Progressive rollout → Production. Mention **red/black deployments**.
3. **Trade-offs (1 mark):** Higher operational complexity, observability burden, cost of redundancy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

> **"With suitable diagrams, describe the role of the following Netflix OSS components in building a resilient microservices system: (a) Eureka, Zuul, Ribbon, Hystrix; (b) Spinnaker, Atlas, and the Simian Army."**

#### **Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Edge & Resilience Stack**

1. **Eureka (2 marks):** Service registry — services register themselves; clients discover them dynamically. Eliminates hard-coded service URLs.
2. **Zuul (2 marks):** Edge gateway — handles routing, authentication, rate-limiting, dynamic routing per A/B test.
3. **Ribbon (1.5 marks):** Client-side load balancer across Eureka-discovered instances.
4. **Hystrix (1.5 marks):** Circuit breaker; isolates failing services, prevents cascading failure. Reference the **bulkhead pattern**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Deployment & Observability Stack**

1. **Spinnaker (3 marks):** Multi-cloud CD platform. Draw the pipeline from Section 4.3. Discuss **canary analysis** and **automated rollback**.
2. **Atlas + Lumen (2 marks):** Telemetry stack — time-series metrics, real-time dashboards. Powers *what changed?* and *is the canary healthy?* decisions.
3. **Simian Army (2 marks):** Re-iterate **Chaos Monkey, Latency Monkey, Security Monkey**. Discuss the **principle of continuous failure injection** in production.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students LOSE marks on this case study:**

1. **Writing only about "what Netflix is"** — Examiners want **Software Engineering concepts** mapped to Netflix's practices, not corporate history. Mentioning AWS or microservices without linking to **SDLC, design patterns, or quality assurance** scores zero.

2. **Forgetting to draw a diagram** — A 14-mark answer *must* include at least **one architecture or pipeline diagram**. Use **labelled boxes and arrows**; even a hand-drawn schematic scored high in past KTU evaluations.

3. **Missing the "Why"** — Stating *"Netflix uses microservices"* is 0 marks. Stating *"Netflix uses microservices **because** the monolithic architecture caused deployment bottlenecks and cascading failures during the 2011 AWS outage"* is full marks.

4. **Conflating Chaos Engineering with simple testing** — Chaos Engineering is **experimentation in production**, not unit testing. State the **principles** (steady-state hypothesis, blast radius, continuous).

5. **Ignoring trade-offs** — Netflix's architecture has costs: **operational complexity, observability burden, multi-region cost, polyglot persistence maintenance**. Acknowledging trade-offs signals a **senior-level understanding** and earns the last 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Netflix is the **canonical case study** for **cloud-native microservices architecture**.
- ✅ The **2011 AWS EBS outage** is the **triggering event** for Netflix's architectural shift — always mention it.
- ✅ **Strangler Fig Pattern** = incremental migration from monolith to microservices (Martin Fowler, 2004).
- ✅ **Microservices + Bulkhead Pattern + Circuit Breaker (Hystrix)** = Netflix's resilience triad.
- ✅ **Eureka = Service Discovery, Zuul = API Gateway, Ribbon = Load Balancing, Archaius = Dynamic Config.**
- ✅ **Chaos Engineering = Simian Army** (Chaos, Latency, Doctor, Janitor, Security, 10-18 Monkeys).
- ✅ **Continuous Delivery = Spinnaker** (Canary → Progressive Rollout → Auto Rollback).
- ✅ **Open Connect** = Netflix's proprietary CDN, embedded in **1,000+ ISP networks** worldwide.
- ✅ Availability target = **99.99%** → maximum **~52.56 minutes** downtime/year.
- ✅ Cassandra consistency rule: **R + W > N** for strong consistency.
- ✅ **Netflix OSS** is **open-source** — students can study the actual code on GitHub (`github.com/Netflix`).
- ✅ Software Engineering concepts tested: **SDLC, Architectural Patterns, Design Patterns, Quality Assurance, Continuous Delivery, Software Evolution, Risk Management.**
- ✅ Always end your answer with **lessons learned** — KTU rewards **reflective engineering thinking**.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Case Study: Netflix Notes**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme & Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L6).*

topics.title: Software Maintenance and its Types - Adaptive, Preventive, Corrective, and Perfective Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software Maintenance and its Types - Adaptive, Preventive, Corrective, and Perfective Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IEEE 1219 / ISO/IEC 14764)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Maintenance** is the totality of activities required to keep a software system operational, responsive to changing user requirements, and adapted to evolving technical environments **after** its initial deployment and acceptance into the operational environment.

The **IEEE Standard 1219-1998** formally defines software maintenance as:

> *"The modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, to improve performance or other attributes, or to adapt the product to a changed environment."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for **PECST411**, software maintenance is positioned as the *largest phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)* — typically consuming **60% to 80%** of the total lifetime cost of any non-trivial software product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" View

Imagine you have just built a beautiful 10-floor apartment building (this is your *software deployment*).

| Real-World Activity | Software Maintenance Parallel |
|---|---|
| The municipal corporation changes the sewage line connection | **Adaptive Maintenance** (changes in OS, hardware, regulations) |
| A door hinge becomes loose and needs fixing | **Corrective Maintenance** (bug fixing after delivery) |
| Residents ask for a gym, a garden, or a smart lock | **Perfective Maintenance** (new features, enhancements) |
| You repaint walls and reinforce pillars *before* the monsoon | **Preventive Maintenance** (refactoring, code optimization) |

Just as a building never truly "finishes" — it constantly needs upkeep — software is **never truly "done"**. It evolves from the moment it is deployed until it is officially retired (a phase known as **software retirement** or **end-of-life**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology — "Modification" vs "Development"**
> Maintenance is fundamentally different from *new development*. In maintenance, the engineer is working with **pre-existing, often undocumented, legacy code**, written by someone else, with constrained budgets, under time pressure, and without the freedom to redesign the system from scratch.

### 1.3 Why Software Maintenance is the Dominant Cost

According to industry data referenced in standard SE textbooks:

- **40–80%** of total software lifecycle cost is attributed to maintenance.
- For a typical long-lived enterprise system, maintenance costs can exceed **$10,000 per day**.
- The average software spends **more than 15 years** in the maintenance phase.

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU asks this question so often:** Examiners in PECST411 repeatedly test whether students can distinguish the **four types** of maintenance, because this forms the conceptual foundation for the *Software Configuration Management (SCM)* module and the *Software Evolution* sub-topics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Types of Maintenance

The **ISO/IEC 14764** standard classifies software maintenance into four distinct primary categories. Each addresses a different *trigger* and a different *intent* for the modification.

#### 2.1.1 Corrective Maintenance — "The Bug Fixer"

- **Trigger:** A defect (fault) is reported in the operational software.
- **Intent:** Restore the system to its originally specified behavior.
- **Scope:** Approximately **20%** of total maintenance effort (industry average).
- **Activities:** Debugging, fault localization, patch generation, regression testing.
- **KTU Buzzwords:** *Defect repair, fault correction, error removal, post-delivery bug fixing.*

#### 2.1.2 Adaptive Maintenance — "The Chameleon"

- **Trigger:** The *external environment* in which the software operates changes.
- **Intent:** Keep the software usable in a changed technical or business environment.
- **Scope:** Approximately **25%** of total maintenance effort.
- **Examples of environment change:**
  - Migration from Windows 10 to Windows 11
  - Upgrading the database from Oracle 12c to Oracle 19c
  - New government tax regulation
  - New hardware platform (ARM-based servers)
  - New third-party API contract change
- **KTU Buzzwords:** *Environment adaptation, platform migration, regulatory compliance.*

#### 2.1.3 Perfective Maintenance — "The Wish-List Fulfiller"

- **Trigger:** A *new or changed user requirement* (functional improvement).
- **Intent:** Improve or extend the system's capabilities based on user feedback.
- **Scope:** Approximately **50%** of total maintenance effort (the *largest* category).
- **Examples:**
  - Adding a "forgot password" feature
  - Adding a new report to a billing system
  - Improving the UI responsiveness
  - Adding a new payment gateway
- **KTU Buzzwords:** *Feature enhancement, functional extension, new requirements.*

#### 2.1.4 Preventive Maintenance — "The Doctor's Check-Up"

- **Trigger:** A *latent fault or future risk* is anticipated (proactive).
- **Intent:** Reduce the probability of future failures and increase software maintainability/reliability.
- **Scope:** Approximately **5%** of total maintenance effort (often neglected!).
- **Examples:**
  - Code refactoring to reduce cyclomatic complexity
  - Updating outdated libraries to remove known security vulnerabilities (e.g., Log4Shell)
  - Improving documentation
  - Performance optimization of hot paths
  - Adding unit tests for legacy modules
- **KTU Buzzwords:** *Proactive modification, refactoring, software rejuvenation.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "CAPP"**
> **C**orrective (bugs) → **A**daptive (environment) → **P**erfective (features) → **P**reventive (future-proofing)
> If you remember **C-A-P-P**, you will never mix up the four types in the exam.

### 2.2 The IEEE 1219 Maintenance Process Model

The IEEE standard defines maintenance as a **6-step cyclical process**:

1. **Problem / Modification Identification & Classification**
2. **Analysis (Feasibility Study)**
3. **Design of the Modification**
4. **Implementation (Coding)**
5. **Regression / System Testing**
6. **Acceptance Testing & Delivery**

### 2.3 Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution (1975–1996)

Prof. M.M. Lehman at Imperial College London proposed **8 Laws** governing how large-scale real-world systems evolve. KTU frequently asks about the first 4:

| Law | Statement | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Law of Continuing Change** | A program used in a real-world environment must change or become progressively less useful. | Maintenance is inevitable. |
| **Law of Increasing Complexity** | As a system evolves, its complexity increases unless work is done to maintain or reduce it. | Justifies **Preventive Maintenance**. |
| **Law of Declining Quality** | The quality of a system will decline unless rigorously maintained and adapted. | Justifies **Corrective Maintenance**. |
| **Law of Conservation of Organisational Stability** | The average effective global activity rate in an evolving system is invariant. | Maintenance teams have a fixed capacity. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although Software Engineering is not a math-heavy subject, KTU expects numerical literacy for metrics. The following table is essential:

| Metric | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}$ | hours | Average operational time before failure (for non-repairable modules) |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | hours | Average time between two consecutive failures |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i}{N}$ | hours | Average time taken to repair and restore service |
| **System Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | percentage | The fraction of time the system is operational |
| **Maintenance Cost Ratio (MCR)** | $\text{MCR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{maintenance}}}{C_{\text{total\ lifecycle}}} \times 100\%$ | percentage | Proportion of total cost spent on maintenance |
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | defects/KLOC | Quality of the code base being maintained |
| **Perfective Effort Fraction (PEF)** | $\text{PEF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{perfective}}}{E_{\text{total\ maintenance}}} \times 100\%$ | percentage | Share of effort consumed by new features |
| **Mean Time to Change (MTTC)** | $\text{MTTC} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Time\ to\ implement\ change}}{\text{Number\ of\ changes}}$ | hours | How quickly the system can be adapted/perfectively modified |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule for Markdown:** All vertical bars in formulas above have been replaced with `\vert` or placed inside `\dfrac` to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your answer sheets, always use the bar symbol `\vert` for divisibility and absolute value.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** Perfective maintenance adds UPI/NetBanking features every quarter.
- **Healthcare:** Adaptive maintenance ensures EMR systems comply with HIPAA/ABDM updates.
- **Aviation:** Preventive maintenance (DO-178C compliance) avoids catastrophic failure.
- **Open Source (e.g., Linux Kernel):** A continuous cycle of all four maintenance types by a global community.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section rigorously derives the standard maintenance metrics. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

### 3.1 Derivation of System Availability

**Given:** A system is observed over a long period. Let $T_{\text{up}}$ be the total time it is operational and $T_{\text{down}}$ be the total time it is down (under repair or failed). Let the total observation period be:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the basic availability ratio:**

By definition, **Availability** is the fraction of total time the system is functioning:

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T_{\text{total}}$ in terms of up and down time:**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume a statistically steady state with $N$ failure events:**
If we observe $N$ failure events, then $T_{\text{up}}$ can be approximated as $N \times \text{MTBF}$ and $T_{\text{down}}$ as $N \times \text{MTTR}$. Substituting:

$$
A = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTBF} + N \cdot \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $N$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $100\%$ to express as a percentage:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU formula**. Memorize it exactly. It is asked directly in Part A and indirectly in Part B numerical questions.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — "The KTU Bank Server"

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B style):**
A bank's online transaction server has an MTTF of **1,800 hours** and an MTTR of **20 hours**. Calculate the MTBF and the system availability. If management invests in preventive maintenance that increases MTTF to 2,400 hours, what is the new availability?

**Step 1 — Calculate the original MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}} = 1800 + 20 = 1820 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the original availability:**

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}}}{\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{1820}{1820 + 20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{1820}{1840} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{old}} = 0.98913 \times 100\% = 98.913\%
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the new MTBF after preventive maintenance:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} = 2400 + 20 = 2420 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the new availability:**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{2420}{2420 + 20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{2420}{2440} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0.99180 \times 100\% = 99.180\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the engineering result:**

The improvement in availability is:

$$
\Delta A = A_{\text{new}} - A_{\text{old}} = 99.180\% - 98.913\% = 0.267\%
$$

For a 24/7 banking system, this **0.267%** improvement translates to approximately **70 hours of additional uptime per year**, directly justifying the **preventive maintenance** investment.

### 3.3 The Maintenance Effort Distribution Matrix

KTU frequently asks students to *categorize* a given maintenance activity. Use this analytical matrix to verify your answer:

| Activity Performed | Correct Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Fixing a NullPointerException reported by a user | **Corrective** | Responding to a defect in delivered code. |
| Updating the app to support iOS 17 | **Adaptive** | OS environment change. |
| Adding a "Download Invoice" button to the user dashboard | **Perfective** | New feature requested by users. |
| Refactoring a module to reduce its cyclomatic complexity from 25 to 10 | **Preventive** | Proactive maintainability improvement. |
| Changing VAT calculation logic from 12% to 15% per new law | **Adaptive** | External regulatory environment change. |
| Adding unit tests to legacy code with no tests | **Preventive** | Improving future maintainability. |
| Patching a SQL Injection vulnerability (CVE-2024-XXXX) | **Corrective** | Eliminating a known defect/security bug. |
| Adding a Dark Mode to the UI | **Perfective** | New user-facing feature/enhancement. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Maintenance Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode["Software Maintenance IEEE 1219"]
    rootNode --> primNode["Primary Activities"]
    rootNode --> supportingNode["Supporting Activities"]
    rootNode --> transitionNode["Transition Activities"]

    primNode --> correctiveNode["Corrective Maintenance"]
    primNode --> adaptiveNode["Adaptive Maintenance"]
    primNode --> perfectiveNode["Perfective Maintenance"]
    primNode --> preventiveNode["Preventive Maintenance"]

    correctiveNode --> correctiveDesc["20 percent effort - Bug fixing after deployment"]
    adaptiveNode --> adaptiveDesc["25 percent effort - Adapting to environment changes"]
    perfectiveNode --> perfectiveDesc["50 percent effort - New features and enhancements"]
    preventiveNode --> preventiveDesc["5 percent effort - Proactive refactoring and improvement"]

    supportingNode --> supportDesc["Documentation - Training - Configuration Management"]
    transitionNode --> transitionDesc["Migration - Retirement - End of Life"]
```

```mermaid
    stepA["Step 1: Problem Identification and Classification"]
    stepB["Step 2: Analysis and Feasibility Study"]

```


### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Maintenance Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userReq["User Reports Issue or Requests Change"]
    classify{"Classify the Maintenance Type"}

    adaptPath["Adaptive Path - Adapt to new environment"]

    preventPath["Preventive Path - Refactor proactively"]
    commonBase["Common Engineering Base: Analysis, Design, Code, Test, Deploy"]
    systemBack["Updated Software System in Production"]
    userReq --> classify
    classify --> correctPath
    classify --> adaptPath
    classify --> perfectPath
    classify --> preventPath
    correctPath --> commonBase
    adaptPath --> commonBase
    perfectPath --> commonBase
    preventPath --> commonBase
    commonBase --> systemBack
    systemBack -.Feedback Loop.-> userReq
```
