<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define software maintenance. List its four primary types as per the IEEE 1219 standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Software maintenance is the modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, improve performance or other attributes, or adapt the product to a changed environment (IEEE 1219, 1998).
>
> **Four Types (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Corrective Maintenance** — Fixing defects reported after delivery.
> 2. **Adaptive Maintenance** — Adapting the software to changes in the external environment.
> 3. **Perfective Maintenance** — Adding new features or improving performance as per new user requirements.
> 4. **Preventive Maintenance** — Proactive modifications to improve maintainability and prevent future failures.

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Perfective Maintenance and Preventive Maintenance with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Perfective Maintenance | Preventive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | New or changed user requirement | Anticipated future risk |
| **Goal** | Improve functionality / add features | Improve maintainability / prevent future bugs |
| **Reactive/Proactive** | Reactive to user demand | Proactive by engineering team |
| **Example** | Adding a "Forgot Password" feature to a login system | Refactoring a legacy module to reduce its cyclomatic complexity |
| **Effort Share** | ~50% of total maintenance effort | ~5% of total maintenance effort |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing "Adaptive" with "Perfective". Remember — *Adaptive = Environment changes* (external); *Perfective = User wants new features* (internal demand).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing the percentage effort distribution as equal (25% each). It is **NOT** equal. Perfective dominates at ~50%, Preventive is often neglected at ~5%.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to mention the **IEEE/ISO standard reference number** in the definition. Examiners explicitly award 0.5 marks for "IEEE 1219" or "ISO/IEC 14764".
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In numerical questions, not converting final values to **percentage** form for availability. Always write $A = 99.18\%$, not just $0.9918$.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Explain the four types of software maintenance in detail with a real-world example for each. State the approximate percentage effort distribution among them.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The four types of software maintenance, as per the IEEE 1219 standard, are:

**1. Corrective Maintenance (≈20% effort) [1.5 marks]**
This type of maintenance deals with the *correction of faults* discovered after the software has been delivered and is in operation. These faults are reported by users during real-world usage.
- *Example:* A user reports that pressing the "Submit" button on a registration form results in a `500 Internal Server Error`. The development team debugs the code, finds a `NullPointerException` in the user service, fixes it, and redeploys the patch.

**2. Adaptive Maintenance (≈25% effort) [1.5 marks]**
This type of maintenance is performed to *keep the software usable* in a changing external environment. The software's core functionality is not defective; the environment around it has evolved.
- *Example:* A web application originally built for MySQL 5.7 must be migrated to PostgreSQL 16 due to a company-wide database standardization policy. The team rewrites the data access layer and updates the connection drivers.

**3. Perfective Maintenance (≈50% effort) [2 marks]**
This is the *largest category* of maintenance. It is initiated by *new or changed user requirements* to enhance the system's functional capabilities, performance, or usability. It is essentially a continuation of development activities within the maintenance envelope.
- *Example:* Based on user feedback, the team adds a "Dark Mode" toggle to a mobile banking app, adds a CSV export feature to the transaction history, and integrates a new UPI payment gateway.

**4. Preventive Maintenance (≈5% effort) [2 marks]**
This is *proactive* maintenance performed to *reduce the probability of future failures* and to improve the long-term maintainability of the system. It is the most under-funded category in practice.
- *Example:* The team identifies that an old authentication module uses a deprecated encryption algorithm. Before a security breach occurs, they refactor the module to use AES-256, add unit tests, and update the documentation.

**Effort Distribution Summary:**
- Perfective: **50%** (largest)
- Adaptive: **25%**
- Corrective: **20%**
- Preventive: **5%** (smallest, often neglected)

---

**Q3(b) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Compute the system availability of an ATM network given the following data: MTTF = 3000 hours, MTTR = 12 hours. If the bank invests in preventive maintenance that improves MTTF to 4500 hours, recalculate the new availability. Comment on the significance of the improvement.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data (0.5 marks):**
- $\text{MTTF}_{\text{old}} = 3000$ hours
- $\text{MTTR} = 12$ hours (constant)
- $\text{MTTF}_{\text{new}} = 4500$ hours (after preventive maintenance)

**Step 2 — Compute the old MTBF (1 mark):**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}} = \text{MTTF}_{\text{old}} + \text{MTTR}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}} = 3000 + 12 = 3012 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the old availability (1.5 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}}}{\text{MTBF}_{\text{old}} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{3012}{3012 + 12} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{3012}{3024} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{old}} = 0.99603 \times 100\% = 99.603\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new MTBF (1 mark):**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} = 4500 + 12 = 4512 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new availability (1.5 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{4512}{4512 + 12} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{4512}{4524} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0.99735 \times 100\% = 99.735\%
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on the significance (1.5 marks):**

The improvement in availability is:

$$
\Delta A = 99.735\% - 99.603\% = 0.132\%
$$

In a year ($8760$ hours), this translates to approximately **11.57 additional hours of uptime**. While $0.132\%$ may seem small, for a financial system like an ATM network, even a few hours of extra uptime translates to **thousands of additional successful transactions**, justifying the **preventive maintenance investment** in software reliability engineering.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**Q4(a) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2021]**
**Explain the IEEE 1219 software maintenance process model in detail. How does it differ from the new development process model?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**The IEEE 1219 Maintenance Process (5 marks):**
The IEEE Standard 1219-1998 defines software maintenance as a structured six-step process:

1. **Problem / Modification Identification and Classification:** The maintenance request is logged, classified (as Corrective/Adaptive/Perfective/Preventive), and prioritized based on severity and impact.
2. **Analysis (Feasibility Study):** The team analyzes the proposed change for technical feasibility, cost, schedule, and risk. This is the **mirror image** of a new project's feasibility study.
3. **Design of the Modification:** Detailed design of the change to be made. The constraint here is that the design must integrate with the **existing, pre-existing architecture**.
4. **Implementation (Coding):** The modification is coded, often within legacy codebases with limited or no documentation. The engineer must perform *reverse engineering* to understand the code first.
5. **Regression and System Testing:** The modified system is tested not just for the new change but also to ensure **no existing functionality has been broken** (regression). This is a major cost driver in maintenance.
6. **Acceptance Testing and Delivery:** The modified system is validated, accepted by the user, and deployed into production.

**Differences from New Development (2 marks):**

| Aspect | New Development | Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | A blank slate / greenfield | A pre-existing, often legacy system |
| **Documentation** | Usually available and current | Often missing, outdated, or incorrect |
| **Code Author** | The same team that built it | A different team, often with no tribal knowledge |
| **Testing** | Primarily forward-looking | Primarily **regression-focused** to prevent breakage |
| **Architecture** | Freedom to choose | Constrained by the existing architecture |
| **Risk** | Risk of *building* the wrong thing | Risk of *breaking* a working system |
| **Tools** | Standard IDEs, fresh toolchain | Must coexist with old build systems, obsolete libraries |

---

**Q4(b) [7 marks]. [KTU University Exam — May 2022]**
**State Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution. Explain the "Law of Increasing Complexity" and the "Law of Continuing Change" with examples.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Statement of Lehman's Laws (2 marks):**
Prof. M.M. Lehman proposed 8 laws governing the evolution of large-scale, real-world software systems. The first four are most frequently tested:

1. Law of Continuing Change
2. Law of Increasing Complexity
3. Law of Declining Quality
4. Law of Conservation of Organisational Stability
*(Plus four more on global activity rate, growth of user population, etc.)*

**Explanation of the Two Laws (5 marks):**

**1. Law of Continuing Change (2.5 marks):**
*Statement:* "A program used in a real-world environment must change or become progressively less useful."
- *Meaning:* Real-world environments (hardware, OS, regulations, user needs) are constantly changing. If the software does not change in lockstep, it becomes obsolete.
- *Example:* A desktop application built for Windows XP in 2002 is essentially useless in 2024 if it is not ported to Windows 11. The underlying environment has changed, demanding **adaptive maintenance** and **perfective maintenance** continuously.

**2. Law of Increasing Complexity (2.5 marks):**
*Statement:* "As a system evolves, its complexity increases unless work is done to maintain or reduce it."
- *Meaning:* Every new feature or bug fix adds a little complexity. Over time, the system becomes harder to understand, harder to test, and more prone to bugs — unless **preventive maintenance** (refactoring, code cleanup, simplification) is performed.
- *Example:* A banking application that has had 500 patches applied over 10 years will have sprawling conditional logic, dead code, and tangled dependencies — often called a *"Big Ball of Mud"*. Preventive maintenance (refactoring) is the only way to counteract this entropy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maintenance consumes 60–80% of total software lifecycle cost.**
- **IEEE 1219** is the definitive standard; cite it in every definition.
- **Four Types = CAPP: Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive.**
- **Corrective (20%)** = bug fixing after delivery.
- **Adaptive (25%)** = adapting to environment changes (OS, hardware, regulations).
- **Perfective (50%)** = the largest category, adding new features.
- **Preventive (5%)** = the most neglected but most valuable for long-term health.
- **System Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$.
- **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**.
- **Lehman's Law of Continuing Change** justifies the *necessity* of maintenance.
- **Lehman's Law of Increasing Complexity** justifies *preventive* maintenance.
- The IEEE 1219 process is **6 steps** (Identify → Analyze → Design → Implement → Test → Deliver).
- **Perfective > Adaptive > Corrective > Preventive** in terms of effort share.
- **Distinction Trick:** If the *environment* changed → Adaptive. If the *user* asked for a new feature → Perfective. If a *bug* was reported → Corrective. If the *team* is improving code proactively → Preventive.
- **Regression testing** is the *most critical* testing activity in maintenance (not unit testing of the new feature).
- **Cyclomatic complexity reduction** is a textbook example of preventive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boehm's Maintenance Models (both legacy and non-legacy)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boehm's Maintenance Models (both legacy and non-legacy)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boehm's Maintenance Models** are two distinct conceptual frameworks proposed by **Barry W. Boehm** that describe how software maintenance activities should be organized, scoped, and funded across the software life cycle. The two models differ fundamentally in their philosophical stance toward maintenance:

- **Boehm's Legacy Maintenance Model (1983):** Treats maintenance as a *post-delivery, secondary* activity. Maintenance is reactive and largely corrective/adaptive, applied to systems that have been transitioned from active development into an operational, sustainment phase.
- **Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model (2000+):** Treats maintenance as *equivalent to new development*. It uses an incremental, risk-driven, spiral-style approach in which existing systems are continuously evolved through planned enhancements, re-engineering, and reuse.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST411 Module 3):**
> The two Boehm models are studied to understand the *evolution of maintenance philosophy* — from a "fix-when-broken" attitude (legacy) to an integrated, life-cycle-wide engineering discipline (non-legacy).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The House Analogy:**

- **Legacy Model** is like owning an *old ancestral house*. After the architect hands it over, you live in it and call a mason every time a wall cracks (corrective), repaint when colors fade (adaptive), or add a small shelf (perfective). The architect is rarely called back. Maintenance is a *different* activity, done by a different team, with a smaller budget.
- **Non-Legacy Model** is like a *modern smart-home that is upgraded every few years*. The same architects, designers, and engineers continuously redesign, refit, and rewire the home using the latest materials. Maintenance, modernization, and new construction are *the same kind of work*, planned together.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Constants / Standard Metrics)

- **Maintenance Cost Ratio:** Roughly **60% to 80%** of total software life-cycle cost is consumed by maintenance in legacy systems.
- **Corrective Maintenance:** Fixing residual defects discovered after delivery — typically **~20%** of maintenance effort in legacy systems.
- **Adaptive Maintenance:** Modifying software for new environments (OS, hardware, regulations) — typically **~25%**.
- **Perfective Maintenance:** Adding new functional features requested by users — typically **~50%** (the largest share).
- **Preventive Maintenance:** Improving maintainability, restructuring code to prevent future failures — typically **~5%**.
- **Re-engineering:** Restructuring an existing system to make it *understandable* and *changeable* without changing its external behavior.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Analyzing software to extract its design, architecture, and requirements for re-use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Models?**
> Software engineering matured between 1983 and 2000. The rise of **object-oriented programming**, **component-based development**, **COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) integration**, and **agile methods** forced a re-think: maintenance could no longer be treated as a *residual* activity. Boehm updated his model accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Boehm's Legacy Maintenance Model — Deep Dive

The **Legacy Model** emerged from Boehm's landmark 1983 analysis of DoD (U.S. Department of Defense) software projects. Its key principles:

**Step 1: Maintenance is a *separate* life-cycle phase.**
After the system is delivered and accepted, it transitions to a *maintenance phase* managed by a *maintenance team* (often different from the development team). Funding, staffing, and tooling differ from development.

**Step 2: Maintenance is *primarily reactive*.**
The team waits for change requests (CRs) or problem reports (PRs) and acts on them. There is little proactive planning.

**Step 3: Maintenance is *under-resourced* relative to development.**
Because organizations historically viewed maintenance as "less creative," it received less experienced staff and lower budgets — yet consumed 60–80% of total cost. This created a **paradox of under-investment**.

**Step 4: The four maintenance activities dominate.**
Corrective → Adaptive → Perfective → Preventive (in order of typical volume: Perfective > Adaptive > Corrective > Preventive).

**Step 5: Maintenance difficulty grows non-linearly.**
As software ages, the *ripple effect* of changes increases. Modifying a module in year 5 of a 1-million-line system is far more expensive than in year 1 — Boehm estimated a **compound complexity growth** that raises per-change cost over time.

**Step 6: End-of-life is implicit.**
Systems are eventually retired, often with *no* documented retirement plan — leading to "legacy graveyards."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this model mattered historically:**
> It was the *first formal model* that explicitly recognized maintenance as the *dominant* cost in software economics, not a footnote. It forced the industry to acknowledge that *most software work is maintenance*.

### 2.2 Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model — Deep Dive

In the early 2000s, Boehm realized that modern systems (built with OOP, components, services) blur the boundary between "development" and "maintenance." The **Non-Legacy Model** is built on these principles:

**Step 1: Maintenance *is* development.**
Every change request is treated as a *mini-development project* — with its own analysis, design, implementation, test, and review cycle.

**Step 2: Spiral/risk-driven approach.**
Boehm's earlier **Spiral Model** is applied to maintenance. Each change goes through: *Define objectives → Identify and resolve risks → Develop & test → Plan next iteration*.

**Step 3: Reuse and COTS are central.**
Instead of fixing old code, the team may **replace** a module with a COTS product or an existing component. Maintenance becomes *evolution* by *substitution*.

**Step 4: Re-engineering and reverse engineering are first-class activities.**
Legacy code can be *systematically restructured* to improve maintainability before further changes are made.

**Step 5: Continuous, planned evolution.**
The system has a **roadmap** of planned enhancements. Maintenance is *scheduled*, not just *requested*.

**Step 6: Risk and value drive prioritization.**
Changes are evaluated by their **business value** and **technical risk** — not by a simple FIFO queue.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Total Life-Cycle Cost (TLCC) | $\text{TLCC} = C_{\text{dev}} + C_{\text{maint}}$ | Sum of development and maintenance cost over full life |
| Maintenance Cost Ratio | $R_{\text{maint}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{maint}}}{\text{TLCC}} \times 100\%$ | Typically 60% to 80% in legacy systems |
| Average Maintenance Effort Distribution | $E_{\text{perfective}} \approx 50\% \;;\; E_{\text{adaptive}} \approx 25\% \;;\; E_{\text{corrective}} \approx 20\% \;;\; E_{\text{preventive}} \approx 5\%$ | Standard empirical Boehm distribution |
| Per-Change Cost Growth | $C_n = C_0 \cdot (1+r)^{n}$ | Compound growth in cost per change at year $n$, rate $r$ |
| Risk Priority (Spiral) | $\text{Risk Priority} = P_{\text{fail}} \times \text{Impact}$ | Probability of failure multiplied by consequence |
| Maintenance Productivity Index | $\text{MPI} = \dfrac{\text{Number of CRs closed}}{\text{Person-months of effort}}$ | Throughput measure for maintenance teams |
| Reuse Savings | $S_{\text{reuse}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{reuse}}}{C_{\text{new}}}$ | Fraction of cost saved by reusing components |
| Maintainability Index (Halstead-derived) | $\text{MI} = 171 - 5.2 \cdot \ln V - 0.23 \cdot CC - 16.2 \cdot \ln L$ | Where $V$ = Halstead volume, $CC$ = cyclomatic complexity, $L$ = LOC |
| Cost of Change Multiplier | $C_{\text{post-release}} = k \cdot C_{\text{pre-release}}$, typically $k \in [10, 100]$ | Defects cost 10$\times$ to 100$\times$ more to fix after release |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin **software economics decisions** — whether to maintain, re-engineer, or replace a legacy system. Maintenance vs. replacement break-even is computed using TLCC projections.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defense:** Legacy systems (e.g., avionics on 30-year-old aircraft) are maintained using the **legacy model**; modern digital cockpits use the **non-legacy** approach.
- **Banking & Finance:** Core banking systems still rely on legacy maintenance, but a *migration layer* applies the non-legacy approach for new digital services.
- **Telecommunications:** Switch software, often 20+ years old, is maintained through structured change management — a hybrid of both models.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Long-Term Maintenance Cost under the Legacy Model

The legacy model is characterized by **compound complexity growth**. Let us derive the total maintenance cost over $N$ years given a per-change cost that grows annually.

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

Let:
- $C_0$ = cost of the *first* change request in year 1
- $r$ = annual growth rate of per-change cost (typically $r \in [0.05, 0.20]$)
- $k$ = average number of change requests per year
- $N$ = total years the system is in maintenance

**Step 2: Cost of changes in year $n$.**

The cost of an average change in year $n$ grows geometrically:

$$
C_n = C_0 \cdot (1+r)^{n}
$$

**Step 3: Total cost in a single year.**

If $k$ changes occur in year $n$, the total cost for that year is:

$$
T_n = k \cdot C_n = k \cdot C_0 \cdot (1+r)^{n}
$$

**Step 4: Total maintenance cost over $N$ years.**

Sum the geometric series from $n = 1$ to $N$:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} k \cdot C_0 \cdot (1+r)^{n}
$$

**Step 5: Factor out the constants.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = k \cdot C_0 \cdot \sum_{n=1}^{N} (1+r)^{n}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the geometric series closed form.**

For a geometric series $\sum_{n=1}^{N} x^n = \dfrac{x \cdot (x^{N} - 1)}{x - 1}$ with $x = 1+r$:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = k \cdot C_0 \cdot \frac{(1+r)\left[(1+r)^{N} - 1\right]}{r}
$$

**Step 7: Final compact expression.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = k \cdot C_0 \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{N+1} - (1+r)}{r}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Take $C_0 = \$\,10{,}000$ per change, $k = 50$ changes/year, $r = 0.10$ (10% annual cost growth), $N = 10$ years.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 50 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{(1.10)^{11} - (1.10)}{0.10}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{11} \approx 2.8531
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 500{,}000 \cdot \frac{2.8531 - 1.10}{0.10} = 500{,}000 \cdot 17.531
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} \approx \$8{,}765{,}500
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Doubling the life of the system from 10 to 20 years does *not* double the maintenance cost — it more than **triples** it due to compound growth. This is why Boehm argued for *re-engineering* every 7–10 years.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boehm's Maintenance Effort Distribution (Lientz & Swanson Data)

Boehm referenced empirical data from Lientz & Swanson's 1980 study. The distribution is derived by classifying a sample of change requests:

**Step 1:** Let $N_{\text{total}} = N_c + N_a + N_p + N_v$ be the total number of change requests, where:
- $N_c$ = corrective
- $N_a$ = adaptive
- $N_p$ = perfective
- $N_v$ = preventive

**Step 2:** Empirical observations from typical enterprise systems:

$$
N_p \approx 0.50 \cdot N_{\text{total}}, \quad N_a \approx 0.25 \cdot N_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
N_c \approx 0.20 \cdot N_{\text{total}}, \quad N_v \approx 0.05 \cdot N_{\text{total}}
$$

**Step 3:** The corresponding effort shares (E) are:

$$
E_{\text{perfective}} \approx 50\%, \quad E_{\text{adaptive}} \approx 25\%
$$

$$
E_{\text{corrective}} \approx 20\%, \quad E_{\text{preventive}} \approx 5\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often think *corrective* (bug fixing) dominates maintenance. In reality, **perfective** (new features) is the largest category, because users keep requesting enhancements.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Legacy vs Non-Legacy

| Dimension | Legacy Maintenance Model (1983) | Non-Legacy Maintenance Model (2000+) |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophy** | Maintenance is post-delivery, separate | Maintenance = new development |
| **Driver** | Defect reports and change requests (reactive) | Business value and risk (proactive) |
| **Process** | Linear: receive → fix → release | Spiral / incremental: plan → risk-analyze → evolve |
| **Team** | Often separate, junior team | Same team handles development and maintenance |
| **Tools** | Basic source control, debuggers | Full IDEs, reverse-engineering, COTS registries |
| **Funding** | Lower than development | Equal to development |
| **Code Strategy** | Modify existing code in place | Reuse, refactor, re-engineer, replace |
| **Maintenance Types** | Corrective + adaptive dominant | Perfective + preventive dominant |
| **Planning Horizon** | Short-term, ad-hoc | Long-term, road-mapped |
| **Risk Handling** | Implicit, post-hoc | Explicit, risk-driven (Spiral) |
| **End-of-Life** | Unplanned, abrupt | Planned retirement / migration |
| **Suitability** | Old, monolithic, COBOL-era systems | Modern, component-based, OO, service-oriented systems |
| **Limit** | Becomes unsustainable as code ages | Requires mature reuse infrastructure |

### 3.4 Decision Logic: When to Use Which Model

$$
\text{Use Legacy Model when:} \quad
\begin{cases}
\text{System age} > 15 \text{ years} \\
\text{Architecture} = \text{monolithic, undocumented} \\
\text{Team skill} = \text{low familiarity with code} \\
\text{Change volume} < 20 \text{ CRs/year}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Use Non-Legacy Model when:} \quad
\begin{cases}
\text{System uses components / services} \\
\text{Continuous deployment pipeline exists} \\
\text{Team has OOP / reuse expertise} \\
\text{Change volume} > 100 \text{ CRs/year} \\
\text{Strong CI/CD and automated testing}
\end{cases}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boehm's Legacy Maintenance Model — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Delivered Software System] --> B1[Operational Phase Begins]
    B1 --> C1[Maintenance Phase Starts]
    C1 --> D1[Receive Change Request CR or Problem Report PR]
    D1 --> E1[Classify Request Type]
    E1 --> F1[Corrective]
    E1 --> F2[Adaptive]
    E1 --> F3[Perfective]
    E1 --> F4[Preventive]
    F1 --> G1[Analyze Impact]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Modify Existing Codebase]
    H1 --> I1[Test the Change]
    I1 --> J1{Defects Found?}
    J1 -->|Yes| G1
    J1 -->|No| K1[Release Patch to Operations]
    K1 --> D1
    K1 --> L1[System Retired Eventually]
```

### 4.2 Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model — Spiral / Incremental Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Existing Software System] --> B2[Define Evolution Objectives]
    B2 --> C2[Identify and Analyze Risks]
    C2 --> D2{Strategy Selection}
    D2 -->|Reuse| E1[Integrate COTS or Existing Component]
    D2 -->|Refactor| E2[Re-engineer Module]
    D2 -->|Replace| E3[Substitute with New Component]
    D2 -->|Extend| E4[Add New Functionality]
    E1 --> F2[Develop and Test Increment]
    E2 --> F2
    E3 --> F2
    E4 --> F2
    F2 --> G2[Customer Review and Feedback]
    G2 --> H2[Plan Next Iteration]
    H2 --> B2
    G2 --> I2[Continuous Evolution]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Side-by-Side Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEGACY[LEGACY MAINTENANCE MODEL 1983]
        L1[Reactive CR Queue] --> L2[Modify Code]
        L2 --> L3[Patch Release]
        L3 --> L1
    end
    subgraph NONLEGACY[NON LEGACY MAINTENANCE MODEL 2000]
        N1[Planned Evolution Roadmap] --> N2[Risk Driven Spiral Cycle]
        N2 --> N3[Reuse or Re-engineer]
        N3 --> N4[Increment Release]
        N4 --> N1
    end
    LEGACY --> COMPARE[Key Shift: From Fixing to Evolving]
    NONLEGACY --> COMPARE
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Legacy vs Non-Legacy Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: New or Existing System] --> S2{System Age and Architecture}
    S2 -->|Old monolithic| S3[Legacy Model]
    S2 -->|Modern component based| S4[Non Legacy Model]
    S3 --> S5[Strength: Stable predictable process]
    S4 --> S6[Strength: Continuous innovation]
    S5 --> S7[Limit: Aging code rising cost]
    S6 --> S8[Limit: Requires mature reuse infrastructure]
    S7 --> S9[Migration Trigger: Re-engineer after 7 to 10 years]
    S8 --> S9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** Notice how the legacy model is a *tight feedback loop* (reactive), whereas the non-legacy model is a *spiraling outward* evolution (proactive). The arrows in the non-legacy diagram return to *Define Objectives*, signalling iterative planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain Boehm's four types of software maintenance activities.*

**Model Answer:**

Barry Boehm classified software maintenance into four activities:

1. **Corrective Maintenance (≈20%):** Fixing residual defects (bugs) discovered after the software is in operation. Aims to restore the system to its specified behavior.
2. **Adaptive Maintenance (≈25%):** Modifying the software to adapt it to changes in the external environment — new operating system, hardware platform, regulatory requirements, or third-party interfaces.
3. **Perfective Maintenance (≈50%):** Adding new functional features, improving usability and performance, in response to evolving user requirements. It is the largest share.
4. **Preventive Maintenance (≈5%):** Restructuring or refactoring code to improve maintainability and prevent future defects, even though no current problem exists.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Naming all four types = 2 marks; brief explanation = 1 mark. Always mention the *empirical percentage share* to show depth.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State any three differences between Boehm's legacy and non-legacy maintenance models.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Legacy Model (1983) | Non-Legacy Model (2000+) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maintenance is a *separate, post-delivery* phase | Maintenance is *equivalent to new development* |
| 2 | *Reactive* — driven by change requests and bug reports | *Proactive* — driven by a planned evolution roadmap |
| 3 | Corrective and adaptive maintenance dominate | Perfective and preventive maintenance dominate |
| 4 | Low reuse; in-place code modification | Heavy reuse of COTS, components, and re-engineered modules |
| 5 | Spiral / incremental / risk-driven *not* used | Spiral / risk-driven approach is central |

*(Any three differences carry 1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (a or b Internal Choice)**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail Boehm's *Legacy Maintenance Model* with its characteristics, advantages, and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain in detail Boehm's *Non-Legacy Maintenance Model*. Compare it with the legacy model on at least five dimensions. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Boehm's Legacy Maintenance Model (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Boehm's legacy maintenance model, proposed in 1983, treats maintenance as a *post-delivery sustainment activity* in which a separate maintenance team handles change requests, bug reports, and adaptations of an already-released system.

**Key Characteristics (3 marks):**
1. **Separate Life-Cycle Phase** — Maintenance begins only after delivery and acceptance; it is a distinct phase, not interleaved with development.
2. **Reactive and User-Driven** — Activities are triggered by *change requests (CRs)* and *problem reports (PRs)* from users; the team waits rather than plans.
3. **Four Maintenance Types** — Corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive, with the empirical effort distribution of 20 : 25 : 50 : 5.
4. **Under-Resourced Team** — Often staffed with less experienced developers and lower budgets than the development team, despite consuming 60–80% of total software cost.
5. **Growing Per-Change Cost** — Due to compound complexity growth, the cost per change increases year over year.
6. **Implicit End-of-Life** — Retirement is often unplanned; systems linger in "legacy graveyards."

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Simple, well-understood process; easy to manage with basic tools.
- Clear separation of roles between developers and maintainers.
- Suitable for stable, rarely-changed systems.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- High long-term cost due to compound complexity.
- Loss of original development knowledge over time.
- Inability to keep up with rapidly changing business requirements.
- No emphasis on reuse, leading to redundant code.
- Reactive culture discourages innovation.

---

### Part (b) — Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The non-legacy maintenance model, articulated by Boehm in the early 2000s, views maintenance as *equivalent to new development*. Each change is treated as a *mini-development project* using an incremental, spiral, risk-driven process with strong emphasis on reuse, re-engineering, and continuous evolution.

**Key Characteristics (3 marks):**
1. **Maintenance = Development** — No sharp phase boundary; the same team handles both.
2. **Spiral / Risk-Driven Process** — Each change goes through: define objectives → identify risks → develop & test → plan next.
3. **Reuse-First Strategy** — New requirements are first checked against COTS catalogues and existing component libraries before custom code is written.
4. **Re-engineering and Refactoring** — Old code is systematically restructured to improve maintainability.
5. **Planned Evolution** — A multi-year roadmap guides enhancements based on business value.
6. **High Tooling Maturity** — IDEs, automated tests, CI/CD pipelines, and reverse-engineering tools are standard.

**Five-Dimension Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Legacy | Non-Legacy |
|---|---|---|
| Phase Boundary | Sharp (post-delivery) | Blurred (continuous) |
| Driver | CRs and PRs (reactive) | Business value & risk (proactive) |
| Process | Linear, ad-hoc | Spiral, iterative |
| Code Strategy | In-place modification | Reuse → Refactor → Replace |
| Team & Tools | Junior team, basic tools | Senior team, full dev environment |

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Discuss the *Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model* in detail. Explain how the Spiral Model is applied to maintenance activities. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A legacy system receives on average 50 change requests per year. The cost of the first change is ₹10,000. If the per-change cost grows at 10% annually, estimate the *total* maintenance cost over 10 years. Justify whether the organization should re-engineer the system instead. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Non-Legacy Model & Spiral Application (7 marks)

**Explanation of Non-Legacy Model (4 marks):**
The non-legacy model, also called the *Maintenance = New Development* model, was Boehm's response to the increasing complexity and pace of change in modern software systems. It recognizes that:
- Modern OO and component-based systems are *designed* to be changed; the same engineering discipline used in initial development must apply to every modification.
- Maintenance is a *continuous, risk-managed* activity, not a residual one.
- The boundary between "new project" and "maintenance project" is artificial and should be dissolved.

**Application of the Spiral Model to Maintenance (3 marks):**
Boehm's Spiral Model, originally developed for new development, is applied to maintenance in four quadrants for *each* significant change:

1. **Quadrant 1 — Determine Objectives, Alternatives, Constraints:** Define what the change should achieve; identify candidate strategies (in-place fix, refactor, COTS replacement, full rewrite); list constraints (cost, time, risk).
2. **Quadrant 2 — Evaluate Alternatives, Identify and Resolve Risks:** For each candidate strategy, analyze risk. Choose the lowest-risk path; resolve risks by prototyping, benchmarking, or stakeholder review.
3. **Quadrant 3 — Develop and Verify the Next-Level Product:** Implement the chosen strategy with full development discipline — design, code, unit test, integration test.
4. **Quadrant 4 — Plan Next Phases:** Review the outcome with stakeholders, update the evolution roadmap, and decide on the next iteration.

This cycle repeats for every change, making maintenance a *continuous, planned evolution* rather than a reactive firefight.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Estimation of Maintenance Cost (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $k = 50$ change requests per year
- $C_0 = ₹\,10{,}000$ per change (year 1)
- $r = 0.10$ (10% annual growth)
- $N = 10$ years

**Step 1: Apply the legacy model formula** *(derived in Section 3.1)*:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = k \cdot C_0 \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{N+1} - (1+r)}{r}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 50 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{(1.10)^{11} - (1.10)}{0.10}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $(1.10)^{11}$:**

$$
(1.10)^{11} \approx 2.8531
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 500{,}000 \cdot \frac{2.8531 - 1.10}{0.10}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 500{,}000 \cdot \frac{1.7531}{0.10} = 500{,}000 \cdot 17.531
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} \approx ₹\,8{,}765{,}500
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation and Decision (2 marks):**

Over 10 years, the organization will spend approximately **₹ 87.66 lakhs** in maintenance, of which the *last* year alone costs more than the *first 3 years combined* due to compound growth.

- Cost in year 1: $50 \times 10{,}000 = ₹\,5{,}00{,}000$
- Cost in year 10: $50 \times 10{,}000 \times (1.10)^9 \approx ₹\,11{,}72{,}000$

**Recommendation (1 mark):** Yes, the organization should consider *re-engineering* (non-legacy approach) before year 7–8. A one-time re-engineering investment (estimated at 1.5× to 2× the annual maintenance cost) can *reset* the complexity curve, drop $C_0$ significantly, and reduce the 10-year cumulative cost. If the system's business value justifies continued operation, the non-legacy model offers a better long-term return.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing "Maintenance" with "Bug Fixing":** Many students define maintenance only as corrective. You *will* lose marks if you omit adaptive, perfective, and preventive types.
> 2. **Forgetting the percentages:** In any question on maintenance types, including the empirical 20/25/50/5 distribution earns you an extra mark and shows depth.
> 3. **Mixing up legacy vs non-legacy:** Legacy = reactive + post-delivery. Non-legacy = proactive + maintenance = development. Do *not* write them as synonymous.
> 4. **Numerical errors in geometric series:** When computing compound cost, students often forget the $(1+r)^{N+1}$ exponent (off-by-one error). Always check your base case: at $N = 1$, the formula should give $k \cdot C_0 \cdot (1+r)$.
> 5. **Skipping justification in design questions:** In 14-mark questions, "compare" or "justify" sub-parts require a *conclusion* — do not leave the comparison hanging.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Boehm proposed TWO maintenance models** — legacy (1983) and non-legacy (2000+). Both are syllabus-essential.
- ✅ **Legacy Model:** maintenance is *post-delivery*, *reactive*, *separate* from development, *under-resourced*.
- ✅ **Non-Legacy Model:** maintenance *is* development; *proactive*, *spiral*, *reuse-driven*, *planned*.
- ✅ **Four Maintenance Types** with empirical effort share: Perfective **50%** > Adaptive **25%** > Corrective **20%** > Preventive **5%**.
- ✅ **Maintenance consumes 60%–80%** of total software life-cycle cost in legacy systems.
- ✅ **Compound cost growth formula:** $T_{\text{total}} = k \cdot C_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{N+1} - (1+r)}{r}$.
- ✅ **Per-change cost growth** is typically 5%–20% per year; the *cost of change multiplier* post-release is **10× to 100×** pre-release.
- ✅ **Spiral Model** is the engine of the non-legacy approach — four quadrants repeated for every change.
- ✅ **Re-engineering** is a first-class activity in the non-legacy model, used to "reset" the complexity curve.
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Legacy model suits old monolithic systems with < 20 CRs/year; non-legacy suits modern, component-based systems with > 100 CRs/year and mature CI/CD.
- ✅ **Key terms** to define in any answer: *change request (CR)*, *problem report (PR)*, *re-engineering*, *reverse engineering*, *COTS*, *spiral model*, *re-engineering index*.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** confusing "perfective" with "preventive"; treating maintenance as bug-fixing only; forgetting the percentages; mixing up the two models.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for the Two Models:**
> **L**egacy = **L**agging, **L**inear, **L**ow-budget.
> **N**on-Legacy = **N**ew development style, **N**imble spiral, **N**o phase boundary.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk and its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk and its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Risk

In the context of **Software Engineering** (as per the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECST411 syllabus**), **Risk** is formally defined as:

> **Risk** is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a **positive or negative effect** on one or more **project objectives** such as scope, schedule, cost, and quality.

A risk is characterized by two key parameters:
- **Probability (P)** — the likelihood that the risk event will occur.
- **Impact (I)** — the consequence (loss/gain) on the project if the event occurs.

Mathematically, the basic exposure is expressed as:
$$\text{Risk Exposure} = P(\text{Probability}) \times I(\text{Impact})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** A risk is **NOT** a problem that has already happened. It is a *future, uncertain* event. Problems = Issues (definite). Risks = Possibilities (uncertain). The Board Examiner specifically tests this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **trekking through a dense Western Ghats forest** 🌲 (Kerala context). You know that:
- Heavy rain **might** occur → that is a **risk** (uncertain).
- The path **could** be slippery → that is a **risk**.
- Your bag **could** tear → that is a **risk**.

You don't stop trekking — instead, you pack a raincoat, wear grip shoes, and carry a strong bag. That preparation is **Risk Management**.

Similarly, in software projects:
- Customer requirements **might** change → Risk.
- A key developer **might** resign → Risk.
- Server outage **might** occur during deployment → Risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two sides of Risk:**
> - **Negative Risk (Threat):** Will harm the project (e.g., delay, budget overrun).
> - **Positive Risk (Opportunity):** Will benefit the project (e.g., new technology reduces cost).

## 1.3 Why is Risk Management Important?

- **Software projects are inherently risky** — high failure rate (~70% according to industry reports like CHAOS Report).
- Unmanaged risks lead to **schedule slippage, cost overruns, and quality degradation**.
- A formal risk management process is mandated by the **IEEE Standard 1540** and the **SEI/CMMI framework**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the student's ability to (a) define risk, (b) classify types of risks, and (c) outline the risk management process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Risks (Classification Framework)

Risks in software projects are classified along multiple dimensions. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECST411 Module 4**, the following classification is high-yield:

### A. Based on the **Nature / Source** of Risk

| # | Risk Type | Description | Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Project Risks** | Risks that affect the **schedule, cost, or resources** of the project. | Slipped schedule, wrong cost estimation. |
| 2 | **Technical Risks** | Risks affecting **quality, design, implementation, and technology**. | Use of untested third-party APIs, poor architecture. |
| 3 | **Business Risks** | Risks that threaten the **viability of the software** in the market. | Competitor launches similar product, loss of customer support. |
| 4 | **Product Risks** | Risks affecting the **product being built** (functionality, performance). | Unacceptable performance, security vulnerabilities. |
| 5 | **Process Risks** | Risks related to the **SDLC process** followed. | Inadequate testing process, skipping reviews. |

### B. Based on **Knowledge / Predictability**

| # | Risk Type | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Known Risks** | The project team can **identify and prepare** for these in advance. |
| 2 | **Unknown Risks** | The team **cannot anticipate** these — they arise from outside the project control. |
| 3 | **Knowable Unknowns** | Risks whose **existence is known** but whose **occurrence is uncertain**. |
| 4 | **Unknowable Unknowns** | Risks whose **existence itself is unknown** — true black swans. |

### C. Other Categorizations Often Asked in KTU

- **Generic Risks** → Common to all projects (e.g., staff turnover).
- **Product-Specific Risks** → Specific to a particular product.

## 2.2 The Risk Management Process (KTU 2024 Module 4 Core Topic)

Risk Management is a **continuous, iterative process** consisting of **four (sometimes six) phases**:

1. **Risk Identification** — *What can go wrong?*
2. **Risk Analysis (Assessment)** — *How likely? How severe?*
3. **Risk Planning (Response Planning)** — *What will we do?*
4. **Risk Monitoring & Control** — *Did the plan work? Are new risks emerging?*

## 2.3 Risk Identification Techniques

- ✅ **Risk Checklists** — Lists derived from past projects.
- ✅ **Brainstorming** — Group ideation sessions.
- ✅ **Delphi Technique** — Anonymous expert consensus.
- ✅ **SWOT Analysis** — Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats.
- ✅ **Cause-Effect Diagrams** — Ishikawa fishbone diagrams.
- ✅ **Expert Judgment** — Consultation with subject-matter experts.

## 2.4 Risk Analysis (Qualitative vs Quantitative)

### Qualitative Analysis
- Uses the **Risk Probability-Impact (P-I) Matrix**.
- Categorical values: **Low, Medium, High** (sometimes 1-5 scale).
- Output: A **prioritized list of risks**.

### Quantitative Analysis
- Uses numerical techniques like:
  - **Probability Distribution Models**
  - **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**
  - **Decision Trees**
  - **Monte Carlo Simulation**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|------------------|------------|-----|
| **Risk Exposure (RE)** | $RE = P \times I$ | Quantifies overall risk impact. |
| **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = P \times C$ | Quantitative cost-based risk analysis. |
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = P \times S \times D$ | Used in FMEA-style analysis (Probability × Severity × Detectability). |
| **Risk Reduction Leverage (RRL)** | $RRL = \frac{RE_{\text{before}} - RE_{\text{after}}}{Cost_{\text{of reduction}}}$ | Measures efficiency of mitigation strategy. |
| **P-I Matrix Cell Value** | $\text{Value} = P_{\text{rank}} \times I_{\text{rank}}$ | Used in qualitative risk categorization. |
| **Total Project Risk (TPR)** | $TPR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times I_i)$ | Sum of all individual risk exposures. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Note:** Risk Exposure, EMV, and RPN are **dimensionless ratios** (or in currency units for EMV). The P-I matrix uses **rank values (1–5)** as per **IEEE 1540** standards.

## 2.6 Risk Response / Planning Strategies

| Strategy | Meaning | When to Use |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Avoidance** | Eliminate the risk by changing the plan. | When the impact is catastrophic. |
| **Minimization (Mitigation)** | Reduce P or I to acceptable levels. | For most high-likelihood risks. |
| **Contingency Planning** | Pre-defined action plan if risk occurs. | For risks with residual exposure. |
| **Transfer** | Shift risk to a third party (e.g., insurance, outsourcing). | For financial/operational risks. |
| **Acceptance (Retention)** | Acknowledge and absorb the risk. | When risk exposure is small. |
| **Exploitation (for Opportunities)** | Ensure the positive risk occurs. | For opportunities that benefit the project. |
| **Enhancement (for Opportunities)** | Increase P or I of a positive risk. | To capitalize on favorable conditions. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** **"A-M-C-T-A-E-E"** → **A**void, **M**inimize, **C**ontingency, **T**ransfer, **A**ccept, **E**xploit, **E**nhance.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used in **NASA mission-critical software** (e.g., Mars Rover fault tolerance).
- Applied in **banking/fintech** for security and regulatory risks.
- Embedded in **agile sprints** as "risk burndown" charts.
- Mandated by **CMMI Level 3+** organizations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Exposure Calculation

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

Let:
- $P$ = Probability of the risk event occurring (0 ≤ P ≤ 1).
- $I$ = Impact (cost, time, or quality loss) in respective units.

**Step 2: Express the basic risk equation.**

By definition, the exposure of a single risk to the project is the **product** of how likely it is and how bad it would be:

$$RE = P \times I$$

**Step 3: Justify the multiplication.**

- If $P = 0$, the event will not occur → exposure must be **zero**.
- If $I = 0$, the event has no consequence → exposure must be **zero**.
- A linear increase in either factor should proportionally increase exposure → hence **multiplication** (not addition).

**Step 4: Extend to multiple risks (Total Project Risk).**

For a project with $n$ independent risks:

$$TPR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} RE_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times I_i)$$

**Step 5: Apply independence assumption (caveat).**

The summation above assumes risks are **statistically independent**. For correlated risks, variance-based models are used:

$$Var(TPR) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2 + 2 \sum_{i<j} Cov(RE_i, RE_j)$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, the **independent assumption** is the default unless the question explicitly states dependence.

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

**Step 1: Define EMV concept.**

EMV is the **average monetary outcome** of a risk event over many occurrences.

**Step 2: Base formula.**

$$EMV = P_{\text{event}} \times C_{\text{event}}$$

where:
- $P_{\text{event}}$ = probability the event occurs.
- $C_{\text{event}}$ = monetary cost (positive value) of the event.

**Step 3: Account for multiple scenarios (generalized form).**

For $n$ mutually exclusive scenarios:

$$EMV_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \times V_j$$

where $V_j$ is the value (positive or negative) of scenario $j$.

**Step 4: Numerical Worked Example.**

A software project estimates:
- 30% chance of a server outage costing ₹2,00,000.
- 10% chance of a security breach costing ₹10,00,000.
- 60% chance of no incident.

Then:

$$EMV = (0.30 \times 2{,}00{,}000) + (0.10 \times 10{,}00{,}000) + (0.60 \times 0)$$

$$EMV = 60{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 0$$

$$EMV = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** The project should budget approximately **₹1,60,000** as a contingency reserve for this risk category.

## 3.3 Derivation: Risk Reduction Leverage (RRL)

**Step 1: Define purpose.**

RRL measures the **efficiency** of a mitigation strategy in terms of risk reduction per unit cost.

**Step 2: Formula derivation.**

Let:
- $RE_{\text{before}}$ = Risk Exposure before mitigation.
- $RE_{\text{after}}$ = Risk Exposure after mitigation.
- $C_{\text{mitigation}}$ = Cost of implementing the mitigation.

The reduction in exposure is:

$$\Delta RE = RE_{\text{before}} - RE_{\text{after}}$$

The leverage (efficiency) is:

$$RRL = \frac{\Delta RE}{C_{\text{mitigation}}}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

- $RRL > 1$ → Mitigation is **cost-effective**.
- $RRL = 1$ → Mitigation cost equals exposure reduction (break-even).
- $RRL < 1$ → Mitigation is **not cost-effective** — consider acceptance.

**Step 4: Worked Example.**

- Initial risk exposure: $RE_{\text{before}} = 0.4 \times 100 = 40$.
- After investing ₹15 in mitigation: $RE_{\text{after}} = 0.1 \times 100 = 10$.
- Cost of mitigation: ₹15.

$$RRL = \frac{40 - 10}{15} = \frac{30}{15} = 2.0$$

✅ Since $RRL = 2.0 > 1$, the mitigation is **highly cost-effective**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: P-I (Probability-Impact) Grid

The standard qualitative risk matrix is a **5×5 grid** with values assigned per IEEE 1540:

| Probability ↓ / Impact → | Very Low (1) | Low (2) | Moderate (3) | High (4) | Very High (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Very High (5)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **High (4)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **Moderate (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **Low (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Very Low (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Cell Value Formula:**

$$CV = P_{\text{rank}} \times I_{\text{rank}}$$

**Color Coding (standard practice):**
- 🟢 **Green (CV ≤ 4):** Acceptable — monitor.
- 🟡 **Yellow (5 ≤ CV ≤ 12):** Mitigate — plan response.
- 🔴 **Red (CV ≥ 15):** Critical — escalate / avoid.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Risk Management Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Risk Identification]
    B --> C[Risk Classification]
    C --> D[Qualitative Analysis - P-I Matrix]
    D --> E[Quantitative Analysis - EMV / Monte Carlo]
    E --> F{Is Risk Acceptable?}
    F -->|No| G[Risk Response Planning]
    F -->|Yes| H[Document and Monitor]
    G --> G1[Avoidance]
    G --> G2[Minimization]
    G --> G3[Contingency]
    G --> G4[Transfer]
    G --> G5[Acceptance]
    G1 --> I[Risk Monitoring and Control]
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    G4 --> I
    G5 --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{New Risks Detected?}
    J -->|Yes| B
    J -->|No| K[Project Closure]
```

## 4.2 Risk Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Software Project Risks] --> PR[Project Risks]
    R --> TR[Technical Risks]
    R --> BR[Business Risks]
    R --> PDR[Product Risks]
    R --> PSR[Process Risks]
    PR --> PR1[Schedule Slippage]
    PR --> PR2[Cost Overrun]
    PR --> PR3[Resource Shortage]
    TR --> TR1[Technology Obsolescence]
    TR --> TR2[Integration Failure]
    TR --> TR3[Algorithm Complexity]
    BR --> BR1[Market Loss]
    BR --> BR2[Regulatory Change]
    BR --> BR3[Competitor Product]
    PDR --> PDR1[Performance Issues]
    PDR --> PDR2[Security Vulnerabilities]
    PDR --> PDR3[Usability Defects]
    PSR --> PSR1[Inadequate Testing]
    PSR --> PSR2[Skipped Reviews]
    PSR --> PSR3[Poor Configuration Management]
```

## 4.3 Risk Response Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identified Risk] --> Q1{Is Probability HIGH?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Impact SEVERE?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is Impact SEVERE?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| AV[Avoidance Strategy]
    Q2 -->|No| MI[Minimization Strategy]
    Q3 -->|Yes| TR[Transfer Strategy]
    Q3 -->|No| AC[Acceptance Strategy]
    AV --> EX[Execute Plan]
    MI --> EX
    TR --> EX
    AC --> EX
    EX --> MN[Continuous Monitoring]
    MN --> RR[Re-evaluate Risk Exposure]
    RR --> Start
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RMMM (Risk Mitigation, Monitoring, Management)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        A1[Project Plan]
        A2[Past Risk Database]
        A3[Expert Inputs]
    end
    subgraph ID[Identification Engine]
        B1[Risk Checklists]
        B2[Brainstorming Module]
        B3[SWOT Analyzer]
    end
    subgraph AN[Analysis Engine]
        C1[Qualitative P-I Matrix]
        C2[Quantitative EMV Calculator]
        C3[Monte Carlo Simulator]
    end
    subgraph PL[Planning Engine]
        D1[Avoidance Planner]
        D2[Mitigation Planner]
        D3[Contingency Planner]
    end
    subgraph MN[Monitoring Engine]
        E1[Risk Register Updater]
        E2[Trigger Event Detector]
        E3[Status Reporter]
    end
    IN --> ID --> AN --> PL --> MN
    MN -.Feedback Loop.-> ID
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Hint:** When asked to "explain the risk management process", drawing the **4-phase cycle** (Identify → Analyze → Plan → Monitor) and labelling it as **continuous** fetches full marks.
