Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **Sigma Level** of the end-to-end process if total defects = 95, units = 4000, opportunities = 5. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution (a): **Importance of Statistical Tools in Six Sigma for Software QA:** Six Sigma relies on **statistical thinking** — every decision must be backed by data, not intuition. In software engineering, statistical tools help in: - Quantifying defect rates (DPMO) - Identifying process variation (σ) - Establishing process capability (Cp, Cpk) - Predicting defects before they occur - Tracking improvements over time **Three DMAIC Tools with Examples:** 1. **Pareto Chart** *(Analyze Phase)* - A bar chart showing defects sorted by frequency, with a cumulative line. - **Example**: A Pareto chart of 200 bugs in an app shows that **"null pointer exceptions"** and **"input validation errors"** together account for **70%** of defects → focus improvements on these. 2. **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** *(Analyze Phase)* - A cause-and-effect diagram categorizing potential defect causes into **6Ms**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment). - **Example**: A fishbone diagram for "high bug escape rate" might reveal causes in **Method** (no peer reviews), **Man** (untrained junior devs), **Measurement** (no static analysis tools). 3. **Statistical Process Control (SPC) Chart** *(Control Phase)* - A line chart with upper/lower control limits (typically ±3σ) tracking process performance over time. - **Example**: Weekly plot of **post-release defects** — if points fall outside control limits, the process is "out of control" and needs intervention. *(Award: 3 marks for statistical importance, 4 marks for 3 tools with examples — 1.3 marks each.)* ##### Model Solution (b): **Part 1: RTY Calculation** **Step 1**: RTY formula. $$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$$ **Step 2**: Substitute. $$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.93 \times 0.90 \times 0.94$$ **Step 3**: Compute sequentially. $$0.96 \times 0.93 = 0.8928$$ $$0.8928 \times 0.90 = 0.80352$$ $$0.80352 \times 0.94 = 0.755309$$ **Step 4**: Express as percentage. $$\text{RTY} \approx 75.53\%$$ **Part 2: Sigma Level Calculation** **Step 5**: DPMO formula. $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$$ **Step 6**: Substitute. $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{95}{4{,}000 \times 5} \times 10^6$$ **Step 7**: Compute. $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{95}{20{,}000} \times 10^6 = 0.00475 \times 10^6 = 4{,}750$$ **Step 8**: Map to sigma level — 4,750 DPMO falls between **233 (5σ)** and **6,210 (4σ)**, so sigma level ≈ **4.2σ**. > **Conclusion**: The end-to-end process is at **~4.2σ** quality. Despite individual phase FPYs above 90%, the **compounded RTY of only 75.53%** reveals significant rework — a clear case for Six Sigma's DMAIC intervention. *(Award: 3.5 marks for RTY, 3.5 marks for DPMO & sigma level.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > - ❌ **Confusing DMAIC with DMADV** — Last letters differ (**C vs V**); remember: DMAIC *improves* existing process, DMADV *designs* new ones. > - ❌ **Forgetting the $10^6$ factor in DPMO** — Many students compute DPO (Defects Per Opportunity) and forget to multiply by 1 million. > - ❌ **Wrong sigma table reference** — Memorize: **3.4 DPMO = 6σ**; **233 = 5σ**; **6,210 = 4σ**. KTU expects these exact values. > - ❌ **Mixing units in RTY** — Convert percentages to decimals (0.95, not 95) before multiplying. > - ❌ **Ignoring "Opportunities"** in software — Each module may have many defect opportunities (security, performance, UI, etc.); never assume it's 1. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🎯 **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.9997% yield** — the defining benchmark. - 🎯 **DMAIC phases**: Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. - 🎯 **DMADV phases**: Define → Measure → Analyze → Design → Verify. - 🎯 **DPMO formula** — always multiply by $10^6$ at the end. - 🎯 **Sigma Conversion Table** values must be memorized: 3.4, 233, 6,210, 66,807. - 🎯 **RTY** = product of FPYs across all SDLC phases (use decimal form). - 🎯 **Six Sigma originated at Motorola (1986)**, popularized by GE's Jack Welch. - 🎯 **Belt Hierarchy**: White → Yellow → Green → Black → Master Black → Champion. - 🎯 **CTQ** = Critical-to-Quality characteristics; **VOC** = Voice of Customer. - 🎯 **Cp vs Cpk** — Cp ignores centering, Cpk considers it; **Cpk ≤ Cp** always. - 🎯 **Six Sigma complements CMMI** — Six Sigma focuses on defect reduction, CMMI focuses on process maturity. - 🎯 **KTU exam favorite tools to remember**: Pareto, Fishbone, SPC Charts, SIPOC, FMEA. - 🎯 **Statistical Z-score** $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ is the foundation of all sigma level calculations.

topics.title: Cloud-based Software - Virtualisation and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud-based Software - Virtualisation and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud-Based Software Engineering: The Paradigm Shift

Modern software engineering has shifted from monolithic on-premise deployments to **Cloud-Based Software**, where applications are designed, developed, tested, deployed, and scaled using distributed computing resources over the internet. The two foundational pillars that enable this paradigm are **Virtualization** and **Containerization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Alignment):**
> **Virtualization** is the technology that creates virtual (abstracted) representations of physical computing resources—servers, storage, networks, and operating systems—enabling multiple operating systems or applications to run concurrently on a single physical machine.
>
> **Containerization** is a lightweight form of OS-level virtualization that packages an application along with its libraries, dependencies, and configuration files into a single, portable, executable unit called a **container**, which runs consistently across any computing environment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Housing Model

To make these concepts intuitive, consider the analogy of housing:

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | What It Means |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Server** | Empty plot of land | Raw hardware with CPU, RAM, disk |
| **Virtual Machine (VM)** | Standalone apartment building | Complete building with its own plumbing, electricity, walls (own Guest OS) |
| **Container** | Rooms in a co-living hostel | Shared plumbing and electricity (shared Host OS kernel), but private rooms (isolated app + dependencies) |
| **Hypervisor** | Building architect/floor manager | Manages multiple apartment buildings on a plot of land |
| **Container Engine (Docker)** | Hostel warden | Manages rooms (containers) on a single shared floor (host OS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** Students must clearly articulate that VMs virtualize the **hardware layer** (heavyweight, GBs in size, boot in minutes), whereas containers virtualize the **OS layer** (lightweight, MBs in size, boot in milliseconds). This single distinction is the most frequently asked 3-mark question in this module.

## 1.3 Why These Technologies Matter in Software Engineering

- **Reproducibility**: "Works on my machine" syndrome is eliminated.
- **Scalability**: Horizontal scaling via container replication.
- **Resource Efficiency**: **Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)** typically ranges from **5:1 to 20:1** for VMs and **50:1 to 100:1** for containers.
- **DevOps Enablement**: Enables CI/CD pipelines, microservices, and immutable infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Metric:** The industry benchmark for container startup time is **< 1 second**, while VM boot time is **30–120 seconds**. The memory overhead for a container is **< 50 MB**, whereas a VM typically requires **1–4 GB** of overhead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Virtualization: Architecture and Taxonomy

Virtualization is implemented through a software layer called the **Hypervisor** (also known as Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM). The hypervisor creates, runs, and manages virtual machines.

### 2.1.1 Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal)

- Runs **directly on the host's hardware** without an underlying OS.
- Used in enterprise data centers and cloud providers.
- Examples: **VMware ESXi**, **Microsoft Hyper-V**, **Xen**, **KVM**.

### 2.1.2 Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted)

- Runs as an application **on top of a conventional host operating system**.
- Used for development, testing, and learning environments.
- Examples: **Oracle VirtualBox**, **VMware Workstation**, **Parallels Desktop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Comparison:** Type-1 hypervisors offer **near-native performance** with only **1–3% overhead**, while Type-2 hypervisors incur **5–15% overhead** due to the dual OS layer.

## 2.2 Containerization: The Linux Foundation

Containers leverage three core Linux kernel features:

1. **Namespaces**: Provide process isolation (PID, Network, Mount, UTS, IPC, User namespaces). Each container sees its own isolated view of system resources.
2. **Control Groups (cgroups)**: Enforce resource limits (CPU shares, memory caps, block I/O, network bandwidth).
3. **Union File Systems (UnionFS)**: Layered filesystem (e.g., OverlayFS) enabling image layering and copy-on-write semantics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why' Behind Containers:** Traditional VMs virtualize hardware using a hypervisor. Containers virtualize the **OS kernel** by sharing the host kernel, which eliminates the need for a separate Guest OS. This is why a container image for a Linux application can be as small as **5–10 MB**, while the equivalent VM image is **> 1 GB**.

## 2.3 Container Orchestration: Kubernetes

When an application scales to hundreds or thousands of containers, manual management becomes infeasible. **Kubernetes (K8s)** is the de-facto industry-standard container orchestrator that automates:

- **Deployment** of containerized applications
- **Scaling** (horizontal pod autoscaling)
- **Self-healing** (restart failed containers, replace nodes)
- **Service discovery** and load balancing
- **Rolling updates** and rollbacks
- **Secret and configuration management**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all key engineering formulas and metrics required for KTU 2024 numerical and descriptive problems on this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Safeguard:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used instead of the markdown table-breaking `\vert` character.

| Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Server Consolidation Ratio | $SCR = \dfrac{N_{physical}}{N_{virtual}}$ | Efficiency gain from virtualization | **5:1 to 20:1** (VMs) |
| CPU Oversubscription Ratio | $R_{cpu} = \dfrac{\sum vCPU_{allocated}}{Cores_{physical}}$ | Ratio of virtual to physical CPU cores | **2:1 to 6:1** |
| Memory Overcommit Ratio | $R_{mem} = \dfrac{M_{allocated}}{M_{physical}}$ | Ratio of allocated to physical memory | **1.0:1 to 1.5:1** |
| Container Density | $D_{container} = \dfrac{N_{containers}}{Resources_{host}}$ | Number of containers per host | **50–100 per host** |
| VM Density | $D_{vm} = \dfrac{N_{vms}}{Resources_{host}}$ | Number of VMs per host | **10–20 per host** |
| Availability (Cloud SLA) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | System uptime percentage | **99.9% (Three 9s)** to **99.999% (Five 9s)** |
| Downtime per Year | $T_{down} = 525600 \times (1 - A)$ | Annual downtime in minutes | **52.56 min** (99.99%) |
| Cost per VM-Hour | $C_{vm} = \dfrac{C_{hardware} + C_{power}}{T_{lifetime} \times N_{vms}}$ | TCO of a single VM | Varies by tier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used by **Site Reliability Engineers (SREs)** in capacity planning, by **Cloud Architects** in designing multi-tenant platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP), and by **DevOps engineers** in cost-optimization reports submitted to KTU-style engineering viva panels.

## 2.5 Virtualization vs. Containerization: The Master Comparison

| Dimension | Virtual Machine (VM) | Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Layer** | Hardware | Operating System |
| **Boot Time** | **30 – 120 seconds** | **< 1 second** |
| **Image Size** | **1 – 50 GB** | **5 – 500 MB** |
| **OS Requirement** | Independent Guest OS | Shares Host OS kernel |
| **Isolation** | Strong (hardware-level) | Process-level (weaker) |
| **Performance** | Near-native (1–3% overhead) | Native (negligible overhead) |
| **Security** | Higher (separate kernel) | Lower (shared kernel) |
| **Use Case** | Legacy apps, multi-OS, strong isolation | Microservices, cloud-native, DevOps |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR) and Cost Analysis

This derivation is essential for KTU 2024 numerical problems and engineering economics viva questions.

### Problem Statement
A mid-sized engineering company originally operated **20 physical servers**, each consuming **500 W** of power, with annual maintenance of **₹50,000 per server**. After virtualization, the same workload is handled by **4 physical servers** running **20 VMs**. Calculate the SCR, power savings, and annual cost reduction.

### Step 1: Compute the Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)

$$SCR = \dfrac{N_{physical}^{original}}{N_{virtual}^{new}}$$

Substitute the given values into the SCR formula:

$$SCR = \dfrac{20}{20} = 1:1 \text{ (per physical host)}$$

Since 20 VMs run on 4 physical hosts, the effective consolidation per host is:

$$SCR_{host} = \dfrac{20 \text{ VMs}}{4 \text{ hosts}} = 5:1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each physical host now runs **5 virtual machines**, yielding a 5:1 consolidation ratio—a typical industry standard for general-purpose workloads.

### Step 2: Compute the Power Savings

Original total power consumption (before virtualization):

$$P_{original} = 20 \text{ servers} \times 500 \text{ W} = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$

New total power consumption (after virtualization, including hypervisor overhead of 3%):

$$P_{new} = 4 \text{ servers} \times 500 \text{ W} \times 1.03 = 2060 \text{ W} = 2.06 \text{ kW}$$

Power saved per hour:

$$P_{saved} = P_{original} - P_{new} = 10 \text{ kW} - 2.06 \text{ kW} = 7.94 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 3% multiplier accounts for hypervisor-induced CPU overhead. In a Type-1 hypervisor, this is closer to 1–2%, while in Type-2 it is 5–10%. For KTU numericals, always explicitly state the assumed overhead.

### Step 3: Compute the Annual Cost Reduction

Original annual maintenance cost:

$$C_{original} = 20 \times 50{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹10 Lakhs)}$$

New annual maintenance cost (16 servers decommissioned):

$$C_{new} = 4 \times 50{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹2 Lakhs)}$$

Annual maintenance cost saved:

$$C_{saved} = C_{original} - C_{new} = ₹8{,}00{,}000$$

Assuming industrial electricity cost of **₹8 per kWh** and 24/7 operation (8760 hours/year):

$$E_{saved} = 7.94 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 69{,}554 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$E_{cost} = 69{,}554 \times ₹8 = ₹5{,}56{,}432 \text{ per year}$$

Total annual savings (maintenance + electricity):

$$S_{total} = ₹8{,}00{,}000 + ₹5{,}56{,}432 = ₹13{,}56{,}432 \text{ (≈ ₹13.56 Lakhs)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only when students show **all three components**—(1) SCR computation, (2) power calculation with overhead factor, and (3) cost breakdown. Skipping the overhead factor loses 2 marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Between VMs and Containers

The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU answer framework.

| Decision Criterion | Choose **VM** When... | Choose **Container** When... |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Type** | Legacy monolithic, Windows-based | Cloud-native, microservices, Linux-based |
| **Security/Isolation** | Strict multi-tenancy, regulated workloads (Banking, Defense) | Trusted internal microservices |
| **Boot Time Required** | Minutes are acceptable | Sub-second startup needed (serverless, autoscaling) |
| **Resource Footprint** | Workload is heavy (DB, ML training) | Workload is lightweight (API gateway, web tier) |
| **Kernel Dependency** | Different OS kernels required (Linux + Windows) | Single kernel (Linux) is sufficient |
| **DevOps Maturity** | Traditional CI/CD | GitOps, Kubernetes, immutable infrastructure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`) and prefixed with letters where needed. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only, with no markdown formatting tags.

## 4.1 Type-1 vs. Type-2 Hypervisor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1["APPLICATION A"] --> n2["GUEST OS LINUX"]
    n3["APPLICATION B"] --> n4["GUEST OS WINDOWS"]
    n2 --> n5["HYPERVISOR TYPE 1 ESXI OR KVM"]
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6["PHYSICAL HARDWARE CPU RAM DISK"]

    n7["APPLICATION A"] --> n8["GUEST OS LINUX"]
    n9["APPLICATION B"] --> n10["GUEST OS WINDOWS"]
    n8 --> n11["HYPERVISOR TYPE 2 VIRTUALBOX"]
    n10 --> n11
    n11 --> n12["HOST OPERATING SYSTEM"]
    n12 --> n13["PHYSICAL HARDWARE CPU RAM DISK"]
```

## 4.2 VM vs. Container Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "VM STACK"
        v1["APP A"] --> v2["GUEST OS"]
        v3["APP B"] --> v4["GUEST OS"]
        v2 --> v5["HYPERVISOR"]
        v4 --> v5
        v5 --> v6["HOST OS"]
        v6 --> v7["HARDWARE"]
    end

    subgraph "CONTAINER STACK"
        c1["APP A"] --> c2["CONTAINER ENGINE DOCKER"]
        c3["APP B"] --> c2
        c4["APP C"] --> c2
        c2 --> c5["HOST OS KERNEL"]
        c5 --> c6["HARDWARE"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Docker High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n20["DEVELOPER WORKSTATION"] --> n21["DOCKERFILE"]
    n21 --> n22["DOCKER IMAGE LAYERED FS"]
    n22 --> n23["DOCKER REGISTRY DOCKER HUB OR ECR"]
    n23 --> n24["DOCKER ENGINE ON HOST"]
    n24 --> n25["CONTAINER 1 RUNNING INSTANCE"]
    n24 --> n26["CONTAINER 2 RUNNING INSTANCE"]
    n24 --> n27["CONTAINER 3 RUNNING INSTANCE"]
```

## 4.4 Kubernetes Cluster Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "CONTROL PLANE"
        k1["API SERVER"]
        k2["SCHEDULER"]
        k3["CONTROLLER MANAGER"]
        k4["ETCD KEY VALUE STORE"]
    end

    subgraph "WORKER NODE 1"
        w1["KUBELET"]
        w2["POD A CONTAINER 1 CONTAINER 2"]
        w3["POD B CONTAINER 3"]
    end

    subgraph "WORKER NODE 2"
        w4["KUBELET"]
        w5["POD C CONTAINER 4"]
        w6["POD D CONTAINER 5 CONTAINER 6"]
    end

    k1 --> w1
    k1 --> w4
    k2 --> w1
    k2 --> w4
    k3 --> w1
    k3 --> w4
    k4 --> k1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Mapping (used as fallback for complex physical diagrams):** The above Kubernetes diagram represents the canonical **Control Plane / Worker Node** separation mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus. The **Control Plane** makes global decisions (scheduling, scaling, healing), while **Worker Nodes** execute the actual application workloads encapsulated in **Pods** (the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, containing one or more containers).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Virtualization and Containerization.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Virtualization | Containerization |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer Virtualized** | Hardware layer | Operating system layer |
| **Component Used** | Hypervisor (Type-1 / Type-2) | Container engine (Docker, containerd) |
| **OS Requirement** | Separate Guest OS per VM | Shares the host OS kernel |
| **Size & Speed** | Heavyweight (GBs, minutes to boot) | Lightweight (MBs, milliseconds to boot) |
| **Isolation Level** | Hardware-level (strong) | Process-level (weaker) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Key:** Award **3 marks** only if the student provides **at least 3 distinct comparison points**. Award **1 mark** for a generic one-line answer.

### Question 2: What is a Hypervisor? List its types with one example each.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Hypervisor** (or Virtual Machine Monitor) is software that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines on a single physical host by abstracting hardware resources.

- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on hardware. Example: **VMware ESXi**.
- **Type-2 (Hosted):** Runs on a host OS. Example: **Oracle VirtualBox**.

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 4 Pitfalls):**
> - Students commonly **draw diagrams without labels** and lose **2–3 marks**. Always label all layers (App, Container, Engine, OS, Hardware).
> - Confusing **"Image" with "Container"** is the **#1 mistake**. An image is a static template; a container is a running instance of that image.
> - Writing **"Docker is a hypervisor"** is a **direct 0-mark answer** in KTU valuation. Docker is a **container engine**, not a hypervisor.
> - Skipping the **overhead percentage** in SCR/numerical problems costs **2 marks** as per the official model answer scheme.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a) Explain the architecture of Type-1 and Type-2 Hypervisors with neat diagrams. Discuss their advantages and disadvantages.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor:**
- Installed directly on physical hardware without any host OS.
- Acts as a thin OS that allocates hardware resources to guest VMs.
- **Examples:** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, KVM.
- **Advantages:** Near-native performance, high stability, better resource control, ideal for production data centers.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires dedicated hardware, complex setup, vendor lock-in.

**Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor:**
- Installed as an application on top of a conventional host operating system.
- The host OS mediates between the hardware and the VMs.
- **Examples:** Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop.
- **Advantages:** Easy to install, suitable for development/testing, runs alongside host applications.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher overhead due to double OS layer, slower I/O, not suitable for production-grade workloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Requirement (1 mark):** Include the layered stack: `VM → Guest OS → Hypervisor (Type-1) → Hardware` versus `VM → Guest OS → Hypervisor (Type-2) → Host OS → Hardware`. Diagrams are mandatory for full marks.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Docker. List its key components.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Docker Architecture (3-Tier Model):**

1. **Docker Client:** The user-facing CLI tool (`docker build`, `docker run`). Communicates with the Docker Daemon via REST API.
2. **Docker Host:** Contains the **Docker Daemon (dockerd)**, which manages Docker objects (images, containers, networks, volumes).
3. **Docker Registry:** Centralized repository for Docker images. Public: **Docker Hub**. Private: **AWS ECR, Azure ACR, Google GCR**.

**Key Components:**
- **Dockerfile:** Declarative recipe for building an image.
- **Docker Image:** Read-only template with layered filesystem (UnionFS).
- **Docker Container:** Runnable instance of an image.
- **Docker Volumes:** Persistent storage mechanism.
- **Docker Networks:** Virtual networks for container communication (bridge, host, overlay).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Diagram = 2 marks, Client/Daemon/Registry explanation = 3 marks, Components list = 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**(a) Compare and contrast Virtual Machines and Containers across 6 key parameters. Justify when to prefer one over the other in a cloud-native software engineering context.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Layer** | Hardware | OS |
| **Boot Time** | 30–120 s | < 1 s |
| **Image Size** | 1–50 GB | 5–500 MB |
| **Isolation** | Strong (separate kernel) | Weaker (shared kernel) |
| **Resource Overhead** | 1–4 GB RAM per VM | < 50 MB per container |
| **Portability** | Lower (hypervisor-specific) | High (runs anywhere with container engine) |

**Justification for Cloud-Native Context:**
- **Prefer Containers** for: microservices, stateless APIs, CI/CD pipelines, Kubernetes orchestration, serverless functions, and rapid autoscaling.
- **Prefer VMs** for: stateful legacy databases, Windows-only enterprise apps, multi-tenant SaaS requiring strong isolation, and GPU-intensive ML training.

---

**(b) Explain the architecture of Kubernetes. Describe the role of the Control Plane and Worker Nodes in container orchestration.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Kubernetes (K8s) Architecture** consists of two main layers:

**1. Control Plane (Master Node):**
- **API Server (`kube-apiserver`):** Frontend for the K8s control plane; exposes the K8s API.
- **Scheduler (`kube-scheduler`):** Assigns newly created Pods to appropriate Worker Nodes based on resource availability.
- **Controller Manager (`kube-controller-manager`):** Runs controller processes (Node Controller, Replication Controller, Endpoints Controller).
- **etcd:** Distributed, consistent key-value store for all cluster state and configuration.

**2. Worker Nodes:**
- **Kubelet:** Agent that communicates with the API server and ensures containers in Pods are running.
- **kube-proxy:** Maintains network rules for inter-service communication.
- **Container Runtime:** Software like **containerd** or **CRI-O** that runs the containers.
- **Pods:** The smallest deployable unit; encapsulates one or more containers sharing network and storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Orchestration:** Kubernetes automates the entire application lifecycle—**deployment, scaling, self-healing, rolling updates, and service discovery**—making it the cornerstone of modern cloud-native software engineering.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin this before KTU exams):**

- **Virtualization** abstracts **hardware**; **Containerization** abstracts the **OS layer**.
- **Hypervisor Type-1** (bare-metal) outperforms **Type-2** (hosted) by 5–10%.
- **Container density** is **5–10× higher** than VM density on identical hardware.
- **Docker** = Container Engine; **Kubernetes** = Container Orchestrator.
- **Image** is the static blueprint; **Container** is the running instance.
- **Namespaces + cgroups + UnionFS** = the three pillars of Linux containers.
- **Kubernetes Control Plane** = API Server + Scheduler + Controller Manager + etcd.
- **SCR formula:** $SCR = \dfrac{N_{physical}}{N_{virtual}}$; industry range is **5:1 to 20:1**.
- **Container startup time:** **< 1 second**; VM boot time: **30–120 seconds**.
- **Always include the overhead percentage** (1–15%) in any KTU numerical on virtualization.
- **Common 0-mark trap:** Calling Docker a "hypervisor" — it is a **container engine**.
- **Kubernetes Pod** is the smallest deployable unit, not a container.
- **Cloud SLA levels:** **99.9%** = 8.76 h downtime/year; **99.99%** = 52.56 min/year; **99.999%** = 5.26 min/year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Everything as a Service (IaaS, PaaS, Software as a Service)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Everything as a Service (IaaS, PaaS, Software as a Service)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — "Everything as a Service" (XaaS)

**Everything as a Service (XaaS)** is the umbrella term used in Cloud Computing and modern distributed systems engineering to describe the generalized delivery of virtually every category of computing resource — hardware, software, platform, storage, networking, databases, middleware, and even business processes — as an on-demand, network-accessible, pay-per-use service over the internet. The NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Cloud Computing Reference Architecture formally classifies these into three primary service models: **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)**, **Platform as a Service (PaaS)**, and **Software as a Service (SaaS)**. The "Everything" prefix denotes that the abstraction can be extended downward (e.g., Hardware as a Service, Metal as a Service) or upward (e.g., Function as a Service, Desktop as a Service).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Software Engineering, focus on the **shared responsibility model**, **abstraction level**, and **what the user manages vs. what the provider manages** in each layer. This is a frequently tested concept in Module 4.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery Model"

Imagine you are hungry and want pizza. You have four ways to get it:

| Analogy Level | Real-World Equivalent | Cloud Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| You buy wheat, grow tomatoes, make cheese, bake it yourself | You manage **everything**: servers, OS, runtime, app, data | **On-Premise (Traditional)** |
| You are given an oven, gas, and pizza base — you only add toppings | Provider manages **hardware + OS**; you manage the app and data | **IaaS** |
| You are given a half-baked pizza — you just add final toppings and bake | Provider manages **hardware + OS + middleware/runtime**; you only manage the app | **PaaS** |
| A hot pizza is delivered to your door — you just eat it | Provider manages **everything**; you just consume the service | **SaaS** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The higher you go in the stack, the **less you control** but the **more you focus on your core business logic**. The lower you go, the **more flexibility and control** you retain, but the **more engineering overhead** you carry.

### 1.3 The Three Core Service Models — At a Glance

1. **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS):** Provides raw compute, storage, and network resources on demand. The consumer controls the OS, storage, and applications but does not manage the underlying cloud infrastructure. *Example: Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine, Microsoft Azure VMs.*

2. **Platform as a Service (PaaS):** Provides a managed platform (OS + middleware + runtime + development tools) on which the consumer can deploy their own applications without worrying about the infrastructure beneath. *Example: Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.*

3. **Software as a Service (SaaS):** Provides ready-to-use, fully functional applications running on the cloud infrastructure. The consumer simply uses the software, often via a thin client (browser). *Example: Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox.*

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Metric to Remember:** A standard cloud service deployment typically promises **99.9% (Three-Nines) to 99.99% (Four-Nines) availability**, translating to roughly **8.77 hours/year** down to **52.6 minutes/year** of allowed downtime. SaaS providers like Google Workspace aim for **99.9%**, while IaaS SLAs often guarantee **99.95%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Decomposition of the Cloud Stack

The cloud service models are best understood as a **layered abstraction stack** (sometimes called the *SPI model* — Software, Platform, Infrastructure). Each layer consumes the services of the layer beneath it and provides services to the layer above it.

**Layer 1 (Bottom):** Physical Hardware — Servers, storage arrays, network switches, data center facilities.
**Layer 2:** Virtualization Hypervisor — Abstracts physical hardware into virtual machines.
**Layer 3:** Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) — Exposes VMs, raw storage, and virtual networks.
**Layer 4:** Platform as a Service (PaaS) — Adds OS, runtime (e.g., Node.js, JVM), middleware, and developer tools.
**Layer 5:** Software as a Service (SaaS) — Hosts the finished application accessible via browser/API.
**Layer 6 (Top):** The End User / Business Process.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown of Each Model

#### **I. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)**

- **Why it exists:** Building physical data centers is capital-intensive, slow, and inefficient for variable workloads. IaaS virtualizes physical resources to deliver elastic, pay-as-you-go infrastructure.
- **What the provider manages:** Physical servers, storage, network equipment, host hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM), power, cooling, physical security.
- **What the consumer manages:** Guest Operating System, middleware, runtime, application code, and data.
- **How it works (operational view):**
  1. User requests a VM instance (e.g., "2 vCPUs, 8 GB RAM, 100 GB SSD").
  2. The cloud orchestrator (e.g., OpenStack Nova) provisions a VM on a physical host.
  3. The user is given remote access (SSH/RDP) and root/admin privileges.
  4. The user installs any OS, software stack, and application.
- **Real-world utility:** Best for system administrators, DevOps teams, lift-and-shift migrations, and high-performance computing workloads (e.g., genomic sequencing, weather modeling).

#### **II. Platform as a Service (PaaS)**

- **Why it exists:** Application developers spend ~40–60% of their time on boilerplate (environment setup, dependency management, scaling, patching) rather than business logic. PaaS abstracts this away.
- **What the provider manages:** Everything in IaaS **plus** the OS, runtime engine, middleware (e.g., web servers, message queues), database engines, and pre-built developer tools (CI/CD, monitoring).
- **What the consumer manages:** Application code, configuration, and data only.
- **How it works (operational view):**
  1. Developer writes code locally and pushes to a Git repository linked to the PaaS provider.
  2. The PaaS build system (e.g., Heroku Buildpacks) detects the language/framework.
  3. The platform compiles, tests, and deploys the app to dynamically managed containers or dynos.
  4. Auto-scaling, load balancing, and failover are handled transparently.
- **Real-world utility:** Ideal for agile startups, microservices development, API backends, and rapid prototyping.

#### **III. Software as a Service (SaaS)**

- **Why it exists:** Most end-users (and even enterprises) do not want to install, patch, or maintain software. SaaS delivers applications ready-to-use.
- **What the provider manages:** **Everything** — hardware, OS, middleware, runtime, application code, and data storage. The provider is also responsible for backups, updates, and security patches.
- **What the consumer manages:** Limited user-level configuration (preferences, permissions) and their own data inputs.
- **How it works (operational view):**
  1. User logs in via a web browser or mobile app.
  2. Requests are routed to the provider's multi-tenant application cluster.
  3. The application processes the request, queries a shared/isolated database, and returns the result.
  4. Updates are pushed transparently with zero user intervention.
- **Real-world utility:** CRM (Salesforce), email (Gmail), collaboration (Slack, Teams), file storage (Dropbox, Google Drive).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield comparison** for KTU exams. Memorize the "Who Manages What" column.

| Feature | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Infrastructure as a Service | Platform as a Service | Software as a Service |
| **Analogy** | Renting a furnished empty house | Renting a serviced apartment | Staying in a hotel |
| **User Manages** | OS, Middleware, Runtime, App, Data | App and Data only | Only user inputs and preferences |
| **Provider Manages** | Hardware, Virtualization, Networking, Storage | IaaS layers + OS, Middleware, Runtime, Dev Tools | Everything end-to-end |
| **Target User** | System Admins, DevOps Engineers, Architects | Application Developers | End Users, Business Teams |
| **Flexibility** | **Highest** (full control) | Medium (constrained to platform tools) | Lowest (no code access) |
| **Ease of Use** | Lowest (requires technical skill) | Medium (focus on code, not infra) | **Highest** (point-and-click) |
| **Time to Deploy** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours | Immediate (seconds) |
| **Cost Model** | Pay per VM/hour | Pay per app instance/req | Subscription (per user/month) |
| **Scalability** | Manual or scripted | Automatic (built-in) | Automatic (provider-managed) |
| **Examples** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute, DigitalOcean | Heroku, Google App Engine, AWS Beanstalk | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Zoom |
| **Common Use Case** | Disaster recovery, HPC, test environments | API development, web app deployment | Email, CRM, collaboration |
| **Control Level** | High (root access) | Medium (code only) | Low (config only) |
| **Maintenance Burden** | User | Provider (mostly) | Provider (fully) |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

In modern Software Engineering practice, the choice between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS directly impacts the **SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle)**, **DevOps pipelines**, and **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)**. A typical microservices architecture might combine all three: **IaaS** for the Kubernetes control plane, **PaaS** (e.g., Heroku or Render) for business microservices, and **SaaS** (e.g., Datadog, GitHub) for monitoring and version control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a conceptual architecture topic rather than a numerical engineering derivation, the "analytical matrix" below provides a rigorous, step-by-step comparison, decision logic, and scenario analysis — the equivalent of a derivation for cloud architecture questions.

### 3.1 Analytical Step 1 — Mapping the Responsibility Matrix

The definitive way to compare the three service models is to use the **shared responsibility model** — a tabular matrix that lists every layer of the technology stack and identifies who (provider vs. consumer) is accountable for its operation, security, and maintenance.

**Technology Stack Layers (top to bottom):**

1. **Application / Software** (your business logic)
2. **Data** (your information assets)
3. **Runtime** (e.g., JVM, Node.js, Python interpreter)
4. **Middleware** (e.g., web server, message queue)
5. **Operating System** (e.g., Linux, Windows Server)
6. **Virtualization** (hypervisor layer)
7. **Servers** (physical compute)
8. **Storage** (physical disks/SAN)
9. **Networking** (physical switches, routers, firewalls)

**Responsibility Mapping (Provider vs. Consumer):**

| Stack Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | Consumer | Consumer | Provider |
| Data | Consumer | Consumer | Provider |
| Runtime | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| Middleware | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| Operating System | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| Virtualization | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Servers | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Storage | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Networking | Provider | Provider | Provider |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** If you are asked "In SaaS, who is responsible for the data?" — the answer is nuanced. The **provider manages the database engine and infrastructure**, but the **customer is responsible for access controls, data classification, and compliance** of the data *content*. This is a high-scoring distinction in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.2 Analytical Step 2 — Deriving the Cost Equation

The **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for each model can be approximated as a weighted sum. While exact values vary, the general formula is:

$$
\text{TCO} = C_{\text{infra}} + C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{people}} + C_{\text{downtime}} - C_{\text{elasticity}}
$$

**Where each term represents:**

$$
C_{\text{infra}} = \text{Procurement cost of hardware/platform per month}
$$

$$
C_{\text{license}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{LicenseFee}_i \times \text{NumberOfUsers}_i)
$$

$$
C_{\text{people}} = \text{Number of FTE engineers} \times \text{Avg. salary} \times \text{Time fraction}
$$

$$
C_{\text{downtime}} = \text{Annual downtime hours} \times \text{Revenue lost per hour}
$$

$$
C_{\text{elasticity}} = \text{Cost saved by auto-scaling vs. over-provisioning}
$$

**Cost Trade-off Analysis (step-by-step reasoning):**

- **Step 1:** In **IaaS**, $C_{\text{infra}}$ is moderate, but $C_{\text{people}}$ is **highest** because the customer needs system administrators and DevOps engineers.
- **Step 2:** In **PaaS**, $C_{\text{infra}}$ is bundled and abstracted, $C_{\text{people}}$ drops because developers no longer manage infrastructure, but $C_{\text{license}}$ may rise due to platform fees.
- **Step 3:** In **SaaS**, $C_{\text{license}}$ is **highest** (per-user subscription), $C_{\text{people}}$ is **lowest**, and $C_{\text{downtime}}$ is **lowest** because the provider absorbs SLA penalties.

### 3.3 Analytical Step 3 — Decision Tree for Model Selection

**Q1: Does the user want to write custom code?**
- **Yes** → Go to Q2.
- **No** → Choose **SaaS**.

**Q2: Does the user need to control the OS or runtime?**
- **Yes** → Choose **IaaS**.
- **No** → Choose **PaaS**.

**Q3 (deeper): Is the workload highly variable (bursty)?**
- **Yes** → PaaS or IaaS with auto-scaling groups.
- **No** → SaaS if a suitable off-the-shelf product exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision tree is the **expected answer structure** for any 7-mark "Compare and contrast" question in KTU Module 4.

### 3.4 Analytical Step 4 — Worked Example Scenario

**Scenario:** A KTU-affiliated engineering college wants to host a student attendance portal. Predict the recommended service model and justify it.

**Solution Path:**

- **Step 1:** Identify constraints — Budget-limited, small IT team, requires a custom attendance form, must integrate with university databases.
- **Step 2:** Eliminate **SaaS** because no off-the-shelf product matches the unique college workflow.
- **Step 3:** Compare IaaS vs. PaaS — The team has 1–2 developers but no dedicated sysadmin. Therefore, **managing OS, security patches, and load balancers on IaaS is a burden**.
- **Step 4:** Conclusion — **PaaS** is optimal. The developers can push their Node.js/Python code to a platform like Heroku, and the platform handles SSL certificates, scaling, and downtime.

**Verdict:** PaaS offers the **best trade-off between developer control and operational overhead** for this use case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud Service Stack — Layered Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the layered cloud service stack, the user's visibility of each layer, and the flow of control between consumer and provider.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L6["Layer 6: End User and Business Process"]
        A1["User accesses application via browser or API"]
    end

    subgraph L5["Layer 5: SaaS - Software as a Service"]
        B1["Ready to use application example: Gmail, Office 365"]
        B2["Provider manages 100 percent of stack"]
        B3["User only configures preferences"]
    end

    subgraph L4["Layer 4: PaaS - Platform as a Service"]
        C1["Application code written by developer"]
        C2["Provider manages OS, runtime, middleware, tools"]
        C3["Developer focuses on business logic only"]
    end

    subgraph L3["Layer 3: IaaS - Infrastructure as a Service"]
        D1["Virtual machines, storage, networks provisioned on demand"]
        D2["Provider manages hardware, hypervisor, datacenter"]
        D3["User installs OS, middleware, and application"]
    end

    subgraph L2["Layer 2: Virtualization Hypervisor"]
        E1["VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V abstract physical hardware"]
    end

    subgraph L1["Layer 1: Physical Hardware"]
        F1["Servers, storage arrays, network switches"]
        F2["Power, cooling, physical security"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B1 -.->|"calls into"| C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C1 -.->|"runs on VMs from"| D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D1 -.->|"powered by"| E1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows (`-->`) represent *direct control flow*. Dotted arrows (`-.->`) represent *dependency / consumption*. A solid arrow moving **upward** means the user directly interacts with that layer. A dotted arrow moving **downward** means the upper layer *consumes services* from the lower layer.

### 4.2 Shared Responsibility Matrix — Block Architecture Flow

The next diagram shows how responsibility shifts progressively from the consumer to the provider as we ascend the stack from IaaS to SaaS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONSUMER["Consumer Responsibility"]
        CR1["Application Code"]
        CR2["Data and Content"]
        CR3["Operating System"]
        CR4["Runtime and Middleware"]
    end

    subgraph PROVIDER["Provider Responsibility"]
        PR1["Virtualization Layer"]
        PR2["Physical Servers"]
        PR3["Storage Systems"]
        PR4["Network Infrastructure"]
        PR5["Facility and Power"]
    end

    subgraph SHIFT["Shifting Boundary per Service Model"]
        SH1["IaaS Boundary: OS upward is yours"]
        SH2["PaaS Boundary: Application code upward is yours"]
        SH3["SaaS Boundary: Only data and access is yours"]
    end

    CR1 --> SHIFT
    CR2 --> SHIFT
    CR3 --> SH1
    CR4 --> SH2
    PROVIDER --> SHIFT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Draw the shared responsibility model," label each block clearly. Examiners award marks for *correct identification of boundary* between consumer and provider, not just for the box count.

### 4.3 Service Model Decision Flowchart

This flowchart operationalizes the decision logic derived in Section 3.3, useful for any scenario-based question in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Does the user need to write custom code?"}
    Q2{"Does the user need OS or runtime control?"}
    Q3{"Is workload bursty and unpredictable?"}
    A1["Choose SaaS - example: Google Workspace, Salesforce"]
    A2["Choose IaaS - example: AWS EC2, Azure VM"]
    A3["Choose PaaS - example: Heroku, Google App Engine"]
    A4["Choose PaaS with auto-scaling - example: AWS Elastic Beanstalk"]

    Q1 -->|No| A1
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| A4
    Q3 -->|No| A3
```

### 4.4 Diagram Legend & Interpretation Notes

- **Rectangular nodes** represent components, services, or layers.
- **Diamond nodes** (`{ }`) represent decision points.
- **Arrows** represent control or data flow direction.
- **Subgraphs** group related concepts visually (e.g., Provider vs. Consumer).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Everything as a Service" (XaaS). List the three primary service models defined by NIST.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Everything as a Service (XaaS) is a generalized cloud computing concept in which every conceivable computing resource — including infrastructure, platforms, software, storage, networking, and even business processes — is delivered as an on-demand, network-accessible service over the internet on a pay-per-use basis.

The three primary service models defined by the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (SP 145-2)** are:

1. **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS):** Provides raw compute, storage, and networking resources. *Example: Amazon EC2.*
2. **Platform as a Service (PaaS):** Provides a managed runtime and tools for application development. *Example: Google App Engine.*
3. **Software as a Service (SaaS):** Provides ready-to-use applications. *Example: Microsoft Office 365.*

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming three models, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between IaaS and PaaS with respect to (a) who manages the operating system, and (b) target user.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | IaaS | PaaS |
|---|---|---|
| (a) **OS Management** | The **consumer** manages the operating system — they install, patch, and configure it on the rented virtual machine. | The **provider** manages the operating system as part of the bundled platform. |
| (b) **Target User** | System administrators, DevOps engineers, and infrastructure architects who need full control. | Application developers who want to focus on writing code without managing infrastructure. |

*(1.5 marks for OS distinction + 1.5 marks for target user distinction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question] — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of "Everything as a Service" (XaaS). With a neat layered diagram, describe IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS, clearly stating the responsibility of the cloud provider and the consumer at each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A startup company is developing a mobile-first social networking application. The team has 3 developers and no dedicated system administrator. The workload is highly variable (events trigger 10x traffic spikes). Recommend the most suitable cloud service model and justify your answer with at least three technical reasons.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of XaaS Architecture [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Everything as a Service (XaaS) is the cloud computing paradigm in which all layers of the IT stack — from raw hardware up to finished applications — are abstracted and delivered as on-demand services over the internet with elastic, metered pricing.

**Step 2 — Layered Architecture (3 marks):**
The XaaS stack is organized into the following layers, from bottom to top:

1. **Physical Layer:** Servers, storage disks, network switches, datacenter facilities with power and cooling.
2. **Virtualization Layer:** Hypervisors (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM) abstract physical hardware into virtual machines.
3. **IaaS Layer:** The provider exposes VMs, virtual storage volumes, and virtual networks as programmable resources. The consumer installs and manages the **OS, middleware, runtime, application, and data**.
4. **PaaS Layer:** The provider also manages the **OS, runtime, middleware, and development tools**. The consumer only uploads **application code and data**.
5. **SaaS Layer:** The provider manages **everything** end-to-end. The consumer only uses the **finished application** via a browser or thin client.

**Step 3 — Responsibility Distribution (3 marks):**

| Layer | IaaS Consumer Manages | PaaS Consumer Manages | SaaS Consumer Manages |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ |
| Data | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ (content only) |
| Runtime | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ |
| Middleware | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ |
| OS | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ |
| Hardware & Virtualization | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |

**Diagram Note (carries 1 internal mark):** Draw a 5-layer stack with arrows showing which layer each model "stops" at from the consumer's perspective.

---

**(b) Recommendation and Justification [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Need (1 mark):**
The startup needs to deploy a mobile-first social networking app with bursty, unpredictable traffic and a small team lacking sysadmin expertise.

**Step 2 — Evaluate Each Option (3 marks):**
- **SaaS:** Discarded. No off-the-shelf SaaS product can build a custom social network with proprietary algorithms.
- **IaaS:** Possible but inefficient. The team would need to manually configure autoscaling groups, load balancers, OS patching, and security hardening — diverting developers from product work.
- **PaaS:** Ideal. The platform handles scaling, load balancing, runtime patching, and database management.

**Step 3 — Justification (3 marks):**

1. **Auto-Scaling for Bursty Workloads:** PaaS platforms (e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine) offer built-in horizontal auto-scaling that automatically spins up additional instances during traffic spikes (e.g., 10x surge during events) and scales down during quiet periods, optimizing cost.
2. **Low Operational Overhead:** With no dedicated sysadmin, the team cannot afford the IaaS overhead of OS patching, security hardening, and middleware updates. PaaS offloads this entirely to the provider.
3. **Developer Productivity:** PaaS provides pre-configured runtimes (e.g., Node.js, Python, Java) and managed databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, MongoDB), allowing the 3 developers to focus on building the social graph and recommendation engine.
4. **Cost Efficiency:** PaaS follows a pure pay-per-use model; the startup pays only for active compute time, which is critical for a cash-constrained startup.

**Final Answer:** **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** is the recommended model.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question — Alternative Choice] — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS along the following dimensions: (i) abstraction level, (ii) control over infrastructure, (iii) responsibility for security patches, (iv) typical end user, and (v) time to market. Provide one real-world cloud service example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An established bank wants to migrate its legacy core banking system to the cloud. The system runs on a specific Linux version with custom kernel modules and requires bare-metal-like performance. The bank has a large IT team of 50+ engineers. Which service model would you recommend and why? Discuss two trade-offs of your recommendation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table [7 Marks]:**

| Dimension | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Abstraction Level** | Low (close to hardware) | Medium (managed runtime) | High (fully managed) |
| (ii) **Control Over Infrastructure** | High (root/admin access) | Medium (code only) | None (config only) |
| (iii) **Security Patches** | Consumer responsible for OS and middleware | Provider handles OS/runtime; consumer secures app | Provider handles all patches |
| (iv) **Typical End User** | Sysadmins, DevOps | Application developers | Business users, general public |
| (v) **Time to Market** | Slowest (days/weeks) | Fast (hours) | Instant (minutes) |
| **Real-World Example** | Amazon EC2 | Google App Engine | Microsoft Office 365 |

*(1 mark per dimension, 2 marks for the examples row — give 2.5 marks for 5 dimensions + 1.5 marks for examples, totaling 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Recommendation for the Bank [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify Constraints (1 mark):**
- Specific Linux kernel version required.
- Custom kernel modules (deep OS-level customization).
- Bare-metal performance needed.
- Large in-house IT team (50+ engineers) available.

**Step 2 — Choose the Model (2 marks):**
**IaaS is the correct choice.** The bank's requirements (custom kernel modules, specific Linux version, bare-metal-like performance) demand a level of low-level OS control that PaaS and SaaS explicitly do not provide. With 50+ engineers, the bank has the workforce to manage the OS, runtime, and middleware.

**Step 3 — Trade-off 1 — Operational Cost (2 marks):**
IaaS is the most **operationally expensive** of the three models because the bank must still pay for OS licensing, sysadmins, and security patching. However, this cost is offset by the bank's large existing engineering team — the headcount cost is already sunk.

**Step 4 — Trade-off 2 — Vendor Lock-in and Agility (2 marks):**
IaaS gives the bank full control but also full responsibility. Auto-scaling, failover, and high availability must be manually architected. The bank cannot benefit from the *built-in* elasticity of PaaS. Migrating to a different IaaS provider later may require significant re-architecting.

**Final Answer:** **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** is the recommended model, specifically a **bare-metal IaaS** or **dedicated host** offering (e.g., AWS EC2 Dedicated Hosts, Azure Dedicated Host) to satisfy the kernel-level control and performance requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "managing" with "owning":** Students often write "the user manages the hardware in IaaS." This is **wrong**. In IaaS, the **provider** still owns and manages the hardware. The user only manages the **guest OS upward**. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Treating PaaS and SaaS as identical:** Students often blur the distinction. Remember: in **PaaS, you write code**; in **SaaS, you only use the finished product**. This distinction is worth **at least 2 marks** in any comparison question.
> 3. **Forgetting real-world examples:** A theoretical answer without naming **AWS EC2, Heroku, or Gmail** is considered incomplete. Always pair definitions with examples.
> 4. **Confusing vertical scaling with horizontal scaling:** PaaS provides **horizontal** auto-scaling (adding more instances), not vertical scaling (adding RAM/CPU to one instance). Examiners test this.
> 5. **Ignoring the shared responsibility model:** Many students skip the responsibility table, which is the **single most scoring element** in a 14-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU examination. Each bullet is a high-yield point.

- ✅ **XaaS** = Everything as a Service; umbrella term for cloud delivery models.
- ✅ **NIST defines three primary models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS.
- ✅ **IaaS = renting hardware (virtualized).** You manage: OS, middleware, runtime, app, data. *Example: AWS EC2.*
- ✅ **PaaS = renting a platform.** You manage: app, data. Provider manages: OS, runtime, middleware. *Example: Heroku.*
- ✅ **SaaS = renting software.** You manage: only your input data. Provider manages: everything. *Example: Gmail.*
- ✅ **The "Pizza Analogy":** IaaS = dough + oven, PaaS = pre-made base, SaaS = delivered pizza.
- ✅ **Flexibility is inversely proportional to ease of use:** IaaS = max control, max effort. SaaS = min control, min effort. PaaS = middle ground.
- ✅ **Auto-scaling is the #1 differentiator** between PaaS and IaaS — PaaS makes it transparent.
- ✅ **Cost model:** IaaS = pay-per-VM-hour, PaaS = pay-per-app-resource, SaaS = subscription per user.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility Model:** Always draw a table — this is the *single highest-scoring diagram* in Module 4.
- ✅ **SLA availability:** 99.9% (three-nines) ≈ 8.77 hours downtime/year; 99.99% (four-nines) ≈ 52.6 minutes/year.
- ✅ **The "provider-managed" boundary moves up** as you go from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Saying "user manages hardware in IaaS" — **the provider always manages hardware**.
- ✅ **Decision rule for exam scenarios:** Custom code + need OS control → **IaaS**. Custom code + don't need OS control → **PaaS**. No custom code → **SaaS**.
- ✅ **Other related XaaS extensions** worth knowing: **FaaS (Function as a Service)**, **DBaaS (Database as a Service)**, **DaaS (Desktop as a Service)**, **MaaS (Monitoring as a Service)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservices Architecture - Microservices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microservices Architecture - Microservices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microservices Architecture** (often called "Microservices") is a distinctive method of developing software systems that emphasizes decomposing a single application into a **suite of small, independent, loosely coupled, and autonomously deployable services**. Each service runs in its own process, communicates through lightweight mechanisms (typically HTTP/REST APIs or messaging brokers), and is organized around a **specific business capability**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, microservices represent the evolutionary successor to the Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) paradigm, governed by principles derived from Domain-Driven Design (DDD), Continuous Delivery, and DevOps culture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** "Microservices is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of loosely coupled, fine-grained services, each implementing a single business capability, communicating via lightweight protocols, and independently deployable by fully automated deployment machinery."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialized Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine a traditional **monolithic restaurant** where a single chef prepares the appetizer, main course, and dessert all at one station, sharing the same utensils, oven, and cutting board.

- If the oven breaks → **the entire menu fails**.
- If the chef falls sick → **no food is served**.
- Adding a new dish requires **disrupting the existing workflow**.

Now imagine a **microservices restaurant** — a *brigade de cuisine*:
- The **Pastry Chef** handles only desserts.
- The **Grill Chef** handles only grilled items.
- The **Saucier** handles only sauces.
- Each operates on a **separate counter**, uses **independent tools**, and reports to the **Head Waiter (API Gateway)** when the order ticket comes in.

| Restaurant Role | Microservice Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Specialized Station | Single Service |
| Independent Toolset | Isolated Database / Codebase |
| Order Ticket (Pass) | Lightweight API Call (HTTP/MQ) |
| Head Waiter | API Gateway |
| Menu Card | Service Registry / Catalog |
| Health Inspector | Circuit Breaker / Monitoring |

If the oven breaks in the pastry section → **only desserts stop**; the rest of the kitchen keeps running. That is the **resilience and autonomy** microservices promise.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Terms to Memorize for KTU Viva / Short Questions:**
> - **Service Granularity:** The scope/size of an individual service (fine-grained in microservices).
> - **Bounded Context:** A DDD term defining the boundary within which a model is valid.
> - **Polyglot Persistence:** Using different database technologies (SQL, NoSQL, Graph) for different services.
> - **12-Factor App:** A methodology for building SaaS apps optimized for microservices.
> - **Conway's Law:** "Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations."

**Industry-Standard Metrics** (for board answers on scalability):
- **Latency Target:** typically **< 200 ms** for inter-service calls.
- **Service Independence:** **> 95%** independent deployability is the golden benchmark.
- **Team Size per Service:** Amazon's "Two-Pizza Team" rule → **6–8 engineers** per service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Nine Defining Characteristics of Microservices

According to **Lewis & Fowler (2014)** — the canonical academic reference — every genuine microservice-based system exhibits the following characteristics. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"List and explain any 5 characteristics of microservices."*

1. **Componentization via Services** — The application is broken into replaceable, independently deployable components (services), each a unit of software with a stable interface.
2. **Organized Around Business Capability** — Teams are cross-functional and own the *end-to-end* lifecycle of a product (e.g., "Orders", "Payments", "Shipping").
3. **Products, Not Projects** — The team owns the service "forever" — they treat it as a product with a roadmap, not as code to be handed off.
4. **Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes** — Logic stays *inside* the service; the transport (HTTP, message queue) carries only data. No business logic in the ESB/pipe.
5. **Decentralized Governance** — Each team picks its own tech stack (Polyglot Programming) and database (Polyglot Persistence).
6. **Decentralized Data Management** — Each service owns its private database. **No shared database across services.**
7. **Infrastructure Automation** — Heavy CI/CD pipelines; infrastructure is provisioned as code (Terraform, Ansible, Kubernetes manifests).
8. **Design for Failure** — Services *expect* other services to fail. Implementation patterns: **Circuit Breaker, Bulkhead, Timeout, Retry**.
9. **Evolutionary Design** — Systems evolve over time; legacy code is replaced incrementally rather than via big-bang rewrites.

## 2.2 Monolithic vs. Microservices — Comparative Analysis

This is the **#1 most asked comparative question** in KTU Module-4 examinations.

| Parameter | Monolithic Architecture | Microservices Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Unit** | Single deployable unit (one WAR/EAR) | Multiple independent deployable units |
| **Technology Stack** | Homogeneous (one language/DB) | Polyglot (heterogeneous) |
| **Scalability** | Horizontal scaling = clone the *entire* app | Scale only the hot service (e.g., Payment) |
| **Fault Isolation** | Low — one bug can crash the system | High — failure contained within service |
| **Data Layer** | Single shared relational database | Database-per-service (decentralized) |
| **Team Structure** | Siloed (UI, DB, Backend teams) | Cross-functional product teams |
| **Time to Deploy** | High — change needs full re-deployment | Low — only changed service redeployed |
| **Complexity** | Low at start, grows quadratically | High upfront, scales linearly |
| **Communication** | In-process function calls | Network calls (REST, gRPC, MQ) |
| **Cost (Initial)** | **Low** | **High** |
| **Best Suited For** | Small teams, simple domains, MVPs | Large teams, complex domains, hyper-scale |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick Trick:** If the question gives you a system with *"high traffic on one module only"* or *"frequent deployments of independent features"* → Answer **Microservices**. If it says *"small team, single domain, prototype"* → Answer **Monolithic**.

## 2.3 High-Yield Architectural Patterns & Formulas

### 2.3.1 The Service Decomposition Heuristic

A commonly cited heuristic to decide *where* to draw service boundaries is based on **Domain-Driven Design**:

$$
N_{services} \approx B_{contexts} + I_{integration}
$$

Where:
- $N_{services}$ = Recommended number of microservices
- $B_{contexts}$ = Number of distinct **Bounded Contexts** identified in the domain
- $I_{integration}$ = Number of additional glue services (API Gateway, Aggregator)

### 2.3.2 The Granularity Trade-off (Cost Function)

Microservices add **network overhead**. The cost per call becomes:

$$
C_{call} = C_{CPU} + C_{network} + C_{serialization} + C_{latency}
$$

- In a **monolith**: $C_{network} \approx 0$ (in-process call, microseconds).
- In **microservices**: $C_{network}$ can be **1–50 ms** depending on region.

This is why KTU answers must stress: *"Do not decompose arbitrarily; aggregate where collaboration is too tight."*

### 2.3.3 Availability Math (the Need for Resilience)

For a chain of $N$ synchronous services each with availability $A_i$:

$$
A_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} A_i
$$

**Example:** If each service has **99.9%** (3-nines) availability, then for **5 services**:

$$
A_{system} = (0.999)^{5} \approx 0.995 = 99.5\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Notice the **exponential decay**. A 3-nines service chain of just 5 services drops to 99.5%. This is *why* microservices demand **async communication, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation** — synchronous chains are *toxic* at scale.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microservices are the **de facto architectural style** in industry for:
- **E-commerce giants:** Amazon, Flipkart (Catalog, Cart, Payment, Fulfillment).
- **Streaming platforms:** Netflix (700+ microservices — famously documented).
- **Banking/Fintech:** RBI-mandated scalability, fault isolation for transactions.
- **Ride-sharing:** Uber's Domain-Oriented Microservice Architecture (DOMA).
- **IoT & Telemetry:** Smart city/industrial IoT pipelines.

In the **KTU Software Engineering context**, microservices answer the question: *"How do we build systems that are independently deployable, scalable, and resilient as the domain grows?"* — directly aligned with **CO4 (Architectural Design)** of the PECST411 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Decomposing a Monolith into Microservices

The KTU question *"Explain the step-by-step process of migrating from monolithic to microservices architecture"* is a frequent 14-marker. We derive the answer formally.

### Step 1 — Identify the Domain (Bounded Context Analysis)

Using **Domain-Driven Design**, decompose the business into sub-domains:

**Example Domain: Online Bookstore**

| Sub-Domain | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Catalog Management | Core | Books, Authors, Inventory |
| Order Processing | Core | Cart, Checkout, Order Lifecycle |
| Payment | Core | Transactions, Refunds |
| User Account | Supporting | Profiles, Authentication |
| Notifications | Supporting | Email, SMS, Push |
| Analytics | Generic | Clickstream, Reports |

**Reasoning:** The KTU expectation is to show *why* you grouped features into services — by **business capability**, not by technical layer.

### Step 2 — Apply the Strangler Fig Pattern

The **Strangler Fig Pattern** (Martin Fowler) dictates incremental migration:

$$
\text{New System}_{t+1} = \text{New Microservices} \cup f(\text{Legacy}_{t})
$$

Where $f(\cdot)$ is the **routing function** (API Gateway / Reverse Proxy) that decides:
- Is the request for a *migrated* feature? → Route to new microservice.
- Is the request for a *legacy* feature? → Route to monolith.

### Step 3 — Apply the Two-Pizza Team Mapping

Apply Amazon's organizational rule: **each service is owned by a team of 6–8 engineers**. Map:

$$
\text{Team}_{i} \leftrightarrow \text{Service}_{i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

The mapping is **bijective** — every service has exactly one owning team, and every team owns exactly one service (excluding platform teams).

### Step 4 — Define Communication Contracts

Two choices, weighed using a decision matrix:

$$
\text{Decision} = \arg\max_{p \in \{REST, gRPC, MQ\}} \left[ \text{Score}(p, \text{latency}) + \text{Score}(p, \text{coupling}) + \text{Score}(p, \text{throughput}) \right]
$$

- **REST (HTTP/JSON):** Best for public APIs, low throughput, high human readability.
- **gRPC (HTTP/2 + Protobuf):** Best for internal service-to-service, low latency, polyglot.
- **Message Queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ):** Best for async, event-driven, decoupled flows.

### Step 5 — Decentralize the Data Layer (Database-per-Service)

For each service, allocate an independent datastore:

$$
\forall\, i \neq j : \quad \text{DB}_{i} \cap \text{DB}_{j} = \emptyset
$$

**No direct cross-service queries.** Cross-service data is obtained via:
1. **API Composition:** Service A calls Service B's API.
2. **Event-Driven Sync:** Service A subscribes to events published by Service B.
3. **CQRS + Event Sourcing:** Read models materialized via event streams.

### Step 6 — Implement Cross-Cutting Concerns

| Concern | Pattern | Tooling Example |
|---|---|---|
| Service Discovery | Client-side or Server-side discovery | Eureka, Consul, Kubernetes DNS |
| API Gateway | Single entry point | Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway |
| Circuit Breaker | Fault isolation | Hystrix (legacy), Resilience4j, Istio |
| Distributed Tracing | Observability | Jaeger, Zipkin, OpenTelemetry |
| Centralized Logging | Aggregation | ELK Stack, Loki, Splunk |
| Configuration | Centralized config | Spring Cloud Config, Vault |
| Container Orchestration | Auto-scaling | Kubernetes, Docker Swarm |

### Step 7 — Verify and Decommission

Use the **Parallel Run / Shadow Run** strategy:

$$
\text{Confidence} = 1 - \frac{\text{Discrepancies in shadow traffic}}{\text{Total shadow traffic}}
$$

Once $\text{Confidence} \geq 99.99\%$, **decommission the legacy path** in $f(\cdot)$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Design Pattern Reference

| Pattern | Category | Purpose | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Gateway** | Edge | Single entry, auth, rate limiting | Always in public-facing systems |
| **Service Registry** | Discovery | Service instance lookup | Dynamic cloud environments |
| **Circuit Breaker** | Resilience | Prevent cascade failure | Calls to potentially-failing services |
| **Saga** | Data Consistency | Distributed transactions | Multi-service business workflows |
| **CQRS** | Data | Separate read/write models | High read throughput, audit needs |
| **Event Sourcing** | Data | Store state as event log | Auditing, temporal queries |
| **Bulkhead** | Resilience | Isolate thread pools | Prevent resource exhaustion |
| **Sidecar** | Infra | Co-locate helper process | Logging, proxy, config |
| **Database per Service** | Data | Enforce loose coupling | Always in true microservices |
| **BFF (Backend for Frontend)** | Edge | Tailored APIs per UI | Mobile + Web + Desktop clients |
| **Choreography** | Coordination | Services react to events | Loosely coupled, async flows |
| **Orchestration** | Coordination | Central coordinator | Complex multi-step workflows |

## 3.3 Trade-off Curve — The "Microservice Premium"

The cost of moving from monolith to microservices is not zero. The **break-even point** can be modeled as:

$$
T_{breakeven} = \frac{C_{migration}}{B_{agility} + B_{scale} - C_{overhead}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{migration}$ = Cost to refactor + set up CI/CD + DevOps.
- $B_{agility}$ = Productivity gain from independent deployments.
- $B_{scale}$ = Infra savings from targeted scaling.
- $C_{overhead}$ = Ongoing network, observability, and ops cost.

**Rule of Thumb:** $T_{breakeven} \leq 12\text{ months}$ is considered a healthy migration in industry. If a KTU answer shows breakeven > 24 months, the **monolith is likely a better choice**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Microservices Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Applications Web Mobile IoT] --> gateway[API Gateway Auth RateLimit Routing]
    gateway --> svc1[Service Catalog]
    gateway --> svc2[Service Orders]
    gateway --> svc3[Service Payments]
    gateway --> svc4[Service Users]
    gateway --> svc5[Service Notifications]
    registry[Service Registry Eureka Consul] -.-> gateway
    registry -.-> svc1
    registry -.-> svc2
    registry -.-> svc3
    registry -.-> svc4
    registry -.-> svc5
    config[Centralized Config Server] -.-> svc1
    config -.-> svc2
    config -.-> svc3
    config -.-> svc4
    config -.-> svc5
    svc1 --> db1[(Catalog DB SQL)]
    svc2 --> db2[(Orders DB NoSQL)]
    svc3 --> db3[(Payments DB SQL)]
    svc4 --> db4[(Users DB SQL)]
    svc5 --> db5[(Notifications DB Cache)]
    mq[Message Broker Kafka RabbitMQ] -.-> svc2
    mq -.-> svc3
    mq -.-> svc5
    obs[Observability Logs Metrics Traces] -.-> svc1
    obs -.-> svc2
    obs -.-> svc3
    obs -.-> svc4
    obs -.-> svc5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in a KTU exam:**
> - The **solid arrows** denote *synchronous HTTP/REST requests*.
> - The **dashed arrows** denote *asynchronous / control-plane interactions* (discovery, config, messaging, observability).
> - The **databases are private** — note there is no horizontal link between db1, db2, db3. That visually enforces the **Database-per-Service** rule.

## 4.2 Monolith vs. Microservices — Side-by-Side Structural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MONOLITH [Monolithic Architecture]
        MClient[Client] --> MApp[Single Application]
        MApp --> MAuth[Auth Module]
        MApp --> MCat[Catalog Module]
        MApp --> MPay[Payment Module]
        MApp --> MDB[(Shared Database)]
    end
    subgraph MICROSERVICES [Microservices Architecture]
        MSClient[Client] --> MSGateway[API Gateway]
        MSGateway --> MSA[Auth Service]
        MSGateway --> MSC[Catalog Service]
        MSGateway --> MSP[Payment Service]
        MSA --> MSDBA[(Auth DB)]
        MSC --> MSDBC[(Catalog DB)]
        MSP --> MSDBP[(Payment DB)]
    end
```

> **Key Visual Insight:** The monolith has a *vertical* layered structure with a **shared database at the bottom** (tight coupling). Microservices have a *horizontal* parallel structure with **isolated data stores** (loose coupling).

## 4.3 Request Flow — Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Patterns

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant G as API Gateway
    participant O as Order Service
    participant P as Payment Service
    participant N as Notification Service
    participant M as Message Queue
    U->>G: POST /checkout
    G->>O: Create Order
    O->>P: Process Payment sync
    P-->>O: Payment Success
    O-->>G: Order Confirmed
    G-->>U: 200 OK
    O->>M: Publish OrderCreated Event async
    M->>N: Consume Event
    N->>U: Send Confirmation Email SMS
```

> **Reading aid for the board:** Lines 1–6 are **synchronous** (blocking, real-time). Lines 7–10 are **asynchronous** (fire-and-forget via MQ). This is the canonical *hybrid* pattern — *use sync where the user is waiting, use async for side effects.*

## 4.4 Fallback Functional Architecture (If Mermaid is constrained)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EDGE[Edge Layer: API Gateway Load Balancer WAF] --> COORD[Coordination Layer: Service Discovery Config Server]
    EDGE --> DOMAIN[Domain Layer: Business Microservices]
    COORD --> DOMAIN
    DOMAIN --> DATA[Data Layer: Database per Service Polyglot Persistence]
    DOMAIN --> CROSS[Cross-Cutting Layer: Circuit Breaker Distributed Tracing Centralized Logging Security]
    DATA --> CROSS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Microservices Architecture. List any FOUR characteristics.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

**Definition:** Microservices Architecture is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, loosely coupled, independently deployable services, each focused on a single business capability and communicating via lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST or messaging).

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Componentization via Services** — The system is broken into replaceable service units.
2. **Organized Around Business Capability** — Cross-functional teams own the full stack of a feature.
3. **Decentralized Data Management** — Each service owns its private database; no shared schema.
4. **Design for Failure** — Services are built assuming peers will fail (circuit breakers, retries, timeouts).

*(2 additional for extra credit: Infrastructure Automation, Smart Endpoints & Dumb Pipes.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Compare Monolithic and Microservices Architecture on any FIVE parameters.**
