**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Monolithic | Microservices |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment** | Single unit | Multiple independent units |
| **Technology** | Homogeneous | Polyglot (heterogeneous) |
| **Scalability** | Whole-app scaling | Per-service scaling |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low (single point of failure) | High (isolated failures) |
| **Data Layer** | Shared central DB | DB per service (decentralized) |
| **Team Structure** | Specialized (UI, DB, BE silos) | Cross-functional product teams |
| **Time-to-Market** | Slows as code base grows | Fast — parallel teams deploy independently |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse *scalability* with *elasticity*. Microservices improve both **horizontal scalability** (clone hot services) AND **elasticity** (auto-scale in/out). Just saying "microservices are scalable" is **too vague for full marks** — mention *per-service* scaling.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule (ESE 2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with a **module-level internal choice**. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **principles and characteristics** of Microservices Architecture. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and challenges** of adopting microservices in a large-scale enterprise system. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

Microservices Architecture is guided by a set of well-established principles formalized by Martin Fowler and James Lewis. We discuss them under three categories:

**1. Design Principles**
- **Single Responsibility:** Each service implements one bounded business capability.
- **Loose Coupling:** Services evolve independently; interface changes are backward-compatible.
- **High Cohesion:** All related logic and data for a capability lives inside one service.
- **Independent Deployability:** Each service has its own CI/CD pipeline and release cadence.

**2. Organizational Principles**
- **Conway's Law Inversion:** Team topology mirrors service topology.
- **Product Teams, Not Project Teams:** Teams own services perpetually.
- **You Build It, You Run It:** DevOps culture; developers carry pager duty.

**3. Technological Principles**
- **Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes:** Logic stays in services; transport only carries data.
- **Decentralized Governance:** No central "Architecture Council" dictating tech stack.
- **Polyglot Programming & Persistence:** Right tool for the right job.
- **Infrastructure as Code:** Terraform, Helm, Ansible.
- **Design for Failure:** Bulkheads, circuit breakers, retries, timeouts, fallbacks.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Advantages (≈3.5 marks):**
- **Independent Deployability** → Faster time-to-market.
- **Targeted Scalability** → Cost-efficient cloud scaling.
- **Technology Freedom** → Polyglot, best-fit tools.
- **Fault Isolation** → Resilience, no single point of failure.
- **Parallel Development** → Multiple teams work concurrently.
- **Reusability** → Services are independently composable.

**Challenges (≈3.5 marks):**
- **Distributed System Complexity** — Network latency, partial failures.
- **Data Consistency** — No ACID transactions across services; need Saga/CQRS.
- **Operational Overhead** — Need mature DevOps, observability, and orchestration.
- **Testing Complexity** — Integration, contract, and chaos testing are non-trivial.
- **Initial Cost & Learning Curve** — High upfront investment in tooling and skills.
- **Security Surface** — More network endpoints = larger attack surface.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Examiners check **balance** in this answer. Many students write *all advantages, no challenges* and lose up to **3 marks**. Always structure as "**Pros X**" + "**Cons Y**" with at least 3 points each.

---

### **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Actual Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat **architectural diagram**, describe the **key components of a Microservices Architecture**. Explain the role of API Gateway, Service Registry, and Circuit Breaker. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the **Saga Pattern** for managing distributed transactions in microservices. How does it differ from the **Two-Phase Commit (2PC)** protocol? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Diagram (draw during exam — use the structure from Section 4.1):**

```
[Client] → [API Gateway] → [Microservices A, B, C, …]
                ↓                ↑
        [Service Registry]   [Service Registry]
                ↓                ↓
        [Centralized Config]   [Circuit Breaker wraps each call]
                                  ↓
                          [Observability / Logging]
```

**Role of Key Components:**

1. **API Gateway (≈2.5 marks):**
   - Single entry point for all client requests.
   - Responsibilities: Request routing, **authentication & authorization**, **rate limiting**, **request/response transformation**, **protocol translation** (e.g., REST → gRPC).
   - Tools: Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway, Spring Cloud Gateway.

2. **Service Registry (≈2.5 marks):**
   - Maintains a dynamic catalog of running service instances and their network locations.
   - Enables **client-side discovery** (client queries registry) or **server-side discovery** (load balancer queries registry).
   - Tools: Netflix Eureka, HashiCorp Consul, etcd, Kubernetes DNS.

3. **Circuit Breaker (≈2 marks):**
   - Prevents **cascading failures** by detecting when a downstream service is slow/unavailable and **temporarily short-circuiting** the call.
   - Three states: **Closed** (normal), **Open** (block calls), **Half-Open** (probe recovery).
   - Tools: Resilience4j, Hystrix (deprecated), Istio service mesh.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**The Distributed Transaction Problem (≈1 mark):**
In microservices, a single business workflow (e.g., "Book a Trip") spans multiple services — Flight Booking, Hotel Booking, Payment. Traditional **ACID transactions** with 2PC are infeasible because:
- Services have **independent databases** (no global lock manager).
- 2PC is a **blocking protocol** that doesn't scale horizontally.
- 2PC has a **single coordinator** as a single point of failure.

**The Saga Pattern (≈4 marks):**
A Saga is a sequence of **local transactions** $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$, where each $T_i$ updates one service's database. Each step publishes an event/message that triggers the next step. If any $T_i$ fails, **compensating transactions** $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_{i-1}$ are executed to **semantically undo** prior steps.

**Two Coordination Styles:**
- **Choreography:** No central coordinator. Services publish events (`OrderCreated`, `PaymentProcessed`); others react. *Loose coupling, but hard to trace.*
- **Orchestration:** A central **Saga Orchestrator** explicitly tells each service what to do next. *Easier to manage, but introduces a coordinator dependency.*

**Saga vs. 2PC Comparison (≈2 marks):**

| Property | Saga | Two-Phase Commit (2PC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency Model** | Eventual consistency | Strong (ACID) |
| **Locking** | No global locks | Holds locks until commit |
| **Scalability** | **High** | **Low** |
| **Latency** | Low (no waiting) | High (coordinator waits) |
| **Failure Handling** | Compensating transactions | In-doubt transactions, rollback |
| **Coupling** | Service-level | Database-level (tight) |
| **Use Case Fit** | Microservices, cloud-native | Monolith RDBMS, single DB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often say *"Saga provides ACID guarantees."* This is **factually wrong** — Saga provides **Atomicity at the business level** (all-or-nothing *eventually*) and **Eventual Consistency**, not strict ACID. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 5 Minute Power-Refresh**

- [ ] **Definition:** Microservices = *suite of small, independent, loosely coupled, business-capability-focused services.*
- [ ] **Origin:** Martin Fowler & James Lewis (2014).
- [ ] **9 Characteristics:** Componentization, Business Capability, Products-not-Projects, Smart Endpoints/Dumb Pipes, Decentralized Governance, Decentralized Data, Infra Automation, Design for Failure, Evolutionary Design.
- [ ] **Database-per-Service** is a **non-negotiable** rule.
- [ ] **No shared schema** between services → cross-service queries go via **APIs or Events**.
- [ ] **API Gateway** is the **single entry point** — handles auth, routing, throttling.
- [ ] **Service Registry** handles **dynamic service discovery** (Eureka, Consul).
- [ ] **Circuit Breaker** has 3 states: **Closed → Open → Half-Open**.
- [ ] **Saga Pattern** = eventual consistency via **local transactions + compensating actions**.
- [ ] **Choreography vs. Orchestration:** Decentralized events vs. centralized orchestrator.
- [ ] **Strangler Fig Pattern** = safe, incremental migration from monolith.
- [ ] **Availability math:** $A_{system} = \prod A_i$ — *exponential decay* motivates resilience.
- [ ] **Avoid synchronous chains** > 3 services — use **async messaging** for fan-out.
- [ ] **Conway's Law** governs team-to-service mapping.
- [ ] **Microservices Premium:** $T_{breakeven} \leq 12$ months is the healthy threshold.
- [ ] **Smart Endpoints + Dumb Pipes** — *never put business logic in the message broker*.
- [ ] **Trade-off:** Microservices are *not* always better — Monolith first, then decompose when needed.
- [ ] **12-Factor App** methodology is the **de facto compliance checklist** for cloud-native microservices.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always end a microservices answer with a **balanced conclusion** — *"Microservices offer superior scalability, agility, and resilience for complex domains, but they introduce distributed-systems complexity, operational overhead, and require mature DevOps culture. They are not a universal solution."* This **balanced closing line** is a KTU scoring differentiator and frequently earns the final 0.5–1 mark in Part-B answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microservices Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microservices Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: MICROSERVICES ARCHITECTURE
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microservices Architecture (also known as Microservice Architecture)** is an architectural style that structures an application as a collection of **small, autonomous, loosely coupled services**, where each service is:
- **Self-contained** and implements a single business capability
- **Independently deployable** in an automated production environment
- **Organized around business domains** rather than technical layers
- **Owned by a small, long-lived team**
- **Communicates through well-defined, lightweight mechanisms** (typically HTTP/REST APIs, gRPC, or message brokers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Martin & Fowler):** "An approach to developing a single application as a suite of small services, each running in its own process and communicating with lightweight mechanisms, often an HTTP resource API."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Insight:** Microservices is NOT just "small services." It is a paradigm shift in how we think about software boundaries — the boundary is drawn around **business capability**, not around **technical layers** (UI layer, database layer, etc.).

---

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy: The Shopping Mall

Imagine a massive **department store** (this is your *Monolithic Application*). Everything happens under one roof:
- Electronics, groceries, clothes, and pharmacy are all crammed together
- If the roof leaks, the entire store shuts down
- Renovation of the pharmacy requires closing the whole building
- Adding a new section (say, a cafe) is a major structural project

Now imagine a **modern shopping mall** (this is your *Microservices Architecture*):
- Each store (electronics, groceries, pharmacy) is an **independent business**
- Each has its own entrance, billing system, inventory, and staff
- One store closing for renovation does NOT affect others
- A new store can be added by just leasing a new space
- Each store communicates with others only through well-defined interfaces (the mall's common delivery corridors, shared customer loyalty programs)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** "MALL = Microservices: **M**any **A**utonomous **L**oosely-coupled **L**ittle-services"

---

### 1.3 The Nine Defining Characteristics (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Componentization via Services** | Replaceable units, not libraries |
| 2 | **Organized around Business Capability** | Cross-functional teams, not layered teams |
| 3 | **Products not Projects** | "You build it, you run it" philosophy |
| 4 | **Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes** | Business logic in services, not in ESB |
| 5 | **Decentralized Governance** | Right tool for the right job |
| 6 | **Decentralized Data Management** | Each service owns its database |
| 7 | **Infrastructure Automation** | CI/CD, automated testing, deployment |
| 8 | **Design for Failure** | Assume services WILL fail; handle it gracefully |
| 9 | **Evolutionary Design** | Replace services gradually, not big-bang rewrites |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing "Microservices" with "SOA (Service-Oriented Architecture)." They are NOT the same. SOA uses a central **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)** with heavy protocols (SOAP, WS-*), while Microservices use **lightweight, decentralized** communication (REST, gRPC, message queues).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core 'Why' — Problems with Monolithic Architecture

A monolith is a single deployable unit where **all functionality resides in one codebase**. Let us break down its failure modes:

- **Tight Coupling** → A change in one module breaks unrelated modules
- **Single Point of Failure** → One bug in payment code can take down the whole site
- **Technology Lock-in** → Stuck with one language/framework forever
- **Scaling Inefficiency** → Must scale the entire app, even if only one feature is hot
- **Deployment Bottleneck** → Every change requires redeploying the whole app
- **Team Coordination Overhead** → 50 developers committing to one repo = merge hell

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Trade-off:** Monoliths are **simpler to build and deploy initially** but become a nightmare at scale. Microservices are **harder to build initially** but provide **massive flexibility at scale**.

---

### 2.2 Decomposition Strategy: How to Slice the Monolith

The core engineering question is: **"Where do I draw the service boundaries?"**

#### Approach 1: Decomposition by Business Capability
Break services along the natural business functions:

```
E-commerce Monolith
        |
        +--> Order Management Service
        +--> Inventory Service  
        +--> Payment Service
        +--> Shipping Service
        +--> User Account Service
        +--> Notification Service
```

#### Approach 2: Decomposition by Domain-Driven Design (DDD) Bounded Contexts
- Identify **subdomains** (core, supporting, generic)
- Each subdomain becomes a microservice
- Use **Context Mapping** to define relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) for Services:** A microservice should do **ONE thing** and do it **WELL**. If the service name contains "AND" (e.g., "OrderAndPaymentService"), it is a code smell — split it.

---

### 2.3 Communication Patterns

#### Pattern A: Synchronous Communication
- **REST over HTTP** (most common)
- **gRPC** (high-performance, uses Protocol Buffers)
- **GraphQL** (client-driven queries)

#### Pattern B: Asynchronous Communication
- **Message Queues**: RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka
- **Event Streaming**: Kafka, AWS Kinesis
- **Pub/Sub Systems**: Google Pub/Sub, Amazon SNS

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (from KTU expected answers):** "Use **synchronous (REST/gRPC)** for **queries** (read operations). Use **asynchronous (events/messages)** for **state changes** (write operations that trigger downstream work)."

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Microservices is conceptual, but KTU examiners love quantitative reasoning. Memorize these metrics and formulas.

| # | Metric / Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|------------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Total scalability = sum of individual service scalabilities | requests/sec |
| 2 | $A_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | System availability = product of all service availabilities | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 3 | $L_{total} = L_{network} + L_{processing} + L_{queue}$ | End-to-end latency = network + processing + queueing latency | milliseconds (ms) |
| 4 | $T_{deploy} = T_{build} + T_{test} + T_{release}$ | Total deployment time for one service | minutes |
| 5 | $C_{comm} = n(n-1)/2$ | Communication complexity for $n$ services (worst case) | connection pairs |
| 6 | $T_{failure\_blast} = 1/n$ | Failure blast radius (fraction of users impacted) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Coupling_{ms} \propto \frac{1}{Cohesion}$ | Service quality: high cohesion $\Rightarrow$ low coupling | qualitative |
| 8 | $RT_{p95} = 1.65 \cdot \sigma + \mu$ | 95th percentile response time | ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Highlight (Availability Cascade):** If you have **5 services** each with **99.9% availability**, the system availability is:
> 
> $$A_{system} = 0.999^5 \approx 0.995$$
> 
> This is why microservices **demand** design patterns like **Circuit Breakers, Retries, and Bulkheads**.

---

### 2.5 The CAP Theorem Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> **CAP Theorem** (Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance) — In a distributed microservices system, **partition tolerance is mandatory** (network failures are inevitable). So you must choose between **CP** (Consistency) or **AP** (Availability) per service.

| Choice | Meaning | When to Use |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **CP** | Strong consistency, may fail under network partition | Banking, inventory, billing |
| **AP** | Always available, eventual consistency | Social feeds, recommendations, analytics |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Service Decomposition Cost Model

Let us mathematically derive **when microservices become economically justified** over a monolith. This is a common KTU 14-mark analytical question.

#### Step 1: Define the Cost Function for a Monolith

The total cost of a monolithic system $C_M$ includes:
- **Coordination cost** (merge conflicts, integration testing): grows quadratically with team size
- **Deployment cost** (full redeploy for any change): grows linearly with system size

$$C_M = \alpha \cdot n^2 + \beta \cdot s + \gamma$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of developers
- $s$ = system size (lines of code, in KLOC)
- $\alpha$ = coordination cost coefficient
- $\beta$ = deployment cost coefficient
- $\gamma$ = fixed infrastructure cost

#### Step 2: Define the Cost Function for Microservices

For microservices, coordination cost drops (smaller teams per service) but **operational complexity** rises (DevOps, monitoring, inter-service communication):

$$C_{\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( \alpha_i \cdot n_i^2 \right) + \beta \cdot s + \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2$$

Where:
- $k$ = number of services
- $n_i$ = team size for service $i$ (smaller than $n$)
- $\delta$ = per-service operational cost (monitoring, CI/CD pipeline)
- $\epsilon$ = inter-service communication complexity cost

#### Step 3: Equate and Solve the Break-Even Point

Setting $C_M = C_{\mu}$ to find the breakeven team size $n^*$:

$$\alpha \cdot n^2 + \beta \cdot s + \gamma = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i \cdot n_i^2 + \beta \cdot s + \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2$$

Simplifying by canceling $\beta \cdot s$:

$$\alpha \cdot n^2 + \gamma = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i \cdot n_i^2 + \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2$$

Assuming $n_i \approx n/k$ and $\alpha_i \approx \alpha$ (homogeneous services):

$$\alpha \cdot n^2 + \gamma = k \cdot \alpha \cdot \left( \frac{n}{k} \right)^2 + \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2$$

$$\alpha \cdot n^2 + \gamma = \frac{\alpha \cdot n^2}{k} + \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2$$

Rearranging to solve for $k$:

$$\alpha \cdot n^2 - \frac{\alpha \cdot n^2}{k} = \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2 - \gamma$$

$$\alpha \cdot n^2 \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{1}{k} \right) = \delta \cdot k + \epsilon \cdot k^2 - \gamma$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** As team size $n$ grows, the LHS (monolith coordination cost) grows quadratically, eventually exceeding the RHS (microservices operational cost). This defines the **migration threshold**.

#### Step 4: The Simplified Heuristic (Melvin Conway's Law)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conway's Law (1968):** "Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations."

For KTU exams, the practical heuristic is:

$$k_{optimal} \approx \sqrt{\frac{\alpha \cdot n^2}{\epsilon}}$$

Where:
- If $k_{optimal} > k_{current}$ $\Rightarrow$ **More decomposition needed**
- If $k_{optimal} < k_{current}$ $\Rightarrow$ **Over-decomposed; merge services**

---

### 3.2 The Analytical Matrix: Microservices Design Patterns

| # | Pattern Name | Purpose | When to Use |
|---|--------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **API Gateway** | Single entry point for clients; handles routing, auth, rate-limiting | Always in client-facing systems |
| 2 | **Service Registry & Discovery** | Services find each other dynamically (e.g., Eureka, Consul) | When services scale up/down frequently |
| 3 | **Circuit Breaker** | Prevents cascading failures (e.g., Hystrix, Resilience4j) | For any synchronous inter-service call |
| 4 | **Saga Pattern** | Manages distributed transactions across services | When a business process spans multiple services |
| 5 | **Event Sourcing** | Store state as a sequence of events | When audit trail is critical (banking) |
| 6 | **CQRS** | Separate read/write models | When read and write workloads are very different |
| 7 | **Bulkhead** | Isolate failures to one part of the system | To prevent one slow service from blocking all threads |
| 8 | **Database per Service** | Each service owns its data store | Always (core principle) |
| 9 | **Strangler Fig Pattern** | Gradually replace monolith with microservices | During migration from monolith |
| 10 | **Sidecar Pattern** | Deploy helper components alongside main service (e.g., Envoy proxy) | For cross-cutting concerns (logging, security) |

---

### 3.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Application Question Example:** "How would Netflix use microservices to handle 200 million users streaming globally?"

**Step-by-step application:**

1. **Decomposition:** Netflix has 700+ microservices (Playback, Recommendations, Billing, User Profiles, Search, etc.)
2. **API Gateway:** Zuul handles all incoming traffic
3. **Service Discovery:** Eureka registers and locates services
4. **Circuit Breaker:** Hystrix wraps every remote call to prevent cascading failures
5. **Chaos Engineering:** Netflix **deliberately kills production services** (Chaos Monkey) to test resilience
6. **Data Decentralization:** Cassandra (NoSQL) chosen for write-heavy workloads; MySQL for billing

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Microservices Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[CLIENT LAYER Web Mobile IoT]
    gateway[API GATEWAY Routing Auth Rate Limiting]
    registry[SERVICE REGISTRY Eureka Consul]
    config[CONFIG SERVER Centralized Config]
    
    subgraph BUSINESS_SERVICES
        orderSvc[ORDER SERVICE]
        paymentSvc[PAYMENT SERVICE]
        inventorySvc[INVENTORY SERVICE]
        userSvc[USER SERVICE]
        notifySvc[NOTIFICATION SERVICE]
    end
    
    subgraph DATA_LAYER
        orderDB[(ORDER DB PostgreSQL)]
        paymentDB[(PAYMENT DB PostgreSQL)]
        invDB[(INVENTORY DB MongoDB)]
        userDB[(USER DB MySQL)]
    end
    
    msgBus[ASYNC MESSAGE BUS Kafka RabbitMQ]
    monitor[OBSERVABILITY Logs Metrics Traces]
    
    client --> gateway
    gateway --> orderSvc
    gateway --> paymentSvc
    gateway --> userSvc
    gateway --> inventorySvc
    
    registry -.-> orderSvc
    registry -.-> paymentSvc
    registry -.-> inventorySvc
    registry -.-> userSvc
    
    config -.-> orderSvc
    config -.-> paymentSvc
    
    orderSvc --> msgBus
    paymentSvc --> msgBus
    inventorySvc --> msgBus
    
    msgBus --> notifySvc
    
    orderSvc --> orderDB
    paymentSvc --> paymentDB
    inventorySvc --> invDB
    userSvc --> userDB
    
    orderSvc -.-> monitor
    paymentSvc -.-> monitor
    inventorySvc -.-> monitor
    userSvc -.-> monitor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows = synchronous calls (REST/gRPC). Dotted arrows = control plane interactions (registry lookups, config pushes, telemetry). The **message bus** is the asynchronous backbone.

---

### 4.2 The Strangler Fig Migration Pattern (Monolith to Microservices)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    legacy[LEGACY MONOLITH]
    newSvcA[NEW SERVICE A]
    newSvcB[NEW SERVICE B]
    newSvcC[NEW SERVICE C]
    router[FACE ROUTER INTERCEPTOR]
    user1[USERS]
    
    user1 --> router
    router -->|Route 1| legacy
    router -->|Route 2| newSvcA
    router -->|Route 3| newSvcB
    router -->|Route 4| newSvcC
    
    legacy -.->|Future Phase| newSvcC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Martin Fowler named this after the **strangler fig tree** that grows around a host tree, eventually replacing it. This is the **only recommended way** to migrate to microservices — NEVER do a "big-bang rewrite."

---

### 4.3 The Saga Pattern (Distributed Transaction Management)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CO as ORDER SERVICE
    participant PS as PAYMENT SERVICE
    participant IS as INVENTORY SERVICE
    participant SH as SHIPPING SERVICE
    
    CO->>PS: Step 1 Deduct Payment
    PS-->>CO: Step 1 Success
    CO->>IS: Step 2 Reserve Inventory
    IS-->>CO: Step 2 Success
    CO->>SH: Step 3 Schedule Shipping
    SH-->>CO: Step 3 Success
    
    Note over CO,SH: Compensation if Step 3 fails
    CO->>IS: Compensate Release Inventory
    CO->>PS: Compensate Refund Payment
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The Saga pattern uses **compensating transactions** instead of ACID rollbacks because microservices span multiple databases. There is no global transaction!

---

### 4.4 Circuit Breaker State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> closed
    closed --> open: Failure threshold exceeded
    open --> halfOpen: Timeout period elapsed
    halfOpen --> closed: Test call succeeds
    halfOpen --> open: Test call fails
    closed --> closed: Normal operation
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State Meanings:**
> - **CLOSED:** Normal — all requests pass through
> - **OPEN:** Service is failing — fail fast, do not call
> - **HALF_OPEN:** Test if the downstream service has recovered
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define Microservices Architecture. List any four characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

Microservices Architecture is an architectural style that structures an application as a suite of small, independently deployable services, each running in its own process and communicating via lightweight mechanisms like HTTP/REST APIs.

**Four characteristics:**
1. **Componentization via Services** — Replaceable units replacing in-process libraries
2. **Organized around Business Capability** — Cross-functional teams owning end-to-end functionality
3. **Decentralized Data Management** — Each service owns its own database
4. **Design for Failure** — Services are designed assuming other services may fail

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between Monolithic Architecture and Microservices Architecture.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Monolithic | Microservices |
|--------|------------|---------------|
| **Deployment** | Single deployable unit | Multiple independent units |
| **Scaling** | Scale entire application | Scale individual services |
| **Technology** | Single tech stack | Polyglot (mixed tech) |
| **Failure Impact** | One bug can crash entire system | Failures isolated per service |
| **Team Structure** | Large teams, layered | Small cross-functional teams |
| **Database** | Single shared database | Database per service |
| **Initial Complexity** | Low | High |
| **Long-term Complexity** | High | Manageable |

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Expected Pattern]**
> ### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** Explain the nine characteristics of Microservices Architecture in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The nine characteristics of Microservices Architecture, as defined by Lewis and Fowler, are:

**(1) Componentization via Services:**
Traditional applications use in-process function calls or library linking for componentization. Microservices replace these with **out-of-process services** that communicate via remote calls (HTTP/REST, gRPC). This makes services **independently replaceable and upgradeable** without touching other components.

**(2) Organized around Business Capability:**
Instead of having separate UI teams, database teams, and server-side teams (horizontal layering), microservices use **vertical slicing** — each team owns one end-to-end business function (e.g., the "Delivery" team handles delivery UI, business logic, and database).

**(3) Products not Projects:**
A project mindset: "build it, hand it over, move on." A product mindset: **"You build it, you run it."** The same team that develops a microservice also operates it in production, including 24/7 support.

**(4) Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes:**
Traditional SOA used a heavy **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)** with business logic embedded in the middleware. Microservices push **all business logic into the endpoints (services themselves)** and use **dumb pipes** (simple HTTP/gRPC/Kafka) for transport.

**(5) Decentralized Governance:**
No central standards committee deciding "everyone must use Java + Oracle." Each team picks the **right tool for the job** — Python for ML services, Erlang for telecom, Go for high-throughput services.

**(6) Decentralized Data Management:**
Each service owns its **private database**. No service can directly access another service's database. Cross-service data is reconciled via APIs or events. This avoids the "one giant shared database" anti-pattern.

**(7) Infrastructure Automation:**
Microservices require **CI/CD pipelines, automated testing, and continuous deployment**. Netflix, Amazon, and Google deploy **thousands of times per day** — this is impossible without heavy automation (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Spinnaker).

**(8) Design for Failure:**
Microservices accept that **services will fail** (network outages, hardware crashes, software bugs). They implement patterns like **circuit breakers, retries with exponential backoff, bulkheads, and graceful degradation**.

**(9) Evolutionary Design:**
Microservices enable **gradual replacement** of old services. The **Strangler Fig Pattern** is used to incrementally migrate from monolith to microservices without a risky big-bang rewrite.

---

**Q.A (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **API Gateway Pattern** and **Service Registry & Discovery** in Microservices. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**API Gateway Pattern:**

The API Gateway is the **single entry point** for all client requests. It sits between clients and the internal microservices.

```
+--------+     +-----------+     +---------+ +---------+ +---------+
| Client | --> | API GATEWAY| --> | Order   | | Payment | | User    |
| (Web)  |     |            |     | Service | | Service | | Service |
+--------+     |            |     +---------+ +---------+ +---------+
               | - Routing  |
               | - Auth     |
               | - Logging  |
               | - Rate     |
               |   Limiting |
               +------------+
```

**Responsibilities of API Gateway:**
- **Request Routing:** Forwards `/orders/*` to Order Service, `/payments/*` to Payment Service
- **Authentication/Authorization:** Validates JWT tokens before passing to backend
- **Rate Limiting:** Prevents abuse (e.g., 100 req/sec per user)
- **Load Balancing:** Distributes traffic across multiple instances
- **Response Aggregation:** Combines multiple service responses into one (reduces client round-trips)
- **SSL Termination:** Handles HTTPS encryption/decryption

**Examples:** Netflix Zuul, AWS API Gateway, Kong, NGINX

**Service Registry & Discovery:**

In a dynamic microservices environment, services **scale up and down constantly**. Hardcoding service URLs is impossible. The **Service Registry** solves this.

```
+---------------------+
|  SERVICE REGISTRY   |     (e.g., Eureka, Consul, Zookeeper)
|  -----------------  |
|  order-svc   : 4    |
|  payment-svc : 2    |
|  user-svc    : 3    |
+---------------------+
        ^
        |  Register/Heartbeat
        |
+-------+-------+     +-----------+     +-----------+
| Order Service |     | Payment   |     | User      |
| (3 instances)  |     | Service   |     | Service   |
+----------------+     +-----------+     +-----------+
```

**Working Mechanism:**
1. **Registration:** When a service instance starts, it registers itself with the registry (sends IP, port, service name).
2. **Heartbeat:** The instance sends periodic heartbeats (e.g., every 30 seconds) to prove it is alive.
3. **Discovery:** When Service A wants to call Service B, A asks the registry: "Give me a healthy instance of B."
4. **Deregistration:** When a service shuts down gracefully, it deregisters itself.

**Two Discovery Styles:**
- **Client-Side Discovery:** Client queries registry, picks instance, calls directly (e.g., Netflix Eureka + Ribbon)
- **Server-Side Discovery:** Client hits a load balancer which queries the registry (e.g., AWS ALB + ECS)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Alternative Pattern]**
> ### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**Q.B (a)** Compare Monolithic, SOA, and Microservices Architectures. Discuss the challenges of Microservices. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Monolithic | SOA | Microservices |
|-----------|------------|-----|---------------|
| **Service Size** | Single unit | Medium-large services | Small, focused services |
| **Coupling** | Tight (in-process) | Loose (ESB-mediated) | Very loose (REST/events) |
| **Data Storage** | Single shared DB | Often shared | Database per service |
| **Communication** | Function calls | SOAP/ESB (heavy) | REST/gRPC/Kafka (light) |
| **Governance** | Centralized | Centralized standards | Decentralized |
| **Deployment** | Single deploy | Complex ESB deploys | Independent per service |
| **Best For** | Small apps, startups | Enterprise integration | Large-scale, cloud-native |

**Challenges of Microservices:**

1. **Distributed System Complexity:** Network latency, partial failures, and message loss become first-class concerns
2. **Data Consistency:** No global ACID transactions — must use Saga pattern, eventual consistency
3. **Network Security:** More service-to-service communication = larger attack surface
4. **Operational Overhead:** Requires mature DevOps culture (CI/CD, monitoring, logging)
5. **Service Boundaries:** Wrong decomposition leads to "distributed monolith" — worst of both worlds
6. **Testing Complexity:** End-to-end testing across services is harder than monolith unit testing
7. **Debugging Difficulty:** A single user request may traverse 10+ services — requires distributed tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin)
8. **Data Fragmentation:** Reports/joins across services require separate aggregation mechanisms

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **Circuit Breaker Pattern** and **Saga Pattern** with diagrams. Why is the Circuit Breaker critical for microservices? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit Breaker Pattern:**

The Circuit Breaker prevents **cascading failures** in microservices. When a downstream service is failing, the circuit breaker **trips open** and immediately returns an error without even calling the failed service — this prevents thread pool exhaustion.

**Three States (refer Section 4.4 diagram):**
- **CLOSED:** Requests flow normally. Failures are counted.
- **OPEN:** Threshold exceeded (e.g., 5 failures in 10 seconds). All requests fail fast for a cooldown period (e.g., 30 seconds).
- **HALF_OPEN:** After cooldown, one trial request is sent. If it succeeds → back to CLOSED. If it fails → back to OPEN.

**Why is Circuit Breaker Critical?**
1. **Prevents Thread Pool Exhaustion:** Without it, all threads wait on a slow downstream service
2. **Fail-Fast Behavior:** Returns error in milliseconds instead of 30-second timeouts
3. **Self-Healing:** Gives downstream service time to recover
4. **User Experience:** Graceful degradation — show cached data instead of a blank screen

**Example:** Hystrix (Netflix), Resilience4j, Polly (.NET)

**Saga Pattern:**

A Saga is a sequence of **local transactions** where each service performs its work and publishes an event/message for the next service. If any step fails, **compensating transactions** are executed to undo the previous steps.

**Example: E-commerce Order Saga**

```
1. Order Service   → Create Order (PENDING)
2. Payment Service → Charge Customer ✓
3. Inventory Svc   → Reserve Stock ✓
4. Shipping Svc    → Schedule Shipment ✗ FAILS!
                    ↓
   COMPENSATION:
   3'. Inventory   → Release Reservation
   2'. Payment     → Refund Customer
   1'. Order       → Mark as CANCELLED
```

**Two Saga Coordination Styles:**
- **Choreography:** Each service publishes events; others listen (no central coordinator)
- **Orchestration:** A central Saga Orchestrator commands each service (more control, less coupling)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT write generic web definitions** — KTU expects Martin-Fowler terminology, Lewis-Fowler characteristics
> 2. **Do NOT skip diagrams** — Every 7-mark question expects at least one diagram (lose 2 marks otherwise)
> 3. **Do NOT confuse CAP theorem consistency choices** — clearly state CP vs AP with example services
> 4. **Do NOT forget "Database per Service"** — this is the #1 most-tested principle
> 5. **Always mention "Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes"** — examiners love this Lewis-Fowler term
> 6. **Numerical formulas must be derived, not just stated** — show the algebraic steps
> 7. **Saga compensating transactions are NOT rollbacks** — emphasize this distinction

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the exam hall:**

- [x] Microservices = suite of small, autonomous, business-aligned services
- [x] Nine characteristics (Lewis & Fowler) — memorize all 9
- [x] Monolith vs SOA vs Microservices — know the differences precisely
- [x] API Gateway = single entry point; responsibilities include routing, auth, rate limiting
- [x] Service Registry = dynamic service location (Eureka, Consul)
- [x] Circuit Breaker has 3 states: CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN
- [x] Saga pattern uses **compensating transactions**, NOT rollbacks
- [x] Database per service is a **NON-NEGOTIABLE** principle
- [x] CAP theorem: in microservices, partition tolerance is mandatory → choose CP or AP per service
- [x] Strangler Fig pattern for monolith migration (gradual, not big-bang)
- [x] Smart Endpoints, Dumb Pipes — push logic into services, not the middleware
- [x] Decentralized data + decentralized governance
- [x] Design for failure — assume services will fail
- [x] **System Availability = product of individual service availabilities** ($A_{system} = \prod A_i$)
- [x] Communication complexity for $n$ services = $n(n-1)/2$ (worst case)
- [x] Always include at least one Mermaid/diagram in 7-mark questions
- [x] Use real-world examples: Netflix (700+ services), Amazon, Uber, Airbnb
- [x] Avoid distributed monolith anti-pattern — this is the #1 failure mode of microservices adoption
- [x] CQRS + Event Sourcing for high-scale, audit-heavy systems
- [x] Sidecar pattern for cross-cutting concerns (logging, security via Envoy/Istio)
- [x] Conway's Law: system design mirrors team communication structure
- [x] Microservices is NOT always the right answer — for small teams and small apps, monolith is often superior

---

topics.title: Microservice Deployment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST411 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microservice Deployment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microservice Deployment** is the systematic process of packaging, releasing, managing, and orchestrating independently deployable, loosely coupled service units (microservices) across distributed infrastructure environments. Each microservice represents a self-contained business capability with its own runtime process, data store, and deployment lifecycle, often encapsulated within containers and managed through orchestrators.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, microservice deployment encompasses the **operationalization phase** of the microservices architecture lifecycle, bridging **Continuous Integration (CI)**, **Continuous Delivery (CD)**, and runtime governance under the **DevOps paradigm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST411 Module 4):**
> Microservice Deployment focuses on the *how* of releasing microservices — covering **containerization (Docker)**, **orchestration (Kubernetes)**, **deployment patterns (Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling)**, **CI/CD pipelines**, and **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **restaurant chain (McDonald's, KFC)** versus a **single home kitchen**:

| Aspect | Home Kitchen (Monolith Deployment) | Restaurant Chain (Microservice Deployment) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooking Style** | One chef prepares the entire meal sequentially | Specialized stations: grill, fries, beverages run in parallel |
| **Failure Impact** | If the stove breaks, no food is served | If the fryer fails, burgers and drinks still flow |
| **Scaling** | You must cook more of *everything* to serve more customers | You add fryers only when fries demand spikes |
| **Update** | Redesigning the menu means rebuilding the kitchen | A new burger recipe updates only the grill station |

**Microservice Deployment** is the *logistics manager* who places each specialized station in the right location, scales stations independently, replaces failed stations without shutting the chain, and updates recipes (code) for one station at a time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Deployment Granularity:** A well-engineered microservice deployment should support **independent release cycles** measured in minutes-to-hours, not weeks. The industry benchmark is **Deployment Frequency** — a core DORA metric where elite performers deploy **on-demand, multiple times per day**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Must Memorize)

- **Container**: A lightweight, portable, OS-level virtualization unit bundling application code + dependencies.
- **Image**: An immutable, read-only template used to instantiate containers.
- **Orchestrator**: A control-plane system (e.g., Kubernetes) that automates container placement, scaling, and healing.
- **Service Mesh**: A dedicated infrastructure layer (e.g., Istio, Linkerd) handling inter-service communication.
- **Rolling Update**: A strategy that incrementally replaces old service instances with new ones.
- **Blue-Green Deployment**: Two identical production environments (Blue = current, Green = new) with instant traffic switching.
- **Canary Deployment**: Gradual rollout to a small percentage of users before full release.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 The Microservice Deployment Lifecycle (Operational View)

The deployment of a microservice follows a **5-stage pipeline** aligned with modern DevOps practice:

### Stage 1 — Source Commit & Build
- Developer pushes code to a **Version Control System (VCS)** such as Git.
- **CI Server** (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) triggers an automated build.
- A **container image** is generated from a `Dockerfile`.

### Stage 2 — Image Registry Push
- The built image is tagged (e.g., `v1.4.2`) and pushed to a **container registry** (Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, Azure Container Registry).
- This is the **immutable artifact** that flows through subsequent stages.

### Stage 3 — Orchestrated Release
- The **orchestrator** (Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Nomad) pulls the image.
- A **deployment manifest** (YAML for Kubernetes) declares:
  - Desired replica count
  - Resource limits (CPU, memory)
  - Health probes (liveness, readiness)
  - Network policies
- The orchestrator **schedules** pods onto nodes using bin-packing algorithms.

### Stage 4 — Traffic Steering & Validation
- The new version receives traffic via one of the **deployment strategies** (Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling).
- **Observability tools** (Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger) collect metrics, logs, and traces to validate the **SLOs** (Service Level Objectives).

### Stage 5 — Promotion or Rollback
- If SLOs are met → **Promote** to 100 percent traffic.
- If SLOs breach → **Automated Rollback** to the previous stable version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each step matters:** Skipping image immutability leads to "it works on my machine" issues. Skipping health probes causes traffic to hit unready pods. Skipping observability turns deployments into blind bets.

## 2.2 The 5 Major Deployment Patterns — KTU Formula Sheet

The table below is the **single most important comparison** for any exam question on this topic. KTU examiners frequently test the ability to **distinguish these patterns** and **match them to scenarios**.

| **Parameter** | **Rolling Update** | **Blue-Green** | **Canary** | **Recreate** | **A/B Testing** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategy Formula** | $N_{new} = \lceil R \cdot p \rceil$ | $T_{switch} = 0$ (instant) | $p_{canary} \in [1\%, 10\%]$ | $N_{old} = 0$ first | $T_{split} = f(userID)$ |
| **Downtime** | **Zero** | **Zero** | **Zero** | **Yes (brief)** | **Zero** |
| **Rollback Speed** | Slow (gradual) | Instant (switch back) | Fast (reroute traffic) | N/A (redeploy old) | Instant |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | 1x baseline | **2x baseline** (parallel env) | $\sim 1.1$x baseline | 1x baseline | 1.5x–2x baseline |
| **Risk Level** | Medium | Low | Very Low | High | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Routine releases | Critical, risky releases | Validating real user behavior | Major schema breaks | Feature experimentation |
| **Traffic Routing** | Gradual pod replacement | DNS or Load Balancer flip | Weighted routing in mesh | All-or-nothing | Header-based routing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation decoded (for the exam):**
> - $N_{new}$ = number of new-version replicas
> - $R$ = total replica count
> - $p$ = rollout progress fraction (0 to 1)
> - $p_{canary}$ = percentage of users routed to the new version
> - $T_{switch}$ = traffic switch time
> - $T_{split}$ = traffic split function

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microservice deployment is the **operational backbone** of every modern large-scale platform:

- **Netflix** deploys thousands of microservices per day using **Spinnaker** for blue-green and canary releases.
- **Amazon** uses microservice deployment to scale its e-commerce platform during Prime Day without downtime.
- **Uber** uses **Kubernetes-based canary deployments** to roll out pricing algorithm changes to a small city first.
- **Banking systems** use blue-green deployments to ensure **zero-downtime compliance** for regulatory changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Rolling Update Replica Calculation

This is the **most common numerical problem** KTU asks. Given a service with $R$ total replicas, determine how many new-version pods exist after progress fraction $p$.

### Given
- Total desired replicas: $R = 10$
- Rollout progress: $p = 0.4$ (40 percent complete)

### Step 1 — Define the rollout formula
The number of new-version replicas after progress $p$ is:

$$
N_{new} = \lceil R \cdot p \rceil
$$

The number of old-version replicas still running is:

$$
N_{old} = R - N_{new}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
N_{new} = \lceil 10 \cdot 0.4 \rceil
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the inner product

$$
N_{new} = \lceil 4.0 \rceil
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the ceiling function

$$
N_{new} = 4
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate old-version replicas

$$
N_{old} = 10 - 4 = 6
$$

### Step 6 — Verification
At $p = 1.0$ (full rollout):

$$
N_{new} = \lceil 10 \cdot 1.0 \rceil = 10
$$

$$
N_{old} = 10 - 10 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

At $p = 0.0$ (rollout start):

$$
N_{new} = \lceil 10 \cdot 0.0 \rceil = 0
$$

$$
N_{old} = 10 - 0 = 10 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the ceiling function?** Kubernetes enforces `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable` constraints, meaning the number of new pods must always be a discrete integer — never a fractional pod.

## 3.2 Derivation: Canary Traffic Distribution Math

Suppose you have a service receiving **$U$ total users per second** and you want to route **$p_{canary}$ percent** to the new version.

### Given
- Total users: $U = 5000$ requests per second
- Canary percentage: $p_{canary} = 5\%$

### Step 1 — Define traffic split
Users on canary version:

$$
U_{canary} = U \cdot p_{canary}
$$

Users on stable version:

$$
U_{stable} = U - U_{canary}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
U_{canary} = 5000 \cdot 0.05
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$
U_{canary} = 250 \text{ users/sec}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate stable traffic

$$
U_{stable} = 5000 - 250 = 4750 \text{ users/sec}
$$

### Step 5 — Promotion decision formula
Promote canary to full rollout if the **error rate** of the canary is within a threshold $\epsilon$ of the stable version:

$$
\vert E_{canary} - E_{stable} \vert \leq \epsilon
$$

Where:
- $E_{canary}$ = error rate of new version
- $E_{stable}$ = error rate of old version
- $\epsilon$ = acceptable error differential (typically 0.1 percent)

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Containerizing and Deploying a Microservice

This walkthrough models the **practical flow** KTU expects you to describe in 14-mark questions.

### Step 1 — Write the Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install --production
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

### Step 2 — Build the image

```
docker build -t orderservice:v1.2.0 .
```

### Step 3 — Push to registry

```
docker push registry.ktu.ac.in/orderservice:v1.2.0
```

### Step 4 — Author the Kubernetes Deployment manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 5
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order
        image: registry.ktu.ac.in/orderservice:v1.2.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "256Mi"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 3000
```

### Step 5 — Apply the manifest

```
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
```

### Step 6 — Verify rollout status

```
kubectl rollout status deployment/order-service
```

### Step 7 — Rollback if needed

```
kubectl rollout undo deployment/order-service
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microservice Deployment Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end microservice deployment topology** — from developer commit to end-user traffic. This is the **canonical answer sketch** for 14-mark architectural questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    devA[Developer Commits Code] --> repoA[Git Repository]
    repoA --> ciA[CI Pipeline Jenkins]
    ciA --> imgA[Build Container Image]
    imgA --> regA[Container Registry]
    regA --> orchA[Kubernetes Orchestrator]
    orchA --> nodeA[Node 1 Pods]
    orchA --> nodeB[Node 2 Pods]
    orchA --> nodeC[Node 3 Pods]
    nodeA --> meshA[Service Mesh Istio]
    nodeB --> meshA
    nodeC --> meshA
    meshA --> lbA[Load Balancer]
    lbA --> userA[End Users]
    orchA --> monA[Prometheus Monitoring]
    monA --> grafA[Grafana Dashboard]
    monA --> alertA[Alertmanager Rollback Trigger]
    alertA --> orchA
    classDef infra fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef user fill:#059669,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef obs fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class devA,repoA,ciA,imgA,regA,orchA,nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,meshA,lbA infra
    class userA user
    class monA,grafA,alertA obs
```

## 4.2 Deployment Strategy Decision Flow (Block Diagram)

This block diagram helps examiners visualize **how a deployment strategy is selected** based on risk and infrastructure cost constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[New Microservice Release] --> q1A{Criticality Level}
    q1A --> highA[High Criticality Banking Health]
    q1A --> medA[Medium Criticality Ecommerce]
    q1A --> lowA[Low Criticality Internal Tool]
    highA --> bgA[Blue Green Deployment]
    medA --> rollA[Rolling Update]
    lowA --> recrA[Recreate Strategy]
    bgA --> costA[Cost 2x Baseline]
    rollA --> costA
    recrA --> costA
    bgA --> validA[Instant Rollback]
    rollA --> validA
    recrA --> validA
    classDef strat fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cost fill:#ea580c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class bgA,rollA,recrA strat
    class costA,validA cost
```

## 4.3 CI/CD Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    phase1A[Source Commit] --> phase2A[Unit Tests]
    phase2A --> phase3A[Build Image]
    phase3A --> phase4A[Integration Tests]
    phase4A --> phase5A[Push to Staging]
    phase5A --> phase6A[Canary Deploy 5 percent]
    phase6A --> phase7A{Metrics Healthy}
    phase7A --> yesA[Promote to 100 percent]
    phase7A --> noA[Auto Rollback]
    classDef pass fill:#16a34a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef fail fill:#dc2626,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class yesA pass
    class noA fail
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Explain the concept of Blue-Green deployment in microservices. Mention one major drawback.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Blue-Green deployment is a release strategy in which **two identical production environments** — Blue (current stable version) and Green (new version) — are maintained simultaneously.

- The new version is deployed to the **Green environment** and tested in isolation.
- Once validated, a **load balancer or router** switches 100 percent of user traffic from Blue to Green instantaneously.
- If the new version fails, traffic is switched back to Blue — enabling **zero-downtime rollback**.

**Major Drawback:** It requires **double the infrastructure cost** (2x baseline) since both environments run in parallel, plus data synchronization challenges between the two environments.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **infrastructure cost** or the **data synchronization issue** between Blue and Green environments. Both points are mandatory for full marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between Containerization and Virtualization in microservice deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Containerization (Docker)** | **Virtualization (VMware, KVM)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Level** | OS-level | Hardware-level |
| **Boot Time** | **Seconds** (milliseconds in practice) | **Minutes** |
| **Image Size** | MBs (lightweight) | GBs (heavy) |
| **Resource Overhead** | Minimal (shares host kernel) | High (separate OS per VM) |
| **Isolation** | Process-level | Full OS-level |
| **Density on Host** | Hundreds of containers | 10–20 VMs typically |

Containers package the microservice code **with its dependencies** but **share the host OS kernel**, making them far more efficient for microservice deployment where **density and startup speed** are critical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Module 4 questions carry an **internal choice** — you answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end CI/CD deployment pipeline** for a microservice-based application. List the tools used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Rolling Update, Blue-Green, and Canary deployment strategies**. For an online banking transaction service, justify which strategy is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) CI/CD Deployment Pipeline (7 marks)**

A **Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipeline** automates the journey of microservice code from developer commit to production release.

**Pipeline Stages:**

1. **Source Stage:** Developer pushes code to a Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket).
2. **Build Stage:** CI server (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) compiles the code and runs **unit tests**.
3. **Image Stage:** A **Docker image** is built using the Dockerfile and tagged with a semantic version (e.g., `paymentservice:v2.1.0`).
4. **Test Stage:** Integration tests, security scans (Snyk, Trivy), and quality gates execute against the image.
5. **Registry Stage:** The validated image is pushed to a **container registry** (Docker Hub, AWS ECR, Azure ACR).
6. **Deploy-to-Staging Stage:** Kubernetes (or similar orchestrator) deploys the image to a **staging cluster**.
7. **Canary Stage:** A small percentage of production traffic (5–10 percent) is routed to the new version.
8. **Monitor & Promote Stage:** Prometheus/Grafana validate SLOs; if healthy, the deployment is promoted to 100 percent; if unhealthy, an automatic rollback occurs.

**Tools Mapping:**

| **Stage** | **Industry-Standard Tools** |
|---|---|
| Source | Git, GitHub, GitLab |
| CI | Jenkins, GitHub Actions, CircleCI |
| Containerization | Docker, Podman |
| Registry | Docker Hub, AWS ECR, Harbor |
| Orchestration | Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Nomad |
| Service Mesh | Istio, Linkerd |
| Monitoring | Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger |
| IaC | Terraform, Ansible, Helm |

---

**(b) Deployment Strategy Comparison (7 marks)**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| **Criterion** | **Rolling Update** | **Blue-Green** | **Canary** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Incrementally replaces old pods with new pods | Maintains two parallel environments and switches traffic atomically | Routes a small subset of users to the new version first |
| **Downtime** | Zero | Zero | Zero |
| **Rollback Speed** | Slow (gradual reversal) | Instantaneous | Fast (revert traffic split) |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | 1x | 2x | ~1.1x |
| **Risk Exposure** | Medium (real user traffic throughout) | Low (validated before switch) | Very Low (small blast radius) |
| **Rollback Granularity** | Per-pod | Environment-level | Percentage-based |
| **Best For** | Routine, low-risk updates | Critical, high-risk releases | Validating new features with real users |

**Justification for Online Banking Transaction Service:**

For an **online banking transaction service**, the recommended strategy is **Blue-Green Deployment**.

**Reasoning:**

1. **Zero-downtime requirement:** Banking transactions cannot tolerate even a 1-second outage. Blue-Green enables instant atomic traffic switching.
2. **Regulatory compliance:** Financial systems must demonstrate **immediate rollback capability** to satisfy RBI/SEC audit trails. Blue-Green offers the fastest rollback.
3. **Data integrity:** Green environment can be validated against a **mirror database** before any live transaction touches it, preventing data corruption.
4. **Predictable behavior:** Banking users expect deterministic behavior — partial canary rollouts may cause inconsistent experiences for users with partially-deployed features.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students choose **Canary** for banking. While valid, you must justify why Blue-Green is **superior** for this specific use case by emphasizing **regulatory compliance and instant rollback**. A weak justification costs 2–3 marks.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of Kubernetes in microservice deployment**. Describe the function of **Pods, Deployments, Services, and Ingress** with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)**? Explain how **Terraform** and **Helm** are used in microservice deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) Role of Kubernetes in Microservice Deployment (7 marks)**

**Kubernetes (K8s)** is the de-facto **container orchestrator** for microservice deployments. It automates the **deployment, scaling, healing, and networking** of containerized microservices.

**Core Kubernetes Objects:**

1. **Pod** — The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. A pod encapsulates **one or more tightly coupled containers**, shared storage, and network identity.
   - Example: A pod containing the `payment-service` container and a `log-shipping` sidecar container.

2. **Deployment** — A declarative controller that manages a **replicated set of identical pods**. It enables rolling updates, rollbacks, and scaling.
   - Example: A `Deployment` manifest declaring `replicas: 5` ensures 5 instances of the payment service are always running.

3. **Service** — A stable **network endpoint** (virtual IP + DNS name) that load-balances traffic across a dynamic set of pods.
   - Example: A `ClusterIP` Service named `payment-service` routes requests from other microservices to whichever pods are healthy.

4. **Ingress** — An **HTTP/HTTPS router** that exposes services to external traffic, with features like TLS termination, path-based routing, and host-based routing.
   - Example: `payments.ktu.ac.in` is routed to the `payment-service`, while `orders.ktu.ac.in` is routed to the `order-service`.

**Why Kubernetes matters:**

- **Self-healing:** Restarts failed pods automatically.
- **Auto-scaling:** Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales replicas based on CPU/memory or custom metrics.
- **Rolling updates:** Zero-downtime deployments.
- **Service discovery:** Built-in DNS for inter-service communication.

---

**(b) Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — Terraform and Helm (7 marks)**

**Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through **machine-readable definition files** rather than manual configuration.

**Terraform (by HashiCorp):**

- **Purpose:** Provisions and manages **cloud infrastructure** — virtual machines, networks, load balancers, databases, Kubernetes clusters.
- **Language:** HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language).
- **Workflow:** `terraform init` → `terraform plan` → `terraform apply`.
- **Use in Microservice Deployment:** Creates the EKS/GKE/AKS cluster, VPC, subnets, and security groups before any application code is deployed.

**Helm (Kubernetes Package Manager):**

- **Purpose:** Packages Kubernetes manifests into **versioned, reusable templates** called **charts**.
- **Use in Microservice Deployment:** A single `helm install` command can deploy a microservice with all its Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets — parameterized via `values.yaml`.
- **Example:** `helm upgrade --install order-service ./order-chart --version 1.4.0 --set image.tag=v1.4.0`

**Synergy Between Terraform and Helm:**

| **Layer** | **Tool** | **Responsibility** |
|---|---|---|
| **Infrastructure Layer** | Terraform | Creates the cluster, networking, and cloud resources |
| **Application Layer** | Helm | Deploys and configures microservices on the cluster |

Together, they enable **fully reproducible, version-controlled, automated microservice deployments** — a cornerstone of modern DevOps practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Terraform** (infrastructure provisioning) with **Helm** (application packaging). Marks are deducted when students say Terraform "deploys containers" — Terraform *provisions* the cluster; Helm *deploys* the workloads.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

- [ ] **Microservice deployment** = independently releasing, scaling, and updating loosely coupled services.
- [ ] **Containerization (Docker)** packages code + dependencies into immutable images.
- [ ] **Orchestration (Kubernetes)** automates pod placement, scaling, healing, and rolling updates.
- [ ] **Rolling Update** = incremental replacement; zero downtime; best for routine releases.
- [ ] **Blue-Green** = two parallel environments; instant rollback; **2x cost**; best for critical systems.
- [ ] **Canary** = small traffic percentage first; very low risk; best for validating new features.
- [ ] **Recreate** = downtime acceptable; best for major breaking changes.
- [ ] **A/B Testing** = header-based routing; best for feature experimentation.
- [ ] **Rolling update formula:** $N_{new} = \lceil R \cdot p \rceil$
- [ ] **Canary traffic formula:** $U_{canary} = U \cdot p_{canary}$
- [ ] **Promotion criterion:** $\vert E_{canary} - E_{stable} \vert \leq \epsilon$
- [ ] **CI/CD Pipeline stages:** Source → Build → Test → Image → Registry → Deploy → Monitor → Promote/Rollback.
- [ ] **Kubernetes core objects:** Pod, Deployment, Service, Ingress.
- [ ] **Terraform** = infrastructure provisioning (cluster, network, cloud).
- [ ] **Helm** = application packaging on Kubernetes (charts, values.yaml).
- [ ] **Service Mesh (Istio)** = handles inter-service communication, traffic shaping, mTLS.
- [ ] **Observability stack:** Prometheus (metrics) + Grafana (visualization) + Jaeger (tracing).
- [ ] **DORA Metrics:** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, MTTR, Change Failure Rate.
- [ ] **Key deployment commands:** `kubectl apply`, `kubectl rollout status`, `kubectl rollout undo`.
- [ ] **Examiner's golden rule:** Always justify *why* a deployment strategy is chosen for a *specific* domain (banking → Blue-Green, ecommerce → Rolling, social app → Canary).
